People  of  impeccable\nrectitude have to be handpicked.\u201d [187]\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_761\">100.  Critical analysis of <a href=\"/doc/1164880/\" id=\"a_1515\">Articles 124</a><a href=\"/doc/1682952/\" id=\"a_1516\">, 217</a> and <a href=\"/doc/140653939/\" id=\"a_1517\">124-A</a> and  <a href=\"/doc/61056230/\" id=\"a_1518\">124-B</a>  leads  to\nthe position that the Executive Branch of Government cannot push through  an\n\u2018undeserving candidate\u2019 so long as at least  two  members  representing  the\nJudicial Branch are united  in  their  view  as  to  unsuitability  of  that\ncandidate. Even one eminent person and a  single  judicial  member  of  NJAC\ncould effectively stall entry of an unworthy  appointment.   Similarly,  the\njudicial members also cannot push through persons of their choice unless  at\nleast one other member belonging to  the  non-judicial  block  supports  the\ncandidate proposed by them.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_762\">101.  A democratic form  of  government  is  perhaps  the  best  institution\ninvented for preservation of liberties.   At least that  is  the  belief  of\nsocieties which adopt this model of  governance.     True,  there  are  many\nvariants of democracy.  Analysis of the variants is  outside  the  scope  of\nthis judgment.  Under any constitutional model,  primary  responsibility  to\npreserve liberties of  the  people  is  entrusted  to  the  legislative  and\nexecutive branches.   Such entrustment is predicated on the  structural  and\nempirical assumption that legislators chosen periodically  would  strive  to\nprotect the liberties of their \u201conly masters \u2013 the  people\u201d.   This  is  for\ntwo reasons operating in tandem.   They are the obligation to discharge  the\ntrust reposed and  the  fear  of  losing  the  glory  of  being  the  chosen\nrepresentative.   An  in  built  possibility  in  the  system  of   periodic\nelections.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_763\">102.  To assume or  assert  that  judiciary  alone  is  concerned  with  the\npreservation of liberties and does that job well, is an assumption  that  is\ndogmatic, bereft of evidentiary basis and  historically  disproved.  Eminent\nconstitution jurist and teacher Laurence H. Tribe has the following  to  say\nin the context of the American experience.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_764\">\u201cNo one should assume that the Supreme Court need always  strike  down  laws\nand executive actions in order to protect our liberties.   On the  contrary,\nsometimes the Court best guarantees our rights by deferring to, rather  than\noverruling, the political branches.   When the Supreme Court, from  1900  to\n1937, struck down dozens of child labor laws,  minimum  wage  laws,  working\ncondition regulations, and  laws  protecting  workers;  rights  to  organize\nunions, on the ground that such  rules  infringed  on  property  rights  and\nviolated \u201cliberty of contract,\u201d the only rights the Court really  vindicated\nwere the rights to be overworked,  underpaid,  or  unemployed.    The  Court\neventually reversed itself on these  issues  when  it  recognized  that,  in\ntwentieth-century America, such laws are not intrusions upon  human  freedom\nin any meaningful sense, but are instead entirely reasonable and  just  ways\nof combating economic subjugation.   In upholding a minimum wage law in  the\nwatershed case of West Coast Hotel v. Parrish, the Supreme  Court  concluded\nin 1937 that, in the light of \u201crecent economic  experience\u201d,  such  statutes\nwere justified because they prevent \u201cthe exploitation of a class of  workers\nin ways detrimental to their health and well being.\u201d\n\nNaturally, in this  imperfect  world,  the  Supreme  Court  has  not  always\nguarded our liberties as jealously as it should.   During  the  First  World\nWar and again  in  the  McCarthy  era,  the  Court  often  shrank  from  the\naffirmation of our rights to think and speak as we  believe.    And  in  the\nwar hysteria following  bombing  of  Pearl  Harbor,  the  Supreme  Court  in\nKorematsu  v.  United  States  upheld  the  imprisonment  of  thousands   of\nAmericans of Japanese ancestry who had committed no  crime.    In  light  of\nsuch lapses, some have argued that when it comes to  protecting  fundamental\nrights, the Supreme Court is essentially redundant: on  most  occasions  the\nCongress and the President will adequately  safeguard  our  rights,  and  in\nthose difficult times when the political  branches  cannot  be  counted  on,\nneither can the Court.\u201d[188]\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_765\">103.  Our experience is not dissimilar.   Judgments  in  A.K.  Gopalan[189],\nSankalchand[190] and ADM Jabalpur[191] (to mention a few) should lead to  an\nidentical inference that in difficult times when political  branches  cannot\nbe counted upon, neither  can  the  Judiciary.    The  point  sought  to  be\nhighlighted is that judiciary is not the  ONLY  constitutional  organ  which\nprotects liberties of the people.  Accordingly, primacy to  the  opinion  of\nthe judiciary in the matter of judicial appointments is not  the  only  mode\nof  securing  independence  of  judiciary  for  protection   of   liberties.\nConsequently, the assumption that primacy of  the  Judicial  Branch  in  the\nappointments process is an essential element and thus  a  basic  feature  is\nempirically flawed without any basis either in  the  constitutional  history\nof the Nation or any other  and  normatively  fallacious  apart  from  being\ncontrary to political theory.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_766\">104.  I now deal with the submission that presence of the  law  minister  in\nthe  NJAC  undermines  independence   of   judiciary.   According   to   the\npetitioners, the presence of a member of the Executive  invariably  has  the\neffect of shifting the power dynamics.  The presence of the Law Minister  in\nthe NJAC which confers 1/6  of  the  voting  power  per  se  undermines  the\nindependence of the judiciary.  The submission is untenable.  The  Executive\nwith a vast administrative machinery under its control is capable of  making\nenormous and valuable contribution to the selection process.  The  objection\nis justified to some extent on the trust deficit in the Executive Branch  in\nthe constitutional sense[192], to be a component  of  the  NJAC.   The  same\nlogic applies a fortiari to the Judicial branch, notwithstanding the  belief\nthat  it  is  the  least  dangerous   branch.   The   Constituent   Assembly\nemphatically declined to repose  exclusive  trust  even  in  the  CJI.    To\nwholly eliminate the Executive  from  the  process  of  selection  would  be\ninconsistent with the foundational premise that government  in  a  democracy\nis by chosen representatives  of  the  people.   Under  the  scheme  of  our\nConstitution, the Executive  is  chartered  clear  authority  to  administer\ncritical areas such as defence of the realm, internal security,  maintenance\nof public order, taxation, management of  fiscal  policies  and  a  host  of\nother aspects, touching every aspect of the  administration  of  the  Nation\nand lives of its people. In this context, to hold that it should be  totally\nexcluded from the process of appointing judges  would  be  wholly  illogical\nand inconsistent with the foundations of  the  theory  of  democracy  and  a\ndoctrinal heresy.  Such exclusion has no parallel  in  any  other  democracy\nwhose models were examined by the Constituent Assembly and none  other  were\nbrought to our notice  either.   Established  principles  of  constitutional\ngovernment, practices in other democratic  constitutional  arrangements  and\nthe fact that the Constituent Assembly provided a  role  for  the  Executive\nclearly  prohibit  the  inference  that  Executive  participation   in   the\nselection process abrogates a basic feature.  The Attorney General is  right\nin his submission that exclusion of the Executive Branch is  destructive  of\nthe basic feature of checks  and  balances  \u2013  a  fundamental  principle  in\nconstitutional theory.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_767\">105.  That takes me to  the  second  provision  which  is  under  challenge.\n<a href=\"/doc/140653939/\" id=\"a_1519\">Article 124A.</a>(1)(d) which stipulates that the NJAC  should  consist  of  two\neminent persons[193].  Considerable debate took place during the  course  of\nhearing regarding validity of this provision, the gist of which is  captured\nin the judgment of Khehar, J.  The attack is again on the  ground  that  the\nprovision is utterly  without  guidance  regarding  the  choice  of  eminent\npersons.  Petitioners argued that (i) there could be  bipartisan  compromise\nbetween the party in power and the opposition, resulting in sharing the  two\nslots earmarked for eminent  persons.   Such  possibility  would  eventually\nenable  political  parties  to  make  appointments   purely   on   political\nconsiderations, thereby destroying  independence  of  judiciary;  (ii)  even\nassuming that the two  eminent  persons  nominated  are  absolute  political\nneutrals, but are strangers to the judicial system, they would not  be  able\nto make any meaningful contribution to the selection process, as they  would\nhave no resources to collect appropriate  data  relevant  for  the  decision\nmaking process; (iii) the possibility of two  eminent  persons  vetoing  the\ncandidature of a person approved unanimously by the three  judicial  members\nof the NJAC itself is destructive of the basic structure.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_768\">106.  Transparency is a vital factor  in  constitutional  governance.   This\nCourt in innumerable cases noted that constitutionalism demands  rationality\nin every sphere of State action.  <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1643138\" id=\"span_306\">In the context  of  judicial  proceedings,\nthis Court held in <a href=\"/doc/1643138/\" id=\"a_1520\">Naresh Shridhar Mirajkar &amp; Ors. v. State  of  Maharashtra\n&amp; Anr.</a>[194]:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_478\">\u201c20. \u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026.Public trial in open court  is  undoubtedly  essential  for  the\nhealthy, objective and fair administration of justice.  Trial  held  subject\nto the public scrutiny and gaze naturally acts as a check  against  judicial\ncaprice or vagaries, and  serves  as  a  powerful  instrument  for  creating\nconfidence of the public in the fairness, objectivity, and  impartiality  of\nthe administration of justice. Public confidence in  the  administration  of\njustice is of such great significance that there can be no two  opinions  on\nthe broad proposition  that  in  discharging  their  functions  as  judicial\ntribunals, courts must generally hear causes in open  and  must  permit  the\npublic admission to the court-room. As Bentham has observed:\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_479\">\u201cIn the darkness of secrecy sinister interest,  and  evil  in  every  shape,\nhave full swing. Only in proportion as publicity has place can  any  of  the\nchecks  applicable  to  judicial  injustice  operate.  Where  there  is   no\npublicity there is no justice. Publicity is the very soul of justice. It  is\nthe keenest spur to exertion, and surest of all  guards  against  improbity.\nIt keeps the Judge himself while trying under trial (in the sense that)  the\nsecurity of securities is publicity.\u201d\n\nTransparency is an aspect of rationality.   The  need  for  transparency  is\nmore in the case of appointment process.  Proceedings of the collegium  were\nabsolutely opaque and inaccessible  both  to  public  and  history,  barring\noccasional leaks.  Ruma Pal , J. is on record -\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_480\">\u201cConsensus within the collegium is sometimes resolved  through  a  trade-off\nresulting in dubious  appointments  with  disastrous  consequences  for  the\nlitigants  and  the  credibility   of   the   judicial   system.    Besides,\ninstitutional independence has also been compromised by  growing  sycophancy\nand \u2018lobbying\u2019 within the system.\u201d[195]\n\nOne beneficial purpose the induction of  representatives  of  civil  society\nwould hopefully serve is that it acts as a check on  unwholesome  trade-offs\nwithin the collegium and  incestuous  accommodations  between  Judicial  and\nExecutive branches.  To believe that members of the  judiciary  alone  could\nbring valuable inputs to the appointment process requires great conceit  and\ndisrespect for the civil society.  Iyer, J. cautioned -\n\u201c74. \u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026 And when criteria for transfers of Judges are put forward  by  the\nPresident which may upset past practices we  must,  as  democrats,  remember\nLearned Hand who once said that the spirit of liberty is \u201cthe  spirit  which\nis not too sure that it is right\u201d.  That great Judge was fond  of  recalling\nCromwell\u2019s statement : \u201cI beseech ye in the bowels of Christ, think that  ye\nmay be mistaken.\u201d  He told a Senate Committee.  \u201cI should like to have  that\nwritten over the portals of every church,  every  school  and  every  court-\nhouse, any may I say, of every legislative body in  the  United  States.   I\nshould like to have every court  begin  \u201cI  beseech  ye  in  the  bowels  of\nChrist, think that we may be mistaken.\u201d (Yale Law Journal : Vol.71  :  1961,\nNovember part).\u201d[196]\n                                                         [emphasis supplied]\n\nReplace \u201ctransfers\u201d and \u201cPresident\u201d  with  \u201cappointments\u201d  and  \u201cParliament\u201d\nand Iyer, J\u2019s admonition is custom made to answer the  objections  (ii)  and\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_481\">(iii) of the petitioners.\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_769\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"21266288\" id=\"span_307\">107.  There  is  a  possibility  that  the  apprehension  expressed  by  the\npetitioners might come true.  The possibility of abuse of a power  conferred\nby the Constitution is no ground for denying the authority  to  confer  such\npower.   Bachawat, J. in <a href=\"/doc/21266288/\" id=\"a_1521\">I.C. Golak Nath</a> (supra) opined as follows:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_482\">\u201c235. It is said that the Parliament is abusing its power  of  amendment  by\nmaking too many frequent changes. If the Parliament has the  power  to  make\nthe amendments, the choice of making any particular amendment must  be  left\nto it. Questions of policy cannot be debated in this Court. The  possibility\nof abuse of  a  power  is  not  the  test  of  its  existence.  In  Webb  v.\nOutrim\u00a0[1907] A.C. 81, Lord Hobhouse said, &quot;If they find  that  on  the  due\nconstruction of the Act a legislative power falls within S. 92, it would  be\nquite wrong of them to deny its existence because  by  some  possibility  it\nmay of be abused, or limit the range which otherwise would be  open  to  the\nDominion Parliament&quot;. With reference to the doctrine of implied  prohibition\nagainst the exercise of power ascertained in accordance with ordinary  rules\nof construction, Knox C.J., in the Amalgamated Society of Engineers  v.  The\nAdelaide Steamship Company Limited 129  C.L.R.  151,  said,  &quot;It  means  the\nnecessity of protection against the aggression of some outside and  possibly\nhostile body. It is based on distrust, lest powers, if once conceded to  the\nleast degree, might be abused to the  point  of  destruction.  But  possible\nabuse of power is no reason in British law for limiting  the  natural  force\nof the language creating them&quot;.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_483\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"322504\" id=\"span_308\">However, it was a  dissenting  opinion.   But  this  Court  in  <a href=\"/doc/322504/\" id=\"a_1522\">I.R.  Coelho</a>\n(supra), Sabharwal, J. speaking for a unanimous Bench of nine  Judges,  held\nas follows:</span>\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_484\">\u201c76.  \u00a0It is also contended that the power to pack  up  laws  in  the  Ninth\nSchedule in absence of any indicia in <a href=\"/doc/282612/\" id=\"a_1523\">Article\u00a031B</a>\u00a0has been abused  and  that\nabuse is likely to continue. It is submitted that the Ninth  Schedule  which\ncommenced with only 13 enactments has now a  list  of  284  enactments.  The\nvalidity  of  <a href=\"/doc/282612/\" id=\"a_1524\">Article\u00a031B</a>\u00a0is  not  in  question  before  us.  Further,  mere\npossibility of abuse is not a relevant test to determine the validity  of  a\nprovision. The people, through the Constitution, have vested  the  power  to\nmake laws in their representatives through Parliament in the same manner  in\nwhich they have entrusted  the  responsibility  to  adjudge,  interpret  and\nconstrue  law  and  the  Constitution  including  its  limitation   in   the\njudiciary. We, therefore, cannot  make  any  assumption  about  the  alleged\nabuse of the power.\u201d\n                                                         [emphasis supplied]\nIn the final analysis, all power could be misused including judicial  power.\n The remedy is not to deny grant of power but  to  structure  it  so  as  to\neliminate the potential for  abuse.   The  power  to  nominate  two  eminent\npersons is conferred upon three  high  constitutional  functionaries  \u2013  the\nPrime Minister, the Leader of the Opposition and the CJI.  It is  elementary\npolitical knowledge that the Prime Minister and  the  Leader  of  Opposition\nwould always have conflicting political interests  and  would  rarely  agree\nupon any issue.  Nonetheless, possibility of a bipartisan compromise  cannot\nbe ruled out.  Though, the presence of CJI in the Committee should  normally\nbe a strong deterrent, the possibility of the  CJI  failing  to  perceive  a\npolitical compromise or  helplessness  in  the  event  of  such  compromise,\ncannot be ruled out.\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_770\">108. It is incontestable that nomination of eminent persons  is  not  immune\nto judicial review.  There is thus possibility of delay  in  functioning  of\nNJAC and inevitably the process of appointments  to  CONSTITUTIONAL  COURTS.\nIt is, therefore, essential that there must  be  an  entrenched  process  of\nnomination of eminent persons which eliminates risk of  possible  bipartisan\ncompromises.  The only conceivable curative is to incorporate  another  tier\nof scrutiny in the process  of  nomination.   In  my  considered  view,  the\nfollowing safeguard would bring this process within permissible contours  of\nthe basic  feature  simultaneously  eliminating  the  \u2018delay  factor\u2019.   The\nCommittee contemplated under <a href=\"/doc/14173262/\" id=\"a_1525\">Article 124-A(1)(a)</a> should prepare a  panel  of\nthree members for each of the two categories of the  nominees  (for  eminent\npersons) \u2013 in all a panel of six  persons.   Such  panel  should  be  placed\nbefore the full house of the Supreme Court for  voting.   Nominees  securing\nthe highest vote  in  each  of  the  two  categories  should  eventually  be\nnominated as eminent members  of  the  NJAC.   Such  procedure  would  still\npreserve  the  choice  of  eminent  persons  primarily  with  the  Committee\ncontemplated under <a href=\"/doc/140653939/\" id=\"a_1526\">Article 124-A</a>, while incorporating  sufficient  safeguard\nagainst possible abuse of the power by the Committee.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_771\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_309\">109.  The third provision whose validity is  under  attack  is  <a href=\"/doc/1164880/\" id=\"a_1527\">Article  124</a>\nB(c), which obligates NJAC to ensure  that  the  person  recommended  is  of\nability and integrity.  The challenge is on the ground  that  the  AMENDMENT\ndoes not lay down any guidelines to be followed by the  NJAC  for  assessing\nability and integrity.  Even in the  absence  of  any  express  declaration,\nsuch an obligation is inherent and  implied,  having  regard  to  functional\nresponsibilities  entrusted  to  the  NJAC.    The  precision  is  only   an\nabundanti cautela.  Perhaps prompted  by  certain  bad  experiences  of  the\npast, both pre and post <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1528\">Second Judges</a> case.</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_772\">110.  Having regard to  the  nature  (i)  of  the  document  by  which  such\nobligation is created; (ii) the composition of the body  (NJAC)  upon  which\nthe obligation is  cast;  and  (iii)  the  nature  of  the  assignment,  the\nargument is required to be rejected.  NJAC  is  a  constitutional  authority\ncreated to perform an important constitutional  function.   Its  charter  is\nthe  Constitution   itself.    Notwithstanding,   the   prolixity   of   our\nConstitution, a constitution is not expected or required to spell out  every\nminute detail regarding administration of the State.  In the context of  the\nAmerican Constitution, it is said that the Constitution is an  intentionally\nincomplete,   often   deliberately   indeterminate   structure    for    the\nparticipatory evolution of  political  ideals  and  governmental  practices.\nConstitutions enumerate structural arrangements of  Government  and  specify\nthe outer limits of powers of each organ of the State.  Within such  limits,\nhow the various organs of the  State  ought  to  discharge  their  allocated\nfunctions  is  a  matter  of  detail,  either  to  be  provided  by  law  or\nconvention. All written democratic Constitutions are full of abstract  moral\ncommands!\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_773\">111.  Three members of the highest judicial body of this country,  a  member\nof the  Union  Cabinet  and  two  eminent  persons  chosen  by  a  Committee\nconsisting  of  three  exalted  office  holders   under   the   Constitution\nconstitute the NJAC.  To suggest that the NJAC requires detailed  guidelines\nexpressly spelt out in the text of the Constitution  amounts  to  judicially\nmandating inflexible standards for  constitutional  drafting.  The  task  of\nexpounding a Constitution is crucially different from that of  construing  a\nstatute.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_774\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1748256\" id=\"span_310\">112.  Provisions of the Constitution are not to be interpreted  in  a  broad\nand liberal way.  They are not to be construed in  the  manner  in  which  a\npiece of subordinate legislation or, for that  matter,  even  a  statute  is\nrequired to be interpreted.  This Court in S.R. Bommai had  an  occasion  to\nconsider this question.  Dealing with the authority of the  President  under\n<a href=\"/doc/8019/\" id=\"a_1529\">Article 356</a> of the Constitution of India and whether the  exercise  of  such\nauthority by the President is amenable to judicial review on the  parameters\nenunciated by this Court in <a href=\"/doc/1748256/\" id=\"a_1530\">Barium Chemicals Ltd. v. Company Law Board</a>,  AIR\n1967 SC 295, rejected the submission.</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_775\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1748256\" id=\"span_311\">\u201c35.  \u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026 The test <a href=\"/doc/1748256/\" id=\"a_1531\">laid down by</a> this Court  in  <a href=\"/doc/1748256/\" id=\"a_1532\">Barium  Chemicals  Ltd.  v.\nCompany Law Board</a> and subsequent decisions for  adjudging  the  validity  of\nadministrative action can have no application for testing  the  satisfaction\nof the President under <a href=\"/doc/8019/\" id=\"a_1533\">Article 356.</a> It must be  remembered  that  the  power\nconferred by <a href=\"/doc/8019/\" id=\"a_1534\">Article 356</a> is of an extraordinary nature to  be  exercised  in\ngrave emergencies and, therefore, the  exercise  of  such  power  cannot  be\nequated to the power exercised  in  administrative  law  field  and  cannot,\ntherefore, be tested by the same yardstick. \u2026\u2026.</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_776\">255.  \u2026\u2026..  The exercise of the power under <a href=\"/doc/8019/\" id=\"a_1535\">Article 356</a> is a  constitutional\nexercise  of  the  power.  The  normal   subjective   satisfaction   of   an\nadministrative  decision  on  objective  basis  applied  by  the  courts  to\nadministrative  decisions  by  subordinate  officers  or  quasi-judicial  or\nsubordinate legislation does not apply to  the  decision  of  the  President\nunder <a href=\"/doc/8019/\" id=\"a_1536\">Article 356.</a>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_777\">373.  \u2026\u2026..  So  far  as  the  approach  adopted  by  this  Court  in  Barium\nChemicals6  is  concerned,  it   is   a   decision   concerning   subjective\nsatisfaction of an authority created by a statute.  The  principles  evolved\nthen cannot ipso facto be extended  to  the  exercise  of  a  constitutional\npower under <a href=\"/doc/8019/\" id=\"a_1537\">Article 356.</a> Having regard to the  fact  that  this  is  a  high\nconstitutional power exercised by the highest constitutional functionary  of\nthe Nation, it may not be appropriate to adopt the tests applicable  in  the\ncase of action taken by statutory or administrative  authorities  \u2014  nor  at\nany rate, in their entirety.\u201d\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_778\">113.  Such a test is relevant  only  for  bodies  created  by  statutes  and\nsubordinate legislation.  The functioning  of  any  constitutional  body  is\nonly disciplined by appropriate  legislation.   Constitution  does  not  lay\ndown any guidelines for the functioning of the President and Prime  Minister\nnor the Governors or the  Chief  Ministers.  Performance  of  constitutional\nduties entrusted to them is structured  by  legislation  and  constitutional\nculture. The provisions of the Constitution cannot be read like a last  will\nand testament lest it becomes  one.    Even  prior  to  the  AMENDMENT,  the\nconstitutional text had no express guidelines for the President and the  CJI\nto follow.  It is however nobody\u2019s case  that  the  pre-AMENDMENT  selection\nscenario conferred any uncanalised  discretion  and  therefore  resulted  in\nsome  undesirable  judicial  appointments.  If  in  practice,   occasionally\npersonal preferences outweighed concerns of  public  interest  resulting  in\nundesirable appointments, it is not because of  constitutional  silences  in\nthis area but because of  shortcomings  in  the  ethical  standards  of  the\nparticipants in the selection process. After the AMENDMENT,  the  obligation\nis unvaried.  The only change is in the composition of the players  to  whom\nthe task is entrusted and the mode of performing the task is altered with  a\nview to achieve greater degree of transparency  in  the  selection  process.\nTo contend that the AMENDMENT is destructive of the  basic  structure  since\nit does not lay down any guidelines tantamounts to holding  that the  design\nof the Constitution as originally enacted is defective!\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_779\">114.  The next submission which is required to  be  dealt  is  that  <a href=\"/doc/93595316/\" id=\"a_1538\">Section\n6(6)</a> of the ACT which stipulates that if any two members  of  the  NJAC   do\nnot agree with the recommendation proposed by the NJAC, the NJAC  shall  not\nrecommend such candidate.  In the  opinion  of  the  petitioners,  it  is  a\nprovision which confers veto power on two members of  the  NJAC  to  scuttle\nproposals.   It  is  submitted  that  though  the  provision   is   facially\ninnocuous, in practice, this would result in giving the  Executive  a  power\nof veto to reject the proposals made by the three judicial  members  of  the\nNJAC.  Such  a  provision  is  violative  of  the  basic  structure  of  the\nConstitution.  It is further argued that though the provision  is  not  part\nof the AMENDMENT, since the AMENDMENT and the ACT  are  made  simultaneously\nand  the  ACT  being  complementary  to  the  AMENDMENT,  the  ACT  must  be\nunderstood to be a part of the  design  of  the  AMENDMENT  and,  therefore,\n<a href=\"/doc/93595316/\" id=\"a_1539\">Section 6(6)</a> is required to be struck down on the ground it is violative  of\nthe basic structure of the Constitution.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_780\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_312\">115.   The  respondents  submitted  that  <a href=\"/doc/93595316/\" id=\"a_1540\">Section  6(6)</a>  of  the  ACT   only\nprescribes a special majority for sanctifying the recommendations  of  NJAC.\nPrescription of special majorities  in  law  is  a  known  phenomenon.   The\nConstitution itself prescribes special majorities  in  certain  cases.   For\nexample, <a href=\"/doc/1389240/\" id=\"a_1541\">Article 368(2)</a> prescribes  a  special  majority  for  amending  the\nConstitution.  Similarly, <a href=\"/doc/41081345/\" id=\"a_1542\">Article 124(4)</a> prescribes a special  majority  for\nthe impeachment of judges of the CONSTITUTIONAL COURTS.  It is  argued  that\nthe petitioners presumption that only Government  could  take  advantage  of\nthe prescription under <a href=\"/doc/93595316/\" id=\"a_1543\">Section 6(6)</a> is totally baseless.  In  a  given  case\nit may happen that two judicial members  of  the  NJAC  can  turn  down  the\nproposal of the NJAC.  Learned Attorney General also submitted that  such  a\nprescription of a special majority is also a  part  of  the  regime  created\nunder <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1544\">Second Judges</a> case and, therefore, there is  nothing  constitutionally\nobjectionable in such a prescription.</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_781\">116.  The question whether the content of <a href=\"/doc/93595316/\" id=\"a_1545\">Section 6(6)</a> confers  a  power  of\nveto or prescribes  a  special  majority  is  only  of  semantic  relevance.\nWhatever name we call it, the result is the same.  The two  members  of  the\nNJAC  can  override  the  opinion  of  the  other   four   and   stall   the\nrecommendation.  I do not find anything  inherently  illegal  about  such  a\nprescription.  For the purpose of the present case, I do not  even  want  to\nembark upon an enquiry whether the constitutional fascination for the  basic\nstructure  doctrine  be  made  a  Trojan  horse  to  penetrate  the   entire\nlegislative camp.  <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Neg\" data-docid=\"60799\" id=\"span_313\">For my part, I would like  to  examine  the  question  in\ngreater detail before answering the question.  There are  conflicting  views\nof this Court on this proposition.[197]  In my opinion, such an  enquiry  is\nnot required in this  case  in  view  of  the  majority  decision  that  the\nAMENDMENT  is  unsustainable.   Some  of  the  learned   counsel   for   the\npetitioners placed reliance on <a href=\"/doc/60799/\" id=\"a_1546\">S.R. Bommai</a> case as a justification  for  the\ninvocation of the doctrine of basic structure.</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_782\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Neg\" data-docid=\"60799\" id=\"span_314\">117.  Only to indicate but not  determine  conclusively  the  scope  of  the\nenquiry to answer the submission of the petitioners, I examine  <a href=\"/doc/60799/\" id=\"a_1547\">S.R.  Bommai</a>\ncase.   The question before  this  Court  was  whether  the  action  of  the\nPresident in invoking the powers  under  <a href=\"/doc/8019/\" id=\"a_1548\">Article  356</a>  was  constitutionally\ntenable?  In other words, whether the material on which the President  acted\nwas constitutionally relevant for the invocation  of  powers  under  Article</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_783\">356.  The submission of the petitioners  before  this  Court  was  that  the\nexercise of powers under <a href=\"/doc/8019/\" id=\"a_1549\">Article 356</a> was inconsistent with two  features  of\nthe Constitution, i.e. the democracy and federalism, therefore,  destructive\nof the basic  structure,  as  the  Presidential  action  under  <a href=\"/doc/8019/\" id=\"a_1550\">Article  356</a>\nresulted  in  the  super  session  of  the  democratically   elected   State\nGovernments by the Union Government.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_784\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"60799\" id=\"span_315\">118.  Repelling the contention, this Court held that secularism is also  one\nof the basic features of the Constitution.  The conscious  inaction  of  the\nvarious State Governments  and  consequential  failure  to  prevent  certain\nactivities which in the opinion of the petitioners (endorsed by  this  court\nby the judgment) would ultimately result in the destruction of  the  secular\nfabric of the Constitution has certainly a relevant  consideration  for  the\nexercise of extraordinary powers vested in the President under <a href=\"/doc/8019/\" id=\"a_1551\">Article  356.</a>\n  Because <a href=\"/doc/8019/\" id=\"a_1552\">Article 356</a> obligates  the  President  to  resort  to  the  action\ncontemplated thereunder only if the President is satisfied that a  situation\nhas arisen in which the Government of the State  cannot  be  carried  on  in\naccordance with the provisions of the Constitution.  Failure  of  the  State\nGovernment to prevent activities which are bound  to  destroy  the  communal\nharmony  between  people  following   different   religions   is   certainly\ninconsistent with the constitutional obligation of the State to  upheld  the\nConstitution of which secularism is a basic feature.  <a href=\"/doc/60799/\" id=\"a_1553\">S.R.  Bommai</a>  case  is\nno authority for the proposition that  the  validity  of  a  legislation  is\namenable to judicial review on the ground of the basic structure doctrine.</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_785\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"52257\" id=\"span_316\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"990570\" id=\"span_317\">119.  The fiasco created in <a href=\"/doc/990570/\" id=\"a_1554\">Dinakaran</a> case (<a href=\"/doc/52257/\" id=\"a_1555\">supra) and Shanti  Bhushan</a>  case\n(supra) would justify the participation of the members of the civil  society\nin the  process  to  eliminate  from  the  selection  process  the  maladies\ninvolved in the process pointed out by Ruma Pal, J.  The abovementioned  two\nare not the only cases where the system failed.  It is a  matter  of  public\nrecord that in the last 20 years, after the advent of the collegium  system,\nnumber of recommendations made by the collegia of High  Courts  came  to  be\nrejected by the collegium of the Supreme Court.  There are also cases  where\nthe collegium of this Court quickly retraced its steps having  rejected  the\nrecommendations of a particular  name  made  by  the  High  Court  collegium\ngiving scope for a great deal of speculation as to the  factors  which  must\nhave weighed with the collegium to  make  such  a  quick  volteface.    Such\ndecisions may be justified in some cases and may not in other cases.   There\nis no accountability in this regard.  The records are absolutely beyond  the\nreach of any person including the judges of this Court  who  are  not  lucky\nenough to become the Chief Justice of India.  Such a state of  affairs  does\nnot either enhance the credibility  of  the  institution  or  good  for  the\npeople of this country.</span></span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_786\">120.  For all the abovementioned reasons,  I  would  upheld  the  AMENDMENT.\nHowever, in view of the majority decision, I do not see any  useful  purpose\nin examining the constitutionality of the ACT.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_787\">121.   Only  an  independent  and  efficient  judicial  system  can   create\nconfidence in the society which it serves. The ever increasing  pendency  of\nmatters before various CONSTITUTIONAL COURTS of this country is clearly  not\na certificate  of  efficiency.   The  frequency  with  which  the  residuary\njurisdiction of this Court under <a href=\"/doc/427855/\" id=\"a_1556\">Article 136</a> is invoked  seeking  correction\nof errors committed by the High Courts, some of which are trivial  and  some\nprofound coupled with bewildering number of conflicting  decisions  rendered\nby the various benches of this Court  only  indicate  that  a  comprehensive\nreform of the system is overdue.  Selection process of  the  Judges  to  the\nCONSTITUTIONAL COURTS is only  one  of  the  aspect  of  such  reforms.   An\nattempt in that direction, unfortunately, failed to secure the  approval  of\nthis Court leaving this Court with the  sole  responsibility  and  exclusive\naccountability of the efficiency of the legal  system.   I  only  part  with\nthis case recollecting  the  words  of  Macaulay  \u2013  \u201creform  that  you  may\npreserve\u201d[198].  Future alone can tell whether  I  am  rightly  reminded  of\nthose words or not.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_788\">                                                              \u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026..J.\n                                                          ( J. Chelameswar )\nNew Delhi;\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_789\">October 16, 2015.\n</p>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_790\">                                                   REPORTABLE\n      IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA\n      CIVIL ORIGINAL JURISDICTON\n      WRIT PETITION (CIVIL) NO.13 OF 2015\n\nSupreme Court Advocates-on-Record-\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_791\">Association and another                      \u2026Petitioners\n                           Versus\nUnion of India                                     ..Respondent\nWITH\nWRIT PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 14 OF 2015\nWRIT PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 18 OF 2015\nWRIT PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 23 OF 2015\nWRIT PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 24 OF 2015\nWRIT PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 70 OF 2015\nWRIT PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 83 OF 2015\nTRANSFER PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 391 OF 2015\nWRIT PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 108 OF 2015\nWRIT PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 124 OF 2015\nWRIT PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 209 OF 2015\n                                        O R D E R\nMadan B. Lokur, J.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_792\">1.          I have  had  the  benefit  of  going  through  the  draft  order\nprepared by my learned brothers  Justice  Khehar,  Justice  Chelameswar  and\nJustice Kurian Joseph.  While endorsing the view  expressed  by  my  learned\nbrothers Justice Khehar and Justice Chelameswar, I would like to add  a  few\nwords on the procedural aspect of dealing with an application for recusal.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_793\">2.          Justice Khehar has mentioned in Paragraph 17 of the draft  order\nas follows:-\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_485\">\u201cThe decision to remain as a member of the  reconstituted  Bench  was  mine,\nand mine alone.\u201d\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_794\">3.          In my respectful opinion, when an application is  made  for  the\nrecusal of a judge from hearing a case,  the  application  is  made  to  the\nconcerned judge and not to the Bench  as  a  whole.  Therefore,  my  learned\nbrother Justice Khehar is absolutely correct in stating  that  the  decision\nis entirely his, and I respect his decision.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_795\">4.       In a detailed order pronounced in Court on its own motion v.  State\n&amp; Others[199] reference was made to a decision of the Supreme Court  of  the\nUnited States in Jewell Ridge Coal Corporation v.  Local  No.  6167,  United\nMine Workers of America[200], wherein it was held that  a  complaint  as  to\nthe qualification of a justice of the Supreme Court  to  take  part  in  the\ndecision of a cause cannot properly be addressed to the  Court  as  a  whole\nand it is the responsibility of each justice to determine  for  himself  the\npropriety of withdrawing from a case.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_796\">5.          This view was adverted to by Justice Rehnquist  in  Hanrahan  v.\nHampton[201] in the following words:-\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_486\">\u201cPlaintiffs-respondents and their counsel in these cases have moved  that  I\nbe recused from the proceedings in this  case  for  the  reasons  stated  in\ntheir 14-page motion and their five appendices filed with the Clerk of  this\nCourt on April 3, 1980.   The  motion  is  opposed  by  the  state-defendant\npetitioners in the action.  Since generally  the  Court  as  an  institution\nleaves such motions, even though they be addressed to it,  to  the  decision\nof the individual Justices to whom they refer, see Jewell Ridge  Coal  Corp.\nv. Mine Workers, 325 U.S. 897 (1945)  (denial  of  petition  for  rehearing)\n(Jackson, J., concurring), I shall treat  the  motion  as  addressed  to  me\nindividually.  I have considered the motion, the  Appendices,  the  response\nof the state defendants, 28 U.S.C. 455 (1976 ed.  And  Supp.  II),  and  the\ncurrent American Bar Association Code of Judicial Conduct,  and  the  motion\nis accordingly denied.\u201d\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_797\">6.          The issue of recusal  may  be  looked  at  slightly  differently\napart from the legal nuance.  What would happen  if,  in  a  Bench  of  five\njudges, an application is moved  for  the  recusal  of  Judge  A  and  after\nhearing the application Judge A decides to recuse  from  the  case  but  the\nother four judges  disagree  and  express  the  opinion  that  there  is  no\njustifiable reason for Judge A to recuse from the hearing?  Can Judge  A  be\ncompelled to hear the case even though he/she is desirous of  recusing  from\nthe hearing? It  is  to  get  over  such  a  difficult  situation  that  the\napplication for recusal is actually to  an  individual  judge  and  not  the\nBench as a whole.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_798\">7.          As far as the view  expressed  by  Justice  Kurian  Joseph  that\nreasons should be given while deciding an application for recusal,  I  would\nprefer not to join that decision.  In the first place, giving or not  giving\nreasons was not an issue before us. That reasons are presently  being  given\nis a different matter  altogether.   Secondly,  the  giving  of  reasons  is\nfraught with some difficulties.  For example,  it  is  possible  that  in  a\ngiven case, a learned judge of the High Court  accepts  an  application  for\nhis/her recusal from a case and one of the parties challenges that order  in\nthis Court.  Upon hearing the parties, this Court comes  to  the  conclusion\nthat the reasons given by the learned judge  were  frivolous  and  therefore\nthe order is incorrect and is then set aside.  In such an  event,  can  this\nCourt pass a consequential order requiring the learned  judge  to  hear  the\ncase even though he/she genuinely believes that he/she should not  hear  the\ncase?\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_799\">8.          The issue of recusal from hearing a case is not as simple as  it\nappears. The questions thrown up are quite significant and since it  appears\nthat  such  applications  are  gaining  frequency,  it  is  time  that  some\nprocedural and substantive rules are framed in this regard.  If  appropriate\nrules are framed, then, in a given case, it  would  avoid  embarrassment  to\nother judges on the Bench.</p><pre id=\"pre_9\">\n\n                                                    \u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026J\nNew Delhi                               (Madan B. Lokur)\nOctober 16, 2015\n\n\n                                                                  REPORTABLE\n                        IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA\n\n                         CIVIL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION\n\n                      WRIT PETITION (C) NO. 13 OF 2015\n\nSupreme Court Advocates-on-Record Association\n\u2026Petitioners\n&amp; Anr.\n                                   versus\n\nUnion of India\n\u2026Respondent\n                                    WITH\n\n|WRIT PETITION (C) NO. 23 OF 2015   |WRIT PETITION (C) NO. 70 OF 2015     |\n</pre><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_800\">|WRIT PETITION (C) NO. 83 OF 2015   |TRANSFER PETITION (C) NO.391 OF 2015 |\n|WRIT PETITION (C) NO. 108 OF 2015  |WRIT PETITION (C) NO. 124 OF 2015    |\n|WRIT PETITION (C) NO. 14 OF 2015   |WRIT PETITION (C) NO. 18 OF 2015     |\n|WRIT PETITION (C) NO. 24 OF 2015   |WRIT PETITION (C) NO. 209 OF 2015    |\n|WRIT PETITION (C) NO. 309 OF 2015  |WRIT PETITION (C) NO. 310 OF 2015    |\n|WRIT PETITION (C) NO. 323 OF 2015  |TRANSFER PETITION (C) NO. 971 OF 2015|\n|WRIT PETITION (C) NO. 341 OF 2015  |                                     |\n\n                               J U D G M E N T\n\nMadan B. Lokur, J.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_801\">1.     The  questions  for   consideration   are:   Firstly,   whether   the\n<a href=\"/doc/195108305/\" id=\"a_1557\">Constitution  (Ninety-ninth  Amendment)  Act,  2014</a>  which  substitutes  and\nreplaces the extant procedure for the appointment of judges of  the  Supreme\nCourt and the High Courts with a radically different procedure  impinges  on\nthe independence of the judiciary and violates the basic  structure  of  the\nConstitution;   Secondly,  whether  the   <a href=\"/doc/142995521/\" id=\"a_1558\">National   Judicial   Appointments\nCommission Act, 2014</a> is a constitutionally valid legislation.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_802\">2.    In my opinion, the <a href=\"/doc/195108305/\" id=\"a_1559\">Constitution  (Ninety-ninth  Amendment)  Act,  2014</a>\n(for short the 99th Constitution Amendment Act) alters the  basic  structure\nof the Constitution by introducing substantive changes  in  the  appointment\nof judges to the Supreme Court and the High  Courts  and  rewriting  <a href=\"/doc/1995948/\" id=\"a_1560\">Article\n124(2)</a>  and  <a href=\"/doc/259647/\" id=\"a_1561\">Article  217(1)</a>  of   the   Constitution,   thereby   seriously\ncompromising the independence  of  the  judiciary.  Consequently,  the  99th\nConstitution Amendment Act is unconstitutional. Since the 99th  Constitution\nAmendment  Act  is  unconstitutional,  the  <a href=\"/doc/142995521/\" id=\"a_1562\">National  Judicial  Appointments\nCommission Act, 2014</a> (for short the <a href=\"/doc/142995521/\" id=\"a_1563\">NJAC Act</a>) which  is  the  child  of  the\n99th Constitution Amendment Act cannot independently survive on the  statute\nbooks. Even otherwise,  it  violates  <a href=\"/doc/367586/\" id=\"a_1564\">Article  14</a>  of  the  Constitution  by\nenabling substantive arbitrariness in  the  appointment  of  judges  to  the\nSupreme Court and the High Courts.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_803\">3.    Having had the benefit  of  reading  the  draft  judgment  of  Justice\nKhehar, Justice Kurian Joseph  and  Justice  Adarsh  Kumar  Goel,  I  am  in\nrespectful agreement with the conclusions arrived  at  with  regard  to  the\nconstitutional validity  of the 99th Constitution Amendment Act  but  prefer\nto supplement them with additional reasons. I am in respectful  disagreement\nwith the view of Justice Chelameswar. I believe all the submissions made  by\nvarious learned counsel led  by  Mr.  Fali  S.  Nariman  on  behalf  of  the\npetitioners and by Mr. Mukul Rohatgi the learned Attorney-General on  behalf\nof the respondents have been noted and dealt with by Justice Khehar  in  his\ndraft judgment and in respect of some of them, I  have  nothing  to  add  to\nwhat has already been said.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_804\">Historical background\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_805\">4.     George  Santayana,  philosopher,  essayist,  poet  and  novelist   is\nbelieved to have said something to  the  effect  that:  \u2018Those  who  do  not\nremember their past are condemned to repeat their  mistakes.\u2019  Keeping  this\nin mind, it is essential to appreciate the evolution of the process for  the\nappointment of judges in the  Indian  judiciary,  the  various  alternatives\ndiscussed and debated and then to consider and analyze  the  solution  given\nby the <a href=\"/doc/195108305/\" id=\"a_1565\">Constitution (Ninety-ninth Amendment)  Act,  2014</a>  and  the  <a href=\"/doc/142995521/\" id=\"a_1566\">National\nJudicial Appointments Commission Act, 2014</a>. This is  important  for  another\nreason \u2013 some of the \u2018mistakes\u2019 made before  Constituent  Assembly  accepted\nthe Constitution of India, have been revived and enacted,  even  though  the\nConstituent Assembly debated and rejected them.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_806\">5.    Section 101 of the Government of India  Act,  1919  provided  for  the\nappointment of the Chief Justice and judges of the High  Court  and  Section\n102 provided for their tenure. It was provided that  the  appointment  shall\nbe made by  His  Majesty  and  the  judge  shall  hold  office  \u2018during  His\nMajesty\u2019s pleasure.\u2019 Since the appointment  process  and  the  tenure  of  a\njudge  depended  upon  the  Crown\u2019s  pleasure,  perhaps  the  issue  of  the\nindependence of the judiciary was not the subject of  discussion  in  India.\nIn any event, nothing was pointed out in this regard one way  or  the  other\nduring the submissions made by learned counsel.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_807\">6.    <a href=\"/doc/93056159/\" id=\"a_1567\">The Government of India Act, 1935</a> partially changed the procedure  for\nthe appointment of judges to the High Courts and introduced a procedure  for\nthe appointment of judges to the Federal Court constituted by the said  Act.\n Section 200 and 201 dealt with the appointment of  judges  of  the  Federal\nCourt and while the Crown continued to  make  the  appointments  (apparently\nwithout any formal consultation process), their tenure was fixed at the  age\nof 65 years. Removal  of  a  judge  was  possible  only  on  the  ground  of\nmisbehavior or of infirmity of mind or body. Section 201  provided  for  the\nsalary, allowances, leave and pension of a  judge  and  this  could  not  be\nvaried to his/her  disadvantage  after  appointment.  Section  220  and  221\nrelated to the appointment of a judge of the High Court and  the  provisions\nthereof were more or less similar to the  appointment  of  a  judge  of  the\nFederal Court.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_808\">7.    <a href=\"/doc/93056159/\" id=\"a_1568\">The Government of India Act, 1935</a> gave a semblance of  an  independent\njudiciary in that it provided some basic requirements of  independence  such\nas eligibility for appointment, security of  tenure  including  the  removal\nprocess, assurance of salary, allowances and  pension  etc.  Again,  nothing\nspecific was shown to us, one way or the other, which could throw  light  on\nthe contemporaneous  practice  regarding  the  appointment  process  or  the\nindependence of the judiciary. A general  practice  on  the  appointment  of\njudges was, however, subsisting  and  this  has  been  adverted  to  by  the\nSupreme Court of Pakistan in Al-Jehad Trust v. Federation of  Pakistan.[202]\nIt was observed that ever since 1911 when the Indian  High  Courts  Act  was\nenacted and certainly from 1915/1919 onwards when the  <a href=\"/doc/93056159/\" id=\"a_1569\">Government  of  India\nAct</a>  was  enacted,  the  recommendation  of  the  Chief  Justice   for   the\nappointment of a judge was accepted even though the appointment of  a  judge\nwas a matter of the pleasure of the Crown. It was said:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_487\">\u201cAct of appointment of a Chief Justice or a Judge in the superior  Court  is\nan executive act. No doubt this power is vested in the Executive  under  the\nrelevant Articles of the Constitution, but the question is, as to  how  this\npower is to be exercised. Conventions can  be  pressed  into  service  while\nconstruing  a  provision  of  the  Constitution  and  for  channelising  and\nregulating the exercise of power under the Constitution: whereas  under  the\nIslamic Jurisprudence, a convention which is termed as  Urf  has  a  binding\nforce on the basis of various Islamic sources,  it  has  been  a  consistent\npractice which has acquired the status of  convention  during  pre-partition\ndays of India as well as post-partition period that the  recommendations  of\nthe Chief Justice of a High Court and  the  Chief  Justice  of  the  Supreme\nCourt in India as well as in Pakistan have been  consistently  accepted  and\nacted upon except in very rare cases. The practice of  consultation  of  the\nChief Justice of a High Court and the Indian  Federal  Court  was  obtaining\neven under  the  Indian  High  Courts  Act  [1911]  as  well  as  under  the\n<a href=\"/doc/93056159/\" id=\"a_1570\">Government of India Act</a> 1915, though the appointment of Judges  of  superior\nCourts in  India  was  a  matter  of  pleasure  vested  in  the  Crown.  The\nrecommendations of the Chief Justices even in those days were accepted as  a\nmatter of course.\u201d\n\nSapru Committee\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_809\">8.    The issue of the appointment of judges (for Independent  India)  first\ncame up for discussion (as it appears) before the Sapru Committee. A  Report\nprepared  by  this  Committee  in  1945  dealt  with  the  Legislature,  the\nExecutive and the Judiciary in Chapter V thereof.  The  relevant  paragraphs\npertaining to  the  appointment  of  judges  are  paragraphs  259,  261  and\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_810\">268.[203] The Committee was of the opinion  that  the  independence  of  the\njudiciary is of \u2018supreme importance for  the  satisfactory  working  of  the\nConstitution and nothing can be more detrimental  to  the  well-being  of  a\nProvince or calculated to undermine public confidence than  the  possibility\nof executive interference with the strength and independence of the  highest\ntribunal of the Province.\u2019 It was  clear  that  it  desired  to  secure  the\n\u2018absolute independence\u2019 of the High Court and to put the judges above  party\npolitics or  influences.  The  Committee  proposed  a  limited  consultative\nsystem of appointment of judges completely leaving out the  Legislature  and\nthe Executive. The Committee proposed consultation only between the Head  of\nthe State and the Chief Justice of India for  appointments  to  the  Supreme\nCourt and for the High Courts, in addition, the Head of the Unit  (Province)\nand the Chief Justice of the High Court.  The  relevant  paragraphs  of  the\nReport read as follows:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_488\">\u201c259. In our Recommendation No.13 we first recommend that there shall  be  a\nSupreme Court for the Union and a High Court in each of the units.  Then  in\nthe second clause we recommend that the strength of judges in each of  these\nCourts at the inception of the Union as well as the salaries to be  paid  to\nthem shall be fixed in the <a href=\"/doc/237570/\" id=\"a_1571\">Constitution Act</a> and no  modification  in  either\nshall  be  made  except  on  the  recommendation  of  the  High  Court,  the\nGovernment concerned and the Supreme Court and  with  the  sanction  of  the\nHead of the State, provided, however, that the salary of no judge  shall  be\nvaried to his disadvantage during his term of office. In sub-clause  (3)  we\nrecommend:-\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_489\">\u201c(a) The Chief Justice of India shall be appointed by the Head of the  State\nand the other judges of the Supreme Court shall be appointed by the Head  of\nthe State in consultation with the Chief Justice of India.\u201d\n\u201c(b) The Chief Justice of a High Court shall be appointed  by  the  Head  of\nthe State in consultation with the Head of the Unit and  the  Chief  Justice\nof India.\u201d\n\u201c(c) Other judges of a High Court shall be appointed  by  the  Head  of  the\nState in consultation with the Head of the Unit, the Chief  Justice  of  the\nHigh Court concerned and the Chief Justice of India.\u201d\n\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_490\">261.  Our main object in making  these  recommendations  is  to  secure  the\nabsolute independence of  the  High  Court  and  to  put  them  above  party\npolitics or influences.  Without some such safeguards, it is not  impossible\nthat  a  Provincial  Government  may   under   political   pressure   affect\nprejudicially the strength of the High Court within its jurisdiction or  the\nsalary of its  Judges.   If  it  is  urged  that  the  High  Court  and  the\nGovernment concerned will be more or less interested parties in the  matter,\nthe intervention of the Supreme Court and of the Head  of  the  State  would\nrule out all possibility of the exercise of political or  party  influences.\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_811\">The imposition of these conditions, may, on a superficial view, seem  to  be\ninconsistent with the theoretical autonomy of the  Provinces,  but,  in  our\nopinion, the independence of the High Court and of the  judiciary  generally\nis of supreme importance for the satisfactory working  of  the  Constitution\nand nothing can be more detrimental to  the  well-being  of  a  Province  or\ncalculated to undermine public confidence than the possibility of  executive\ninterference with the strength and independence of the highest  tribunal  of\nthe Province.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_812\">268.  We now come to  the  method  of  appointment  of  Judges.   Under  the\nexisting law Judges of High Courts and of the Federal  Court  are  appointed\nby the Crown. We have recommended that the Chief Justice of India should  be\nappointed by the Head of the State.  In this connection we  would  refer  to\nour discussion of the phrase \u2018Head of the State\u2019 in Chapter  VI.   Similarly\nwe have recommended that the other Judges of  the  Supreme  Court  shall  be\nappointed by the Head of the State in consultation with  the  Chief  Justice\nof India. The Chief Justice of a High Court shall be appointed by  the  Head\nof the State in consultation with  the  Head  of  the  Unit  and  the  Chief\nJustice of India, and the other judges of a High Court  shall  be  appointed\nby the Head of the State in consultation with the  Head  of  the  Unit,  the\nChief Justice of the High Court concerned and the Chief  Justice  of  India.\nWe have deliberately placed  the  appointment  of  these  Judges,  including\nJudges of the Provincial High Courts outside the purview of party  politics,\nand we make  the  same  observations  as  above  in  justification  of  this\nprovision notwithstanding its  seeming  interference  with  the  theoretical\nautonomy of the Provinces.\u201d\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_813\">9.    As mentioned, \u2018Head of State\u2019 was  discussed  in  Chapter  VI  of  the\nReport and in so far as the judiciary is concerned, the Head  of  State  was\nexpected to act \u2018on his own\u2019 as the occupant of the office of Head of  State\nand not on the advice of the Federal Ministry. More specifically,  the  Head\nof State was to act on his/her own in the matter of appointment and  removal\nof judges. This is what was said in the Report:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_491\">\u201cThe Union will be a democratic federal State and the Head of the State  who\nwill replace both the Governor-General  and  the  Crown  Representative  and\nmight be given  a  suitable  indigenous  designation,  if  necessary  should\nexercise such functions as are given to  him  only  on  the  advice  of  his\nFederal Ministry, barring a few very exceptional cases, to  be  specifically\nmentioned in the <a href=\"/doc/237570/\" id=\"a_1572\">Constitution Act</a>, where discretion is given to him  to  act\non his own or on advice other than that of  the  Federal  Ministry  (1)  for\navoiding political or communal graft, or (2) for taking  the  initiative  in\nthe national interest, especially in exceptional and fast moving  situations\nsuch as exist at the  present  day.   Under  exception  (1)  will  fall  the\nsuggestions we have made  under  paragraph  13  of  our  recommendations  as\nregard the alteration of the strength of High  Courts  and  the  appointment\nand removal of judges of the Supreme Court and the High Courts.\u201d[204]\n\nAd hoc Committee on the Supreme Court\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_814\">10.   After the Constituent Assembly was formed, an Ad hoc Committee on  the\nSupreme Court was set up which presented its Report of  21st  May,  1947  to\nthe Constituent Assembly.  Paragraph 14 of the Report  is  of  relevance  to\nthe issue of appointment of judges of the Supreme  Court.  It  accepted,  in\nprinciple, the qualification for the appointment of judges  to  the  Supreme\nCourt, as mentioned in the <a href=\"/doc/93056159/\" id=\"a_1573\">Government  of  India  Act,  1935</a>  but  found  it\ninexpedient \u2018to leave the power of appointing judges of  the  Supreme  Court\nto the unfettered discretion of the President of the  Union.\u2019  It  made  two\nsuggestions  in  the  appointment  procedure,  both  of  which  necessitated\nconsultation between the President and the Chief Justice of  India  and  the\nopinion of a panel of 11 (eleven) persons comprising of,  inter  alia,  some\nChief Justices of the High Courts, some members of both the  Houses  of  the\nCentral Legislature and some law officers of  the  Union.  It  was  proposed\nthat the executive  be  kept  out  of  the  appointment  process.  The  said\nparagraph reads as follows:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_492\">\u201c14. The qualifications of the judges of the Supreme Court may be laid  down\non terms very similar to those in the Act of 1935 as regards the  judges  of\nthe Federal Court, the possibility being borne in mind (as  in  the  Act  of\n1935) that judges of the superior courts even  from  the  States  which  may\njoin the Union may be found fit to occupy a seat in the  Supreme  Court.  We\ndo not think that it will be expedient to  leave  the  power  of  appointing\njudges of the Supreme Court to the unfettered discretion  of  the  President\nof the Union. We recommend that either  of  the  following  methods  may  be\nadopted. One method is that the President should in  consultation  with  the\nChief Justice of the Supreme Court (so far, as  the  appointment  of  puisne\njudges is  concerned)  nominate  a  person  whom  he  considers  fit  to  be\nappointed to the Supreme Court and the nomination should be confirmed  by  a\nmajority of at least 7 out of a panel of 11 composed of some  of  the  Chief\nJustices of the High Courts of the constituent units, some members  of  both\nthe Houses of the Central Legislature and some of the law  officers  of  the\nUnion. The other method is that the  panel  of  11  should  recommend  three\nnames out of which the President, in consultation with  the  Chief  Justice,\nmay select a judge  for  the  appointment.  The  same  procedure  should  be\nfollowed for the appointment of the Chief Justice except of course  that  in\nthis case there will be no consultation with the Chief  Justice.  To  ensure\nthat the panel will be both independent [and] command confidence  the  panel\nshould not be an ad hoc body but  must  be  one  appointed  for  a  term  of\nyears.\u201d[205]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_815\">11.   There was clearly a divergence of opinion between the Sapru  Committee\nand the Ad hoc Committee on the consultation process for the appointment  of\njudges. The Sapru Committee felt that the appointment of  judges  should  be\nleft to the Head of State acting on his/her own while the Ad  hoc  Committee\ndid not approve of the appointment process being  left  to  the  \u2018unfettered\ndiscretion of the President\u2019 but suggested it to be broad-based involving  a\npanel.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_816\">12.   However, what is apparent from both the Report of the Sapru  Committee\nand the Report of the Ad hoc Committee is that the executive was not  to  be\ninvolved at all in  the  process  of  appointment  of  judges.  This  is  of\nconsiderable significance.[206]\n\nMemorandum on the Union Constitution and Draft Clauses\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_817\">13.   On 30th May,  1947  the  Constitutional  Advisor  to  the  Constituent\nAssembly, Sir B.N. Rau submitted a Memorandum on the Union Constitution  and\nDraft Clauses. The Memorandum provided in Chapter VI (The Union  Judicature)\nthat there shall be  a  Supreme  Court  \u2018with  powers  and  jurisdiction  as\nrecommended by the ad hoc Committee on the Union  Judiciary.\u2019[207]   In  the\ndraft clauses of the Union Constitution appended to the Memorandum,  it  was\nprovided that every judge of the Supreme Court shall  be  appointed  by  the\nPresident with the approval of not less than 2/3rd of  the  members  of  the\nCouncil of State.[208] In this regard, the Law Commission of India notes  in\nits 80th Report as follows:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_493\">\u201cThe  Constitutional  Adviser,  in  his  memorandum  dated  May  30th,  1947\nsuggested that the appointment of Judges should be  made  by  the  President\nwith the approval of at least two-thirds  of  the  Council  of  State.   The\nCouncil of State, according to him, was to be a body  in  the  nature  of  a\nPrivy Council for  advising  the  President  on  certain  matters  on  which\ndecisions were required on independent  non-party  lines.   The  Council  of\nState was to include the Chief Justice of India among its  members  and  its\ncomposition was to be such as to secure freedom from  party  bias.   Such  a\nCouncil of State, it was suggested by the Constitutional Adviser,  would  be\na  satisfactory  substitute  for  the  panel  recommended  by  the   Special\nCommittee.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_494\">The Union  Constitution  Committee  did  not  accept  the  proposal  of  the\nConstitutional Adviser for setting up of a Council of State,  and  suggested\nthat the procedure  for  the  appointment  of  judges  should  be  that  the\nPresident should consult the Chief Justice and  such  other  judges  of  the\nSupreme Court as might be necessary.\u201d[209]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_818\">14.   It appears that by this time, the independence of  the  judiciary  was\ntaken  for  granted,  the  only  question  being  the  procedure   for   the\nappointment of judges \u2013 whether it should be  the  exclusive  responsibility\nof the President or it should be broad-based involving a panel or a  Council\nof State.  In any event, the exclusion of the executive in  the  appointment\nprocess appears to have been taken as accepted.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_819\">Union Constitution Committee\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_820\">15.   The Union Constitution Committee  which  presented  a  Report  to  the\nConstituent Assembly on 4th July,  1947  did  not  adopt  the  proposal  for\nsetting up a Council of State. Consequently, an  alternative  procedure  for\nthe appointment of a judge of the Supreme Court was suggested,  namely,  for\nthe appointment by consultation between the President and the Chief  Justice\nof the Supreme Court and such other judges of the Supreme Court  and  judges\nof the High Court as may  be  necessary.     In  other  words,  the  limited\nconsultative  process  as  originally  envisaged  by  the  Sapru   Committee\n(between the President and the Chief Justice of India) was  accepted  though\nwith modifications. Chapter IV paragraph 18 of the  Report  concerns  itself\nwith the appointment of judges of  the  Supreme  Court  and  this  reads  as\nfollows:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_495\">\u201c18.\u00a0Supreme Court.--There shall be a Supreme Court with  the  constitution,\npowers and jurisdiction recommended by the\u00a0ad  hoc\u00a0Committee  on  the  Union\nJudiciary, except that a judge of the Supreme Court shall  be  appointed  by\nthe President after consulting the Chief Justice and such  other  judges  of\nthe Supreme Court as also judges of the High Courts as may be necessary  for\nthe purpose.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_496\">[NOTE - The\u00a0ad hoc\u00a0Committee\u00a0on the Supreme Court has observed that it  will\nnot be expedient to leave the power of  appointing  judges  of  the  Supreme\nCourt to the unfettered discretion of the President of the Federation.  They\nhave suggested two alternatives, both of which involve the setting up  of  a\nspecial  panel  of  eleven  members.  According  to  one  alternative,   the\nPresident, in consultation with the Chief Justice, is to nominate  a  person\nfor appointment as puisne judge and the nomination has to  be  confirmed  by\nat least seven members of the panel. According  to  the  other  alternative,\nthe panel should recommend three names,  out  of  which  the  President,  in\nconsultation with the Chief Justice, is to select one for  the  appointment.\nThe provision suggested in the above clause  follows  the  decision  of  the\nUnion Constitution Committee.]\u201d[210]\n\nAgain, the executive had no role to  play  in  the  appointment  of  judges,\nspecifically of the Supreme Court.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_497\">Provincial Constitution Committee\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_821\">16.   With regard to the High Courts, a  Report  of   27th  June,  1947  was\nsubmitted  to  the  Constituent  Assembly  by  the  Provincial  Constitution\nCommittee. Part II thereof pertained to the  Provincial  Judiciary  and  the\nrecommendations made for  the  appointment  of  judges  of  the  High  Court\nincorporated the provisions of the <a href=\"/doc/93056159/\" id=\"a_1574\">Government of India  Act,  1935</a>  and  the\nrecommendations made by the Union  Constitution  Committee.  These  read  as\nfollows:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_498\">      \u201cThe Provincial Judiciary\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_499\">1. The provisions of the <a href=\"/doc/93056159/\" id=\"a_1575\">Government of India  Act,  1935</a>,  relating  to  the\nHigh Court  should  be  adopted  mutatis  mutandis;  but  judges  should  be\nappointed by the President of the Federation in consultation with the  Chief\nJustice of the Supreme Court, the Governor of the  Province  and  the  Chief\nJustice of the High Court of the Province (except when the Chief Justice  of\nthe High Court himself is to be appointed).\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_500\">2. The judges of the High Court shall receive such emoluments and\nallowances as may be determined by Act of the Provincial Legislature and\nuntil then such as are prescribed in Schedule............\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_501\">3. The emoluments and allowances of the judges shall not be diminished\nduring their term of office.\u201d[211]\n\nThe above discussion indicates that the executive was to be kept out of  the\nprocess of appointing judges to the Supreme Court and the High Courts.  This\nis clear from the views  of:  (1)  The  Sapru  Committee;  (2)  The  Ad  hoc\nCommittee on the Supreme Court; (3) The Union  Constitution  Committee,  and\n(4) The Provincial Constitution Committee. This will have some bearing  when\nthe  composition  of  the  National  Judicial  Appointments  Commission   is\nexamined.\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_822\">17.   In this background pertaining to the judiciary,  the  first  draft  of\nthe Constitution was placed before the Drafting Committee in October,  1947.\n This was followed by another (revised) draft submitted to the President  of\nthe Constituent Assembly on 21st February, 1948. There  was  no  significant\nchange between these two drafts as  far  the  appointment  process  for  the\nFederal  Judicature  (or  the  High  Courts  in  the  Provinces/States)   is\nconcerned. But, it is important to note that the Drafting Committee did  not\nthrow overboard the view of any of the committees mentioned above, that  is,\nto keep the executive out of the process of appointment of judges.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_823\">Conference of Chief Justices\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_824\">18.    Wide  publicity  was  given  to  the  Draft  Constitution  to  enable\ninterested persons to express their views through comments and  suggestions.\nThe views expressed by the Conference of Chief Justices (the  Chief  Justice\nof the Federal Court and Chief Justices of the High Courts), the  Minorities\nSub-Committee and the Advisory Committee on Fundamental  Rights,  Minorities\nand  Tribal  and  Excluded  Areas  are  important  since  they  explain  the\ninterplay  between  the  Executive  and  the  Judiciary  in  the  matter  of\nappointment of judges.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_825\">19.    These  views  also  make  it  clear  that  almost  immediately  after\nIndependence (or thereabouts)  the  executive  began  to  interfere  in  the\nappointment  of  judges  of  the  High  Courts.  This  interference  by  the\nexecutive (or in the present day language, the political executive)  is  the\ngenesis of the problem that we are grappling with even today.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_826\">20.   The Conference of Chief Justices was held  on  26th  and  27th  March,\n1948 to consider the proposals  in  the  Draft\u00a0Constitution  concerning  the\njudiciary.\u00a0\u00a0A Memorandum representing the views of the Federal Court and  of\nthe Chief Justices representing all the Provincial High Courts of the  Union\nof India was prepared and submitted by the Conference.[212] This  Memorandum\nis  of  immense  importance  in  understanding  the  prevailing  appointment\nprocess.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_827\">21.   Very briefly, in what may  be  described  as  the  \u2018preamble\u2019  to  the\nMemorandum, a few salient points were assumed and noticed.  It  was  assumed\nthat the independence and integrity of the  judiciary  is  of  the  \u2018highest\nimportance\u2019 not only to the judges but to the citizens seeking  resort  from\na court of law against the high handed and illegal exercise of power by  the\nexecutive. It was noticed that there is  a  tendency  to  whittle  down  the\npowers,  rights  and  authority  of  the  judiciary  which,  if  allowed  to\ncontinue, would be \u2018most  unfortunate\u2019.  Therefore,  there  was  a  need  to\ncounteract this tendency which was likely to grow with greater  power  being\nplaced in the hands of the political parties. It was said:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_502\">\u201cWe have assumed that it is recognized on all hands  that  the  independence\nand integrity of the judiciary in a democratic system of  government  is  of\nthe highest importance and interest not  only  to  the  judges  but  to  the\ncitizens at large who may have to seek redress in the last resort in  courts\nof law against any illegal acts or the high-handed exercise of power by  the\nexecutive.  Thanks to the system of administration  of  justice  established\nby the British in this country, the judiciary until now has,  in  the  main,\nplayed and independent role in  protecting  the  rights  of  the  individual\ncitizen  against  encroachment  and  invasion  by   the   executive   power.\nUnfortunately, however, a tendency has, of late, been noticeable to  detract\nfrom the status and dignity of the  judiciary  and  to  whittle  down  their\npowers, rights an authority which if unchecked would  be  most  unfortunate.\nWhile we recognize that the Draft Constitution  proposes  to  liberalize  in\nsome respects the existing safeguards against executive interference and  to\nenlarge their present powers, it is felt that further  provision  should  be\nmade in the same direction in order effectively to counteract the  aforesaid\ntendency which is bound to become more pronounced as more power passes  into\nthe  hands  of  political  parties  who  will  control  and   dominate   the\ngovernmental machinery in the  years  to  come.   In  making  the  following\nproposals  and  suggestions,  the  paramount  importance  of  securing   the\nfearless  functioning  of  an  independent,  incorruptible   and   efficient\njudiciary has been steadily kept in view.\u201d\n\nThe Memorandum specifically pointed out  (sadly)  that  after  15th  August,\n1947 the appointment of judges to the High Courts, on merit, was not  always\nassured  in  view  of  the  practice  followed  (by  some   States).   Also,\nrecommendations by the Chief Justice of  the  High  Court  were  not  always\nforwarded to the  Central  Government,  implying  thereby  that  some  other\nrecommendations were forwarded.   In this regard it was said:\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_503\">\u201cDiscussions at the conference revealed that the  procedure  followed  after\n15th August 1947 does not in practice always ensure appointment  being  made\npurely on merit without political, communal and party  considerations  being\nimported into the matter.  Though  it  is  acknowledged  readily  enough  in\nprinciple that such considerations should  not  influence  the  appointment,\nthis is not always kept in view in working the procedure in  practice.   The\nChief Justice sends his recommendation to the Premier who consults his  Home\nMinister.  The recommendation of the Premier is then forwarded to  the  Home\nMinistry at the Centre without even sending the recommendation of the  Chief\nJustice along with it, the prescribed procedure being apparently  understood\nas not rendering it obligatory for the Premier to do so.\u201d\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_828\">22.   Consequently, a modified  procedure  for  making  recommendations  was\nunanimously recommended by  the  Conference  which  would  ensure  that  the\nrecommendation of the Chief Justice  reaches  the  President  and  that  the\nappointment be made with the concurrence of the Chief Justice  of  India  to\navoid any political pressures. It was said:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_504\">\u201cThe Chief Justice should send his recommendation in  that  behalf  directly\nto the President. After consultation with the Governor the President  should\nmake the appointment with the concurrence of the  Chief  Justice  of  India.\nThis procedure would obviate the need for the  Chief  Justice  of  the  High\nCourt discussing the matter with the  Premier  and  his  Home  Minister  and\n\u201cjustifying\u201d his recommendations before  them.
