It  would  also  ensure  the\nrecommendation of the Chief Justice of the High Court  being  always  placed\nbefore the appointing authority, namely, the President.  The  necessity  for\nobtaining the \u201cconcurrence\u201d of the Chief Justice of India  would  provide  a\nsafeguard against political and party pressure at the  highest  level  being\nbrought to bear in the matter.\u201d\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_829\">23.   Significantly, the  Memorandum  tacitly  and  implicitly  acknowledged\nthat apart from a recommendation for the appointment of a judge  of  a  High\nCourt originating from the Chief Justice of the High Court,  recommendations\nwere being made by or at the instance of the  political  executive.  Whether\nsuch a procedure was right or wrong was not considered but it was  suggested\nthat in the event of such a recommendation being made,  the  concurrence  of\nthe Chief Justice of India should be  obtained  before  the  appointment  is\nmade. The Memorandum proposed that <a href=\"/doc/1465272/\" id=\"a_1576\">Article 193(1)</a> of the Draft  Constitution\nconcerning the appointment of a judge of a High Court should read as under:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_505\">\u201cEvery judge of the High Court shall be appointed by the President by a\nwarrant under his hand and seal on the recommendation of the Chief Justice\nof the High Court after consultation with the Governor of the State and\nwith the concurrence of the Chief Justice of India \u2026\u201d\n\nThe Memorandum acknowledged that a recommendation for the appointment  of  a\njudge of the High  Court  could  also  be  made  by  the  President  (in  an\nindividual capacity). In the event of such a proposal  (by  the  President),\nthere was no likelihood of the Chief Justice of India not accepting it  and,\ntherefore, the concurrence of the Chief Justice of India  was  not  required\nto be incorporated in the Constitution. It was, therefore, noted:\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_506\">\u201cWe do not think it necessary to make any provision in the Constitution  for\nthe possibility of the Chief Justice of  India  refusing  to  concur  in  an\nappointment proposed by the President. Both  are  officers  of  the  highest\nresponsibility and so far no case of such  refusal  has  arisen  although  a\nconvention now exists that such appointments should be made after  referring\nthe matter to the Chief Justice of India and obtaining his  concurrence.  If\nper chance such a situation were ever to arise it could of course be met  by\nthe President making a different proposal, and no  express  provision  need,\nit seems to us, be made in that behalf.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_507\">The foregoing applies mutatis mutandis to the appointment of the  judges  of\nthe Supreme Court, and <a href=\"/doc/1042703/\" id=\"a_1577\">article 103(2)</a> may also be suitably modified\u2026..\u201d\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_830\">24.   The significance of this Memorandum cannot be  overemphasized  and  it\ncan be summarized as follows: (1) The  independence  and  integrity  of  the\njudiciary was of the highest importance. (2) A  tendency  had  developed  in\nthe executive to whittle down the power and authority of the judiciary.  (3)\nIt was noted that recommendations for the appointment of a judge of  a  High\nCourt originate from the Chief Justice  of  the  High  Court.  Occasionally,\nsuch recommendations are suppressed  by  the  executive  at  the  provincial\nlevel. It was proposed that recommendations made by the Chief Justice  ought\nto be forwarded directly to the President for being processed  so  that  the\npolitical executive at the provincial level cannot suppress it. (4)  It  was\nacknowledged that the political  executive  at  the  provincial  level  also\nmakes recommendations (though not always on merits) directly to the  Central\nGovernment, without the knowledge of the Chief Justice of  the  High  Court.\nSuch recommendations ought to be accepted only with the concurrence  of  the\nChief Justice of India, and this should  be  taken  care  of  in  the  Draft\nConstitution.  (5)  It  was  acknowledged  that  a  recommendation  for  the\nappointment of a judge of a High Court (or the Supreme Court) could be  made\nby  the  President  (personally  \u2013  \u2018Both  are  officers  of   the   highest\nresponsibility\u2026..\u2019). This would normally be accepted by  the  Chief  Justice\nof India and therefore  no  provision  for  the  concurrence  of  the  Chief\nJustice of India was required to  be  made  in  this  regard  in  the  Draft\nConstitution. However, if the Chief Justice  of  India  were  to  refuse  to\naccept the recommendation, the situation  could  be  met  by  the  President\nmaking a different  proposal.  This  is  because,  it  was  noted,  that  \u2018a\nconvention now exists that such appointments should be made after  referring\nthe matter to the Chief Justice of India and obtaining his concurrence.\u2019\n\nAmendments to <a href=\"/doc/594857/\" id=\"a_1578\">Article 61</a> and <a href=\"/doc/825474/\" id=\"a_1579\">Article 62</a> of the Draft Constitution\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_831\">25.    The  Minorities  Sub-Committee  and   the   Advisory   Committee   on\nFundamental Rights, Minorities and Tribal and  Excluded  Areas  adverted  to\nand considered <a href=\"/doc/594857/\" id=\"a_1580\">Article 61</a> and <a href=\"/doc/825474/\" id=\"a_1581\">Article  62  (amongst  others)</a>  of  the  Draft\nConstitution. <a href=\"/doc/594857/\" id=\"a_1582\">Article 61</a> and <a href=\"/doc/825474/\" id=\"a_1583\">Article 62</a> of the  Draft  Constitution  pertain\nto the Council of Ministers to  aid  and  advice  the  President  and  other\nprovisions as to Ministers. In  this  regard,  Shiva  Rao  mentions  in  his\nexcellent effort  \u2018The  Framing  of  India\u2019s  Constitution  \u2013  A  Study\u2019  as\nfollows:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_508\">\u201cThere was considerable discussion in the Minorities  Sub-Committee  and  in\nthe Advisory Committee on Fundamental  Rights,  Minorities  and  Tribal  and\nExcluded Areas on the need for the inclusion of minority representatives  in\nthe  Union  and  State  Cabinets\u2026\u2026..  They  considered  that  it  would   be\nsufficient if, following the precedent furnished by the <a href=\"/doc/93056159/\" id=\"a_1584\">Government of  India\nAct</a> of 1935, an Instrument of Instructions was drawn up, to be  included  as\na schedule to the Constitution, enjoining the Governors  and  the  President\nas far as practicable to include members  of  the  minority  communities  in\ntheir Ministries.  In the Draft Constitution of February 1948,  however,  an\nInstrument of Instructions for this purpose was drawn up only for  Governors\nbut not for the President.  Possibly in order to rectify this omission,  the\nDrafting  Committee  decided,  on  further  consideration  of  the  articles\nrelating to the Council of Ministers, that  an  Instrument  of  Instructions\nfor the President would also be necessary\u201d[213]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_832\">26.   Apparently, pursuant to this, the Drafting Committee gave a notice in\nOctober 1948 of an amendment to <a href=\"/doc/825474/\" id=\"a_1585\">Article 62</a> proposing to add the following\nclause:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_509\">\u201cIn the choice of his Ministers and the  exercise  of  his  other  functions\nunder this Constitution, the President shall  be  generally  guided  by  the\nInstructions set out in Schedule III-A, but the validity  of  anything  done\nby the President shall not be called in question on the ground that  it  was\ndone otherwise than in accordance with such Instructions.\u201d\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_833\">27.   Schedule III-A incorporated the  Instrument  of  Instructions  to  the\nPresident and this is important and it reads as follows:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_510\">                             New Schedule III-A\n                              [<a href=\"/doc/825474/\" id=\"a_1586\">Article 62(5a)]</a>\n\n                        INSTRUCTIONS TO THE PRESIDENT\n\nIn these instructions, unless the context otherwise requires, the term\n\u201cPresident\u201d shall include every person for the time being discharging the\nfunctions, of, or acting as, the President according to the provisions of\nthis Constitution.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_511\">xxx\nxxx\n(1) The President shall make rules  for  the  constitution  of  an  Advisory\nBoard consisting  of  not  less  than  fifteen  members  of  the  Houses  of\nParliament to be elected by both Houses in accordance  with  the  system  of\nproportional representation by means of the  single  transferable  vote  for\nthe purpose of advising the  President  in  the  matter  of  making  certain\nappointments under this Constitution and shall take all necessary steps  for\nthe due constitution of such Board as soon as may be after the  commencement\nof this Constitution.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_512\">(2) Such rules shall provide that the Leader of the Opposition, if  any,  in\neither House of Parliament shall, if he  is  not  elected  to  the  Advisory\nBoard, be nominated to the Board by the President.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_513\">(3) Such rules shall also define the terms of office of the members  of  the\nAdvisory Board and its procedure and may contain such  ancillary  provisions\nas the President may consider necessary.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_514\">5.   (1)     In making any appointment of \u2013\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_515\">(a)  the Chief Justice of India or any other judge of the Supreme Court;\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_834\">(b) the Chief Justice or any other judge of a High Court;\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_835\">(c) an Ambassador in a foreign State;\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_836\">(d) the Auditor-General of India;\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_837\">(e)  the  Chairman  or  any  other  member  of  the  Union  Public   Service\nCommission;\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_838\">(f) any member of the Commission to superintend, direct and control all\nelections to Parliament and elections to the offices of President and Vice-\nPresident,\nThe President shall consult the Advisory Board constituted under paragraph\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_839\">4.\n(2) The President shall also consult the Advisory Board so constituted in\nmaking appointment by virtue of the powers conferred on him by this\nConstitution to any other office under the Government of India or the\nGovernment of a State other than the office of Governor of a State, if\nParliament by resolutions passed by both Houses recommend to the President\nthat the Advisory Board shall be consulted in making appointment to such\noffice.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_840\">6. (1) In making appointment of judges of the Supreme Court and of the High\nCourts, the President shall before obtaining the advice of the Advisory\nBoard shall follow the following procedure:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_516\">(a) In the case of appointment of the Chief Justice of India, he shall\nconsult the judges of the Supreme Court and the Chief Justices of the High\nCourts within the territory of India except the States for the time being\nspecified in Part III of the First Schedule.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_517\">(b) In the case of appointment of a judge of the Supreme Court other than\nthe Chief Justice of India, he shall consult the Chief Justice of India and\nthe other judges of the Supreme Court and also the Chief Justices of the\nHigh Courts within the territory of India except the States for the time\nbeing specified in Part III of the First Schedule.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_518\">(c) In the case of appointment of the Chief Justice of a High Court, he\nshall consult the Governor of the State in which the High Court has its\nprincipal seat, and the Chief Justice of India.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_519\">(d) In the case of appointment of a judge of a High Court other than the\nChief Justice, he shall consult the Governor of the State in which the High\nCourt has its principal seat, the Chief Justice of India and the Chief\nJustice of the High Court.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_520\">(2) The President shall place the recommendations of the authorities\nconsulted by him under sub-paragraph (1) before the Advisory Board at the\ntime of obtaining the advice of that Board with regard to any appointment\nreferred to in that sub-paragraph.\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_841\">7. xxx\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_842\">8. xxx\u201d[214]\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_843\">28.   It is significant that the Instrument of Instructions  also  kept  the\nexecutive completely out of the picture in so  far  as  the  appointment  of\njudges is concerned. No one from  the  executive  was  to  be  consulted  or\ninvolved in the appointment process.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_844\">29.   The Drafting Committee  also  proposed,  apparently  in  view  of  the\ninsertion of Schedule III-A that <a href=\"/doc/1042703/\" id=\"a_1587\">Article 103(2)</a> of  the  Draft  Constitution\n(relating  to  the  appointment  of  judges  of  the   Supreme   Court   and\ncorresponding to <a href=\"/doc/1995948/\" id=\"a_1588\">Article  124(2)</a>  of  the  Constitution  of  India)[215]  be\nmodified as follows:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_521\">\u201c(i)  the words \u201cafter consultation with such of the judges of  the  Supreme\nCourt and of the High Courts in the States  as  may  be  necessary  for  the\npurpose\u201d be deleted in clause (2); and\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_522\">(ii) the first proviso to clause (2) be deleted .\u201d[216]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_845\">30.   In other words, the President was not expected to consult the  Council\nof Ministers at all or to act on its advice but was  to  consult  the  Chief\nJustice of India and other judges and then take the advice of  the  Advisory\nBoard. This was a mixture of the Sapru Committee recommendation of the  Head\nof State (or President as the high office came to be designated)  acting  on\nhis/her own and yet the President not having \u2018unfettered discretion\u2019 in  the\nappointment of judges.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_846\">31.   All the proposals, including those given by the  Conference  of  Chief\nJustices,  the  Minorities  Sub-Committee  and  the  Advisory  Committee  on\nFundamental  Rights,  Minorities  and  Tribal  and  Excluded   Areas,   were\nconsidered by the Drafting Committee and on 4th November,  1948  the  second\ndraft of the Constitution was introduced in the Constituent Assembly by  Dr.\nB.R. Ambedkar, Chairman of the Drafting Committee. However, the decision  of\nthe Drafting Committee taken in October, 1948 was not  incorporated  in  the\nDraft Constitution. Therefore,  Dr.  Ambedkar  moved  an  amendment  in  the\nConstituent Assembly on  31st  December,  1948  to  insert  clause  (5)a  in\n<a href=\"/doc/825474/\" id=\"a_1589\">Article 62</a> of  the  Draft  Constitution.   The  amendment  proposed  by  Dr.\nAmbedkar reads as follows:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_523\">\u201cThat after clause 5 of <a href=\"/doc/825474/\" id=\"a_1590\">Article 62</a> the following new clause be inserted:-\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_524\">(5)a In the choice of his Ministers and the exercise of his other  functions\nunder this Constitution, the President shall  be  generally  guided  by  the\ninstructions set out in Schedule III-A, but the validity  of  anything  done\nby the President shall not be called in question on the ground that  it  was\ndone otherwise than in accordance with such instructions.\u201d\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_847\">32.   The amendment was discussed briefly and  adopted  by  the  Constituent\nAssembly on the same day. Although the decision of  the  Drafting  Committee\nwas to insert clause (5)a in  <a href=\"/doc/825474/\" id=\"a_1591\">Article  62</a>  of  the  Draft  Constitution  and\nsimultaneously delete a part of clause (2)  of  <a href=\"/doc/41049/\" id=\"a_1592\">Article  103</a>  of  the  Draft\nConstitution, the amendment relating  to  the  deletion  of  clause  (2)  of\n<a href=\"/doc/41049/\" id=\"a_1593\">Article 103</a> of the Draft  Constitution  was  apparently  not  moved  by  Dr.\nAmbedkar. It is not clear why. As far as the Instrument of  Instructions  is\nconcerned, it is pointed out by Granville Austin that it was  not  actually,\nbut implicitly, adopted by the Constituent Assembly.[217]\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_848\">33.   A combined reading of the views of the Drafting  Committee  read  with\nthe Instrument of Instructions and the insertion of clause (5)a  in  <a href=\"/doc/825474/\" id=\"a_1594\">Article\n62</a> of the Draft Constitution indicates that the thinking  at  the  time  was\nthat in the matter of appointment of judges the  President  was  to  act  in\nhis/her individual capacity. This is very significant  otherwise  there  was\nabsolutely no need for an Instrument of Instructions or  an  Advisory  Board\nto be set up or for the complete exclusion of the Council  of  Ministers  or\nthe executive in the appointment  of  judges.  However,  this  thinking  was\nlater on given up.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_849\">Constituent Assembly Debates\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_850\">34.    This  historical  background  has  an  impact  on  understanding  the\nsubsequent debate in the Constituent Assembly that took place  on  23rd  and\n24th May, 1949 when <a href=\"/doc/41049/\" id=\"a_1595\">Article 103</a> of the  Draft  Constitution  was  considered\nand debated in the Constituent Assembly.  It needs to be emphasized at  this\nstage that when the debate took place on 23rd and 24th May, 1949 it  was  in\nthe backdrop of the fact that clause  (5)a  had  already  been  inserted  in\n<a href=\"/doc/825474/\" id=\"a_1596\">Article 62</a> of the Draft Constitution  to  the  effect  that  in  respect  of\nseveral matters, including the appointment of judges,  the  President  would\nact in his/her individual capacity and the  Council  of  Ministers  was  not\neven in the picture. The debate will be referred to a little later.\n</p><p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_851\">35.   After a few months,  on  11th  October,  1949  the  President  of  the\nConstituent Assembly was informed by Mr. T.T. Krishnamachari  that  Schedule\nIII-A is not being moved and that it could be taken out  of  the  list.   He\nalso moved for the deletion of Schedule  IV  from  the  Draft  Constitution.\nExplaining the move to delete Schedule IV from  the  Draft  Constitution  it\nwas stated that the matter should be  left  entirely  to  convention  rather\nthan be put in the body of the Constitution as a Schedule in  the  shape  of\nan Instrument of Instructions and that there is a  fairly  large  volume  of\nopinion which favours that idea.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_852\">36.   Dr. Ambedkar added as follows:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_525\">\u201cSir, with regard to the Instrument of Instructions, there  are  two  points\nwhich have  to  be  borne  in  mind.   The  purpose  of  the  Instrument  of\nInstructions as was originally devised in the British Constitution  for  the\nGovernment of the colonies was to give certain directions  to  the  head  of\nthe States as to how they should exercise their  discretionary  powers  that\nwere vested in them.  Now the Instrument of Instructions were  effective  in\nso far as the particular Governor or  Viceroy  to  whom  these  instructions\nwere given was subject to the authority of the Secretary of  State.   If  in\nany particular matter which was of a serious  character,  the  Governor  for\ninstance, persistently refused to carry out the Instrument  of  Instructions\nissued to him, it was open to the Secretary of  State  to  remove  him,  and\nappoint another and  thereby  secure  the  effective  carrying  out  of  the\nInstrument of Instructions. So far as our Constitution is  concerned,  there\nis no functionary created by it  who  can  see  that  these  Instruments  of\nInstructions is carried out faithfully by the Governor.\nSecondly, the discretion which we are  going  to  leave  with  the  Governor\nunder this Constitution is very very meagre.  He has hardly  any  discretion\nat all.  He has to act on the advice of the Prime Minister in the matter  of\nthe selection of Members of the Cabinet.  He has also to act on  the  advice\nof the Prime Minister and  his  Ministers  of  State  with  respect  to  any\nparticular executive or legislative action that he takes.   That  being  so,\nsupposing the Prime Minister does not propose, for  any  special  reason  or\ncircumstances, to include in his Cabinet members of the minority  community,\nthere is nothing which the Governor can do, notwithstanding  the  fact  that\nwe shall be charging him through this particular Instrument of  Instructions\nto act in a particular manner.  It is therefore felt,  having  regard  under\nthe Constitution who can enforce this, that no  such  directions  should  be\ngiven.  They are useless and can serve no  particular  purpose.   Therefore,\nit was  felt  in  the  circumstances  it  is  not  desirable  to  have  such\nInstrument of Instructions which really can be effective in a different  set\nof circumstances which can by no stretch of imagination be deemed  to  exist\nafter the new Constitution comes into  existence.   That  is  the  principal\nreason  why  it  is  felt  that   this   Instrument   of   Instructions   is\nundesirable.\u201d[218]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_853\">37.   On the basis of  the  above  discussion,  Schedule  IV  to  the  Draft\nConstitution was deleted and a motion to that effect was adopted.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_854\">38.   Thereafter on 14th October, 1949 an amendment was moved  by  Mr.  T.T.\nKrishnamachari to omit clause (5)a of <a href=\"/doc/825474/\" id=\"a_1597\">Article 62</a> of the Draft  Constitution.\n It was stated that since Schedule III-A was not moved, this clause  becomes\nsuperfluous and therefore its omission was  moved.  The  amendment  to  omit\nclause (5)a of <a href=\"/doc/825474/\" id=\"a_1598\">Article 62</a> of the Draft Constitution was adopted. In  support\nof this, Dr. Ambedkar [perhaps the main advocate of clause  (5)a]  had  this\nto say, while  emphasizing  constitutional  obligations  and  constitutional\nconventions:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_526\">\u201cEvery Constitution, so far as it relates  to  what  we  call  parliamentary\ndemocracy, requires three different organs of the State, the executive,  the\njudiciary  and  the  legislature.  I  have  not  anywhere   found   in   any\nConstitution  a  provision  saying  that  the  executive  shall   obey   the\nlegislature, nor have I found anywhere in any Constitution a provision  that\nthe executive shall obey the judiciary. Nowhere is such a  provision  to  be\nfound. That is because it is generally understood  that  the  provisions  of\nthe Constitution are  binding  upon  the  different  organs  of  the  State.\nConsequently, it is to be presumed that those  who  work  the  Constitution,\nthose who compose the Legislature and those who compose  the  executive  and\nthe judiciary know their functions, their limitations and their  duties.  It\nis therefore to be expected that if the executive is honest in  working  the\nConstitution, then the executive is bound to obey  the  Legislature  without\nany kind of compulsory obligation laid down in the Constitution.\nSimilarly, if the executive is honest in working the Constitution,  it  must\nact in accordance with the judicial decisions given by  the  Supreme  Court.\nTherefore my submission is that this is a matter of one organ of  the  State\nacting within its own limitations and obeying the  supremacy  of  the  other\norgans of the State. In so far as the  Constitution  gives  a  supremacy  to\nthat is a matter of constitutional  obligation  which  is  implicit  in  the\nConstitution itself.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_527\">I remember, Sir, that you raised this question and I looked it up and I  had\nwith me two decisions of the King&#x27;s Bench Division which I  wanted  one  day\nto bring here and refer in the House so as to make the  point  quite  clear.\nBut I am sorry I had no notice today of this point being  raised.  But  this\nis the answer to the question that has been raised.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_528\">No  constitutional  Government  can  function  in  any  country  unless  any\nparticular constitutional authority remembers the fact  that  its  authority\nis limited by the Constitution and that if there is  any  authority  created\nby the Constitution which has to decide between  that  particular  authority\nand any other authority, then  the  decision  of  that  authority  shall  be\nbinding upon any other organ. That is the sanction which  this  Constitution\ngives in order to see that the President shall  follow  the  advice  of  his\nMinisters, that the executive shall not exceed in  its  executive  authority\nthe law made by Parliament and that the executive shall  not  give  its  own\ninterpretation of the law which is in conflict with  the  interpretation  of\nthe judicial organ created by the Constitution.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_529\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"788847\" id=\"span_318\"><a href=\"/doc/788847/\" id=\"a_1599\">Shri H V. Kamath</a> : If in any particular case  the  President  does  not  act\nupon the advice of his Council of Ministers, will that be  tantamount  to  a\nviolation of the Constitution and will he be liable to impeachment ?\nThe Honourable Dr. B. R. Ambedkar: There is not the  slightest  doubt  about\nit.\u201d[219]\n\nReferring  to  this  extremely  important   exposition,   Granville   Austin\nconcludes:</span>\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_530\">\u201cFrom this, one is forced to deduce that Ambedkar and  the  members  of  the\nDrafting Committee, perhaps under pressure from Nehru or Patel, had come  to\nthe conclusion that the written provisions of a  non-justiciable  Instrument\nof Instructions and the tacit conventions of cabinet  government  had  equal\nvalue: both were legally unenforceable, but both  provided  a  mechanism  by\nwhich  the  legislature  could  control  the  Executive;  and  of  the  two,\nconventions were the tidiest and the  simplest  way  of  limiting  Executive\nauthority.\u201d[220]\n\nTransposing  this  to  the  relationship  between  the  Judiciary  and   the\nExecutive, it is quite clear that Dr. Ambedkar and  indeed  the  Constituent\nAssembly was of the view that constitutional obligations and  constitutional\nconventions must be respected, unwritten though they may be.   And,  one  of\nthese constitutional obligations and constitutional conventions is that  the\nview of the judiciary must be respected  by  the  executive  not  only  with\nrespect to judicial decisions  but  also  in  other  matters  that  directly\nimpact on the independence of the judiciary.\n</blockquote>\n\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_531\">Debates on 23rd and 24th May, 1949\n\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_855\">39.   It is important to appreciate that the  Constituent  Assembly  Debates\n(for short  the  CAD)  to  which  our  attention  was  drawn  refer  to  the\nappointment of a judge of the Supreme Court  and  not  specifically  to  the\nappointment of a judge of a High Court. But the sum  and  substance  of  the\ndebate is equally applicable to the appointment of a judge of a High  Court.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_856\">40.   On 23rd and 24th May, 1949 three  significant  amendments  to  <a href=\"/doc/1042703/\" id=\"a_1600\">Article\n103(2)</a> of the Draft Constitution relating to the appointment  of  judges  of\nthe Supreme Court were considered in the  Constituent  Assembly.  The  first\nwas moved by Prof.  K.T.  Shah  (Bihar:  General)  who  suggested  that  the\nappointment of a judge of the Supreme Court  should  be  after  consultation\nwith the Council of State. This suggestion was intended to  avoid  political\ninfluence, party maneuvers and pressures in  the  appointment  process.  The\nsecond was moved by Prof. Shibban Lal Saksena  (United  Provinces:  General)\nwho suggested that the appointment of the Chief Justice of India be  subject\nto confirmation by two-thirds majority of the total  number  of  Members  of\nParliament assembled in a joint session of both the  Houses  of  Parliament.\nThe third was moved by Mr. B. Pocker Sahib (Madras:  Muslim)  who  suggested\nthat the appointment of a  judge  of  the  Supreme  Court  should  have  the\nconcurrence of the Chief Justice of India. In support of his  amendment  Mr.\nB. Pocker Sahib  extensively  referred  to  and  relied  on  the  Memorandum\nsubmitted by the Conference of Chief Justices. As he put it:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_532\">\u201cI submit, Sir, the views expressed by  the  Federal  Court  and  the  Chief\nJustice of the various High Courts assembled in conference are  entitled  to\nthe highest weight before this Assembly, before this  provision  is  passed.\nIt is of the highest importance that the Judges of the Supreme Court  should\nnot be made to feel that their existence or their appointment  is  dependent\nupon political considerations  or  on  the  will  of  the  political  party.\nTherefore, it is  essential  that  there  should  be  sufficient  safeguards\nagainst political influence being brought to bear on such  appointments.  Of\ncourse, if a Judge owes his appointment to a political party,  certainly  in\nthe course of his career as a Judge, also as an  ordinary  human  being,  he\nwill certainly be bound to have some consideration for the  political  views\nof the authority that has appointed him. That the  Judges  should  be  above\nall these political considerations cannot be  denied.  Therefore,  I  submit\nthat one of the chief conditions mentioned in the procedure laid down,  that\nis the concurrence of the Chief Justice of India in the appointment  of  the\nJudges of the Supreme Court, must be fulfilled. This has been insisted  upon\nin this memo. and  that  is  a  very  salutary  principle  which  should  be\naccepted by this House. I submit, Sir, that it is of the highest  importance\nthat the President must not only consult the Chief  Justice  of  India,  but\nhis concurrence should be  obtained  before  his  colleagues,  that  is  the\nJudges of the Supreme Court, are appointed. It has  been  very  emphatically\nstated in this memo. that it is absolutely  necessary  to  keep  them  above\npolitical influences. No doubt, it  is  said  in  this  procedure  that  the\nGovernor of the State also may be consulted; but that is a matter  of  minor\nimportance. It is likely that the Governor  may  also  have  some  political\ninclinations. Therefore, my amendment has omitted the name of the  Governor.\nThat the judiciary should be above  all  political  parties  and  above  all\npolitical consideration cannot be denied. I do not want to  enter  into  the\ncontroversy at present, which was debated yesterday,  as  to  the  necessity\nfor the independence of the judiciary so far as the executive is  concerned.\nIt is a matter which should receive very serious consideration at the  hands\nof this House and I hope the Honourable  the  Law  Minister  will  also  pay\nserious attention to this aspect of the question, particularly  in  view  of\nthe fact that this recommendation has been made by  the  Federal  Court  and\nthe Chief Justice of the other High Court assembled in conference. I do  not\nthink, Sir, that there can be any higher  authority  on  this  subject  than\nthis conference of the Federal Court and the Chief Justices of  the  various\nHigh Courts in India.\u201d[221]\n\nMr. Mahboob Ali  Baig  Sahib  (Madras:  Muslim)  moved  a  somewhat  similar\namendment. The reason given by Mr. Mahboob Ali Baig Sahib was:\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_533\">\u201cUnder our proposed constitution the President would be  the  constitutional\nHead of the executive. And the  constitution  envisages  what  is  called  a\nparliamentary democracy. So the President  would  be  guided  by  the  Prime\nMinister or the Council of  Ministers  who  are  necessarily  drawn  from  a\npolitical  party.  Therefore  the  decision  of  the  President   would   be\nnecessarily influenced by party considerations. It  is  therefore  necessary\nthat the concurrence of the Chief Justice is made a  pre-requisite  for  the\nappointment of a Judge of the Supreme Court  in  order  to  guard  ourselves\nagainst party influences that may be brought to bear  upon  the  appointment\nof Judges.\u201d[222]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_857\">41.   It is clear that both these Hon\u2019ble  Members  made  the  \u2018concurrence\u2019\nsuggestion since they desired the appointment of  a  judge  of  the  Supreme\nCourt to be free from any sort of political or executive  interference.   It\nappears that these amendments were  moved  unmindful  of  the  insertion  of\nclause (5)a in <a href=\"/doc/825474/\" id=\"a_1601\">Article 62</a> of  the  Draft  Constitution  and  Schedule  III-A\nthereto.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_858\">42.   Be that as it may, there appears to have been some discordance in  the\nviews and  perception  of  different  persons  on  the  exact  role  of  the\nPresident in  the  process  of  appointment  of  judges.  Is  the  President\nexpected to act on the advice of the Council  of  Ministers  or  in  his/her\npersonal capacity?\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_859\">43.   One view, as expressed by Dr. Ambedkar was that  the  President  would\nbe guided by the Council of Ministers. The  other  view  or  perception  was\nthat with  the  insertion  of  clause  (5)a  in  <a href=\"/doc/825474/\" id=\"a_1602\">Article  62</a>  of  the  Draft\nConstitution and  Schedule  III-A  the  President  was  to  act  in  his/her\nindividual capacity and not be guided by the Council of Ministers since  the\nexecutive was to be kept completely out of the appointment  process.  It  is\nnot clear which of the two views found favour with Mr. B. Pocker  Sahib  and\nMr. Mahboob Ali Baig Sahib \u2013 but both were clear that  the  President  could\nbe put under political or party pressure in the recommendation of  a  person\nfor appointment and that this should be avoided and the  pressure  could  be\nnegated by the requirement of  the  concurrence  of  the  Chief  Justice  of\nIndia, an impartial person.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_860\">44.   But what is more significant is that  Mr.  B.  Pocker  Sahib  and  Mr.\nMahboob  Ali  Baig  Sahib  adverted  only  to  a  recommendation   for   the\nappointment  of  a  judge  by  the  President  \u2013  hence  the  necessity   of\nconcurrence by the Chief Justice of India. They did  not,  quite  obviously,\nadvert to the recommendation for the appointment of a  judge  by  the  Chief\nJustice of India.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_861\">45.   It is in this background of divergence of perceptions that the  speech\nof Dr. Ambedkar on 24th May, 1949 should be  appreciated.  Replying  to  the\ndebate, Dr. Ambedkar stated:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_534\">\u201cNow, Sir, with regard to the numerous amendments that have been  moved,  to\nthis article, there are really three  issues  that  have  been  raised.  The\nfirst is, how are the Judges of the  Supreme  Court  to  be  appointed?  Now\ngrouping the different amendments  which  are  related  to  this  particular\nmatter, I find three different proposals. The first  proposal  is  that  the\nJudges of the Supreme Court should be appointed with the concurrence of  the\nChief Justice. That is one view. The other view  is  that  the  appointments\nmade by the President should be subject to the  confirmation  of  two-thirds\nvote by Parliament;  and  the  third  suggestion  is  that  they  should  be\nappointed in consultation with the Council of States.\nWith regard to this matter, I quite agree that the point raised  is  of  the\ngreatest importance. There can be no difference  of  opinion  in  the  House\nthat our judiciary must both be independent of the executive and  must  also\nbe competent in itself. And the question is how these two objects  could  be\nsecured.  There  are  two  in  other  countries.  In   Great   Britain   the\nappointments  are  made  by  the  Crown,  without  any  kind  of  limitation\nwhatsoever, which means by the executive of the day. There is  the  opposite\nsystem in the United States where, for instance,  officers  of  the  Supreme\nCourt as well as other officers of the State shall be made [appointed]  only\nwith the concurrence of the Senate in the United States. It seems to  me  in\nthe circumstances in which we live today, where the sense of  responsibility\nhas not grown to the same extent to which we find it in the  United  States,\nit would  be  dangerous  to  leave  the  appointments  to  be  made  by  the\nPresident, without any kind of reservation or limitation, that  is  to  say,\nmerely on the advice of the executive of the day. Similarly, it seems to  me\nthat to make every appointment which the executive wishes  to  make  subject\nto  the  concurrence  of  the  Legislature  is  also  not  a  very  suitable\nprovision. Apart from its being cumbrous, it also involves  the  possibility\nof the appointment being influenced  by  political  pressure  and  political\nconsiderations. The draft article, therefore, steers  a  middle  course.  It\ndoes not make the President the supreme and the absolute  authority  in  the\nmatter of making appointments. It does not also import the influence of  the\nLegislature.  The  provision  in  the  article  is  that  there  should   be\nconsultation of persons who are ex hypothesi, well qualified to give  proper\nadvice in matters of this sort,  and  my  judgment  is  that  this  sort  of\nprovision may be regarded as sufficient for the moment.\nWith regard to the question of the concurrence  of  the  Chief  Justice,  it\nseems  to  me  that  those  who  advocate  that  proposition  seem  to  rely\nimplicitly both on the impartiality of the Chief Justice and  the  soundness\nof his judgment. I personally feel no doubt that  the  Chief  Justice  is  a\nvery eminent person. But after all the Chief Justice is a man with  all  the\nfailings, all the sentiments and all  the  prejudices  which  we  as  common\npeople have; and I think, to allow the  Chief  Justice  practically  a  veto\nupon the appointment of judges is really to transfer the  authority  to  the\nChief Justice which we are not prepared to vest  in  the  President  or  the\nGovernment of the  day.  I,  therefore,  think  that  is  also  a  dangerous\nproposition.\u201d[223]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_862\">46.    Dr. Ambedkar was quite clear that there could  be  no  difference  of\nopinion that the judiciary should  be  independent  of  the  executive,  yet\ncompetent.  He was of the view that it would be  \u2018dangerous\u2019  to  leave  the\nappointment  of  judges  to  the  President  without  any   reservation   or\nlimitation, that is to say, merely on the advice of  the  executive  of  the\nday. Dr. Ambedkar  seems  to  have  lost  sight  of  the  existence  of  the\nInstrument of Instructions  (or  it  was  \u2018given  up\u2019  by  him)  since  that\ndocument mentioned the advice of the Advisory Board and  not  the  executive\nand also that that document enabled the President to  act  on  his/her  own,\nand not on the advice of the executive.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_863\">47.   If this dichotomy between the role of the President and the  executive\nand the binding or non-binding effect of the advice of the executive on  the\nPresident is appreciated, the views of Dr. Ambedkar become  very  clear.  He\nwas quite  clear  that  the  executive  was  not  to  have  primacy  in  the\nappointment process nor  did  he  want  the  President  to  have  unfettered\ndiscretion to accept or reject  the  advice  of  the  executive  or  act  on\nhis/her own. As far as the concurrence of the Legislature is concerned,  Dr.\nAmbedkar felt that the process would be cumbrous  with  the  possibility  of\npolitical pressure and considerations.  It  is  in  this  context  that  Dr.\nAmbedkar said that he was steering a middle course and was not  prepared  to\ngrant a veto to the President (rejecting the  advice  of  the  executive  or\nacting on his/her own) in  the  appointment  of  judges,  executive  primacy\nhaving already been rejected by him. Under the circumstances, he  felt  that\n\u2018this sort of provision [consultation with the Chief Justice of  India]  may\nbe regarded as sufficient for the moment.\u2019\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_864\">48.   With regard to the \u2018concurrence\u2019 of the Chief  Justice  of  India  (as\nagainst consultation with the Chief Justice of India) in the appointment  of\na judge of the Supreme Court, Dr. Ambedkar  was  of  the  opinion  that  the\nChief Justice, despite his eminence, had all the  failings,  sentiments  and\nprejudices of common people and to confer on him a power of veto,  which  is\nnot vested in the President or the  Government  of  the  day  (that  is  the\nexecutive), would be a \u2018dangerous proposition\u2019.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_865\">49.   Dr. Ambedkar was of the  view  that  neither  the  President  nor  the\nGovernment of the day (the executive) nor the Chief Justice of India  should\nhave the final word in the matter of the appointment  of  judges.  Who  then\nwould have the final say in the event of a  difference  of  opinion  between\nthe President or the Government of the day or the Chief Justice of India  on\nthe appointment of a particular person as a  judge?  Dr.  Ambedkar  did  not\ndirectly address this question  since  he  did  not  visualize  a  stalemate\narising in this regard.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_866\">50.   A small diversion - apart  from  the  reasons  already  mentioned  for\nkeeping the executive out of the decision-taking process in the  appointment\nof judges, it would be of interest  to  know  that,  on  a  different  topic\naltogether, while replying  to  the  debate  \u2018on  acceptance  of  office  by\nmembers of the judiciary after retirement\u2019 Dr. Ambedkar  observed  that  the\njudiciary is very rarely engaged in deciding  issues  between  citizens  and\nthe Government. He said:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_535\">\u201cThe judiciary decides cases in which the Government has,  if  at  all,  the\nremotest interest, in fact no interest at all. The judiciary is  engaged  in\ndeciding the issue between citizens and very  rarely  between  citizens  and\nthe Government. Consequently the chances of influencing  the  conduct  of  a\nmember of the judiciary by the Government are very remote, and  my  personal\nview, therefore, is that the provisions which are  applied  to  the  Federal\nPublic Services Commission  have  no  place  so  far  as  the  judiciary  is\nconcerned.\u201d[224]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_867\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"753224\" id=\"span_319\">51.   Times have changed dramatically  since  then  and  far  from  disputes\n\u2018very rarely\u2019  arising  between  citizens  and  the  Government,  today  the\nGovernment is unashamedly the biggest litigant in the country. It  has  been\nnoticed in Supreme  Court  Advocates  on  <a href=\"/doc/753224/\" id=\"a_1603\">Record  Association  v.  Union  of\nIndia</a>[225] that:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_536\">\u201cNo one can deny that the State in the present  day  has  become  the  major\nlitigant and the  superior  courts  particularly  the  Supreme  Court,  have\nbecome centres for turbulent controversies, some of which with a flavour  of\npolitical repercussions and the  Courts  have  to  face  tempest  and  storm\nbecause their vitality is a  national  imperative.  In  such  circumstances,\ntherefore, can the Government, namely, the major litigant  be  justified  in\nenjoying absolute authority in nominating and  appointing  its  arbitrators.\nThe answer would be in the  negative.  If  such  a  process  is  allowed  to\ncontinue, the independence of judiciary in the long run  will  sink  without\nany trace.\u201d[226]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_868\">52.   Given this fact situation, since there was  this  reason  in  1949  to\ninsulate the judiciary and  the  appointment  process  from  the  direct  or\nindirect influence of the executive and political or party pressures,  there\nis all the more reason to do so today if the independence of  the  judiciary\nis to be maintained.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_869\">53.   In England too the executive is the \u2018most frequent litigator\u2019 and  the\nposition seems to be no  better  than  in  our  country.  In  a  lecture  on\nJudicial Independence, Lord Phillips[227] had this to say:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_537\">\u201cIn modern society the individual citizen is subject to controls imposed  by\nthe executive in respect of almost every aspect of life.  The  authority  to\nimpose most of those  controls  comes,  directly  or  indirectly,  from  the\nlegislature. The citizen  must  be  able  to  challenge  the  legitimacy  of\nexecutive  action  before  an  independent  judiciary.  Because  it  is  the\nexecutive that exercises the power of  the  State  and  because  it  is  the\nexecutive, in one form or another, that is the most  frequent  litigator  in\nthe courts, it is from executive pressure or influence that  judges  require\nparticularly to be protected.\u201d[228]\n\nSummation\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_870\">54.   The discussion leading up to  the  Constituent  Assembly  Debates  and\nrelating to the appointment of judges clearly brings out that:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_538\">(1) The independence of the judiciary  was  unflinchingly  accepted  by  all\npolicy and decision makers;\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_539\">(2) The appointment of judges of the Supreme Court and the High  Courts  was\nto be through a consultative process between the  President  and  the  Chief\nJustice of India, neither of whom had unfettered discretion in the matter;\n(3) In any event, the political executive had no role or a very little  role\nto play in the decision-taking process. Notwithstanding this, the  political\nexecutive did interfere in the  appointment  process  as  evidenced  by  the\nMemorandum prepared in the Conference of  Chief  Justices  by,  inter  alia,\nrecommending  persons  for  appointment  as  judges  of  the   High   Court.\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_871\">Resultantly, the appointment of judges to the High Courts was not always  on\nmerit and sometimes without the recommendation of the Chief Justice  of  the\nHigh Court;\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_872\">      (4) A  constitutional  convention  existed  that  the  appointment  of\njudges should be made in conformity with the views of the Chief  Justice  of\nIndia;\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_873\">(5) The proposal for the appointment of a judge of the Supreme  Court  or  a\nHigh Court could originate from the President (although  it  never  did)  or\nthe Chief Justice of India and regardless of the origin, it  would  normally\nbe accepted.  However,  the  possibility  of  the  President  giving  in  to\npolitical or party pressures was not outside the realm of imagination.\n(6) Historically, the Chief Justice of India was  always  consulted  in  the\nmatter of appointment of judges,  and  conventionally  his  concurrence  was\nalways  taken  regardless  of  whether  a  recommendation  for   appointment\noriginated from the Chief  Justice  of  the  High  Court  or  the  political\nexecutive. It is in this  light  that  the  discussion  in  the  Constituent\nAssembly on the issue of appointment of judges to the Supreme Court and  the\nHigh Courts deserves to be appreciated.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_874\">(7) It remained a grey area  whether  in  the  appointment  of  judges,  the\nPresident was expected to act on  his/her  own  or  on  the  advice  of  the\npolitical executive.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_875\">Views of the Law Commission of India\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_876\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"251494\" id=\"span_320\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"112850760\" id=\"span_321\">55.   The issue of the appointment of judges of the Supreme  Court  and  the\nHigh Courts was first addressed, after Independence, in the 14th  Report  of\nthe Law Commission of India (for short the LCI), then  in  the  80th  Report\nand finally in the 121st Report. (A reference was made in the  214th  Report\nand the 230th Report but they are of no immediate  consequence).  The  issue\nalso came to be addressed in <a href=\"/doc/112850760/\" id=\"a_1604\">S.P.  Gupta  v.  Union  of  India</a>[229]  and  in\n<a href=\"/doc/251494/\" id=\"a_1605\">Subhash Sharma v. Union of India</a>.[230] It was also  the  subject  matter  of\nthree Constitution amendment Bills and  two  other  pronouncements  of  this\nCourt rendered by larger Benches. This is mentioned only  to  highlight  the\ncomplexity of the issue and the  constant  search  for  some  stability  and\ncertainty in the appointment process in relation to the independence of  the\njudiciary. It has been said with regard to the selection of  judges  in  the\nUnited States, and this would equally apply to our country:</span></span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_540\">\u201cIt is fairly certain that no single subject has consumed as many  pages  in\nlaw reviews and law-related publications over  the  past  50  years  as  the\nsubject of judicial selection.\u201d[231]\n\n\n1 (a) 14th Report \u2013 26.9.1958\n\n\n2 Appointment of judges of the Supreme Court\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_322\">3</span>\n\n\n4 56. Within less than a decade of the  promulgation  of  the  Constitution,\nthe process of appointment of judges of  the  Supreme  Court  and  the  High\nCourts came in for sharp criticism from the LCI. Chapter 5 and Chapter 6  of\nthe 14th Report of the LCI relating,  inter  alia,  to  the  appointment  of\njudges to the Supreme Court and  judges  to  the  High  Courts  respectively\nmakes for some sad reading, more  particularly  since  the  Attorney-General\nfor India was the Chair of the LCI.[232] It must be noted here that the  LCI\ntravelled through the length and breadth of the country for about  one  year\nand examined as many as  473  witnesses  from  a  cross-section  of  society\nbefore giving its Report. It also adopted a  novel  procedure  of  co-opting\ntwo members from the States that were visited so as to understand the  local\nproblems. The monumental and authoritative work can only be admired.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_541\">57.   The LCI observed that the Constitution endeavored  to  put  judges  of\nthe  Supreme  Court  \u2018above  executive  control\u2019.   It   very   specifically\nacknowledged  the  importance  of  safeguarding  the  independence  of   the\njudiciary and observed that \u2018It is obvious that the selection of the  Judges\nconstituting a Court of such pivotal  importance  to  the  progress  of  the\nnation must be a responsibility to be exercised with great care.\u2019[233]\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_542\">58.   Thereafter three central issues were adverted to \u2013  (1)  Communal  and\nregional considerations had prevailed in making  the  selection  of  judges.\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_877\">(2) The general impression was that executive influence was exerted now  and\nagain from the highest quarters in  respect  of  some  appointments  to  the\nBench. (3) The best talent among the judges of the High Courts did not  find\nits way to the Supreme Court.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_878\">59.   The Report said:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_543\">      \u201cIt is widely felt that  communal  and  regional  considerations  have\nprevailed in making the selection of the Judges.  The  idea  seems  to  have\ngained ground that the component States of India should have,  as  it  were,\nrepresentation on the Court.  Though we  call  ourselves  a  secular  State,\nideas of communal representation, which were viciously planted in  our  body\npolitic by the British,  have  not  entirely  lost  their  influence.   What\nperhaps is still more to be regretted is the general  impression,  that  now\nand again executive influence exerted from the  highest  quarters  has  been\nresponsible for some appointments to the Bench.   It  is  undoubtedly  true,\nthat the best talent among the Judges of the  High  Courts  has  not  always\nfound its way to the Supreme Court.   This  has  prevented  the  Court  from\nbeing looked upon by the subordinate Courts and the  public  generally  with\nthat  respect  and  indeed,  reverence   to   which   it   by   its   status\nentitled.\u201d[234]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_879\">60.   On the basis of its findings, the LCI recommended,  inter  alia,  that\n\u2018communal and regional considerations should play no part in the  making  of\nappointments to the Supreme Court.\u2019  However, the LCI did  not  proffer  any\nsolution to the vexed issue of making more satisfactory appointments to  the\nSupreme Court.\n</p>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_323\">5</span>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_880\">6 Appointment of judges of the High Courts\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_881\">61.   Similarly, Chapter 6 of  the  Report  concerning  the  appointment  of\njudges to the High Courts makes for equally sad reading.   The  inadequacies\nin the appointments made were pointed out as: (1) The selections  have  been\nunsatisfactory  and  induced  by  executive  influence.  (2)  There  is   no\nrecognizable principle for making the  appointments  and  considerations  of\npolitical expediency or regional or communal sentiments have played a  role.\n(3) Merit has been ignored in making appointments.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_882\">62.   It was said that these inadequacies were well founded  and  there  was\nacute public dissatisfaction with the appointments made:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_544\">\u201cWe have visited all the High Court centres and on all hands we  have  heard\nbitter and reviling criticism about the  appointments  made  to  High  Court\njudiciary give in recent years.  This criticism has  been  made  by  Supreme\nCourt  Judges,  High  Court  Judges,  Retired  Judges,  Public   Prosecutors\nnumerous  representatives,  associations  of   the   Bar,   principals   and\nprofessors of Law  Colleges  and  very  responsible  members  of  the  legal\nprofession all over the country.  One of the State Governments had to  admit\nthat some of the selections did  not  seem  to  be  good  and  that  careful\nscrutiny was necessary.  The almost universal chorus of comment is that  the\nselections are unsatisfactory and that they have been induced  by  executive\ninfluence. It has been said that these selections appears to have  proceeded\non  no  recognizable  principle  and  seem  to  have  been   made   out   of\nconsideration of political expediency or regional  or  communal  sentiments.\nSome of the members of the Bar appointed to the Bench  did  not  occupy  the\nfront rank in the profession either in the matter of legal equipment  or  of\nthe volume of their practice at the bar.   A  number  of  more  capable  and\ndeserving persons appear to have been ignored  for  reasons  that  can  stem\nonly from political or communal or similar  grounds.   Equally  forceful  or\neven more unfavourable  comments  have  been  made  in  respect  of  persons\nselected form the services.  We are convinced that the  views  expressed  to\nus  show  a  well  founded  and  acute  public  dissatisfaction   at   these\nappointments.\u201d[235]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_883\">63.   On the procedure followed for the appointment of a judge of  the  High\nCourt and the administrative working of <a href=\"/doc/1682952/\" id=\"a_1606\">Article  217</a>  of  the  Constitution,\nthe LCI had this to say:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_545\">\u201cThe Chief Justice forwards his recommendation to the Chief Minister who  in\nturn forwards this recommendation in consultation with the Governor  to  the\nMinister of Home Affairs in the Central Government. If, however,  the  Chief\nMinister does not agree with the recommendation of  the  Chief  Justice,  he\nmakes his own recommendation. It appears that in  such  a  case,  the  Chief\nJustice  is  given  an  opportunity  for  making   his   comments   on   the\nrecommendation made by the Chief Minister. This practice  is  not,  however,\ninvariably  followed  so  that,  in  some  cases   it   happens   that   the\nrecommendation made by the Chief Minister does not come to the knowledge  of\nthe Chief Justice. The rival  recommendations  are  then  forwarded  to  the\nMinister of Home Affairs who, in consultation  with  the  Chief  Justice  of\nIndia, advises the President as to the selection  to  be  made.  The  person\nrecommended by the Chief Minister may be, and occasionally is,  selected  in\npreference to the person recommended by the Chief Justice.\u201d[236]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_884\">64.   The LCI recorded that no less a personage than the  Chief  Justice  of\nIndia had this to say about executive interference  in  the  appointment  of\njudges to the High Courts (for reasons other than merit):\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_546\">      \u201cThe Chief Minister now has a hand direct or indirect  in  the  matter\nof the appointment to the High Court Bench.  The inevitable result has  been\nthat the High Court appointments  are  not  always  made  on  merit  but  on\nextraneous considerations of community, caste, political  affiliations,  and\nlikes  and  dislikes  have  a  free  play.   This   necessarily   encourages\ncanvassing which, I am sorry to say, has become the order of the  day.   The\nChief Minister holding a political office dependent on the goodwill  of  his\nparty followers may well be induced to listen and give  way  to  canvassing.\nThe Chief Justice on the other hand does not hold his office  on  sufferance\nof any party and he knows the advocates and their merits and demerits and  a\nrecommendation by the Chief Justice is therefore more likely to be on  merit\nalone that the one made by the Chief Minister who  may  know  nothing  about\nthe comparative legal acumen of the advocates.\u201d[237]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_885\">65.   To conclude this aspect, the Report observes that  extraneous  factors\nhave influenced the appointments and that there seems to be  canvassing  for\nappointment as a judge of the High Court:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_547\">\u201cThis indeed is a dismal picture and would seem to show that the  atmosphere\nof communalism, regionalism and political patronage, have in a  considerable\nmeasure influenced appointments to the High Court Judiciary.\nApart from this very disquieting feature, the prevalence of  canvassing  for\njudgeships is also a distressing development. Formerly, a member of the  Bar\nwas invited to accept a judgeship and he considered  it  a  great  privilege\nand honour. Within a few years of Independence, however, the judgeship of  a\nHigh Court  seems  to  have  become  a  post  to  be  worked  and  canvassed\nfor.\u201d[238]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_886\">66.   Based on its findings, the  LCI  reached  the  following  conclusions,\namongst others:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_548\">\u201c(8) Many unsatisfactory appointments have been made to the High  Courts  on\npolitical regional and communal or other grounds with the  result  that  the\nfittest men have not been appointed.  This has resulted in a  diminution  in\nthe out-turn of work of the Judges.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_549\">(9) These unsatisfactory appointments have  been  made  notwithstanding  the\nfact that in the vast majority of cases, appointments  have  been  concurred\nin by the Chief Justice of the High Court and the Chief Justice of India.\n(10) Consultation with the State executive is necessary before  appointments\nare made to the High Court.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_550\">(11) While it should be open to the  State  executive  to  express  its  own\nopinion on a name proposed by the Chief Justice, it should not  be  open  to\nit to propose a nominee of its own and forward it to the Centre.\n(12) The role of the State  executive  should  be  confined  to  making  its\nremarks about the nominee proposed by the Chief  Justice  and  if  necessary\nasking the Chief Justice to make a fresh recommendation.\n(14)  <a href=\"/doc/1682952/\" id=\"a_1607\">Article 217</a> of the Constitution should be amended to  provide  that  a\nJudge of a High Court should be appointed only on the recommendation of  the\nChief Justice of that State and with the concurrence of  the  Chief  Justice\nof India.\u201d[239]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_887\">67.   Unlike in the appointment of judges to  the  Supreme  Court,  the  LCI\nsuggested, for the High Courts, that <a href=\"/doc/1682952/\" id=\"a_1608\">Article 217</a> of the  Constitution  ought\nto be amended to incorporate the concurrence of the Chief Justice  of  India\nto the appointment. This recommendation was made  so  that,  in  future,  no\nappointment could be made without the concurrence of the  Chief  Justice  of\nIndia.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_888\">68.   The Report was  considered  in  Parliament  on  23rd,  24th  and  25th\nNovember, 1959 and the Government of the day gave its point of view, as  did\nseveral Hon\u2019ble Members. But what is more important is that  in  the  debate\non 24th November, 1959 it was stated by Shri Govind  Ballabh  Pant,  Hon\u2019ble\nMinister of Home Affairs that  since  1950,  as  many  as  211  judges  were\nappointed to the High Courts and out of these except one \u2018were made  on  the\nadvice, with the consent and concurrence of the Chief Justice of India.  And\nout of the 211, 196 proposals which were accepted by the Government had  the\nsupport of all persons who were connected with this matter.\u2019[240]\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_889\">69.   A little later it was stated:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_551\">\u201cBut as I said, these 196 appointments were  made  in  accordance  with  the\nunanimous advice of the Chief Justice of the High Court, the Chief  Minister\nof the State, the Governor and the  Chief  Justice  of  India.   There  were\nfifteen cases in which there was a difference of opinion between  the  Chief\nJustice and the Chief Minister or the Governor. So, these  cases  also  were\nreferred to the Chief Justice of India.  In some of these  he  accepted  the\nproposal made by the Chief Minister and in others he accepted the advice  or\nthe suggestion received from the Chief Justice of the High  Court.   But  we\non our part had his advice along with that of the Chief Justice of the  High\nCourt concerned and of the Chief Minister concerned. So, these cases do  not\neven come to five per cent. But even there, so far as we are concerned,  out\nof these 211 cases, as I  said,  except  in  one  case  where  there  was  a\ndifference of opinion between the Chief Minister and the Chief  Justice,  we\nhad accepted in 210 cases the advice of the Chief Justice of India.\u201d[241]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_890\">70.   On the next day, that is, 25th November, 1959 Shri A.K. Sen,  Minister\nof Law reiterated the statement made by the  Home  Minister.   He  clarified\nthat in one case where there was a difference  of  opinion,  the  Government\naccepted the advice of the Chief Justice of the High Court  (not  the  Chief\nMinister) rather than the advice of the Chief Justice of India.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_891\">71.   The discussion ended  with  an  Hon\u2019ble  Member  suggesting  that  the\nrecommendations of the LCI be taken note of and implemented  as  quickly  as\npossible.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_892\">72.   What is of  importance  in  this  Report  (apart  from  several  other\nconclusions) is that there had been instances  where  a  recommendation  for\nappointment as a judge of the High Court was  made  by  the  Chief  Minister\nwithout the knowledge of the Chief Justice and that canvassing had begun  to\ntake place for appointment as a judge of the High Court.  But in all  cases,\nexcept one, the concurrence of the Chief Justice of India was taken.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_893\">(b) 80th Report \u2013 10.8.1979\n\n\n1 Appointment of judges of the Supreme Court\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_894\">73.   The 80th Report of the LCI was submitted on 10th August, 1979  and  it\nwas mainly prepared by Justice H.R. Khanna when he was its Chair.[242]\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_895\">74.    It  was  observed  that  an  independent  judiciary   is   absolutely\nindispensible  for  ensuring  the  Rule  of  Law.  Generally  in  regard  to\nappointment  of  judges,  it  was  observed  that  wrong  appointments  have\naffected the image of the Courts and have undermined the confidence  of  the\npeople in them. Further, it was observed that an  appointment  not  made  on\nmerit but because  of  favouritism  or  other  ulterior  considerations  can\nhardly command real and spontaneous respect of the Bar and that  the  effect\nof an improper appointment is felt not only  for  the  time  being  but  its\nrepercussions are felt long thereafter.[243]\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_896\">75.   In this background, and in relation to the appointment  of  judges  of\nthe Supreme Court, it was concluded that (1)  Only  persons  who  enjoy  the\nhighest reputation for independence, dispassionate approach  and  detachment\nshould be elevated to the Supreme Court. (2) No one should  be  appointed  a\njudge  of  the  Supreme  Court  unless  he  has  severed  affiliations  with\npolitical parties for at least 7 (seven)  years.  (3)  A  person  should  be\nappointed as a judge if he has distinguished himself for  his  independence,\ndispassionate  approach  and  freedom  from  political  prejudice,  bias  or\nleaning.[244]\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_897\">76.   Significantly, the LCI recommended adopting a consultative process  in\nthat the Chief  Justice  of  India  should  consult  his  three  senior-most\ncolleagues while making a  recommendation  for  an  appointment.  He  should\nreproduce their views while making the recommendation. This  would  minimize\nthe chances of any possible arbitrariness or favouritism.[245]\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_898\">77.   These recommendations were incorporated by the LCI in its  summary  of\nrecommendations. I am concerned with the following recommendation:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_552\">\u201c(32) The Chief Justice of India, while recommending the name  of  a  person\nfor appointment as a Judge of the Supreme Court  should  consult  his  three\nsenior most colleagues and should in  the  communication  incorporating  his\nrecommendation specify the result of such  consultation  and  reproduce  the\nviews of each of his colleagues so consulted regarding  his  recommendation.\nThe role of  these  colleagues  would  be  confined  to  commenting  on  the\nrecommendation of the  Chief  Justice.   Such  consultation  would  minimize\npossible arbitrariness or favoritism\u201d[246]\n\n2 Appointment of judges of the High Court\n\n\n3 78. In relation to the appointment of judges of the  High  Court,  it  was\ngenerally observed  by  the  LCI  in  Chapter  6  of  the  Report  that  the\nprevailing impression was that their appointment \u2018has not been  always  made\non merit and that this has affected the image of the High Courts.\u2019 [247]\n\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_553\">79.   The LCI suggested a consultation process  for  the  appointment  of  a\njudge of the High Court. It was suggested that  the  Chief  Justice  should,\nwhen making a recommendation, consult his  two  senior-most  colleagues  and\nindicate their views in writing. This would  have  a  \u2018healthy  effect\u2019  and\nconsiderably minimize the chances of  possible  favoritism.  It  was  opined\nthat any recommendation of the Chief Justice which is concurred with by  the\ntwo senior-most  judges  should  normally  be  accepted.  The  LCI  was,  in\nprinciple, against the selection of persons as judges of the High  Court  on\ngrounds or considerations of religion, caste or region.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_554\">80.   With regard to the  recommendations  originating  from  the  political\nexecutive it was said:\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_555\">\u201cAnother question which has engaged attention is as to whether the  role  of\nthe Chief Minister should be that of commenting on the name  recommended  by\nthe Chief Justice, or whether, in case he disagrees with the  recommendation\nof the Chief Justice, he (the  Chief  Minister)  can  also  suggest  another\nname.  This question was agitated in the past, and after  due  consideration\nit was decided that the  Chief  Minister  would  be  entitled,  in  case  he\ndisagrees with the recommendation of the Chief Justice  to  suggest  another\nname.  The Chief Minister in such an event has to  invite  the  comments  of\nthe Chief Justice and send the matter thereafter along with the comments  of\nthe Chief Justice, to the Union Minister of Law and  Justice.   In  view  of\nthe fact that a decision <a href=\"/doc/251494/\" id=\"a_1609\">referred to above</a> has already been taken after  due\nconsideration, we need not say anything further in the matter.\u201d[248]\n\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_556\">81.   Keeping all these factors in mind, some of  the  recommendations  made\nby the LCI were as follows:\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_557\">\u201c(3) When making a recommendation for appointment  of  a  judge  of  a  High\nCourt, the Chief Justice should consult his two seniormost  colleagues.  The\nChief Justice, in his letter recommending the appointment, should state  the\nfact of such consultation and indicate the views of his  two  colleagues  so\nconsulted.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_558\">(4) Any recommendation of the Chief Justice which  carries  the  concurrence\nof his two seniormost colleagues should normally be accepted.\n(7) The Commission is, in principle, against selection  to  the  High  Court\nBench on ground of religion, caste or region.   Merit  should  be  the  only\nconsideration.  Even when matters of State policy make it necessary to  give\nrepresentation to persons belonging  to  some  religion,  caste  or  region,\nevery effort should be made to select the best person.  The number  of  such\nappointments should be as few as possible.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_559\">(12) On the question whether the role of the Chief Minister should  be  that\nonly of commenting on the name recommended by the Chief Justice, or  whether\nthe Chief Minister can also suggest another name,  a  decision  has  already\nbeen taken and nothing further need be said in the matter.\u201d[249]\n\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_560\">82.   Generally speaking, the LCI was of the view  that  the  constitutional\nscheme  of  appointment  of  judges  was   basically   sound,   had   worked\nsatisfactorily and did not call for any radical change, though some  aspects\nneeded improvement. The recommendations mentioned above were  made  in  that\nlight.\n</blockquote>\n\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_899\">(c) 121st Report \u2013 31.7.1987\n\n\n1 A new forum for judicial appointments\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_900\">83.   It is important to note  that  this  Report  was  prepared  after  the\ndecision of this Court in S.P. Gupta.  In its 121st Report,  the  LCI  noted\nthat over the last four decades, mounting dissatisfaction  has  been  voiced\nover the method and strategy of selection  and  the  selectees  to  man  the\nsuperior judiciary.[250]  Further, in paragraph 7.1 of its Report,  the  LCI\nnoted that  \u2018Everyone  is  agreed  that  the  present  scheme  or  model  or\nmechanism for recruitment to superior judiciary has failed  to  deliver  the\ngoods.\u2019 This was with reference to  the  executive  primacy  theory  in  the\nappointment of judges propounded in  S.P. Gupta.  In view of  this  the  LCI\nrecommended a new broad-based  model  called  a  National  Judicial  Service\nCommission.[251]\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_901\">84.   The LCI observed that two models were available  for  the  appointment\nof judges. The first was  the  existing  model  which  conferred  overriding\npowers on the executive in selecting and appointing judges. But, <a href=\"/doc/192717/\" id=\"a_1610\">Article  50</a>\nof the Constitution mandates a separation  between  the  Executive  and  the\nJudiciary. The second model involved diluting (not excluding) the  authority\nof the executive by associating more people in the decision  making  process\nand setting up a body in which the judiciary  has  a  pre-eminent  position.\nThis participatory model was called by the  LCI  as  the  National  Judicial\nService Commission.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_902\">85.   The Commission was envisaged as a  multi-member  body  headed  by  the\nChief Justice of India whose \u2018pre-eminent position should not be diluted  at\nall\u2019, his predecessor in office, three senior-most  judges  of  the  Supreme\nCourt, three Chief Justices of the High Courts in order of their  seniority,\nthe Law Minister, the Attorney-General for  India  and  an  outstanding  law\nacademic. Thus, an 11 (eleven) member body was proposed by the LCI  for  the\nselection and appointment of judges  of  the  Supreme  Court  and  the  High\nCourts. To give effect to the recommendation, it was  proposed  to  suitably\namend the Constitution.[252]\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_903\">86.    The  recommendation  of  the  LCI  was  partially  accepted  by   the\ngovernment of the day and the Constitution (Sixty-seventh  Amendment)  Bill,\n1990 was introduced in Parliament.   This  will  be  adverted  to  a  little\nlater.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_904\">Arrears Committee \u2013 1989-90\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_905\">87.   Between 11th and 13th December, 1987 a Conference  of  Chief  Justices\nwas held with the Chief Justice of  India  in  the  Chair.   The  Conference\ndiscussed, inter alia, issues relating to  arrears  of  cases  in  the  High\nCourts and the District Courts in the country.  Grave concern was  expressed\nover the problem of arrears and it was pointed out by  most  Chief  Justices\nthat delay in the appointment of judges  is  responsible  for  the  arrears.\nEven after recommendations are sent, the Chief Justice has  to  wait  for  a\nlong time for the Government to make the appointment with  the  result  that\nfor a number of years Courts have been  working  with  about  50%  of  their\nstrength.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_906\">88.   After a detailed discussion of the matter, it was decided  to  appoint\na committee of Chief Justices to thoroughly examine the issues raised and  a\nResolution was passed appointing such a committee.  The composition  of  the\ncommittee called the Arrears Committee changed over a  period  of  time  but\nfinally it consisted of Chief Justice V.S.  Malimath  (Kerala  High  Court),\nChief Justice P.D. Desai (Calcutta High Court) and Chief  Justice  Dr.  A.S.\nAnand (Madras High Court).  The Arrears Committee gave  its  Report  in  two\nvolumes to the Conference of Chief Justices held  between  31st  August  and\n2nd  September,  1990  which  accepted  the  Reports,  subject  to   a   few\nmodifications.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_907\">89.   Chapter 5 of Volume 2 of the  Report  deals  with  the  unsatisfactory\nappointment of judges to the High Courts.  It was observed  by  the  Arrears\nCommittee that unsatisfactory  appointments  have  contributed  in  a  large\nmeasure to the accumulation of arrears in the High Courts.  It was  observed\nthat merit and  merit  alone,  coupled  with  a  reputation  for  integrity,\nsuitability and capability has to be the criterion for selection  of  judges\nand  judges  not  selected  on  that  basis  or   who   are   appointed   on\nconsiderations other than merit, may not be  able  to  act  impartially  and\nfairly.  It was noted that for this reason the selection  of  judges  should\nbe made with utmost care and concern.[253]\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_908\">90.   The Arrears Committee also considered the Report given in  the  recent\npast by the Satish Chandra Committee which was of the  confirmed  view  that\nsome judges have not been directly recommended by the Chief Justice  of  the\nHigh Court but have been foisted on the High Court and that  if  this  trend\ncontinued, it would be very difficult for the Chief Justice  to  effectively\ntransact the judicial business of the Court.[254]\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_909\">91.   Thereafter, the selection of a judge of the  High  Court  for  reasons\nother than merit was discussed and it was observed as follows:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_561\">\u201cThe selection of a person, on considerations  other  than  merit,  has  far\nreaching consequences and does more damage than what  apparently  meets  the\neye.  Such an appointee does not even receive from the members  of  the  Bar\nthe measure of respect and  co-operation  which  is  imperative  for  proper\nadministration of justice.  He may not have confidence even in  himself  and\na command over the proceedings of the Court.  All this would be at the  cost\nof proper administration of justice.
