The effect would be felt not  only  on\nthe quality but also on the quantity of the work turned out.\nAccording to Satish Chandra Committee, the sea change  which  has  gradually\ncome into the political  process  is  directly  responsible  for  the  grave\ndeterioration and the fall in the high  standards  of  appointments  to  the\nHigh Court  Bench  previously  maintained.  Barring  exceptions,  the  Chief\nMinisters to-day have come to think that even filling up  vacancies  on  the\nHigh Court Bench is a matter  of  patronage,  political  or  otherwise.   It\nnoticed that formerly members of the Bar were invited to accept  judge-ship.\nNow, the judge-ship of the High Court seems to have  become  a  post  to  be\ncanvassed for.  It was found that as long as  the  State  executive  has  an\neffective  hand  in  such  appointments,  this  disquieting  feature   would\ncontinue and that it could be remedied only by providing  the  safeguard  of\nthe executive having no final say in the matter of appointment and that  the\nlast word in the matter should be of the Chief Justice  of  the  High  Court\nconcerned and  the  Chief  Justice  of  India.   The  Committee,  therefore,\nsuggested amendment of the Constitution, as a  guarantee  for  ensuring  the\nquality, that an appointment to the High Court must have the concurrence  of\nthe Chief Justice of India and should not be  made  merely  in  consultation\nwith him.  An amendment was suggested to <a href=\"/doc/259647/\" id=\"a_1611\">Article 217(1)</a> of the  Constitution\non those lines.\u201d[255]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_910\">92.   It was concluded that for the judicial system to function  effectively\nand for the people to have faith and confidence in it,  the  appointment  of\njudges  should  be  made  only  on  considerations  of  merit,  suitability,\nintegrity and capability and not on  political  expediency  or  regional  or\ncommunal sentiments.  It was observed in this regard as follows:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_562\">\u201cThis Committee is of the firm view that to ensure that the judicial  system\nfunctions effectively and to maintain  both  the  quality  and  quantity  of\njudicial work, as well as the  faith  and  confidence  of  the  public,  the\nappointments  be  made  only  on  considerations  of   merit,   suitability,\nintegrity and capability and not of  political  expediency  or  regional  or\ncommunal sentiments. The apprehension that the recommendation  made  by  him\nmay not meet with the approval of the  executive,  may  sometimes  induce  a\nChief Justice to propose the name of a person who does not measure upto  the\nrequisite standard, which is rather unfortunate.  It is fundamental for  the\npreservation of the independence of the  judiciary  that  it  be  free  from\nthreats and pressures from any quarter.  It is the  duty  of  the  State  to\nensure that the judiciary occupies, and is seen to occupy, such position  in\nthe polity that it can effectively perform the functions entrusted to it  by\nthe Constitution and that can be done only if the process of appointment  is\nleft unpolluted.\u201d [256]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_911\">93.   Commenting on the  existing  system  of  appointment  of  judges,  the\nArrears Committee reviewed the system in Chapter 6 of  the  Report.  Amongst\nother things it was observed that the system of appointment  of  judges  had\nbeen prevailing for four decades and it was  functioning  satisfactorily  so\nlong as well-established conventions were honoured and followed and that  it\nis not the system that has failed but those operating it had  failed  it  by\nallowing it to be perverted.  It was observed as follows:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_563\">\u201cThe present system of appointment of Judges to the High Courts has been  in\nvogue for about four decades. It functioned satisfactorily as  long  as  the\nwell-established conventions were honoured and followed.  The  gradual,  but\nsystematic violation and virtual annihilation of the  conventions  over  the\npast  two  decades  or  so  is  essentially  responsible  for  the   present\nunfortunate situation.  Has  the  system,  therefore,  failed  or  have  the\nconcerned failed the system is an all important  question.  It  is  apparent\nthat the system has not failed, but all those concerned with  operating  the\nsystem have failed it by allowing it to be perverted.\u201d[257]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_912\">94.   While dealing with the Memorandum of Procedure in  existence  at  that\ntime for the  appointment  of  judges,  the  Arrears  Committee  was  rather\nscathing in its observations to the effect that there had been  cases  where\nthere was agreement between the Chief Justice of India,  the  Chief  Justice\nof the concerned High Court and the Governor of the State but the Union  Law\nMinister either choose not to make the appointment or  inordinately  delayed\nthe appointment.  It was observed that  sometimes  the  Union  Law  Minister\nadopted a pick and choose policy to appoint judges or disturb the  order  in\nwhich the recommendations were made.  There had been political  interference\nin  this  regard   and   undesirable   influence   of   extra-constitutional\nauthorities  in  the  appointment  of  judges.   The   appointment   process\ntherefore was undermined leaving the executive  to  appoint  judges  not  on\nexcellence but on influence.  It was observed as follows:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_564\">\u201cThere are cases that even  where  the  Chief  Justice  of  India  on  being\nconsulted, agrees with the recommendation made by the Chief Justice  of  the\nconcerned High Court which is also concurred  to  by  the  Governor  of  the\nState  and  forwards  his  recommendation  to  the   Union   Law   Minister,\nappointments  are  either  not  made  or  made   after   inordinate   delay.\nSometimes, the Union Law Minister even adopts the \u201cpick and  choose\u201d  policy\nto appoint Judges out of the list of  selectees  recommended  by  the  Chief\nJustice of the High Court duly concurred in by the Chief  Justice  of  India\nor makes appointments by disturbing the order in which  the  recommendations\nhave been made.  The malady has become more acute in view of  the  political\ninterference   and   undesirable   influence   of   \u201cextra    constitutional\nauthorities\u201d in the appointment of  judges.   Thus,  the  authority  of  the\nChief Justice of India and the role of the Chief Justice of the High  Courts\nin the matter of appointment of superior judiciary have, to a great  extent,\nbeen  undermined,  leaving  to  the  executive  to  appoint  Judges  not  on\n\u201cexcellence\u201d but on  \u201cinfluence\u201d.   Thus,  merit,  ability  and  suitability\nwhich undoubtedly the Chief Justice of the High Court  is  the  most  proper\nperson to  judge,  are  sacrificed  at  the  altar  of  political  or  other\nexpediency.  This attitude is essentially responsible for the  deterioration\nand the fall in the  high  standards  of  appointments  to  the  High  Court\nBenches.  It is unfortunate, but absolutely true, that the  Chief  Ministers\nhave come to think and the Union Law Minister has come to believe  that  the\nvacancy in the High Court Bench is a matter  of  political  patronage  which\nthey are entitled to distribute or dole out to their favourites.  This  veto\npower with the executive has played havoc in the matter  of  appointment  of\nJudges.\u201d[258]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_913\">95.   In its recommendations, the Arrears Committee recommended dilution  of\nthe role of the executive and measures  to  avoid  the  existing  system  of\nappointment from being perverted.  It was recommended as follows:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_565\">\u201cThe role of the executive in the matter of appointment of judges should  be\ndiluted and that the cause for most of the ills in the  functioning  of  the\npresent system could be traced back to the  veto  power  of  the  executive.\nThis, indeed, is capable of being remedied by making certain  amendments  to\n<a href=\"/doc/1682952/\" id=\"a_1612\">Article 217</a> providing  for  concurrence  of  the  Chief  Justice  of  India,\ninstead of consultation with him, in the matter of appointment of Judges  of\nthe High Courts.\u201d[259]\n\u201cThe Committee is of the view that the present constitutional  scheme  which\nwas framed by  the  founding  fathers  after  great  deliberation  and  much\nreflection is intrinsically sound and that it worked in the true  spirit  it\ndoes not require any radical change.  In order to guard against and  obviate\nthe perversion revealed in the operation of the scheme,  the  Committee  has\nmade  suitable  recommendations.  The  Committee  believes  that  if   these\nrecommendations are given  effect  to,  there  would  not  be  any  need  to\nsubstitute it by a different mechanism.\u201d[260]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_914\">96.   In view of the scathing indictment of the  system  of  appointment  of\njudges where the executive had the \u2018ultimate  power\u2019[261]  which  was  being\nabused and perverted  to  take  away  the  independence  of  the  judiciary,\ncontrary to the intention of the Constituent Assembly, there was  no  option\nbut to have a fresh look into the entire issue of appointment of judges  and\nthat eventually led to the issue being referred in the  early  1990\u2019s  to  a\nBench of 9 (nine) judges of this Court. Quite  clearly,  the  executive  had\nmade a mess of the  appointment  of  judges,  taken  steps  to  subvert  the\nindependence of the judiciary, gone against the grain of the  views  of  the\nConstituent Assembly and acted in a  manner  that  a  responsible  executive\nought not to.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_915\">97.   Post Independence till the early 1990s, the  judiciary  saw  the  slow\nbut sure interference of the executive in the appointment  of  judges.  This\nwas in the form of the executive recommending persons to the  Chief  Justice\nof the High Court for appointment as a judge of the High Court.  There  were\noccasions when the executive completely by-passed the Chief Justice  of  the\nHigh Court and directly recommended persons  to  the  Union  Government  for\nappointment as judges. The third stratagem adopted by the executive  was  to\nwithhold recommendations made by the Chief Justice and instead  forward  its\nown recommendation to the Union Government. The fourth method was to  reopen\napproved recommendations on some pretext or the other. The fifth method  was\nto delay processing a recommendation made by the Chief Justice.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_916\">98.   Tragically, almost all the appointments made during  this  period  had\nthe concurrence (as a constitutional convention) of  the  Chief  Justice  of\nIndia and yet, there was criticism of some of the appointments  made.  While\nthe independence of the judiciary  was  maintained  at  law,  it  was  being\nslowly eroded both from  within  and  without  through  the  appointment  of\n\u2018unsuitable\u2019 judges with merit occasionally taking a  side  seat.  The  14th\nReport of the LCI was generally critical of the  appointments  made  to  the\nHigh  Courts  and  in  this  regard  reliance  was  placed  by  the  LCI  on\ninformation collected from various sources including judges of  the  Supreme\nCourt. It is true that the 80th Report of the LCI  found  nothing  seriously\nwrong with the system of appointment  of  judges,  but  it  still  needed  a\nchange. The Arrears Committee, however, was derisive of the existing  system\nof appointment of judges and made some positive recommendations  within  the\nexisting system, while the 121st  Report  of  the  LCI  suggested  wholesale\nchanges.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_917\">99.   This discussion in  the  historical  perspective  indicates  that  the\nappointment of judges plays a crucial and critical role in the  independence\nof the judiciary in the real sense of the term. If judges can be  influenced\nby political considerations and  other  extraneous  factors,  the  judiciary\ncannot  remain  independent  only  by  securing  the   salary,   allowances,\nconditions of service and pension of such  judges.  The  meat  lies  in  the\ncaliber of the judges and not their perks.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_918\">100.  In  his  concluding  address  to  the  Constituent  Assembly  on  26th\nNovember, 1949 Dr. Rajendra Prasad  referred  to  the  independence  of  the\njudiciary and had this to say:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_566\">\u201cWe have provided  in  the  Constitution  for  a  judiciary  which  will  be\nindependent.  It is difficult to suggest anything more to make  the  Supreme\nCourt and the High Courts independent of the  influence  of  the  executive.\nThere is an attempt  made  in  the  Constitution  to  make  even  the  lower\njudiciary independent of any outside or extraneous influence.   One  of  our\narticles makes it easy for the State Governments to introduce separation  of\nexecutive from judicial functions and placing  the  magistracy  which  deals\nwith criminal cases on similar footing as civil courts.  I can only  express\nthe hope that this long overdue  reform  will  soon  be  introduced  in  the\nStates.\u201d[262]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_919\">101.  Providing for an independent judiciary  is  not  enough  \u2013  access  to\nquality justice achieved through the appointment of  independent  judges  is\nequally important. It has been said of the judges during apartheid in  South\nAfrica:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_567\">\u201cNow during apartheid judges had the formal  guarantees  of  independence  -\nlife tenure, salary, administrative autonomy - that  judges  in  the  United\nStates of America, Canada, the United  Kingdom,  New  Zealand  or  Australia\nhad. It is in seeing why it was the case that apartheid-era judges  for  the\nmost part lacked independence even though  they  had  its  formal  trappings\nthat we see that judicial independence is also  a  kind  of  dependence;  it\ndepends on something positive - the judicial pursuit of the justice  of  the\nlaw. One has to ask not only what judges have to be shielded from  in  order\nto be independent, but what we want them to be independent for.\u201d[263]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_920\">102.  This review indicates that one of the important features of  the  Rule\nof Law and the independence of the judiciary  is  the  appointment  process.\nIt is, therefore, necessary to objectively appreciate the evolution  of  the\nappointment process post Independence and how the Judiciary understood it.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_921\">Judicial pronouncements\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Issue\" id=\"p_922\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Neg\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_324\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Neg\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_325\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Neg\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_326\">103.  The question of the appointment of judges (mainly of the High  Courts)\ncame up for consideration in this Court on  three  occasions.  The  decision\nrendered in each of these cases is not only of considerable  importance  but\nalso indicates the complexity in the appointment of judges and the  struggle\nby the Bar to maintain the independence  of  the  judiciary  from  executive\ninterference and encroachment. These three cases  are  referred  to  as  the\n<a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1613\">First Judges</a> case,[264] the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1614\">Second Judges</a> case[265]  and  the  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1615\">Third  Judges</a>\ncase.[266] There have been other significant pronouncements on  the  subject\nand they will be considered at the appropriate stage.</span></span></span>\n</p>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_327\">1</span>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_923\">2 First judges case \u2013 30.12.1981\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_924\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_328\">104.  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1616\">The First Judges</a> case is important  for  several  reasons,  but  I  am\nconcerned with a few of  them.  These  are:  (1)  The  independence  of  the\njudiciary  was  held  to  be  a  part  of   the   basic   feature   of   the\nConstitution.[267] This was the first judgment to so hold.\n(2) The appointment of a judge is serious business and is  recognized  as  a\nvery vital  component  of  the  independence  of  the  judiciary.  \u2018What  is\nnecessary is to have Judges who are prepared to  fashion  new  tools,  forge\nnew methods, innovate new strategies and evolve  a  new  jurisprudence,  who\nare judicial statesmen with a social vision and a creative faculty  and  who\nhave, above all, a deep sense of commitment  to  the  Constitution  with  an\nactivist approach and obligation for accountability, not  to  any  party  in\npower nor to the opposition nor to the classes which are vociferous  but  to\nthe half-hungry millions of India who are  continually  denied  their  basic\nhuman rights. We need Judges who are alive to the  socio-economic  realities\nof Indian life, who are anxious to wipe every tear from every eye, who  have\nfaith in the constitutional values and who  are  ready  to  use  law  as  an\ninstrument for achieving the constitutional objectives. This has to  be  the\nbroad blueprint of the  appointment  project  for  the  higher  echelons  of\njudicial service. It is only if appointments of Judges are made  with  these\nconsiderations weighing predominantly with the appointing authority that  we\ncan have a truly independent judiciary committed only  to  the  Constitution\nand to the people of India.\u2019[268] Justice  Venkataramiah,  however,  was  of\nthe view that the independence of the judiciary is relatable only  to  post-\nappointment and that \u2018It is difficult to hold that merely because the  power\nof appointment is with the executive,  the  independence  of  the  judiciary\nwould become impaired. The true principle is  that  after  such  appointment\nthe executive should  have  no  scope  to  interfere  with  the  work  of  a\nJudge.\u2019[269]\n(3) In the appointment of a judge of the Supreme Court or  the  High  Court,\nthe word \u2018consultation\u2019 occurring in <a href=\"/doc/1995948/\" id=\"a_1617\">Article 124(2)</a> and  in  <a href=\"/doc/259647/\" id=\"a_1618\">Article  217(1)</a>\nof the Constitution does  not  mean  \u2018concurrence\u2019.[270]  However,  for  the\npurposes of consultation, each constitutional  functionary  must  have  full\nand identical  facts  relating  to  the  appointment  of  a  judge  and  the\nconsultation should be based on this identical material.[271]\n(4) In the event of a disagreement between the constitutional  functionaries\nrequired  to  be  consulted  in  the  appointment  of  a  judge,  the  Union\nGovernment would decide whose opinion should  be  accepted  and  whether  an\nappointment should be made or not. In such an  event,  the  opinion  of  the\nChief Justice  of  India  has  no  primacy.[272]  The  \u2018ultimate  power\u2019  of\nappointment  of  judges  to  the  superior  Courts  rests  with  the   Union\nGovernment.[273]  (This  is  completely  contrary  to  the   view   of   the\nConstituent Assembly and Dr. Ambedkar).</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_925\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Neg\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_329\">(5) The extant system of appointment of judges is not  an  ideal  system  of\nappointment. The idea  of  a  consultative  panel  (called  a  collegium  or\nJudicial Commission) was floated as a replacement. This body was to  consist\nof persons expected to have knowledge  of  persons  who  might  be  fit  for\nappointment on the Bench and possessed of qualities  required  for  such  an\nappointment. Countries like Australia and New Zealand \u2018have veered round  to\nthe view that there should be a Judicial Commission for appointment  of  the\nhigher judiciary.\u2019[274] Incidentally, we were informed during the course  of\nhearing that even about 35 years after the  decision  in  the  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1619\">First  Judges</a>\ncase  neither  Australia  nor  New  Zealand  have  established  a   Judicial\nCommission as yet.</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_926\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"PARTY\" data-docid=\"1113583\" id=\"span_330\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"PARTY\" data-docid=\"4572688\" id=\"span_331\">105.  On the meaning of \u2018consultation\u2019 for the purposes  of  <a href=\"/doc/1995948/\" id=\"a_1620\">Article  124(2)</a>\nand <a href=\"/doc/259647/\" id=\"a_1621\">Article 217(1)</a> of the Constitution, Justice Bhagwati who spoke  for  the\nmajority relied upon <a href=\"/doc/4572688/\" id=\"a_1622\">Union of India v. Sankalchand Himmatlal Sheth</a>[275]  and\n<a href=\"/doc/1113583/\" id=\"a_1623\">R. Pushpam v. State of Madras</a>[276] to hold that:</span></span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_568\">\u201cEach of the constitutional functionaries required  to  be  consulted  under\nthese two articles must have for his consideration full and identical  facts\nbearing upon appointment or non-appointment of the  person  concerned  as  a\nJudge and the opinion of each of them taken on identical  material  must  be\nconsidered by the Central Government before it takes a decision  whether  or\nnot to appoint the person concerned as a Judge.\u201d[277]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_927\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_332\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_333\">106.  The majority view in the  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1624\">First  Judges</a>  case  was  overruled  in  the\n<a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1625\">Second Judges</a> case and it was held that \u2018consultation\u2019 in  <a href=\"/doc/1682952/\" id=\"a_1626\">Article  217</a>  and\n<a href=\"/doc/1164880/\" id=\"a_1627\">Article 124</a> of the Constitution meant that \u2018primacy\u2019 in the  appointment  of\njudges must rest with the Chief Justice of India.</span></span><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_334\">[278] The evolution of  the\ncollegium system and a  Judicial  Commission  will  be  discussed  a  little\nlater, although it must be noted that the seeds  thereof  were  sown  (apart\nfrom the Reports of the LCI) in the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1628\">First Judges</a> case.</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_928\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_335\">107.  I do not think it necessary to further discuss the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1629\">First  Judges</a>  case\nsince it has been elaborately considered by Justice Khehar.</span>\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_929\">3 Subhash Sharma\u2019s case\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_930\">108.  <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_336\">In a writ petition filed in this Court  praying  for  filling  up  the\nvacancies of judges in the Supreme Court and  several  High  Courts  of  the\ncountry, a three judge Bench was of the view  that  the  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1630\">First  Judges</a>  case\nrequired reconsideration.[279] It was observed  that  the  decision  of  the\nmajority not only rejects the primacy of the  Chief  Justice  of  India  but\nalso whittles down the significance of \u2018consultation\u2019.</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_931\">109.  It was noted that the  Constitution  (Sixty-seventh  Amendment)  Bill,\n1990 was pending consideration in  Parliament  and  that  the  Statement  of\nObjects and Reasons for  the  Amendment  Act  acknowledged  that  there  was\ncriticism of the  existing  system  of  appointment  of  judges  (where  the\nexecutive had the primacy) and that this needed change, hence the  need  for\nan Amendment Act.[280]\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_932\">110.  On the issue of executive interference in the appointment  of  judges,\nthe Bench found that interference went to the extent  of  impermissibly  re-\nopening the  appointment  process  even  though  a  recommendation  for  the\nappointment of a judge had been accepted by the Chief Justice of  India.  It\nwas observed:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_569\">\u201cFrom the affidavits filed by the Union of India and the statements made  by\nlearned Attorney General on the different  occasions  when  the  matter  was\nheard we found that the Union Government  had  [pic]adopted  the  policy  of\nreopening recommendations even though the  same  had  been  cleared  by  the\nChief Justice of India on the basis that there had in the  meantime  been  a\nchange in the personnel of the Chief Justice of the High Court or the  Chief\nMinister of the State. The selection of a person  as  a  Judge  has  nothing\npersonal either to the  Chief  Justice  of  the  High  Court  or  the  Chief\nMinister of the  State.  The  High  Court  is  an  institution  of  national\nimportance  wherein  the  person  appointed  as  a  Judge  functions  in  an\nimpersonal manner. The process  of  selection  is  intended  to  be  totally\nhonest and upright with a view to finding out the most suitable  person  for\nthe vacancy. If in a given case the Chief Justice  of  the  High  Court  has\nrecommended and the name has been considered by the Chief Minister and  duly\nprocessed through the Governor so  as  to  reach  the  hands  of  the  Chief\nJustice of India through the Ministry of Justice and the  Chief  Justice  of\nIndia as the highest judicial authority in the country, on  due  application\nof his mind, has given finality to the process at his  level,  there  cannot\nordinarily be any justification for  reopening  the  matter  merely  because\nthere has been a change in the personnel of the Chief Justice or  the  Chief\nMinister of the State concerned.\u201d[281]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_933\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_337\">111.   Apart  from  the  above,  the  Bench  was  of  the  view   that   the\ninterpretation  given  by  the  majority  in  the  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1631\">First  Judges</a>   case   to\n\u2018consultation\u2019 was not correctly appreciated in the  constitutional  scheme.</span>\n<span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_338\">It was also felt that the role of the institution of the  Chief  Justice  of\nIndia in the constitutional scheme had been  denuded  in  the  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1632\">First  Judges</a>\ncase. Keeping all these factors in mind,  particularly  the  functioning  of\nthe appointment process and the  acknowledgement  of  the  Union  Government\nthat a change was needed, it was observed:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_570\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"112850760\" id=\"span_339\">\u201cThe  view  taken  by  Bhagwati,  J.,  Fazal  Ali,  J.,   Desai,   J.,   and\nVenkataramiah, J., to which we will presently advert, in  our  opinion,  not\nonly seriously detracts from  and  denudes  the  primacy  of  the  position,\nimplicit in the constitutional scheme, of the Chief Justice of India in  the\nconsultative process  but  also  whittles  down  the  very  significance  of\n\u201cconsultation\u201d as required to be understood  in  the  constitutional  scheme\nand context. This bears both  on  the  substance  and  the  process  of  the\nconstitutional scheme\u2026.. Consistent  with  the  constitutional  purpose  and\nprocess it becomes imperative that the role of the institution of the  Chief\nJustice of India be recognised as of crucial importance  in  the  matter  of\nappointments to the Supreme Court and the High Courts of the States. We  are\nof  the  view  that  this  aspect  dealt  with  in   <a href=\"/doc/112850760/\" id=\"a_1633\">Gupta</a>   case   requires\nreconsideration by a larger bench.\u201d[282]</span>\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_934\">112.  The issues for consideration of a larger Bench  were  then  formulated\nin the following words:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_571\">\u201cThe points which require to be reconsidered relate to and  arise  from  the\nviews of the majority opinion touching the  very  status  of  \u201cconsultation\u201d\ngenerally and in particular with  reference  to  \u201cconsultation\u201d  with  Chief\nJustice of India and, secondly, as to the primacy of the role of  the  Chief\nJustice of India. The content and quality of consultation may  perhaps  vary\nin different situations in the interaction between  the  executive  and  the\njudicial   organs   of   the   State   and   some   aspects   may    require\nclarification.\u201d[283]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_935\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_340\">113.  It was also observed that a view was expressed  in  the  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1634\">First  Judges</a>\ncase that the government of the State could  initiate  a  proposal  for  the\nappointment of a judge but that the proposal could not be sent  directly  to\nthe Union Government, but should first be sent to the Chief Justice  of  the\nHigh Court.[284]  Notwithstanding this clear exposition, the  procedure  was\nbeing distorted by the executive and  a  proposal  for  the  appointment  of\njudge of the High Court was being sent directly to the Union Government.  It\nwas said in this regard:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_572\">\u201cBut it has been mentioned that a practice is sought to be  developed  where\nthe executive government of the State sends up  the  proposals  directly  to\nthe Centre without reference to the Chief Justice of the State.  This  is  a\ndistortion of the constitutional scheme and is wholly impermissible. So  far\nas the executive is  concerned,  the  \u2018right\u2019  to  initiate  an  appointment\nshould be limited to suggesting appropriate names to the  Chief  Justice  of\nthe High Courts or the Chief Justice of India. If the recommendation  is  to\nemanate directly from a source other than that of the Chief Justices of  the\nHigh Courts in the case of the High Courts and the Chief  Justice  of  India\nin the case of both the High Courts  and  the  Supreme  Court  it  would  be\ndifficult for an appropriate selection to be made. It has been  increasingly\nfelt over the decades that there has been an anxiety  on  the  part  of  the\ngovernment of the day to assert its choice  in  the  ultimate  selection  of\nJudges. If the power to recommend would vest  in  the  State  Government  or\neven the Central Government, the picture is likely to  be  blurred  and  the\nprocess of selection ultimately may turn out to be difficult.\u201d[285]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_936\">114.  By-passing the Chief Justice of  the  High  Court  in  the  matter  of\nrecommending a person for appointment as a judge of the High  Court  was  an\nunhealthy practice that the political executive of the State was  trying  to\nestablish since around the  time  of  Independence.  This  \u2018subterfuge\u2019  was\ndeprecated on more than one occasion, as noticed above.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_937\">115.  Another practice that the political executive was trying to  establish\nwas to recommend persons for appointment as a judge of  the  High  Court  to\nthe Chief Justice of that High Court. In this context, it  was  also  stated\nin Subhash Sharma (as quoted above) that: \u2018It  has  been  increasingly  felt\nover the decades that  there  has  been  an  anxiety  on  the  part  of  the\ngovernment of the day to assert its choice  in  the  ultimate  selection  of\nJudges.\u2019[286] This unequivocally indicates that  the  malaise  of  executive\ninterference  in  appointing  judges  to  the  superior   judiciary,   first\nhighlighted in the Memorandum emanating from the Chief  Justices  Conference\nand then by the LCI in its 14th Report, continued in some form or the  other\nthrough the entire  period  from  Independence  till  the  early  1990s.  In\naddition, the recommendation given in the 14th Report of the LCI in  Chapter\n6 regarding the executive not being entitled to \u2018propose a  nominee  of  its\nown and forward it  to  the  Centre\u2019  was  not  given  the  due  weight  and\nconsideration that it deserved from the executive.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_938\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_341\">116.  Quite clearly, some complex issues arose in the matter of  appointment\nof judges primarily due to the interference of the political  executive  and\nthese needed consideration by a larger Bench. Well established and  accepted\nconstitutional conventions were sought to be disregarded  by  the  political\nexecutive. If the independence of the judiciary was  to  be  maintained  and\nparliamentary democracy was to be retained, the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1635\">First Judges</a>  case  and  the\nappointment process needed a fresh look.</span>\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_939\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_342\">4 <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1636\">Second Judges</a> case \u2013 6.10.1993</span>\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_940\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_343\">117.  As mentioned above, the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1637\">Second  Judges</a>  case  was  the  result  of  an\nacknowledgement that: (1) The existing system of appointment  of  judges  in\nwhich the executive had the \u2018ultimate power\u2019  needed  reconsideration  since\nthat  \u2018ultimate  power\u2019  was  being  abused;  (2)  The  existing  system  of\nappointment of judges resulted in  some  appointments  in  which  merit  was\noverlooked due to executive interference or for  extraneous  considerations.\nThe Chief Justice of the  High  Court  was  occasionally  by-passed  by  the\npolitical executive and a recommendation for the appointment of a person  as\na judge of the High Court was made directly to the  Union  Government.  This\nunfortunate situation had continued for more than 40 years  and  an  attempt\nto bring about a change was made and so a Constitution  Amendment  Bill  was\nintroduced in Parliament, but it lapsed.</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_941\">118.  <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_344\">In the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1638\">Second Judges</a> case it was held  by  Justice  Pandian:  (1)  The\nselection and appointment of a proper and  fit  candidate  to  the  superior\njudiciary is one of the inseparable and vital conditions  for  securing  the\nindependence  of  the  judiciary.[287]  \u2018The  erroneous  appointment  of  an\nunsuitable person is bound to produce irreparable damage  to  the  faith  of\nthe community in the  administration  of  justice  and  to  inflict  serious\ninjury to the  public  interest...\u2019[288]   (2)  Yet  another  facet  of  the\nindependence of the judiciary is the separation between  the  executive  and\nthe judiciary (including the superior judiciary)[289] postulated by  <a href=\"/doc/192717/\" id=\"a_1639\">Article\n50</a> of the  Constitution.</span><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_345\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_346\">[290]  (3)  The  Memorandum  of  Procedure  for  the\nselection and appointment of judges filed by the Union of  India along  with\nthe written submissions relating to the pre <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1640\">First  Judges</a>  case  period  and\nthe extant procedure as mentioned in the 121st Report of  the  LCI  relating\nto the post <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1641\">First Judges</a> case  period  are  more  or  less  the  same.  They\nindicate that the recommendation for filling up a  vacancy  in  the  Supreme\nCourt is initiated by the Chief Justice of India and the recommendation  for\nfilling up a vacancy in the High Court is initiated by the Chief Justice  of\nthe High Court. The Chief Minister of a State may  recommend  a  person  for\nfilling up a vacancy in the High Court,  but  that  is  to  be  routed  only\nthrough the Chief Justice of the High Court.</span></span><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"4572688\" id=\"span_347\">[291] (4) Reiterating  the  view\nexpressed in <a href=\"/doc/4572688/\" id=\"a_1642\">Sankalchand Sheth and the First Judges</a> case it  was  held  that\nfor the purposes of consultation, the materials  before  the  President  and\nthe Chief Justice of India must be identical.[292] (5) For  the  appointment\nof a judge of the Supreme Court (under <a href=\"/doc/1995948/\" id=\"a_1643\">Article 124(2)</a> of  the  Constitution)\nor a judge of a High  Court  (under  <a href=\"/doc/259647/\" id=\"a_1644\">Article  217(1)</a>  of  the  Constitution)\nconsultation with the Chief Justice of India is mandatory.[293] (6)  In  the\nprocess of constitutional consultation in selecting judges  to  the  Supreme\nCourt or the High Court and transfer  of  judges  of  the  High  Court,  the\nopinion of the Chief Justice of  India  is  entitled  to  primacy.[294]  (7)\nAgreeing with the majority opinion written by Justice  J.S.  Verma,  it  was\nheld that if there are weighty and cogent  reasons  for  not  accepting  the\nrecommendation of the Chief Justice  of  India  for  the  appointment  of  a\njudge, then the appointment may not be made. However, if  the  \u2018weighty  and\ncogent\u2019 reasons are not acceptable to the Chief Justice of  India,  and  the\nrecommendation is reiterated, then the appointment shall be  made.</span><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_348\">[295]  (8)\nThe majority opinion in the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1645\">First Judges</a> case regarding the primacy  of  the\nexecutive in the matter of appointment of judges was overruled.[296]</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_942\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_349\">119.  Justice  Ahmadi  dissented  with  the  opinion  of  the  majority  and\nconcluded: (1) Judicial independence  is  ingrained  in  our  constitutional\nscheme and <a href=\"/doc/192717/\" id=\"a_1646\">Article 50</a> of the Constitution  \u2018illuminates  it\u2019.[297]  (<a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1647\">2)  The\nFirst Judges</a> case was not required to be overruled but on  the  question  of\nprimacy in the matter of appointment of judges, the  opinion  of  the  Chief\nJustice of India is entitled to \u2018graded weight\u2019.[298]</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_943\">120.  Justice Kuldip Singh agreed with the majority and  laid  great  stress\non constitutional conventions that had evolved  over  several  decades.  The\nlearned judge held: (1) Security  of  tenure  is  not  the  only  source  of\nindependence of the judiciary but \u2018there has to be an independent  judiciary\nas an institution.\u2019[299] (2) Independence of the judiciary  is  inextricably\nlinked and connected with  the  constitutional  process  of  appointment  of\njudges of the higher judiciary. There cannot  be  an  independent  judiciary\nwhen the power of appointment of judges vests  in  the  executive.[300]  (3)\nThe  President  is  bound  by  the  advice   given   by   the   Council   of\nMinisters.[301] (4) A constitutional convention  is  established  since  the\n<a href=\"/doc/93056159/\" id=\"a_1648\">Government of India Act, 1935</a> (I would add the words \u2018at  least\u2019)  that  the\nappointment of judges was invariably made with the concurrence of the  Chief\nJustice of India. <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_350\">The opinion and recommendation of  the  Chief  Justice  of\nIndia in the matter of appointment of judges binds the  executive.[302]  (5)\nIn the matter of appointment of judges, consultation with the Chief  Justice\nof India is mandatory.[303] (6) In the consultation  process  under  <a href=\"/doc/1995948/\" id=\"a_1649\">Article\n124(2)</a> and <a href=\"/doc/259647/\" id=\"a_1650\">217(1)</a> of the Constitution, the advice and recommendation of  the\nChief Justice of India is binding on the executive and  must  be  the  final\nword. The majority view in the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1651\">First Judges</a>  case  does  not  lay  down  the\ncorrect law.[304]\n(7) For the purposes of <a href=\"/doc/1995948/\" id=\"a_1652\">Article 124(2)</a> and <a href=\"/doc/259647/\" id=\"a_1653\">217(1)</a> of the  Constitution,  the\nChief Justice of India and the Chief Justice of  the  High  Court  mean  the\nfunctionaries representing their respective Court.[305]</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_944\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_351\">121.  One of the more interesting facts pointed out by Justice Kuldip  Singh\nis that from 1st January, 1983 (after  the  decision  in  the  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1654\">First  Judges</a>\ncase) till 10th April, 1993 (that is during  a  period  of  ten  years)  the\nopinion of the Chief Justice of India was not accepted by the  President  in\nas many as seven cases. This is worth contrasting with a part of the  period\nbefore the \u2018ultimate power\u2019 theory was propounded when the  opinion  of  the\nChief Justice of India was not accepted by the President only  in  one  case\nand <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1655\">in that case</a>, the opinion of the Chief Justice of the  High  Court  (not\nthe political executive) was accepted. This is what the  learned  judge  had\nto say:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_573\">\u201cMr S.K. Bose, Joint Secretary, Department of Justice, Ministry of  Law  and\nJustice has filed an affidavit dated April 22, 1993 before us. In para 6  of\nthe said affidavit it is stated as under:\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_574\">\u201cAs regards the appointments of Judges made,  not  in  consonance  with  the\nviews expressed by the Chief Justice of India, it is respectfully  submitted\nthat since January 1, 1983 to April 10, 1993, there  have  been  only  seven\nsuch cases, five of these were in 1983 (2 in January 1983, 2 in  July  1983,\n1 in August 1983); one in September 1985 and one in March  1991,  out  of  a\ntotal of 547 appointments made during this period.\u201d\nIt is thus obvious from the facts and figures given by the executive  itself\nthat in actual practice the recommendations of the Chief  Justice  of  India\nhave invariably been accepted.\u201d[306]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_945\">122.  Justice Verma speaking for the majority held: (1) Independence of  the\njudiciary has to be safeguarded not only by  providing  security  of  tenure\nand  other  conditions  of  service,  but  also  by   preventing   political\nconsiderations  in  making  appointments   of   judges   to   the   superior\njudiciary.[307]\n(2) In the matter of  appointment  of  judges,  primacy  was  given  to  the\nexecutive in the Government of India Act, 1919 and the <a href=\"/doc/93056159/\" id=\"a_1656\">Government  of  India\nAct, 1935</a> but in the constitutional scheme,  primacy  of  the  executive  is\nexcluded.[308]\n(3) The Chief Justice of India and the Chief Justice of the High  Court  are\n\u2018best equipped to know  and  assess  the  worth  of  a  candidate,  and  his\nsuitability for appointment  as  a  superior  judge.\u2019  In  the  event  of  a\ndifference of opinion between the executive and the judiciary,  the  opinion\nof the Chief Justice of India should have the greatest weight. [This  echoed\nDr. Ambedkar\u2019s view that consultation would be between persons who are  well\nqualified to give advice in matters of this sort.] Therefore, since  primacy\nis not with the executive, then in such a situation, it must  lie  with  the\nChief Justice of India.[309] This certainly does not exclude  the  executive\nfrom the appointment process. The executive might be aware (unlike  a  Chief\nJustice) of some antecedents or some information relatable to  the  personal\ncharacter or trait of a lawyer or a judge which might have a bearing on  the\npotential of a person becoming a good judge.[310] This might form the  basis\nfor rejecting a recommendation for the appointment of a person  as  a  judge\nby the Chief Justice of India.[311]\n(4) Primacy of the opinion of the Chief Justice of India is not  to  his/her\nindividual opinion but to the collective opinion of  the  Chief  Justice  of\nIndia and his/her senior colleagues or those who  are  associated  with  the\nfunction of appointment of judges.[312] Therefore,  the  President  may  not\naccept the recommendation of a person for appointment as  a  judge,  if  the\nrecommendation of the Chief  Justice  of  India  is  not  supported  by  the\nunanimous opinion of the other senior judges.[313] The President may  return\nfor reconsideration a unanimous recommendation for  good  reasons.  However,\nin the latter event, if the Chief Justice of  India  and  the  other  judges\nconsulted  by  him/her,  unanimously  reiterate  the  recommendation   \u2018with\nreasons for not withdrawing the recommendation, then that appointment  as  a\nmatter of healthy convention ought to be made.\u2019[314] (The key word  here  is\nunanimous \u2013 both at the stage of  the  initial  recommendation  and  at  the\nstage of reiteration).\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_946\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_352\">(5) For appointing  a  judge  of  the  Supreme  Court  or  the  High  Court,\nconsultation with the Chief Justice of India or the  Chief  Justice  of  the\nHigh Court is mandatory.[315]\n(6) The President in <a href=\"/doc/1995948/\" id=\"a_1657\">Articles 124(2)</a> and <a href=\"/doc/259647/\" id=\"a_1658\">217(1)</a> of  the  Constitution  means\nthe President acting in  accordance  with  the  advice  of  the  Council  of\nMinisters with the Prime Minister at the head.[316]\n(7) The advice given by the Council of Ministers to the President should  be\nin  accord  with  the  Constitution.  Such  an  advice  is  binding  on  the\nPresident. Since the opinion of the Chief  Justice  of  India  (representing\nthe Judiciary) has finality, the advice of the Council of Ministers  to  the\nPresident must be in accordance with the opinion of  the  Chief  Justice  of\nIndia.[317]\n(8) The convention is that the  appointment  process  is  initiated  by  the\nChief Justice of India for the appointment of a judge to the  Supreme  Court\nand by the Chief Justice of the High Court for the appointment  of  a  judge\nto the High Court. There is no reason to depart from this convention.[318]\n(9) The law  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1659\">laid  down  in</a>  the  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1660\">First  Judges</a>  case  is  not  the  correct\nview.[319]</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_947\">123.  In his otherwise dissenting opinion,  Justice  Punchhi  supported  the\nview taken by Justice Verma to the  extent  that  the  executive  could  not\ndisapprove the views of the Chief Justice of  India  or  the  views  of  the\nChief Justice of the High Court (as the case may be) when  a  recommendation\nis made for the appointment of a judge to a superior court.[320]\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_948\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_353\">124.  The most significant feature of the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1661\">Second  Judges</a>  case  is  that  it\nintroduced what has come to be called a \u2018collegium system\u2019  of  consultation\nfor the appointment of judges of the Supreme Court and the High  Courts.  As\nfar as the Chief Justice of India is concerned, the collegium system  broad-\nbased his/her role in the appointment of judges of the High Courts  and  the\nSupreme Court and (in one sense) diluted his/her  role  in  the  appointment\nprocess by taking it out of the individualized or personalized role  of  the\nChief Justice of India as thought of by Dr. Ambedkar. The consultative  role\nof the Chief Justice of  India  in  <a href=\"/doc/1164880/\" id=\"a_1662\">Article  124</a>  of  the  Constitution  was\nradically transformed through a pragmatic interpretation of that  provision.\nHow did this happen?</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_949\">125.  <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_354\">In the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1663\">Second Judges</a> case certain norms  were  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1664\">laid  down  by</a>  Justice\nVerma in the matter of appointment of judges.  These  norms  were:  For  the\nappointment of judges in the Supreme Court, the Chief Justice of India  must\nascertain the views of the two senior-most judges of the Supreme  Court  and\nof the senior-most judge in the Supreme Court from the  High  Court  of  the\ncandidate concerned. Through this process, the  individual  opinion  of  the\nChief Justice of India was substituted by the collective opinion of  several\njudges. In this sense the opinion of the  Chief  Justice  of  India  in  the\nconsultative process was made broad-based and ceased to  be  individualized.\nAt this stage it is worth recalling the words  of  Dr.  Ambedkar  that  \u2018the\nChief Justice, despite his eminence, had all the  failings,  sentiments  and\nprejudices of common people.\u2019 The apprehension or  fear  that  Dr.  Ambedkar\nhad in this regard in case the Chief Justice of India  were  to  act  in  an\nindividual or personal capacity was  now  buried.[321]  A  somewhat  similar\nnorm was laid down for consultation for the appointment of a  judge  of  the\nHigh Court. This is what was said:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_575\">\u201cThis opinion has to be formed in  a  pragmatic  manner  and  past  practice\nbased on convention is a safe guide. In matters relating to appointments  in\nthe Supreme Court, the opinion given by the Chief Justice of  India  in  the\nconsultative process has to be formed taking into account the views  of  the\ntwo seniormost Judges of the Supreme Court. The Chief Justice  of  India  is\nalso expected to ascertain  the  views  of  the  senior-most  Judge  of  the\nSupreme Court whose opinion is likely to be  significant  in  adjudging  the\nsuitability of the candidate, by reason of the fact that he  has  come  from\nthe same High Court, or otherwise. <a href=\"/doc/1995948/\" id=\"a_1665\">Article  124(2)</a>  is  an  indication  that\nascertainment of the views of some other Judges  of  the  Supreme  Court  is\nrequisite. The object underlying <a href=\"/doc/1995948/\" id=\"a_1666\">Article 124(2)</a> is achieved in  this  manner\nas the Chief Justice of  India  consults  them  for  the  formation  of  his\nopinion. This provision in <a href=\"/doc/1995948/\" id=\"a_1667\">Article 124(2)</a> is  the  basis  for  the  existing\nconvention which requires the Chief Justice of India to consult some  Judges\nof the Supreme Court before making his  recommendation.  This  ensures  that\nthe opinion of the Chief Justice of  India  is  not  merely  his  individual\nopinion, but an opinion formed collectively by a body of  men  at  the  apex\nlevel in the judiciary.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_576\">In matters relating to appointments in the High Courts,  the  Chief  Justice\nof India is expected to take into account the views  of  his  colleagues  in\nthe Supreme Court who are likely to be conversant with the  affairs  of  the\nconcerned High Court. The Chief Justice of  India  may  also  ascertain  the\nviews of one or more  senior  Judges  of  that  High  Court  whose  opinion,\naccording to the Chief Justice of India, is likely to be significant in  the\nformation of his opinion. The opinion of  the  Chief  Justice  of  the  High\nCourt would be entitled to the greatest  weight,  and  the  opinion  of  the\nother functionaries involved must be given due weight, in the  formation  of\nthe opinion of the Chief Justice of India. The opinion of the Chief  Justice\nof the High Court must be formed after ascertaining the views  of  at  least\nthe two seniormost Judges of the High Court.\u201d[322]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_950\">126.  The importance  of  the  role  of  the  Chief  Justice  of  India  was\nacknowledged in that it was observed that the constitutional convention  was\nthat no appointment should be made by the  President  under  <a href=\"/doc/1995948/\" id=\"a_1668\">Article  124(2)</a>\nand <a href=\"/doc/259647/\" id=\"a_1669\">Article 217(1)</a> of the Constitution unless it was in conformity with  the\nfinal opinion of the Chief Justice of India. It was said:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_577\">\u201cThe opinion of the Chief Justice of India,  for  the  purpose  of  <a href=\"/doc/1995948/\" id=\"a_1670\">Articles\n124(2)</a> and <a href=\"/doc/259647/\" id=\"a_1671\">217(1)</a>, so given, has primacy in the matter of all  appointments;\nand no appointment can be made by the President under  these  provisions  to\nthe Supreme Court and the High Courts, unless it is in conformity  with  the\nfinal  opinion  of  the  Chief  Justice  of  India,  formed  in  the  manner\nindicated.\u201d[323]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_951\">127.  The \u2018manner indicated\u2019 was that if a  recommendation  is  returned  by\nthe executive (for cogent reasons) to the Chief Justice  of  India  and  the\nChief Justice of India reiterates  the  recommendation  with  the  unanimous\nagreement of the judges earlier consulted, then the  appointment  should  be\nmade \u2018as a matter of healthy convention\u2019. This is  what  was  said  in  this\ncontext:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_578\">\u201cNon-appointment of anyone recommended,  on  the  ground  of  unsuitability,\nmust be for good reasons, disclosed to the Chief Justice of India to  enable\nhim to reconsider and withdraw his recommendation on  those  considerations.\nIf the Chief Justice of India does not find it  necessary  to  withdraw  his\nrecommendation even thereafter, but the other Judges of  the  Supreme  Court\nwho have been consulted in the matter are of the view that it  ought  to  be\nwithdrawn, the non-appointment of that person, for reasons to  be  recorded,\nmay be permissible in the public interest. If the non-appointment in a  rare\ncase, on this ground, turns out  to  be  a  mistake,  that  mistake  in  the\nultimate public interest is less harmful than a wrong appointment.  However,\nif after due consideration of the reasons disclosed to the Chief Justice  of\nIndia, that recommendation is reiterated by the Chief Justice of India  with\nthe unanimous agreement of the Judges of the Supreme Court consulted in  the\nmatter, with reasons for  not  withdrawing  the  recommendation,  then  that\nappointment as a matter of healthy convention ought to be made.\u201d[324]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_952\">128.  The norms took the form of conclusions  that  became  binding  on  the\nJudiciary  and  the  Executive.  It  is  not  necessary  to  reproduce   the\nconclusions arrived at.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_953\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_355\">129.  An important aspect of the appointment process, which was adverted  to\nby Justice Verma, is the constitutional convention that  the  recommendation\nmust be initiated by and must originate from the Chief Justice of  the  High\nCourt (for appointment to the High Court) and  from  the  Chief  Justice  of\nIndia (for appointment to the  Supreme  Court).   In  the  event  the  Chief\nMinister of a State recommends a person for appointment as a  judge  of  the\nHigh Court, it must be routed only through the Chief  Justice  of  the  High\nCourt. It is then for the said Chief Justice to consult his colleagues  (and\nothers, if necessary) and  decide  whether  or  not  the  person  should  be\nformally recommended. If the Chief Justice  of  the  High  Court  recommends\nthat person, the procedure as mentioned in  the  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1672\">Second  Judges</a>  case  would\nthereafter follow. If the Chief Justice of the High  Court  decides  not  to\nrecommend that  person  for  appointment,  the  matter  stands  closed  and,\ntherefore, the question of making an appointment without the consent of  the\nChief Justice of India  simply  does  not  and  cannot  arise.  It  is  this\nconstitutionally and conventionally accepted procedure, which is  apparently\nnot acceptable to the political executive, that has  led  to  the  political\nexecutive by-passing  the  Chief  Justice  of  a  High  Court  and  directly\nrecommending to the Union Government a person for appointment as a judge  of\nthe High Court.</span> <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_356\">Be that as it  may,  the  majority  view  expressed  in  the\n<a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1673\">Second Judges</a> case restored the constitutional  position  envisaged  by  Dr.\nAmbedkar by diluting the individual authority of the Chief Justice of  India\nand conferring it on a collegium of judges, which is perhaps  in  consonance\nwith the views of Dr. Ambedkar.</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_954\">130.  According to the learned Attorney-General,  these  conclusions  turned\n<a href=\"/doc/1995948/\" id=\"a_1674\">Article 124(2)</a> and <a href=\"/doc/259647/\" id=\"a_1675\">Article 217(1)</a> of the Constitution \u2018on  their  head\u2019  and\neven Justice Verma, the author  of  the  judgment  felt  that  the  decision\nrequired a rethink. The reference was  to  an  interview  given  by  Justice\nVerma post his retirement. In that, it was said by Justice Verma:\u00a0\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_579\">\u201cMy 1993 judgment which holds the field, was  very  much  misunderstood  and\nmisused. It was in that context that I said the working of the judgment  now\nfor  some  time  is  raising  serious  questions,  which  cannot  be  called\nunreasonable. Therefore some kind of rethink is required.\u201d[325]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_955\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_357\">131.  It appears that the misunderstanding of the  decision  in  the  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1676\">Second\nJudges</a> case continues even today, especially  by  the  political  executive.\nThe misunderstanding is not due to any  lack  of  clarity  in  the  decision\nrendered by this Court but due to the discomfort  in  the  \u2018working  of  the\njudgment\u2019.</span> <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_358\">I say this because it was  submitted  by  the  learned  Attorney-\nGeneral and learned counsel for some States  that  the  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1677\">Second  Judges</a>  case\nleft the executive with no role (or  no  effective  role)  to  play  in  the\nappointment of a judge of the Supreme Court or the High  Court  particularly\nsince the opinion of the executive is now rendered meaningless. Nothing  can\nbe further from the truth. The executive continues to have a vital  role  to\nplay and in some cases, the final say in the appointment of a  judge  \u2013  the\nmisunderstanding of the judgment is due to the  completely  and  regrettably\ndefeatist attitude of the Union of India and the States or their  view  that\nin the matter of appointment of judges, it is their way or the highway.  The\nConstitution of India is a sacred document and not a Rubik\u2019s cube  that  can\nbe manipulated and maneuvered by the political executive any which way  only\nto suit its immediate needs.</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_956\">132.  <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_359\">In an article found on the website of the Tamil  Nadu  State  Judicial\nAcademy, Justice Verma adverted to the appointment  process  in  the  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1678\">Second\nJudges</a> case and the role of the executive and said:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_580\">\u201cThe clear language of the decision leaves no room for any  doubt  that  the\nexecutive has a participatory role in these  appointments;  the  opinion  of\nthe  executive  is  weightier  in  the  area  of  antecedents  and  personal\ncharacter and conduct of the candidate;  the  power  of  non-appointment  on\nthis  ground  is  expressly  with   the   executive,   notwithstanding   the\nrecommendation of the CJI; and that  doubtful  antecedents  etc.  are  alone\nsufficient for non-appointment by the executive.  The  decision  also  holds\nthat the opinion of the judicial collegium, if not unanimous does  not  bind\nthe executive to make the appointment.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_581\">Some reported instances in the recent  past  of  the  executive  failing  to\nperform its duty by exercise of this power even when the  recommendation  of\nthe judicial collegium was not unanimous and the  then  President  of  India\nhad returned it for reconsideration, are not only inexplicable  but  also  a\nmisapplication of the decision, which the CJI, Balakrishnan rightly says  is\nbinding during its validity. Such instances only prove the prophecy  of  Dr.\nRajendra Prasad that the Constitution will be as  good  as  the  people  who\nwork it. Have any system you like, its worth and  efficacy  will  depend  on\nthe worth of the people who work it! It is, therefore, the  working  of  the\nsystem   that   must   be   monitored    to    ensure    transparency    and\naccountability.\u201d[326]\n\nA little later in the article Justice Verma says (and this is also  adverted\nto in the interview referred to by the learned Attorney-General):\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_582\">\u201cThe recent aberrations are in the application of the  Second  Judge\u2019s  case\nin making the appointments, and not because  of  it.  This  is  what  I  had\npointed out in my letter of 5 December 2005 to CJI, Y.K.Sabharwal with  copy\nto  the  two  senior  most   judges,   who   included   the   present   CJI,\nK.G.Balakrishnan.\u201d\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_957\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_360\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_361\">133.  The misunderstanding is, therefore, of the political executive and  no\none else. However, as pointed  out  by  the  learned  Attorney-General,  the\nmerits or demerits of the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1679\">Second Judges</a> case is not in issue after the  99th\nConstitution Amendment  Act  and  therefore  no  further  comment  is  made,\nalthough it must be said, quite categorically, that the political  executive\nhas completely misunderstood the scope and impact of the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1680\">Second Judges</a>  case\nand the working of the collegium system.</span></span>\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_958\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_362\">5 <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1681\">Third Judges</a> case \u2013 28.10.1998</span>\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_959\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_363\">134.  Special Reference No. 1 of 1998 is commonly referred to as  the  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1682\">Third\nJudges</a> case. The President sought the advisory opinion of this  Court  under\n<a href=\"/doc/210155/\" id=\"a_1683\">Article 143</a> of the Constitution on the following, amongst other, questions:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_583\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_364\">\u201c(1) whether the expression \u2018consultation with the Chief Justice  of  India\u2019\nin <a href=\"/doc/259647/\" id=\"a_1684\">Articles 217(1)</a> and <a href=\"/doc/182777/\" id=\"a_1685\">222(1)</a> requires  consultation  with  a  plurality  of\nJudges in the formation of the opinion of the  Chief  Justice  of  India  or\ndoes the sole individual opinion of the Chief Justice  of  India  constitute\nconsultation within the meaning of the said articles.\n(3) whether <a href=\"/doc/1995948/\" id=\"a_1686\">Article 124(2)</a> as  interpreted  in  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1687\">the  said  judgment</a>  [<a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1688\">Second\nJudges</a> case] requires the Chief Justice of India to  consult  only  the  two\nseniormost Judges or whether there should be  wider  consultation  according\nto past practice.</span>\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_584\">(4) whether the Chief Justice of India is entitled  to  act  solely  in  his\nindividual capacity, without consultation with other Judges of  the  Supreme\nCourt  in  respect  of  all  materials  and  information  conveyed  by   the\nGovernment  of  India  for  non-appointment  of  a  Judge  recommended   for\nappointment;\u201d\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_960\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_365\">135.  At the outset, it must be  noted  that  the  learned  Attorney-General\nstated at the  hearing  of  the  Presidential  Reference  that  the  Central\nGovernment was neither seeking a review nor a reconsideration of the  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1689\">Second\nJudges</a> case.</span> <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_366\">Therefore, the answers to the  Presidential  Reference  do  not\ndepart from the conclusions arrived at by this Court in  the  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1690\">Second  Judges</a>\ncase. In that sense, this opinion did not take  the  substantive  discussion\nmuch further though it substantially resolved  some  procedural  issues  and\nfilled in the gaps relating to the process of appointment of judges  to  the\nsuperior judiciary. In  any  event,  the  answers  to  the  three  questions\nmentioned above are:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_585\">\u201c1. The expression  \u201cconsultation  with  the  Chief  Justice  of  India\u201d  in\n<a href=\"/doc/259647/\" id=\"a_1691\">Articles  217(1)</a>  and  <a href=\"/doc/182777/\" id=\"a_1692\">222(1)</a>  of  the  Constitution   of   India   requires\nconsultation with a plurality of Judges in the formation of the  opinion  of\nthe Chief Justice of  India.  The  sole  individual  opinion  of  the  Chief\nJustice of India does not constitute \u201cconsultation\u201d within  the  meaning  of\nthe said articles.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_586\">3. The Chief Justice of India must make a recommendation to appoint a  Judge\nof the Supreme Court and to transfer a Chief Justice or puisne  Judge  of  a\nHigh Court in consultation with the four seniormost  puisne  Judges  of  the\nSupreme Court. Insofar as an appointment to the  High  Court  is  concerned,\nthe recommendation must be made in  consultation  with  the  two  seniormost\npuisne Judges of the Supreme Court.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_587\">4. The Chief Justice  of  India  is  not  entitled  to  act  solely  in  his\nindividual capacity, without consultation with other Judges of  the  Supreme\nCourt, in respect of materials and information conveyed  by  the  Government\nof India for non-appointment of a Judge recommended for appointment.\u201d[327]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_961\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_367\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_368\">136.  The decision in the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1693\">Second Judges</a> case read with the opinion given  by\nthis Court to the various questions raised in the Presidential Reference  or\nthe <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1694\">Third Judges</a>  case  fully  settled  the  controversies  surrounding  the\nprocedure to be adopted  in  the  appointment  of  judges  to  the  superior\njudiciary. Issues of primacy  of  views  and  consultation  with  the  Chief\nJustice of India were all answered by the decision and the opinion.</span></span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_962\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_369\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_370\">137.  It is important to note  that  the  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1695\">Third  Judges</a>  case  modified  one\nimportant norm or conclusion of the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1696\">Second  Judges</a>  case.</span></span>  <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_371\">The  modification\nwas that the \u2018collegium\u2019 for appointment of judges in the Supreme Court  was\nexpanded to consist of the Chief  Justice  of  India  and  four  senior-most\njudges rather than the two senior-most judges as  concluded  in  the  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1697\">Second\nJudges</a> case. In this manner, the consultation  with  the  Chief  Justice  of\nIndia was further broad-based. It was clarified in conclusion 9 as follows:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_588\">\u201c9. Recommendations made by the Chief Justice  of  India  without  complying\nwith  the  norms  and  requirements  of   the   consultation   process,   as\naforestated, are not binding upon the Government of India.\u201d\n\nThis conclusion is important, but seems to have been ignored  or  overlooked\nby the President.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_589\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"167993993\" id=\"span_372\">6 Samsher Singh\u2019s case\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_373\">7</span>\n\n\n8 138.      For a complete picture of the  judicial  pronouncements  on  the\nsubject, it is also necessary to refer to  the  decision  rendered  by  this\nCourt in <a href=\"/doc/167993993/\" id=\"a_1698\">Samsher Singh v. State of Punjab</a>.[328]</span>\n\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_590\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"PARTY\" data-docid=\"272706\" id=\"span_374\">139.  This case related to the termination of the services of  two  officers\nof the subordinate judicial service by the Governor of the State. The  issue\nwas whether the  Governor  could  exercise  his  discretion  in  the  matter\npersonally or should act on the advice of  the  Council  of  Ministers.  The\njudicial officers contended that the Governor was obliged  to  exercise  his\npersonal discretion and reliance was placed  on  <a href=\"/doc/272706/\" id=\"a_1699\">Sardari  Lal  v.  Union  of\nIndia</a>[329] in which it was held that for invoking  the  \u2018pleasure  doctrine\u2019\nunder <a href=\"/doc/1674593/\" id=\"a_1700\">Article 311(2)</a> of the Constitution, the personal satisfaction  of  the\nPresident is necessary for dispensing with an inquiry under  clause  (c)  of\nthe proviso to <a href=\"/doc/1674593/\" id=\"a_1701\">Article 311(2)</a> of the Constitution. On the  other  hand,  the\nState contended that the Governor was obliged to act only on the  advice  of\nthe Council of Ministers.</span>\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_591\">140.  This Court speaking through Chief Justice A.N. Ray  (for  himself  and\nfour other learned judges) overruled Sardari Lal and held that the  decision\ndid not correctly state the law.  It  was  held  that  under  the  Rules  of\nBusiness, the decision of the concerned Minister or officer is the  decision\nof the President or the Governor as the case may be. It was then concluded:\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_592\">\u201cFor the foregoing reasons we hold that the President or the  Governor  acts\non the aid and advice of the Council of Ministers with  the  Prime  Minister\nat the head in the case of the Union and the Chief Minister at the  head  in\nthe case of State in all matters which vests in the Executive whether  those\nfunctions are executive or legislative in character. Neither  the  President\nnor the Governor is to exercise  the  executive  functions  personally.  The\npresent appeals concern the  appointment  of  persons  other  than  District\nJudges to the Judicial Services of the State which is  to  be  made  by  the\nGovernor  as  contemplated  in  <a href=\"/doc/205445/\" id=\"a_1702\">Article  234</a>  of  the   Constitution   after\nconsultation with the State Public Service Commission and  the  High  Court.\nAppointment or dismissal or removal of persons  belonging  to  the  Judicial\nService of the State  is  not  a  personal  function  but  is  an  executive\nfunction of the Governor exercised in accordance  with  the  rules  in  that\nbehalf under the Constitution.\u201d[330]\n\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_593\">141.  In a separate but concurring  judgment  authored  by  Justice  Krishna\nIyer (for himself and Justice Bhagwati) the view expressed by Chief  Justice\nRay was accepted in the following words:\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_594\">\u201cWe declare the law of this branch  of  our  Constitution  to  be  that  the\nPresident and Governor, custodians of all executive and other  powers  under\nvarious articles shall,  by  virtue  of  these  provisions,  exercise  their\nformal constitutional powers only upon and in accordance with the advice  of\ntheir Ministers save in a few  well-known  exceptional  situations.  Without\nbeing dogmatic or exhaustive, these situations relate to (a) the  choice  of\nPrime Minister (Chief Minister), restricted though this  choice  is  by  the\nparamount consideration that he should command a majority in the House;  (b)\nthe dismissal of a Government which has lost its majority in the House,  but\nrefuses to quit office; (c) the dissolution of the House where an appeal  to\nthe country is necessitous, although in this area the head of  State  should\navoid getting involved  in  politics  and  must  be  advised  by  his  Prime\nMinister (Chief Minister) who will eventually take  the  responsibility  for\nthe step.\u201d[331]\n\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_595\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1720564\" id=\"span_375\">142.  An additional reason was given by the two learned  judges  for  coming\nto this conclusion and that is also important for our present purposes.  The\nadditional reason relates to the independence of the  judiciary.  For  this,\nreference was made to <a href=\"/doc/1720564/\" id=\"a_1703\">Jyoti Prokash Mitter v. Chief Justice,  Calcutta</a>.[332]\n The question <a href=\"/doc/1720564/\" id=\"a_1704\">in that case</a> related to the determination  of  the  age  of  a\nsitting  judge  of  the   High   Court   under   <a href=\"/doc/604649/\" id=\"a_1705\">Article   217(3)</a>   of   the\nConstitution.[333] This Court held that the age determination should  be  by\nthe President uninfluenced by the views of the executive.</span> <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"392667\" id=\"span_376\">This  was  on  the\nground that were the executive to make the determination of  the  age  of  a\nsitting  judge,  it  would  \u2018seriously  affect  the  independence   of   the\nJudiciary.\u2019 This view was subsequently  reiterated  in  <a href=\"/doc/392667/\" id=\"a_1706\">Union  of  India  v.\nJyoti Prokash Mitter</a>.[334]</span>\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_596\">143.  The learned judges then held, on  the  basis  of  the  scheme  of  the\nConstitution that had already been adverted to,  that  the  President  means\nthe Council of Ministers and the independence  of  the  judiciary  has  been\nsafeguarded by <a href=\"/doc/604649/\" id=\"a_1707\">Article 217(3)</a> of the Constitution by  making  mandatory  the\nconsultation  with  the  Chief  Justice  of   India   in   regard   to   age\ndetermination.  This   would   prevent   the   possibility   of   extraneous\nconsiderations entering into the decision of the Minister if he/she  departs\nfrom the views of the Chief Justice of  India.  It  was  held  that  in  all\nconceivable cases, consultation with the Chief Justice of  India  should  be\naccepted by the executive and if there is a departure from the views of  the\nChief Justice of India, the Court can examine the issue in the light of  the\navailable facts. In such a \u2018sensitive subject\u2019 the last word should be  with\nthe Chief Justice of India. On this interpretation,  it  becomes  irrelevant\nwho formally decides the issue. This is what was held:\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_597\">\u201cIn the light of the scheme of the Constitution  we  have  already  referred\nto, it is doubtful  whether  such  an  interpretation  as  to  the  personal\nsatisfaction of the President is correct.  We  are  of  the  view  that  the\nPresident means, for all practical purposes, the Minister or the Council  of\nMinisters as the case may be, and his opinion, satisfaction or  decision  is\nconstitutionally  secured  when  his  Ministers  arrive  at   such   opinion\nsatisfaction or decision. The independence of  the  Judiciary,  which  is  a\ncardinal principle of the Constitution and has been  relied  on  to  justify\nthe deviation, is guarded by the relevant article making  consultation  with\nthe  Chief  Justice  of  India  obligatory.   In   all   conceivable   cases\nconsultation with that highest dignitary of Indian justice will  and  should\nbe accepted  by  the  Government  of  India  and  the  Court  will  have  an\nopportunity to examine if any other extraneous  circumstances  have  entered\ninto the verdict of the Minister, if he departs from the  counsel  given  by\nthe Chief Justice of India. In practice the last word in  such  a  sensitive\nsubject must belong to the Chief Justice of  India,  the  rejection  of  his\nadvice being ordinarily  regarded  as  prompted  by  oblique  considerations\nvitiating the order.
