In this view it is immaterial whether the President  or\nthe Prime  Minister  or  the  Minister  for  Justice  formally  decides  the\nissue.\u201d[335]\n\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_598\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_377\">144.  This decision is important for three key reasons: (1)  It  recognized,\njudicially, the independence of the judiciary. (This was  before  the  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1708\">First\nJudges</a> case which recognized that the independence of the  judiciary  was  a\nbasic feature of the Constitution).  (2) It cleared the  air  by  concluding\nthat the President was obliged to act  on  the  advice  of  the  Council  of\nMinisters,  even  on  the  issue  of  appointment  of   judges.   This   was\n\u2018formalized\u2019 by the <a href=\"/doc/50073852/\" id=\"a_1709\">Constitution (Forty-second  Amendment)  Act,  1976</a>.</span>  <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_378\">(3)\nIn a sense, this decision was a precursor to the primacy conclusion  in  the\n<a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1710\">Second Judges</a> case with the last word on the subject being  with  the  Chief\nJustice of India.</span>\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_599\">145.  There are two observations  that  need  to  be  made  at  this  stage.\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_963\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_379\">Firstly, Justice Krishna Iyer  penned  the  decision  in  Samsher  Singh  on\nbehalf of Justice Bhagwati as well. Surprisingly, Justice Bhagwati  did  not\nrefer to this decision in the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1711\">First Judges</a> case.</span> <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_380\">The  significance  of  this\nfailure is that while in Samsher Singh  it  was  held  by  Justice  Bhagwati\nthat the \u2018last word\u2019 must belong to the  Chief  Justice  of  India,  in  the\n<a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1712\">First Judges</a> case  it was  held  by  Justice  Bhagwati  that  the  \u2018ultimate\npower\u2019 is with the executive. This  completely  divergent  view,  though  in\ndifferent circumstances, is inexplicable since the underlying  principle  is\nthe same, namely, the status of the Chief Justice of  India  with  reference\nto the affairs concerning the judiciary.</span> <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_381\">The second observation is that  the\n\u2018last word\u2019 theory was not and has not been questioned by the  executive  in\nany case, even in  the  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1713\">Second  Judges</a>  case.  Therefore,  the  \u2018last  word\u2019\nprinciple having been accepted, there is now no reason to go back on  it  or\nto repudiate it.  It may be mentioned in the \u2018last word\u2019 context  that  ever\nsince the Constitution came to be enacted, writes Granville Austin,  quoting\nfrom Chief Justice Mehr Chand Mahajan\u2019s \u2018A Pillar of Justice\u2019:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_600\">\u201cNehru \u2018has always acted in accordance with  the  advice  of  the  CJI\u2019,  he\nrecalled,  except  in  rare  circumstances,   despite   efforts   by   state\npoliticians with \u2018considerable pull\u2019 to influence him.\u201d[336]\n\n\n9 Sankalchand Sheth\u2019s case\n\n\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_601\">146.  Another decision of considerable significance is  Union  of  India  v.\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_964\">Sankalchand Himatlal Sheth.[337] That case  pertained  to  the  transfer  of\njudges from one High Court to another  and  the  interpretation  of  <a href=\"/doc/182777/\" id=\"a_1714\">Article\n222(1)</a> of the  Constitution.[338]  Referring  to  the  independence  of  the\njudiciary as also <a href=\"/doc/192717/\" id=\"a_1715\">Article 50</a> of the Constitution  it  was  said  by  Justice\nY.V. Chandrachud:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_602\">\u201cHaving envisaged that the judiciary, which ought to act  as  a  bastion  of\nthe rights and freedom of the people, must be immune from the influence  and\ninterference of  the  executive,  the  Constituent  Assembly  gave  to  that\nconcept  a  concrete  form  by  making  various  provisions  to  secure  and\nsafeguard  the  independence  of  the   judiciary.   <a href=\"/doc/192717/\" id=\"a_1716\">Article   50</a>   of   the\nConstitution,  which  contains  a  Directive  Principle  of  State   Policy,\nprovides that the State shall take steps to separate the judiciary from  the\nexecutive in the public services of the State.\u201d\n\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_603\">147.  On the meaning  of  consultation  by  the  President  with  the  Chief\nJustice of India in the context of <a href=\"/doc/819787/\" id=\"a_1717\">Article 222</a> of the Constitution,  it  was\nheld that it has to be full and effective consultation  and  not  formal  or\nunproductive. It was said:\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_604\">\u201cArticle 222(1) which requires the President to consult  the  Chief  Justice\nof India is founded on the principle that in a  matter  which  concerns  the\njudiciary vitally, no decision ought to be taken by  the  executive  without\nobtaining the views of the Chief Justice  of  India  who,  by  training  and\nexperience, is in the  best  position  to  consider  the  situation  fairly,\ncompetently and objectively. But there can be  no  purposeful  consideration\nof a matter, in the absence of facts  and  circumstances  on  the  basis  of\nwhich alone the nature of the problem involved can be  appreciated  and  the\nright decision taken. It must, therefore, follow that while  consulting  the\nChief Justice, the President must make the relevant data  available  to  him\non the basis of which he can offer to  the  President  the  benefit  of  his\nconsidered opinion. If the facts necessary to arrive at a proper  conclusion\nare not made available to the Chief Justice, he must ask for  them  because,\nin casting on the President the obligation to  consult  the  Chief  Justice,\nthe Constitution at the same time must be taken to have imposed  a  duty  on\nthe Chief Justice to express  his  opinion  on  nothing  less  than  a  full\nconsideration of the matter on which he is entitled  to  be  consulted.  The\nfulfilment by the President, of his constitutional obligation to place  full\nfacts before the Chief Justice and the performance by  the  latter,  of  the\nduty to elicit facts which are necessary to arrive at  a  proper  conclusion\nare parts of the same process and  are  complementary  to  each  other.  The\nfaithful observance of these may well earn a  handsome  dividend  useful  to\nthe administration of justice. Consultation within the  meaning  of  <a href=\"/doc/182777/\" id=\"a_1718\">Article\n222(1)</a>, therefore, means full and effective,  not  formal  or  unproductive,\nconsultation.\u201d[339]\n\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_605\">148.  It was observed that though \u2018consultation\u2019 did not mean  \u2018concurrence\u2019\nyet, as held in Samsher Singh consultation with the Chief Justice  of  India\nshould be accepted and in such  a  sensitive  subject  the  last  word  must\nbelong to the Chief Justice of India. It  was  noted  that  if  there  is  a\ndeparture from the counsel of the Chief Justice of India,  the  Court  would\nhave the opportunity to examine if  any  extraneous  considerations  entered\ninto the decision.[340]\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_606\">149.  This view was reiterated by Justice  Krishna  Iyer  (for  himself  and\nJustice Fazl Ali).[341] Significantly, it was added that: \u2018It  seems  to  us\nthat the word, \u2018consultation\u2019 has been used in <a href=\"/doc/819787/\" id=\"a_1719\">Article 222</a> as  a  matter  of\nconstitutional courtesy in view of the fact that two very  high  dignitaries\nare concerned in the matter, namely, the President and the Chief Justice  of\nIndia.\u2019[342]\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_607\">150.  The greater significance of Sankalchand Sheth lies in the  conclusion,\nrelying upon  R. Pushpam, that for a meaningful consultation,  both  parties\nmust have for consideration full and identical facts. It was said:\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_608\">\u201cThe word \u2018consult\u2019 implies a conference  of  two  or  more  persons  or  an\nimpact of two or more minds in respect of a topic in order  to  enable  them\nto evolve a correct, or at least, a satisfactory solution\u201d.  In  order  that\nthe two minds may be able to confer and  produce  a  mutual  impact,  it  is\nessential that each must have  for  its  consideration  full  and  identical\nfacts, which can at once constitute both the source and  foundation  of  the\nfinal decision.\u201d[343]\n\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_609\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_382\">151.   This  view  was  accepted  in  the  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1720\">First  Judges</a>  case  by   Justice\nBhagwati,[344] Justice Fazal Ali,[345]  Justice  V.D.  Tulzapurkar[346]  and\nJustice D.A. Desai.</span><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_383\">[347] It was also accepted in the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1721\">Second Judges</a>  case  by\nJustice Pandian.[348]\n\n\nMemorandum of Procedure \u2013 30.6.1999</span>\n\n\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_610\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_384\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_385\">152.  Following up on the  decision  and  opinion  rendered  in  the  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1722\">Second\nJudges</a> case and  the  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1723\">Third  Judges</a>  case,  the  Minister  for  Law  in  the\nGovernment of India framed and prepared one Memorandum of Procedure for  the\nappointment of a judge of the Supreme Court and another for the  appointment\nof a judge of the High Court. These were shared with the  Chief  Justice  of\nIndia. None of the each successive Chief Justices of India  have  complained\nor criticized any of the Memoranda or adversely commented  on  them,  or  at\nleast we have not been told of any such  complaint  or  objection.  No  one,\nincluding  any  successive  Law  Minister  of  the  Government   of   India,\ncomplained that the Memoranda were unworkable or  caused  any  hindrance  or\ndelay in the appointment of judges or did not correctly  reflect  the  views\nof this Court in the two decisions mentioned above  or  that  they  did  not\nconform to any provision of the Constitution, either in letter or in  spirit\nor even  otherwise,  or  at  least  we  have  not  been  told  of  any  such\nconstraint. These Memoranda remained  operational  and  the  appointment  of\njudges to the  superior  judiciary  made  subsequent  thereto  has  been  in\nconformity with them. No one complained about the inability  to  effectively\nwork any Memorandum of Procedure.</span></span>\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_611\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_386\">153.  We were invited by Mr. Fali S. Nariman to mention  the  procedure  for\nthe appointment of judges  both  in  public  interest  and  for  reasons  of\ntransparency. The Memorandum of Procedure for the appointment of  judges  of\nthe Supreme Court and the High Court are available on  the  website  of  the\nDepartment of Justice of the Government of India[349] and  therefore  it  is\nnot  necessary  to  make  a  detailed  mention  of  the  procedure.  Similar\nMemoranda have been referred  to  in  the  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1724\">Second  Judges</a>  case  by  Justice\nPandian.[350]</span>\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_612\">154.  A  reading  of  the  Memoranda  makes  it  explicit  that  a  proposal\nrecommending the appointment of a judge of a High Court shall  be  initiated\nby the Chief Justice of the High  Court.  However,  if  the  Chief  Minister\ndesires to recommend the name of any person he should forward  the  same  to\nthe Chief Justice for his consideration. Although it is  not  clearly  spelt\nout, it is implicit that the Chief Justice is  not  obliged  to  accept  the\nsuggestion of the Chief Minister.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_613\">155.  It is also significant and important to note that  in  the  Memoranda,\nconsultation by the judges in the collegium with \u2018non-judges\u2019 for making  an\nappointment to the Supreme Court is postulated and it is not prohibited  for\nmaking an appointment to the High  Court.  That  is  to  say,  a  \u2018collegium\njudge\u2019 is not prohibited from taking  the  opinion  of  any  person,  either\nconnected with the legal profession or  otherwise  for  taking  an  informed\ndecision regarding the suitability or otherwise of a person for  appointment\nas a judge of the High Court or the Supreme Court. That this is not  unknown\nis clear from a categorical statement  of  Justice  Verma  in  an  interview\nthat:\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_614\">\u201cFor every Supreme Court appointment, I consulted senior lawyers  like  Fali\nS. Nariman and Shanthi Bhushan. I used to consult five or six  top  lawyers.\nI used to consult even  lawyers  belonging  to  the  middle  level.  Similar\nconsultation took place in the case of High Courts. I  recorded  details  of\nevery consultation. I wish all my correspondence is made public.\u201d\n\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_615\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_387\">156.  Therefore, during the  evolution  of  the  system  of  appointment  of\njudges four cobwebs were cleared. They were: (1) The role of  the  President\n\u2013 he/she was expected to act on the advice of the Council of Ministers  even\nin the appointment  of  judges;  (2)  The  initial  recommendation  for  the\nappointment of a judge of a High Court  was  to  originate  from  the  Chief\nJustice of the High Court and for the appointment of a judge of the  Supreme\nCourt from  the  Chief  Justice  of  India;  (3)  Consultation  between  the\nPresident and the Chief Justice of  India  is  an  integrated  participative\nprocess with the result  that  the  President  has  the  final  say  in  the\nappointment of a judge under certain circumstances and the Chief Justice  of\nIndia (in consultation with and on the unanimous view of  the  other  judges\nconsulted by him/her) has the final say  under  certain  circumstances;  and\n(4) The Union of India accepted these  propositions  without  hesitation  in\nthe <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1725\">Third Judges</a> case.</span>\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_965\">Amendments to the Constitution\n\n\n\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_966\">157.   Apart  from  judicial  discourses  on  the  appointment  of   judges,\nParliament too has had  its  share  of  discussions.  On  as  many  as  four\noccasions, it was proposed to amend the  Constitution  in  relation  to  the\nprocedure for the appointment of judges of the Supreme Court  and  the  High\nCourts. These proposed amendments are considered below.\n</p>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_967\">1 (a) The Constitution (Sixty-seventh Amendment) Bill, 1990\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_388\">2</span>\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_968\">158.  The Constitution (Sixty-seventh Amendment) Bill, 1990  was  introduced\nin the Lok Sabha on 18th May, 1990 and it proposed  to  set  up  a  National\nJudicial Commission (for short  the  NJC),  though  not  in  line  with  the\nrecommendations of the LCI. The composition of the NJC was to vary with  the\nsubject matter of concern,  namely,  the  appointment  of  a  judge  of  the\nSupreme Court or the appointment of a judge of the High Court.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_969\">159.  For the appointment of a judge of the Supreme Court, in terms  of  the\nproposed Article 307A of the Constitution, the NJC was  to  consist  of  the\nChief Justice of India and two other judges of the  Supreme  Court  next  in\nseniority to the Chief Justice of India. For the appointment of a  judge  of\nthe High Court, the NJC was to consist of the Chief Justice  of  India,  the\nChief Minister or Governor (as the case may be) of the concerned State,  one\nother judge of the Supreme Court next in seniority to the Chief  Justice  of\nIndia, the Chief Justice of the High Court and the judge of the  High  Court\nnext in seniority to the Chief Justice of  the  High  Court.  There  was  no\nprovision for the appointment of the Chief Justice of  India  or  the  Chief\nJustice of the High Court.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_970\">160.  The procedure for the transaction of business of the  NJC  was  to  be\ndetermined by the President in consultation with the Chief Justice of  India\nand was subject to any law made by Parliament.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_971\">161.  The Amendment Act also provided that in the event  the  recommendation\nof the NJC is not accepted,  the  reasons  therefor  shall  be  recorded  in\nwriting.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_972\">162.  The Bill was criticized (in  part)  by  the  Arrears  Committee  which\nstated that:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_616\">\u201cThe Committee is unable to find any logic or  justification  for  different\ncommissions\u2026.Keeping in view the objects and reasons  for  the  constitution\nof  the  commission,  namely,  to  obviate  the   criticism   of   executive\narbitrariness in the matter  of  appointment  and  transfer  of  High  Court\njudges  and  to  prevent  delay  in  making  appointments,   there   is   no\njustification for the executive through the Chief  Minister  to  be  on  the\ncommission.  Instead of removing the vice of  executive  interference  which\nhas vitiated the working of the present system the  presence  of  the  Chief\nMinister on the recommendatory body actual alleviates him  from  the  status\nof a mere  consultee  to  the  position  of  an  equal  participant  in  the\nselection process of the recommendatory body.  By making the Chief  Minister\nan equal party when he is not equipped to offer any view in  regard  to  the\nmerit, ability, competency, integrity and suitability of the candidates  for\nappointments, the scope of executive interference is enhanced.\u201d[351]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_973\">163.  The Bill was not taken up for consideration due to the dissolution  of\nthe Lok Sabha in May, 1991.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_974\">3 (b) The Constitution (Ninety-eighth Amendment) Bill, 2003\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_975\">164.  On 22nd February, 2000 \u2013 barely 8 months after  the  issuance  of  the\n(Revised) Memorandum of Procedure mentioned above \u2013 the Government of  India\nissued a notification  setting  up  a  National  Commission  to  Review  the\nWorking of the Constitution (for short the NCRWC), including  the  procedure\nfor the appointment of judges  of  the  superior  judiciary.  The  terms  of\nreference of the NCRWC were as follows:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_617\">\u201cThe Commission shall examine, in the light of\u00a0the experience  of  the  past\n50 years, as to how best the Constitution can respond to the changing  needs\nof efficient, smooth and effective system of governance  and  socio-economic\ndevelopment of modern India within the framework of parliamentary  democracy\nand to recommend changes, if any, that are required  in  the  provisions  of\nthe Constitution without interfering with its basic structure or features.\u201d\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_976\">165.  On 26th September, 2001 an  Advisory  Panel  of  the  NCRWC  issued  a\nConsultation Paper on Superior Judiciary.[352] This  Paper  dealt  with  the\nprocedure for appointment of judges  of  the  Supreme  Court  and  the  High\nCourts, the age of retirement of judges, the transfer of judges of the  High\nCourts and the procedure for dealing with \u2018deviant\u2019 behavior of a judge  and\nfor his/her removal.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_977\">166.  <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_389\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_390\">In the context of appointment of judges  of  the  superior  judiciary,\nparagraph 8.20 of the Paper is significant  since  it  tacitly  acknowledges\nthat the procedure evolved over the years particularly as a  result  of  the\n<a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1726\">Second Judges</a> case  and  the  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1727\">Third  Judges</a>  case  was  quite  satisfactory.\nParagraph 8.20 reads as follows:</span></span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_618\">\u201c8.20\u00a0Purpose of 67th\u00a0Amendment Bill served by the judgement  in  SCAORA:\u00a0We\nhave set out hereinabove the several  methods  of  appointment  (to  Supreme\nCourt and High Courts) suggested  by  the  various  bodies,  committees  and\norganizations.\u00a0\u00a0We  have  also  set  out  the  method   and   procedure   of\nappointment  devised  by  the   1993   decision   of   the   Supreme   Court\nin\u00a0SCAORA[353]\u00a0and in the 1998 opinion rendered under <a href=\"/doc/210155/\" id=\"a_1728\">Article 143.</a> It  would\nbe evident therefrom that the 1993 decision gives effect  to  the  substance\nof the  Constitution  (Sixty-seventh  Amendment)  Bill,  without  of  course\ncalling it a \u2018National Judicial Commission\u2019, and without  the  necessity  of\namending the Constitution as suggested by the said Amendment  Bill.\u00a0\u00a0Indeed,\nit carries forward the object underlying the Amendment Bill  by  making  the\nrecommendations of the Chief Justice of India and his colleagues binding  on\nthe President.\u00a0\u00a0The 1998 opinion indeed enlarges the  \u2018collegium\u2019.\u00a0\u00a0In  this\nsense, the purpose of the said Amendment Bill evidenced by  the  proviso  to\n<a href=\"/doc/1995948/\" id=\"a_1729\">Article 124(2)</a> and the Explanation appended  thereto,  is  served,  speaking\nbroadly.\u00a0\u00a0The method of appointment evolved by these  decisions  has  indeed\nbeen hailed by several jurists and is held out  as  a  precedent  worthy  of\nemulation by U.K. and others.\u00a0\u00a0(See the opinion of Lord Templeman, a  member\nof the House of Lords, cited hereinabove.)\u00a0\u00a0The said decisions lay down  the\nproposition that the \u201cconsultation\u201d contemplated by  <a href=\"/doc/1164880/\" id=\"a_1730\">Articles  124</a>  and  <a href=\"/doc/1682952/\" id=\"a_1731\">217</a>\nshould be a real and effective consultation and that having  regard  to  the\nconcept  of  Judicial  independence,  which  is  a  basic  feature  of   the\nConstitution, the opinion rendered by the  Chief  Justice  of  India  (after\nconsulting his colleagues) shall be binding  upon  the  Executive.\u00a0\u00a0In  this\nview of the matter, much  of  the  expectations  from  a  National  Judicial\nCommission (N.J.C) have been  met.\u00a0\u00a0The  said  Constitution  Amendment  Bill\nwas, it would appear, prepared after a wide and elaborate consultation  with\nall the political parties and other stakeholders.\u00a0\u00a0However,  the  aspect  of\ndisciplinary jurisdiction remains unanswered.\u00a0\u00a0We may  however  discuss  the\nconcept of an N.J.C. which  may  cover  both  appointments  and  matters  of\ndiscipline.\u201d\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_978\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_391\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_392\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_393\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_394\">167.  The Paper acknowledged that the  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1732\">Second  Judges</a>  case  and  the  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1733\">Third\nJudges</a> case \u2018speaking  broadly\u2019  served  the  purpose  of  the  Constitution\n(Sixty-seventh Amendment) Bill and that \u2018much of  the  expectations  from  a\nNational Judicial Commission (N.J.C)  have  been  met.\u2019  The  shortfalls  in\nexpectations  were  not  specified  in  the  Paper  except   that   of   the\ndisciplinary jurisdiction which did not arise and was not dealt with in  the\n<a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1734\">Second Judges</a> case or the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1735\">Third Judges</a> case. However,  it  is  important  to\nnote that a dispassionate jurist Lord Templeman, a member of  the  House  of\nLords held the view that the system of appointment of judges in India  ought\nto  be  followed  in  England  as  well.  Apart  from  him,  the  system  of\nappointment of judges <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1736\">laid down by</a>  these  decisions  \u2018has  been  hailed  by\nseveral jurists and is held out as a precedent worthy of emulation\u2019.</span></span></span></span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_979\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_395\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_396\">168.  Be that as it may,  the  NCRWC  submitted  its  Report  to  the  Prime\nMinister on 31st March, 2002. In Chapter 7 of the  Report  relating  to  the\njudiciary,  the  NCRWC  recommended   in   paragraph   7.3.7   thereof   the\nestablishment of a National Judicial Commission (for short the NJC). It  was\nobserved  that  such  a  commission  was  necessary   for   \u2018the   effective\nparticipation of both the executive and the judicial wings of the  State  as\nan integrated scheme for the machinery for appointment of  judges\u2019  in  line\nwith the integrated participatory consultative  process  suggested  by  this\nCourt in the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1737\">Second Judges</a> case and the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1738\">Third Judges</a> case. This is what  the\nNCRWC had to say:</span></span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_619\">\u201cThe matter relating to manner of appointment of  judges  had  been  debated\nover a decade.\u00a0\u00a0The Constitution (Sixty-seventh Amendment)  Bill,  1990  was\nintroduced  on  18th\u00a0May,  1990  (9th   Lok   Sabha)   providing   for   the\ninstitutional frame work of National Judicial  Commission  for  recommending\nthe appointment of  judges  to  the  Supreme  Court  and  the  various  High\nCourts.\u00a0\u00a0Further, it appears that latterly there is  a  movement  throughout\nthe world to move  this  function  away  from  the  exclusive  fiat  of  the\nexecutive  and  involving   some   institutional   frame   work   whereunder\nconsultation with the judiciary at some level is provided for before  making\nsuch appointments.\u00a0\u00a0The system of  consultation  in  some  form  is  already\navailable in Japan, Israel  and  the  UK.\u00a0\u00a0The  Constitution  (Sixty-seventh\nAmendment) Bill, 1990 provided for a  collegium  of  the  Chief  Justice  of\nIndia and two other judges of the Supreme Court for  making  appointment  to\nthe Supreme Court. However,\u00a0it would be worthwhile to have  a  participatory\nmode with the participation of both  the  executive  and  the  judiciary  in\nmaking such recommendations. The Commission proposes the composition of  the\nCollegium which gives due importance  to  and  provides  for  the  effective\nparticipation of both the executive and the judicial wings of the  State  as\nan integrated scheme for the  machinery  for  appointment  of  judges.  This\nCommission,  accordingly,  recommends  the  establishment  of   a   National\nJudicial Commission under the Constitution.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_620\">The National Judicial Commission for appointment of judges  of  the  Supreme\nCourt shall comprise of:\n</blockquote><pre id=\"pre_10\">The Chief Justice of India:                  Chairman\n Two senior most judges of the Supreme Court:      Member\n The Union Minister for Law and Justice:                 Member\n</pre><blockquote id=\"blockquote_621\">One eminent person nominated by the\u00a0President  after  consulting  the  Chief\nJustice of India:                                  Member\nThe recommendation for the establishment of a National  Judicial  Commission\nand its composition are to be treated as integral in view  of  the  need  to\npreserve the independence of the judiciary.\u201d[354]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_980\">169.  Pursuant  to  the  recommendations  of  the  NCRWC,  the  Constitution\n(Ninety-eighth Amendment) Bill, 2003 was  introduced  in  Parliament  on  or\nabout 8th May, 2003.  The Statement of  Objects  and  Reasons  of  the  Bill\nstates, inter alia, that the Government of India has been committed  to  the\nsetting up of an NJC for appointment of judges of the Supreme  Court,  Chief\nJustices and Judges of the High Courts as well as their transfer  so  as  to\nprovide for the effective  participation  of  both  the  executive  and  the\njudicial wings of the Government.  It  is  mentioned  that  the  NCRWC  also\nconsidered this matter and recommended the establishment of an NJC.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_981\">170.  The Statement of Objects and Reasons refers to the composition of  the\nNJC and while the NCRWC had recommended the nomination in  the  NJC  of  one\neminent person by the President of India after consulting the Chief  Justice\nof India, the Constitution  (Ninety-Eighth  Amendment)  Bill  modified  this\nrecommendation and proposed that one eminent citizen  be  nominated  by  the\nPresident of India in consultation with the Prime Minister of  India  for  a\nperiod of three years.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_982\">171.   The  Constitution  (Ninety-eighth  Amendment)   Bill   proposed   the\ninsertion of Chapter IVA in  the  Constitution  consisting  of  one  Article\nnamely Article 147A.  This Article related to the establishment of  the  NJC\nin terms of the Statement of Objects and Reasons.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_983\">172.  The Bill was not  passed  in  any  House  of  Parliament  due  to  the\ndissolution of the Lok Sabha in March 2004 and the general  elections  being\ncalled.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_984\">4 (c) The Constitution (One Hundred and Twentieth Amendment) Bill, 2013\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_985\">173.  A third attempt was made to amend the Constitution  for  the  purposes\nof appointment of judges  of  the  superior  judiciary.   This  was  by  the\nintroduction of the  Constitution  (One  Hundred  and  Twentieth  Amendment)\nBill, 2013 introduced in the Rajya Sabha on 24th August 2013.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_986\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_397\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_398\">174.  The Statement of Objects and Reasons  to  the  Bill  referred  to  the\n<a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1739\">Second Judges</a> case and the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1740\">Third Judges</a> case as well as  the  Memorandum  of\nProcedure.  It was mentioned that the Memorandum confers upon the  judiciary\nitself the power of appointment of judges of  the  superior  judiciary.[355]\nIt was further stated that after a review  of  the  pronouncements  of  this\nCourt  and  relevant  constitutional  provisions,  a  broad  based  judicial\nappointment commission could be established for making  recommendations  for\nthe selection of judges.  This commission would provide  a  meaningful  role\nto the executive and the judiciary to present their viewpoint and  make  the\nparticipants accountable while introducing  transparency  in  the  selection\nprocess.  The Statement of Objects  and  Reasons  also  mentioned  that  the\nproposed Bill would enable equal participation  of  the  judiciary  and  the\nexecutive in the appointment of judges to the superior  judiciary  and  also\nmake the system more accountable and thereby increase the confidence of  the\npublic in the judiciary.</span></span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_987\">175.  The Constitution (One Hundred and Twentieth Amendment)  Bill  proposed\nthe insertion of <a href=\"/doc/140653939/\" id=\"a_1741\">Article 124A</a> in the Constitution establishing a  commission\nknown as the  National  Judicial  Appointments  Commission  (for  short  the\nNJAC).  The composition of the NJAC, the appointment of its Chairperson  and\nMembers, their qualifications, conditions  of  services,  tenure,  functions\nand the procedure as  well  as  the  manner  of  selection  of  persons  for\nappointment as Chief Justice of India, judges of the  Supreme  Court,  Chief\nJustices and other judges of the High Courts was to be provided by law  made\nby Parliament.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_988\">176.  The Constitution  (One  Hundred  and  Twentieth  Amendment)  Bill  was\npassed by the Rajya Sabha on 5th  September  2013  but  the  Lok  Sabha  was\ndissolved in May 2014 before the Bill could be sent to it  and  the  general\nelections called.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_989\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_399\">177.  Strangely, the Statement of Objects and Reasons completely  overlooked\nthe fact that there already was \u2018equal participation of  the  judiciary  and\nthe executive in the appointment of judges to the  superior  judiciary.\u2019  In\nthe <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1742\">Second Judges</a> case it was clearly, explicitly and  unequivocally  stated\nthat:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_622\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_400\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_401\">\u201cThe process of appointment of Judges to the  Supreme  Court  and  the  High\nCourts is an integrated \u2018participatory consultative process\u2019  for  selecting\nthe best and most suitable persons available for appointment;  and  all  the\nconstitutional functionaries must perform  this  duty  collectively  with  a\nview primarily to reach an agreed decision,  subserving  the  constitutional\npurpose, so that the occasion of primacy does not arise.\u201d[356]\n\nHowever, in the event of a difference of opinion, one of the  constitutional\nauthorities must have the final say and given the constitutional  convention\nover the decades the final say ought to be with the Chief Justice of  India,\nthe head of the judiciary in India under certain circumstances and with  the\nPresident under certain circumstances. Otherwise, a  stalemate  or  deadlock\nsituation could arise which  the  Constituent  Assembly  obviously  did  not\nanticipate from two constitutional functionaries.  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1743\">The  Second  Judges</a>  case\nand the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1744\">Third Judges</a> case gave this shared responsibility to  the  President\nand the Chief Justice of India.[357] For the appointment of a judge  of  the\nSupreme Court, the collegium of  5  (five)  judges  must  make  a  unanimous\nrecommendation. The President  is  entitled  to  turn  down  a  4-1  or  3-2\nrecommendation. If the unanimous recommendation does not  find  favour  with\nthe President  for  strong  and  cogent  reasons  and  is  returned  to  the\ncollegium for reconsideration, and it is unanimously  reiterated,  then  the\nPresident  is  obliged  to  accept  the  recommendation.  However,  if   the\nreiteration is not unanimous, then the President is entitled  to  turn  down\nthe recommendation. The theory  which  the  Constitution  (One  Hundred  and\nTwentieth Amendment) Bill, 2013  [and  subsequently  the  Constitution  (One\nHundred and Twenty-first Amendment) Bill,  2014]  sought  to  demolish  that\n\u2018judges appoint judges\u2019 is non-existent.</span></span>\n</blockquote>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_402\">5</span>\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_623\">6 (d) The Constitution (One Hundred and Twenty-first Amendment) Bill, 2014\n\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_990\">178.   The  fourth  and  final  attempt  (presently  successful  and   under\nchallenge  in  these  petitions)  to  amend  the  Constitution  was  by  the\nintroduction on 11th August, 2014  of  the  Constitution  (One  Hundred  and\nTwenty-first Amendment) Bill, 2014.  This Bill was passed by the  Lok  Sabha\non 13th August, 2014 and by the  Rajya  Sabha  on  14th  August,  2014.   It\nreceived the ratification of more than one half of the  States  as  required\nby <a href=\"/doc/1389240/\" id=\"a_1745\">Article 368(2)</a> of  the  Constitution  and  received  the  assent  of  the\nPresident on 31st December, 2014 when it became  the  Constitution  (Ninety-\nninth Amendment) Act 2014.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_991\">179.  It may be mentioned en passant that the learned Solicitor General  was\nrequested  to  place  on  record  the  procedure  adopted   by   the   State\nLegislatures for ratification of the Constitution (One Hundred  and  Twenty-\nfirst Amendment) Bill, 2014 but that information was  not  forthcoming,  for\nreasons that are not known. The intention was not to question the factum  of\nratification but only to understand the process and to add  transparency  to\nthe process, since there have been instances in the United States where  the\ncourts have examined the issue of the ratification of an  amendment  to  the\nConstitution.[358] Transparency is not a one-way street.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_992\">180.  <a href=\"/doc/187701010/\" id=\"a_1746\">Section 1(2)</a> of the Constitution  (Ninety-ninth  Amendment)  Act  2014\nprovides that it  shall  come  into  force  on  such  date  as  the  Central\nGovernment may by  notification  in  the  official  gazette,  appoint.   The\nappointed date is 13th April, 2015.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_993\">181.  Simultaneous with the passage of the  Constitution  (One  Hundred  and\nTwenty-First  Amendment)  Bill,  Parliament  also  considered  the  National\nJudicial Appointment Commission Bill, 2014.   The  Bill  was  introduced  in\nParliament on 11th August, 2014. It was passed by  the  Lok  Sabha  on  13th\nAugust, 2014 and by the Rajya Sabha on  14th  August,  2014.   <a href=\"/doc/142995521/\" id=\"a_1747\">The  National\nJudicial Appointments  Commission  Act</a>  also  received  the  assent  of  the\nPresident on 31st December, 2014 and it was brought into force by a  gazette\nnotification issued on 13th April, 2015.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_994\">182.  <a href=\"/doc/195108305/\" id=\"a_1748\">Both the Constitution  (Ninety-ninth  Amendment)  Act,  2014</a>  and  the\n<a href=\"/doc/142995521/\" id=\"a_1749\">National Judicial Appointments Commission Act, 2014</a> are challenged  in  this\nand a batch of connected writ petitions.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_995\">Conclusions on the factual background\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_996\">183.  The conclusions that can  be  drawn  from  the  background  historical\nfacts are as follows:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_624\">(1) The independence  of  the  judiciary  has  been  always  recognized  and\nacknowledged by all concerned.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_625\">(2) Prior to Independence, the appointment of a judge to  a  superior  court\nwas entirely the discretion of the Crown.   The  Constituent  Assembly  felt\nthat such a \u2018supreme and absolute\u2019 power should not vest  in  the  President\nor the government  of  the  day  or  the  Chief  Justice  of  India  (as  an\nindividual) and therefore a fetter was placed on  that  power  by  requiring\nthe President to mandatorily consult the Chief Justice of  India  (with  the\ndiscretion to consult other judges) for the appointment of a  judge  to  the\nSupreme Court. For the appointment of  a  judge  of  the  High  Court  also,\nconsultation with the Chief Justice of India  was  mandatory.  In  addition,\nconsultation with the Chief Justice of the High Court and  the  Governor  of\nthe State was mandatory. Significantly, there is no mention of  consultation\nwith anybody from civil society.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_626\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_403\">(3) Any doubt about the individual role of the President in the  process  of\nappointment of judges came to rest and it was clear that the  President  was\nexpected to act only on the advice of the Council of Ministers.\n(4) Similarly, the Chief Justice of India is  not  expected  to  act  in  an\nindividual or personal capacity  but  must  consult  his/her  senior  judges\nbefore making a recommendation for the appointment of a judge.\n(5)  Dr.  Ambedkar  and  the  Constituent  Assembly  did  not   accept   the\n\u2018unfettered discretion\u2019 theory in the CAD but  this  view  was  subsequently\nrejected in the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1750\">First Judges</a> case which  brought  in  the  \u2018ultimate  power\u2019\ntheory propounded by Justice Bhagwati and Justice Desai.\n(6) Executive interference in  the  appointment  process  (with  perhaps  an\ninformal method of \u2018take over\u2019) had started around the time of  Independence\nand got aggravated post Independence, peaking towards the end of the 1980s.\n (7) Not a single instance was given to us where the  President  recommended\na person for appointment as a judge of the Supreme Court or the High  Court.</span>\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_997\"> <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_404\">The Chief Minister of a State might have made  a  recommendation  (although\nno instance was given to us) but that was required to be routed through  the\nChief Justice of the High Court, as per the Memorandum of Procedure.\n(8) Only one instance was given to us, pre the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1751\">First Judges</a>  case  where  an\nappointment as a judge of the High Court was made  without  the  concurrence\nof the Chief Justice of India.</span> <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_405\"><a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1752\">Post the First Judges</a> case as many  as  seven\nsuch appointments were made.</span> <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_406\">This is a clear indication that  the  \u2018ultimate\npower\u2019 theory propounded in the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1753\">First Judges</a> case translated into  \u2018absolute\nexecutive primacy\u2019. The dream of Dr. Ambedkar became a nightmare, thanks  to\nthe political executive.</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_998\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_407\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_408\">(9) The \u2018ultimate power\u2019 theory or the \u2018absolute executive  primacy\u2019  theory\nis now diluted and the last word in  the  appointment  of  a  judge  of  the\nSupreme Court is shared between the  President  and  the  Chief  Justice  of\nIndia in terms of  the  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1754\">Second  Judges</a>  case  and  the  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1755\">Third  Judges</a>  case.\nHistorically, giving the last word to the executive has been  criticized  by\nno less than the Attorney-General Shri M.C. Setalvad  who  chaired  the  Law\nCommission of India when the 14th Report was  given.  That  system  has  not\nworked well at all as noted from time to time.</span></span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_999\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_409\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_410\">(10) The National Commission to Review the Working of  the  Constitution  as\nwell as a responsible judge from the House of  Lords  were  of  the  opinion\nthat the procedure for appointment of judges as  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1756\">laid  down  in</a>  the  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1757\">Second\nJudges</a> case and  the  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1758\">Third  Judges</a>  case  broadly  serves  the  purpose  of\nmaintaining the independence of  the  judiciary  and  providing  a  suitable\nmethod for appointment of judges of the superior Courts.</span></span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1000\">184.  This is not to say that the \u2018collegium system\u2019 is perfect. Hardly  so.\nDuring the course of hearing, some critical comments were made  with  regard\nto the appointment of some judges to this Court which, it was  submitted  by\nthe learned Attorney-General would not have been possible were  it  not  for\nthe failure of the collegium system. Even the petitioners were  critical  of\nthe collegium system. However, I must express my anguish at  the  manner  in\nwhich an \u2018attack\u2019 was launched by some learned  counsel  appearing  for  the\nrespondents. It was  vitriolic  at  times,  lacking  discretion  and  wholly\nunnecessary. Denigrating judges is the easiest thing to  do  \u2013  they  cannot\nfight back \u2013 and the surest way to  ensure  that  the  judiciary  loses  its\nindependence and the people lose  confidence  in  the  judiciary,  which  is\nhardly advisable. The Bar has  an  equal  (if  not  greater)  stake  in  the\nindependence of the judiciary  and  the  silence  of  the  Bar  at  relevant\nmoments  is  inexplicable.  The  solution,  in  the  larger  canvas,  is   a\ndemocratic audit, an audit limited to the judiciary and the Rule of Law.  If\nsome positive developments can  be  incorporated  in  the  justice  delivery\nsystem (in the larger context) they should be so incorporated.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1001\">185.  In this context,  it  is  interesting  to  recall  the  words  of  Dr.\nAmbedkar on the working of the Constitution:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_627\">\u2018\u2026 however good a Constitution may be, it is sure to turn  out  bad  because\nthose who are called to work it, happen to be  a  bad  lot.  However  bad  a\nConstitution may be, it may turn out to be good if those who are  called  to\nwork it, happen to be a good lot. The working of  a  Constitution  does  not\ndepend wholly upon the nature of the Constitution.\u201d[359]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1002\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_411\">186.  Both the \u2018absolute executive primacy\u2019 system or the  \u2018ultimate  power\u2019\ntheory and the \u2018collegium system\u2019 of appointment of judges  of  the  Supreme\nCourt and the High  Courts  were  acceptable  systems  in  their  time.  The\n\u2018executive primacy\u2019 system was, unfortunately, abused by the  executive  and\nthe judiciary could do precious little about it, bound as the judges are  by\nthe Rule of Law.  It is  because  of  this  abuse  that  the  constitutional\nprovisions were revisited at the instance of the Bar of this Court  and  the\nrevisit gave the correct interpretational insight  into  our  constitutional\nhistory and the constitutional provisions. It is this insight that  resulted\nin the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1759\">Second Judges</a> case and a meaningful and pragmatic  interpretation  of\nthe Constitution.</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1003\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_412\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_413\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_414\">187.  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1760\">That the Second Judges</a> case was correctly decided by the majority  was\naccepted in the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1761\">Third Judges</a> case by the Attorney-General and, what is  more\nimportant, by the President (aided and advised by the Council of  Ministers)\nwho did not seek a reversal of the dicta <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1762\">laid  down  in</a>  the  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1763\">Second  Judges</a>\ncase.</span></span></span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1004\">188.  To say, as was conveyed to us during the hearing  of  the  case,  that\nthe collegium system has failed and that it needs replacement would  not  be\na correct or a fair post mortem. It is true that there  has  been  criticism\n(sometimes scathing) of the decisions of the collegium, but it must  not  be\nforgotten that the executive had an equally important participative role  in\nthe integrated process of the appointment  of  judges.  That  the  executive\nadopted a defeatist or an I-don\u2019t-care attitude  is  most  unfortunate.  The\ncollegium cannot be blamed for all the ills in the appointment of  judges  -\nthe political executive has to share the blame equally if  not  more,  since\nit mortgaged its constitutional responsibility of  maintaining  a  check  on\nwhat may be described as the erroneous decisions of the collegium.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1005\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_415\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_416\">189.  To say that the executive had no role to play  (as  was  suggested  to\nus) is incorrect to say the least, as is clear from a close reading  of  the\n<a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1764\">Second Judges</a> case and the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1765\">Third Judges</a> case.  Even the  President  did  not\nthink so. In fact, the  President  was  clearly  of  the  opinion  that  the\nexecutive or at least  the  Head  of  State  had  a  role  to  play  in  the\nappointment of judges. This evident from an article titled  \u201cMerit\u201d  in  the\nAppointment of Judges\u2019[360] which  quotes  from  an  issue  of  India  Today\nmagazine of 25th January, 1999 the following noting made  by  the  President\nconcerning the appointment of judges of the Supreme Court:</span></span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_628\">\u201cI would like to record my views that while recommending the appointment  of\nSupreme Court judges, it would be consonant with  constitutional  principles\nand the nation&#x27;s social objectives if persons belonging to  weaker  sections\nof society like SCs and STs, who comprise 25 per  cent  of  the  population,\nand  women  are  given  due  consideration.  Eligible  persons  from   these\ncategories  are   available   and   their   under-representation   or   non-\nrepresentation would not be justifiable. Keeping vacancies unfilled is  also\nnot desirable given the need for representation  of  different  sections  of\nsociety and the volume of work the Supreme Court is required to handle.\u201d\n\nThe Chief Justice of India is reported to have responded as follows:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_629\">\u201cI would like to  assert  that  merit  alone  has  been  the  criterion  for\nselection of Judges  and  no  discrimination  has  been  done  while  making\nappointments. All eligible candidates,  including  those  belonging  to  the\nScheduled Castes and Tribes, are considered by us while  recommending  names\nfor appointment as Supreme Court Judges.  Our  Constitution  envisages  that\nmerit alone is the criterion for all appointments to the Supreme  Court  and\nHigh Courts. And we  are  scrupulously  adhering  to  these  provisions.  An\nunfilled vacancy may not cause as much harm as a wrongly filled vacancy.\u201d\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1006\">190.  All that was needed to keep the collegium system on the rails was  the\nunstinted cooperation of  the  executive  and  an  effective  implementation\nstrategy, with serious and meaningful introspection and  perhaps  some  fine\ntuning and tweaking to make it more effective. Unfortunately, the  executive\ndid not respond positively, perhaps  due  to  its  misunderstanding  of  the\ndecisions of this Court.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1007\">191.   On  the  other  hand,  an  independent  and  impartial  jurist,  Lord\nTempleman  praised  the  integrated  consultative   collegium   system   and\nrecommended it as a method that the British  could  follow  with  advantage.\nThe learned judge wrote:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_630\">\u201cHowever, having regard to the earlier experience in India  of  attempts  by\nthe executive to influence the personalities and  attitudes  of  members  of\nthe judiciary,  and  having  regard  to  the  successful  attempts  made  in\nPakistan to control the judiciary, and  having  regard  to  the  unfortunate\nresults of the appointment of Supreme Court judges of the United  States  by\nthe President subject to approval by Congress, the majority decision of  the\nSupreme Court of India in the Advocates  on  Record  case  marks  a  welcome\nassertion of the independence of the judiciary and is  the  best  method  of\nobtaining appointments of integrity and quality, a  precedent  method  which\nthe British could follow with advantage.\u201d[361]\n\n\nWhile others shower praise on our system of appointment of  judges,  we  can\nonly heap scorn!\n\n\n\n\n<span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_417\">Preliminary issue \u2013 reconsideration of the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1766\">Second Judges</a> case and the  Third\nJudges case</span>\n\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1008\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_418\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_419\">192.  With this rather detailed history, the preliminary  objections  raised\nby the learned Attorney-General need consideration.  The  learned  Attorney-\nGeneral  raised  three  preliminary  issues:  (1)  The  writ  petitions  are\npremature and not maintainable since the  99th  Constitution  Amendment  Act\nand the NJAC Act have not come  into  force;  (2)  The  writ  petitions  are\npremature and not maintainable  since  the  National  Judicial  Appointments\nCommission has not been constituted and so there is  no  adverse  impact  of\nthe 99th Constitution Amendment Act and the NJAC  and  no  facts  have  been\npleaded by the petitioners in this regard; (3) This batch of cases ought  to\nbe heard by a Bench of 9 (nine) or more judges since the  decision  of  this\nCourt in the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1767\">Second Judges</a> case[362] and the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1768\">Third Judges</a> case[363]  do  not\nlay down the correct law but require reconsideration. It was submitted  that\nthe decisions have the effect of usurping the powers of the President  under\n<a href=\"/doc/1995948/\" id=\"a_1769\">Article  124(2)</a>  and  <a href=\"/doc/259647/\" id=\"a_1770\">Article  217(1)</a>  of  the  Constitution  and  that  the\njudiciary has  effectively  converted  the  appointment  of  judges  to  the\nSupreme Court and the High Courts from \u2018consultation\u2019 between the  President\nand the Chief Justice of India  (as  occurring  in  <a href=\"/doc/1995948/\" id=\"a_1771\">Article  124(2)</a>  of  the\nConstitution) into \u2018concurrence\u2019 of the Chief Justice of  India  and  giving\nbirth to a \u2018right to insist\u2019 on the acceptance of a  recommendation  of  the\nChief Justice of India. Moreover,  the  doctrine  of  separation  of  powers\nbetween the Legislature, the Executive and the  Judiciary  has  been  thrown\noverboard as also  the  system  of  checks  and  balances  inherent  in  the\nConstitution. To decide  this  particular  preliminary  issue,  the  learned\nAttorney-General referred to the separation of powers in  our  Constitution,\nthe law and the principles on which this  Court  should  proceed  to  decide\nwhether an earlier or prior decision rendered requires to be reconsidered.</span></span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_1009\">193.  As far as the first preliminary objection is concerned, it was  raised\nbefore the 99th Constitution Amendment  Act  and  the  NJAC  Act  came  into\nforce. Now the  preliminary  objection  does  not  survive  since  the  99th\nConstitution Amendment Act and the NJAC Act have in fact been  brought  into\nforce. The second preliminary objection has no substance since the  question\nin these petitions relates to the basic structure of  the  Constitution  and\nthe independence of the judiciary. It would be facetious  to  say  that  the\nwrit petitions should have been filed after an adverse  impact  is  felt  by\nthe alteration of the basic structure of  the  Constitution  and  after  the\nindependence of the judiciary is bartered  away.  If  the  petitioners  were\nexpected to wait that long it would perhaps be too late. That  apart,  since\nwe have heard these petitions at length, it is  advisable  to  pronounce  on\nthe substantive issues  raised.  Really  speaking,  it  is  only  the  third\npreliminary objection that needs consideration.\n</p>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_420\">1</span>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1010\">2 The third preliminary objection and the separation of powers\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1011\">194.  The issue of the separation of powers has been the subject  matter  of\ndiscussion in several cases. Broadly, the consistent view of this Court  has\nbeen that while the Constitution recognizes the separation of powers, it  is\nnot a rigid separation and there is some overlap.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1012\">195.  <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1318432\" id=\"span_421\">In <a href=\"/doc/1318432/\" id=\"a_1772\">Ram Jawaya Kapur v. State of  Punjab</a>[364]  it  was  held  by  Chief\nJustice Mukherjea speaking for this Court:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_631\">\u201cIt may not be possible to frame an exhaustive definition of what  executive\nfunction means and implies. Ordinarily  the  executive  power  connotes  the\nresidue  of  governmental  functions  that  remain  after  legislative   and\njudicial functions are taken away. The Indian Constitution  has  not  indeed\nrecognised the doctrine of separation of powers  in  its  absolute  rigidity\nbut the functions of the different parts or branches of the Government  have\nbeen sufficiently differentiated and consequently it can very well  be  said\nthat our Constitution does not contemplate assumption, by one organ or  part\nof  the  State,  of  functions  that  essentially  belong  to  another.  The\nexecutive indeed can exercise the  powers  of  departmental  or  subordinate\nlegislation when such powers are delegated to it by the legislature. It  can\nalso, when so empowered exercise judicial functions in a  limited  way.  The\nexecutive Government, however, can never go against the  provisions  of  the\nConstitution or of any law.\u201d[365]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1013\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"257876\" id=\"span_422\">196.  The separation of powers in our Constitution is not  as  rigid  as  in\nthe United States. One of the elements of the separation of  powers  is  the\nsystem of checks and balances. This too is recognized  by  our  Constitution\nand <a href=\"/doc/1712542/\" id=\"a_1773\">Article 226</a> and <a href=\"/doc/981147/\" id=\"a_1774\">Article 32 (judicial review)</a> is one of the  features  of\nchecks and balances. It was so held  in  <a href=\"/doc/257876/\" id=\"a_1775\">Kesavananda  Bharati  v.  State  of\nKerala</a>[366] where it was said  by  Justice  Shelat  and  Justice  Grover  as\nfollows:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_632\">\u201cThere is ample evidence in the Constitution  itself  to  indicate  that  it\ncreates a system of checks and balances by reason of  which  powers  are  so\ndistributed that none of the three organs it sets  up  can  become  so  pre-\ndominant as to disable the others from  exercising  and  discharging  powers\nand functions entrusted to them. Though the Constitution does not  lay  down\nthe principle of separation of powers in all its rigidity as is the case  in\nthe United States Constitution yet it  envisages  such  a  separation  to  a\ndegree as was found in Ranasinghe case.[367] The  judicial  review  provided\nexpressly in our Constitution by means of <a href=\"/doc/1712542/\" id=\"a_1776\">Articles 226</a> and <a href=\"/doc/981147/\" id=\"a_1777\">32</a> is one of  the\nfeatures upon which hinges the system of checks and balances.\u201d[368]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1014\">197.  <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"936707\" id=\"span_423\">In <a href=\"/doc/936707/\" id=\"a_1778\">Indira Nehru Gandhi v. Raj Narain</a>[369] the constitutional  validity\nof the <a href=\"/doc/36314713/\" id=\"a_1779\">Constitution (Thirty-ninth Amendment) Act, 1975</a>  was  challenged.  By\nthis Amendment Act, <a href=\"/doc/169871995/\" id=\"a_1780\">Article 39-A</a> was inserted in the  Constitution  and  the\nchallenge was, inter alia, to clause (4) thereof.[370] While  striking  down\nthe offending clause, it was held by Justice H.R. Khanna:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_633\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"959306\" id=\"span_424\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"293632\" id=\"span_425\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"968805\" id=\"span_426\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1018531\" id=\"span_427\">\u201cA declaration that an order made by a court of  law  is  void  is  normally\npart of the judicial function and is not a  legislative  function.  Although\nthere is in the Constitution of India no rigid separation of powers, by  and\nlarge the spheres of judicial function and legislative  function  have  been\ndemarcated and it is not permissible for the legislature  to  encroach  upon\nthe judicial sphere. It has accordingly been held that a  legislature  while\nit is entitled to change with retrospective effect the law which formed  the\nbasis of the judicial decision, it is not permissible to the legislature  to\ndeclare the judgment of the court to  be  void  or  not  binding  (see  <a href=\"/doc/1018531/\" id=\"a_1781\">Shri\nPrithvi Cotton Mills Ltd. v. Broach Borough  Municipality</a>,  <a href=\"/doc/968805/\" id=\"a_1782\">Janapada  Sabha,\nChhindwara v. Central Provinces Syndicate  Ltd.</a>,  <a href=\"/doc/293632/\" id=\"a_1783\">Municipal  Corporation  of\nthe City of Ahmedabad v. New Shorock Spg. &amp;  Wvg.  Co.  Ltd.</a>  and  <a href=\"/doc/959306/\" id=\"a_1784\">State  of\nTamil Nadu v. M. Rayappa Gounder</a>).\u201d[371] (Internal citations omitted).</span></span></span></span>\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1015\">198.  Justice Mathew held that ours is a cooperative  federalism  that  does\nnot contain any rigid separation of powers and  there  exists  a  system  of\nchecks and balances. Harold Laski was quoted as saying that  \u2018Separation  of\npowers does not mean the equal balance of powers.\u2019[372] In that  context  it\nwas held  that  the  exercise  of  judicial  power  by  the  Legislature  is\nimpermissible. The learned judge expressed the view that:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_634\">\u201cMontesquieu was the first to conceive of the three functions of  Government\nas exercised by three organs, each juxtaposed against  others.  He  realised\nthat the efficient operation of Government  involved  a  certain  degree  of\noverlapping and that the theory of checks and balances required  each  organ\nto impede too great an aggrandizement of authority by the other two  powers.\nAs Holdsworth says, Montesquieu convinced the world that he  had  discovered\na new constitutional principle which was universally valid. The doctrine  of\nseparation of governmental powers is not a mere  theoretical,  philosophical\nconcept.  It  is  a  practical,  work-a-day  principle.  The   division   of\nGovernment into three branches does not imply, as its critics would have  us\nthink, three  watertight  compartments.  Thus,  legislative  impeachment  of\nexecutive officers or judges,  executive  veto  over  legislation,  judicial\nreview of administrative or  legislative  actions  are  treated  as  partial\nexceptions which need explanation.\u201d[373]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1016\">199.  Justice Y.V. Chandrachud made a distinction between the separation  of\npowers as understood in the United States and Australia  and  as  understood\nin India and expressed the following view in this regard:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_635\">\u201cThe American Constitution provides for a rigid separation  of  governmental\npowers into three basic divisions, the executive, legislative and  judicial.\nIt is an essential principle of that Constitution that powers  entrusted  to\none department  should  not  be  exercised  by  any  other  department.  The\nAustralian Constitution follows the same pattern of distribution of  powers.\nUnlike these Constitutions, the Indian Constitution does not expressly  vest\nthe three kinds of power in three different organs of  the  State.  But  the\nprinciple of separation of powers is not a magic  formula  for  keeping  the\nthree organs of the State within the strict confines of their functions.  As\nobserved by Cardozo,  J.  in  his  dissenting  opinion  in  Panama  Refining\nCompany v. Ryan[374] the  principle  of  separation  of  powers  \u201cis  not  a\ndoctrinaire concept to be made use of with pedantic rigour.  There  must  be\nsensible approximation, there must be elasticity of adjustment  in  response\nto the practical necessities of Government which cannot  foresee  today  the\ndevelopments of tomorrow in their nearly infinite variety\u201d.  Thus,  even  in\nAmerica, despite the theory that the legislature cannot delegate  its  power\nto the executive, a host  of  rules  and  regulations  are  passed  by  non-\nlegislative bodies, which have been judicially recognized as valid.\u201d[375]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1017\">200.  <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1939993\" id=\"span_428\">In <a href=\"/doc/1939993/\" id=\"a_1785\">Minerva Mills Ltd. v. Union of India</a>[376] Justice  Bhagwati  opined\nthat the Constitution has devised a structure for the separation  of  powers\nand checks and balances and held:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_636\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"257876\" id=\"span_429\">\u201cIt is clear from the majority decision in  <a href=\"/doc/257876/\" id=\"a_1786\">Kesavananda  Bharati</a>  case  that\nour Constitution is a controlled Constitution which confers  powers  on  the\nvarious authorities created and recognised by it and defines the  limits  of\nthose powers. The Constitution is suprema lex,  the  paramount  law  of  the\nland and there is no authority, no department or branch of the  State  which\nis  above  or  beyond  the  Constitution  or  has  powers   unfettered   and\nunrestricted by the Constitution. The Constitution has devised  a  structure\nof power relationship with checks and balances and limits are placed on  the\npowers of every authority or instrumentality under the  Constitution.  Every\norgan of  the  State,  be  it  the  executive  or  the  legislature  or  the\njudiciary, derives its authority from the Constitution and  it  has  to  act\nwithin the limits of such authority.\u201d[377]</span>\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1018\">201.  A little later, it was observed by the learned judge:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_637\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1569395\" id=\"span_430\">\u201cIt is a fundamental principle of our  constitutional  scheme,  and  I  have\npointed this out in the preceding paragraph, that every organ of the  State,\nevery  authority  under  the  Constitution,  derives  its  power  from   the\nConstitution and has to act within the limits of such power.  But  then  the\nquestion arises as to which authority must decide what  are  the  limits  on\nthe power conferred upon each organ or  instrumentality  of  the  State  and\nwhether such limits are transgressed or exceeded. Now there are  three  main\ndepartments of  the  State  amongst  which  the  powers  of  government  are\ndivided; the  executive,  the  legislature  and  the  judiciary.  Under  our\nConstitution we have no rigid separation of powers as in the  United  States\nof America, but there is a broad demarcation, though, having regard  to  the\ncomplex nature of governmental functions, certain degree of  overlapping  is\ninevitable. The reason for this broad separation  of  powers  is  that  \u201cthe\nconcentration of powers in  any  one  organ  may\u201d  to  quote  the  words  of\nChandrachud, J., (as he then was) in <a href=\"/doc/1569395/\" id=\"a_1787\">Indira Gandhi</a> case \u2018by  upsetting  that\nfine balance between the three organs, destroy the fundamental  premises  of\na democratic government to which we are pledged\u2019.\u201d[378]</span>\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1019\">202.  <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"322504\" id=\"span_431\">In <a href=\"/doc/322504/\" id=\"a_1788\">I.R. Coelho v. State of  Tamil  Nadu</a>[379]  it  was  held  by  Chief\nJustice Sabharwal speaking for the Court that the doctrine of separation  of\npowers is a part of the basic structure of the Constitution. It was held:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_638\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"257876\" id=\"span_432\">\u201cThe separation of powers between Legislature, Executive and  the  Judiciary\nconstitutes basic structure, has been found in <a href=\"/doc/257876/\" id=\"a_1789\">Kesavananda Bharati</a>  case  by\nthe majority.</span> <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1569395\" id=\"span_433\">Later, it was  reiterated  in  <a href=\"/doc/1569395/\" id=\"a_1790\">Indira  Gandhi</a>  case.  A  large\nnumber of judgments have reiterated that the separation of powers is one  of\nthe basic features of the Constitution.\u201d[380]</span>\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1020\">203.  <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"693176\" id=\"span_434\">In  <a href=\"/doc/693176/\" id=\"a_1791\">Bhim Singh v. Union of India</a>[381] it was held that  separation  of\npowers is an essential feature of the Constitution and in modern  governance\nstrict separation is neither possible nor desirable. There is  no  violation\nof the principle of separation of powers if  there  is  an  overlap  of  the\nfunction of one branch of governance with another, but if one  branch  takes\nover an essential function of another branch, then there is a  violation  of\nthe principle. It was  observed  by  Justice  Sathasivam  speaking  for  the\nCourt, while considering the  constitutional  validity  of  the  Members  of\nParliament Local Area Development Scheme:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_639\">\u201cThe concept  of  separation  of  powers,  even  though  not  found  in  any\nparticular  constitutional  provision,  is  inherent  in  the   polity   the\nConstitution has adopted. The aim of separation of powers is to achieve  the\nmaximum extent of accountability of each branch of the Government.\nWhile understanding this concept [of  separation  of  powers],  two  aspects\nmust be borne in mind. One,  that  separation  of  powers  is  an  essential\nfeature of the Constitution.  Two,  that  in  modern  governance,  a  strict\nseparation is neither  possible,  nor  desirable.  Nevertheless,  till  this\nprinciple  of  accountability  is  preserved,  there  is  no  violation   of\nseparation of powers. We arrive at the same conclusion when  we  assess  the\nposition within the constitutional text. The Constitution does not  prohibit\noverlap  of  functions,  but  in  fact  provides  for  some  overlap  as   a\nparliamentary democracy. But what it prohibits is such exercise of  function\nof the other branch  which  results  in  wresting  away  of  the  regime  of\nconstitutional accountability.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_640\">Thus, the test for the violation of separation of powers must  be  precisely\nthis. A law would be violative of separation of powers not if it results  in\nsome overlap of functions of different branches of  the  State,  but  if  it\ntakes over an essential function of the other branch  leading  to  lapse  in\nconstitutional accountability.\u201d[382]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1021\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"41744252\" id=\"span_435\">204.  Finally, in <a href=\"/doc/41744252/\" id=\"a_1792\">State of Tamil Nadu v. State of Kerala</a>[383]  there  is  an\nelaborate discussion on the separation of powers with reference  to  several\ncases decided by this Court.</span><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1649309\" id=\"span_436\">[384] It was held therein that in  view  of  the\ndoctrine of the separation of powers (and for other  reasons  as  well)  the\n<a href=\"/doc/134026170/\" id=\"a_1793\">Kerala Irrigation and Water Conservation (Amendment)  Act,  2006</a>  passed  by\nthe Kerala Legislature is unconstitutional since it  seeks  to  nullify  the\ndecision of this Court in <a href=\"/doc/1649309/\" id=\"a_1794\">Mullaperiyar  Environmental  Protection  Forum  v.\nUnion of India</a>.[385]</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1022\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_437\">205.   The  submission  of  the  learned  Attorney-General  was   that   the\nappointment of a judge of the Supreme Court or a High Court is an  executive\nfunction and this has been so held even in the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1795\">Second Judges</a>  case.  Justice\nAhmadi held that the appointment of judges is an executive function[386]  as\ndid  Justice  Verma.</span><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_438\">[387]  By  an  unsustainable   interpretation   of   the\nConstitution (an interpretation which, according to  the  learned  Attorney-\nGeneral must have made Dr. Ambedkar  turn  in  his  grave),  this  executive\nfunction has been taken over or usurped by the judiciary  and  that  is  the\nreason why the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1796\">Second Judges</a>  case  requires  to  be  reconsidered  and  the\ncorrect constitutional position deserves to be restored. In other words,  by\na process of judicial encroachment, the separation of power theory has  been\nbroken down by this Court, in  violation  of  the  basic  structure  of  the\nConstitution.</span>\n</p>\n\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1023\">Constituent Assembly Debates and the third preliminary issue\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1024\">206.  <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Neg\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_439\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Neg\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_440\">In further support of his contention that the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1797\">Second Judges</a>  case  and\nthe  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1798\">Third  Judges</a>  case  do  not  lay  down  the  correct  law   and   need\nreconsideration, the learned Attorney-General placed great reliance  on  the\nCAD. It is necessary, therefore, to consider the  law  on  the  subject  and\nthen the debates.</span></span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1025\">207.  <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"427436\" id=\"span_441\">In <a href=\"/doc/427436/\" id=\"a_1799\">Administrator-General of Bengal v. Prem Lal Mullick</a>[388] the  Privy\nCouncil did not approve of a reference to debates in the  Legislature  as  a\nlegitimate aid to the construction of a statute. It was held:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_641\">\u201cTheir Lordships observe that the two learned  Judges  who  constituted  the\nmajority in the Appellate Court, although they do not  base  their  judgment\nupon them, refer to the proceedings of the  Legislature  which  resulted  in\nthe passing of the Act of 1874 [Administrator-General\u2019s Act]  as  legitimate\naids to the construction of Section 31. Their Lordships think  it  right  to\nexpress their dissent from that proposition. The same reasons which  exclude\nthese considerations when the clauses of an Act of the  British  Legislature\nare under  construction  are  equally  cogent  in  the  case  of  an  Indian\nstatute.\u201d\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1026\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1857950\" id=\"span_442\">208.  This view was partially accepted, with reference to the  CAD  in  <a href=\"/doc/1857950/\" id=\"a_1800\">A.K.\nGopalan v. State of Madras</a>[389] by Chief  Justice  Harilal  Kania  who  held\nthat reference may be made to the CAD  with  great  caution  and  only  when\n\u2018latent ambiguities are to be  resolved.\u2019[390]  The  learned  Chief  Justice\nobserved:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_642\">\u201cOur attention  was  drawn  to  the  debates  and  report  of  the  drafting\ncommittee of the Constituent Assembly in respect  of  the  wording  of  this\nclause. The report may be read not to control the meaning  of  the  article,\nbut may be seen in case of ambiguity. In Municipal Council of Sydney v.  The\nCommonwealth[391] it was thought that individual opinion of members  of  the\nConvention expressed in the debate cannot be referred to for the purpose  of\nconstruing the Constitution.  The  same  opinion  was  expressed  in  United\nStates v.
