Wong Kim Ark.[392] The result appears to be that while it  is  not\nproper to take into consideration the  individual  opinions  of  Members  of\nParliament or Convention to construe the meaning of the  particular  clause,\nwhen a question is raised whether a certain phrase or expression was up  for\nconsideration at all or not, a reference to the debates  may  be  permitted.\nIn the present case the debates were referred to show  that  the  expression\n\u201cdue process of law\u201d was known to exist in  the  American  Constitution  and\nafter a discussion was not  adopted  by  the  Constituent  Assembly  in  our\nConstitution. <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Neutral\" data-docid=\"427436\" id=\"span_443\">In <a href=\"/doc/427436/\" id=\"a_1801\">Administrator  General  of  Bengal  v.  Premlal  Mullick</a>  a\nreference to the proceedings  of  the  legislature  which  resulted  in  the\npassing of the Act was not considered legitimate aid in the construction  of\na particular section. The same reasons were held as cogent for  excluding  a\nreference to such debates in construing an Indian  statute.  Resort  may  be\nhad to these sources with great caution and  only  when  latent  ambiguities\nare to be resolved.\u201d[393]</span>\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1027\">209.  This view was endorsed by Fazl Ali, J who referred to  the  expression\n\u2018due process of law\u2019 which was originally interpreted by the  United  States\nSupreme Court as referring to matters  of  procedure  but  was  subsequently\nwidened to cover substantive law as well. The learned judge held:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_643\">\u201cIn the course of the arguments, the learned  Attorney-General  referred  us\nto the proceedings in the Constituent Assembly for the  purpose  of  showing\nthat the article as originally drafted  contained  the  words  \u201cwithout  due\nprocess of law\u201d but these words were  subsequently  replaced  by  the  words\n\u201cexcept according to procedure established by law\u201d. In  my  opinion,  though\nthe proceedings or discussions in the Assembly  are  not  relevant  for  the\npurpose of construing the meaning of the expressions  used  in  <a href=\"/doc/1199182/\" id=\"a_1802\">Article  21</a>,\nespecially when they are plain and unambiguous, they are  relevant  to  show\nthat the Assembly intended to avoid the use of the expression  \u201cwithout  due\nprocess of law\u201d\u2026\u2026.  In  the  earliest  times,  the  American  Supreme  Court\nconstrued \u201cdue process of law\u201d to  cover  matters  of  procedure  only,  but\ngradually the  meaning  of  the  expression  was  widened  so  as  to  cover\nsubstantive law also, by laying emphasis on the word \u201cdue\u201d.[394]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1028\">210.  Justice Patanjali Sastri was of the same opinion and  so  the  learned\njudge held as follows:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_644\">\u201cLearned counsel drew attention to the speeches made by several  members  of\nthe Assembly on the floor of the House for explaining, as  he  put  it,  the\n\u201chistorical background\u201d. A speech made in the course  of  the  debate  on  a\nbill could at best be indicative of the subjective intent  of  the  speaker,\nbut it could not reflect the inarticulate mental processes lying behind  the\nmajority vote which carried the bill. Nor is it reasonable  to  assume  that\nthe minds of all those legislators were in  accord.  The  Court  could  only\nsearch for the objective intent of the legislature primarily  in  the  words\nused in the enactment, aided by  such  historical  material  as  reports  of\nstatutory committees, preambles etc. I attach no importance,  therefore,  to\nthe speeches made by some of the members of the Constituent Assembly in  the\ncourse of the debate on <a href=\"/doc/609295/\" id=\"a_1803\">Article 15 (now <a href=\"/doc/1199182/\" id=\"a_1804\">Article 21</a>)</a>\u201d.[395]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1029\">211.  Justice  Mukherjea noted  the  concession  of  the  learned  Attorney-\nGeneral that the CAD are not admissible to explain the meaning of the  words\nused \u2013 a position quite the opposite from what is now taken by  the  learned\nAttorney-General. The  learned  judge  then  observed  that  such  extrinsic\nevidence is best left out of account and held as follows:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_645\">\u201cThe learned Attorney-General has  placed  before  us  the  debates  in  the\nConstituent Assembly centering round the adoption of this recommendation  of\nthe Drafting Committee and he has referred us to  the  speeches  of  several\nmembers of the Assembly who played an important part in the shaping  of  the\nConstitution. As  an  aid  to  discover  the  meaning  of  the  words  in  a\nConstitution, these debates are of doubtful value. \u201cResort  can  be  had  to\nthem\u201d&#x27;  says  Willoughby,  \u201cwith  great  caution  and   only   when   latent\nambiguities are to be solved. The proceedings may  be  of  some  value  when\nthey clearly point out the purpose of the provision. But when  the  question\nis of abstract meaning, it will be difficult  to  derive  from  this  source\nmuch material assistance in interpretation.\u201d\nThe learned Attorney-General concedes that these debates are not  admissible\nto explain the meaning of the words used and he wanted to use them only  for\nthe purpose of showing that  the  Constituent  Assembly  when  they  finally\nadopted the recommendation of the Drafting Committee, were  fully  aware  of\nthe implications of the differences between the old form of  expression  and\nthe new. In my opinion, in interpreting the Constitution, it will be  better\nif such extrinsic evidence is left out of account.  In  matters  like  this,\ndifferent members act upon different impulses  and  from  different  motives\nand it is quite possible that some  members  accepted  certain  words  in  a\nparticular sense, while others took them in a different light.\u201d[396]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1030\">212.  Justice S.R. Das specifically stated that he expresses no  opinion  on\nthe question of admissibility or otherwise  of  the  CAD  to  interpret  the\nConstitution.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1031\">213.  <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1334665\" id=\"span_444\">In <a href=\"/doc/1334665/\" id=\"a_1805\">State of Travancore-Cochin v.  The  Bombay  Co.  Ltd.</a>[397]  it  was\nunanimously held that reference to  the  CAD  is  unwarranted  and  such  an\nextrinsic aid to the interpretation of statutes is not admissible.  Speaking\nfor the Court, Chief Justice Patanjali Sastri held:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_646\">\u201cIt remains only to point out that the use made by the learned Judges  below\nof the speeches made by the members  of  the  Constituent  Assembly  in  the\ncourse of the debates on the draft Constitution is  unwarranted.  That  this\nform of extrinsic aid to the interpretation of statutes  is  not  admissible\nhas been generally accepted in England, and the same rule has been  observed\nin the construction  of  Indian  statutes  \u2014  see  Administrator-General  of\nBengal v. Prem Lal Mallick. <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1857950\" id=\"span_445\">The reason behind the rule was explained by  one\nof us in <a href=\"/doc/1857950/\" id=\"a_1806\">Gopalan</a> case thus:</span>\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_647\">\u201cA speech made in the course of the debate  on  a  bill  could  at  best  be\nindicative of the subjective  intent  of  the  speaker,  but  it  could  not\nreflect the inarticulate mental  process  lying  behind  the  majority  vote\nwhich carried the bill. Nor is it reasonable to assume  that  the  minds  of\nall those legislators were in accord,\u201d\nor, as it is more tersely put in an American case\u2014\n\u201cThose who did not speak may not have agreed with those who did;  and  those\nwho spoke might differ from each other \u2014  United  States  v.  Trans-Missouri\nFreight Association.[398]\u201d[399]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1032\">214.  <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"21266288\" id=\"span_446\">In <a href=\"/doc/21266288/\" id=\"a_1807\">Golak Nath v. State of Punjab</a>[400] Chief Justice  Subba  Rao  noted\nthe submissions of the petitioners, one of which was:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_648\">\u201cThe debates in the Constituent Assembly,  particularly  the  speech  of  Mr\nJawahar Lal Nehru, the first Prime Minister of India, and the  reply  of  Dr\nAmbedkar, who piloted the Bill  disclose  clearly  that  it  was  never  the\nintention of the makers of the Constitution by putting  in  <a href=\"/doc/594125/\" id=\"a_1808\">Article  368</a>  to\nenable the Parliament to repeal the fundamental  rights;  the  circumstances\nunder which the amendment moved by Mr H.V. Kamath, one  of  the  members  of\nConstituent Assembly, was withdrawn and <a href=\"/doc/594125/\" id=\"a_1809\">Article  368</a>  was  finally  adopted,\nsupport the contention that amendment of Part III is outside  the  scope  of\nArticle 368.\u201d[401]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_1033\">215.  The submissions of the learned Attorney-General were  also  noted  and\none of which was,  again,  diametrically  opposed  to  the  submission  made\nbefore us by the learned Attorney-General:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_649\">\u201cDebates in the Constituent Assembly cannot be relied  upon  for  construing\n<a href=\"/doc/594125/\" id=\"a_1810\">Article 368</a> of the Constitution and even if they can be,  there  is  nothing\nin the debates to prove positively that  fundamental  rights  were  excluded\nfrom amendment.\u201d[402]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1034\">216.  The learned Chief Justice (speaking for the majority) referred to  the\nCAD and observed:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_650\">\u201cWe have referred to  the  speeches  of  Pandit  Jawaharlal  Nehru  and  Dr.\nAmbedkar not with a view to interpret the provisions of <a href=\"/doc/594125/\" id=\"a_1811\">Art. 368</a>,  which  we\npropose to do on its own  terms,  but  only  to  notice  the  transcendental\ncharacter  given  to  the  fundamental  rights  by  two  of  the   important\narchitects of the Constitution.\u201d[403]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1035\">217.  Justice Wanchoo dealt with the issue a bit more elaborately and  on  a\nconsideration of the law (drawing support from Prem  Lal  Mullick  and  A.K.\nGopalan) held that the  CAD  could  not  be  looked  into  for  interpreting\n<a href=\"/doc/594125/\" id=\"a_1812\">Article 368</a>  of  the  Constitution  and  that  the  said  Article  \u2018must  be\ninterpreted on the  words  thereof  as  they  finally  found  place  in  the\nConstitution.\u2019 It was said:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_651\">\u201cCopious references were made during the course of arguments to  debates  in\nParliament and it is urged that it is open to this Court to  look  into  the\ndebates in order to interpret <a href=\"/doc/594125/\" id=\"a_1813\">Article 368</a> to find out the intention  of  the\nConstitution-makers. We are of opinion that we cannot and  should  not  look\ninto the debates that took place in the Constituent  Assembly  to  determine\nthe interpretation of <a href=\"/doc/594125/\" id=\"a_1814\">Article 368</a> and the scope and extent of the  provision\ncontained therein.  It  may  be  conceded  that  historical  background  and\nperhaps what was accepted or what was rejected by the  Constituent  Assembly\nwhile the Constitution was being  framed,  may  be  taken  into  account  in\nfinding out the scope and extent of <a href=\"/doc/594125/\" id=\"a_1815\">Article 368.</a> But we have no  doubt  that\nwhat was spoken in the  debates  in  the  Constituent  Assembly  cannot  and\nshould not be looked into in order to interpret Article 368\u2026\u2026\u2026..\nWe are therefore of opinion that it is not possible  to  read  the  speeches\nmade in the Constituent Assembly in order to interpret  <a href=\"/doc/594125/\" id=\"a_1816\">Article  368</a>  or  to\ndefine its extent and scope and to determine what it takes in  and  what  it\ndoes not. As to the historical facts, namely, what was accepted or what  was\navoided in the Constituent Assembly in connection with <a href=\"/doc/594125/\" id=\"a_1817\">Article  368</a>,  it  is\nenough to say that we have not been able to find any help from the  material\nrelating to  this.  There  were  proposals  for  restricting  the  power  of\namendment under <a href=\"/doc/594125/\" id=\"a_1818\">Article 368</a> and making fundamental rights  immune  therefrom\nand there were counter proposals before the Constituent Assembly for  making\nthe power of amendment  all-embracing.  They  were  all  either  dropped  or\nnegatived and in the  circumstances  are  of  no  help  in  determining  the\ninterpretation of <a href=\"/doc/594125/\" id=\"a_1819\">Article  368</a>  which  must  be  interpreted  on  the  words\nthereof as they finally found place in the Constitution, and on those  words\nwe have no doubt that there are no implied limitations of any  kind  on  the\npower to amend given therein.\u201d[404]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1036\">218.  Justice Bachawat concluded his judgment by referring to the  issue  of\nthe CAD being an aid to  interpreting  the  Constitution.  In  rather  terse\nwords, the learned judge rejected the submission made  in  this  regard  and\nrelied upon State of Travancore-Cochin. This is what was said:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_652\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1334665\" id=\"span_447\">\u201cBefore concluding this judgment I must refer to some of the  speeches  made\nby the members of the Constituent Assembly in the course of debates  on  the\ndraft constitution. These speeches cannot be used as aids  for  interpreting\nthe Constitution. See <a href=\"/doc/1334665/\" id=\"a_1820\">State of Travancore-Cochin and others  v.  Bombay  Co.\nLtd. Accordingly, I</a> do not rely on them as aids to construction.\u201d[405]</span>\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1037\">219.  Justice Bachawat also  makes  a  rather  interesting  reference  to  a\nspecial article written by Sir B.N. Rau  (Constitutional  Adviser)  on  15th\nAugust, 1948.  Sir Benegal remarked:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_653\">\u201cIt seems rather illogical that a constitution should be settled  by  simple\nmajority by an assembly elected indirectly on a very limited  franchise  and\nthat it should not be capable  of  being  amended  in  the  same  way  by  a\nParliament elected - and perhaps for  the  most  part  elected  directly  by\nadult suffrage.\u201d[406]\n\nThis is mentioned, without any comment,  only  to  throw  open  the  thought\nwhether the interpretation of the Constitution can be tied down  forever  to\nthe views expressed by a few Hon\u2019ble Members of  the  Constituent  Assembly,\nwho were undoubtedly extremely learned and visionary  but  who  nevertheless\nconstituted \u2018an assembly elected indirectly on a very limited franchise\u2019.\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1038\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1235907\" id=\"span_448\">220.  In Kesavananda Bharati  it  was  held  by  Chief  Justice  Sikri  that\n\u2018speeches made by members of  the  legislature  in  the  course  of  debates\nrelating to  the  enactment  of  a  statute  cannot  be  used  as  aids  for\ninterpreting any provisions of the statute.\u2019 The learned Chief Justice  held\nthat the same rule is applicable to provisions of the Constitution  as  well\nand for this reliance was placed, inter  alia,  on  Prem  Lal  Mullick,  A.K\nGopalan, State of Travancore-Cochin and  Golak  Nath.  <a href=\"/doc/1235907/\" id=\"a_1821\">Explaining  Union  of\nIndia v. H.S. Dhillon</a>[407] the learned Chief Justice said:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_654\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1235907\" id=\"span_449\"><a href=\"/doc/1235907/\" id=\"a_1822\">\u201cIn Union of India v. H.S. Dhillon I</a>, on  behalf  of  the  majority,  before\nreferring to the speeches observed at p. 58 that \u201cwe are  however,  glad  to\nfind from the following extracts from the debates  that  our  interpretation\naccords with what was intended\u201d. There is no harm  in  finding  confirmation\nof one\u2019s interpretation in debates but it is  quite  a  different  thing  to\ninterpret  the  provisions  of  the  Constitution  in  the  light   of   the\ndebates.\u201d[408]</span>\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1039\">221.  Apart from relying on case law, the  learned  Chief  Justice  gave  an\nadditional reason for concluding that reliance on the CAD was not  advisable\nfor  interpreting  the  provisions  of  the  Constitution.  This   is   best\nunderstood in the words of the learned Chief Justice:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_655\">\u201cThere is an additional reason for not referring to debates for the  purpose\nof interpretation. The Constitution, as far as most  of  the  Indian  States\nwere concerned, came into operation only because of the  acceptance  [pic]by\nthe Ruler or Rajpramukh. This is borne out by  the  following  extract  from\nthe statement of Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel in  the  Constituent  Assembly  on\nOctober 12, 1949, (CAD, Vol. X, pp. 161-63):\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_656\">\u201cUnfortunately we have no properly constituted legislatures in the  rest  of\nthe States (apart from Mysore, Saurashtra and Travancore and  Cochin  Union)\nnor will it be possible to have legislatures constituted in them before  the\nConstitution of India emerges in its final  form.  We  have,  therefore,  no\noption but to make the Constitution operative in these States on  the  basis\nof its acceptance by the Rulers or the Rajpramukh, as the case may  be,  who\nwill no doubt consult his Council of Ministers.\u201d\nIn accordance with this statement, declarations were issued  by  the  Rulers\nor Rajpramukhs accepting the Constitution.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_657\">It seems to me that when a Ruler or Rajpramukh or the people  of  the  State\naccepted the Constitution of India in its final form, he did not  accept  it\nsubject to the speeches made during the Constituent  Assembly  debates.  The\nspeeches can, in my view, be relied on only in order to see  if  the  course\nof the progress of a particular provision or provisions throws any light  on\nthe historical background or shows that a common understanding or  agreement\nwas arrived at between certain sections of the people.\u201d[409]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1040\">222.  Justice Hegde and Justice A.K Mukherjea also held that reliance  could\nnot be placed on the CAD to interpret any  provision  of  the  Constitution.\nReference was made to State of Travancore-Cochin and it was held:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_658\">\u201cFor finding out the true scope of <a href=\"/doc/108209556/\" id=\"a_1823\">Article  31(2)</a>  as  it  stands  now,  the\nlearned Advocate-General of Maharashtra as  well  as  the  Solicitor-General\nhas taken us through the history of this  article.  According  to  them  the\narticle as it stands now truly represents the intention of the Constitution-\nmakers. In support of that contention, we  were  asked  to  go  through  the\nConstituent Assembly debates relating to  that  article.  In  particular  we\nwere invited to go through the speeches made by  Pandit  Nehru,  Sir  Alladi\nKrishnaswami Ayyar, Dr Munshi  and  Dr  Ambedkar.  In  our  opinion,  it  is\nimpermissible for us to do so. It is a  well-settled  rule  of  construction\nthat speeches made by members of a Legislature  in  the  course  of  debates\nrelating to  the  enactment  of  a  statute  cannot  be  used  as  aids  for\ninterpreting any of  the  provisions  of  the  statute.  The  same  rule  is\napplicable when we  are  called  upon  to  interpret  the  provisions  of  a\nConstitution.\u201d[410]\n\nThe learned judges observed that no decision was  brought  to  their  notice\ndissenting with the view mentioned above.\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1041\">223.  Justice H.R Khanna was also of the  opinion  that  the  CAD  could  be\nreferred only for the limited purpose of  determining  the  history  of  the\nconstitutional provision. The CAD \u2018cannot form the basis for construing  the\nprovisions of the Constitution.\u2019 The learned judge  further  said  that  the\nintention of the draftsman of a statute would have to be gathered  from  the\nwords used. The learned judge said:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_659\">\u201cThe speeches in the Constituent Assembly, in my opinion,  can  be  referred\nto  for  finding  the  history  of  the  Constitutional  provision  and  the\nbackground against which the said provision was drafted.  The  speeches  can\nalso shed light to show as to what was the mischief which was sought  to  be\nremedied and what was  the  object  which  was  sought  to  be  attained  in\ndrafting the provision. The speeches cannot, however,  form  the  basis  for\nconstruing the provisions of the Constitution. The task of interpreting  the\nprovision  of  the  Constitution  has  to  be  done  independently  and  the\nreference to the speeches made in the Constituent Assembly does not  absolve\nthe[pic]court from performing that task. The draftsmen are supposed to  have\nexpressed their intentions in the words used  by  them  in  the  provisions.\nThose words are  final  repositories  of  the  intention  and  it  would  be\nultimately from the words  of  the  provision  that  the  intention  of  the\ndraftsmen would have to be gathered.\u201d[411]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1042\">224.  Justice Y.V. Chandrachud relied upon State of Travancore-Cochin,  A.K.\nGopalan and Golak Nath to conclude:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_660\">\u201cDebates  of  the  Constituent  Assembly  and  of  the   First   Provisional\nParliament were extensively read out to us during the course  of  arguments.\nI read the speeches with interest, but in my opinion, the  debates  are  not\nadmissible as aids to construction of constitutional provisions.\u201d[412]\n\nA little later it was said:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_661\">\u201cIt is hazardous to rely upon parliamentary debates  as  aids  to  statutory\nconstruction. Different speakers have different motives and  the  system  of\n\u201cParty Whip\u201d leaves no warrant for assuming that those  who  voted  but  did\nnot speak were of identical persuasion. That assumption may be difficult  to\nmake even in regard to those who speak. The safest course is to  gather  the\nintention  of  the  legislature  from  the  language  it  uses.   Therefore,\nparliamentary proceedings  can  be  used  only  for  a  limited  purpose  as\nexplained in Gopalan case.\u201d[413]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1043\">225.  A contrary view  was  rhetorically  expressed  by  Justice  Jaganmohan\nReddy but it was eventually held that the CAD could aid  in  interpretation,\nbeing  \u2018valuable  material\u2019  unlike  legislative  debates  which  could   be\nmotivated  by  partisan  views  and  party  politics.  Constituent  Assembly\nDebates were not motivated by such partisan considerations. It was said:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_662\">\u201cSpeaking for myself, why should we not look  into  them  [CAD]  boldly  for\nascertaining what was the intention of our framers and how  they  translated\nthat intention? What is the rationale for  treating  them  as  forbidden  or\nforbidding material. The Court in a constitutional matter, where the  intent\nof the framers of the Constitution as embodied in the  written  document  is\nto be ascertained, should look  into  the  proceedings,  the  relevant  data\nincluding any speech which may  throw  light  on  ascertaining  it.  It  can\nreject them as unhelpful, if they throw no light or throw only dim light  in\nwhich nothing can be discerned\u2026\u2026\u2026.. In proceedings of a  legislature  on  an\nordinary draft bill, as I said earlier, there may be a partisan  and  heated\ndebate, which often times may not throw any light on the issues  which  come\nbefore the Court but the proceedings in a [pic]Constituent Assembly have  no\nsuch partisan nuances and their only concern  is  to  give  the  national  a\nworking instrument with its basic structure and  human  values  sufficiently\nbalanced and stable enough to  allow  an  interplay  of  forces  which  will\nsubserve the needs of future generations. The highest  Court  created  under\nit and charged with the duty of  understanding  and  expounding  it,  should\nnot, if it has to catch the objectives  of  the  framers,  deny  itself  the\nbenefit of the guidance derivable from the records of  the  proceedings  and\nthe deliberations of the Assembly.\u201d[414]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1044\">226.  Justice K.K. Mathew supported the view  of  Justice  Jaganmohan  Reddy\nand observed that: \u2018Logically, there is no  reason  why  we  should  exclude\naltogether the speeches made  in  the  Constituent  Assembly  by  individual\nmembers if they throw any light which will resolve  latent  ambiguity  in  a\nprovision of  Constitution.\u2019  The  learned  judge  went  on  to  hold  in  a\nsubsequent paragraph of the decision:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_663\">\u201cIf  the  debates  in  the  Constituent  Assembly  can  be  looked  into  to\nunderstand the legislative  history  of  a  provision  of  the  Constitution\nincluding its derivation, that is, the  various  steps  leading  up  to  and\nattending its enactment, to ascertain the intention of  the  makers  of  the\nConstitution, it is difficult to see why the  debates  are  inadmissible  to\nthrow light on the purpose and general intent of the provision.  After  all,\nlegislative  history  only  tends  to  reveal  the  legislative  purpose  in\nenacting the provision and thereby sheds light upon legislative  intent.  It\nwould be drawing an invisible distinction if resort to debates is  permitted\nsimply to show the legislative history and the same is not allowed  to  show\nthe  legislative  intent  [pic]in  case   of   latent   ambiguity   in   the\nprovision.\u201d[415]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1045\">227.  In Samsher Singh in their concurring  opinion,  Justice  Krishna  Iyer\n(for himself and Justice P.N. Bhagwati) extensively referred to the CAD  for\narriving at their conclusion, while Chief Justice Ray (for himself and  four\nother learned judges) made no reference to the CAD.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1046\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1363234\" id=\"span_450\">228.   Be  that  as  it  may,  reference  to  the  CAD  again  came  up  for\nconsideration in <a href=\"/doc/1363234/\" id=\"a_1824\">Indra Sawhney v. Union  of  India</a>.[416]  Speaking  for  the\nlearned Chief Justice,  Justice  M.N.  Venkatachaliah,  Justice  Ahmadi  and\nhimself, Justice B.P. Jeevan Reddy clarified that  though  the  CAD  or  the\nspeeches of Dr. Ambedkar cannot be  ignored,  they  are  not  conclusive  or\nbinding on the Court but can be relied upon as  an  aid  to  interpreting  a\nconstitutional provision. The CAD  were  referred  to  for  \u2018furnishing  the\ncontext and the objective\u2019 to be achieved by clause (4)  of  <a href=\"/doc/211089/\" id=\"a_1825\">Article  16</a>  of\nthe Constitution. Reference was made, inter alia, to Golaknath, Dhillon  and\nKesavananda Bharati and it was held:</span>\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_664\">\u201cWe are aware that what is said during these debates is  not  conclusive  or\nbinding upon the Court because several members may  have  expressed  several\nviews, all of which may not be reflected in the provision  finally  enacted.\nThe speech of Dr Ambedkar on this aspect, however,  stands  on  a  different\nfooting. He was not only  the  Chairman  of  the  Drafting  Committee  which\ninserted the expression \u201cbackward\u201d in draft <a href=\"/doc/298655/\" id=\"a_1826\">Article 10(3) [</a>it was not  there\nin the original draft <a href=\"/doc/298655/\" id=\"a_1827\">Article 10(3)]</a>, he was virtually  piloting  the  draft\nArticle. In his speech, he explains the reason behind draft  clause  (3)  as\nalso the reason for  which  the  Drafting  Committee  added  the  expression\n\u201cbackward\u201d in the clause. In this situation, we fail to understand  how  can\nanyone ignore his speech while trying to ascertain the meaning of  the  said\nexpression. That the debates in Constituent Assembly can be relied  upon  as\nan aid to interpretation of a constitutional provision is  borne  out  by  a\nseries of decisions of this Court\u2026\u2026\u2026  Since  the  expression  \u201cbackward\u201d  or\n\u201cbackward class of citizens\u201d is not defined in the  Constitution,  reference\nto such debates is permissible to  ascertain,  at  any  rate,  the  context,\nbackground and objective behind them. Particularly, where  the  Court  wants\nto ascertain the \u2018original intent\u2019 such reference may be unavoidable.\u201d[417]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1047\">229.  <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"199141576\" id=\"span_451\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"66020071\" id=\"span_452\">In <a href=\"/doc/66020071/\" id=\"a_1828\">S.R. Chaudhuri v. State of Punjab</a>[418]  it  was  held  that  it  is\nsettled that the  CAD  may  be  relied  upon  \u2018as  an  aid  to  interpret  a\nconstitutional provision because it is the function of  the  court  to  find\nout the intention of  the  framers  of  the  Constitution.\u2019  This  view  was\nfollowed by me in <a href=\"/doc/199141576/\" id=\"a_1829\">Manoj Narula v. Union of India</a>.[419]</span></span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1048\">230.  <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"512761\" id=\"span_453\">In <a href=\"/doc/512761/\" id=\"a_1830\">T.M.A. Pai Foundation v.  State  of  Karnataka</a>[420]  Justice  Khare\nreferred to Kesavananda Bharati and observed therein  that  though  the  CAD\nare not conclusive, yet they can throw  light  into  the  intention  of  the\nframers in enacting provisions  of  the  Constitution.  On  this  basis  the\nlearned judge held:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_665\">\u201cThus, the accepted view appears to be that the report  of  the  Constituent\nAssembly  debates  can  legitimately  be  taken   into   consideration   for\nconstruction of the provisions of the Act or the Constitution.\u201d[421]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1049\">231.  Justice Variava (for  himself  and  Justice  Bhan)  also  referred  to\nKesavananda Bharati and held that though the CAD  are  not  conclusive,  but\n\u2018in a  constitutional  matter  where  the  intent  of  the  framers  of  the\nConstitution  is  to  be  ascertained,  the  Court  should  look  into   the\nproceedings and the relevant  data,  including  the  speeches,  which  throw\nlight on ascertaining the intent.\u2019\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1050\">232.  Justice Syed Shah  Quadri  stated  an  interesting  principle  in  the\nfollowing words:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_666\">\u201cThe correct way to interpret an article is to go by its plain language  and\nlay bare the meaning it conveys. It would no doubt be  useful  to  refer  to\nthe historical and political background which  supports  the  interpretation\ngiven by the court and in  that  context  the  debates  of  the  Constituent\nAssembly would be the best record of  understanding  all  those  aspects.  A\nhost of considerations might have  prompted  the  people  of  India  through\nMembers of Constituent Assembly to adopt, enact and to  give  to  themselves\nthe Constitution. We are really  concerned  with  what  they  have  adopted,\nenacted and given to themselves in these documents. We cannot and we  should\nnot cause scar on it which would take years for the  coming  generations  to\nremove from its face.\u201d[422]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1051\">233.  The learned judge then went on to hold, relying on Prem  Lal  Mullick,\nA.K. Gopalan, State of  Travancore-Cochin,  Kesavananda  Bharati  and  Indra\nSawhney that \u2018admissibility of speeches made  in  the  Constituent  Assembly\nfor interpreting provisions of the  Constitution  is  not  permissible\u2019  and\nthat \u2018The preponderance of opinion appears to me not to rely on the  debates\nin the Constituent Assembly or the Parliament to interpret a  constitutional\nprovision although they may be relevant for  other  purposes.\u2019  The  learned\njudge  quoted  a  sentence  from  Black  Clawson   International   Ltd.   v.\nPapierwerke Waldhof-Aschaffenburg Aktiengesellschaft[423] to  the  following\neffect:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_667\">\u201cWe are seeking not what Parliament meant  but  the  true  meaning  of  what\nParliament said.\u201d[424]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1052\">234.  In re: Special Reference No. 1  of  2002  (Gujarat  Assembly  Election\nMatter)[425]  the  issue  of  relying  on  the  CAD  again   came   up   for\nconsideration. Justice Khare  (for  the  Chief  Justice,  Justice  Bhan  and\nhimself) referred to Kesavananda Bharati and held:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_668\">\u201cConstituent  Assembly  Debates  although  not  conclusive,  yet  show   the\nintention of the framers of the Constitution in enacting provisions  of  the\nConstitution and  the  Constituent  Assembly  Debates  can  throw  light  in\nascertaining the intention behind such provisions.\u201d[426]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1053\">235.  <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1240581\" id=\"span_454\">In a decision rendered by the Constitutional Court of the Republic  of\nSouth Africa in <a href=\"/doc/1240581/\" id=\"a_1831\">The State v. T. Makwanyane</a>[427] a brief survey  of  the  law\nin the United States Supreme Court, German  Constitutional  Court,  Canadian\nSupreme Court, this Court, European Court of Human  Rights  and  the  United\nNations Committee on Human Rights was carried  out  and  it  was  held  (per\nJustice Chaskalson):</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_669\">In countries in which the constitution is similarly the supreme law,  it  is\nnot unusual for the courts to have regard to the circumstances  existing  at\nthe time the constitution was adopted, including the  debates  and  writings\nwhich formed part of the process.  The  United  States  Supreme  Court  pays\nattention to such matters, and its judgments frequently contain  reviews  of\nthe legislative history of the provision in question,  including  references\nto debates, and statements made, at the time the provision was adopted.  The\nGerman Constitutional Court also has regard to such evidence.  The  Canadian\nSupreme Court has held such evidence to be admissible, and has  referred  to\nthe historical background including the pre-confederation  debates  for  the\npurpose of interpreting provisions of the  Canadian  Constitution,  although\nit attaches less weight to such information than the United  States  Supreme\nCourt does. It also has regard to ministerial statements  in  Parliament  in\nregard to the purpose of particular legislation. <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1857950\" id=\"span_455\">In India,  whilst  speeches\nof individual members of Parliament or the  Convention  are  apparently  not\nordinarily admissible, the reports of drafting committees can, according  to\nSeervai, \u201cbe a helpful extrinsic aid to construction.\u201d Seervai  cites  Kania\nCJ in <a href=\"/doc/1857950/\" id=\"a_1832\">A. K. Gopalan v The State</a> for the proposition that whilst  not  taking\n\u201c...into consideration the individual opinions of Members of  Parliament  or\nConvention to construe the meaning of a particular clause, when  a  question\nis raised whether a certain phrase or expression was  up  for  consideration\nat all or not, a reference to debates may be permitted.\u201d The European  Court\nof Human Rights and the United Nations Committee on Human Rights  all  allow\ntheir  deliberations  to  be  informed   by   travaux   preparatoires.\u201d[428]\n(Internal citations omitted)</span>\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1054\">236.  Earlier, on a consideration of the law in England  it  was  held  (per\nJustice Chaskalon):\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_670\">\u201cDebates in Parliament, including statements made by  Ministers  responsible\nfor legislation, and explanatory memoranda providing reasons for  new  bills\nhave not been admitted as background material. It is,  however,  permissible\nto take notice of the report of a judicial commission  of  enquiry  for  the\nlimited purpose  of  ascertaining  \u201cthe  mischief  aimed  at  the  statutory\nenactment in question.\u201d These principles were derived in part  from  English\nlaw. <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1799678\" id=\"span_456\">In England, the courts have recently  relaxed  this  exclusionary  rule\nand have held, in <a href=\"/doc/1799678/\" id=\"a_1833\">Pepper (Inspector of Taxes) v Hart</a> that,  subject  to  the\nprivileges of the House of Commons:</span>\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_671\">...reference to Parliamentary material should be permitted as an aid to  the\nconstruction of legislation which is ambiguous or  obscure  or  the  literal\nmeaning of which leads to an absurdity. Even in  such  cases  references  in\ncourt  to  Parliamentary  material  should  only  be  permitted  where  such\nmaterial  clearly  discloses  the  mischief  aimed  at  or  the  legislative\nintention lying behind  the  ambiguous  or  obscure  words.\u201d[429]  (Internal\ncitations omitted)\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1055\">237.  It is quite clear that the overwhelming view of  the  various  learned\njudges  in  different  decisions  rendered  by  this  Court  and  in   other\njurisdictions as well is that: (1) A reference may be made to the CAD or  to\nParliamentary debates (as  indeed  to  any  other  \u2018relevant  material\u2019)  to\nunderstand the context in which the constitutional or  statutory  provisions\nwere framed and to gather the intent of the law makers but only if there  is\nsome ambiguity or uncertainty or incongruity or obscurity  in  the  language\nof the provision. A reference to the CAD or the Parliamentary debates  ought\nnot  to  be  made  only  because  they  are  there;[430]  (2)  The  CAD   or\nParliamentary  debates  ought  not  to  be  relied  upon  to  interpret  the\nprovisions of the Constitution or the statute if there is  no  ambiguity  in\nthe language used. These provisions ought to be interpreted independently  \u2013\nor at least, if reference is made to the CAD or Parliamentary  debates,  the\nCourt should not be unduly influenced by the speeches made. Confirmation  of\nthe interpretation may be sought from the CAD or the  Parliamentary  debates\nbut not vice versa.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1056\">238.  This discussion has been  necessitated    by  the  submission  of  the\nlearned Attorney-General that the Constituent Assembly did not  intend  that\nfor the appointment of a judge of the Supreme Court or  of  the  High  Court\nthe concurrence of the  Chief  Justice  of  India  is  necessary.  The  word\n\u2018consultation\u2019 in <a href=\"/doc/1164880/\" id=\"a_1834\">Article 124</a> of the Constitution and in <a href=\"/doc/1682952/\" id=\"a_1835\">Article 217</a> of  the\nConstitution did not and could not mean \u2018concurrence\u2019.  This,  according  to\nthe learned Attorney-General is specifically and clearly borne out from  the\nCAD. In fact,  the  learned  Attorney-General  drew  our  attention  to  the\ndiscussion that took place in the Constituent  Assembly  on  23rd  and  24th\nMay, 1949.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1057\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_457\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_458\">239.  It was submitted that under the circumstances there was  no  ambiguity\nin the meaning of the word \u2018consultation\u2019 and a reference  to  the  CAD  was\nnecessary, applying the dictum of Chief Justice Sikri, only to  confirm  the\ninterpretation of \u2018consultation\u2019 as not meaning  \u2018concurrence\u2019.  It  is  for\nthis reason, apart from others that the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1836\">Second Judges</a>  case  and  the  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1837\">Third\nJudges</a> case required reconsideration.</span></span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1058\">240.  The learned Attorney-General also drew our attention to the  following\nexpression  of  opinion  by  Mr.  T.T.  Krishnamachari  in  the  Constituent\nAssembly on 27th May, 1949 in relation to clause (3) of  the  draft  <a href=\"/doc/1283667/\" id=\"a_1838\">Article\n122</a> concerning the  officers  and  servants  and  expenses  of  the  Supreme\nCourt.[431] The contention  was  that  it  was  not  the  intention  of  the\nConstituent Assembly to make the Chief  Justice  of  India  or  the  Supreme\nCourt above the executive or the Legislature thereby discarding  the  theory\nof separation of powers,  and  if  \u2018consultation\u2019  is  interpreted  to  mean\n\u2018concurrence\u2019, then that  would  be  the  inevitable  result.  Reliance  was\nplaced on the following speech:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_672\">\u201cWhile I undoubtedly support the amendment moved by Dr.  Ambedkar,  I  think\nit should be understood by the Members of this  House,  and  I  do  hope  by\nthose people who will be administering justice and  also  administering  the\ncountry in the future that this is a  safeguard  rather  than  an  operative\nprovision. The only thing about it is that a matter like the  employment  of\nstaff by the Judges should be placed ordinarily outside the purview  of  the\nExecutive which would otherwise have  to  take  the  initiative  to  include\nthese items in the budget for  the  reason  that  the  independence  of  the\nJudiciary should be maintained and that the Judiciary should not  feel  that\nthey are subject to favours that the Executive  might  grant  to  them  from\ntime to time and which would  naturally  influence  their  decision  in  any\nmatter they have to take where the interests of the Executive  of  the  time\nbeing happens to be concerned. At the same time, Sir, I think it  should  be\nmade clear that it is not the intention of this House or of the  framers  of\nthis Constitution that they want to create specially favoured  bodies  which\nin themselves becomes an Imperium in Imperio, completely independent of  the\nExecutive and the Legislature and operating as a sort of  superior  body  to\nthe general body politic. If that were so,  I  think  we  should  be  rather\nchary of introducing a provision of this nature, not  merely  in  regard  to\nthe Supreme Court but also in regard to the Auditor-General,  in  regard  to\nthe Union Public Service Commission,  in  regard  to  the  Speaker  and  the\nPresident of the two House of Parliament and so on, as we  will  thereby  be\ncreating a number of bodies which are placed in such a  position  that  they\nare bound to come into conflict with the Executive  in  every  attempt  they\nmake to display their superiority. In actual practice, it is better for  all\nthese bodies to more or less fall in line with the regulations  that  obtain\nin matters of recruitment to the public services,  conditions  of  promotion\nand salaries paid to their staff.\u201d[432]\n\nReplying to this debate, Dr. Ambedkar clarified the position that there  was\nno question of creating an Imperium in Imperio. Dr. Ambedkar said:\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_673\">\u201cMr. President, Sir, I would just like to make a few observations  in  order\nto clear the position. Sir, there is no doubt that  the  House  in  general,\nhas agreed that the independence of the Judiciary from the Executive  should\nbe made as clear and definite as we could make it by law. At the same  time,\nthere is the fear that in the name of the independence of the Judiciary,  we\nmight be creating, what my  Friend  Mr.  T.  T.  Krishnamachari  very  aptly\ncalled an &quot;Imperium in Imperio&quot;. We do not want to  create  an  Imperium  in\nImperio, and  at  the  same  time  we  want  to  give  the  Judiciary  ample\nindependence so that it can act without fear or favour of the Executive.  My\nfriend, if they will carefully examine the provisions of the  new  amendment\nwhich I have proposed in place of the original <a href=\"/doc/1283667/\" id=\"a_1839\">article 122</a>, will  find  that\nthe new article proposes to steer a middle course. It refuses to  create  an\nImperium  in  Imperio,  and  I  think  it  gives  the  Judiciary   as   much\nindependence as is  necessary  for  the  purpose  of  administering  justice\nwithout fear or favour. I need not therefore, dilate on all  the  provisions\ncontained in this new article 122\u2026..\u201d[433]\n\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_674\">241.  It is quite clear from the above that the endeavour  of  Dr.  Ambedkar\nwas to ensure the independence of the judiciary from the  executive  without\ncreating any power imbalance and this, therefore, needed steering  a  middle\ncourse whether in the appointment of judges or the officers of  the  Supreme\nCourt.  There  can  be  no  doubt  about  this  at  all.  But  what  is  the\n\u2018independence of the judiciary\u2019 and how can it be maintained  and  does  the\n99th Constitution Amendment Act impact on that independence? These are  some\ntroubling questions that need an answer with reference to the  issue  before\nus, namely, the constitutional validity of the 99th  Constitution  Amendment\nAct.\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_675\">1 Judicial pronouncements and the third preliminary issue\n\n\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_676\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"PARTY\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_459\">242.  The learned Attorney-General submitted that in any  event  the  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1840\">Second\nJudges</a> case requires reconsideration. There is  large  volume  of  case  law\nwhich gives guidance on the circumstances when an earlier decision  of  this\nCourt should be reconsidered.</span>  <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_460\">It  is  necessary  to  consider  these  cases\nbefore deciding whether a platform for reconsideration of the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1841\">Second  Judges</a>\ncase has been made.</span>\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_677\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"424874\" id=\"span_461\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"608874\" id=\"span_462\">243.  <a href=\"/doc/608874/\" id=\"a_1842\">Bengal Immunity Co. Ltd. v. State of Bihar &amp; Ors.</a>[434]  concerned  the\ninterpretation of <a href=\"/doc/433847/\" id=\"a_1843\">Article 286</a> of the Constitution which, it  was  contended,\nhad been incorrectly interpreted in <a href=\"/doc/424874/\" id=\"a_1844\">State of Bombay  v.  The  United  Motors\n(India) Ltd.</a>[435] This Court addressed the issue  of  reconsideration  of  a\nprevious decision rendered by it. Chief Justice Das (speaking  for  himself,\nJustice Vivian Bose and Justice Syed Jafer  Imam)  discussed  the  judgments\ndelivered in England, Australia, the United States and by the Privy  Council\nand was of the view (for several reasons) that a previous decision  rendered\nby this Court could be departed from. It was observed that it was  not  easy\nto amend the Constitution and if an erroneous interpretation was put upon  a\nprovision thereof it could \u2018conceivably be perpetuated or may  at  any  rate\nremain unrectified for a considerable time to the great detriment to  public\nwell being.\u2019 It was held, inter alia, that if this Court  was  convinced  of\nits error and \u2018baneful effect\u2019 on the general interests of the public of  an\nerroneous interpretation of a provision of the Constitution, then  there  is\nnothing in the Constitution that prevents this Court in departing  from  its\nearlier decision. It could also depart from a previous decision  if  it  was\nvague or inconsistent or plainly erroneous. It was held  that  the  doctrine\nof stare decisis \u2018is not an inflexible rule of law and cannot  be  permitted\nto perpetuate our errors to the detriment to  the  general  welfare  of  the\npublic or a considerable section thereof.\u2019</span></span>\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_678\">244.  In a significant passage  (one  that  will  have  a  bearing  on  this\nsubject), it was observed:\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_679\">\u201cThe majority decision does not merely  determine  the  rights  of  the  two\ncontending parties to the Bombay appeal. Its effect is far  reaching  as  it\naffects the rights of all consuming public.  It  authorises  the  imposition\nand levying of a tax by the State on an interpretation of  a  constitutional\nprovision  which  appears  to  us  to  be  unsupportable.  To  follow   that\ninterpretation will result in perpetuating what, with humility  we  say,  is\nan error and in perpetuating a tax  burden  imposed  on  the  people  which,\naccording to our considered opinion, is manifestly and wholly  unauthorised.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_680\">It is not an ordinary pronouncement declaring  the  rights  of  two  private\nindividuals inter se. It involves an adjudication on  the  taxing  power  of\nthe States as against the consuming public generally.  If  the  decision  is\nerroneous, as indeed we conceive it to be, we  owe  it  to  that  public  to\nprotect them against the illegal tax burdens which the  States  are  seeking\nto impose on the strength of that erroneous recent decision.\u201d[436]\n\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_681\">245.  Justice N.H. Bhagwati also reviewed  several  decisions  from  various\njurisdictions and agreed with Chief  Justice  Das  but  drew  a  distinction\nbetween   reconsideration   of   a   previous   decision   concerning    the\ninterpretation  of  a  provision  of  a  legislative   enactment   and   the\ninterpretation of a  provision  of  the  Constitution.  While  an  erroneous\ninterpretation of the  former  by  the  Court  could  be  corrected  by  the\nLegislature, it was not easy  to  amend  the  Constitution  to  correct  its\nerroneous interpretation by the Court. It is for this  reason  that  Justice\nN.H.  Bhagwati  held  that  if  the  previous  decision   interpreting   the\nprovisions of the Constitution was \u2018manifestly wrong or erroneous\u2019 and  that\n\u2018public interest\u2019 demanded its reconsideration then the  Court  should  have\nno hesitation in doing so.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_682\">246.  Justice Jagannadhadas also  held  that  this  Court  is  competent  to\nreconsider its earlier decisions. It was  added  that:  \u2018But,  it  does  not\nfollow that such power can be exercised without  restriction  or  limitation\nor that a prior decision can be  reversed  on  the  ground  that,  on  later\nconsideration, the Court disagrees with the prior  decision  and  thinks  it\nerroneous.\u2019 It was  held  that  though  the  power  to  reconsider  a  prior\ndecision does exist, the actual exercise of that power  should  be  confined\n\u2018within very narrow limits.\u2019 The learned Judge preferred to adopt  the  view\nexpressed by Justice Dixon of the  High  Court  of  Australia  in  Attorney-\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1059\">General for N.S.W. v. The Perpetual Trustee  Co.  Ltd.[437]  to  the  effect\nthat a prior decision should not be reconsidered simply because an  opposite\nconclusion is to be preferred.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1060\">247.  Justice Venkatarama Aiyar also held the view  that  this  Court  could\nreconsider an earlier decision rendered by it. However,  the  learned  Judge\nwas of the opinion that the power to reconsider should  be  \u2018exercised  very\nsparingly and only in exceptional circumstances, such  as  when  a  material\nprovision of law had been overlooked, or where a fundamental  assumption  on\nwhich the decision is based, turns out to be mistaken.\u2019  Agreeing  with  the\nview canvassed by Justice Jagannadhadas  (and  Justice  Dixon)  the  learned\nJudge posed the following question and also  answered  it:  \u2018Can  we  differ\nfrom a previous decision of this Court, because a view contrary to  the  one\ntaken therein appears to be preferable? I would unhesitatingly answer it  in\nthe negative, not because the view  previously  taken  must  necessarily  be\ninfallible but because it is important  in  public  interest  that  the  law\ndeclared should be certain and final rather than that it should be  declared\nin one sense or the other.\u2019\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1061\">248.  Justice B.P. Sinha  agreed  with  Justice  Jagannadhadas  and  Justice\nVenkatarama Aiyar and held that a previous judgment of this Court ought  not\nto be reviewed simply because another view may be taken  of  the  points  in\ncontroversy. This  Court  should  review  its  previous  decisions  only  in\nexceptional circumstances. It was observed that \u2018Definiteness and  certainty\nof the legal position are essential conditions for the growth  of  the  rule\nof law.\u2019\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1062\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Neutral\" data-docid=\"1166220\" id=\"span_463\">249.   Lt.  Col.  <a href=\"/doc/1166220/\" id=\"a_1845\">Khajoor  Singh  v.  Union  of  India</a>[438]  concerned   the\ninterpretation of <a href=\"/doc/1712542/\" id=\"a_1846\">Article 226</a> of the Constitution  and  <a href=\"/doc/981147/\" id=\"a_1847\">Article  32(2-A)</a>  of\nthe Constitution (as applicable to Jammu &amp; Kashmir).  Though  Justice  Subba\nRao (dissenting) and  Justice  Das  Gupta  (concurring)  delivered  separate\njudgments, they did not advert to  the  question  of  reconsideration  of  a\ndecision of this Court. Chief Justice B.P. Sinha speaking for the  remaining\nlearned judges took the view that  a  previous  decision  rendered  by  this\nCourt may be reconsidered if there are \u2018clear and compelling reasons\u2019 to  do\nso or if there is a fair amount of unanimity that the previous  decision  is\n\u2018manifestly wrong\u2019 or if it is demonstrated that the  earlier  decision  was\nerroneous \u2018beyond all reasonable doubt\u2019  particularly  on  a  constitutional\nissue.  If  any  inconvenience  is  felt  on  the  interpretations  of   the\nprovisions of the Constitution under consideration, then the  remedy  \u2018seems\nto be a constitutional amendment.\u2019</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1063\">250.  <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1512520\" id=\"span_464\">In <a href=\"/doc/1512520/\" id=\"a_1848\">Keshav Mills v. CIT</a>[439] the question  for  consideration  was  the\nscope of the High Court\u2019s powers under <a href=\"/doc/166015046/\" id=\"a_1849\">Section 66(4)</a> of the Income Tax  Act,\n1922.</span> <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"460379\" id=\"span_465\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"911300\" id=\"span_466\">It was submitted by the  learned  Attorney-General  that  two  earlier\ndecisions on the  subject,  that  is,  <a href=\"/doc/911300/\" id=\"a_1850\">New  Jehangir  Vakil  Mills  Ltd.  v.\nCIT</a>[440] and <a href=\"/doc/460379/\" id=\"a_1851\">Petlad Turkey Red  Dye  Works  Co.  Ltd.,  Petlad  v.  CIT</a>[441]\nneeded reconsideration. In considering this submission,  it  was  held  that\nwhen  this  Court  interprets  a  statutory  provision,  merely  because  an\nalternative view different from an opinion earlier expressed by  this  Court\nis more reasonable is not necessarily an adequate reason  for  reconsidering\nthe earlier opinion.  This Court should ask itself the question  whether  in\nthe interests of the public good or  for  any  other  valid  and  compulsive\nreasons, it is necessary that the earlier decision should be  revised.  This\nCourt held:</span></span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_683\">\u201cWhen this Court decides questions of law, its decisions are, under  <a href=\"/doc/882644/\" id=\"a_1852\">Article\n141</a> binding on all courts within the territory of India and so  it  must  be\nthe constant endeavour and concern of this Court to introduce  and  maintain\nan element of certainty and continuity in the interpretation of law  in  the\ncountry\u2026..That is not to say that if on a subsequent occasion, the Court  is\nsatisfied that  its  earlier  decision  was  clearly  erroneous,  it  should\nhesitate to correct the error; but before a previous decision is  pronounced\nto be plainly erroneous, the Court must be satisfied with a fair  amount  of\nunanimity amongst its members that a revision of  the  said  view  is  fully\njustified.\u201d[442]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1064\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"352854\" id=\"span_467\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1293557\" id=\"span_468\">251.  <a href=\"/doc/1293557/\" id=\"a_1853\">Maganlal Chhaganlal v. Municipal Corporation  of  Greater  Bombay</a>[443]\nconcerned the validity of  proceedings  under  <a href=\"/doc/22122408/\" id=\"a_1854\">Chapter  V-A  of  the  Bombay\nMunicipal  Corporation  Act,  1888</a>  and  the  <a href=\"/doc/12004336/\" id=\"a_1855\">Bombay   Government   Premises\n(Eviction) Act, 1955</a> in the  context  of  the  decision  of  this  Court  in\n<a href=\"/doc/352854/\" id=\"a_1856\">Northern India Caterers v. State of Punjab</a>.[444] Justice H.R.  Khanna  alone\nconsidered the question of overruling an earlier  decision  of  this  Court,\nnamely, in Northern India Caterers. It was observed that  certainty  in  law\nwould be eroded if a decision that \u2018held the field\u2019  for  several  years  is\nreadily overruled \u2013 \u2018certainty and continuity are essential  ingredients  of\nrule of law.\u2019 It was held that  if  two  views  are  possible  then,  simply\nbecause the earlier decision does not take a view that  is  more  acceptable\nwould not be a ground for  overruling  the  earlier  decision.   An  earlier\ndecision ought to be overruled only  for  compelling  reasons  otherwise  it\nwould create \u2018uncertainty, instability and confusion if the  law  propounded\nby this Court on the basis of which numerous cases  have  been  decided  and\nmany transactions have taken place is held  to  be  not  the  correct  law.\u2019\nJustice Khanna observed that new ideas and developments in the field of  law\nand that the fullness of experience and indeed subsequent experience  cannot\nbe wished away. The learned judge held:</span></span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_684\">\u201cAs in life so in law things are not static. Fresh vistas and  horizons  may\nreveal themselves as a result of the impact of new  ideas  and  developments\nin different fields of life. Law, if it has to satisfy human  needs  and  to\nmeet the problems of life, must adapt itself to cope  with  new  situations.\nNobody is so gifted with foresight that he can  divine  all  possible  human\nevents in advance and prescribe proper rules for each of  them.  There  are,\nhowever, certain verities which are of the essence of the rule  of  law  and\nno law can afford to do away with them. At  the  same  time  it  has  to  be\nrecognized that there is a continuing process of the growth of law  and  one\ncan retard it only at the risk of alienating law  from  life  itself.  There\nshould not be much hesitation to abandon  an  untenable  position  when  the\nrule to be discarded was in  its  origin  the  product  of  institutions  or\nconditions which have gained a new  significance  or  development  with  the\nprogress of years. It sometimes happens that the rule of law which  grew  up\nin remote generations may in the fullness of experience be  found  to  serve\nanother generation badly. The Court cannot allow itself to be tied  down  by\nand become  captive  of  a  view  which  in  the  light  of  the  subsequent\nexperience has been found to be patently erroneous, manifestly  unreasonable\nor  to  cause  hardship  or  to  result  in   plain   iniquity   or   public\ninconvenience.\u201d[445]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1065\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1290222\" id=\"span_469\">252.  <a href=\"/doc/1290222/\" id=\"a_1857\">Ganga Sugar Corporation v. State of Uttar Pradesh</a>[446] related to  the\nconstitutional validity of a levy under the <a href=\"/doc/74335918/\" id=\"a_1858\">U.P.  Sugarcane  (Purchase  Tax)\nAct, 1961</a>. The decision does not contain  any  detailed  discussion  on  the\nsubject of reconsideration of an earlier decision of this Court. But it  was\nnevertheless held that decisions of a Constitution Bench  must  be  accepted\nas final unless the subject is of fundamental importance  to  national  life\nor the reasoning of the previous decision is so plainly erroneous  that  \u2018it\nis wiser to be ultimately right rather than to be consistently wrong.  Stare\ndecisis is not a ritual of convenience but a rule with  limited  exceptions.\nPronouncements by Constitution Benches should not be treated  so  cavalierly\nas to be revised frequently.\u2019</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1066\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Neg\" data-docid=\"505842\" id=\"span_470\">253.  A rather exhaustive reference to the cases and the law  <a href=\"/doc/1290222/\" id=\"a_1859\">laid  down  in</a>\ndifferent jurisdictions was adverted  to  in  <a href=\"/doc/505842/\" id=\"a_1860\">Union  of  India  v.  Raghubir\nSingh</a>.[447] This decision concerned itself with the grant of solatium  under\nthe  <a href=\"/doc/7832/\" id=\"a_1861\">Land  Acquisition  Act,  1894</a>  as  amended  by  the  Land   Acquisition\n(Amendment) Act, 1984. Reference was made to  the  \u2018guidelines\u2019  culled  out\nfrom the decisions of  the  House  of  Lords[448]  which  suggest  that  the\nfreedom to reconsider an earlier decision ought to be  exercised  sparingly;\na  decision  ought  not  to  be  overruled  if  it  upsets  the   legitimate\nexpectation of persons who have  made  arrangements  based  on  the  earlier\ndecision or causes great uncertainty in the  law;  decisions  involving  the\ninterpretation of statutes or documents ought not to be overruled except  in\nrare or exceptional circumstances; if the consequences of departing from  an\nearlier decision are not foreseeable; merely  because  an  earlier  decision\nwas wrongly taken is not a good enough justification for overruling  it.  On\nthe other hand, a prior decision ought to be overruled \u2018if  in  relation  to\nsome broad issue or principle it is not considered just or in  keeping  with\ncontemporary social conditions or modern conceptions of public policy.\u2019</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1067\">254.  Reference was also made to several decisions earlier rendered by  this\nCourt (including those mentioned above)  and  though  no  new  or  different\nprinciples or guidelines were laid down, the law as  stated  by  this  Court\nwas iterated, and it was observed: \u2018It is not necessary to refer to all  the\ncases on the point. The broad guidelines are easily deducible from what  has\ngone before. The possibility of further defining  these  guiding  principles\ncan  be  envisaged  with  further  juridical  experience,  and  when  common\njurisprudential values linking different national systems of law may make  a\nconsensual pattern possible. But that lies in the future.\u2019\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1068\">255.  Echoing the views expressed in Maganlal Chhaganlal and Raghubir  Singh\nwith regard to acknowledging changes with the passage  of  time  and  modern\nconceptions of public policy, it was said:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_685\">\u201cNot infrequently,  in  the  nature  of  things  there  is  a  gravity-heavy\ninclination to follow the groove set by precedential law.  Yet  a  sensitive\njudicial conscience often persuades the mind to search for a  [pic]different\nset of norms more responsive to the  changed  social  context.  The  dilemma\nbefore the Judge poses the task of finding a new  equilibrium  prompted  not\nseldom by the desire to reconcile opposing mobilities. The competing  goals,\naccording to Dean Roscoe Pound, invest the  Judge  with  the  responsibility\n\u201cof proving to mankind that the law was something fixed and  settled,  whose\nauthority was beyond question, while at the same time enabling  it  to  make\nconstant readjustments and occasional radical changes under the pressure  of\ninfinite and variable human desires\u201d. The reconciliation suggested  by  Lord\nReid in The Judge as Law Maker lies in  keeping  both  objectives  in  view,\n\u201cthat the law shall be certain, and that it shall be  just  and  shall  move\nwith the times\u201d.\u201d [449] (Internal citations have been omitted).\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1069\">256.  <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"557776\" id=\"span_471\">In <a href=\"/doc/557776/\" id=\"a_1862\">Gannon Dunkerley &amp; Co. v.  State  of  Rajasthan</a>[450]  the  question\nrelated to \u2018the imposition of tax on  the  transfer  of  property  in  goods\ninvolved in the execution of works contracts.</span> <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"732612\" id=\"span_472\">The power to impose  this  tax\nbecame available to the State Legislatures as a  result  of  the  amendments\nintroduced in the Constitution by the <a href=\"/doc/144486009/\" id=\"a_1863\">Constitution  (Forty-sixth  Amendment)\nAct, 1982</a>.\u2019 The constitutional validity  of  this  Amendment  Act  had  been\nupheld in <a href=\"/doc/732612/\" id=\"a_1864\">Builders\u2019 Association of India v. Union of India</a>.[451] One of  the\nissues raised was whether Builders\u2019 Association had been  correctly  decided\nor not. This Court  did  not  add  to  the  discourse  on  the  subject  but\nconcluded,  relying  upon  Khajoor  Singh,  Keshav  Mills  and  Ganga  Sugar\nCorporation that there  was  no  occasion  to  reconsider  the  decision  in\nBuilders\u2019 Association.</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1070\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"471272\" id=\"span_473\">257.  Another decision (which is  rather  interesting)  on  the  subject  of\nreconsideration of an earlier decision is <a href=\"/doc/471272/\" id=\"a_1865\">Pradeep  Kumar  Biswas  v.  Indian\nInstitute of Chemical Biology</a>.[452]  The  question  before  this  Court  was\nwhether the Council for Scientific and Industrial Research was  \u2018the  State\u2019\nas \u2018defined\u2019 in <a href=\"/doc/609139/\" id=\"a_1866\">Article 12</a> of the Constitution.</span> <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"435970\" id=\"span_474\">The answer to this  question\nrequired consideration of an earlier unanimous decision  of  this  Court  in\n<a href=\"/doc/435970/\" id=\"a_1867\">Sabhajit Tewary v. Union of  India</a>[453]  which  had  stood  undisturbed  for\nabout 25 years. While answering this question, this  Court  did  not  detail\nthe law on the subject of reconsideration of an  earlier  decision  of  this\nCourt, but on a consideration of the facts  (and  the  law)  concluded  that\nSabhajit Tewary had been wrongly  decided  and  was  overruled.  This  Court\nreferred to Maganlal Chhaganlal and Raghubir Singh and held:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_686\">\u201cFrom whichever perspective the facts are considered, there can be no  doubt\nthat the conclusion reached in Sabhajit Tewary was erroneous. \u2026\u2026.\nIn the assessment of the facts, the Court had  assumed  certain  principles,\nand sought precedential support from decisions  which  were  irrelevant  and\nhad \u201cfollowed a  groove  chased  amidst  a  context  which  has  long  since\ncrumbled.\u201d[454] Had  the  facts  been  closely  scrutinised  in  the  proper\nperspective, it could have led and can only  lead  to  the  conclusion  that\nCSIR is a State within the meaning of <a href=\"/doc/609139/\" id=\"a_1868\">Article 12.</a>\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_687\">Should Sabhajit Tewary still stand as an authority even on the facts  merely\nbecause it has stood for 25 years? We think  not.  Parallels  may  be  drawn\neven on the facts leading to an untenable interpretation of <a href=\"/doc/609139/\" id=\"a_1869\">Article  12</a>  and\na consequential denial of the benefits of fundamental rights to  individuals\nwho would otherwise be entitled to them and\n\u201c[T]here is nothing in our Constitution which  prevents  us  from  departing\nfrom a previous decision if we are convinced of its error  and  its  baneful\neffect on the general interests of the public\u201d.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_688\">Since on a re-examination of the question we have  come  to  the  conclusion\nthat the decision was plainly erroneous, it is our duty to say  so  and  not\nperpetuate our mistake.\u201d [455] (Internal citations have been omitted).\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1071\">258.  One of the more interesting aspects of Pradeep Kumar  Biswas  is  that\nout of the 7 (seven)  learned  judges  constituting  the  Bench,  5  learned\njudges overruled the unanimous decision of another set of 5  learned  judges\nin Sabhajit Tewary. Two of the learned judges in Pradeep Kumar Biswas  found\nthat Sabhajit Tewary had been correctly decided. In  other  words,  while  a\ntotal of 7 learned judges took a particular view on an  issue  of  fact  and\nlaw, that view was  found  to  be  incorrect  by  5  learned  judges,  whose\ndecision  actually  holds  the  field  today.  Is  the  weight  of   numbers\nirrelevant? Is it that only the numbers  in  a  subsequent  Bench  are  what\nreally matters? <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"PARTY\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_475\">What would have been the position if only 4  learned  judges\nin Pradeep Kumar Biswas had decided to overrule Sabhajit  Tewary  while  the\nremaining 3 learned  judges  found  no  error  in  that  decision?  Would  a\ndecision  rendered  unanimously  by  a  Bench  of  5  learned  judges  stand\noverruled by the decision of 4 learned judges in a  subsequent  Bench  of  7\nlearned judges? Pradeep Kumar Biswas presents a rather  anomalous  situation\nwhich needs to be addressed by  appropriate  rules  of  procedure.  If  this\nanomaly is perpetuated then the unanimous decision of 9  learned  judges  in\nthe <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1870\">Third Judges</a> case can be overruled (as sought by the  learned  Attorney-\nGeneral) by 6 learned judges in a Bench of 11  learned  judges,  with  5  of\nthem taking a different view, bringing the total tally of judges having  one\nview to 14 and having another view to 6, with the  view  of  the  6  learned\njudges being taken as the law!</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1072\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"257876\" id=\"span_476\">259.  Be that as it may, two other decisions of importance  on  the  subject\nof reconsidering a prior decision of this Court are <a href=\"/doc/257876/\" id=\"a_1871\">Kesavananda Bharati  and\nthe Second Judges</a> case.</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1073\">260.  In Kesavananda Bharati it was pithily stated  by  Chief  Justice  S.M.\nSikri that the  question  before  the  Court  was  whether  Golak  Nath  was\ncorrectly decided. The learned Chief Justice observed:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_689\">\u201cHowever, as I see it, the question whether  Golak  Nath  case  was  rightly\ndecided or not does not matter because the real issue is  different  and  of\nmuch greater importance,  the  issue  being:  what  is  the  extent  of  the\namending power conferred by <a href=\"/doc/594125/\" id=\"a_1872\">Article 368</a>  of  the  Constitution,  apart  from\n<a href=\"/doc/772605/\" id=\"a_1873\">Article 13(2)</a>, on Parliament ?\u201d[456]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1074\">261.  It follows from this that where a matter  is  of  \u2018great  importance\u2019,\nthis Court may refer the issue to a larger Bench to  reconsider  an  earlier\ndecision of this Court.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1075\">262.  <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_477\">In the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1874\">Second Judges</a> case it was observed by Justice Pandian  that  an\nearlier decision rendered by this Court  may  be  reconsidered  if,  amongst\nothers,  \u2018exceptional  and  extraordinarily  compelling\u2019  circumstances   so\nwarrant. It was observed that \u2018no decision  enjoys  absolute  immunity  from\njudicial review or reconsideration on a fresh outlook of the  constitutional\nor legal interpretation and in the light of the  development  of  innovative\nideas,  principles  and  perception  grown  along  with   the   passage   of\ntime.\u2019[457] Recalling the  observations  in  Maganlal  Chhaganlal,  Raghubir\nSingh and Pradeep Kumar Biswas it was held that:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_690\">\u201cTherefore, in exceptional and extraordinarily compelling  circumstances  or\nunder new set of conditions, the court is on a  fresh  outlook  and  in  the\nlight of the development of  innovative  ideas,  principles  and  perception\ngrown along with the passage of time, obliged by legal and moral  forces  to\nreconsider its earlier ruling  or  decision  and  if  necessitated  even  to\noverrule or  reverse  the  mistaken  decision  by  the  application  of  the\n\u2018principle of retroactive invalidity\u2019. Otherwise  even  the  wrong  judicial\ninterpretation that the Constitution or law has received over  decades  will\nbe holding the field for ages to come without that  wrong  being  corrected.\nIndeed, no historic precedent and long-term practice can supply  a  rule  of\nunalterable decision.\u201d[458]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1076\">263.   There  is  absolutely  no  dispute  or  doubt  that  this  Court  can\nreconsider (and set aside) an earlier decision rendered by it. But what  are\nthe circumstances under which the reconsideration can be sought? This  Court\nhas debated and discussed the issue on several occasions as mentioned  above\nand the broad principles that can be culled out from the  various  decisions\nsuggest that:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_691\">(1) If the decision concerns an interpretation of the Constitution,  perhaps\nthe bar for reconsideration might  be  lowered  a  bit  (as  in  Kesavananda\nBharati). Although the remedy of amending the Constitution is  available  to\nParliament, not all amendments  are  easy  to  carry  out.  Some  amendments\nrequire  following  the   procedure   of   ratification   by   the   States.\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1077\">Nevertheless, where a constitutional issue is  involved,  the  necessity  of\nreconsideration should be shown beyond all reasonable doubt, the  remedy  of\namending the Constitution always being available to Parliament.\n(2) If the decision concerns the imposition of  a  tax,  then  too  the  bar\nmight be lowered a bit since the tax burden would affect a large section  of\nthe public. However, the general principles  for  requiring  reconsideration\ndo not necessarily fall by the wayside.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1078\">(3) If the decision concerns the fundamental rights of the people, then  too\nthe bar might be lowered for obvious reasons.  However  again,  the  general\nprinciples for requiring reconsideration must be adhered to.\n(4) In other cases, the Court must be convinced that  the  earlier  decision\nis plainly erroneous and has a baneful effect on  the  public;  that  it  is\nvague or inconsistent or manifestly wrong.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1079\">(5) If  the  decision  only  concerns  two  contending  private  parties  or\nindividuals, then perhaps it might not be advisable to reconsider  it.  Each\nand every error of law cannot obviously be corrected by this Court.\n(6) The power to  reconsider  is  not  unrestricted  or  unlimited,  but  is\nconfined within narrow limits and must  be  exercised  sparingly  and  under\nexceptional circumstances  for  clear  and  compelling  reasons.  Therefore,\nmerely because a view different from or contrary to what has been  expressed\nearlier is preferable is no reason to reconsider an  earlier  decision.  The\nendeavour of this Court must always be to ensure that the  law  is  definite\nand certain and continuity in the interpretation of the law is maintained.\nIn this regard, Raghubir Singh  presents  an  interesting  picture.  <a href=\"/doc/981477/\" id=\"a_1875\">Section\n23(2)</a>  of  the  Land  Acquisition  Act,  1894  (as  amended  in  1984)   was\ninterpreted by this Court on 14th February, 1985 in  K.  Kamalajammanniavaru\nv. Special Land Acquisition Officer.<span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1169408\" id=\"span_478\">[459] That decision  was  overruled  six\nmonths later on 14th August, 1985  in  <a href=\"/doc/1169408/\" id=\"a_1876\">Bhag  Singh  v.  Union  Territory  of\nChandigarh</a>.[460] That decision was in turn overruled on 16th  May,  1989  in\nRaghubir Singh and the law laid in Kamalajammanniavaru  was  reiterated.  It\nis this uncertainly and absence of continuity in the law  that  is  required\nto be avoided.</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1080\">(7) An earlier decision may be reconsidered if a material provision  of  law\nis overlooked[461] or a fundamental assumption is found to be  erroneous  or\nif there are valid and compulsive or compelling reasons or if the  issue  is\nof fundamental importance to national life. However, it might  not  be  wise\nto overrule a decision if people have changed their position  on  the  basis\nof the  existing  law.
