Jagannadha Rao (Retired)  to  the  Minister  for  Law  and\nJustice that \u2018the Planning  Commission  and  Finance  Commission  must  make\nadequate provision in consultation with the  Chief  Justice  of  India,  for\nrealization of the basic human rights of \u2018access  to  justice\u2019  and  \u2018speedy\njustice\u2019 both civil and criminal. The present allocation of  0.071%,  0.078%\nand 0.07% of the Plan outlay in the 9th,  10th  and  11th  Plan  are  wholly\ninsufficient.\u2019 Financial independence is one area which is also critical  to\nthe independence of the judiciary but is among the least discussed.\n</p>\n\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1155\">Independence of the judiciary and the appointment process\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1156\">334.  We must proceed on the basis that the independence  of  the  judiciary\nis vital to democracy and  there  ought  to  be  a  separation  between  the\nexecutive and the judiciary. The independence of the judiciary  begins  with\nthe  appointment  of  a  judge.  Granville  Austin  says:  \u2018An   independent\njudiciary begins with who appoints what calibre of  judges.\u2019[533]   It  must\nbe appreciated and acknowledged that  methodological  independence,  namely,\nthe recommendation and appointment of judges  to  a  superior  Court  is  an\nimportant facet of the independence of the judiciary.[534] If  a  person  of\ndoubtful  ability  or  integrity  is  appointed  as  a  judge,  there  is  a\nprobability of his/her succumbing  to  internal  or  external  pressure  and\ndelivering  a  tainted  verdict.  This  will  strike  at  the  root  of  the\nindependence of the judiciary and destroy the faith of the common person  in\nfair  justice  delivery.  Therefore,  there  is  a  great   obligation   and\nresponsibility on all  constitutional  functionaries,  including  the  Chief\nJustice of India and the President, to ensure that not  only  are  deserving\npersons appointed as judges, but  that  deserving  persons  are  not  denied\nappointment.[535]\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1157\">335.  Chief Justice  Marshall  in  Marbury  v.  Madison   observed  that  in\nrespect of the commissioning of all  officers  of  the  United  States,  the\nclauses in the Constitution and the laws  of  the  United  States  \u2018seem  to\ncontemplate three distinct operations\u2019, namely:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_721\">\u201c1. The  nomination.  This  is  the  sole  act  of  the  president,  and  is\ncompletely voluntary.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_722\">2. The appointment. This is also the act of the president,  and  is  also  a\nvoluntary act, though it can only be performed by and with  the  advice  and\nconsent of the senate.\u00a0\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_723\">3. The commission. To grant  a  commission  to  a  person  appointed,  might\nperhaps be deemed a duty enjoined by  the  constitution.  \u201cHe  shall,\u201d  says\nthat instrument, \u201ccommission all the officers of the United States.\u201d[536]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1158\">336.  Transposing this to the appointment of  judges  in  our  country,  the\nfirst step is a recommendation (or nomination) of  persons  for  appointment\nas judges. Historically, the recommendation is made by the Chief Justice  of\nIndia for the appointment of a judge of the Supreme Court and by  the  Chief\nJustice of a High Court for appointment  of  a  judge  to  the  High  Court.\nOccasionally, the Chief Minister of a State  also  makes  a  recommendation,\nbut that is required to be routed through the  Chief  Justice  of  the  High\nCourt. There is no instance of the President  recommending  any  person  for\nappointment as a judge of the Supreme Court.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1159\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_530\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_531\">337.  The second step is the appointment of a judge  and  this  is  possible\nonly through a consultative participatory process between the President  and\nthe Chief Justice of India. It is in this process that there has  been  some\ninterpretational disagreement, but the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1985\">Second  Judges</a>  case  and  the  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1986\">Third\nJudges</a> case have laid that to rest with a shared primacy and  responsibility\nbetween the President and the Chief Justice of India. This has already  been\ndiscussed above.</span></span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1160\">338.  The third step is  the  issuance  of  a  warrant  of  appointment  (or\ncommission). It is quite clear  that  the  warrant  of  appointment  can  be\nissued only by the President. There is not and cannot be any  dispute  about\nthis. Under the circumstances it is clear that  the  executive  function  of\nthe President remains intact, unlike what the learned Attorney-General  says\nand there  is  no  scope  for  the  recitation  of  the  \u2018judges  appointing\njudges\u2019mantra.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1161\">339.  It is perhaps this simple  three-step  process  that  the  Constituent\nAssembly intended. But this got distorted over  the  years,  thanks  to  the\ninterference by the political executive in the first and second steps.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1162\">340.  In a Report entitled  \u2018Judicial  Independence:  Law  and  Practice  of\nAppointments to the European  Court  of  Human  Rights\u2019[537]  the  interplay\nbetween the  Rule  of  Law,  the  independence  of  the  judiciary  and  the\nappointment of judges is commented upon and in a reference to  international\nstandards, it is said that the appointment of judges plays  a  key  role  in\nsafeguarding the independence of the judiciary. This is what was said:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_724\">\u201cThe independence of the judiciary is one of the cornerstones  of  the  rule\nof law. Rather than  being  elected  by  the  people,  judges  derive  their\nauthority and legitimacy from their independence  from  political  or  other\ninterference. It is clear from the  existing  international  standards  that\nthe  selection  and  appointment  of  judges  plays  a  key  role   in   the\nsafeguarding of  judicial  independence  and  ensuring  the  most  competent\nindividuals are selected.\u201d\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1163\">341.  India is a part of the Commonwealth and  The  Commonwealth  Principles\non the accountability of and the relationship between the three branches  of\ngovernment[538] provide, inter alia,  with  regard  to  the  appointment  of\njudges, as follows:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_725\">\u201cAn independent, impartial, honest and competent judiciary  is  integral  to\nupholding the rule of law,  engendering  public  confidence  and  dispensing\njustice. The function of the judiciary is to interpret  and  apply  national\nconstitutions and legislation, consistent with  international  human  rights\nconventions and international law, to the extent permitted by  the  domestic\nlaw of each  Commonwealth  country.  To  secure  these  aims:  (a)  Judicial\nappointments should be made on the basis of clearly defined criteria and  by\na  publicly  declared  process.  The  process  should  ensure:  equality  of\nopportunity for all who are eligible for  judicial  office;  appointment  on\nmerit; and that appropriate consideration is  given  to  the  need  for  the\nprogressive attainment of gender equity and the removal  of  other  historic\nfactors of discrimination;\u201d[539]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1164\">342.  Jack Straw was the Lord Chancellor in the United Kingdom from 2007  to\n2010. He delivered the  64th  series  of  Hamlyn  Lectures  in  2012  titled\n\u2018Aspects of Law Reform \u2013 An Insider\u2019s Perspective\u2019. The 3rd lecture in  that\nseries  was  delivered  by  him  on  4th   December,   2012   on   \u2018Judicial\nAppointments\u2019. In that lecture, he says:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_726\">\u201cThe appointment of judges -  by  whom,  according  to  what  standards  and\nprocess, and with what outcome \u2013 is of critical importance.  To  maintain  a\njudiciary that is independent, which makes good decisions, and in  whom  the\npublic can continue  to  have  confidence,  we  need  to  appoint  the  most\nmeritorious candidates and secure a  judiciary  that  is  as  reflective  as\npossible of the society it is serving.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_727\">And we need to get it right first time, every time, because, once  appointed\nto a full-time salaried position, judges may  not  be  removed  from  office\nother than in the most extreme of circumstances.\u201d[540]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1165\">343.  Therefore, in the appointment of a judge, it is not  only  (negatively\nexpressed) that a \u2018wrong person\u2019 should not  be  appointed  but  (positively\nexpressed) the best talent, amongst lawyers and judicial officers should  be\nappointed as judges of the High Court and the best  amongst  the  judges  of\nthe High Courts or amongst advocates  or  distinguished  jurists  should  be\nappointed to the Supreme Court. It has been stated in  the  14th  Report  of\nthe LCI that the selection  of  judges  is  of  pivotal  importance  to  the\nprogress of the nation and that responsibility must be exercised with  great\ncare.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1166\">344.   In  the  Report  on  Judicial  Independence:  Law  and  Practice   of\nAppointments to the European Court of Human Rights, great emphasis was  laid\non the procedure  for  the  appointment  of  judges  and  the  criteria  for\nappointment. It was said:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_728\">\u201cThe issue of how judges are appointed is important in two respects.  First,\nappointment  procedures  impact   directly   upon   the   independence   and\nimpartiality of the judiciary. Since the legitimacy and credibility  of  any\njudicial institution depends upon public confidence in its independence,  it\nis imperative  that  appointment  procedures  for  judicial  office  conform\nto\u2014and  are  seen  to  conform  to\u2014international   standards   on   judicial\nindependence. It would be anomalous and unacceptable if the Court  [European\nCourt of Human  Rights]  failed  to  meet  the  international  human  rights\nstandards that it is charged with implementing,  including  the  requirement\nthat cases are heard by an independent and impartial court of law.\nSecond, without the effective implementation of \u2018objective  and  transparent\ncriteria based on proper professional  qualification,\u2019  there  is  the  very\nreal possibility that the  judges  selected  will  not  have  the  requisite\nskills and abilities to discharge their mandate.  Declining  standards  will\nultimately impact negatively on the standing of the  Court  [European  Court\nof Human Rights], as well as on the application  and  development  of  human\nrights law on the international and (ultimately) national level.\u201d\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1167\">345.  <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_532\">In the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1987\">First Judges</a> case, the question of  appointment  of  judges  as\nbeing integral to the independence of the judiciary was  not  an  issue  but\nJustice Venkataramiah expressed the view that it is difficult to  hold  that\nif the appointment of judges is left to the executive, it  will  impair  the\nindependence of the judiciary. The learned judge was of the view that it  is\nonly  \u2018after  such  appointment  the  executive  should  have  no  scope  to\ninterfere with the work of a judge.\u2019[541] This view is,  with  respect,  far\ntoo narrow and constricted. However, Justice D.A.  Desai  held  a  different\nview which was expressed in the following words:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_729\">\u201cNow, the independence of the judiciary can  be  fully  safeguarded  not  by\nmerely conferring security on the Judges during their term of office but  by\nensuring in addition that persons who are independent, upright  and  of  the\nhighest character are appointed as Judges. Moreover,  there  is  always  the\nfear that appointments left to the absolute  discretion  of  the  appointing\nexecutive could be influenced by party considerations.\u201d[542]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1168\">346.  <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_533\">In the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1988\">Second Judges</a> case  Justice  Pandian  was  quite  explicit  and\nexpressed the view that the selection and appointment of a  proper  and  fit\ncandidate to the superior judiciary is inseparable from the independence  of\nthe judiciary and a vital condition in securing it.[543] Similarly,  Justice\nKuldip Singh also held that there cannot be an  independent  judiciary  when\nthe power of appointment of judges rests with the  executive  and  that  the\nindependence of the judiciary is \u2018inextricably  linked  and  connected  with\nthe  constitutional  process  of  appointment  of  judges  of   the   higher\njudiciary.\u2019[544]  Justice Verma, speaking for the  majority,  expressed  the\nview  that  all  constitutional  authorities  involved  in  the  process  of\nappointing judges of the superior courts  \u2018should  be  fully  alive  to  the\nserious implications of their constitutional obligation and  be  zealous  in\nits discharge in order  to  ensure  that  no  doubtful  appointment  can  be\nmade.\u2019[545] The learned judge further said  that  the  independence  of  the\njudiciary can be  safeguarded  by  preventing  the  influence  of  political\nconsideration  in   making   appointment   of   judges   to   the   superior\njudiciary.[546]</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1169\">347.  There is, therefore, no doubt that the appointment of a judge  to  the\nSupreme Court or the High Court is an integral part of the  independence  of\nthe judiciary. It is not  possible  to  agree  with  the  learned  Attorney-\nGeneral when he says that though the appointment of a judge  is  a  part  of\nthe independence of the judiciary, it is but a small part and certainly  not\na predominant part. I would say that it is really the foundational  part  of\nthe independence of the judiciary.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1170\">348.  Shimon Shetreet has this to say on the appointment of judges:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_730\">\u201cIn any system, the methods of appointment have direct bearing on  both  the\nintegrity and independence  of  the  judges.  Weak  appointments  lower  the\nstatus of the judiciary in the eyes of the public and create  a  climate  in\nwhich  the  necessary  independence  of  the  judiciary  is  likely  to   be\nundermined. Similarly, political appointments that are seen  by  the  public\nas not based on merit may arouse concern about the judge\u2019s independence  and\nimpartiality on the bench. The  quality  of  judicial  appointments  depends\nupon the process and standards applied by the  appointing  authorities,  yet\nevery appointment system has its limitation.  It  is  difficult  to  predict\nwhat sort of judge a man or woman  will  be  and  irreversible  mistakes  in\njudicial  appointments  are  bound  to  occur,  even  when  the  method   of\nappointment is fair and efficient and the standards are high,  as  they  are\nin England. Such errors in selection apply  equally  to  appointing  persons\nwho were unfit for occupying  a  judicial  office  as  well  as  failing  to\nappoint a person who might have been a good judge.\u201d[547]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1171\">349.  How do international conventions  look  at  this  issue?  The  Beijing\nStatement of Principles of the Independence of the Judiciary in the  LAWASIA\nRegion[548] provides, inter alia, as follows:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_731\">\u201cIndependence of the Judiciary requires that; a) The judiciary shall  decide\nmatters before it in accordance with its impartial assessment of  the  facts\nand its understanding of the law  without  improper  influences,  direct  or\nindirect, from any source; and b) The judiciary has  jurisdiction,  directly\nor by way of review, over all issues of a justiciable nature.[549]\n\n\n\n\n\nTo  enable  the  judiciary  to  achieve  its  objectives  and  perform   its\nfunctions, it is essential that judges be chosen  on  the  basis  of  proven\ncompetence, integrity and independence.[550]\nThe mode  of  appointment  of  judges  must  be  such  as  will  ensure  the\nappointment of persons who are best qualified for judicial office.  It  must\nprovide safeguards against improper influences being taken into  account  so\nthat  only  persons  of   competence,   integrity   and   independence   are\nappointed.\u201d[551]\n\n\nThis document was signed by Justice S.C. Agrawal of this Court  representing\nChief Justice A. M. Ahmadi.\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1172\">350.  The Bangalore Principles of Judicial Conduct, 2002 which lay down  six\nessential values for a judge (and which  are  accepted  world-wide  both  in\ncivil law and common law countries) would be totally unworkable if a  person\nappointed as a judge, at the time of  appointment,  lacks  basic  competence\nand independence.[552]  Given all these considerations, it must be held  and\nis held that the process for appointment and the  actual  appointment  of  a\njudge to a High Court or the Supreme Court is  a  predominant  part  of  the\nindependence of the judiciary and, therefore, an integral part of the  basic\nstructure of the Constitution.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1173\">351.  Therefore, the procedure for the appointment of judges of the  Supreme\nCourt or the High Courts can impact on the  independence  of  the  judiciary\nand the basic structure of the Constitution.\n</p>\n\n\n\n\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1174\">The recommendation process\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1175\">352.  How can the President ensure  that  the  most  deserving  persons  are\nappointed as judges or that they are not denied  appointment?  This  is  the\nnub of the controversy before us and this is the problem that has vexed  the\nexecutive, the judiciary, academia, the legal fraternity and  civil  society\nover  several  decades.  Since   justice   delivery   is   undoubtedly   the\nresponsibility of the judiciary, therefore, the judiciary (symbolized as  it\nwere by the Chief Justice of India) is obliged to ensure that only the  most\ndeserving persons are considered for appointment as judges.[553]\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1176\">353.  The process of consideration of a person for appointment  as  a  judge\nis important both at a stage prior to the recommendation being made  by  the\nChief  Justice  of  India   in   consultation   with   his/her   colleagues,\nconstituting a \u2018collegium\u2019 and also after the recommendation is sent by  the\nChief Justice of India to the executive. At  both  stages,  the  process  is\nparticipatory. In  the  pre-recommendation  stage,  it  is  a  participatory\nprocess involving  the  Chief  Justice  of  India  and  his/her  colleagues,\nconstituting the collegium.[554] It is at this stage that the Chief  Justice\nof India takes the opinion of the other judges and anybody else,  if  deemed\nnecessary. This stage also  includes  the  participation  of  the  executive\nbecause it is at this stage that the Chief Justice of India receives  inputs\nfrom the executive about  the  frailties,  if  any,  of  a  person  who  may\neventually be appointed a judge. In the post-recommendation stage  also  the\nprocess is participatory but primarily with the executive in the  event  the\nexecutive has some objection to the appointment of a particular  person  for\nstrong and cogent reasons to be recorded in writing.[555] Therefore, when  a\nperson is considered for appointment as a  judge,  there  is  extensive  and\nintensive participatory consultation within the judiciary before  the  Chief\nJustice of India actually recommends a person for appointment  as  a  judge;\nand after the recommendation is made,  there  is  consultation  between  the\nexecutive and the judiciary before the process is carried further. What  can\nbe a more meaningful  consultation  postulated  by  <a href=\"/doc/1995948/\" id=\"a_1989\">Article  124(2)</a>  of  the\nConstitution?\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1177\">354.  If a person is not recommended for appointment by  the  Chief  Justice\nof India or the Chief Justice of  a  High  Court,  the  chapter  of  his/her\nappointment closes at that stage. And, if there is no difference of  opinion\nbetween the constitutional functionaries about the suitability of  a  person\nfor appointment then, of course, there are no hurdles to the issuance  of  a\nwarrant of appointment.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1178\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_534\">355.  The difficulty in considering and accepting  a  recommendation  arises\nonly if there is a difference of opinion during  consultations  between  the\nexecutive and the judiciary. <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1990\">The Second  Judges</a>  case  effectively  resolves\nthis controversy.</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1179\">356.  At the  pre-recommendation  stage,  it  is  quite  possible  that  the\nexecutive is in possession of material  regarding  some  personal  trait  or\nweakness of character of a lawyer or a judge that is not known to the  Chief\nJustice of India or the Chief Justice  of  the  High  Court  and  which  may\npotentially disentitle that person from being appointed a judge. It is  then\nfor the executive, as a consultant, to bring this  information  or  material\nto the notice of the Chief Justice of India.[556] Since  the  judiciary  has\nthe responsibility of recommending an appropriate candidate for  appointment\nas a judge, primacy is accorded to the view of the judiciary (symbolized  by\nthe view of the Chief Justice of India)  that  will  weigh  and  objectively\nconsider the material or information  and  take  a  final  decision  on  the\ndesirability of the appointment.[557] The Chief Justice of  India  may,  for\ngood reason, accept the view of the executive or may, also for good  reason,\nnot  accept  the  view  of  the  executive.   It  is  in  this  sense   that\n\u2018consultation\u2019 occurring  in  <a href=\"/doc/1995948/\" id=\"a_1991\">Article  124(2)</a>  and  <a href=\"/doc/259647/\" id=\"a_1992\">Article  217(1)</a>  of  the\nConstitution has to be understood. Primacy  to  the  judiciary  is  accorded\nonly to this limited  extent,  but  subject  to  a  proviso  which  will  be\ndiscussed a little later.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1180\">357.  Why is it that limited primacy has been  accorded  to  the  judiciary?\nThat the judiciary is the best suited to take a decision  whether  a  person\nshould be appointed a judge  or  not  is  implicit  in  <a href=\"/doc/1995948/\" id=\"a_1993\">Article  124(2)</a>  and\n<a href=\"/doc/259647/\" id=\"a_1994\">Article 217(1)</a> of the Constitution. In <a href=\"/doc/1995948/\" id=\"a_1995\">Article 124(2)</a> of  the  Constitution,\nthe President is mandated to consult the Chief Justice of  India  and  \u2018such\nof the Judges of the Supreme Court and of the High Courts in the  States  as\nthe President may deem necessary.\u2019 That the President may choose to  consult\neminent persons from the  legal  fraternity  or  civil  society  is  another\nmatter, but the President is not required to do  so.  One  of  the  possible\nreasons for this could be that the Constitution framers were of the  opinion\nthat ultimately  what  is  important  is  the  opinion  of  judges  and  not\nnecessarily of others. Similarly, for the appointment  of  a  judge  of  the\nHigh Court under <a href=\"/doc/259647/\" id=\"a_1996\">Article  217(1)</a>  of  the  Constitution,  the  President  is\nrequired to consult the Chief Justice of India, the Governor  of  the  State\nand the Chief Justice of the High Court \u2013 again not anybody  else  from  the\nlegal fraternity or civil society.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1181\">358.  Similarly, limited primacy is accorded to the political executive.  In\nthe event the judiciary does not make a  unanimous  recommendation  for  the\nappointment of a judge  of  the  Supreme  Court  or  the  High  Courts,  the\nPresident  is  entitled  to  turn  down  the  recommendation.  But  if   the\nrecommendation  is  unanimous  but  returned  for  reconsideration  by   the\nPresident and thereafter unanimously reiterated by the judiciary,  then  the\nCouncil of Ministers is bound by the decision  of  the  judiciary  and  must\nadvise the President accordingly.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1182\">359.  Since the Constitution is a flexible document, neither  the  President\nnor the Chief Justice of India is precluded from taking the  advice  of  any\nperson, lay or professional. In fact, Justice Verma stated in  an  interview\nin this regard as follows:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_732\">\u201cCan you throw light on how, during your tenure  as  the  CJI,  appointments\ntook place?\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_733\">For every Supreme Court appointment, I consulted senior  lawyers  like  Fali\nS. Nariman and Shanthi Bhushan. I used to consult five or six  top  lawyers.\nI used to consult even  lawyers  belonging  to  the  middle  level.  Similar\nconsultation took place in the case of High Courts. I  recorded  details  of\nevery consultation. I wish all my correspondence is made public.  After  the\nappointment, why should it be secret? If there is a good reason  to  appoint\nthe Judges, then at least the doubts people cast on them even now  will  not\nbe there. And if there is a good  reason  why  they  should  not  have  been\nappointed, then it would expose the persons who were responsible  for  their\nappointment.\u201d[558]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1183\"> 360.       It is this pragmatic interpretation  of  the  Constitution  that\nwas  postulated  by  the  Constituent  Assembly,  which  did  not  feel  the\nnecessity of filling up every detail in the document, as indeed it  was  not\npossible to do so.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1184\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_535\">361.  Leaving aside the discussion on  the  textual  interpretation  of  the\nconstitutional  provisions  and  the   Constituent   Assembly   debates,   a\nconstitutional convention has evolved over the last more than seven  decades\nof accepting the opinion of the  Chief  Justice  in  the  appointment  of  a\nperson as a judge of a superior Court. This  constitutional  convention  has\nexisted, if not from the days of the Government  of  India  Act,  1919  then\ncertainly from  the  days  of  the  <a href=\"/doc/93056159/\" id=\"a_1997\">Government  of  India  Act,  1935</a>.  This\nconstitutional convention  has  been  exhaustively  dealt  with  by  Justice\nKuldip Singh in  the  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_1998\">Second  Judges</a>  case  and  it  was  concluded  that  a\nconstitutional convention is as binding as constitutional law.[559]  In  any\nevent, there is no cogent reason to discard a constitutional  convention  if\nit is working well.</span> <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"174974\" id=\"span_536\">At this stage, it is useful to  recall  the  comment  of\nChief Justice Beg in <a href=\"/doc/174974/\" id=\"a_1999\">State of Rajasthan v.  Union  of  India</a>[560]  that:  \u2018\u2026\nconstitutional  practice  and  convention  become  so  interlinked  with  or\nattached to constitutional provisions and are often so important  and  vital\nfor grasping the real purpose  and  function  of  constitutional  provisions\nthat the two cannot often be viewed  apart.\u2019  This  is  precisely  what  has\nhappened in the present case where constitutional conventions and  practices\nare so interlinked to the constitutional provisions that they are  difficult\nto disassemble.</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1185\">362.   It  is  this   constitutional   interpretation   and   constitutional\nconvention that results in binding the recommendation of the  Chief  Justice\nof India on the executive that is  objected  to  by  the  learned  Attorney-\nGeneral as being contrary to the Constitution as framed and it is this  that\nis sought to be \u2018corrected\u2019 by the 99th Constitution Amendment Act.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1186\">363.  The issue may be looked at  from  yet  another  angle.  Assuming,  the\nexecutive rejects the recommendation of the  Chief  Justice  of  India  even\nafter its unanimous reiteration, what is the solution to  the  impasse  that\nis created? The answer is to be found in Samsher  Singh  and  reiterated  in\nSankalchand Sheth. It was held in Samsher Singh that in such an  event,  the\ndecision of the executive is open to judicial scrutiny. It was said:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_734\">\u201cIn all conceivable  cases  consultation  with  that  highest  dignitary  of\nIndian justice will and should be accepted by the Government  of  India  and\nthe Court will have an  opportunity  to  examine  if  any  other  extraneous\ncircumstances have entered into the verdict of the Minister, if  he  departs\nfrom the counsel given by the Chief Justice of India.\u201d[561]\n\nThis view was  reiterated  in  Sankalchand  Sheth.[562]  Of  course,  it  is\nanother matter that no one has a right to  be  appointed  as  a  judge,  but\ncertainly if the unanimous  recommendation  of  the  judiciary  through  the\nChief Justice of India is not accepted by the President,  if  nothing  else,\nat least the record will be put straight and  the  possible  damage  to  the\ndignity, reputation and honour of the person  who  was  recommended  by  the\nChief Justice of India will be restored,  at  least  to  some  extent.  364.\nBut is judicial review necessarily the only answer  to  a  problem  of  this\nnature? Should the executive and  the  judiciary  ever  be  on  a  collision\ncourse in the appointment of a judge? Not only did Dr. Ambedkar  think  that\nsuch a situation would not occur, he never visualized it. Dr. Ambedkar  made\nprovision for virtually every contingency, except a  stalemate  or  deadlock\nsituation - he never imagined that such an eventuality would ever arise.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_735\">365.   That  there  would  be  no  difference  or  little  difference  or  a\nmanageable difference  of  opinion  between  the  President  and  the  Chief\nJustice of India or that the judiciary  should  have  a  final  say  in  the\nmatter so as not to make the  consultation  process  a  mere  formality,  is\nquite apparent from the fact  that  the  Constituent  Assembly  deliberately\ndrew a distinction between the appointment by the President of  a  judge  of\nthe Supreme Court and a judge of the High Court (on the one  hand)  and  the\nappointment by the President of other constitutional  authorities.  For  the\nappointment of a  judge,  it  is  mandated  in  the  Constitution  that  the\nPresident must consult the Chief Justice of India. However, to  appoint  the\nComptroller and Auditor General under <a href=\"/doc/1422498/\" id=\"a_2000\">Article 148</a> of the  Constitution  (for\nexample), the President is under no such obligation to consult anybody  even\nthough the position is one of vital importance. Dr.  Ambedkar  had  said  in\nthis regard:\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_736\">\u201cI  cannot  say  I  am  very  happy  about  the  position  which  the  Draft\nConstitution,  including  the  amendments  which  have  been  moved  to  the\narticles relating to the Auditor-General in  this  House,  assigns  to  him.\nPersonally speaking for myself, I am  of  opinion  that  this  dignitary  or\nofficer is probably the  most  important  officer  in  the  Constitution  of\nIndia. He is the one man who is going to see  that  the  expenses  voted  by\nParliament are not exceeded, or varied from  what  has  been  <a href=\"/doc/174974/\" id=\"a_2001\">laid  down  by</a>\nParliament in what is called the <a href=\"/doc/1152308/\" id=\"a_2002\">Appropriation Act</a>. If this  functionary  is\nto carry out the duties - and his duties, I submit, are far  more  important\nthan the duties even of the judiciary - he should  have  been  certainly  as\nindependent as the Judiciary. But, comparing the articles about the  Supreme\nCourt and the articles  relating  to  the  Auditor-General,  I  cannot  help\nsaying that we have not given him the same independence which we have  given\nto the Judiciary, although I personally feel  that  he  ought  to  have  far\ngreater independence than the Judiciary itself.\u201d[563]\n\nSimilarly, the appointment  of  the  Chief  Election  Commissioner  and  the\nElection Commissioners under  <a href=\"/doc/950881/\" id=\"a_2003\">Article  324</a>  of  the  Constitution  does  not\nrequire the  President  to  consult  anybody,  even  though  free  and  fair\nelections are undoubtedly vital to our  democracy.  Since  the  consultation\nprovision was incorporated only for the appointment of judges,  surely,  the\nConstituent Assembly had good reasons for making this  distinction.  Justice\nKhehar  has  referred  to  other  Presidential  appointments  in  his  draft\njudgment and it is not necessary to repeat them. What is\n\nimportant is  the  \u2018message\u2019  sought  to  be  conveyed  by  the  Constituent\nAssembly and the sanctity given to a recommendation by the Chief Justice  of\nIndia for the appointment of a judge  of  the  Supreme  Court  or  the  High\nCourt.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_737\">366.  It is trite that the Constitution is a living  document.[564]  Keeping\nthis in mind, could it be said  that  a  strained  interpretation  has  been\ngiven to <a href=\"/doc/1995948/\" id=\"a_2004\">Article 124(2)</a> and <a href=\"/doc/259647/\" id=\"a_2005\">Article 217(1)</a> of the Constitution  particularly\nwhen the substitution of \u2018consultation\u2019 with \u2018concurrence\u2019 in the  draft  of\n<a href=\"/doc/1164880/\" id=\"a_2006\">Article  124</a>  was  discussed   in   the   Constituent   Assembly   and   not\naccepted?[565] Definitely not, particularly if one looks at the  context  in\nwhich \u2018consultation\u2019 is used and the purpose for which it is  used,  namely,\nto fetter the discretion of the President by someone who knows  what  is  in\nthe best interests of the judiciary.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_738\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_537\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_538\">367.  But, as mentioned earlier, it is not  necessary  to  dwell  at  length\nupon the correctness or otherwise of the procedure for the appointment of  a\njudge as <a href=\"/doc/174974/\" id=\"a_2007\">laid down in</a> the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2008\">Second Judges</a> case and the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2009\">Third Judges</a> case.  The\nquestion really is whether the change in the  procedure  of  appointment  of\njudges violates the basic structure of the Constitution.  Can the  Judiciary\nbe independent if the appointment process is in the hands  of  the  National\nJudicial Appointments Commission?</span></span>\n</blockquote>\n\n\n\n\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1187\">Amendment of the Constitution through <a href=\"/doc/594125/\" id=\"a_2010\">Article 368</a>\n\n\n\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1188\">368.  Proceeding on the basis, as we should, that the  independence  of  the\njudiciary is a part of the basic structure of  the  Constitution,  and  that\nthe appointment of a judge to the Supreme  Court  or  a  High  Court  is  an\nintegral and foundational part of the independence  of  the  judiciary,  the\nquestion that arises is to what extent,  if  at  all,  can  the  appointment\nprocess be tinkered with by Parliament.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1189\">369.  <a href=\"/doc/594125/\" id=\"a_2011\">Article 368</a> of the Constitution provides for the \u2018Power of  Parliament\nto amend the Constitution and procedure therefor\u2019. While the power is  vast,\nempowering  Parliament  to  add,  vary  or  repeal  any  provision  of   the\nConstitution,  the  breadth  of  that  power  has  inherent  limitations  as\nexplained in Kesavananda Bharati which is that the basic  structure  of  the\nConstitution cannot be altered. What constitutes the basic structure of  the\nConstitution has been considered in several  decisions  of  this  Court  and\ndemocracy (for example) or free and fair elections  or  judicial  review  of\nlegislative action or separation (or distribution)  of  powers  between  the\nLegislature, the Executive and the Judiciary have all  been  held  to  be  a\npart of the basic structure of the Constitution. There is no doubt,  and  no\none has disputed it, that the independence of the judiciary is also  a  part\nof the basic structure of the Constitution.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1190\">370.  The constitutional requirement for amending the Constitution  is:  (a)\nThe amendment may be initiated only  by  the  introduction of   a  Bill  for\nthe purpose;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1191\">(b) The Bill may be moved in either House of Parliament; (c) The Bill  ought\nto be passed in each House by a majority of the  total  membership  of  that\nHouse and by a majority of not less than two-thirds of the members  of  that\nHouse present and voting; (d) The Bill shall be presented to  the  President\nwho shall give his assent to the Bill and thereupon the  Constitution  shall\nstand amended in accordance with the terms of that Bill.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1192\">371.  There is a proviso to <a href=\"/doc/594125/\" id=\"a_2012\">Article 368</a> of  the  Constitution  and  for  the\npresent purposes, the further requirement is that \u2018if such  amendment  seeks\nto make any change\u2019 in Chapter IV  of  Part  V  (The  Union  Judiciary)  and\nChapter V of Part VI (The High Courts in the States)  the  amendment  \u2018shall\nalso require to be ratified by the Legislatures of the States by  resolution\nto  that  effect  passed  by  those  Legislatures  before  the  Bill  making\nprovision for such amendment is presented to the President for assent.\u2019\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1193\">372.  As far the  Constitution  (One  Hundred  and  Twenty-first  Amendment)\nBill, 2014 is concerned, there is no doubt or  dispute  that  the  procedure\nmentioned above was  followed  and  that  it  received  the  assent  of  the\nPresident on 31st December, 2014. To that extent the  Constitution  (Ninety-\nninth) Amendment Act, 2014 is a procedurally valid legislation.\n</p>\n\n\n\n\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1194\">Limitations to amending the Constitution\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1195\">373.  To appreciate the  inherent  limitations  placed  on  Parliament  with\nregard to an amendment to the Constitution, it is necessary to consider  the\nviews constituting the majority in Kesavananda Bharati. In  that  case,  the\nquestion before this Court (as framed by Chief Justice Sikri) was:  What  is\nthe  extent  of  the  amending  power  conferred  by  <a href=\"/doc/594125/\" id=\"a_2013\">Article  368</a>  of   the\nConstitution, apart from <a href=\"/doc/772605/\" id=\"a_2014\">Article 13(2)</a> on Parliament?\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1196\">374.  The learned Chief Justice noted that  the  word  \u2018amendment\u2019  has  not\nbeen defined in the Constitution. In some provisions of the Constitution  it\nhas a narrow  meaning,  while  in  other  provisions  it  has  an  expansive\nmeaning. This view was expressed by Justice Shelat  and  Justice  Grover  as\nwell, who observed that the words \u2018amendment\u2019 and \u2018amend\u2019 have been used  to\nconvey different meanings in different provisions of  the  Constitution.  In\nsome Articles these words have a narrow meaning while in others the  meaning\nis much larger or broader. The word is not one of  precise  import  and  has\nnot been used in different provisions of  the  Constitution  to  convey  the\nsame meaning. This is of some significance since it is on  this  basis  that\nthis Court referred to the  CAD  to  interpret  the  words  \u2018amendment\u2019  and\n\u2018amend\u2019.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1197\">375.  On a reading of various provisions of  the  Constitution  the  learned\nChief  Justice  concluded   that   the   expression   \u2018amendment   of   this\nConstitution\u2019 occurring in <a href=\"/doc/594125/\" id=\"a_2015\">Article 368</a> thereof would mean  any  addition  or\nchange in any provision of the Constitution \u2018within the  broad  contours  of\nthe Preamble and the  Constitution  to  carry  out  the  objectives  in  the\nPreamble and the directive principles. Applied  to  fundamental  rights,  it\nwould mean that while fundamental rights  cannot  be  abrogated,  reasonable\nabridgments  of  fundamental  rights  can  be   effected   in   the   public\ninterest.\u2019[566] In this context, the learned Chief Justice referred  to  the\nUniversal Declaration of Human Rights to conclude  that  certain  rights  of\nindividuals are inalienable.[567]\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1198\">376.  The learned Chief Justice concluded by holding, inter alia:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_739\">\u201cThe expression \u201camendment of this Constitution\u201d does not enable  Parliament\nto abrogate or take away fundamental rights  or  to  completely  change  the\nfundamental features of the Constitution so  as  to  destroy  its  identity.\nWithin these limits Parliament can amend every article.\u201d[568]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1199\">377.  Justice Shelat and Justice Grover looked at the text  of  <a href=\"/doc/594125/\" id=\"a_2016\">Article  368</a>\nas it stood prior to its amendment by the 24th  Constitution  Amendment  Act\nand observed that there is intrinsic evidence to suggest that  the  amending\npower of Parliament is limited. However widely worded the  power  might  be,\nit cannot be used to render  the  Constitution  to  lose  its  character  or\nnature or identity and it has to be exercised within the  framework  of  the\nConstitution. It was observed that an unlimited power  of  amendment  cannot\nbe conducive to the survival of the Constitution.  On  this  basis,  it  was\nconcluded that:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_740\">\u201cThe meaning of the words  \u201camendment  of  this  Constitution\u201d  as  used  in\n<a href=\"/doc/594125/\" id=\"a_2017\">Article 368</a> must be such which  accords  with  the  true  intention  of  the\nConstitution-makers as ascertainable from  the  historical  background,  the\nPreamble,  the  entire  scheme  of  the  Constitution,  its  structure   and\nframework and the intrinsic evidence in various articles  including  Article\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_741\">368. It is neither possible to give it a narrow meaning nor can such a  wide\nmeaning be given which can enable the amending body to change  substantially\nor entirely the structure and identity of the Constitution.\u201d[569]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1200\">378.  Justice Hegde and Justice Mukherjea observed that <a href=\"/doc/594125/\" id=\"a_2018\">Article  368</a>  cannot\nbe interpreted in a narrow and pedantic manner but must  be  given  a  broad\nand liberal interpretation. It was observed that the  word  \u2018amendment\u2019  has\nno precise meaning and that it is a \u2018colourless\u2019 word. In  fact,  the  words\n\u2018amendment\u2019 and \u2018amend\u2019 have been used  in  the  Constitution  in  different\nplaces  with  different  connotations.  Notwithstanding  this,  the  learned\njudges were of the view that the meaning of these expressions cannot  be  as\nexpansive as to  enable  Parliament  to  change  the  \u2018personality\u2019  of  the\nConstitution since its scheme and  structure  proceed  \u2018on  the  basis  that\nthere are certain basic  features  which  are  expected  to  be  permanent.\u2019\nTherefore, the amending power under  <a href=\"/doc/594125/\" id=\"a_2019\">Article  368</a>  of  the  Constitution  is\nsubject to implied limitations.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1201\">379.  Having considered all these  factors,  the  learned  judges  concluded\nthat:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_742\">\u201cOn a careful consideration of the various  aspects  of  the  case,  we  are\nconvinced that the Parliament has no power to  abrogate  or  emasculate  the\nbasic elements or fundamental features  of  the  Constitution  such  as  the\nsovereignty of India, the democratic character of our polity, the  unity  of\nthe country, the essential features of the individual  freedoms  secured  to\nthe citizens. Nor has the Parliament the power  to  revoke  the  mandate  to\nbuild a welfare State and egalitarian society. These  limitations  are  only\nillustrative and not exhaustive. Despite these limitations,  however,  there\ncan be no question that the amending power is a wide power  and  it  reaches\nevery Article and every part of the Constitution. That power can be used  to\nreshape the Constitution to fulfil the obligation imposed on the  State.  It\ncan also be used to reshape the Constitution  within  the  limits  mentioned\nearlier, to make it an effective instrument for social good. We  are  unable\nto agree with the contention that in order to build a welfare State,  it  is\nnecessary to destroy some of the human freedoms. That, at any  rate  is  not\nthe perspective of our Constitution.\u201d[570]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1202\">380.  Justice Khanna dwelt on the basic structure of  the  Constitution  and\nexpressed the view that \u2018amendment\u2019 postulates the  survival  of  the  \u2018old\u2019\nConstitution without loss of its identity and the  retention  of  the  basic\nstructure or framework of the \u2018old\u2019 Constitution. It was held:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_743\">\u201cAlthough it is permissible under the power of amendment to effect  changes,\nhowsoever important,  and  to  adapt  the  system  to  the  requirements  of\nchanging conditions, it is not permissible to touch  the  foundation  or  to\nalter  the  basic  institutional  pattern.  The  words  \u201camendment  of   the\nConstitution\u201d with all their  wide  sweep  and  amplitude  cannot  have  the\neffect of destroying or abrogating the basic structure or framework  of  the\nConstitution.\u201d\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1203\">381.  Thereafter, Justice Khanna travelled much further than  necessary  and\nheld that as long as the basic structure and framework of  the  Constitution\nis retained, the plenary power of amendment  \u2018would  include  within  itself\nthe power to add, alter or  repeal  the  various  articles  including  those\nrelating to fundamental rights.\u2019 The rationale for this was given  a  little\nlater in the judgment in the following words:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_744\">\u201cThe word \u201camendment\u201d in <a href=\"/doc/594125/\" id=\"a_2020\">Article 368</a> must carry  the  same  meaning  whether\nthe amendment relates to taking away  or  abridging  fundamental  rights  in\nPart III of the Constitution or whether it pertains to some other  provision\noutside Part III of the Constitution.  No  serious  objection  is  taken  to\nrepeal, addition or alteration of provisions of the Constitution other  than\nthose in Part III under the power of amendment  conferred  by  <a href=\"/doc/594125/\" id=\"a_2021\">Article  368.</a>\nThe same approach, in my  opinion,  should  hold  good  when  we  deal  with\namendment relating to fundamental  rights  contained  in  Part  III  of  the\nConstitution. It would be impermissible to differentiate between  scope  and\nwidth of power of amendment when it deals with fundamental  right,  and  the\nscope and width of that power when it deals with  provisions  not  concerned\nwith fundamental rights.\u201d[571]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1204\">382.  The conclusion arrived at by Justice Khanna is stated by  the  learned\njudge in the following words:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_745\">\u201cThe power of amendment under <a href=\"/doc/594125/\" id=\"a_2022\">Article 368</a> does  not  include  the  power  to\nabrogate the Constitution nor does it include the power to alter  the  basic\nstructure or framework of the Constitution. Subject to the retention of  the\nbasic structure or framework of the Constitution, the power of amendment  is\nplenary and includes within itself the power to amend the  various  articles\nof the Constitution, including those relating to fundamental rights as  well\nas those which may be said to relate to essential features.  No  part  of  a\nfundamental right can  claim  immunity  from  amendatory  process  by  being\ndescribed as the essence, or core of that  right.  The  power  of  amendment\nwould also include within itself the power  to  add,  alter  or  repeal  the\nvarious articles.\u201d[572]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1205\">383.  It may be mentioned en passant that the aforesaid  view  expressed  by\nJustice Khanna generated much controversy.  That  was  adverted  to  by  the\nlearned judge in Indira Nehru Gandhi and it was clarified in paragraphs  251\nand 252 of the Report that the \u2018offending\u2019 passages were in the  context  of\nthe extent of the amending power  and  not  in  the  context  of  the  basic\nstructure of the Constitution. The learned judge clarified that  fundamental\nrights were a part of the basic structure of the Constitution but the  right\nto property was not.[573]\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1206\">384.   Simplistically  put,  the  sum  and  substance  of  the  decision  in\nKesavananda Bharati is that it recognized that the Constitution has a  basic\nstructure and that the basic structure of the Constitution  is  unalterable.\nPerhaps to avoid any doubts  and  since  as  many  as  nine  judgments  were\ndelivered by the thirteen judges constituting the Bench, a  summary  of  the\nconclusions was prepared. This summary was signed by nine  of  the  thirteen\njudges. Among the nine signatories were two learned judges who were  in  the\nminority. One of the conclusions agreed upon by the nine learned judges  who\nsigned the summary was: \u2018Article 368 does not  enable  Parliament  to  alter\nthe basic structure or framework of the Constitution.\u2019\n\n\n\n\nJudicial review of an amendment to the Constitution\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1207\">385.  In  Indira  Nehru  Gandhi  it  was  held  that  an  amendment  to  the\nConstitution can be challenged only on the ground of violation of the  basic\nstructure, while a statute  cannot  be  so  challenged.  A  statute  can  be\nchallenged only if it is passed by  a  Legislature  beyond  its  legislative\ncompetence or if it offends <a href=\"/doc/134715/\" id=\"a_2023\">Article 13</a> of the Constitution.[574]\n\u201cThe constitutional amendments may, on the ratio of the  Fundamental  Rights\ncase,[575] be tested on the anvil of basic structure.  But  apart  from  the\nprinciple that a case is only an authority for what it decides, it does  not\nlogically follow from the majority judgment in the Fundamental  Rights  case\nthat  ordinary  legislation  must  also  answer   the   same   test   as   a\nconstitutional amendment. Ordinary laws have to answer two tests  for  their\nvalidity: (1) The law must be  within  the  legislative  competence  of  the\nlegislature  as  defined  and  specified  in  Chapter  I,  Part  XI  of  the\nConstitution, and (2) it must not offend against the provisions  of  <a href=\"/doc/1010805/\" id=\"a_2024\">Article\n13(1)</a> and <a href=\"/doc/1456610/\" id=\"a_2025\">(2)</a> of  the  Constitution.  \u201cBasic  structure\u201d,  by  the  majority\njudgment, is not a part of the fundamental rights nor indeed a provision  of\nthe Constitution. The  theory  of  basic  structure  is  woven  out  of  the\nconspectus of the[pic]Constitution and the amending power  is  subjected  to\nit because it is a constituent power. \u201cThe power to  amend  the  fundamental\ninstrument cannot carry with it the power to destroy its essential  features\n\u2014 this, in brief, is the arch of  the  theory  of  basic  structure.  It  is\nwholly out of place in matters relating to the  validity  of  ordinary  laws\nmade under the Constitution.\u201d\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1208\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"184521\" id=\"span_539\">386.  A similar view was taken in <a href=\"/doc/184521/\" id=\"a_2026\">State of Karnataka v. Union of  India</a>[576]\nwherein  the  above  passage  from  Indira  Nehru  Gandhi  was  quoted  with\napproval. It was said by Justice  Untwalia  in  a  concurring  judgment  for\nhimself, Justice Shinghal and Justice Jaswant Singh:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_746\">\u201cMr. Sinha also contended that an ordinary law cannot go against  the  basic\nscheme or the fundamental  backbone  of  the  Centre-State  relationship  as\nenshrined in the Constitution. He put his argument  in  this  respect  in  a\nvery ingenious way because he felt difficulty in  placing  it  in  a  direct\nmanner by saying that an ordinary law cannot violate the basic structure  of\nthe Constitution. <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1240174\" id=\"span_540\">In the case of <a href=\"/doc/1240174/\" id=\"a_2027\">Smt Indira Nehru Gandhi v. Shri Raj  Narain</a>\nsuch an argument was expressly rejected by this Court. We may  rest  content\nby referring  to  a  passage  from  the  judgment  of  our  learned  brother\nChandrachud, J. which runs thus\u2026\u2026\u2026..\u201d</span>\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1209\">387.   <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1903935\" id=\"span_541\">In  <a href=\"/doc/1903935/\" id=\"a_2028\">Kuldip  Nayar  v.  Union  of  India</a>[577]  a  Constitution   Bench\nreiterated the above view in the following words:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_747\">\u201cThe basic structure theory imposes limitation on the  power  of  Parliament\nto amend the Constitution. An amendment to the  Constitution  under  <a href=\"/doc/594125/\" id=\"a_2029\">Article\n368</a> could be challenged on the ground of violation of  the  basic  structure\nof the Constitution. An ordinary legislation cannot be  so  challenged.  The\nchallenge to a law made, within its legislative  competence,  by  Parliament\non the ground of violation of the basic structure  of  the  Constitution  is\nthus not available to the petitioners.\u201d\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1210\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"63489929\" id=\"span_542\">388.  Finally, in <a href=\"/doc/63489929/\" id=\"a_2030\">Ashoka Kumar Thakur v. Union of  India</a>[578]  it  was  held\nthat a law can be challenged if it violates a provision of the  Constitution\nbut an amendment to the Constitution can be challenged only if  it  violates\na basic feature of the Constitution which is a part of its basic  structure.\nIt was held:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_748\">\u201cFor determining whether a particular feature of the  Constitution  is  part\nof the basic structure or not, it has to  be  examined  in  each  individual\ncase keeping in mind  the  scheme  of  the  Constitution,  its  objects  and\npurpose and the integrity of the Constitution as  a  fundamental  instrument\nfor the country\u2019s governance. It may be noticed  that  it  is  not  open  to\nchallenge the ordinary legislations on the  basis  of  the  basic  structure\nprinciple. State legislation can be challenged on the  question  whether  it\nis  violative  of  the  provisions  of  the  Constitution.  But  as  regards\nconstitutional amendments, if any challenge is made on the  basis  of  basic\nstructure, it has to  be  examined  based  on  the  basic  features  of  the\nConstitution.\u201d\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1211\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"181443842\" id=\"span_543\">389.  A different opinion was expressed in <a href=\"/doc/181443842/\" id=\"a_2031\">Madras Bar Association  v.  Union\nof India</a>[579] wherein it was held that the view that  an  amendment  to  the\nConstitution can be challenged on the  ground  of  violation  of  the  basic\nstructure of the Constitution is made applicable to legislation  also.  This\nwas assumed to be a logical extension of a principle. It was held:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_749\">\u201cThis Court has repeatedly held that an amendment to the provisions  of  the\nConstitution would not be sustainable if it violated the  \u201cbasic  structure\u201d\nof the Constitution, even though the  amendment  had  been  carried  out  by\nfollowing the procedure contemplated under \u201cPart XI\u201d  of  the  Constitution.\nThis leads to the determination that the \u201cbasic  structure\u201d  is  inviolable.\nIn our view, the same would apply to  all  other  legislations  (other  than\namendments to the Constitution) as well, even  though  the  legislation  had\nbeen enacted by following the  prescribed  procedure,  and  was  within  the\ndomain  of  the  enacting  legislature,  any  infringement  to  the   \u201cbasic\nstructure\u201d would be unacceptable.\u201d\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1212\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"184521\" id=\"span_544\">390.  For the purposes of the present discussion, I would prefer  to  follow\nthe view expressed by a Bench of seven learned judges in <a href=\"/doc/184521/\" id=\"a_2032\">State of  Karnataka\nv. Union of India</a> that it is only an amendment of the Constitution that  can\nbe challenged on the ground that it violates  the  basic  structure  of  the\nConstitution \u2013 a  statute  cannot  be  challenged  on  the  ground  that  it\nviolates the basic structure of the Constitution.  [The  only  exception  to\nthis perhaps could be  a  statute  placed  in  the  Ninth  Schedule  of  the\nConstitution].  The principles for challenging the  constitutionality  of  a\nstatute are quite different.</span>\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1213\">Challenge to the 99th Constitution Amendment Act \u2013 the preliminaries\n\n\n1 (a) Limitations to the challenge\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1214\">391.   The  first  submission  made  by  the  learned  Attorney-General  for\nupholding the constitutionality of the 99th Constitution Amendment  Act  was\non the basis of Kesavananda Bharati. It was submitted  that  a  Constitution\nAmendment Act can be challenged as violating  the  basic  structure  of  the\nConstitution within limited parameters, that is, only  if  it  \u2018emasculates\u2019\nthe Constitution, or \u2018abrogates\u2019 it or completely  changes  its  fundamental\nfeatures so as to destroy its identity or personality or shakes the  pillars\non which it rests. While accepting that the independence  of  the  judiciary\nis one such pillar, it was  submitted  that  a  change  in  the  method  and\nprocedure in the appointment of a judge of  the  Supreme  Court  or  a  High\nCourt does not emasculate, abrogate or shake the foundations or the  pillars\nof the independence of the judiciary.  Consequently  the  99th  Constitution\nAmendment  Act  does  not  fall  foul  of  the  basic   structure   of   the\nConstitution.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1215\">392.  This argument fails to appreciate  that  a  majority  of  the  learned\njudges constituting the Bench that decided Kesavananda Bharati were  of  the\nopinion  that  it  is  enough  to  declare  a  constitutional  amendment  as\nviolating  the  basic  structure  if  it   alters   the   basic   structure.\nUndoubtedly, some of the learned judges have used very strong words  in  the\ncourse of their judgment \u2013 emasculate, destroy, abrogate, and  substantially\nchange the identity etc. but when  it  came  to  stating  what  is  the  law\nactually laid down, the majority decided that \u2018Article 368 does  not  enable\nParliament  to   alter   the   basic   structure   or   framework   of   the\nConstitution.\u2019[580]\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1216\">393.  This was reiterated and explained by Justice Khanna  in  Indira  Nehru\nGandhi. The words \u2018destroy\u2019 and \u2018abrogate\u2019 etc. were used with reference  to\nthe words \u2018amendment\u2019 and \u2018amendment of the Constitution\u2019 which  is  to  say\nthat \u2018amendment\u2019 and \u2018amendment of the Constitution\u2019 cannot  be  interpreted\nexpansively as meaning \u2018destroy\u2019 or  \u2018abrogate\u2019  etc.  but  have  a  limited\nmeaning. The words \u2018destroy\u2019 and \u2018abrogate\u2019  etc.   were  not  used  in  the\ncontext  of  destroying  or  abrogating   the   basic   structure   of   the\nConstitution.  The learned judge clearly said that \u2018the power  of  amendment\nunder <a href=\"/doc/594125/\" id=\"a_2033\">Article 368 [</a>of the Constitution] does not enable  the  Parliament  to\nalter the basic structure of [or] framework of the Constitution\u2026.\u2019 In  fact,\nthis was  the  precise  submission  of  learned  counsel  for  the  election\npetitioner, namely, that the constitutional  amendment  \u2018affects  the  basic\nstructure or framework of the Constitution and  is,  therefore,  beyond  the\namending power under <a href=\"/doc/594125/\" id=\"a_2034\">Article 368 [</a>of the  Constitution].\u2019[581]  The  learned\njudge explained this crucial distinction in the following words:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_750\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Neg\" data-docid=\"257876\" id=\"span_545\">\u201cThe proposition that the power of amendment  under  <a href=\"/doc/594125/\" id=\"a_2035\">Article  368</a>  does  not\nenable  Parliament  to  alter  the  basic  structure  of  framework  of  the\nConstitution was <a href=\"/doc/184521/\" id=\"a_2036\">laid down by</a> this Court by a majority of  7  to  6  in  the\ncase of <a href=\"/doc/257876/\" id=\"a_2037\">His Holiness Kesavananda Bharati v.  State  of  Kerala</a>.  Apart  from\nother reasons which were given in some  of  the  judgments  of  the  learned\nJudges  who  constituted  the  majority,  the  majority   dealt   with   the\nconnotation of the word \u201camendment\u201d. It was held that the  words  \u201camendment\nof the Constitution\u201d in <a href=\"/doc/594125/\" id=\"a_2038\">Article 368</a> could not have the effect of  destroying\nor abrogating the basic structure of the Constitution. Some of us  who  were\nparties to that case took a different view and came to the  conclusion  that\nthe words \u201camendment of the Constitution\u201d in <a href=\"/doc/594125/\" id=\"a_2039\">Article 368</a> did  not  admit  of\nany limitation.</span> <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"257876\" id=\"span_546\">Those of us who were in the  minority  in  <a href=\"/doc/257876/\" id=\"a_2040\">Kesavananda</a>  case\nmay still hold the same view as was given expression to <a href=\"/doc/257876/\" id=\"a_2041\">in  that  case</a>.  For\nthe purpose of the present case, we shall  have  to  proceed  in  accordance\nwith the law as <a href=\"/doc/257876/\" id=\"a_2042\">laid down by</a> the majority in that case.\u201d[582]</span>\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1217\">394.  While dealing with  the  constitutional  validity  of  Clause  (4)  of\n<a href=\"/doc/190157089/\" id=\"a_2043\">Article 329-A</a> of the Constitution as introduced  by  the  39th  Constitution\nAmendment Act, Justice Khanna  expressed  the  view  that  if  a  principle,\nimperative rule or postulate of the basic structure of the  Constitution  is\nviolated, then the constitutional amendment loses its immunity from  attack.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1218\">\u201cThe question to be decided  is  that  if  the  impugned  amendment  of  the\nConstitution violates a principle which is part of the  basic  structure  of\nthe Constitution, can it enjoy immunity  from  an  attack  on  its  validity\nbecause of the fact  that  for  the  future,  the  basic  structure  of  the\nConstitution remains unaffected. The answer to the  above  question,  in  my\nopinion, should be in the negative. What has to be seen in such a matter  is\nwhether the amendment contravenes or runs counter to an imperative  rule  or\npostulate  which  is  an  integral  part  of  the  basic  structure  of  the\nConstitution. If so, it would be an impermissible  amendment  and  it  would\nmake no difference whether it relates to one  case  or  a  large  number  of\ncases. If an amendment striking at the basic structure of  the  Constitution\nis not permissible, it would not acquire validity by being related  only  to\none case. To accede to the argument advanced in support of the  validity  of\nthe amendment would be tantamount to holding that  even  though  it  is  not\npermissible to change the basic structure of the Constitution, whenever  the\nauthority concerned deems it proper to make such an amendment, it can do  so\nand circumvent the bar to the making of such an amendment  by  confining  it\nto one case. What is prohibited cannot become  permissible  because  of  its\nbeing confined to one matter.\u201d[583]\n\nIn conclusion it was said by Justice Khanna as follows:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_751\">\u201cAs a result of the above, I strike down clause (4) of <a href=\"/doc/190157089/\" id=\"a_2044\">Article 329-A</a> on  the\nground that it violates the principle of free and fair  elections  which  is\nan essential postulate of democracy and which in its turn is a part  of  the\nbasic structure of the Constitution inasmuch as (1) it abolishes  the  forum\nwithout providing for another forum for going into the dispute  relating  to\nthe validity of the election of the appellant and  further  prescribes  that\nthe said dispute shall not be governed by any  election  law  and  that  the\nvalidity of the said election shall be  absolute  and  not  consequently  be\nliable to be assailed, and (2)  it  extinguishes  both  the  right  and  the\nremedy to challenge the validity of the aforesaid election.\u201d[584]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1219\">395.  Similarly, Justice K.K. Mathew who was in the minority in  Kesavananda\nBharati expressed the view  (in  Indira  Nehru  Gandhi)  that  the  majority\ndecision was that by an amendment, the basic structure of  the  Constitution\ncannot be damaged or destroyed, and the  learned  judge  proceeded  on  that\nbasis and held that Clause (4) of  <a href=\"/doc/190157089/\" id=\"a_2045\">Article  329-A</a>  of  the  Constitution  as\nintroduced by the 39th Constitution Amendment Act damaged or  destroyed  the\nbasic structure of the Constitution.[585]\n</p><p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_1220\">396.  Justice Y.V. Chandrachud who too was in the  minority  in  Kesavananda\nBharati took the view  that  according  to  the  majority  opinion  in  that\ndecision  the  principle  that  emerged  was  that  <a href=\"/doc/594125/\" id=\"a_2046\">Article   368</a>   of   the\nConstitution \u2018does not  confer  power  on  Parliament  to  alter  the  basic\nstructure or framework of the Constitution.\u2019[586] The learned judge  further\nsaid that the ratio decidendi in Kesavananda Bharati was that \u2018the power  of\namendment [in <a href=\"/doc/594125/\" id=\"a_2047\">Article 368</a>  of  the  Constitution]  cannot  be  exercised  to\ndamage  or  destroy  the  essential  elements  or  basic  structure  of  the\nConstitution, whatever these expressions may comprehend.\u2019[587]\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1221\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1939993\" id=\"span_547\">397.  The issue again came up for consideration in <a href=\"/doc/1939993/\" id=\"a_2048\">Minerva  Mills  v.  Union\nof India</a>.[588] The question <a href=\"/doc/1939993/\" id=\"a_2049\">in that case</a> was whether <a href=\"/doc/11284129/\" id=\"a_2050\">Section 4</a>  and  Section\n55 of the 42nd Constitution Amendment Act transgress the limitation  of  the\namending power of <a href=\"/doc/594125/\" id=\"a_2051\">Article 368</a> of the Constitution. Speaking for himself  and\nthe other learned judges in the majority (Justice A.C  Gupta,  Justice  N.L.\nUntwalia  and  Justice  P.S.  Kailasam)  it  was  held  by   Chief   Justice\nChandrachud that:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_752\">\u201cIn Kesavananda Bharati, this Court  held  by  a  majority  that  though  by\n<a href=\"/doc/594125/\" id=\"a_2052\">Article 368</a> Parliament is given the power to amend  the  Constitution,  that\npower cannot be exercised  so  as  to  damage  the  basic  features  of  the\nConstitution or so as to destroy  its  basic  structure.  The  question  for\nconsideration in this  group  of  petitions  under  <a href=\"/doc/981147/\" id=\"a_2053\">Article  32</a>  is  whether\n<a href=\"/doc/11284129/\" id=\"a_2054\">Sections 4</a> and 55 of the Constitution (42nd Amendment) Act, 1976  transgress\nthat limitation on the amending power.\u201d[589]\n\nA little later in the judgment, it was held as follows:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_753\">\u201cThe summary of the various judgments in Kesavananda Bharati was  signed  by\nnine out of the thirteen Judges. Para 2 of the summary  reads  to  say  that\naccording to the majority, \u201cArticle 368 does not enable Parliament to  alter\nthe basic structure or framework of the Constitution\u201d. Whether  or  not  the\nsummary is a legitimate part of the judgment, or is  per  incuriam  for  the\nscholarly reasons cited by authors,  it  is  undeniable  that  it  correctly\nreflects the majority view.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_754\">The question which we have to determine on the basis of  the  majority  view\nin Kesavananda Bharati is whether the amendments introduced  by  <a href=\"/doc/11284129/\" id=\"a_2055\">Sections  4</a>\nand 55 of the Constitution (42nd  Amendment)  Act,  1976  damage  the  basic\nstructure of the Constitution by destroying any of  its  basic  features  or\nessential elements.\u201d[590]\n\nIt appears from the above exposition of the ratio decidendi  in  Kesavananda\nBharati that the  words  \u2018alter\u2019  and  \u2018damage\u2019  are  used  interchangeably.\nSimilarly, \u2018damage the basic features\u2019 and  \u2018destroy  the  basic  structure\u2019\nare used interchangeably with \u2018damage the basic structure\u2019 and \u2018destroy  the\nbasic features\u2019.[591] The bottom line is what is contained in the  \u2018summary\u2019\nof Kesavananda Bharati, namely: <a href=\"/doc/594125/\" id=\"a_2056\">Article 368</a> does not  enable  Parliament  to\nalter the basic structure or framework of the Constitution.  There  are  two\nreasons for this. Firstly, it is a contemporaneous exposition of  the  views\nof the majority in Kesavananda Bharati and there is no  other  or  different\nexposition and secondly,  the  exposition  is  by  the  majority  of  judges\nthemselves (including two in the minority) and by no other.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_755\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1195357\" id=\"span_548\">398.  It may be  mentioned  that  some  misgivings  were  expressed  \u2018about\u2019\nMinerva Mills in <a href=\"/doc/1195357/\" id=\"a_2057\">Sanjeev  Coke  Manufacturing  Co.  v.  Bharat  Coking  Coal\nLtd.</a>[592] The misgivings were not spelt out by the Bench except that  it  is\nstated that the case \u2018has left us perplexed\u2019 seemingly for the  reason  that\nno question had arisen regarding the constitutional validity  of  <a href=\"/doc/11284129/\" id=\"a_2058\">Section  4</a>\nand Section 55 of the 42nd Constitution Amendment Act.[593] This  is  rather\nodd since the majority decision in Minerva Mills  begins  by  stating:  \u2018The\nquestion for consideration in this group of petitions under  <a href=\"/doc/981147/\" id=\"a_2059\">Article  32</a>  is\nwhether <a href=\"/doc/11284129/\" id=\"a_2060\">Sections 4</a> and 55 of the Constitution  (42nd  Amendment)  Act,  1976\ntransgress that limitation on the  amending  power.\u2019  Justice  Bhagwati  who\npartly dissented from the  views  of  the  majority  also  stated  that  the\nconstitutional validity of <a href=\"/doc/11284129/\" id=\"a_2061\">Sections 4</a>  and  55  of  the  Constitution  (42nd\nAmendment)  Act,  1976  were  under  challenge.[594]  However,  it  is   not\nnecessary to enter into this thicket, but it  must  be  noted  that  Sanjeev\nCoke  did  not  disagree  with  Minerva  Mills  in  its   understanding   of\nKesavananda Bharati.</span>\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_756\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"102852\" id=\"span_549\">399.  More recently, in <a href=\"/doc/102852/\" id=\"a_2062\">M. Nagaraj  v.  Union  of  India</a>[595]  it  was  held\n(rephrasing Justice Khanna in Indira Nehru Gandhi) that the basic  structure\ndoctrine is really a check on the amending power of  Parliament.  The  basic\nstructure of the Constitution consists  of  constitutional  principles  that\nare so fundamental that they limit the amending power of Parliament. It  was\nconcluded that the basic  structure  theory  is  based  on  the  concept  of\nconstitutional identity (rephrasing Justice Bhagwati in Minerva  Mills).  It\nwas then said:</span>\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_757\">\u201cThe basic structure jurisprudence is a  preoccupation  with  constitutional\nidentity. <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"257876\" id=\"span_550\">In <a href=\"/doc/257876/\" id=\"a_2063\">Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala</a>  it  has  been  observed\nthat \u201cone cannot legally use the Constitution  to  destroy  itself\u201d.  It  is\nfurther  observed  \u201cthe  personality  of  the   Constitution   must   remain\nunchanged\u201d. Therefore, this Court in Kesavananda Bharati  while  propounding\nthe  theory  of  basic  structure,  has  relied   upon   the   doctrine   of\nconstitutional identity.  The  word  \u201camendment\u201d  postulates  that  the  old\nConstitution survives without loss of its identity despite  the  change  and\nit continues even though it has been subjected to alteration.  This  is  the\nconstant theme of the opinions  in  the  majority  decision  in  Kesavananda\nBharati. To destroy its identity is to abrogate the basic structure  of  the\nConstitution. This is the principle of  constitutional  sovereignty\u2026...  The\nmain object behind the theory of the constitutional identity  is  continuity\nand within that continuity of identity,  changes  are  admissible  depending\nupon the situation and circumstances of the day.\u201d</span>\n\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_758\">400.  The \u2018controversy\u2019 is now set at rest with  the  decision  rendered  in\nI.R. Coelho where alteration of the basic structure  has  been  accepted  as\nthe test to determine the constitutional validity of  an  amendment  to  the\nConstitution. It was said:\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_759\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"257876\" id=\"span_551\">\u201cThe decision in <a href=\"/doc/257876/\" id=\"a_2064\">Kesavananda Bharati</a> case was rendered  on  24-4-1973  by  a\nthirteen-Judge Bench and by majority of seven to six  Golak  Nath  case[596]\nwas overruled.</span> <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Neg\" data-docid=\"257876\" id=\"span_552\">The majority opinion held that <a href=\"/doc/594125/\" id=\"a_2065\">Article  368</a>  did  not  enable\nParliament  to   alter   the   basic   structure   or   framework   of   the\nConstitution.\u201d[597]\n\nAnd again,\n\n<a href=\"/doc/257876/\" id=\"a_2066\">\u201cIn Kesavananda Bharati</a> case the majority held that the power  of  amendment\nof the Constitution under <a href=\"/doc/594125/\" id=\"a_2067\">Article 368</a> did not  enable  Parliament  to  alter\nthe basic structure of the Constitution.\u201d[598]\n\nThe attack, therefore, is not on the basic  structure  of  the  Constitution\nbut on the amending power of Parliament.</span>\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_760\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"PARTY\" data-docid=\"693176\" id=\"span_553\">401.  The learned Attorney-General placed reliance on the following  passage\nfrom the judgment of Justice Krishna  Iyer  in  <a href=\"/doc/693176/\" id=\"a_2068\">Bhim  Singhji  v.  Union  of\nIndia</a>[599] to contend that for a constitutional  amendment  to  violate  the\nbasic structure, it must be  shocking,  unconscionable  or  an  unscrupulous\ntravesty of the quintessence of equal justice.  That  case  dealt  with  the\nconstitutional validity of the <a href=\"/doc/1005850/\" id=\"a_2069\">Urban  Land  (Ceiling  and  Regulation)  Act,\n1976</a> which was placed in the Ninth Schedule to the Constitution by the  40th\nConstitution Amendment  Act,  1976  and  therefore  had  the  protection  of\n<a href=\"/doc/282612/\" id=\"a_2070\">Article 31-B</a> and <a href=\"/doc/198382/\" id=\"a_2071\">Article 31-C</a> of the Constitution. In that context,  it  was\nheld that the question of the basic structure of the Constitution  does  not\narise if the constitutional validity of legislation (as  distinguished  from\na constitutional amendment) is under challenge. It was then said:</span>\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_761\">\u201cThe question of  basic  structure  being  breached  cannot  arise  when  we\nexamine the vires  of  an  ordinary  legislation  as  distinguished  from  a\nconstitutional amendment. Kesavananda Bharati cannot be the last  refuge  of\nthe proprietariat when benign legislation  takes  away  their  \u201cexcess\u201d  for\nsocietal weal. Nor, indeed, can every breach of equality spell  disaster  as\na  lethal  violation  of  the  basic  structure.  Peripheral  inequality  is\ninevitable when large-scale equalisation processes are put into  action.
