If\nall the Judges of the Supreme Court in solemn  session  sit  and  deliberate\nfor half a year to produce  a  legislation  for  reducing  glaring  economic\ninequality their genius will let them down if the essay  is  to  avoid  even\nperipheral  inequalities.  Every  large  cause  claims   some   martyr,   as\nsociologists will know. Therefore, what is a betrayal of the  basic  feature\nis not a mere violation of <a href=\"/doc/367586/\" id=\"a_2072\">Article 14</a>  but  a  shocking,  unconscionable  or\nunscrupulous  travesty  of  the  quintessence  of  equal   justice.   If   a\nlegislation does go that far it shakes the democratic  foundation  and  must\nsuffer the death penalty.\u201d[600]\n\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_762\">402.  This decision dealt with a statute placed in  the  Ninth  Schedule  of\nthe Constitution and is, therefore, a class apart  as  far  as  the  present\ndiscussion is concerned.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_763\">403.  From this analysis, it must be  concluded  that  if  a  constitutional\namendment alters the basic structure of the Constitution, then  it  can  and\nshould be declared unconstitutional. What is of importance is the \u2018width  of\npower\u2019 test propounded by Mr. Palkhivala in Kesavananda Bharati and  adopted\nin M. Nagaraj  and now rechristened in I.R. Coelho as the direct impact  and\neffect test \u2018which means the form of  an  amendment  is  not  relevant,  its\nconsequence would be [the] determinative factor.\u2019[601]\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_764\">404.  In the light of the  above  discussion  the  question,  therefore,  is\nthis:  How  does  the  99th  Constitution  Amendment  Act  alter  the  basic\nstructure of the Constitution, if at all? There is no doubt or dispute  that\nthe independence of the judiciary is a basic structure of the  Constitution.\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1222\">I have already held that the appointment of a judge  to  the  Supreme  Court\nand a High Court is an integral part of the independence of  the  judiciary.\nTherefore, has  the  introduction  of  the  National  Judicial  Appointments\nCommission  by  the  99th  Constitution  Amendment  Act   so   altered   the\nappointment process as to  impact  on  the  independence  of  the  judiciary\nthereby making the 99th Constitution Amendment Act  unconstitutional?    The\n learned  Attorney-General\n\nanswered this in the negative.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1223\">2 (b) Presumption of constitutionality\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_1224\">405.  The learned Attorney-General submitted that there is a presumption  in\nlaw that the 99th Constitution Amendment Act is constitutionally  valid  and\nthat the petitioners have not been able to rebut that presumption.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1225\">406.  <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"4354\" id=\"span_554\">In <a href=\"/doc/4354/\" id=\"a_2073\">Charanjit Lal Chowdhuri v. Union of India</a>[602]  Justice  Fazal  Ali\nexpressed the view  that  \u2018the  presumption  is  always  in  favour  of  the\nconstitutionality of an enactment.\u2019</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1226\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"685234\" id=\"span_555\">407.  Similarly, in <a href=\"/doc/685234/\" id=\"a_2074\">Ram Krishna Dalmia v.  Justice  S.R.  Tendolkar</a>[603]  it\nwas held, on a consideration  of  the  decisions  of  this  Court  by  Chief\nJustice S.R. Das that \u2018there is  always  a  presumption  in  favour  of  the\nconstitutionality of an enactment and the burden is upon him who attacks  it\nto show that there has been a clear  transgressions  of  the  constitutional\nprinciples.\u2019</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1227\">408.  In Kesavananda Bharati it  was  held  by  Justice  Hegde  and  Justice\nMukherjea that:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_765\">\u201cBut the courts generally proceed on the  presumption  of  constitutionality\nof all legislations. The presumption of the  constitutional  validity  of  a\nstatute will also apply to constitutional amendments.\u201d[604]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1228\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"314044\" id=\"span_556\">409.  Finally, in <a href=\"/doc/314044/\" id=\"a_2075\">R.K. Garg v. Union of India</a>[605] it was  held  by  Justice\nBhagwati, speaking for the Court as follows:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_766\">\u201cNow while considering the constitutional validity of a statute said  to  be\nviolative of <a href=\"/doc/367586/\" id=\"a_2076\">Article 14</a>, it is  necessary  to  bear  in  mind  certain  well\nestablished principles which have been evolved by the  courts  as  rules  of\nguidance in discharge of its constitutional  function  of  judicial  review.\nThe first rule is that there is  always  a  presumption  in  favour  of  the\nconstitutionality of a statute and the burden is upon him who attacks it  to\nshow that there  has  been  a  clear  transgression  of  the  constitutional\nprinciples. This rule is based on the assumption, judicially recognised  and\naccepted, that the legislature understands  and  correctly  appreciates  the\nneeds of its own people, its laws are directed to problems made manifest  by\nexperience and  its  discrimination  are  based  on  adequate  grounds.  The\npresumption of constitutionality is  indeed  so  strong  that  in  order  to\nsustain it,  the  Court  may  take  into  consideration  matters  of  common\nknowledge, matters of common report,  the  history  of  the  times  and  may\nassume every state of facts which can be conceived existing at the  time  of\nlegislation.\u201d[606]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1229\">410.  It is not possible to disagree with the  learned  Attorney-General  in\nthis regard.  A statute or a constitutional amendment must always be  deemed\nto be constitutionally valid and it is for those  challenging  the  validity\nto demonstrate a violation of the  Constitution  or  an  alteration  of  the\nbasic structure of the Constitution, as the case  may  be.  As  far  as  the\npetitioners are concerned, it is for them  to  conclusively  show  that  the\n99th  Constitution  Amendment  Act  alters  the  basic  structure   of   the\nConstitution in that it replaces a well  thought-out  and  fully-  discussed\nmethod of appointment of judges  with  another  wherein  the  constitutional\nrole giving significant value to the opinion of the Chief Justice  of  India\nis substantively diminished or perhaps eliminated  and  substituted  by  the\nNJAC. The question is not whether the alternative model is good or not  good\nbut whether it is constitutionally valid or not.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1230\">3 (c) Basis of judgment is removed\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1231\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_557\">411.  The third submission was that <a href=\"/doc/1995948/\" id=\"a_2077\">Article 124(2)</a> of the  Constitution  has\nbeen amended by the 99th Constitution  Amendment  Act  and,  therefore,  the\nbasis of the judgment delivered by this Court in the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2078\">Second Judges</a> case  has\nbeen completely taken away or that the Constitution has  been  amended  with\nthe result that that judgment  cannot  now  be  used  to  interpret  <a href=\"/doc/1995948/\" id=\"a_2079\">Article\n124(2)</a> of the Constitution as it is today.</span> <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_558\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_559\">In other words, the challenge  to\nthe  99th  Constitution  Amendment  Act  will   have   to   be   adjudicated\nindependently and regardless of the law <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2080\">laid down in</a> the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2081\">Second Judges</a>  case\nor the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2082\">Third Judges</a> case.</span></span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1232\">412.    <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1018531\" id=\"span_560\">In    <a href=\"/doc/1018531/\" id=\"a_2083\">Shri   Prithvi   Cotton   Mills   Ltd.   v.   Broach   Borough\nMunicipality</a>[607] it was said by Chief  Justice  Hidayatullah  that  granted\nlegislative competence, it is not sufficient  to  declare  merely  that  the\ndecision of the Court shall not bind for that  is  tantamount  to  reversing\nthe decision in exercise of judicial power which the  Legislature  does  not\npossess or  exercise.  A  Court\u2019s  decision  must  always  bind  unless  the\nconditions on which it is  based  are  so  fundamentally  altered  that  the\ndecision could not have been given in  the  altered  circumstances.  It  was\nsaid:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_767\">\u201cGranted legislative competence, it is  not  sufficient  to  declare  merely\nthat the decision of the Court shall not bind  for  that  is  tantamount  to\nreversing the decision in exercise of judicial power which  the  Legislature\ndoes not possess or exercise. A court\u2019s decision  must  always  bind  unless\nthe conditions on which it is based are so fundamentally  altered  that  the\ndecision  could  not  have  been  given  in   the   altered   circumstances.\nOrdinarily, a court holds a tax to be invalidly imposed  because  the  power\nto tax is wanting or the statute or the rules or both are invalid or do  not\nsufficiently create the  jurisdiction.  Validation  of  a  tax  so  declared\nillegal may be done only if the grounds  of  illegality  or  invalidity  are\ncapable of being removed and are in fact  removed  and  the  tax  thus  made\nlegal.  Sometimes  this  is  done  by  providing  for   jurisdiction   where\njurisdiction had not been properly invested before. Sometimes this  is  done\nby re-enacting retrospectively a valid and legal taxing provision  and  then\nby fiction making the tax already collected to stand  under  the  re-enacted\nlaw. Sometimes the Legislature gives its own meaning and  interpretation  of\nthe law under which tax was collected and by legislative fiat makes the  new\nmeaning binding upon courts. The Legislature may follow any  one  method  or\nall of them and while it does  so  it  may  neutralise  the  effect  of  the\nearlier decision of the court which becomes ineffective after the change  of\nthe law. Whichever method is adopted it must be  within  the  competence  of\nthe  legislature  and  legal  and  adequate  to   attain   the   object   of\nvalidation.\u201d[608]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1233\">413. Similarly, in Indira Nehru Gandhi it was held by Chief Justice  Ray  as\nfollows:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_768\">\u201cThe effect of validation is to change the law so as to alter the  basis  of\nany judgment, which might have been given on the basis of old law  and  thus\nmake the judgment  ineffective.  A  formal  declaration  that  the  judgment\nrendered under the old Act is void, is  not  necessary.  If  the  matter  is\npending in appeal, the appellate court has to give  effect  to  the  altered\nlaw and reverse the judgment. The rendering of  a  judgment  ineffective  by\nchanging its basis by  legislative  enactment  is  not  an  encroachment  on\njudicial power but a legislation within the competence  of  the  Legislature\nrendering the basis of the judgment non est.\u201d\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1234\">414.  <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1337952\" id=\"span_561\">In <a href=\"/doc/1337952/\" id=\"a_2084\">K. Sankaran Nair v. Devaki Amma Malathy Amma</a>[609] it  was  observed\nas follows:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_769\">\u201cIt is now well settled that the legislature cannot  overrule  any  judicial\ndecision without removing the substratum or the foundation of that  judgment\nby a retrospective amendment of the legal provision concerned.\u201d [610]\n\n      It was further stated, relying upon Shri Prithvi Cotton Mills Ltd.  as\nfollows:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_770\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1337952\" id=\"span_562\">\u201cIt is now well settled by a catena of decisions of this Court  that  unless\nthe   legislature   by   enacting   a   competent   legislative    provision\nretrospectively removes the substratum or foundation of any  judgment  of  a\ncompetent court <a href=\"/doc/1337952/\" id=\"a_2085\">the said judgment</a> would remain binding and operative and  in\nthe absence of such a legislative exercise by a  competent  legislature  the\nattempt to upset the binding effect of such judgments rendered  against  the\nparties would remain an incompetent and forbidden exercise  which  could  be\ndubbed as an abortive attempt to legislatively  overrule  binding  decisions\nof courts.\u201d [611]</span>\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1235\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1918801\" id=\"span_563\">415.  Similarly, in <a href=\"/doc/1918801/\" id=\"a_2086\">Bhubaneshwar Singh v. Union of India</a>[612]  reliance  was\nplaced on Shri Prithvi Cotton Mills Ltd.  and  a  host  of  other  decisions\nrendered by this Court and a similar conclusion arrived at in the  following\nwords:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_771\">\u201cFrom time to time controversy has  arisen  as  to  whether  the  effect  of\njudicial pronouncements of the High Court or the Supreme Court can be  wiped\nout by  amending  the  legislation  with  retrospective  effect.  Many  such\nAmending Acts are called Validating Acts, validating the action taken  under\nthe  particular  enactments  by  removing  the   defect   in   the   statute\nretrospectively because of which the statute or the  part  of  it  had  been\ndeclared ultra vires. Such exercise has been held by this Court  as  not  to\namount to encroachment on the judicial power of the courts. The exercise  of\nrendering ineffective  the  judgments  or  orders  of  competent  courts  by\nchanging the very basis by legislation is a well-known device of  validating\nlegislation. This Court has repeatedly  pointed  out  that  such  validating\nlegislation which removes the cause of the invalidity cannot  be  considered\nto be an encroachment on judicial power. At the same  time,  any  action  in\nexercise of the power under any enactment which  has  been  declared  to  be\ninvalid by a court cannot be made  valid  by  a  Validating  Act  by  merely\nsaying so unless the defect which has been  pointed  out  by  the  court  is\nremoved with retrospective effect. The validating  legislation  must  remove\nthe cause of invalidity. Till such defect or the lack of  authority  pointed\nout by the court under a statute is  removed  by  the  subsequent  enactment\nwith retrospective effect, the binding nature of the judgment of  the  court\ncannot be ignored.\u201d[613]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1236\">416.  In Re Cauvery Water Disputes Tribunal[614] it was pithily  stated,  on\na review of several decisions of this Court that:\n</p>\n\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_772\">\u201cThe principle which emerges from these authorities is that the  legislature\ncan change the basis on which a decision is given  by  the  Court  and  thus\nchange the law in general, which will affect a class of persons  and  events\nat large. It cannot, however, set aside an individual decision inter  partes\nand affect their rights and liabilities alone. Such an act on  the  part  of\nthe legislature amounts to exercising the judicial power of  the  State  and\nto functioning as an appellate court or tribunal.\u201d[615]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1237\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1638351\" id=\"span_564\">417.  More recently,  in  State  of  Tamil  Nadu  this  Court  approved  the\nfollowing conclusion  arrived  at  in  <a href=\"/doc/1638351/\" id=\"a_2087\">Indian  Aluminium  Co.  v.  State  of\nKerala</a>[616]:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_773\">\u201cIn exercising legislative  power,  the  legislature  by  mere  declaration,\nwithout anything more,  cannot  directly  overrule,  revise  or  override  a\njudicial decision. It can render judicial decision ineffective  by  enacting\nvalid law on the topic within its legislative field  fundamentally  altering\nor  changing  its  character  retrospectively.  The   changed   or   altered\nconditions are such that the previous decision would not have been  rendered\nby the court, if those conditions had existed at the time of  declaring  the\nlaw as invalid. It  is  also  empowered  to  give  effect  to  retrospective\nlegislation with a deeming date or with effect from a particular  date.  The\nlegislature can change the character of the tax or duty  from  impermissible\nto permissible tax but the tax or levy should answer such character and  the\nlegislature is competent to recover the invalid tax validating  such  a  tax\non removing the invalid base for recovery from the  subject  or  render  the\nrecovery from the State ineffectual. It is competent for the legislature  to\nenact the law with retrospective effect and authorise its agencies  to  levy\nand collect the tax on that basis, make the  imposition  of  levy  collected\nand recovery of the tax made valid, notwithstanding the declaration  by  the\ncourt or the direction given for recovery thereof.\u201d[617]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1238\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_565\">418.  Without commenting on the view  canvassed  by  the  learned  Attorney-\nGeneral that the 99th Constitution Amendment Act has  actually  removed  the\nbasis of the judgment delivered by this Court in the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2088\">Second Judges</a> case  the\nconstitutional validity of the said amendment will nevertheless need  to  be\ntested on that assumption, keeping in mind the above decisions.</span>\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1239\">4 (d) Wisdom of an amendment to the Constitution\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1240\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_566\">419.  The next submission of  the  learned  Attorney-General  was  that  the\nwisdom of Parliament in enacting the 99th Constitution Amendment Act  cannot\nbe disputed. Hence, this Court ought not to substitute its own views on  the\nnecessity or otherwise of the 99th Constitution Amendment Act over  the  law\n<a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2089\">laid down in</a> the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2090\">Second Judges</a> case.</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1241\">420.  In Lochner v. New York[618] Justice  Oliver  Wendell  Holmes  famously\nstated (in dissent) almost a century ago:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_774\">\u201cThis case is decided upon an economic theory which  a  large  part  of  the\ncountry does not entertain. If it were a  question  whether  I  agreed  with\nthat theory, I should desire to study it further and long before  making  up\nmy mind. But I do not conceive that  to  be  my  duty,  because  I  strongly\nbelieve that my agreement or disagreement has nothing to do with  the  right\nof a majority to embody their opinions in law.\u201d\n\nIn other words, one may or may not agree with the content  or  wisdom  of  a\nlegislation, but that has nothing to do with the  correctness  or  otherwise\nof the majority  decision  taken  by  a  Legislature.  This  view  has  been\nfollowed in our country as well.\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1242\">421.  The Courts in our country do not question the wisdom or expediency  of\nthe Legislature enacting a statute, let alone a constitutional amendment.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1243\">422.  <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1857950\" id=\"span_567\">In one of the earliest cases relating to the wisdom of  Parliament  in\nenacting  a  law,  it  was  contended  in  <a href=\"/doc/1857950/\" id=\"a_2091\">A.K.  Gopalan  v.  The  State  of\nMadras</a>[619] that the <a href=\"/doc/84767110/\" id=\"a_2092\">Preventive Detention Act,  1950</a>  was  unconstitutional.\nJustice Das expressed the view that:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_775\">\u201cThe point to be noted,  however,  is  that  in  so  far  as  there  is  any\nlimitation on the legislative power, the Court must, on  a  complaint  being\nmade to it, scrutinise  and  ascertain  whether  such  limitation  has  been\ntransgressed and  if  there  has  been  any  transgression  the  Court  will\ncourageously declare the law unconstitutional, for the  Court  is  bound  by\nits oath to uphold the Constitution. But outside the limitations imposed  on\nthe  legislative  powers  our  Parliament\u00a0and  the  State  Legislatures  are\nsupreme in  their  respective  legislative  fields  and  the  Court  has  no\nauthority to question the wisdom\u00a0or policy of  the  law  duly  made  by  the\nappropriate legislature.\u201d\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1244\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"493792\" id=\"span_568\">423.  <a href=\"/doc/720619/\" id=\"a_2093\">The Payment of Bonus Act, 1965</a> and the scheme for payment  of  minimum\nbonus were under challenge in <a href=\"/doc/493792/\" id=\"a_2094\">Jalan Trading Company (P) Ltd v. Mill  Mazdoor\nSabha Union</a>.[620] Speaking for the Court, Justice J.C.  Shah  observed  that\nthe wisdom of the scheme selected by the Legislature may be open  to  debate\nbut it would not be invalid merely because some fault can be found with  the\nscheme. It was said:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_776\">\u201cWhether the scheme for  payment  of  minimum  bonus  is  the  best  in  the\ncircumstances, or a more equitable method could have been devised so  as  to\navoid in certain cases undue hardship is irrelevant to the enquiry in  hand.\nIf the classification is not patently arbitrary, the Court will not rule  it\ndiscriminatory merely because it involves hardship or inequality of  burden.\nWith a view to secure a particular object a scheme may be  selected  by  the\nLegislature,  wisdom  whereof  may  be  open  to  debate;  it  may  even  be\ndemonstrated that the scheme is not the best in the  circumstances  and  the\nchoice of the legislature may be shown  to  be  erroneous,  but  unless  the\nenactment fails to satisfy the dual test of intelligible classification  and\nrationality of the relation with the object of  the  law,  it  will  not  be\nsubject  to  judicial  interference  under   <a href=\"/doc/367586/\" id=\"a_2095\">Article   14.</a>   Invalidity   of\nlegislation is not established by merely  finding  faults  with  the  scheme\nadopted by the Legislature to achieve the purpose it has in view.\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1245\">424.  In Kesavananda Bharati it was observed by Chief  Justice  Sikri  that:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_777\">\u2018It is of course for Parliament to  decide  whether  an  amendment  [to  the\nConstitution] is necessary. The  Courts  will  not  be  concerned  with  the\nwisdom of the amendment.\u2019[621]  The learned Chief Justice further  observed:\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_778\">\u2018If Parliament has power to pass the impugned amendment acts,  there  is  no\ndoubt that I have  no  right  to  question  the  wisdom  of  the  policy  of\nParliament.\u2019[622]\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1246\">425.  Similarly, Justice Shelat and Justice Grover held:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_779\">\u201cIt is not for  the  courts  to  enter  into  the  wisdom  or  policy  of  a\nparticular provision in a  Constitution  or  a  statute.  That  is  for  the\nConstitution-makers or for the Parliament or the legislature.\u201d[623]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1247\">426.  Justice A.N. Ray expressed his view in the  following  words:  \u2018Courts\nare not concerned with the wisdom or policy of legislation. The  Courts  are\nequally not concerned with the  wisdom  and  policy  of  amendments  to  the\nConstitution.\u2019[624]\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1248\">427.  Justice Jaganmohan  Reddy  expressed  the  same  sentiments  when  the\nlearned judge said:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_780\">\u201cThe citizen whose rights are affected, no doubt, invokes  the  aid  of  the\njudicial power to vindicate them, but in discharging its  duty,  the  Courts\nhave nothing to do with the wisdom or the policy of the Legislature.\u201d[625]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1249\">428.  On the question of the wisdom  of  a  constitutional  amendment  which\nostensibly improves an existing  situation,  Justice  Khanna  expressed  the\nview that this was not justiciable. The Court cannot substitute its  opinion\nfor that of Parliament in this regard. It was held:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_781\">\u201cWhether the amendment is in fact, an improvement or not, in my opinion,  is\nnot a justiciable matter, and in judging the validity of  an  amendment  the\ncourts would not go into the question as to whether  the  amendment  has  in\neffect brought about an improvement. It is for the special majority in  each\nHouse of Parliament to decide as to whether it constitutes  an  improvement;\nthe courts would not be substituting their  own  opinion  for  that  of  the\nParliament in this respect. Whatever may be the personal  view  of  a  judge\nregarding the wisdom behind or the improving quality  of  an  amendment,  he\nwould be only concerned with the legality of the amendment and this, in  its\nturn,  would  depend  upon  the  question  as  to  whether  the  formalities\nprescribed in <a href=\"/doc/594125/\" id=\"a_2096\">Article 368</a> have been complied with.\u201d[626]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1250\">429.   With reference to the Lochner dissent, Justice Khanna noted that  the\nview was subsequently accepted by  the  US  Supreme  Court  in  Ferguson  v.\nSkrupa[627] in the following words:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_782\">\u201cIn the face of our abandonment of the use of the \u2018vague  contours\u2019  of  the\nDue Process clause to nullify laws which a majority of  the  Court  believed\nto be economically unwise, reliance on Adams v. Tanner[628] is  as  mistaken\nas would be adherence to Adkins v.  Children\u2019s  Hospital[629]  overruled  by\nWest Coast Hotel Co. v. Parrish[630] \u2026..\u2026. We refuse  to  sit  as  a  \u2018super\nlegislature to weigh the wisdom of legislation\u2019, and we emphatically  refuse\nto go back to the time when courts used the Due Process  clause  \u2018to  strike\ndown State laws, regulatory of business and industrial  conditions,  because\nthey may be unwise, improvident, or out of harmony with a particular  school\nof thought\u2019.\u201d[631]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1251\">430.  Justice Khanna reiterated his views in  Indira  Nehru  Gandhi  wherein\nthe learned judge held:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_783\">\u201cBefore dealing with the question  as  to  whether  the  impugned  amendment\naffects the basic structure of the Constitution, I may make  it  clear  that\nthis Court is not concerned with the wisdom behind or the propriety  of  the\nimpugned constitutional amendment. These are matters essentially  for  those\nwho are vested with the authority to make the constitutional amendment.  All\nthat this Court is concerned with is  the  constitutional  validity  of  the\nimpugned amendment.\u201d[632]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1252\">431.  Justice Chandrachud also expressed the same view, that is to say:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_784\">\u201cThe subject-matter of constitutional  amendments  is  a  question  of  high\npolicy and courts are concerned with the interpretation of  laws,  not  with\nthe wisdom of the policy underlying them.\u201d[633]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1253\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1508583\" id=\"span_569\">432.  A similar view was expressed  in  <a href=\"/doc/1508583/\" id=\"a_2097\">Karnataka  Bank  Ltd.  v.  State  of\nAndhra Pradesh</a>[634] wherein it  was  specifically  observed  by  this  Court\nthat:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_785\">\u201cIn pronouncing on the constitutional validity of a statute,  the  court  is\nnot concerned with the wisdom or unwisdom, the justice or injustice  of  the\nlaw. If that which is passed into law is  within  the  scope  of  the  power\nconferred on a legislature and violates no restrictions on that  power,  the\nlaw must be upheld whatever a court may think of it.\u201d[635]\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_570\">5</span>\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1254\">433.  In view  of  the  judicial  pronouncements,  there  is  absolutely  no\ndifficulty in accepting this proposition canvassed by the learned  Attorney-\nGeneral. The constitutional validity of the 99th Constitution Amendment  Act\nhas to be tested on its own merit. The question of  any  Court  substituting\nits opinion for that of the Legislature simply cannot and does not arise.  A\njudge may have a view one way or  the  other  on  the  collegium  system  of\nappointment of judges and on the manner of its  implementation  \u2013  but  that\nopinion cannot colour the application and interpretation of the law  or  the\nreasoning that a judge is expected  to  adopt  in  coming  to  a  conclusion\nwhether the substitute introduced by the 99th Constitution Amendment Act  is\nconstitutionally valid or not. Similarly, a judge may have an opinion  about\nthe National Judicial Appointments Commission \u2013 but again that  view  cannot\nreplace a judicial interpretation of the 99th Constitution Amendment Act  or\nthe <a href=\"/doc/142995521/\" id=\"a_2098\">NJAC Act</a>.\n</p><p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_1255\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_571\">434.   The collegium system of appointment of judges  has  undoubtedly  been\nthe subject of criticism. In fact, Mr. Fali Nariman who led the  submissions\non behalf of the Advocates on Record Association was quite critical  of  the\ncollegium system of appointments.  Some  of  the  learned  counsel  for  the\nrespondents went overboard in their criticism. But personal opinions do  not\nmatter. Lord Templeman of the House of  Lords  was  of  the  view  that  the\ncollegium system of appointments is best suited to ensure  the  independence\nof the judiciary \u2013 but there are other eminent persons who are  critical  of\nthe <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2099\">Second Judges</a> case.</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1256\">435.  In the final analysis, therefore, the Courts must defer to the  wisdom\nof the Legislature and accept their views, as long as they  are  within  the\nparameters of the law, nothing more and  nothing  less.  The  constitutional\nvalidity of  the  99th  Constitution  Amendment  Act  cannot  be  tested  on\nopinions, however strong they may be or however vividly expressed.\n</p>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_572\">6</span>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1257\">7 (e) Needs of the people\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_1258\">436.  It was also submitted by the learned Attorney-General that  Parliament\nis aware of the needs of the people and the people want a  change  from  the\ncollegium system of appointment of judges. Parliament has responded to  this\ndemand and this  Court  should  not  reject  this  demand  only  because  it\nbelieves that the collegium system  is  working  well  and  that  the   99th\nConstitution Amendment Act introduces a different system which  reduces  the\nrole of the judiciary in making appointments by taking away its  primacy  in\nthis regard.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1259\">437.  Apart from the  presumption  that  an  enactment  is  constitutionally\nvalid, there is also a presumption  that  the  Legislature  understands  and\ncorrectly appreciates the  needs  of  the  people.   This  was  observed  in\nCharanjit Lal Chowdhuri and reliance was placed  on  the  following  passage\nfrom Middleton v. Texas Power and Light Co.[636]:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_786\">\u201cIt  must  be  presumed  that  a  legislature  understands   and   correctly\nappreciates the need of its own  people,  that  its  laws  are  directed  to\nproblems made manifest by experience and that its discriminations are  based\nupon adequate grounds.\u201d\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1260\">438.  Similarly, in Ram Krishna Dalmia the presumption that the  Legislature\nunderstands  and  correctly  appreciates  the  needs  of  the   people   was\nreiterated.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1261\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"93885\" id=\"span_573\">439.  Finally in <a href=\"/doc/93885/\" id=\"a_2100\">Mohd. Hanif Quareshi v. State of Bihar</a>[637] this  view  was\nendorsed by Chief Justice S.R. Das speaking for this Court  (though  it  may\nbe mentioned that  this  decision  was  subsequently  overruled  on  another\nissue) in the following words:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_787\">\u201cThe courts, it is accepted, must presume that the  legislature  understands\nand correctly appreciates the needs of its own people,  that  its  laws  are\ndirected  to  problems  made   manifest   by   experience   and   that   its\ndiscriminations are based on adequate grounds.\u201d\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1262\">440.  It was observed (on an issue relating to the constitutionality of  the\ndeath penalty) in Makwanyane[638] as follows:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_788\">\u201cPublic opinion may have some relevance to the enquiry, but  in  itself,  it\nis no substitute for  the  duty  vested  in  the  Courts  to  interpret  the\nConstitution and to uphold its provisions without fear or favour. If  public\nopinion were to be decisive  there  would  be  no  need  for  constitutional\nadjudication. The protection of rights could then  be  left  to  Parliament,\nwhich has a mandate from the public, and is answerable  to  the  public  for\nthe  way  its  mandate  is  exercised,  but  this  would  be  a  return   to\nparliamentary  sovereignty,  and  a  retreat  from  the  new   legal   order\nestablished by the 1993 Constitution\u2026\u2026.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_789\">This Court cannot allow itself to be diverted from its duty  to  act  as  an\nindependent arbiter of the Constitution by making choices on the basis  that\nthey will find favour with the  public.  Justice  Powell&#x27;s  comment  in  his\ndissent in Furman v Georgia bears repetition:\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_790\">...the weight of the evidence indicates that the public  generally  has  not\naccepted  either  the  morality  or  the  social  merit  of  the  views   so\npassionately advocated  by  the  articulate  spokesmen  for  abolition.  But\nhowever one may assess amorphous ebb and flow of  public  opinion  generally\non this volatile issue, this type of inquiry lies at  the  periphery  -  not\nthe core - of the judicial process in constitutional cases.  The  assessment\nof popular opinion  is  essentially  a  legislative,  and  not  a  judicial,\nfunction.[639]\n\nSo too does the comment of Justice Jackson in West Virginia State  Board  of\nEducation v Barnette:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_791\">The very purpose of a Bill of Rights was to withdraw certain  subjects  from\nthe vicissitudes of political controversy, to place them  beyond  the  reach\nof majorities and officials and to establish them as legal principles to  be\napplied by the courts. One&#x27;s right to life, liberty, and property,  to  free\nspeech, a free press, freedom of worship and assembly and other  fundamental\nrights may not be submitted to vote;  they  depend  on  the  outcome  of  no\nelections.[640]\n\nTo put it differently: \u2018The legitimacy of  the  Judicial  Branch  ultimately\ndepends  on  its  reputation  for  impartiality  and  nonpartisanship.\u2019[641]\nPublic opinion, manifested through Parliament  or  otherwise,  really  pales\ninto insignificance over the law that is interpreted impartially  and  in  a\nnon-partisan manner.\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1263\">441.  It must be appreciated that  the  debate  cannot  be  reduced  to  the\nacceptance  of  an  unconstitutional   but   popular   decision   versus   a\nconstitutional  but  unpopular  decision.  All  of  us  are  bound  by   the\nConstitution and judges have to abide by the oath of office  to  uphold  the\nConstitution  and  the  laws,  even  if  the  decision   is   unpopular   or\nunacceptable  to  Parliament.  This  is  the  essence  of  judicial   review\notherwise no law passed by Parliament (obviously having a  popular  mandate)\ncould be struck down as unconstitutional.\n</p>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_574\">8</span>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1264\">9 (f) Passage of time\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_1265\">442.  Finally, it was submitted by the  learned  Attorney-General  that  the\npassage of time over the last over sixty years has shown that the system  of\nappointment  of  judges  that  was  originally  operational  (in  which  the\nexecutive has the \u2018ultimate power\u2019) and the collegium system (in  which  the\njudiciary  had  shared  responsibility)  had  both  yielded  some   negative\nresults. It was submitted that millions of cases are  pending,  persons  who\nshould have been appointed as judges were not  recommended  for  appointment\nand persons who did not deserve to be judges were  not  only  appointed  but\nwere brought to this Court. The 99th Constitution  Amendment  Act  seeks  to\ncorrect  the  imbalances  created  over  a  period   of   time   and   since\nconstitutional experiments are permissible, the 99th Constitution  Amendment\nAct should be allowed to pass muster.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1266\">443.  There is no doubt that with the passage of time changes take place  in\nsociety and in the development of the law.  In fact, the  only  constant  is\nchange.   <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"184660633\" id=\"span_575\">In  <a href=\"/doc/184660633/\" id=\"a_2101\">State  of  West  Bengal  v.  Anwar  Ali  Sarkar</a>[642]  it   was\nacknowledged by Justice Mehr Chand Mahajan that good faith and knowledge  of\nexisting conditions on the part of  the  Legislature  has  to  be  presumed.\nAppreciating this, it was later observed in Ram Krishna Dalmia that:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_792\">\u201cIn order to sustain the presumption  of  constitutionality  the  court  may\ntake into consideration matters  of  common  knowledge,  matters  of  common\nreport, the history of the times and may assume every state of  facts  which\ncan be conceived existing at the time of legislation.\u201d\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1267\">444.  In Kesavananda Bharati Justice Hegde and  Justice  Mukherjea  observed\nthat: \u2018The society grows, its requirements  change.   The  Constitution  and\nthe laws may have to be changed to suit those needs.  No  single  generation\ncan bind the course of the generation to come.\u2019[643]\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1268\">445.  Justice Khanna expressed the view (and  this  was  relied  on  by  the\nlearned Attorney-General) that the Constitution is  also  intended  for  the\nfuture and must contain ample provision for experiment and  trial.  This  is\nwhat Justice Khanna said:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_793\">\u201cIt has also to be borne in mind that a Constitution is not  a  gate  but  a\nroad. Beneath the drafting of a Constitution is the  awareness  that  things\ndo not stand still but move on, that life of a progressive nation, as of  an\nindividual,  is  not  static  and  stagnant  but  dynamic  and  dashful.   A\nConstitution must therefore  contain  ample  provision  for  experiment  and\ntrial in the  task  of  administration.  A  Constitution,  it  needs  to  be\nemphasised, is not a document for fastidious dialectics  but  the  means  of\nordering the life of a people. It had its roots in the past, its  continuity\nis  reflected  in  the  present  and  it  is  intended   for   the   unknown\nfuture.\u201d[644]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1269\">446.  A little later on in the judgment, the learned judge cited  Abrams  v.\nUnited States[645] and quoting Justice Holmes said:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_794\">\u201cThe Constitution of a nation is the outward and  visible  manifestation  of\nthe life of the people and it must respond to the deep pulsation for  change\nwithin. \u201cA Constitution is an experiment as all life is an  experiment.\u201d  If\nthe experiment fails, there must be provision for making another.\u201d[646]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1270\">447.  Fortunately for the people of the country,  the  independence  of  the\njudiciary is not a \u2018task of  administration\u2019  nor  is  the  Constitution  of\nIndia a failed experiment nor is there any need for  \u2018making  provision  for\nanother\u2019. If the basic structure of  the  Constitution  is  to  be  changed,\nthrough experimentation or otherwise, then its overthrow  is  necessary.  It\nis not a simple document that can be experimented with or changed through  a\ncut and paste method. Even though the independence of  the  judiciary  is  a\nbasic structure of the Constitution and being a pillar of democracy  it  can\nbe experimented with, but only if it is possible without altering the  basic\nstructure. The independence of the judiciary is  a  concept  developed  over\ncenturies to benefit the people against  arbitrary  exercise  of  power.  If\nduring experimentation, the independence of the judiciary  is  lost,  it  is\ngone forever and cannot be regained by simply concluding that  the  loss  of\nindependence is a failed experiment. The independence of  the  judiciary  is\nnot physical but metaphysical.  The independence of  the  judiciary  is  not\nlike plasticine that it can be moulded any which way.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1271\">448.  This is not to say that the Constitution must recognize only  physical\nchanges with the passage of time \u2013 certainly not.  New  thoughts  and  ideas\nare generated with the passage of time and  a  line  of  thinking  that  was\nacceptable a few decades ago  may  not  be  acceptable  today  and  what  is\nacceptable today may not be acceptable a decade hence.  But  basic  concepts\nlike democracy, secularism, Rule of Law, independence of the judiciary,  all\nof which are constituents of the basic structure  of  our  Constitution  are\nimmutable as concepts, though nuances may change.  A  failed  experiment  of\nthese basic concepts would  lead  to  disastrous  consequences.  It  is  not\npossible as an experiment to try out a monarchy  or  a  dictatorship  or  to\nconvert India into a religious State for about ten or fifteen years and  see\nhow the experiment works. Nor is it possible to suspend the Rule of  Law  or\ntake away the independence of the judiciary for about ten or  fifteen  years\nand see how the experiment works. These concepts are far  too  precious  for\nexperimentation.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1272\">449.  Yes, the Constitution has  to  be  interpreted  as  a  living  organic\ndocument for years and years to come, but  within  accepted  parameters.  It\nwas said by Chief Justice Dickson of  the  Canadian  Supreme  Court  in  The\nQueen v. Beauregard[647]:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_795\">\u201cThe Canadian Constitution is not locked forever in a 119-year  old  casket.\nIt lives and breathes and is capable  of  growing  to  keep  pace  with  the\ngrowth of the country and its people. Accordingly, if the  Constitution  can\naccommodate, as it has, many subjects unknown  in  1867--airplanes,  nuclear\nenergy, hydroelectric power -- it is surely  not  straining\u00a0section  100\u00a0too\nmuch to say that the word \u2018pensions\u2019, admittedly understood in one sense  in\n1867,  can  today  support  federal  legislation  based   on   a   different\nunderstanding of \u2018pensions\u2019.\u201d[648]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1273\">450.  It is this that Justice Khanna possibly had in mind when  the  learned\njudge spoke of the \u2018unknown future\u2019.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1274\">Challenge to a statute and the package deal\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1275\">451.  The learned Attorney-General also adverted  to  the  legal  bases  for\nchallenging a statute. This was necessary since he desired to segregate  the\nchallenge to the 99th Constitution  Amendment  Act  and  the  NJAC  Act.  In\nprinciple, the segregation would be justified, but as far as  this  case  is\nconcerned,  the  learned  Attorney-General  had   argued   that   the   99th\nConstitution Amendment Act and the NJAC Act were a  \u2018package  deal\u2019  and  in\nthis he is correct. Both were  discussed  and  debated  in  both  Houses  of\nParliament almost at the same time, both were  sent  to  the  President  for\nassent at the same time and were in fact assented to at the  same  time  and\nfinally both were notified at the same time. The only  difference  was  that\nwhile the 99th Constitution Amendment Act had to  undergo  the  ratification\nprocess, the <a href=\"/doc/142995521/\" id=\"a_2102\">NJAC Act</a> did not. It was therefore a \u2018package  deal\u2019  presented\nto the country in which the 99th Constitution Amendment  Act  and  the  NJAC\nAct were so interlinked that one could not operate without reference to  the\nother. In fact, Mr. Nariman submitted that the <a href=\"/doc/142995521/\" id=\"a_2103\">NJAC  Act</a>  should  also  have\nundergone the ratification  process,  but  he  was  unable  to  support  his\nargument with any law, judicial  precedent,  convention  or  practice.  This\nquestion is left open for greater discussion at an appropriate stage  should\nthe occasion arise.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1276\">452.  Be that as it may, in the context of a challenge to a statute, it  was\nsubmitted by the learned Attorney-General that the  principles  for  such  a\nchallenge  are  quite  different  from  a  challenge  to  a   constitutional\namendment. He is right in this submission.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1277\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1732220\" id=\"span_576\">453.  The accepted view is that a Parliamentary statute can be  struck  down\nonly if it is beyond  legislative  competence  or  violates  <a href=\"/doc/134715/\" id=\"a_2104\">Art.13</a>  or  the\nfundamental rights. The  basic  structure  doctrine  is  not  available  for\nstriking down a statute. It was held in <a href=\"/doc/1732220/\" id=\"a_2105\">State of A.P. v. McDowell &amp;  Co</a>[649]\nthat:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_796\">\u201cThe power of Parliament or for  that  matter,  the  State  Legislatures  is\nrestricted in two ways. A law made by  Parliament  or  the  [pic]legislature\ncan be struck down by courts on two grounds and  two  grounds  alone,  viz.,\n(1) lack  of  legislative  competence  and  (2)  violation  of  any  of  the\nfundamental rights guaranteed in Part III of  the  Constitution  or  of  any\nother constitutional provision. There is no third ground.\u201d\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1278\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1398944\" id=\"span_577\">454.  This view was followed in <a href=\"/doc/1398944/\" id=\"a_2106\">Public Services Tribunal Bar Assn  v.  State\nof U.P.</a>[650] in the following words:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_797\">\u201cThe constitutional validity of  an  Act  can  be  challenged  only  on  two\ngrounds viz. (i) lack of legislative competence; and (ii) violation  of  any\nof the fundamental rights guaranteed in Part III of the Constitution  or  of\nany other constitutional provisions. <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1732220\" id=\"span_578\">In <a href=\"/doc/1732220/\" id=\"a_2107\">State of A.P. v. McDowell &amp; Co</a>  this\nCourt has opined that except the above two grounds there is no third  ground\non the basis of which the law made  by  the  competent  legislature  can  be\ninvalidated and that  the  ground  of  invalidation  must  necessarily  fall\nwithin the four corners of the aforementioned two grounds.\u201d</span>\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1279\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1404351\" id=\"span_579\">455.  Earlier, this Court had taken a much broader view of the  issue  of  a\nchallenge  to  a  statute  in  <a href=\"/doc/1404351/\" id=\"a_2108\">Chhotabhai  Jethabhai  Patel  v.   Union   of\nIndia</a>.[651] It was held therein that apart from the question of  legislative\ncompetence and violation of <a href=\"/doc/134715/\" id=\"a_2109\">Article 13</a> of the Constitution, a statute  could\nbe challenged if  its  enactment  was  prohibited  by  a  provision  of  the\nConstitution. It was held as follows:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_798\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1404351\" id=\"span_580\">\u201cIf by reason of <a href=\"/doc/1405898/\" id=\"a_2110\">Article 265</a> every tax has to be imposed by \u201claw\u201d  it  would\nappear to follow that it could only be imposed by a law which  is  valid  by\nconformity to the criteria  <a href=\"/doc/1404351/\" id=\"a_2111\">laid  down  in</a>  the  relevant  Articles  of  the\nConstitution. These are that the law should be (1)  within  the  legislative\ncompetence of the legislature being covered by the  legislative  entries  in\nSchedule VII of the Constitution; (2) the law should not  be  prohibited  by\nany particular provision of the Constitution such as for  example,  <a href=\"/doc/1444398/\" id=\"a_2112\">Articles\n276(2)</a><a href=\"/doc/433847/\" id=\"a_2113\">, 286</a> etc., and (3) the law or the  relevant  portion  thereof  should\nnot be invalid under <a href=\"/doc/134715/\" id=\"a_2114\">Article 13</a> for repugnancy to those  freedom  which  are\nguaranteed by Part III  of  the  Constitution  which  are  relevant  to  the\nsubject-matter of the law.\u201d</span>\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1280\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1686885\" id=\"span_581\">456.  This view was taken  forward  in  <a href=\"/doc/1686885/\" id=\"a_2115\">Kihoto  Hollohan  v.  Zachillhu</a>[652]\nwherein it was held that the  procedure  for  enacting  a  \u2018law\u2019  should  be\nfollowed. Although it is not expressly stated, but it appears  that  if  the\nprocedure is not followed then  the  \u2018law\u2019  to  that  extent  will  have  no\neffect. In this case, it was held that Paragraph 7 of the Tenth Schedule  to\nthe Constitution needed ratification in terms of clause (b) of  the  proviso\nto <a href=\"/doc/1389240/\" id=\"a_2116\">Article 368(2)</a> of the Constitution. It was held:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_799\">\u201cThat having regard to  the  background  and  evolution  of  the  principles\nunderlying the <a href=\"/doc/73335167/\" id=\"a_2117\">Constitution (Fifty-second Amendment) Act, 1985</a>,  insofar  as\nit seeks to introduce the Tenth Schedule in the[pic]Constitution  of  India,\nthe provisions of Paragraph 7 of the Tenth Schedule of the  Constitution  in\nterms and in effect bring about a change in  the  operation  and  effect  of\n<a href=\"/doc/427855/\" id=\"a_2118\">Articles 136</a><a href=\"/doc/1712542/\" id=\"a_2119\">, 226</a> and <a href=\"/doc/1331149/\" id=\"a_2120\">227</a> of the Constitution of India and,  therefore,  the\namendment would require to be ratified in accordance  with  the  proviso  to\nsub-article (2) of <a href=\"/doc/594125/\" id=\"a_2121\">Article 368</a> of the Constitution of India.\u201d\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1281\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1686885\" id=\"span_582\">457.  Strictly speaking, therefore, an amendment to the Constitution can  be\nchallenged only if it alters the basic structure of the Constitution  and  a\nlaw  can  be  challenged  if:  (1)  It  is  beyond  the  competence  of  the\nLegislature; (2) It violates <a href=\"/doc/134715/\" id=\"a_2122\">Article 13</a>  of  the  Constitution;  (3)  It  is\nenacted contrary to a  prohibition  in  the  Constitution;  and  (4)  It  is\nenacted without following the procedure <a href=\"/doc/1686885/\" id=\"a_2123\">laid down in</a> the Constitution.</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1282\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1992295\" id=\"span_583\">458.  At the same time, it has  been  emphasized  by  this  Court  that  the\npossibility of abuse of a provision  of  a  statute  is  not  a  ground  for\nstriking it down. An abuse of power can always be checked  through  judicial\nreview of the action complained of. In <a href=\"/doc/1992295/\" id=\"a_2124\">D.K.  Trivedi  &amp;  Sons  v.  State  of\nGujarat</a>[653] it was said:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_800\">\u201cWhere a statute confers discretionary  powers  upon  the  executive  or  an\nadministrative authority, the validity or constitutionality  of  such  power\ncannot be judged on the assumption that  the  executive  or  such  authority\nwill act in an arbitrary manner in the exercise of the discretion  conferred\nupon it. If the  executive  or  the  administrative  authority  acts  in  an\narbitrary manner, its action would be bad in law and  liable  to  be  struck\ndown by the courts but the  possibility  of  abuse  of  power  or  arbitrary\nexercise of power cannot invalidate the statute conferring the power or  the\npower which has been conferred by it.\u201d\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1283\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"156242701\" id=\"span_584\">459.  Similarly, Justice B.P.  Jeevan  Reddy  (speaking  for  Justice   J.S.\nVerma, Justice  S.C. Agrawal, Justice  A.S. Anand, Justice  B.N. Kirpal  and\nhimself) held in <a href=\"/doc/156242701/\" id=\"a_2125\">Mafatlal Industries Ltd. v. Union of India</a>[654]:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_801\">\u201cIt is equally well-settled that mere possibility of abuse  of  a  provision\nby those in charge of administering it cannot be a ground  for  holding  the\nprovision  procedurally  or  substantively  unreasonable.  <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"174974\" id=\"span_585\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1193965\" id=\"span_586\">In  <a href=\"/doc/1193965/\" id=\"a_2126\">Collector  of\nCustoms v. Nathella Sampathu Chetty</a>, this Court observed:  \u201cThe  possibility\nof abuse of a statute otherwise valid does not impart to it any  element  of\ninvalidity.\u201d It was said in <a href=\"/doc/174974/\" id=\"a_2127\">State of Rajasthan v. Union of India</a>,  \u201cit  must\nbe remembered that merely because power may sometimes be abused,  it  is  no\nground for denying the existence of power. The wisdom of  man  has  not  yet\nbeen able to conceive of a government with power sufficient  to  answer  all\nits legitimate needs and at the same time incapable of mischief\u201d.</span></span> (Also  see\nCommr., H.R.E. v. Sri Lakshmindra  Thirtha  Swamiar  of  Sri  Shirur  Mutt.\u201d\n(Internal citations omitted)\n\n\n<a href=\"/doc/1283667/\" id=\"a_2128\">Article 122</a> of the Constitution\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1284\">460.  Before dealing with the substantive issue of the challenge before  us,\nit may be mentioned that Mr. Fali S. Nariman contended that  Parliament  did\nnot have the competence to pass the <a href=\"/doc/142995521/\" id=\"a_2129\">NJAC Act</a>  until  the  99th  Constitution\nAmendment Act was brought into force or at least it had the  assent  of  the\nPresident.  It is not possible to accept this submission since  the  passage\nof  the  99th  Constitution   Amendment   Act   and   the   NJAC   Act   was\ncontemporaneous, if not more  or  less  simultaneous.  In  view  of  <a href=\"/doc/953264/\" id=\"a_2130\">Article\n122(1)</a>  of  the  Constitution  which  provides  that  the  validity  of  any\nproceedings in Parliament shall not be called in question on the  ground  of\nany alleged irregularity of procedure, it is not possible to delve into  the\nproceedings in Parliament.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1285\">461.  <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"690805\" id=\"span_587\">In  <a href=\"/doc/690805/\" id=\"a_2131\">Babulal Parate v. State of Bombay</a>[655] this Court  added,  by  way\nof a post-script, its view on <a href=\"/doc/953264/\" id=\"a_2132\">Article 122(1)</a> of the  Constitution.   It  was\nobserved that in a given hypothetical situation the  question  will  not  be\nthe  validity  of  proceedings  in  Parliament  but  the  violation   of   a\nconstitutional provision. It was said as follows:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_802\">\u201cIt is advisable, perhaps, to add a few more words about <a href=\"/doc/953264/\" id=\"a_2133\">Art. 122(1)</a> of  the\nConstitution.  Learned counsel for the appellant has  posed  before  us  the\nquestion as to what would be the effect of  that  Article  if  in  any  Bill\ncompletely unrelated to any of the matters referred to in Cls.  (a)  to  (e)\nof <a href=\"/doc/1659104/\" id=\"a_2134\">Art. 3</a> an amendment  was  to  be  proposed  and  accepted  changing  (for\nexample) the name of a State.  We do not think that we need  answer  such  a\nhypothetical question except merely to say that if an amendment is  of  such\na character that it is not really an amendment and is clearly  violative  of\n<a href=\"/doc/1659104/\" id=\"a_2135\">Art. 3</a>, the question then  will  be  not  the  validity  of  proceedings  in\nParliament but the violation of a constitutional provision.\u201d\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1286\">462.  <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"61480953\" id=\"span_588\">In <a href=\"/doc/61480953/\" id=\"a_2136\">Raja Ram Pal v. Lok  Sabha</a>[656]  the  question  of  the  extent  of\njudicial  review  of  parliamentary  matters  came  up  for   consideration.\nSpeaking for Justices K.G. Balakrishnan, D.K. Jain and himself, it was  held\nby Chief Justice Sabharwal,  with  reference  to  the  CAD  that  procedural\nirregularities in Parliament cannot undo or vitiate what happens within  its\nfour  walls,  that  is,   internal   parliamentary   proceedings.   However,\nproceedings that are substantively illegal or unconstitutional,  as  opposed\nto irregular are not protected from judicial scrutiny by <a href=\"/doc/953264/\" id=\"a_2137\">Article  122(1)</a>  of\nthe Constitution.[657]</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1287\">463.  Insofar as the <a href=\"/doc/142995521/\" id=\"a_2138\">NJAC Act</a> is concerned, nothing has been  shown  by  way\nof any substantive illegality in its passage  or  anything  unconstitutional\nin its passage in the sense that any provision of the  Constitution  or  any\nsubstantive rule regulating parliamentary activity has  been  violated.   At\nbest, it can be argued that procedurally  there  was  a  violation  but  our\nattention was drawn to the rules of procedure  and  the  decision  taken  in\naccordance with the rules  which  indicate  that  there  was  no  procedural\nviolation in the introduction of the  <a href=\"/doc/142995521/\" id=\"a_2139\">NJAC  Act</a>  and  its  passage.  Justice\nKhehar has elaborately dealt with this issue in substantial  detail  in  his\ndraft judgment and it is not necessary to repeat what has been said.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1288\">The amendments that are challenged - discussion\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1289\">464.  Though no one has a right to  be  appointed  a  judge  either  of  the\nSupreme Court or a High Court, it does  not  mean  that  the  President  can\ndecline to appoint a person as a judge without any rhyme or reason nor  does\nit mean that the President can appoint  any  eligible  person  as  a  judge.\nUnder the Government of India Act, 1919 and the  <a href=\"/doc/93056159/\" id=\"a_2140\">Government  of  India  Act,\n1935</a> the Crown had the unfettered discretion  to  do  both  or  either.  The\nConstituent Assembly did not give this unfettered  power  to  the  President\nand, therefore, mandated consultation between the President  and  the  Chief\nJustice of India for the appointment of a judge of the Supreme Court.  There\nwere reasons for this as mentioned above. Prior  to  the  99th  Constitution\nAmendment Act, under <a href=\"/doc/1995948/\" id=\"a_2141\">Article 124(2)</a> of the Constitution, the  President  had\nthe discretion to consult some other judges of  the  Supreme  Court  or  the\nHigh Courts, as the President thought necessary for the  purpose.  The  same\nconstitutional  position  prevailed  (mutatis  mutandis)  so  far   as   the\nappointment of a  judge  of  a  High  Court  under  <a href=\"/doc/259647/\" id=\"a_2142\">Article  217(1)</a>  of  the\nConstitution was concerned.  <a href=\"/doc/1995948/\" id=\"a_2143\">Article 124(2)</a> of the  Constitution  had  three\nbasic ingredients: The power of the President to  appoint  a  judge  of  the\nSupreme Court; a  mandatory  requirement  of  consultation  with  the  Chief\nJustice of India; a discretionary consultation  with  other  judges  of  the\nSupreme Court and the High Courts.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1290\">465.  The 99th  Constitution  Amendment  Act  has  completely  changed  this\nconstitutional position and has changed the role of  the  President  in  the\nappointment  process  as   also   substantially   modified   the   mandatory\nconsultation with the Chief Justice of India  and  substituted  or  replaced\nthe entire process by a recommendation of the NJAC.  The table  below  gives\nthe textual changes made in <a href=\"/doc/1995948/\" id=\"a_2144\">Article 124(2)</a> of the Constitution.\n|Pre- Amendment provisions           |Post-Amendment provisions            |\n|124. Establishment and constitution |124. Establishment and constitution  |\n|of Supreme Court. - (1) There shall |of Supreme Court. - (1) There shall  |\n|be a Supreme Court of India         |be a Supreme Court of India          |\n|consisting of a Chief Justice of    |consisting of a Chief Justice of     |\n|India and, until Parliament by law  |India and, until Parliament by law   |\n|prescribes a larger number, of not  |prescribes a larger number, of not   |\n|more than seven other Judges.       |more than seven other Judges.        |\n|                                    |                                     |\n|(2) Every Judge of the Supreme Court|(2) Every Judge of the Supreme Court |\n|shall be appointed by the President |shall be appointed by the President  |\n|by warrant under his hand and seal  |by warrant under his hand and seal on|\n|after consultation with such of the |the recommendation of the National   |\n|Judges of the Supreme Court and of  |Judicial Appointments Commission     |\n|the High Courts in the States as the|referred to in <a href=\"/doc/140653939/\" id=\"a_2145\">article 124A</a> and shall|\n|President may deem necessary for the|hold office until he attains the age |\n|purpose and shall hold office until |of sixty-five years:                 |\n|he attains the age of sixty-five    |                                     |\n|years:                              |                                     |\n|                                    |                                     |\n|Provided that in the case of        |omitted                              |\n|appointment of a Judge other than   |                                     |\n|the Chief Justice, the Chief Justice|                                     |\n|of India shall always be consulted: |                                     |\n|                                    |                                     |\n|Provided further that\u2014 (a) a Judge  |Provided that- (a) a Judge may, by   |\n|may, by writing under his hand      |writing under his hand addressed to  |\n|addressed to the President, resign  |the President, resign his office;    |\n|his office;                         |(b) a Judge may be removed from his  |\n|(b) a Judge may be removed from his |office in the manner provided in     |\n|office in the manner provided in    |clause (4).                          |\n|clause (4).                         |                                     |\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1291\">466.  The composition of the NJAC is provided for in  <a href=\"/doc/140653939/\" id=\"a_2146\">Article  124A</a>  of  the\nConstitution. Therefore,  <a href=\"/doc/140653939/\" id=\"a_2147\">Article  124A</a>  of  the  Constitution  and  <a href=\"/doc/1995948/\" id=\"a_2148\">Article\n124(2)</a> are required to be read in conjunction with  each  other.  The  Chief\nJustice of India is the Chairperson of the NJAC.  The members  of  the  NJAC\nare two other judges of the Supreme Court  next  to  the  Chief  Justice  of\nIndia, the Union Minister in charge of  Law  and  Justice  and  two  eminent\npersons to be nominated by a Committee consisting  of  the  Prime  Minister,\nthe Chief Justice of India and the Leader of Opposition in  the  Lok  Sabha,\nfailing which the leader of the single largest Opposition Party in  the  Lok\nSabha.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1292\">467.  The duty  of  the  NJAC  as  provided  for  in  <a href=\"/doc/61056230/\" id=\"a_2149\">Article  124B</a>  of  the\nConstitution is to recommend persons for appointment as  the  Chief  Justice\nof India, judges of the Supreme Court, Chief Justices  of  High  Courts  and\nother judges of High Courts and to recommend the transfer of Chief  Justices\nand other judges of a High Court from one  High  Court  to  any  other  High\nCourt.  The NJAC has the duty to ensure  that  the  person  recommended  has\nability and integrity.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1293\">468.  <a href=\"/doc/16427836/\" id=\"a_2150\">Article 124C</a> of the Constitution provides that Parliament may  by  law\nregulate the procedure for the appointment of the  Chief  Justice  of  India\nand other judges of the Supreme Court, the Chief Justice  and  other  judges\nof the  High  Courts.  The  Article  empowers  the  NJAC  to  lay  down,  by\nregulations, the procedure for the discharge of its  functions,  the  manner\nof selection of persons for appointment and such other  matters  as  may  be\nconsidered necessary.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1294\">469.  Simultaneous with the above amendments in the <a href=\"/doc/142995521/\" id=\"a_2151\">Constitution,  the  NJAC\nAct</a> was passed by Parliament. <a href=\"/doc/142995521/\" id=\"a_2152\">The NJAC Act</a>  provides  for  recommending  the\nsenior-most judge of the Supreme Court as the Chief Justice of India \u2018if  he\nis considered fit to  hold  the  office\u2019  and  for  recommending  names  for\nappointment as a judge of the Supreme Court persons who are eligible  to  be\nso appointed. Interestingly, the NJAC \u2018shall  not  recommend  a  person  for\nappointment if any two members of the  Commission  do  not  agree  for  such\nrecommendation\u2019 (<a href=\"/doc/125564484/\" id=\"a_2153\">Section 5</a> of the NJAC Act). A  somewhat  similar  procedure\nhas been provided for recommending the appointment of the Chief  Justice  of\na High Court and a judge of a High Court (<a href=\"/doc/20215517/\" id=\"a_2154\">Section 6</a> of the NJAC Act).\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1295\">470.  The President may accept  the  recommendation  of  the  NJAC  for  the\nappointment of a particular person as a judge,  but  may  also  require  the\nNJAC to reconsider its recommendation.  If  the  NJAC  affirms  its  earlier\nrecommendation  the  President  shall  issue  the  warrant  of   appointment\n(<a href=\"/doc/18845777/\" id=\"a_2155\">Section 7</a> of the NJAC Act).\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1296\">471.  The officers and employees of the  NJAC  shall  be  appointed  by  the\nCentral Government in consultation with the NJAC and  the  convener  of  the\nNJAC shall be the Secretary to the Government of India in the Department  of\nLaw and Justice (<a href=\"/doc/198877115/\" id=\"a_2156\">Section 8</a> of the NJAC Act).\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1297\">472.  The procedure for the transfer  of  judges  from  one  High  Court  to\nanother has been left to be determined by regulations to be  framed  by  the\nNJAC (<a href=\"/doc/46047158/\" id=\"a_2157\">Section 9</a> of the Act). Similarly, the  NJAC  shall  frame  regulations\nwith regard to the procedure for the discharge of its functions (<a href=\"/doc/188325285/\" id=\"a_2158\">Section  10</a>\nof the Act).\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1298\">473.  The Central Government is empowered to make Rules  to  carry  out  the\nprovisions of the <a href=\"/doc/142995521/\" id=\"a_2159\">NJAC Act</a> (<a href=\"/doc/125361718/\" id=\"a_2160\">Section 11</a> thereof) and the Commission may  make\nRules to carry out the provisions of the <a href=\"/doc/142995521/\" id=\"a_2161\">NJAC Act</a> (<a href=\"/doc/112842572/\" id=\"a_2162\">Section 12</a> thereof).  The\nRules and Regulations framed by the  Central  Government  and  by  the  NJAC\nshall be laid before Parliament and  these  may  be  modified  if  both  the\nHouses of Parliament agree to  the  modification  and  Parliament  may  also\nprovide that  a  Rule  or  Regulation  shall  have  no  effect  (<a href=\"/doc/184356182/\" id=\"a_2163\">Section  13</a>\nthereof).\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1299\">474.   The  sum  and  substance  of  the  controversy  is   this:   If   the\nestablishment of the NJAC by the 99th Constitution Amendment Act alters  the\nbasic structure of the Constitution,  the 99th  Constitution  Amendment  Act\nand the NJAC Act must be declared unconstitutional. Since the  establishment\nof the NJAC by <a href=\"/doc/140653939/\" id=\"a_2164\">Article 124A</a> of the Constitution  is  integral  to  the  99th\nConstitution Amendment Act and the NJAC Act and they are not  severable  and\ncannot stand alone, they too must be declared unconstitutional.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1300\">475.   While  considering  the   constitutional   validity   of   the   99th\nConstitution Amendment Act and the NJAC Act it is necessary to deal  with  a\nsubmission made with reference to the Constitutional Reform Act  2005  (CRA)\npassed by the British Parliament. This is because it was  referred,  in  the\ncourse of submissions, on more than  one  occasion.  It  was  sought  to  be\nsuggested that judges in the UK Supreme Court are appointed by the  Judicial\nAppointments Commission constituted  in  terms  of  the  CRA  and  there  is\nnothing wrong if a somewhat similar procedure is adopted by  our  Parliament\nwhere judges of the High Courts and the Supreme  Court  are  recommended  by\nthe NJAC.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1301\">476.  The CRA and its working was  adverted  to  by  Jack  Straw,  the  Lord\nChancellor from 2007 to 2010. At that time the Lord Chief  Justice  was  the\nhead of the judiciary in the UK but the  Lord  Chancellor  was  nevertheless\nresponsible \u2018for upholding the independence of the judiciary\u2019.  In  the  3rd\nlecture on \u2018Judicial Appointments\u2019 delivered on 4th December,  2012  of  the\n64th series of Hamlyn Lectures titled \u2018Aspects of Law Reform \u2013 An  Insider\u2019s\nPerspective\u2019 he said:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_803\">\u201cThe  CRA  provided  for  the  establishment  of  an  independent   Judicial\nAppointments Commission (JAC).\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_804\">The JAC was made responsible for  operating  the  appointments  process  and\nmaking recommendations to the Lord Chancellor for all but  the  most  senior\nappointments.  For these very senior appointments (to the Court  of  Appeal,\nand the offices of Head of Division, Lord Chief Justice, and the  president,\ndeputy president and members of the UK Supreme  court),  separate  provision\nwas made for recommendations to be made to the Lord Chancellor by  specially\nconstituted selection panels.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_805\">            For each appointment, the  JAC,  or  the  specially  constituted\nselection panel,\nwas required to make one recommendation to the Lord Chancellor.\u201d[658]\n\n\u201cIn practice, as I found  out  through  painful  experience,  there  were  a\nnumber of problems with this set-up.\u201d[659]\n\n\u201cI accept that the role of the Lord Chancellor in  relation  to  High  Court\nand Court of Appeal appointments should be limited.  But for the two  groups\nof our most senior judges, and for different reasons, in my  view  the  Lord\nChancellor  should  have  a  greater  role  than  is  provided  for  by  the\nConstitutional Reform Act, or than is likely  to  be  provided  for  by  the\ncurrent Crime and Courts Bill.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_806\">The two groups of judges I am talking about  are,  first,  the  most  senior\nmembers of the Court of Appeal \u2013 that is, the Heads  of  Division  and  Lord\nChief Justice- and, second,  the members  of  the  UK  Supreme  Court.   The\nconclusion is the same, but the arguments are different.\u201d[660]\n\nThe \u2018specially constituted selection panel\u2019 for the  appointment  of  judges\nof the UK Supreme Court (for example) is provided for in  Section  26(5)  of\nthe CRA read with Schedule 8 thereof and the  selection  panel  consists  of\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_807\">(a) the President of the Supreme Court, (b)  the  Deputy  President  of  the\nSupreme Court,  (c)  one  member  each  of  (i)  the  Judicial  Appointments\nCommission, (ii) the Judicial Appointments Board  for  Scotland,  (iii)  the\nNorthern Ireland Judicial Appointments Commission. At least  one  member  in\ncategory  (c)  must  be  \u2018non-legally  qualified\u2019.  With  this  sort  of   a\ncomposition of the \u2018specially constituted selection panel\u2019 Jack Straw  could\nnot go against the wishes of the judiciary in respect  of  one  appointment,\nas obliquely referred to by him below:\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_808\">\u201cAll  of  this  is  already  recognized,  in  principle  at  least,  by  the\nConstitutional Reform Act, which provides that  these  two  groups  of  very\nsenior appointments should not be made by the normal  Judicial  Appointments\nCommission process.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_809\">The reality of a connection between the senior judiciary and  the  executive\nis also recognized in almost every other  jurisdiction.   By  far  the  most\nusual approach elsewhere in the world, including in well-functioning common-\nlaw jurisdictions, is for the relevant minister to be recommended  three  to\nfive names, and for that minister then to  be  able  to  choose  from  among\nthese nominees.  In the United Kingdom we  are  very  unusual  in  insisting\nthat the minister receives one name alone.  This is explicable only  in  the\ncontext of where we have come from: the untrammeled discretion of  the  Lord\nChancellor until the mid 1990s, the non-statutory  nature  of  the  pre-2005\narrangements, the opaque decision-making process and the mounting  criticism\nof it.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_810\">But  these  literally  peculiar   arrangements   for   these   very   senior\nappointments,  intended  to  create  a  partnership  approach  between   the\njudiciary and the Lord Chancellor in recognition of the requirements of  the\noffices in question, have proved to be unsatisfactory.\nBoth the detailed wording and the  expectation  in  practice  make  it  very\ndifficult for the Lord Chancellor to exercise even  his  limited  powers  to\nreject or request a reconsideration of a recommendation.  As is a matter  of\nrecord in the press, there was one occasion  when,  as  Lord  Chancellor,  I\nsought to use these powers.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_811\">Since  I  have  always  observed  the  confidentiality  necessary  for   the\nconsideration of such appointments, I am not here going into any  detail.  I\nhope, however, that it will be accepted that I  would  not  have  sought  to\nexercise these powers unless I believed that I had good grounds  within  the\nAct for doing so I did \u2013 good grounds, as  many  can  now  see.  I  went  to\nconsiderable lengths to ensure that  my  actions  could  not  be  construed,\nwhich they were not remotely, as party political. In the event,  the  matter\nwas not seen through to a conclusion. Partisans to  the  appointment  \u2013  not\nanyone directly involved in the process \u2013 leaked  extensive  detail  to  the\npress, an election was looming; I confirmed the appointment.\u201d[661]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1302\">477.  Adverting to this lecture and the actual working of  the  CRA,  it  is\nsaid that for making senior level judicial appointments, it  is  \u2018impossible\nfor the Lord Chancellor to against  the  wishes  of  the  judiciary\u2019.  In  a\nrecent article published in Public Law it is said:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_812\">\u201cJudicial appointments are  the  next  biggest  change,  responsibility  for\nwhich has shifted from the executive in the form of the Lord Chancellor,  to\nthe judiciary. Formally the process is managed by the  independent  Judicial\nAppointments Commission (JAC),  but  in  practice  the  process  is  heavily\ninfluenced by the judiciary at  every  stage.  The  Lord  Chief  Justice  is\nconsulted at the start of each competition. Judges prepare case studies  and\nqualifying tests. Judges write references. A judge sits on the  panels  that\ninterview candidates; and judges are consulted  in  statutory  consultation.\nOn the JAC, 7  of  the  15  commissioners  are  judges.
