Once  the  JAC  has\ncompleted its selection, at lower levels  (Circuit  judges  and  below)  all\njudicial appointments are now formally made by the Lord Chief  Justice,  and\ntribunal appointments are made by the Senior  President  of  Tribunals.  The\nLord Chief Justice\u00a0and SPT are now  responsible  for  97  per  cent  of  all\njudicial  appointments.  At  more  senior  levels  appointments  are   still\nformally decided by the Lord Chancellor;  but  in  practice  it  has  proved\nimpossible for  the  Lord  Chancellor  to  go  against  the  wishes  of  the\njudiciary.\u201d[662]\n\nSo much for the appointment process in the UK  and  the  \u2018judges  appointing\njudges\u2019 criticism in India!\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1303\">478.  It is not possible for any one of us to comment (one way  or  another)\non the CRA except to say that it is not  advisable  to  rely  on  values  of\njudicial independence and conventions and  systems  of  the  appointment  of\njudges in other countries without a full  understanding  of  their  problems\nand issues. We ought to better understand the situation in our country  (and\nthe decisions rendered by this Court) and how best to protect  and  preserve\njudicial independence in the circumstances that exist  in  our  country  and\nnot have grand illusions of the systems in place in other countries.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1304\">Validity of <a href=\"/doc/140653939/\" id=\"a_2165\">Articles 124A</a> and <a href=\"/doc/1995948/\" id=\"a_2166\">124(2)</a> of the Constitution - the package deal\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1305\">479.  The submission of the learned Attorney-General  (as  mentioned  above)\nis that the 99th Constitution Amendment Act and the NJAC Act are a  \u2018package\ndeal\u2019 and one cannot be appreciated without the other. The  discussion  will\nbe in the light of this submission.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1306\">480.   At  the  outset,  it  is  important  to  note  that  the  package  is\nincomplete. The 99th Constitution Amendment Act and the  NJAC  Act  raise  a\nseries of unanswered questions. For example, how is  the  NJAC  expected  to\nperform its duties? Will there be any transparency in  the  working  of  the\nNJAC and if so to what extent? Will privacy concerns of the \u2018candidates\u2019  be\ntaken care of? Will issues of accountability of the NJAC be  addressed?  The\nlearned Attorney-General submitted  that  a  large  number  of  hypothetical\nissues and questions have been raised not only by the petitioners  but  also\nby the Bench and it is not possible to answer all of them in the absence  of\na composite  law  and  regulations  being  framed  in  accordance  with  the\npostulates of the 99th Constitution Amendment Act. This  submission  of  the\nlearned Attorney-General cannot be appreciated particularly in view  of  his\ncontention, raised on more than one occasion, that what is  enacted  by  the\n99th Constitution Amendment Act is a package deal. Unless all  eventualities\nare taken care of, the package deal presented to the  country  is  an  empty\npackage with the wrapping paper in the form of the <a href=\"/doc/142995521/\" id=\"a_2167\">NJAC Act</a> and a ribbon  in\nthe form of the 99th Constitution Amendment Act. If it is  not  possible  to\nanswer all the questions in the  absence  of  a  composite  law,  rules  and\nregulations, what was the hurry in bringing the 99th Constitution  Amendment\nAct and the NJAC Act into force as a half-baked measure?\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1307\">481.  It is true that the Constitution cannot specify and  incorporate  each\nand every detail, particularly procedural details.[663] But the  same  time,\nthe substantive requirements of the NJAC scheme must be  apparent  from  the\n99th Constitution Amendment Act read with the <a href=\"/doc/142995521/\" id=\"a_2168\">NJAC  Act</a>,  particularly  when\nit seeks to overthrow an existing  method  of  appointment  of  judges  that\nmaintains the independence of the judiciary. Vital issues cannot be left  to\nbe  sorted  out  at  a  later  date  through  supplementary  legislation  or\nsupplementary  subordinate  legislation,  otherwise  an  unwholesome  hiatus\nwould be created, making matters worse.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1308\">482.  The package deal must survive as whole or fall  as  a  whole  \u2013  there\ncannot be piecemeal existence.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1309\">483.  Viewed in this light, the constitutional validity  of  <a href=\"/doc/1995948/\" id=\"a_2169\">Article  124(2)</a>\nread with <a href=\"/doc/140653939/\" id=\"a_2170\">Article 124A</a>  of  the  Constitution  as  introduced  by  the  99th\nConstitution Amendment Act is suspect for several reasons.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1310\">(a) The NJAC and the role of the President\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1311\">484.  <a href=\"/doc/1995948/\" id=\"a_2171\">Article 124(2)</a> of  the  Constitution  requires  the  NJAC  constituted\nunder <a href=\"/doc/140653939/\" id=\"a_2172\">Article 124A</a> thereof to make a recommendation  to  the  President  for\nthe appointment of a judge of the Supreme Court or a High  Court.  Mr.  Fali\nS. Nariman pointed out that as far as the  NJAC  is  concerned,  it  is  not\nclear  whether  the  President  means  the  President  acting   in   his/her\nindividual capacity or the Council of  Ministers.  The  President  certainly\ncannot mean the  individual  otherwise  the  procedure  for  appointment  of\njudges postulated by the 99th Constitution Amendment Act and  the  NJAC  Act\nwould be creating an Imperium in  Imperio  which  the  Constituent  Assembly\ndeliberately avoided. On the other hand, if the President means the  Council\nof Ministers, then on what basis can the Council of Ministers/President  ask\nthe NJAC (under the proviso to <a href=\"/doc/18845777/\" id=\"a_2173\">Section 7</a> of the NJAC Act) to reconsider  its\nview? The  Council  of  Ministers/President  is  already  represented  as  a\n\u2018voting  member\u2019  in  the  NJAC  through   the   Law   Minister.   Can   the\nPresident/Council of Ministers/Prime Minister ask for reconsideration  of  a\nrecommendation made by the NJAC to which the Law Minister (a member  of  the\nCabinet) is a party? Would this be permissible particularly  since  the  Law\nMinister represents the Union Government/President in the NJAC and would  it\nnot go against the well established  principle  of  Cabinet  responsibility?\nAlternatively, would it not undermine the authority of the Law  Minister  if\nin a given case the Law Ministers agrees to an appointment but  the  Council\nof Ministers does not  accept  it?  More  importantly,  is  the  Council  of\nMinisters/President an oversight body as far as the NJAC is concerned?\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1312\">485.   Assuming  (despite  the   above   doubts)   that   the   Council   of\nMinisters/President requires the NJAC to reconsider its  recommendation  and\non reconsideration the NJAC reiterates  its  recommendation,  the  President\nwill be bound thereby even if it means  overruling  the  objections  of  the\nChief Justice of India. The objection to  this  process  of  appointment  of\njudges is two-fold. Firstly, the authority that is statutorily conferred  on\nthe NJAC to bind the President by the <a href=\"/doc/142995521/\" id=\"a_2174\">NJAC Act</a>  is  well  beyond  the  power\nconferred by <a href=\"/doc/1995948/\" id=\"a_2175\">Article 124(2)</a> of the Constitution  or  the  99th  Constitution\nAmendment Act. Secondly, in the event of such a reiteration, the opinion  of\nthe Chief Justice of India eventually counts for nothing,  contrary  to  the\nintention of the Constituent Assembly  and  the  constitutional  conventions\nfollowed over decades. Historically, no  appointment  (except  perhaps  one)\nhas been made without the consent of the Chief  Justice  of  India.  Is  the\n99th Constitution Amendment Act intended, wittingly or unwittingly, to  give\na short shrift to the views of the Constituent Assembly  and  constitutional\nconventions and to sublimate the views of the Chief Justice of  India?  This\nprocedure may be contrasted with the  collegium  system  of  appointment  in\nwhich the President could turn down a recommendation made by  the  collegium\nif it was not unanimous. In the present dispensation,  this  entitlement  of\nthe President is taken away, even if the recommendation  is  not  unanimous,\nand thereby the importance of the President is considerably downsized.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1313\">486.  Additionally, the decision of the President is, in one sense, made  to\ndepend upon the opinion of two members of the NJAC, who may in a given  case\nbe the two eminent persons  nominated  to  the  NJAC  in  terms  of  <a href=\"/doc/10460741/\" id=\"a_2176\">Article\n124A(1)(d)</a> of the Constitution.  These  two  eminent  persons  can  actually\nstymie a recommendation of the NJAC  for  the  appointment  of  a  judge  by\nexercising a veto conferred on  each  member  of  the  NJAC  by  the  second\nproviso to sub-section (2) of  <a href=\"/doc/125564484/\" id=\"a_2177\">Section  5</a>  of  the  NJAC  Act,  and  without\nassigning any reason. In other words, the two eminent persons  (or  any  two\nmembers of the NJAC) can stall the appointment  of  judges  without  reason.\nThat this may not  necessarily  happen  with  any  great  frequency  is  not\nrelevant \u2013 that such a situation can occur is disturbing.  As  a  result  of\nthis provision, the responsibility of  making  an  appointment  of  a  judge\neffectively passes over, in part, from the President and the  Chief  Justice\nof India to the members of the NJAC, with a veto being conferred on any  two\nunspecified members, without any specific  justification.  This  is  a  very\nsignificant constitutional change brought about  by  the  99th  Constitution\nAmendment Act which not only impinges upon but radically alters the  process\nof appointment of judges, by shifting the balance  from  the  President  and\nthe Chief Justice  of  India  to  the  NJAC.  To  make  matters  worse,  the\nPresident cannot even seek the views of anybody (other judges or lawyers  or\ncivil  society)  which  was  permissible  prior  to  the  99th  Constitution\nAmendment Act and a part of <a href=\"/doc/1995948/\" id=\"a_2178\">Article 124(2)</a> of the Constitution prior to  its\namendment. It may be  recalled  that  <a href=\"/doc/1995948/\" id=\"a_2179\">Article  124(2)</a>  of  the  Constitution\nenables the President to consult judges of the Supreme Court  and  the  High\nCourt but that entitlement is  now  taken  away  by  the  99th  Constitution\nAmendment Act. The President, in the  process,  is  actually  reduced  to  a\ndummy.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1314\">487.  It may also be recalled that the  President  (as  an  individual)  had\nexpressed a viewpoint as reported in India Today magazine of  25th  January,\n1999 concerning  the  appointment  of  judges  of  the  Supreme  Court.  The\nexistence of such a possibility is now not possible since the President  (as\nan individual) has really no role to play in the appointment process  except\nissuing a warrant of appointment when asked to do so.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1315\">488.  The sum and substance of this discussion is that there is  no  clarity\non the role of the President. In any event, the discretion available to  the\nPresident  to  consult  judges  of  the  Supreme  Court  in  the  matter  of\nappointment of judges is taken  away;  the  decision  of  the  President  is\nsubject  to  the  opinion  of  two  eminent  persons  neither  of  whom   is\nconstitutionally accountable; there is  a  doubt  on  the  well  established\nprinciple of Cabinet responsibility; a statute  -  the  <a href=\"/doc/142995521/\" id=\"a_2180\">NJAC  Act</a>,  not  the\nConstitution binds the President contrary to the  constitutional  framework;\nthe 99th Constitution  Amendment  Act  makes  serious  and  unconstitutional\ninroads into <a href=\"/doc/1995948/\" id=\"a_2181\">Article 124(2)</a> of the Constitution, as originally framed.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1316\">(b) Role of the Chief Justice of India and the Judiciary\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1317\">489.  The Chief Justice of India  is  undoubtedly  the  Chairperson  of  the\nNJAC. However, the participation  of  the  Chief  Justice  of  India  as  an\nindividual and the participation of the judiciary as an institution  in  the\nNJAC is made farcical by the 99th Constitution Amendment Act  and  the  NJAC\nAct. Even though the opinion of the Chief Justice of  India,  a  pre-eminent\nconstitutional authority in the judiciary, regarding the  suitability  of  a\nperson for appointment as a judge is acceptable to a majority of members  of\nthe NJAC, it can be thumbed down by two of its other  members  in  terms  of\n<a href=\"/doc/125564484/\" id=\"a_2182\">Section 5</a> of the NJAC Act. These two  persons  might  be  the  Law  Minister\n(representing the President) and an eminent person or  two  eminent  persons\nneither of whom represent or purport to represent the President,  the  other\npre-eminent  constitutional  authority  in  the  appointment  process  under\n<a href=\"/doc/1995948/\" id=\"a_2183\">Article 124(2)</a> of the Constitution prior to its amendment.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1318\">490.  The 99th Constitution Amendment  Act  reduces  the  Chief  Justice  of\nIndia, despite being the head of the judiciary, to one of six  in  the  NJAC\nmaking a  recommendation  to  the  President  thereby  denuding  him/her  of\nconventional, historical  and  legitimate  constitutional  significance  and\nauthority and substantially skewing the appointment  process  postulated  by\nthe Constituent Assembly and the Constitution.  The  opinion  of  the  Chief\nJustice of India had \u2018graded weight\u2019 or the \u2018greatest weight\u2019 prior  to  the\n99th Constitution Amendment Act. But  now  with  the  passage  of  the  99th\nConstitution Amendment Act and the NJAC Act the Chief Justice  of  India  is\nreduced to a mere voting statistic. Designating the Chief Justice  of  India\nas the Chairperson of the NJAC is certainly not a solace or  a  solution  to\ndownsizing the head of the Judiciary.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1319\">491.  The participation of the judiciary as an institution in  the  NJAC  is\nalso farcical. The 99th Constitution Amendment  Act  does  not  postulate  a\n\u2018veto\u2019 being conferred on  any  person  in  the  NJAC.   <a href=\"/doc/142995521/\" id=\"a_2184\">But  the  NJAC  Act</a>\neffectively gives that power to all members of the  NJAC  despite  the  99th\nConstitution Amendment Act. This is evident from the provisions of the  <a href=\"/doc/142995521/\" id=\"a_2185\">NJAC\nAct</a> which enable two persons, one of them being the  Law  Minister  to  veto\nthe unanimous opinion of  the  three  participating  judges  (including  the\nChief Justice of India). Therefore, even if the Judiciary as a whole and  as\nan institution (that is the three participating judges) is in  favour  of  a\nparticular appointment, that unanimous opinion can be rendered worthless  by\nany two other members of the NJAC, one of whom may  very  well  include  the\nLaw  Minister  representing  the  political  executive  and  another  having\nperhaps nothing to do with justice delivery. This is certainly not what  the\nConstitution, as framed, postulated or intended.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1320\">492.  To get  over  this  outlandish  situation  it  was  suggested  (as  an\nalternative argument) by Mr. K.K.  Venugopal  appearing  for  the  State  of\nMadhya Pradesh that the unanimous opinion of the three participating  judges\nshould have overriding weight, that is a veto over a veto or  a  \u2018tie  break\nvote\u2019.  Mr.  Venugopal  puts  this  Court  in  a  Catch-22  situation.   The\nalternative suggested would clearly amount to  judicial  overreach  and  the\njudiciary rewriting the statute. The only rational course  is  to  interpret\nthe law as it is and if it is constitutionally valid so be it and if  it  is\nconstitutionally invalid so be it.  It  is  not  advisable  or  possible  to\nrewrite the law when the language of the statute is express.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1321\">493.  As mentioned above in considerable detail,  the  independence  of  the\njudiciary took up  so  much  discussion  time  of  several  Committees,  the\nConstituent Assembly and various  other  bodies  and  institutions.  Several\nlegal  luminaries  have  also  devoted  considerable  effort  and  given   a\nthoughtful study to the independence of the judiciary.  There was a  purpose\nto it, namely, that the independence should not be  subverted  via  external\nor  internal  pressures.  Through  the  medium  of  the  99th   Constitution\nAmendment Act  and  the  NJAC  Act,  this  independence  is  subtly  put  to\njeopardy. The President has virtually no role to play in the appointment  of\njudges, the Chief Justice of India is sidelined in the process and a  system\nthat is subject to possible erosion is put in place. Justice O\u2019Connor  said:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_813\">\u2018Judicial independence doesn\u2019t happen all  by  itself\u2026..  It\u2019s  tremendously\nhard to create, and easier than most people imagine to  destroy.\u2019  The  99th\nConstitution Amendment Act and the NJAC Act puts us face to face  with  this\ntruism in respect of the fragile bastion.\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1322\">494.  The sum and  substance  of  this  discussion  is  that  the  unanimous\nopinion of the Judiciary can be rejected  by  two  eminent  persons  or  one\neminent person and the  Law  Minister  (whose  opinion  is  subject  to  the\nopinion of the Council of Ministers, whom he/she represents); the  unanimous\nopinion of the judiciary as an institution, an opinion  that  was  respected\n(and deservedly so) counts for virtually nothing with  the  passage  of  the\n99th Constitution Amendment Act and the  NJAC  Act;  the  Chief  Justice  of\nIndia is rendered, by the 99th Constitution Amendment Act to a  mere  voting\nstatistic and one among six in the NJAC virtually stripping him/her  of  the\nconstitutional responsibility of appointing judges to  the  superior  courts\nand denuding him/her of the authority conferred by  history,  constitutional\nconvention and  the  Constitution;  the  Chief  Justice  of  India  and  the\ninstitution of the judiciary is now subject to a veto by  civil  society  in\nits decisions. The entire scheme of appointment of judges postulated by  the\nConstituent Assembly is made topsy-turvy by the 99th Constitution  Amendment\nAct and the NJAC Act. If this does not alter  the  basic  structure  of  the\nConstitution, what does?\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1323\">(c) Eminent persons and the veto\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1324\">495.  The inspiration for having eminent persons in the NJAC comes from  the\nReport of the NCRWC  which  made  this  recommendation  as  a  part  of  the\ndemocratic process of selecting a judge of the Supreme  Court  or  the  High\nCourt. <a href=\"/doc/10460741/\" id=\"a_2186\">Article 124A(1)(d)</a> of  the  Constitution  provides  for  two  eminent\npersons to be nominated as members of the  NJAC.  The  nomination  is  by  a\nCommittee consisting of the Prime Minister, the Chief Justice of  India  and\nthe Leader of the Opposition in the Lok Sabha or  where  there  is  no  such\nLeader, then the Leader of the single largest Opposition Party  in  the  Lok\nSabha. The first proviso mandates that one of the eminent persons  shall  be\nnominated from amongst  persons  belonging  to  the  Scheduled  Castes,  the\nScheduled Tribes, Other Backward Classes, Minorities or Women.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1325\">496.  The apprehension expressed by some learned counsel appearing  for  the\npetitioners is that  since  no  guidelines  have  been  laid  down  for  the\nnomination of the two eminent persons, there is a possibility  that  persons\nwho are not really eminent may be  nominated  to  the  NJAC  or  that  their\nappointment will be politically motivated.  So  also,  acknowledged  eminent\npersons might not be nominated to the NJAC. But  then,  who  is  an  eminent\nperson?\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1326\">497.  <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"875590\" id=\"span_589\">In <a href=\"/doc/875590/\" id=\"a_2187\">A.K.  Roy  v.  Union  of  India</a>[664]  reference  was  made  to  the\ndifficulty in framing precise definitions. Although the  decision  pertained\nto preventive detention and  criminal  law,  the  following  observation  is\npertinent in the context of the present discussion:</span>\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_814\">\u201cThe impossibility of  framing  a  definition  with  mathematical  precision\ncannot either justify the use of vague expressions or the total  failure  to\nframe any definition at all which  can  furnish,  by  its  inclusiveness  at\nleast, a safe guideline for understanding the  meaning  of  the  expressions\nused  by  the  legislature.  But  the  point  to  note  is  that  there  are\nexpressions  which  inherently  comprehend  such  an  infinite  variety   of\nsituations that definitions, instead of lending to them a definite  meaning,\ncan only succeed either in robbing them of their intended  amplitude  or  in\nmaking  it  necessary  to   frame   further   definitions   of   the   terms\ndefined.\u201d[665]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1327\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_590\">498.  It is also necessary to  notice  the  view  expressed  in  the  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2188\">Second\nJudges</a> case by Justice Verma speaking for the majority.  The  learned  judge\nwas of the opinion that arbitrariness in the exercise of discretion  can  be\nminimized through a collective decision. It was observed as follows:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_815\">\u201cThe rule of law envisages  the  area  of  discretion  to  be  the  minimum,\nrequiring only the application of known principles or guidelines  to  ensure\nnon-arbitrariness, but to that limited extent,  discretion  is  a  pragmatic\nneed. Conferring discretion upon high functionaries and, whenever  feasible,\nintroducing the element of plurality by  requiring  a  collective  decision,\nare  further  checks  against  arbitrariness.  This  is  how  idealism   and\npragmatism are reconciled and integrated, to make the system workable  in  a\nsatisfactory manner. Entrustment of the  task  of  appointment  of  superior\njudges to  high  constitutional  functionaries;  the  greatest  significance\nattached to the view of the Chief Justice of India, who is best equipped  to\nassess the true worth of the candidates  for  adjudging  their  suitability;\nthe opinion of the Chief Justice  of  India  being  the  collective  opinion\nformed after taking into account the views of some of  his  colleagues;  and\nthe executive being permitted to prevent an  appointment  considered  to  be\nunsuitable, for strong reasons disclosed to  the  Chief  Justice  of  India,\nprovide the best method,  in  the  constitutional  scheme,  to  achieve  the\nconstitutional purpose without conferring absolute discretion or  veto  upon\neither the judiciary or the executive, much less in any  individual,  be  he\nthe Chief Justice of India or the Prime Minister.\u201d[666]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1328\">499.  Justice Pandian in a separate but concurring  opinion  held  the  same\nview and expressed it in the following words:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_816\">\u201cIt is essential and vital  for  the  establishment  of  real  participatory\ndemocracy that all sections and classes of people, be they backward  classes\nor Scheduled Castes or Scheduled Tribes or minorities or  women,  should  be\nafforded equal opportunity so  that  the  judicial  administration  is  also\nparticipated in by the outstanding and meritorious candidates  belonging  to\nall sections of the society and not by  any  selective  or  insular  group.\u201d\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_591\">[667]</span>\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1329\">500.  <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"310431\" id=\"span_592\">In Centre for <a href=\"/doc/310431/\" id=\"a_2189\">PIL v. Union of India</a>[668] the question related  to  the\nappointment  of  the  Central  Vigilance  Commissioner  and  the   Vigilance\nCommissioners  under  the  <a href=\"/doc/1166317/\" id=\"a_2190\">Central  Vigilance  Commission  Act,  2003</a>.   The\nrelevant provision was to the effect that a Selection  Committee  consisting\nof the Prime Minister, the Minister of Home Affairs and the  Leader  of  the\nOpposition in the Lok Sabha would make a  recommendation  to  the  President\nwho would then appoint the Central Vigilance Commissioner or  the  Vigilance\nCommissioners, as the case may be, by warrant under  his  or  her  hand  and\nseal.  In this context, this Court held that Parliament had  put  its  faith\nin a High Powered Committee  and  it  is  presumed  that  the  High  Powered\nCommittee entrusted with  wide  discretion  would  exercise  its  powers  in\naccordance with the Act objectively and in a fair and reasonable manner.</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_1330\">501.  It was pointed  out  by  Mr.  Arvind  Datar,  learned  senior  counsel\nappearing for one of  the  petitioners  that  a  large  number  of  statutes\nmention the presence of eminent persons in a body, including some  that  are\nsubject specific. However, it was  pointed  out  by  the  learned  Attorney-\nGeneral that in a random sampling of some of these  statutes,  it  has  been\nfound that none of them has such a High Powered Committee as in the  <a href=\"/doc/1166317/\" id=\"a_2191\">Central\nVigilance Commission  Act</a>  for  nominating  or  recommending  a  person  for\nappointment to a post.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1331\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"PARTY\" data-docid=\"177818293\" id=\"span_593\">502.  Apart from anything else, it was submitted by  the  learned  Attorney-\nGeneral that the  presence  of  eminent  persons  in  the  NJAC  would  lend\ndiversity in the composition of the \u2018selection panel\u2019 and  that  this  would\nnecessarily reflect the views of society.  Reference  in  this  context  was\nmade to Registrar General, High Court of <a href=\"/doc/177818293/\" id=\"a_2192\">Madras v.  R.  Gandhi</a>[669]  wherein\nit was held as follows:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_817\">\u201cAppointments cannot be exclusively made from any isolated group nor  should\nit be pre-dominated by representing a narrow group. Diversity  therefore  in\njudicial appointments to pick up the  best  legally  trained  minds  coupled\nwith a qualitative personality, are the guiding factors that deserve  to  be\nobserved uninfluenced by mere considerations of individual opinions.  It  is\nfor this reason that collective consultative process as  enunciated  in  the\naforesaid decisions has been held to be an  inbuilt  mechanism  against  any\narbitrariness.\u201d[670]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1332\">503.  Under these circumstances,  there  can  be  little  objection  to  the\nparticipation by eminent persons as consultants in the appointment  process.\nIn fact, Justice Verma acknowledged that he had sought the views of  eminent\nlawyers while considering recommendations for the appointment of judges.  If\nthe Committee cannot be trusted to nominate \u2018eminent\u2019  persons,  perhaps  no\nother committee can. The trust placed on the Committee is not  a  simple  or\nstatutory trust but a constitutional trust. In  this  regard,  it  is  worth\nrecalling the words of Justice Krishna Iyer in Bhim Singhji:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_818\">\u201cThe  confusion  between  the  power  and  its  oblique   exercise   is   an\nintellectual fallacy we must guard against.  Fanciful  possibilities,  freak\nexercise  and  speculative  aberrations  are  not   realistic   enough   for\nconstitutional invalidation. The legislature cannot  be  stultified  by  the\nsuspicious improvidence or worse of the Executive.\u201d[671]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1333\">504.  It is, therefore, not  advisable  to  be  alarmist,  as  some  learned\ncounsel for the petitioners were, but at the same  time  possible  abuse  of\npower cannot be wished away, as our recent history  tells  us.  Perhaps  far\nbetter and precise legislative drafting coupled with a healthy debate  is  a\nsolution,  but,  what  is  of  significance  is  the   decision-taking   (as\ndistinguished from  decision-making)  process  of  the  Committee.   It  was\npointed out in Centre for PIL that in  a  situation  such  as  the  present,\nwhere no procedure in the functioning of the  Committee  is  laid  out,  the\nnomination of eminent persons will be through a  majority  decision  of  the\nmembers of the Committee.[672] What this means is that the Chief Justice  of\nIndia would have a subsidiary role in the nomination process  if  he/she  is\nin the minority. What this also means is that an executive  cum  legislative\ninfluence would sneak in in the process of nomination  of  eminent  persons.\nIn other words, from the word \u2018go\u2019 the Chief Justice of India is  sidelined,\ndirectly or indirectly, in the process of appointment of judges of the  High\nCourts and the Supreme Court.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1334\">505.  It is also not possible to accept the contention that the presence  of\neminent persons with a voting right in the NJAC would have no impact on  the\nindependence of the judiciary, but would be beneficial in terms of  bringing\nabout diversity. The same result could very well be achieved,  as  suggested\nby Justice Verma without altering the basic structure of  the  Constitution,\nwithout conferring a veto on the consultants.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1335\">506.  What makes matters worse is  that  in  the  absence  of  a  quorum  or\nunanimity in the nomination of eminent persons,  the  Committee  could  make\nthe nomination without consulting the Chief Justice of India. Therefore,  if\nfor some valid reason, the Chief Justice of India  is  unable  to  attend  a\nmeeting, the Committee could nominate eminent persons (perhaps believing  in\nthe concept of  a  committed  judiciary)  to  the  NJAC  and  influence  its\ndecisions to  accept  a  committed  judiciary  rather  than  an  independent\njudiciary.[673] It is unlikely that this would happen, but if the  political\nexecutive is determined,  at  some  point  of  time,  to  have  a  committed\njudiciary, the nomination of politically active eminent persons to the  NJAC\ndisregarding the view of the Chief Justice of India is a  real  possibility.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1336\">507.  Another objection raised to the  \u2018eminent  person\u2019  category  is  that\nsuch a person might not have  any  knowledge  of  the  requirements  of  the\njudiciary and would not be able to make any effective  contribution  in  the\nselection of a judge. It was submitted that the  eminent  person  must  have\nsome background of law and the judiciary.  In  principle  this  argument  is\nquite attractive, but really has little substance. Several  members  of  the\nConstituent Assembly had no training or  background  in  law  and  yet  they\ncontributed in giving us a glorious Constitution. One of  the  finest  minds\nthat we have today -  Professor  Amartya  Sen  -  has  had  no  training  or\nbackground in law and yet has given us The  Idea  of  Justice  an  important\ncontribution to jurisprudence, the idea  of  justice  in  an  organizational\nsense (niti) and the idea of realized justice (nyaya). Therefore,  it  would\nnot be correct to say that an eminent person in the NJAC (or as  an  outside\nconsultant) must have some connection with the law or justice  delivery.  If\nthe eminent person does have that \u2018qualification\u2019 it might  be  useful,  but\nit certainly need not be absolutely necessary.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1337\">508.  Finally, it was argued that the requirement that  one  eminent  person\nshould be from a specified category as mentioned in  the  first  proviso  to\n<a href=\"/doc/10460741/\" id=\"a_2193\">Article 124A(1)(d)</a> of the  Constitution  is  discriminatory  and  serves  no\npurpose at all. In response, the  learned  Attorney-General  submitted  that\nthe presence of an eminent person, outside the  field  of  law  would  bring\nabout a much needed diversity in the appointment of judges.  The  experience\nin the United Kingdom, as explained by Jack Straw, does  not  seem  to  bear\nout  this  assumption.  In  his  lecture,  he  stated:  \u2018The  assumption  on\ndiversity \u2013 na\u00efve as it turned out \u2013 was that if we changed the process,  we\nwould change the outcome.\u2019 In any event, which  category  should  or  should\nnot be represented in the NJAC through an eminent person  is  essentially  a\nmatter of policy and that policy does not  appear  to  be  perverse  in  any\nmanner, but does require a rethink.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1338\">509.  The real cause for unhappiness is the second proviso to  <a href=\"/doc/112124301/\" id=\"a_2194\">Section  5(2)</a>\nof the NJAC Act which effectively confers a  veto  on  each  member  of  the\nNJAC.  What is objectionable about the veto (a  part  of  the  package  deal\nreferred to by  the  learned  Attorney-General)  is  that  it  can  also  be\nexercised by two eminent persons  whose  participation  in  the  appointment\nprocess was not even imagined by the Constituent  Assembly.  <a href=\"/doc/1995948/\" id=\"a_2195\">Article  124(2)</a>\nof the Constitution (prior to its amendment)  had  only  two  constitutional\nauthorities involved in the appointment process  \u2013  the  President  and  the\nChief Justice of India. The 99th Constitution Amendment Act  has  introduced\na third and a previously non-constitutional \u2018authority\u2019  namely  an  eminent\nperson. Two eminent persons who had no  role  to  play  in  the  appointment\nprocess prior to the 99th Constitution Amendment Act have  suddenly  assumed\nKafkaesque proportions  and  together  they  can  paralyze  the  appointment\nprocess, reducing the President and the Chief Justice of  India  to  ciphers\nfor reasons that might have nothing to do with  the  judicial  potential  or\nfitness and suitability of a person considered for appointment as  a  judge.\nThat  they  might  not  do  so  is  another  matter  altogether  but  in   a\nconstitutional  issue  as  grave  as  the   appointment   of   judges,   all\npossibilities require to be taken into consideration since  it  affects  the\nindependence of the judiciary  and  eventually  the  rights,  including  the\nfundamental rights, of the people. The conferment of a veto  to  any  member\nof the NJAC, eminent person or otherwise,  is  clearly  an  unconstitutional\ncheck on the authority of the President and the Chief Justice of India.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1339\">510.  The sum and substance of this discussion is that in  principle,  there\ncan be no objection to consultation with eminent persons from all  walks  of\nlife in the matter of appointment of judges, but that these eminent  persons\ncan veto a decision that is taken unanimously  or  otherwise  by  the  Chief\nJustice of India (in consultation  with  other  judges  and  possibly  other\neminent persons) is unthinkable \u2013 it confers virtually a  monarchical  power\non the eminent persons in the NJAC, a power without any accountability;  the\ncategories of eminent persons ought not to be limited to  scheduled  castes,\nscheduled tribes, other backward classes, minorities or women but that is  a\nmatter of policy and nothing more can be said  about  this,  except  that  a\nrethink is necessary;  there can be no guidelines for deciding who is or  is\nnot an eminent person for the purposes of nomination to the NJAC,  but  that\nthe choice is left to a  high  powered  committee  is  a  sufficient  check,\nprovided the decision of the committee is unanimous.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1340\">(d) Law Minister\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1341\">511.  The presence of the Law Minister in the NJAC was objected  to  by  the\npetitioners for several reasons.  Principally, it  was  contended  that  the\nUnion of India is the biggest litigant in the courts and  to  have  the  Law\nMinister as a  member  of  the  NJAC  might  prove  detrimental  to  a  fair\nselection, if not counter-productive.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1342\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_594\">512.  It is true that the Union of India is  the  largest  litigant  in  the\ncountry and that was recognized in the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2196\">Second Judges</a> case. It  was  said  by\nJustice Pandian as follows:</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_819\">\u201cNo one can deny that the State in the present  day  has  become  the  major\nlitigant and the  superior  courts  particularly  the  Supreme  Court,  have\nbecome centres for turbulent controversies, some of which with a flavour  of\npolitical repercussions and the  Courts  have  to  face  tempest  and  storm\nbecause their vitality is a  national  imperative.  In  such  circumstances,\ntherefore, can the Government, namely, the major litigant  be  justified  in\nenjoying absolute authority in nominating and  appointing  its  arbitrators.\nThe answer would be in the  negative.  If  such  a  process  is  allowed  to\ncontinue, the independence of judiciary in the long run  will  sink  without\nany trace.\u201d[674]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1343\">513.  Similarly, Justice Kuldip Singh  also  mentioned  that  the  Union  of\nIndia is the single largest litigant  in  the  country.  The  learned  judge\nsaid:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_820\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"112850760\" id=\"span_595\"><a href=\"/doc/112850760/\" id=\"a_2197\">\u201cIn S.P. Gupta</a> case this Court construed the words in  <a href=\"/doc/1995948/\" id=\"a_2198\">Articles  124(2)</a>  and\n<a href=\"/doc/259647/\" id=\"a_2199\">217(1)</a> of the Constitution by taking the clock  back  by  forty  years.  The\nfunctioning of  the  Apex  Judiciary  during  the  last  four  decades,  the\nexpanding  horizon  of,  \u2018judicial   review\u2019,   the   broader   concept   of\n\u2018independence of judiciary\u2019,  practice  and  precedents  in  the  matter  of\nappointment of judges which ripened into conventions and  the  role  of  the\nexecutive being the largest single litigant before the courts, are  some  of\nthe  vital  aspects  which  were  not  adverted  to  by  this  Court   while\ninterpreting the constitutional provisions.\u201d[675]</span>\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1344\">514.  The learned judge expressed the same sentiment far  more  emphatically\nin the following words:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_821\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"112850760\" id=\"span_596\">\u201cThen the question which comes up for consideration  is,  can  there  be  an\nindependent judiciary when the power of appointment of judges vests  in  the\nexecutive? To say yes, would be illogical. The independence of judiciary  is\ninextricably  linked  and  connected  with  the  constitutional  process  of\nappointment of judges of the higher judiciary. \u2018Independence  of  Judiciary\u2019\nis the basic feature of our Constitution  and  if  it  means  what  we  have\ndiscussed above, then the Framers  of  the  Constitution  could  have  never\nintended  to  give  this  power  to  the  executive.  Even   otherwise   the\nGovernments - Central or the State - are parties before the Courts in  large\nnumber of cases.  The  Union  Executive  have  vital  interests  in  various\nimportant matters which come for adjudication before  the  Apex  Court.  The\nexecutive - in one form or the  other  -  is  the  largest  single  litigant\nbefore the courts. In this view  of  the  matter  the  judiciary  being  the\nmediator - between the people  and  the  executive  -  the  Framers  of  the\nConstitution could not have left the final authority to appoint  the  Judges\nof the Supreme Court and of the High Courts in the hands of  the  executive.\nThis Court  in  <a href=\"/doc/112850760/\" id=\"a_2200\">S.P.  Gupta</a>  case  proceeded  on  the  assumption  that  the\nindependence of judiciary is the  basic  feature  of  the  Constitution  but\nfailed  to  appreciate  that  the  interpretation,  it  gave,  was  not   in\nconformity with broader facets  of  the  two  concepts  -  \u2018independence  of\njudiciary\u2019 and \u2018judicial review\u2019 - which are interlinked.\u201d[676]\n\nIn view of this, there can be no doubt that the Government  of  India  is  a\nmajor litigant and for a Cabinet Minister to be participating (and having  a\nveto) in the actual selection of a judge of a  High  Court  or  the  Supreme\nCourt is extremely anomalous.[677]</span>\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1345\">515.  Historically, and I have  quoted  chapter  and  verse  from  virtually\nevery relevant committee in this regard, the executive was  always  intended\nto be kept out of the decision-taking process in the matter  of  appointment\nof judges. What is sought to be achieved by including the  Law  Minister  in\nthe NJAC is to cast a doubt on the wisdom of legal luminaries, Dr.  Ambedkar\nand the Constituent Assembly in keeping the executive out of  the  decision-\ntaking process in the appointment of judges.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1346\">516.  Nevertheless, it is true that inputs from the executive are  important\nin the process of taking a decision whether a person should  or  should  not\nbe appointed as a  judge  of  a  High  Court  or  the  Supreme  Court.   But\nproviding inputs by the executive is quite different  from  the  process  of\ntaking a decision by the executive or the executive being  involved  in  the\nprocess of taking a decision.  While it must be acknowledged  that  the  Law\nMinister is only one of six  in  the  NJAC  but  being  a  Cabinet  Minister\nrepresenting the entire Cabinet and the Government of  India  in  the  NJAC,\nthe Law Minister is undoubtedly a very important  and  politically  powerful\nfigure whose views can, potentially, have a major impact on the  views  that\nother members of the NJAC may hold. Since the Law Minister is, by virtue  of\nthe office held, potentially  capable  of  influencing  the  decision  of  a\nmember of the NJAC, it would be inappropriate for the Law Minister to  be  a\npart of the decision-taking process. The selection process must not only  be\nfair but must appear to be fair.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1347\">517.  It must be  realized  and  appreciated  that  the  tectonic  shift  in\nseveral countries towards constituting a judicial appointment commission  is\ntaking place only to ensure that the executive does not have a role  in  the\nappointment of judges.  The learned  Attorney-General  supported  the  shift\nbut if the trend is to be taken seriously, the  Law  Minister  can  have  no\nplace in any commission or, as in the present case, in the NJAC.  Therefore,\nwhile the 99th Constitution Amendment Act and the NJAC Act  attempt  to  set\nup a body intended to be independent of the executive,  the  NJAC  that  has\nbeen set up has an important member of the political executive as a part  of\nthis body, which is rather anachronistic.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1348\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Neutral\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_597\">518.  It must also be realized that as mentioned in the  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2201\">First  Judges</a>  case\ntwo countries Australia (today having a total of about  200  judges  in  the\nHigh Court and the State Supreme Courts) and New  Zealand  (today  having  a\ntotal of about 20 judges [in the Supreme Court and in the Court of  Appeal])\nwere veering round to having  a  judicial  appointment  commission  for  the\nhigher  judiciary.[678]  We  were  informed  during  the  hearing  of  these\npetitions  that  these  countries  have  not,  even  after   four   decades,\nestablished such commissions, while our country seems to be in a great  rush\nto do so. The issues,  debates,  discussions  and  considerations  in  these\ncountries would be different from ours, but merely because these  and  other\ncountries are looking  towards  a  judicial  appointment  commission  is  no\nreason for India to do so. A reference was also made to  South  Africa  but,\nas everyone knows, diversity issues in that country  are  of  great  concern\npost  apartheid.  It  is,  therefore,  odious  to   compare   the   judicial\nappointment systems in other countries with our country and  to  lift  ideas\nand concepts that might be workable in those countries  without  considering\nwhether they could be adopted or adapted in our country.</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1349\">519.  In Australia, an  article  suggesting  adoption  of  the  UK  Judicial\nAppointments Commission  introduced  by  the  CRA  has  this  to  say  about\njudicial appointments and political patronage (which might  be  possible  in\nthe NJAC as established):\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_822\">\u201cWhile the collective strength and quality of the  Australian  judiciary  is\nnot in doubt, it is the case that  particular  appointments  have  attracted\ncriticism,  either  in  relation  to  the  character  and  ability  of   the\nindividual chosen or their conduct while in office. It is a  notorious  fact\nthat judicial officers have been appointed, including  to  the  High  Court,\nwhose character and intellectual and legal capacities have been doubted  and\nwhose  appointments  have  been  identified  as   instances   of   political\npatronage.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_823\">\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\nWhat is essential is that decisional independence be guaranteed to  judicial\nofficers. The core of judicial independence is  freedom  from  influence  in\nthe central judicial task of adjudicating disputes about legal  rights  that\narise between private parties, between the State and  private  parties,  and\n(in a federation) between components of the State.  The  core  is  protected\nthrough institutional arrangements such  as  tenure,  remuneration  and  the\njurisdictional separation of  powers.  As  we  have  already  noted,  it  is\ninescapable that politics will have  a  role  to  play  in  the  appointment\nprocess. However, if appointments are perceived to be made on the  basis  of\npolitical patronage there is a  threat  to  (at  least  the  appearance  of)\ndecisional independence. It is impossible \u2014 and undesirable \u2014 to remove  the\npolitical entirely from the  appointments  process.  Indeed,  in  our  view,\n\u2018political\u2019   considerations,   in   the   sense   of   responsibility   and\naccountability  for  appointments,  need  to  be  intensified  rather   than\nobscured. What an appointments model should attempt to do is  attenuate  the\ndirect influence of the political branch  on  the  appointment  process  and\nsubject its involvement in the appointment process to  greater  transparency\nand  accountability,  while  preserving  all  the  existing   constitutional\narrangements for ensuring decisional independence. \u201d[679]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1350\">520.  In South Africa, while dealing  with  judicial  appointments,  Justice\nYvonne Mokgoro, former judge of the Constitutional Court had this to say:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_824\">\u201cThus, judicial transformation in South Africa must include a  new  judicial\nappointments procedure which is open and independent of external  influence;\nchanging the demographics of the Bench, in particular with regards  to  race\nand gender as critical aspects of shaping the  form  of  a  judiciary  which\nserves  an  open  and  democratic  society;   appreciating   that   judicial\ncompetence and how judges manage their judicial power and  independence  are\nmajor aspects of enhancing access to justice  and  judicial  accountability.\nEnforcing and embracing the principles and values  of  a  fundamentally  new\nlegal  order  is  also  a  critical  attitudinal  change  that   will   have\nsubstantive implications for the judicial interpretation of the law and  the\ncreation of a new constitutional jurisprudence. These  reforms  are  all  no\ndoubt necessary considerations  for  judicial  transformation.  Courts  must\ntherefore function efficiently so that judges can dispense justice  to  all,\nmost competently. Fundamental to this  principle  is  that  when  appointing\njudges consideration must be given to the need for the judiciary to  reflect\nbroadly the racial and gender composition of South Africa.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_825\">---------\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_826\">In a society such  as  ours,  where  patriarchy  is  so  deeply  entrenched,\naffecting adversely the everyday lives of so many women, including women  in\nthe law, the strategic value of  women\u2019s  participation  on  the  Bench  and\npositions of  power  and  authority  should  not  be  underestimated.  Their\ndevelopment management style, the influence of the unique perspectives  they\nbring to the  adjudicative  task  and  even  the  mere  symbolism  of  their\npresence there could bring enormous returns for the  transformation  process\nitself and respect for women in society at large. The need  for  women  both\nin the judiciary as a  whole  and  in  leadership  positions  in  particular\ncannot be exaggerated. Although, we have come a  long  way,  we  must  agree\nthat we have just scratched the surface. We must step up our  efforts.  Some\nthings must change.\u201d[680]\n\nThe considerations in different countries are, to put it simply,  different.\nWe need to have our own indigenous system suited to our environment and  our\nown requirements.\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1351\">521.  In a Position Paper of 11th  December,  2011  on  the  Appointment  of\nJudges, the Law Society of Botswana emphasized that different legal  systems\nrequire different responses in the appointment of judges. It was said:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_827\">\u201cThroughout the region, the relevance of judicial independence to  the  rule\nof law, democracy and the  protection  and  promotion  of  human  rights  is\nundisputed.  This  acknowledgment  notwithstanding,  judicial   independence\ncontinues to face threats that compromise not  only  individual  judges  but\nmore so the  institutions  vested  with  the  responsibility  of  dispensing\njustice. To that end, judicial independence remains one of the  cornerstones\nof democracy and constitutionalism the world  over,  remaining  the  central\ngoal of most legal systems. It has been noted that the independence  of  the\njudiciary necessitates that  there  should  be  freedom  from  influence  or\ncontrol from the executive and legislative branches of the Government.\nTo achieve this important goal, systems of appointment of judicial  officers\nare seen as crucial to ensuring that the independence of  the  judiciary  is\nachieved. Whilst there is general consensus on the  importance  of  judicial\nindependence, different legal  systems  have  utilized  various  methods  of\nappointing occupants of judicial office. These include;  a)  appointment  by\npolitical  institutions;  b)  appointment  by  the  judiciary   itself;   c)\nappointment by a judicial council (which may include non-judge members)  and\nsometimes d) selection through an electoral system. This  diversity  at  the\nvery least indicates that there exists no  general  consensus  on  the  best\napproach to  guarantee  judicial  independence.  That  notwithstanding,  the\nmechanisms for the appointment  of  judges  remain  crucial  in  maintaining\njudicial independence and public confidence in the judiciary.\u201d[681]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1352\">522.  It was pointed out by the learned Attorney-General that at  all  times\nsince Independence, the Law Minister has been a part of the process  in  the\nappointment of judges.   In  fact  it  is  through  the  Law  Minister  that\nimportant inputs are placed before the Chief Justice of  India  particularly\nwith regard to matters that the Chief Justice of India may not be aware  of,\nsuch as the antecedents and personal traits of the person  being  considered\nfor appointment as a judge.  There is, therefore, no reason to  now  exclude\nthe Law Minister from this process.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1353\">523.  There is a distinction, as mentioned above, between the  Law  Minister\nproviding inputs to the Chief Justice of India and the Law  Minister  having\na say in the final decision regarding the appointment of a judge of  a  High\nCourt or the Supreme Court.  While the former certainly cannot  be  objected\nto and in fact would be necessary, it is the participation in the  decision-\ntaking process that is objectionable.  In  other  words,  the  Law  Minister\nmight be a part of the decision-making process (as the  position  was  prior\nto the 99th Constitution Amendment Act) but ought not to be a  part  of  the\ndecision-taking process. This  distinction  is  quite  crucial.  The  voting\nparticipation of the  Law  Minister  in  the  decision-taking  process  goes\nagainst the grain of the debates in the  Constituent  Assembly  and  clearly\namounts to an alteration of the basic structure of the Constitution.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1354\">524.  It was faintly contended by Mr.  Nariman  that  having  only  the  Law\nMinister of the Government of India as a member of the NJAC and  not  having\nhis/her counterpart from the State Government as a member of  the  NJAC  may\nhave an impact on federalism in our  Constitution.   Apart  from  mentioning\nit, no serious argument was advanced in this  regard,  perhaps  because  the\nprincipal objection is to the representation of the Government of  India  in\nthe NJAC.  In view of the fact that no detailed  submissions  were  made  in\nthis regard, I would not like to express any opinion on this contention.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1355\">525.  The sum and substance of this discussion is that the struggle for  the\nindependence of the judiciary has always been pivoted around  the  exclusion\nof the executive in decision-taking, but the inclusion of the  Law  Minister\nin the NJAC is  counter-productive,  historically  counter-majoritarian  and\ngoes against the grain of various views expressed in  various  committees  \u2013\nmore so since the Law Minister can exercise a veto  in  the  decision-taking\nbody; the presence of the Law Minister in the NJAC  is  totally  unnecessary\nand ill-advised; the presence of the Law Minister in the NJAC casts a  doubt\non the principle of Cabinet responsibility.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1356\">(e) The NJAC and the impact on mandatory consultation\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1357\">526.  <a href=\"/doc/1995948/\" id=\"a_2202\">Article 124(2)</a> of  the  Constitution  as  originally  framed  made  it\nmandatory for the President to consult the Chief Justice  of  India  in  the\nappointment  of  judges.  The  rationale  behind  this  has   already   been\ndiscussed. The 99th Constitution Amendment Act  completely  does  away  with\nthe mandatory  consultation.  The  President  is  not  expected  to  consult\nanybody in the appointment process \u2013 he/she is expected to act only  on  the\nrecommendation of the NJAC. The authority that the  President  had  to  turn\ndown a recommendation made by the collegium, if it  was  not  unanimous,  is\nnow taken away from the President who is obliged to accept a  recommendation\nfrom the NJAC even if it is not unanimous. This is a considerable  whittling\ndown of the  authority  of  the  President  and  a  drastic  change  in  the\nappointment process and in a sense reduces the President (as an  individual)\nto a rubber stamp.[682] Similarly, as mentioned above the Chief  Justice  of\nIndia is reduced to just another number in the NJAC.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1358\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_598\">527.  Mandatory consultation between the President and the Chief Justice  of\nIndia was well thought out by the Drafting  Committee  and  the  Constituent\nAssembly but has now been made farcical by the 99th  Constitution  Amendment\nAct, for the reasons mentioned above. <a href=\"/doc/1995948/\" id=\"a_2203\">Article  124(2)</a>  of  the  Constitution\n(prior to its amendment) placed the  President  and  the  Chief  Justice  of\nIndia on an equal pedestal. It is this that made  the  consultation  between\nthese two constitutional authorities meaningful and made one  constitutional\nauthority act as a check on the other. This was the  \u2018partnership  approach\u2019\nthat the Constituent Assembly had in mind  and  this  was  given  flesh  and\nblood  through,  what  Dr.  Rajeev  Dhavan  referred  to  as  \u2018institutional\nparticipation\u2019 in the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2204\">Second Judges</a>  case.</span>  <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_599\">The  importance  of  the  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2205\">Second\nJudges</a>  case  lies  not  so  much  in  the  shared  responsibility  but  the\n\u2018institutional participation\u2019 of the judiciary in  the  appointment  process\nintegrated with the participation of the President. This is now missing.</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1359\">528.  What is the importance of the mandatory consultation?  There  are  two\ncrucial factors to be carefully considered before a person is  appointed  as\na judge of  the  Supreme  Court  or  a  High  Court.   These  are:  (1)  The\nprofessional skills, judicial potential, suitability and  temperament  of  a\nperson to be a good judge,  and  (2)  The  personal  strengths,  weaknesses,\nhabits and traits of that  person.   As  far  as  the  professional  skills,\njudicial potential, suitability and temperament of a  person  being  a  good\njudge is concerned, the most appropriate  person  to  make  that  assessment\nwould be the Chief Justice of India (in consultation with the other  judges)\nand not somebody from outside the legal fraternity.  On the other  hand,  as\nfar as the personal strengths, weaknesses, habits and  traits  of  a  person\nare concerned, appropriate inputs can come only from  the  executive,  since\nthe Chief Justice of India and other judges may not be aware of them. It  is\nfor this  reason  that  the  Constituent  Assembly  made  it  mandatory  for\nconsultation between the  Chief  Justice  of  India  (as  the  head  of  the\nJudiciary) having vital inputs on the potential of a  person  being  a  good\njudge and the President (as the Head of State acting through the Council  of\nMinisters with the Prime Minister as the head of the  Executive)  being  the\nbest judge to assess the personal traits of a person  being  considered  for\nappointment as a judge.  In other words, the Chief Justice of India  is  the\n\u2018expert\u2019 with regard to potential while the executive is the  \u2018expert\u2019  with\nregard to the antecedents and personal traits. Since  these  two  facets  of\nthe personality of a would-be judge are undoubtedly distinct,  there  cannot\nbe a difference of opinion between the judiciary and the executive  in  this\nregard since they both express an opinion on different facets of a  person\u2019s\nlife.  The Chief Justice of India cannot comment upon the  \u2018expert  opinion\u2019\nof the executive nor can the executive comment upon the \u2018expert opinion\u2019  of\nthe Chief Justice of India.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1360\">529.  It is for the Chief Justice of India as the head of the  judiciary  to\nmanage the justice delivery system and it is for him/her to take  the  final\ncall whether the antecedents or personal traits of a  person  will  or  will\nnot interfere in the discharge of functions as  a  judge  or  will,  in  any\nmanner, impact on the potential of becoming a good judge. As stated by  Jack\nStraw, what is important is that it is necessary to get it right  the  first\ntime and every time. There can be a situation where the personal  traits  of\na person may be such as to disqualify that person from being appointed as  a\njudge and there can  be  a  situation  where  the  personal  traits,  though\nobjected to, would not have any impact whatsoever on the potential  of  that\nperson becoming a good judge. For example, in the  recent  past,  there  has\nbeen considerable debate and discussion, generally but not relating  to  the\njudiciary, with regard to issues of sexual orientation. It is possible  that\nthe executive might have an objection to the sexual orientation of a  person\nbeing considered for appointment as a judge but the Chief Justice  of  India\nmay be of the opinion that that would have no impact on his/her  ability  to\neffectively discharge judicial functions or the potential of that person  to\nbe  a good judge.[683]  In situations such as this, it  is  the  opinion  of\nthe Chief Justice of  India  that  should  have  greater  weight  since,  as\nmentioned earlier, it is for the Chief Justice of India to  efficiently  and\neffectively manage the justice delivery  system  and,  therefore,  the  last\nword should be with the Chief Justice of India, unanimously expressed.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1361\">530.  The 99th Constitution Amendment Act and the NJAC Act not  only  reduce\nthe Chief Justice of India to a number in the  NJAC  but  also  convert  the\nmandatory consultation between the President and the Chief Justice of  India\nto a dumb charade with the  NJAC  acting  as  an  intermediary.  On  earlier\noccasions, Parliament enhanced its power through constitutional  amendments,\nwhich were struck down, inter alia,  in  Indira  Nehru  Gandhi  and  Minerva\nMills.[684]  The  99th   Constitution   Amendment   Act   unconstitutionally\nminimizes the role of the Chief Justice of India  and  the  judiciary  to  a\nvanishing  point  in  the  appointment  of  judges.  It  also   considerably\ndownsizes the role of the President. This effaces  the  basic  structure  of\nthe independence of the judiciary by sufficiently altering  the  process  of\nappointment of judges to the Supreme Court and the High Court, or  at  least\nalters it unconstitutionally thereby striking  at  the  very  basis  of  the\nindependence of the judiciary.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1362\">531.  The entire issue may be looked  at  in  another  light:  Why  did  the\nConstituent Assembly make it mandatory for  the  President  to  consult  the\nChief Justice of India for the appointment of judges of  the  Supreme  Court\nor  the  High  Court  when  equally  important,  if   not   more   important\nconstitutional authorities could  be  appointed  by  the  President  without\nconsulting anybody and in his/her \u2018unfettered discretion\u2019?  The  reason  for\nthe \u2018special\u2019 treatment in the case of  appointments  to  the  judiciary  is\nbecause  the  Constituent  Assembly  appreciated   and   acknowledged   and,\ntherefore,  accepted  the  necessity  of  preserving  and   protecting   the\nindependence  of  the  judiciary,  a  significant  pillar  of  parliamentary\ndemocracy. It also acknowledged that the most appropriate  person  to  guide\nand advice the President in the appointment of judges would  be  none  other\nthan the Chief Justice of India. It was known to  the  Constituent  Assembly\nthat the rights of the people,  including  their  fundamental  rights,  need\nprotection against  arbitrary  executive  power  and  excessive  legislative\naction and unless the judiciary steps in and grants  that  protection,  such\narbitrary power or excessive action can be  misused  and  abused.  This  had\nhappened in pre Independent India and has happened in  our  recent  history.\nThe 99th Constitution Amendment Act and the  NJAC  Act  positively  indicate\n(unconstitutionally) that now the Chief  Justice  of  India  and  the  other\njudges are not necessarily the best persons to advise the President  on  the\nappointment of judges.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1363\">532.  Underscoring the importance of the appointment of  independent  judges\n(to Americans, and this would equally apply to Indians)  it  has  been  said\nthat:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_828\">\u201cJudicial appointments are important because  judges  matter,  not  just  to\nacademics, politicians, and practitioners,  but  to  all  Americans.  Judges\nplay an  increasingly  significant  role  in  everyday  life  decisions.  It\nfollows that the process by which they are  selected  matters.  It  likewise\nfollows that because of the perceived importance of appointing  judges,  the\nappointments process breeds contention.\u201d[685]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1364\">533.  Without an independent judiciary, not only \u2018everyday  life  decisions\u2019\nare affected but a dominant executive can ensure that the  statutory  rights\nwould have no meaning  and the fundamental  rights  of  the  people  of  the\ncountry can  be  easily  trampled  upon.  Highlighting  the  impact  of  the\njudiciary (generally) on the Rule of Law and particularly on the rights  and\ninterests of individuals, Chief Justice Mason of Australia had this to say:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_829\">\u201cAnother factor  relevant  to  the  mode  of  selection  of  judges  is  the\njudiciary\u2019s position as an important branch or  institution  of  government.\nThe judges exercise public power in a way that has substantial  impact  upon\nthe rights and interests of individuals and upon  the  making  of  important\ndecisions by government, government agencies and other organisations.\u201d[686]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1365\">534.  The Constituent Assembly was well aware of the  misuse  and  abuse  of\npower by  the  executive,  having  fought  for  our  freedom  and  knew  and\nunderstood the value of an independent judiciary.  It  is  for  this  reason\nthat the Constituent Assembly gave prime importance to the  independence  of\nthe judiciary and perhaps spent more time debating it than any other topic.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1366\">535.   In  this  regard,  it  is  worth  recalling  the  submission  of  Mr.\nPalkhivala in Kesavananda Bharati while laying the basis for the  \u2018width  of\npower\u2019 test (later adopted in M. Nagaraj) that:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_830\">\u201c\u2026the test of the true width of a power is not how probable it  is  that  it\nmay be exercised but what can possibly be done under it; that the  abuse  or\nmisuse of power is entirely irrelevant; that the question of the  extent  of\nthe power cannot be mixed up with the question  of  its  exercise  and  that\nwhen the real question is as to the width of the power, expectation that  it\nwill never be used is as wholly irrelevant as  an  imminent  danger  of  its\nuse. The court does not decide what is the best and what is  the  worst.  It\nmerely decides what can  possibly  be  done  under  a  power  if  the  words\nconferring it are so construed as to have an unbounded and limitless  width,\nas claimed on behalf of the respondents.\u201d[687]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1367\">536.  Now, consider this -  given the width of  the  power  available  under\nthe 99th Constitution Amendment Act if committed judges  are  appointed  (as\nwas propagated at one point of time and it  can  get  actualized  after  the\n99th Constitution Amendment Act) then no one can  expect  impartial  justice\nas commonly understood from a \u2018committed\u2019 Supreme Court  or  a  High  Court.\nThe Constituent Assembly wished to completely avoid this  and  that  is  why\nconsiderable importance was given to the process of  appointing  judges  and\nthe independence  of  the  judiciary.  \u2018Common  to  all  forms  of  judicial\nfunction is independent, impartial and neutral  adjudication,  though  there\nis a  question  as  to  the  possibility  of  achieving  completely  neutral\nadjudication.\u2019[688] The 99th Constitution Amendment Act  and  the  NJAC  Act\nlead to the clear possibility of a committed judiciary being put  in  place.\nIf this does not violate the  basic  structure  of  the  Constitution,  what\ndoes?\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1368\">537.   The  sum  and  substance  of  this  discussion  is   that   mandatory\nconsultation  between  the  President  and  the  Chief  Justice   of   India\npostulated in  the  Constitution  is  by-passed  \u2013  bringing  about  a  huge\nalteration in the process of appointment of judges;  the  99th  Constitution\nAmendment Act and the NJAC Act have reduced the consultation  process  to  a\nfarce \u2013 a meaningful participatory consultative process  no  longer  exists;\nthe shared responsibility between the President and  the  Chief  Justice  of\nIndia in the appointment of judges is passed on to a body  well  beyond  the\ncontemplation  of  the  Constituent  Assembly;  the  possibility  of  having\ncommitted judges and the consequences of having  a  committed  judiciary,  a\njudiciary that might not be independent is unimaginable.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1369\">(f) The NJAC and the appointment of High Court judges\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1370\">538.  As far as the appointment of a judge of a  High  Court  is  concerned,\nthe 99th  Constitution  Amendment  Act  and  the  NJAC  Act  have  made  two\nextremely significant changes in the process of  appointment.  Firstly,  the\nmandatory requirement for consultation with the Chief Justice  of  the  High\nCourt  has  been  completely  dispensed  with.   <a href=\"/doc/259647/\" id=\"a_2206\">Article   217(1)</a>   of   the\nConstitution as  it  was  originally  enacted  made  it  mandatory  for  the\nPresident to consult the Governor of the State and the Chief Justice of  the\nHigh Court in the appointment of a judge of a High Court. The Chief  Justice\nhas now been left out in the cold. Secondly, the  constitutional  obligation\nand constitutional convention that  has  developed  over  the  last  several\ndecades is that a recommendation for the appointment of a judge of the  High\nCourt originates from the Chief Justice of the  High  Court.  This  has  now\nbeen given a go-bye by the 99th Constitution  Amendment  Act  and  the  NJAC\nAct.  The  entire  initiation  of  the  appointment  process  has  now  been\noverhauled.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1371\">539.  In terms of <a href=\"/doc/101346937/\" id=\"a_2207\">Section 6(2)</a> of the NJAC Act, the recommendation  for  the\nappointment of a judge of a High  Court  cannot  originate  from  the  Chief\nJustice of the High Court but the NJAC  will  seek  a  nomination  for  that\npurpose from the Chief Justice of  the  High  Court.  In  other  words,  the\ninitiative for the appointment of a judge of the High Court is wrested  from\nthe Chief Justice of the High Court by the NJAC.   There  is  a  qualitative\ndifference between the Chief Justice of a High  Court  nominating  a  person\nfor appointment as a judge of a High Court on the  initiative  of  the  NJAC\n(<a href=\"/doc/101346937/\" id=\"a_2208\">Section 6(2)</a> of the NJAC Act)  and  the  Chief  Justice  of  a  High  Court\nrecommending a person for appointment as a judge of a  High  Court  (<a href=\"/doc/259647/\" id=\"a_2209\">Article\n217(1)</a>  of  the  Constitution).  With  such  a  major  departure  from   the\nconstitutional obligation  and  the  constitutional  convention  established\nover the last several decades, the dispensation might  encourage  canvassing\nsupport for a nomination \u2013 a somewhat similar  occurrence  was  looked  down\nupon by the LCI in its 14th Report.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1372\">540.  However, what  is  more  disturbing  and  objectionable  is  that  the\nconsultation process with the Chief  Justice  of  the  High  Court  after  a\nnomination is made by him/her of a person for  appointment  as  a  judge  of\nthat High Court has been done away with. The process of consultation  is  an\nintegrated and participatory process but by virtue of the 99th  Constitution\nAmendment Act and the NJAC Act only a nomination is sought  from  the  Chief\nJustice of a High Court by the NJAC. Thereafter, the Chief  Justice  has  no\nrole to play. This is  clear  from  <a href=\"/doc/56258286/\" id=\"a_2210\">Section  6(7)</a>  of  the  NJAC  Act  which\nmandates the NJAC to elicit in writing the views of  the  Governor  and  the\nChief Minister of the State before recommending a person for appointment  as\na judge of the High Court, but not the views of the Chief  Justice,  who  is\nreduced to a mere nominating officer, whose assigned task is  over  as  soon\nas the nomination is made.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1373\">541.  The combined  effect  of  the  99th  Constitution  Amendment  Act  and\n<a href=\"/doc/20215517/\" id=\"a_2211\">Section 6</a> of the NJAC Act is that the entire control  over  the  appointment\nof a judge of a High Court is taken over by the NJAC  and  the  paradigm  is\ncompletely altered with the Chief Justice of a High Court downgraded from  a\nmandatory  consultant,  and  the  originator   of   a   recommendation   for\nappointment  as  postulated  by  <a href=\"/doc/259647/\" id=\"a_2212\">Article  217(1)</a>  of  the  Constitution   as\nconventionally understood, to someone  who merely  makes  a  nomination  and\nthereafter is not required to  be  consulted  one  way  or  the  other  with\nrespect to the nomination made.  This  drastic  change  in  the  process  of\nappointment of a judge of a High  Court  obviously  has  a  very  long  term\nimpact since it is ultimately from the \u2018cadre\u2019 of  High  Court  judges  that\nmost Supreme Court judges would be appointed, if the  existing  practice  is\nfollowed.  This in turn will obviously  have  a  long  term  impact  on  the\nindependence of the judiciary apart from  completely  altering  the  process\nfor appointment of a judge of a High Court.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1374\">542.  The appointment  of  judges  is  a  very  serious  matter  and  it  is\ndifficult to understate its importance. Referring to  a  view  expressed  by\nShimon Shetreet[689] it is stated by Sarkar Ali Akkas of the  University  of\nRajshahi, Bangladesh that:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_831\">\u201cThe appointment of judges is an important aspect of  judicial  independence\nwhich requires that in administering justice judges should be free from  all\nsorts of direct or indirect interference or  influences.  The  principle  of\nthe independence of the judiciary seeks to ensure the freedom of  judges  to\nadminister justice impartially, without any fear or favour. This freedom  of\njudges has a  close  relationship  with  judicial  appointment  because  the\nappointment system has a direct bearing on the impartiality,  integrity  and\nindependence of judges.\u201d[690]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1375\">543.   Essentially,  the  99th  Constitution  Amendment  Act   replaces   or\nsubstitutes the collegium system of appointment of judges by  the  NJAC.  It\nmust be realized that a judicial appointments commission (by  whatever  name\ncalled) is a worldwide reaction to the executive taking over and  appointing\njudges. No system following the Rule of Law would like to  retain  a  system\nof appointment of judges where the executive plays a major role or  has  the\nlast word on the subject,  hence  the  occasional  clamour  for  a  judicial\nappointments commission. As the Hamlyn lecture of  Jack  Straw  illustrates,\nthe executive desires greater control in the appointment of judges  but  the\njudiciary eventually has the upper hand, as it should \u2013 but not so with  the\nNJAC.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1376\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"868559\" id=\"span_600\">544.  The decision  of  this  Court  in  <a href=\"/doc/868559/\" id=\"a_2213\">Kumar  Padma  Prasad  v.  Union  of\nIndia</a>[691] is an example of how wrong the executive can be in the matter  of\nappointment of judges. In that case, a judicial officer was recommended  for\nappointment as a judge of the Gauhati High Court  at  the  instance  of  the\nChief Minister of Mizoram. The recommendation was agreed  to  by  the  Chief\nJustice of India and the warrant of appointment of  the  recommended  person\nwas issued by the President but it was  subsequently  not  given  effect  to\nsince the person was found not qualified to be appointed as a judge  of  the\nHigh Court. Recently, the Canadian Supreme Court answered a  reference  made\nby the Governor General in Council as a result of which the appointment  and\nswearing in of a judge of the Supreme Court  was  declared  void  ab  initio\nsince he did not possess  the  eligibility  requirement.[692]  Instances  of\nthis nature, fortunately few and far between have shaken  public  confidence\nin a system of appointment of judges where primacy is  with  the  executive,\nhence the desire to shift to an efficacious alternative.  While there  might\nbe a need for a more efficient or better system of  appointment  of  judges,\nthe NJAC is not the stairway to Heaven, particularly in view of the  various\ngaps in its functioning, the NJAC system downgrading the President  and  the\nChief Justice of India and incorporating  a  host  of  other  features  that\nseverely  impact  on  the  appointment  of  judges  and   thereby   on   the\nindependence of the judiciary and thereby on  the  basic  structure  of  the\nConstitution.</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1377\">545.
