It was submitted by the  learned  Attorney-General  that  there  is  a\ndisenchantment with the collegium system of appointment of judges  and  that\nis why it needs to be replaced or substituted and  that  is  precisely  what\nthe 99th Constitution Amendment Act  has  achieved.  The  learned  Attorney-\nGeneral referred to the NJAC as the third  chapter  in  the  appointment  of\njudges - the first  chapter  being  one  in  which  the  executive  had  the\n\u2018ultimate power\u2019 in the appointment process and  the  second  chapter  being\none in which the Executive and the Judiciary have  a  shared  responsibility\nwith the judiciary having institutional participation. This may be  so,  but\nthrough the  99th  Constitution  Amendment  Act  the  NJAC  takes  away  the\nresponsibility not only of the executive but also the shared  responsibility\nof the judiciary and the executive, completely decapitating the  appointment\nsystem given to us by the Constituent Assembly \u2013 a system that  ensures  the\nindependence of the judiciary.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1378\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_601\">546.  Working within the  parameters  suggested  by  the  learned  Attorney-\nGeneral,  namely,  the  presumption  of  constitutionality   of   the   99th\nConstitution Amendment Act, that the basis of the  judgment  in  the  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2214\">Second\nJudges</a> case has been removed, the wisdom of Parliament and the needs of  the\npeople cannot be questioned and that this Court must recognize that  society\nand its requirements have changed with  the  passage  of  time,  it  is  not\npossible to uphold the constitutional  validity  of  the  99th  Constitution\nAmendment Act. The recipe drastically alters the process of  appointment  of\njudges of the  Supreme  Court  and  the  High  Courts  by  taking  away  its\nessential ingredients leading to a constitutional  challenge  that  must  be\naccepted.  547.    Taking  an  overall  and  composite  view  of  the   99th\nConstitution Amendment Act  and  the  NJAC  Act,  rather  than  a  piecemeal\ndiscussion or a dissection of each provision,  there  can  be  little  doubt\nthat <a href=\"/doc/140653939/\" id=\"a_2215\">Article 124A</a> of the  Constitution  (as  amended)  is  unconstitutional.\n<a href=\"/doc/140653939/\" id=\"a_2216\">Article 124A</a> of the  Constitution  having  been  declared  unconstitutional,\nthere is nothing of substance left in <a href=\"/doc/61056230/\" id=\"a_2217\">Article 124B</a> and <a href=\"/doc/16427836/\" id=\"a_2218\">Article 124C</a>  of  the\nConstitution and the other provisions of  the  99th  Constitution  Amendment\nAct, which are not severable and therefore these provisions must be and  are\ndeclared unconstitutional being in  violation  of  and  altering  the  basic\nstructure of the Constitution.</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1379\">548.  The sum and substance of  this  discussion  is  that  the  process  of\ninitiating a recommendation  for  the  appointment  of  a  judge,  generally\naccepted  since  Independence,  has  been  radically  changed,   with   well\nentrenched constitutional conventions being given short  shrift;  the  Chief\nJustice of the High Court has been reduced  to  the  role  of  a  nominating\nofficer, whose opinion is taken only for nomination purposes but  not  taken\nas a consultant in so vital a matter as the  appointment  of  a  judge;  the\nconstitutional importance given to the Chief Justice of  a  High  Court  has\nbeen completely whittled down virtually to a vanishing point.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1380\">Convenor of the NJAC\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1381\">549.  There are some peripheral  issues  that  need  to  be  discussed.  The\ninvolvement of the executive  in  the  NJAC  does  not  stop  with  the  Law\nMinister being one of its\n\nmembers.  The Secretary to the Government of  India  in  the  Department  of\nJustice is the convenor of the NJAC in terms of <a href=\"/doc/130375119/\" id=\"a_2219\">Section  8(3)</a>  of  the  NJAC\nAct.  The  duties  and  responsibilities  of  the  convenor  have  not  been\ndelineated  in  the  <a href=\"/doc/142995521/\" id=\"a_2220\">NJAC  Act</a>  and,  as  mentioned  above,  the  rules  and\nregulations under the Act have not been framed.  It is  therefore  difficult\nto appreciate the functions that the convenor is expected to perform.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1382\">550.  That apart, the Secretary is an officer of the government and  is  not\nanswerable to the NJAC. The Secretary is paid a salary and  allowances  from\nthe government coffers. This is quite unlike officers of the High Courts  or\nthe Supreme Court who are directly  answerable  to  their  respective  Chief\nJustice.  Moreover,  their  salary  and  allowances  are  charged  upon  the\nConsolidated  Fund  of  India.  The  \u2018independence\u2019  of  these  officers  is\nmaintained while that of the Secretary to the Government  of  India  in  the\nDepartment of Justice is not. Moreover, the Secretary holds  a  transferable\nposition and can be changed at the  whims  and  fancies  of  the  executive,\ndepriving the NJAC of continuity and, in a sense, leaving it  high  and  dry\nwhenever it pleases the executive. This is clearly  objectionable.  However,\nto be fair to  the  learned  Attorney-General,  it  was  submitted  that  if\nnecessary a  Registrar  in  the  Supreme  Court  may  be  appointed  as  the\nconvenor, but with respect that is  not  at  all  an  answer  to  the  issue\nraised.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1383\">Transparency\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1384\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"145930141\" id=\"span_602\">551.  In the context of confidentiality requirements, the submission of  the\nlearned Attorney-General was that the  functioning  of  the  NJAC  would  be\ncompletely  transparent.  Justifying  the  need  for  transparency  it   was\nsubmitted that so far the process of appointment of judges in the  collegium\nsystem has been extremely secret in  the  sense  that  no  one  outside  the\ncollegium or the Department of Justice is aware of the recommendations  made\nby the Chief Justice of India for appointment of  a  judge  of  the  Supreme\nCourt or the High Courts. Reference was made to <a href=\"/doc/145930141/\" id=\"a_2221\">Renu v. District  Judge</a>[693]\nto contend that in the matter of appointment in  all  judicial  institutions\n\u2018complete darkness in the light house has to be removed.\u2019[694]</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1385\">552.  In addition to the issue of transparency a submission  was  made  that\nin the matter of appointment of judges, civil society has the right to  know\nwho is being considered for appointment.  <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"223504\" id=\"span_603\">In this regard,  it  was  held  in\n<a href=\"/doc/223504/\" id=\"a_2222\">Indian Express Newspapers v. Union of India</a>[695]  that  the  people  have  a\nright to know.</span> Reliance was placed on Attorney General v.  Times  Newspapers\nLtd.[696]   where  the  right  to  know  was  recognized  as  a  fundamental\nprinciple of the freedom of expression and the freedom of discussion.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1386\">553.  <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"438670\" id=\"span_604\">In <a href=\"/doc/438670/\" id=\"a_2223\">State of U.P. v. Raj Narain</a>[697] the right to know  was  recognized\nas having been derived from the concept of freedom of speech.</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1387\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1351834\" id=\"span_605\">554.  Finally, in <a href=\"/doc/1351834/\" id=\"a_2224\">Reliance Petrochemicals  Ltd.  v.  Proprietors  of  Indian\nExpress Newspapers Bombay (P) Ltd.</a>[698] it was held that the right  to  know\nis a basic right which citizens of a free  country  aspire  in  the  broader\nhorizon of the right to live in this age in our land  under  <a href=\"/doc/1199182/\" id=\"a_2225\">Article  21</a>  of\nour Constitution.</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1388\">555.  The balance between transparency and confidentiality is very  delicate\nand if some sensitive information about a particular person is made  public,\nit can have a far reaching impact on his/her  reputation  and  dignity.  The\n99th Constitution Amendment Act and the NJAC Act have not taken note of  the\nprivacy concerns of  an  individual.   This  is  important  because  it  was\nsubmitted by the learned Attorney-General that the proceedings of  the  NJAC\nwill be completely transparent and any one can have  access  to  information\nthat is available with the NJAC.  This is a rather  sweeping  generalization\nwhich obviously does not take into account the privacy of a person  who  has\nbeen recommended for appointment, particularly as a judge of the High  Court\nor in the first instance as a judge of the Supreme Court. The right to  know\nis not a fundamental right but at best it is an implicit  fundamental  right\nand it is hedged in with the implicit fundamental right to privacy that  all\npeople enjoy. The balance between the  two  implied  fundamental  rights  is\ndifficult to maintain, but the 99th Constitution Amendment Act and the  NJAC\nAct do not even attempt to consider, let alone achieve that balance.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1389\">556.  It is possible to argue that information  voluntarily  supplied  by  a\nperson who is recommended for appointment as a judge might not have a  right\nto privacy, but at the same time,  since  the  information  is  supplied  in\nconfidence, it is possible to argue that it ought not  to  be  disclosed  to\nthird  party  unconcerned  persons.  Also,  if  the  recommendation  is  not\naccepted by the President, does the recommended person have a right to  non-\ndisclosure of the adverse information supplied by the President?  These  are\ndifficult questions to which adequate thought has not been given and  merely\non the basis of a right to know,  the  reputation  of  a  person  cannot  be\nwhitewashed in a dhobi-ghat.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1390\">Doctrine of Revival\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1391\">557.  The learned Solicitor-General submitted that when  a  law  is  amended\nand the amendment is declared unconstitutional, the pre-amendment  law  does\nnot revive. Therefore, even  if  the  99th  Constitution  Amendment  Act  is\ndeclared as altering  the  basic  structure  of  the  Constitution,  <a href=\"/doc/1995948/\" id=\"a_2226\">Article\n124(2)</a> of the Constitution as it existed  prior  to  the  99th  Constitution\nAmendment Act will not automatically revive and the  collegium  system  will\nnot resurface.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1392\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1497372\" id=\"span_606\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1621052\" id=\"span_607\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"135510451\" id=\"span_608\">558.  An interesting discussion is to be found in this regard in  <a href=\"/doc/135510451/\" id=\"a_2227\">West  U.P.\nSugar Mills Assn. v. State of U.P.</a>[699] This Court referred to  <a href=\"/doc/1621052/\" id=\"a_2228\">B.N.  Tewari\nv. Union of India</a>[700] and <a href=\"/doc/1497372/\" id=\"a_2229\">Firm A.T.B.  Mehtab  Majid  &amp;  Co.  v.  State  of\nMadras</a>[701] in  both  of  which  it  was  held  that  if  a  statutory  rule\nsubstitutes a rule and the new rule is struck down or declared invalid,  the\nsubstituted or old rule does not revive since it  ceased  to  exist  on  its\nsubstitution.</span></span></span>  <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"223504\" id=\"span_609\">The same rationale was applied to a  notification  in  <a href=\"/doc/223504/\" id=\"a_2230\">Indian\nExpress Newspapers (Bom) (P) Ltd. v. Union of India</a>.[702]</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1393\">559.  However, it was further held that if a subsequent law is  held  to  be\nvoid such as in a case where the Legislature had no competence to enact  the\nlaw, then the earlier or the old law would revive. It was held:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_832\">\u201cIt would have been a different case where a subsequent law  which  modified\nthe earlier law was held to be void. In such a case, the earlier  law  shall\nbe deemed to have never been modified or repealed and, therefore,  continued\nto be in force. Where it is found that the legislature lacked competence  to\nenact a law, still amends the existing law  and  subsequently  it  is  found\nthat the legislature or the authority was denuded of the power to amend  the\nexisting law, in such a case the old law would revive and continue.\u201d[703]\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1394\">560.  In State of T.N. v. K. Shyam Sunder[704] the two extant views  on  the\nsubject have been noted. In paragraph 56 of the Report, it  is  pointed  out\nthat on the repeal of a statute  it  is  effectively  obliterated  from  the\nstatute books and even if  the  amending  [repealing]  statute  is  declared\nunconstitutional on the ground of lack  of  legislative  competence  in  the\nLegislature, the repealed statute will not revive. This is what was said:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_833\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1017549\" id=\"span_610\"><a href=\"/doc/1017549/\" id=\"a_2231\">\u201cIn State of U.P. v. Hirendra Pal Singh</a> this Court held: (SCC p.  314,  para</span>\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_834\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"108143\" id=\"span_611\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1787277\" id=\"span_612\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"135510451\" id=\"span_613\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1921338\" id=\"span_614\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1621052\" id=\"span_615\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"494297\" id=\"span_616\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1017549\" id=\"span_617\">22)\n\u201c22. It is a settled legal proposition that whenever an Act is repealed,  it\nmust be considered as if it had never existed. The object of  repeal  is  to\nobliterate the Act from the statutory books, except for certain purposes  as\nprovided under <a href=\"/doc/1030013/\" id=\"a_2232\">Section 6</a> of the General Clauses Act, 1897. Repeal is  not  a\nmatter of mere form but is of substance. Therefore, on repeal,  the  earlier\nprovisions stand  obliterated/abrogated/wiped  out  wholly  i.e.  pro  tanto\nrepeal\u2026.\u201d\nThus, undoubtedly, submission made by the learned Senior Counsel  on  behalf\nof the respondents that once the Act stands repealed and  the  amending  Act\nis struck down by the Court being invalid and  ultra  vires/unconstitutional\non  the  ground  of  legislative  incompetence,  the   repealed   Act   will\nautomatically revive is preponderous [preposterous]  and  needs  no  further\nconsideration. This very Bench in <a href=\"/doc/1017549/\" id=\"a_2233\">State  of  U.P.  v.  Hirendra  Pal  Singh</a>,\nafter  placing  reliance  upon  a  large   number   of   earlier   judgments\nparticularly in <a href=\"/doc/494297/\" id=\"a_2234\">Ameer-un-Nissa Begum v. Mahboob Begum</a>, <a href=\"/doc/1621052/\" id=\"a_2235\">B.N. Tewari v.  Union\nof India</a>, India Tobacco Co. Ltd. v. CTO, <a href=\"/doc/1921338/\" id=\"a_2236\">Indian Express Newspapers  (Bombay)\n(P) Ltd. v. Union of India</a>, <a href=\"/doc/135510451/\" id=\"a_2237\">West U.P. Sugar Mills Assn. v.  State  of  U.P.</a>,\n<a href=\"/doc/1787277/\" id=\"a_2238\">Zile Singh v. State of Haryana</a>, <a href=\"/doc/108143/\" id=\"a_2239\">State of Kerala v. Peoples Union</a>  for  Civil\nLiberties  and  Firm  A.T.B.  Mehtab  Majid  and  Co.   reached   the   same\nconclusion.\u201d (Internal citations omitted)\n\nOn the other hand, it is pointed out in paragraph 57 of the Report  that  if\na statute is repealed and the new statute is  declared  unconstitutional  on\nthe ground that  it  violates  the  fundamental  rights  chapter,  then  the\nrepealed statute revives. It was said:</span></span></span></span></span></span></span>\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_835\">\u201cThere is another limb of this legal proposition, that is, where the Act  is\nstruck down by the Court being invalid, on the ground  of  arbitrariness  in\nview of the provisions of <a href=\"/doc/367586/\" id=\"a_2240\">Article 14</a> of the Constitution or being  violative\nof fundamental rights enshrined in Part III of the  Constitution,  such  Act\ncan be  described  as  void  ab  initio  meaning  thereby  unconstitutional,\nstillborn or having no existence at all. In such a situation, the Act  which\nstood repealed, stands revived automatically. (See Behram Khurshid  Pesikaka\nand Mahendra Lal Jaini.)\u201d (Internal citations omitted)\n\nThere does appear to be a doubt (if not a subtle  conflict  of  views)  that\nneeds to be resolved in  the  sense  that  if  a  statute  is  repealed  and\nobliterated from the  statute  books,  under  what  circumstances  does  the\nobliteration vanish, if at all. However, none of these  decisions  make  any\nreference to an amendment of the Constitution, and for  the  present  it  is\nnot necessary to dive into that controversy. This is for the  simple  reason\nthat the issue requires considerable debate, of which we did  not  have  the\nbenefit. Justice Khehar has elaborately dealt with this issue in  his  draft\njudgment but I would like to leave  the  question  open  for  debate  on  an\nappropriate occasion. 561.   But, quite apart from this, if  the  contention\nof the learned Solicitor-General is accepted, then  on  the  facts  of  this\ncase, the result would be calamitous. The simple reason is that if the  99th\nConstitution Amendment Act is struck down as altering  the  basic  structure\nof the Constitution and if <a href=\"/doc/1995948/\" id=\"a_2241\">Article  124(2)</a>  in  its  original  form  is  not\nrevived then <a href=\"/doc/1995948/\" id=\"a_2242\">Article 124(2)</a> of the Constitution minus the words deleted  (by\nthe 99th Constitution Amendment Act) and minus the words struck down  (those\ninserted by the 99th Constitution Amendment Act) would read as follows:</blockquote><pre id=\"pre_11\">\n\n|Article 124(2) as it was  |Article 124(2) after the    |Article 124(2)     |\n|originally                |99th Constitution Amendment |after the 99th     |\n|                          |Act                         |Constitution       |\n|                          |                            |Amendment is struck|\n|                          |                            |down and the       |\n|                          |                            |original Article   |\n|                          |                            |124(2) is not      |\n|                          |                            |revived            |\n|(2) Every Judge of the    |(2) Every Judge of the      |(2) Every Judge of |\n|Supreme Court shall be    |Supreme Court shall be      |the Supreme Court  |\n</pre><blockquote id=\"blockquote_836\">|appointed by the President|appointed by the President  |shall be appointed |\n|by warrant under his hand |by warrant under his hand   |by the President by|\n|and seal after            |and seal on the             |warrant under his  |\n|consultation with such of |recommendation of the       |hand and seal and  |\n|the Judges of the Supreme |National Judicial           |shall hold office  |\n|Court and of the High     |Appointments Commission     |until he attains   |\n|Courts in the States as   |referred to in <a href=\"/doc/140653939/\" id=\"a_2243\">article 124A</a> |the age of         |\n|the President may deem    |and shall hold office until |sixty-five years:  |\n|necessary for the purpose |he attains the age of       |                   |\n|and shall hold office     |sixty-five years:           |                   |\n|until he attains the age  |                            |                   |\n|of sixty-five years:      |                            |                   |\n\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_837\">562.  This would give absolute power to the President to appoint a judge  to\nthe Supreme Court without consulting the Chief Justice of  India  (and  also\nto appoint a judge to a High Court). The result of accepting his  submission\nwould be to create a tyrant, as James  Madison  put  it  in  the  Federalist\nPapers No. 47:\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_838\">\u201cThe accumulation of all powers, legislative, executive, and  judiciary,  in\nthe same hands, whether of one, a few,  or  many,  and  whether  hereditary,\nself-appointed, or elective, may justly be pronounced  the  very  definition\nof tyranny.\u201d\n\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_839\">563.  This was put to the learned Solicitor-General and it was also  put  to\nhim that if his submissions are correct, then it would  be  better  for  the\nUnion of India to have the 99th Constitution Amendment Act  struck  down  so\nthat absolute power resides in the President making him/her an  Imperium  in\nImperio as far as the  appointment  of  judges  is  concerned.  The  learned\nSolicitor-General smiled but obviously had  no  answer  to  give.  It  must,\ntherefore, be held that the constitutional provisions amended  by  the  99th\nConstitution Amendment Act spring back to life on the declaration  that  the\n99th Constitution Amendment Act is unconstitutional.\nConclusions\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_840\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Neg\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_618\">564.  Very briefly, Dr. Ambedkar was of the view that the  President  should\nhave some discretion but not unfettered discretion  in  the  appointment  of\njudges.  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2244\">The Second Judges</a> case acknowledged  that  the  President  has  the\ndiscretion to turn down a  recommendation  made  by  the  Chief  Justice  of\nIndia, but only under certain circumstances. This  was  the  fetter  on  the\ndiscretion of the President. However, the 99th  Constitution  Amendment  Act\nand the NJAC Act have completely taken away the discretion of the  President\nto turn down a recommendation for the appointment of a judge,  reducing  the\nconstitutional significance of the President.</span>\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_841\">565.  Dr. Ambedkar was of the  view  that  the  President  should  have  the\ndiscretion to consult judges of the Supreme Court and  the  High  Courts  in\nrespect of a recommendation for appointment by the Chief Justice  of  India.\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1395\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Neutral\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_619\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Neutral\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_620\">The President was\npresented, by <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2245\">Second Judges</a> case and the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2246\">Third Judges</a> case, with the  result\nof the consultation exercise carried out  by  the  Chief  Justice  of  India\nwhich the Chief Justice of India was mandated to do.  It is over  and  above\nthis that the President was entitled to consult other judges of the  Supreme\nCourt or the High Courts.  However, the 99th Constitution Amendment Act  and\nthe NJAC  Act  have  taken  away  this  freedom  of  consultation  from  the\nPresident,  who  has  no  option  but  to  take  into   account   only   the\nrecommendation of the NJAC and not  travel  beyond  that.  Once  again,  the\nconstitutional significance and importance of the President is  considerably\nreduced, if not taken away.</span></span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1396\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_621\">566.  Dr. Ambedkar was opposed to the concurrence of the  Chief  Justice  of\nIndia (as an individual) in respect of every appointment of  a  judge.   <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2247\">The\nSecond Judges</a> case made it mandatory for the Chief Justice of India to  take\nthe opinion of other judges and also left it open to the  Chief  Justice  of\nIndia to consult persons other than judges in this regard.  The  opinion  of\nthe Chief Justice of India ceased to be an individual opinion  (as  per  the\n\u2018desire\u2019 of Dr. Ambedkar) but became a collective or institutional  opinion,\nthere being a great deal of difference between the two.  However,  the  99th\nConstitution Amendment Act and the NJAC Act have  considerably  limited  and\ncurtailed the authority of the Chief Justice of India (both individually  as\nwell as institutionally) and the Chief Justice of  India  is  now  precluded\nfrom taking the opinion of other judges or of any person outside  the  NJAC.\nThe Chief Justice of India has been reduced to an individual figure from  an\ninstitutional head.</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1397\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_622\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_623\">567.  Dr. Ambedkar was not prepared to  accept  the  opinion  of  the  Chief\nJustice of India (as an individual) as the final word in the appointment  of\njudges.  This is because the Chief Justice of India has frailties  like  all\nof us.  The apprehension of Dr. Ambedkar was allayed by  the  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2248\">Second  Judges</a>\ncase and the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2249\">Third Judges</a>  case  which  made  it  mandatory  for  the  Chief\nJustice of India to express a  collective  opinion  and  not  an  individual\nopinion.</span></span>   <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_624\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_625\">The  collective  and  unanimous  opinion  (duly   reiterated   if\nnecessary) would bind the  President  being  the  collective  and  unanimous\nopinion of persons who were ex hypothesi  \u2018well  qualified  to  give  proper\nadvice in matters of this sort.\u2019  However, the 99th  Constitution  Amendment\nAct and the NJAC Act reversed the process well thought  out  in  the  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2250\">Second\nJudges</a>  case  and  the  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2251\">Third  Judges</a>  case  and   have   taken   away   the\nconstitutional authority of the Chief Justice of India and placed  it  on  a\nplatter for the NJAC to exploit.</span></span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1398\">568.  Given our constitutional history,  the  established  conventions,  the\nviews of various committees over the last seventy years  and  the  views  of\nscores of legal luminaries beginning with Mr. Motilal Setalvad,  the  throes\nthrough which the judiciary has gone through over several  decades  and  the\nprovisions of our Constitution, I hold that the <a href=\"/doc/140653939/\" id=\"a_2252\">Article 124A</a>  as  introduced\nin the Constitution by the <a href=\"/doc/195108305/\" id=\"a_2253\">Constitution (Ninety-ninth Amendment)  Act,  2014</a>\nimpinges on  the  independence  of  the  judiciary  and  in  the  matter  of\nappointment of judges (which is a foundational  and  integral  part  of  the\nindependence of the  judiciary)  and  alters  the  basic  structure  of  the\nConstitution.  It  is  accordingly  declared  unconstitutional.  The   other\nprovisions of the <a href=\"/doc/195108305/\" id=\"a_2254\">Constitution (Ninety-ninth  Amendment)  Act,  2014</a>  cannot\nstand by  themselves  and  are  therefore  also  declared  unconstitutional.\n<a href=\"/doc/142995521/\" id=\"a_2255\">Similarly, the National Judicial Appointments Commission Act,  2014</a>  confers\narbitrary and unchartered powers on various authorities  under  the  statute\nand  it  violates  <a href=\"/doc/367586/\" id=\"a_2256\">Article  14</a>  of  the   Constitution   and   is   declared\nunconstitutional.  Even  otherwise,  the  <a href=\"/doc/142995521/\" id=\"a_2257\">National   Judicial   Appointments\nCommission Act, 2014</a> cannot stand alone in the absence of  the  <a href=\"/doc/195108305/\" id=\"a_2258\">Constitution\n(Ninety-ninth Amendment) Act, 2014</a>.\n</p><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Neg\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_626\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Neg\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_627\"><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1399\">569.  The  result  of  this  declaration  is  that  the  \u2018collegium  system\u2019\npostulated by the  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2259\">Second  Judges</a>  case  and  the  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2260\">Third  Judges</a>  case  gets\nrevived. However, the procedure for appointment of judges as  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2261\">laid  down  in</a>\nthese decisions read with the (Revised) Memorandum of  Procedure  definitely\nneeds fine tuning. We  had  requested  learned  counsel,  on  the  close  of\nsubmissions, to give  suggestions  on  the  basis  that  the  petitions  are\ndismissed and on the basis that the petitions  are  allowed.  Unfortunately,\nwe received  no  response,  or  at  best  a  lukewarm  response.  Under  the\ncircumstances, in my opinion, we need to have  a  \u2018consequence  hearing\u2019  to\nassist us in the matter for steps to be taken in the  future  to  streamline\nthe process and  procedure  of  appointment  of  judges,  to  make  it  more\nresponsive to the needs of the people, to make it more  transparent  and  in\ntune with societal needs, and more particularly, to  avoid  a  fifth  judges\ncase!  I  would,  therefore,  allow  the  petitions  but  list  them  for  a\n\u2018consequence hearing\u2019 on an appropriate date.</p></span></span><pre id=\"pre_12\">\n\n\n\n\n.....\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026J\nNew Delhi;                                      (Madan B. Lokur)\n16th October, 2015\n\n\n                        IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA\n\n                        CIVIL  ORIGINAL  JURISDICTION\n\n                    WRIT PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 13 OF 2015\nSupreme Court Advocates-on-Record-\nAssociation and another                      \u2026     Petitioner(s)\n\n                                   Versus\nUnion of India                               \u2026     Respondent(s)\n                                    WITH\n                    WRIT PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 14 OF 2015\n                                    WITH\n                    WRIT PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 18 OF 2015\n                                    WITH\n                    WRIT PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 23 OF 2015\n                                    WITH\n                    WRIT PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 24 OF 2015\n                                    WITH\n                    WRIT PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 70 OF 2015\n                                    WITH\n                    WRIT PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 83 OF 2015\n                                    WITH\n                  TRANSFER PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 391 OF 2015\n                                    WITH\n                    WRIT PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 108 OF 2015\n                                    WITH\n                    WRIT PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 124 OF 2015\n                                     AND\n                    WRIT PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 209 OF 2015\n                                  O R D E R\nKURIAN, J.:\n\n</pre><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1400\">I wholly agree with the view taken by my esteemed brother,  Chelameswar,  J.\nthat there is no situation warranting recusal  of  Justice  Khehar  in  this\ncase. Now, that we have to pass a detailed and reasoned order as  to  why  a\nJudge need not recuse from a case, I feel it appropriate also to  deal  with\nthe other side of the coin, whether a Judge should  state  reasons  for  his\nrecusal in a particular case.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1401\">One of the reasons for recusal of  a  Judge  is  that  litigants/the  public\nmight entertain a reasonable apprehension about his impartiality.   As  Lord\nChief Justice Hewart said:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_842\">\u201cIt is not merely of some importance but is of fundamental  importance  that\njustice should not only be done, but should manifestly  and  undoubtedly  be\nseen to be done.\u201d[705]\n\nAnd  therefore,  in  order  to  uphold  the  credibility  of  the  integrity\ninstitution, the Judge recuses from hearing the case.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_843\">A Judge of the Supreme Court or  the  High  Court,  while  assuming  Office,\ntakes an oath as prescribed  under  Schedule  III  to  the  Constitution  of\nIndia, that:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_844\">\u201c\u2026 I will bear true faith and allegiance to the Constitution of India as  by\nlaw established, that I will uphold the sovereignty and integrity of  India,\nthat I will duly and faithfully and to the best  of  my  ability,  knowledge\nand judgment perform the  duties  of  my  office  without  fear  or  favour,\naffection or ill-will and that  I  will  uphold  the  Constitution  and  the\nlaws.\u201d\n\n\nCalled upon to discharge the duties of the Office without  fear  or  favour,\naffection or ill-will, it is only desirable, if not proper,  that  a  Judge,\nfor any  unavoidable  reason  like  some  pecuniary  interest,  affinity  or\nadversity with the parties in the case, direct or indirect interest  in  the\noutcome of the litigation, family directly involved  in  litigation  on  the\nsame issue elsewhere, the Judge being  aware  that  he  or  someone  in  his\nimmediate family has an interest, financial or otherwise that could  have  a\nsubstantial bearing as a consequence of  the  decision  in  the  litigation,\netc., to recuse himself from the adjudication of  a  particular  matter.  No\ndoubt, these examples are not exhaustive.\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_845\">Guidelines on the ethical conduct of  the  Judges  were  formulated  in  the\nChief Justices\u2019 Conference held in 1999 known as  \u201cRestatement  of  Judicial\nValues of Judicial Life\u201d. Those principles, as a matter of fact, formed  the\nbasis of \u201cThe Bangalore Principles of Judicial Conduct, 2002\u201d formulated  at\nthe Round Table Meeting of Chief Justices held  at  the  Peace  Palace,  The\nHague. It is seen from the Preamble that the Drafting  Committee  had  taken\ninto consideration thirty two such statements all over the  world  including\nthat of India. On Value 2 \u201cImpartiality\u201d, it is resolved as follows:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_846\">\u201cPrinciple:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_847\">Impartiality is essential to the proper discharge of  the  judicial  office.\nIt applies not only to the decision itself but also to the process by  which\nthe decision is made.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_848\">Application:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_849\">2.1   A judge shall perform his or her judicial duties without favour,  bias\nor prejudice.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_850\">2.2   A judge shall ensure that his or her  conduct,  both  in  and  out  of\ncourt, maintains and enhances  the  confidence  of  the  public,  the  legal\nprofession and litigants in  the  impartiality  of  the  judge  and  of  the\njudiciary.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_851\">2.3   A judge shall, so far as is reasonable, so conduct himself or  herself\nas to minimise the occasions on which it will be necessary for the judge  to\nbe disqualified from hearing or deciding cases.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_852\">2.4   A judge shall not knowingly, while a proceeding is  before,  or  could\ncome before, the judge, make any comment that might reasonably  be  expected\nto affect the outcome of such proceeding or impair the manifest fairness  of\nthe process. Nor shall the judge make any comment  in  public  or  otherwise\nthat might affect the fair trial of any person or issue.\n2.5   A judge shall disqualify himself or herself from participating in  any\nproceedings in which the judge is unable to decide  the  matter  impartially\nor in which it may appear to a reasonable observer that the judge is  unable\nto decide the matter impartially. Such  proceedings  include,  but  are  not\nlimited to, instances where\n2.5.1 the judge has actual bias or prejudice concerning a party or  personal\nknowledge of disputed evidentiary facts concerning the proceedings;\n2.5.2 the judge previously served as a lawyer or was a material  witness  in\nthe matter in controversy; or\n2.5.3 the judge, or  a  member  of  the  judge&#x27;s  family,  has  an  economic\ninterest in the outcome of the matter in controversy:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_853\">Provided that disqualification of a judge shall not be required if no  other\ntribunal can be constituted to deal with the  case  or,  because  of  urgent\ncircumstances, failure to  act  could  lead  to  a  serious  miscarriage  of\njustice.\u201d\n\n\n\n\nThe simple question is, whether the adjudication  by  the  Judge  concerned,\nwould cause a  reasonable  doubt  in  the  mind  of  a  reasonably  informed\nlitigant  and  fair-minded  public  as  to  his   impartiality.   Being   an\ninstitution whose hallmark is transparency,  it  is  only  proper  that  the\nJudge discharging high and noble duties,   at  least  broadly  indicate  the\nreasons for recusing from the case  so  that  the  litigants  or  the  well-\nmeaning public may not entertain any misunderstanding that the  recusal  was\nfor altogether irrelevant reasons like the cases being very  old,  involving\ndetailed consideration, decision on several questions of  law,  a  situation\nwhere the Judge is not  happy  with  the  roster,  a  Judge  getting  unduly\nsensitive about the public perception of his image,  Judge  wanting  not  to\ncause displeasure to  anybody,  Judge  always  wanting  not  to  decide  any\nsensitive or  controversial  issues,  etc.  Once  reasons  for  recusal  are\nindicated, there will not be any room for attributing  any  motive  for  the\nrecusal. To put it differently, it is part of his duty to be accountable  to\nthe Constitution by upholding it without fear or favour, affection  or  ill-\nwill.  Therefore, I am of the view that it is the  constitutional  duty,  as\nreflected in one\u2019s oath, to be transparent and  accountable,  and  hence,  a\nJudge is required to indicate reasons for  his  recusal  from  a  particular\ncase. This would help to curb the tendency for forum shopping.\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_854\">In Public Utilities Commission of District of Columbia et al. v.  Pollak  et\nal.[706], the Supreme Court of United States dealt with a  question  whether\nin  the  District  of  Columbia,  the  Constitution  of  the  United  States\nprecludes a street railway  company  from  receiving  and  amplifying  radio\nprogrammes  through  loudspeakers  in  its   passenger   vehicles.   Justice\nFrankfurter was always averse to the practice and he was of  the  view  that\nit is not proper. His personal  philosophy  and  his  stand  on  the  course\napparently, were known to the people. Even otherwise, he  was  convinced  of\nhis strong position on this issue. Therefore, stating so,  he  recused  from\nparticipating in the case.  To quote his words,\n\n\u201cThe judicial process demands that a judge  move  within  the  framework  of\nrelevant legal rules and the covenanted modes of  thought  for  ascertaining\nthem.  He must think dispassionately and submerge private feeling  on  every\naspect of a case.  There is a good deal of shallow talk  that  the  judicial\nrobe does not change the man within it.  It does.  The fact is that  on  the\nwhole judges do lay  aside  private  views  in  discharging  their  judicial\nfunctions.  This is achieved through training,  professional  habits,  self-\ndiscipline and that  fortunate  alchemy  by  which  men  are  loyal  to  the\nobligation with which they are entrusted.  But it is also true  that  reason\ncannot control the  subconscious  influence  of  feelings  of  which  it  is\nunaware.  When there is ground for believing that such unconscious  feelings\nmay operate in the ultimate judgment, or may not  unfairly  lead  others  to\nbelieve they are operating, judges recuse themselves.  They do  not  sit  in\njudgment.   They  do  this  for  a  variety   of   reasons.    The   guiding\nconsideration is  that  the  administration  of  justice  should  reasonably\nappear to be disinterested as well as be so in fact.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_855\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1282229\" id=\"span_628\">This case for me presents such a situation.  My  feelings  are  so  strongly\nengaged as a victim of the practice in controversy that  I  had  better  not\nparticipate in judicial judgment upon it. I am explicit  as  to  the  reason\nfor my non-participation in this case because I have for some time  been  of\nthe view that it is desirable to state  why  one  takes  himself  out  of  a\ncase.\u201d\n\n\nAccording to Justice Mathew in <a href=\"/doc/1282229/\" id=\"a_2262\">S. Parthasarathi v. State  of  A.P.</a>[707],  in\ncase, the right-minded  persons  entertain  a  feeling  that  there  is  any\nlikelihood of bias  on  the  part  of  the  Judge,  he  must  recuse.   Mere\npossibility  of  such  a  feeling  is  not   enough.    There   must   exist\ncircumstances  where  a  reasonable  and  fair-minded  man  would  think  it\nprobably or likely that the Judge would be prejudiced  against  a  litigant.\nTo quote:</span>\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_856\">\u201cThe tests of  \u201creal  likelihood\u201d  and  \u201creasonable  suspicion\u201d  are  really\ninconsistent with each other.  We think that the  reviewing  authority  must\nmake a determination on the basis of the whole evidence before  it,  whether\na reasonable man would  in  the  circumstances  infer  that  there  is  real\nlikelihood of bias.  The Court must  look  at  the  impression  which  other\npeople have.  This follows from the principle that Justice must not only  be\ndone but seen to be done.  If right minded persons would  think  that  there\nis real likelihood of bias on the part of an inquiring officer, he must  not\nconduct the inquiry; nevertheless, there must be a real likelihood of  bias.\n Surmise or conjecture would not be enough.  <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"608874\" id=\"span_629\">There must exist  circumstances\nfrom which reasonable men  would  think  it  probable  or  likely  that  the\ninquiring officer will be prejudiced  against  the  delinquent.   The  Court\nwill not inquire whether he was really  prejudiced.   If  a  reasonable  man\nwould think on the basis of the existing circumstances that he is likely  to\nbe prejudiced, that is sufficient  to  quash  the  decision  [see  per  Lord\nDenning, H.R. in (Metropolitan Properties Co. (F.G.C.) <a href=\"/doc/608874/\" id=\"a_2263\">Ltd.  v.  Lannon  and\nOthers</a>, etc. [(1968) 3  WLR  694  at  707]).  We  should  not,  however,  be\nunderstood to deny that the Court might with  greater  propriety  apply  the\n\u201creasonable suspicion\u201d test in  criminal  or  in  proceedings  analogous  to\ncriminal proceedings.\u201d\n\nThere may be situations where the mischievous litigants wanting to  avoid  a\nJudge may be because he is known to them to be very strong and  thus  making\nan attempt for forum shopping by raising baseless  submissions  on  conflict\nof interest. In the Constitutional Court of South Africa  in  The  President\nof the Republic of South Africa etc. v. South African Rugby  Football  Union\netc.[708], has made two very relevant observations in this regard:\n\u201cAlthough it is important that justice must  be  seen  to  be  done,  it  is\nequally important that judicial officers discharge their duty to sit and  do\nnot,  by  acceding  too  readily  to  suggestions  of  appearance  of  bias,\nencourage parties to believe that  by  seeking  the  disqualification  of  a\njudge, they will have their case tried by someone thought to be more  likely\nto decide the case in their favour.\u201d\n\u201cIt needs to be said loudly and clearly that the ground of  disqualification\nis a reasonable apprehension that the judicial officer will not  decide  the\ncase impartially or without prejudice, rather than that he will  decide  the\ncase adversely to one party.\u201d\n\n\nUltimately, the question is whether a fair-minded  and  reasonably  informed\nperson, on  correct  facts,  would  reasonably  entertain  a  doubt  on  the\nimpartiality of the Judge. The reasonableness of the  apprehension  must  be\nassessed in the light of the oath of Office he  has  taken  as  a  Judge  to\nadminister justice without fear or favour, affection  or  ill-will  and  his\nability to carry out the oath by  reason  of  his  training  and  experience\nwhereby he is in a position to disabuse his mind of any irrelevant  personal\nbelief or pre-disposition or  unwarranted  apprehensions  of  his  image  in\npublic or difficulty in deciding a  controversial  issue  particularly  when\nthe same is highly sensitive.</span>\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_857\">These issues have been succinctly discussed by the Constitutional  Court  in\nThe President of the Republic of South Africa  (supra),  on  an  application\nfor recusal of four  of  the  Judges  in  the  Constitutional  Court.  After\nelaborately considering the factual matrix as well as  the  legal  position,\nthe Court held as follows:-\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_858\">\u201cWhile litigants have the  right  to  apply  for  the  recusal  of  judicial\nofficers where there is a reasonable apprehension that they will not  decide\na case impartially, this does not give them the right  to  object  to  their\ncases being heard  by  particular  judicial  officers  simply  because  they\nbelieve that such persons will be less likely to decide the  case  in  their\nfavour, than would other judicial officers drawn from  a  different  segment\nof society.  The nature of the judicial function  involves  the  performance\nof  difficult  and  at  times  unpleasant  tasks.   Judicial  officers   are\nnonetheless required to \u201cadminister justice to  all  persons  alike  without\nfear, favour or prejudice, in  accordance  with  the  Constitution  and  the\nlaw\u201d.  To this end they must resist all manner of  pressure,  regardless  of\nwhere it comes  from.   This  is  the  constitutional  duty  common  to  all\njudicial officers.  If they  deviate,  the  independence  of  the  judiciary\nwould be undermined, and in turn, the Constitution itself.\u201d\n                                                         (Emphasis supplied)\n\n\nThe above principles are universal in application.  Impartiality of a  Judge\nis the  sine  qua  non  for  the  integrity  institution.   Transparency  in\nprocedure is one of the major factors  constituting  the  integrity  of  the\noffice of a Judge in conducting  his  duties  and  the  functioning  of  the\ncourt.  The litigants would always like to know though they may not  have  a\nprescribed right to know, as to why a Judge has  recused  from  hearing  the\ncase or despite request, has not recused to  hear  his  case.   Reasons  are\nrequired to be indicated broadly. Of course, in case the disclosure  of  the\nreasons is likely to affect prejudicially any case or cause or  interest  of\nsomeone else, the Judge is  free  to  state  that  on  account  of  personal\nreasons which the Judge does not want to disclose, he has decided to  recuse\nhimself from hearing the case.\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_859\">                                                              .....\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026.J.\n                                                             (KURIAN JOSEPH)\nNew Delhi;\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_860\">October 16, 2015.\n</blockquote>\n\n\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_861\">                        IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA\n\n                        CIVIL  ORIGINAL  JURISDICTION\n\n\n                    WRIT PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 13 OF 2015\n\nSupreme Court Advocates-on-Record-\n</blockquote><pre id=\"pre_13\">Association and another                            \u2026     Petitioner(s)\n\n                                   Versus\nUnion of India                                     \u2026     Respondent(s)\n\n                                    WITH\n                    WRIT PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 23 OF 2015\n                                    WITH\n                    WRIT PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 70 OF 2015\n                                    WITH\n                    WRIT PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 83 OF 2015\n                                    WITH\n                  TRANSFER PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 391 OF 2015\n                                    WITH\n                    WRIT PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 108 OF 2015\n                                    WITH\n                    WRIT PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 124 OF 2015\n                                    WITH\n                    WRIT PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 14 OF 2015\n                                    WITH\n                    WRIT PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 18 OF 2015\n                                    WITH\n                    WRIT PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 24 OF 2015\n                                    WITH\n                   WRIT PETITION (CIVIL) NO.  209 OF 2015\n                                    WITH\n                    WRIT PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 309 OF 2015\n                                    WITH\n                    WRIT PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 310 OF 2015\n                                    WITH\n                    WRIT PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 323 OF 2015\n                                    WITH\n                  TRANSFER PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 971 OF 2015\n                                     AND\n                    WRIT PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 341 OF 2015\n\n                               J U D G M E N T\n\nKURIAN, J.:\n\n</pre><blockquote id=\"blockquote_862\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Neg\" data-docid=\"257876\" id=\"span_630\">Entia  Non  Sunt  Multiplicanda  Sine  Necessitate  (Things  should  not  be\nmultiplied without necessity). This is the first thought which  came  to  my\nmind after reading the judgments  authored  by  my  noble  brothers  Khehar,\nChelameswar, Lokur and Goel, JJ., exhaustively  dealing  with  the  subject.\nThe entire gamut of the issue has been dealt with from all  possible  angles\nafter referring extensively  to  the  precedents,  academic  discourses  and\njudgments of various other countries. Though  I  cannot,  in  all  humility,\nclaim to match the level of such masterpieces, it is a fact that I  too  had\ndrafted my judgment. However, in view of the principle enunciated  above  on\nunnecessary multiplication, I decided to undo major portion of what  I  have\ndone, also for the reason that the judgment of  this  Bench  should  not  be\naccused of Bharati fate (<a href=\"/doc/257876/\" id=\"a_2264\">His Holiness Kesavananda Bharati Sripadagalvaru  v.\nState of  Kerala  and  another</a>[709]  has  always  been  criticized  on  that\naccount).</span>\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_863\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"167993993\" id=\"span_631\">Leaving all legal jargons and using a language of the common man,  the  core\nissue before us is the validity of  the  Constitution                   99th\namendment. It is to be tested on the touchstone of the theory of  the  basic\nstructure. The amendment has introduced  a  new  constitutional  scheme  for\nappointment of Judges to the High Courts and the Supreme Court.  During  the\nfirst phase of the working of the Constitution,  the  Executive  claimed  an\nupper hand in the appointment and the Chief Justice of India  or  the  Chief\nJustices of the High Courts concerned  were  only  to  be  \u2018consulted\u2019,  the\nexpression often understood in  its  literal  sense.  In  other  words,  the\ndecision was taken by the Executive with  the  participation  of  the  Chief\nJustice. This process fell for scrutiny in one of the  celebrated  decisions\nof this Court in <a href=\"/doc/167993993/\" id=\"a_2265\">Samsher Singh v. State of Punjab and another</a>[710].</span>\n<span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"167993993\" id=\"span_632\">In <a href=\"/doc/167993993/\" id=\"a_2266\">Samsher Singh</a> case  (supra),  a  seven-Judge  Bench  of  this  Court,  in\nunmistakable terms, held at paragraph 149 as follows:\n\u201c149. \u2026 The independence of the Judiciary, which is a cardinal principle  of\nthe Constitution and has  been  relied  on  to  justify  the  deviation,  is\nguarded by the relevant article making consultation with the  Chief  Justice\nof India  obligatory.  In  all  conceivable  cases  consultation  with  that\nhighest dignitary of Indian justice will  and  should  be  accepted  by  the\nGovernment of India and the Court will have an  opportunity  to  examine  if\nany other extraneous circumstances have entered  into  the  verdict  of  the\nMinister, if he departs from the counsel  given  by  the  Chief  Justice  of\nIndia. In practice the last word in such a sensitive subject must belong  to\nthe Chief Justice of India, the rejection of  his  advice  being  ordinarily\nregarded as prompted by oblique considerations vitiating the order.</span> <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_633\">In  this\nview it is immaterial whether the President or the  Prime  Minister  or  the\nMinister for Justice formally decides the issue.\u201d\n                                                         (Emphasis supplied)\n\nThis principle, settled by a Bench of seven Judges, should have  been  taken\nas binding by the Bench dealing with the  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2267\">First  Judges</a>  Case  which  had  a\ncoram only of  seven.  Unfortunately,  it  held  otherwise,  though  with  a\nmajority of four against three.</span> <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"167993993\" id=\"span_634\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_635\">Strangely, the presiding Judge in the  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2268\">First\nJudges</a> case and author of the majority view,  was  a  member  who  concurred\nwith the majority in <a href=\"/doc/167993993/\" id=\"a_2269\">Samsher  Singh</a>                       case  (supra)  and\nyet there was not even a reference to that judgment in  the  lead  judgment!</span></span>\n<span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Neg\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_636\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Neg\" data-docid=\"167993993\" id=\"span_637\">Had there been a proper advertence to <a href=\"/doc/167993993/\" id=\"a_2270\">Samsher Singh</a> case  (supra),  probably\nthere would not have been any need for the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2271\">Second Judges</a> Case.</span></span>\n<span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_638\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_639\">It  appears,  the  restlessness  on  the  incorrect  interpretation  of  the\nconstitutional  structure  and  position  of  judiciary  in  the  matter  of\nappointments with the super voice of  the  Executive,  as  endorsed  in  the\n<a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2272\">First Judges</a> Case, called for  a  serious  revisit  leading  to  the  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2273\">Second\nJudges</a> Case. Paragraph 85 of the Judgment gives adequate  reference  to  the\nbackground. To quote:</span></span>\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_864\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_640\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_641\">\u201c85.  Regrettably,  there  are  some  intractable  problems  concerned  with\njudicial administration starting from the  initial  stage  of  selection  of\ncandidates to man the Supreme Court and  the  High  Courts  leading  to  the\npresent malaise. Therefore, it has become inevitable  that  effective  steps\nhave to be taken to improve or retrieve the situation. After taking note  of\nthese problems and realising the devastating  consequences  that  may  flow,\none cannot be a silent spectator or  an  old  inveterate  optimist,  looking\nupon the other constitutional functionaries, particularly the executive,  in\nthe fond  hope  of  getting  invigorative  solutions  to  make  the  justice\ndelivery system more effective and resilient to meet the contemporary  needs\nof  the  society,  which  hopes,  as  experience  shows,  have  never   been\nsuccessful. Therefore, faced with such a piquant situation,  it  has  become\nimperative for us to solve these problems within the  constitutional  fabric\nby interpreting the various provisions of the Constitution relating  to  the\nfunctioning of the judiciary in the light of the letter and  spirit  of  the\nConstitution.\u201d\n                                                         (Emphasis supplied)\n\nThe nine-Judges Bench in the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2274\">Second Judges</a> Case overruled the  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2275\">First  Judges</a>\nCase, after a threadbare analysis of the relevant provisions \u2018in  the  light\nof the letter and spirit of the Constitution\u2019, holding that  appointment  of\nJudges to the High Courts and the Supreme Court forms an  integral  part  of\nthe independence of judiciary, that independence of  judiciary  is  part  of\nthe basic structure  of  the  Constitution  of  India,  and  therefore,  the\nExecutive cannot interfere with the primacy of the judiciary in  the  matter\nof appointments.</span></span>  <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_642\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_643\"><a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2276\">Third  Judges</a>  Case,  in  1998,  is  only  an  explanatory\nextension of the working of the principles in  the  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2277\">Second  Judges</a>  Case  by\ninstitutionalizing the procedure of appointment, introducing the  Collegium.</span></span>\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_865\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_644\">Thus, the structural supremacy of  the  judiciary  in  the  constitutionally\nallotted sphere was restored by the Second and Third Judges Cases.\nApparently, on account of certain allegedly undeserving appointments,  which\nin fact affected the image of the judiciary, the politico Executive  started\na new campaign demanding reconsideration of the  procedure  of  appointment.\nIt was clamoured that the system of Judges appointing Judges is not  in  the\nspirit of  the  Constitution,  and  hence,  the  whole  process  required  a\nstructural alteration, and thus, the  Constitution  99th  Amendment  whereby\nthe selection is left to a third body, the  National  Judicial  Appointments\nCommission  (NJAC).  The  Parliament  also  passed  the  <a href=\"/doc/142995521/\" id=\"a_2278\">National   Judicial\nAppointments Commission Act, 2014</a>, which is only a creature of  Constitution\n99th Amendment. The validity of the Act is also under challenge.\n\u2018What is the big deal about it?\u2019, has been the oft made  observation  of  my\nesteemed brother Khehar, J., the presiding Judge, in the thirty days of  the\nhearing of the case, which  included  an  unusual  two  weeks  long  sitting\nduring the summer vacations with the  hearing  in  three  different  Courts,\nviz., Court Nos. 3, 4 and 6. When it is held, and rightly so, that there  is\nno requirement for reconsideration of the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2279\">Second Judges</a> Case,  the  fate  of\nthe case is sealed; there is no need for any further  deal,  big  or  small.\nThough I generally agree with the analysis and  statement  of  law,  in  the\nmatter of discussion and summarization of the principles on  reconsideration\nof judgments made by Lokur, J. at paragraph 263, I would  like  to  add  one\nmore, as the tenth. Once this Court has addressed an issue on a  substantial\nquestion of law as to the structure of the Constitution and  has  laid  down\nthe law, a request for revisit shall not be  welcomed  unless  it  is  shown\nthat the structural interpretation is palpably  erroneous.</span>  <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_645\">None  before  us\ncould blur the graphic picture on the scheme of appointment  of  Judges  and\nits solid structural base  in  the  Constitution  portrayed  in  the  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2280\">Second\nJudges</a> Case.  This  Bench  is  bound  by  the  ratio  that  independence  of\njudiciary is part of the  basic  structure  of  Constitution  and  that  the\nappointment of Judges to the  High  Courts  and  the  Supreme  Court  is  an\nintegral part of the concept of independence  of  judiciary.  And  for  that\nsimple reason, the Constitution 99th  Amendment  is  bound  to  be  declared\nunconstitutional and I do so. Thus, I wholly agree with the  view  taken  by\nKhehar, Lokur and Goel, JJ., that the amendment is  unconstitutional  and  I\nrespectfully disagree with  the  view  taken  by  Chelameswar,  J.  in  that\nregard. Since it is being held by the majority that the amendment itself  is\nbad, there is no point in dealing with the validity of the creature  of  the\namendment, viz., the <a href=\"/doc/142995521/\" id=\"a_2281\">National Judicial Appointments  Commission  Act,  2014</a>.\nIt does not exist under law. Why then write the  horoscope  of  a  stillborn\nchild!\nHowever, I would like to provide one more prod. Professor Philip  Bobbit  in\nhis famous book \u2018Constitutional Fate Theory of the Constitution\u2019, has  dealt\nwith a  typology  of  constitutional  arguments.  To  him,  there  are  five\narchetypes: historical, textual, structural, prudential  and  doctrinal.  To\nquote from Chapter 1:</span>\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_866\">\u201cHistorical argument is argument that marshals the intent of  the  draftsmen\nof the Constitution and  the  people  who  adopted  the  Constitution.  Such\narguments begin with assertions about the controversies, the attitudes,  and\ndecisions  of  the  period  during  which  the   particular   constitutional\nprovision to be construed was proposed and ratified.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_867\">      The second archetype is textual argument, argument that is drawn  from\na consideration of the present sense of  the  words  of  the  provision.  At\ntimes textual argument is confused with historical argument, which  requires\nthe consideration of evidence extrinsic to  the  text.  The  third  type  of\nconstitutional argument in structural  argument.  Structural  arguments  are\nclaims that a particular principle or practical result is  implicit  in  the\nstructures of government and the  relationships  that  are  created  by  the\nConstitution  among  citizens  and   governments.   The   fourth   type   of\nconstitutional argument is prudential argument. Prudential argument is self-\nconscious to the reviewing institution and need not treat the merits of  the\nparticular controversy (which itself may  or  may  not  be  constitutional),\ninstead advancing particular doctrines according to the practical wisdom  of\nusing the courts in a particular way.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_868\">       Finally,  there  is  doctrinal  argument,   argument   that   asserts\nprinciples derived from precedent or from judicial  or  academic  commentary\non precedent.\u201d\n\nProfessor (Dr.) Upendra Baxi  has  yet  another  tool  \u2013  \u2018episodic\u2019,  which\naccording to him, is often wrongly used in  interpreting  the  Constitution.\nTo Dr. Baxi, \u2018structural\u2019 is the most important argument while  interpreting\nthe Constitution.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_869\">Structural argument is further explained  in  Chapter  6.  To  quote  a  few\nobservations:\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_870\">\u201cStructural arguments are inferences from the  existence  of  constitutional\nstructures and the relationships which the Constitution ordains among  these\nstructures. They  are  to  be  distinguished  from  textual  and  historical\narguments, which construe a particular constitutional passage and  then  use\nthat construction in the reasoning of an opinion.\u201d\n\nxxx                    xxx                   xxx\n\n\u201cStructural arguments are largely factless and depend on deceptively  simple\nlogical moves from the entire Constitutional text rather than  from  one  of\nits parts. At  the  same  time,  they  embody  a  macroscopic  prudentialism\ndrawing not on the peculiar facts  of  the  case  but  rather  arising  from\ngeneral assertions about power and social choice.\u201d\n\nxxx                    xxx                   xxx\n\n\u201cNotice that the structural approach, unlike much doctrinalism, is  grounded\nin the actual text of the Constitution. But,  unlike  textualist  arguments,\nthe passages that are significant are not those of express grants  of  power\nor  particular  prohibitions  but  instead  those  which,  by   setting   up\nstructures of a certain kind, permit us to  draw  the  requirements  of  the\nrelationships among structures.\u201d\n\nProfessor Bobbit has also dealt with  a  sixth  approach  \u2013  ethical,  which\naccording to him, is seldom used in constitutional law. In interpreting  the\nConstitution, all the tools are to be appropriately used, and  quite  often,\nin combination  too.  The  three  constitutional  wings,  their  powers  and\nfunctions under the Constitution, and their  intra  relationship  being  the\nkey issues to be analysed in the present case, I am of  the  view  that  the\n\u2018structural tool\u2019 is to be prominently  applied  for  resolving  the  issues\narising in the case. In support, I shall refer to a recent judgment  of  the\nU.S.  Supreme  Court  in  State   v.   Arizona   Independent   Redistricting\nCommission[711], decided on 29.06.2015. It is  an  interesting  case,  quite\nrelevant to our discussion. U.S. Constitution Article I, <a href=\"/doc/11284129/\" id=\"a_2282\">Section  4</a>  ,Clause\n1 (Election Clause) reads as follows:\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_871\">\u201cThe Times,  Places  and  Manner  of  holding  Elections  for  Senators  and\nRepresentatives, shall be  prescribed  in  each  State  by  the  Legislature\nthereof; but the Congress may  at  any  time  by  Law  make  or  alter  such\nRegulations, except as to the Places of chusing Senators.\u201d\n\nArizona Constitution, Article IV, Part 1, to the extent relevant,  reads  as\nfollows:\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_872\">\u201c<a href=\"/doc/117354837/\" id=\"a_2283\">Section 1</a>. (1) Senate; house of representatives; reservation  of  power  to\npeople. The legislative authority of  the  state  shall  be  vested  in  the\nlegislature, consisting of a senate and a house of representatives, but  the\npeople reserve the power to propose laws and amendments to the  constitution\nand to enact or reject such laws and amendments at the polls,  independently\nof the legislature; and they also reserve, for use at their own option,  the\npower to approve or reject at the polls any act, or item, section,  or  part\nof any act, of the legislature.\u201d\n\n      Thus, under <a href=\"/doc/117354837/\" id=\"a_2284\">Section 1</a>,  people  are  involved  in  direct  legislation\neither by the process known  as  \u2018initiative\u2019  or  \u2018referendum\u2019.  While  the\ninitiative allows the electorate to adopt positive  legislation,  referendum\nis meant as a negative check. Popularly, the process of initiative  is  said\nto correct \u2018sins of  omission\u2019  by  the  Legislature  while  the  referendum\ncorrects \u2018sins of commission\u2019 by the Legislature.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_873\">In 2000, Arizona voters adopted Proposition 106, an initiative aimed at  the\nproblem of gerrymandering. Proposition 106 amended  Arizona&#x27;s  Constitution,\nremoving redistricting authority from the Arizona  Legislature  and  vesting\nit in an  independent  commission,  the  Arizona  Independent  Redistricting\nCommission (AIRC). After the 2010 census, as  after  the  2000  census,  the\nAIRC  adopted  redistricting  maps  for  congressional  as  well  as   state\nlegislative districts. The Arizona Legislature challenged the map which  the\nCommission adopted in 2012 for  congressional  districts  arguing  that  the\nAIRC and its map violated the &quot;Elections Clause&quot; of the U.S. Constitution.\nJustice Ginsburg and four other Justices formed the majority and  held  that\nthe independent commission is competent to  provide  for  redistricting.  To\nquote the main reasoning:\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_874\">\u201cThe Framers may not have imagined the modern initiative  process  in  which\nthe people\u2019s legislative powers is coextensive with the state  legislature\u2019s\nauthority, but the invention of the initiative was in full harmony with  the\nConstitution&#x27;s conception of the people as the font of governmental power.\u201d\n\nHowever, Chief Justice Roberts and three  other  Justices  dissented.  Chief\nJustice Roberts pointed out that the majority position has no basis  in  the\ntext,  structure,  or  history  of  the  Constitution  and  it   contradicts\nprecedents from both  Congress  and  the  Supreme  Court.  The  Constitution\ncontains seventeen provisions referring to the  \u2018Legislature\u2019  of  a  State,\nmany of which cannot possibly  be  read  to  mean  \u2018the  people\u2019.  To  quote\nfurther:\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_875\">\u201cThe  majority  largely  ignores   this   evidence,   relying   instead   on\ndisconnected   observations   about   direct    democracy,    a    contorted\ninterpretation of  an  irrelevant  statute,  and  naked  appeals  to  public\npolicy. Nowhere does the majority explain  how  a  constitutional  provision\nthat vests redistricting authority in &quot;the Legislature&quot; permits a  State  to\nwholly exclude &quot;the Legislature&quot; from  redistricting.  Arizona&#x27;s  Commission\nmight be a \u201cnoble endeavor&quot; although it does not seem  so  &quot;independent&quot;  in\npractice but  the  &quot;fact  that  a  given  law  or  procedure  is  efficient,\nconvenient, and useful ... will not  save  it  if  it  is  contrary  to  the\nConstitution\u201d INS v. Chadha, 462 U.S. 919, 944 (1983).\u201d\n\n              xxx                    xxx                   xxx\n\n\u201cThe constitutional text, structure,  history,  and  precedent  establish  a\nstraightforward rule: Under the Elections Clause,  &quot;the  Legislature&quot;  is  a\nrepresentative body that, when it prescribes election  regulations,  may  be\nrequired to do so within the ordinary lawmaking process, but may not be  cut\nout of  that  process.  Put  simply,  the  state  legislature  need  not  be\nexclusive in congressional districting, but neither may it be excluded.\u201d\n\nxxx                    xxx                   xxx\n\n\u201cThe majority today shows  greater  concern  about  redistricting  practices\nthan about the meaning of the Constitution.  I  recognize  the  difficulties\nthat  arise  from  trying  to   fashion   judicial   relief   for   partisan\ngerrymandering. See Vieth v. Jubelirer, 541 U.S. 267  (2004);  ante,  at  1.\nBut our inability to find a manageable standard in that area  is  no  excuse\nto abandon a standard of meaningful interpretation in this area. This  Court\nhas stressed repeatedly that a law&#x27;s virtues as a policy  innovation  cannot\nredeem its inconsistency with the Constitution.\u201d\n                                                         (Emphasis supplied)\n\nWhile wholly agreeing with the historic, textual, prudential  and  doctrinal\napproaches made by Khehar and Lokur, JJ., my additional  stress  is  on  the\nstructural part. The minority  in  Arizona  case  (supra),  to  me,  is  the\ncorrect approach to be made in this case.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_876\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"167993993\" id=\"span_646\">Separation of powers or say distribution of powers,  as  brother  Lokur,  J.\nterms it, is the tectonic  structure  of  the  Constitution  of  India.  The\nvarious checks and balances are  provided  only  for  maintaining  a  proper\nequilibrium amongst the structures and that is the  supreme  beauty  of  our\nConstitution.  Under  our  constitutional  scheme,  one  branch   does   not\ninterfere  impermissibly  with  the  constitutionally  assigned  powers  and\nfunctions of another branch. The permissible areas of interference  are  the\nchecks and balances. But there are certain exclusive areas for each,  branch\nwhich Khehar, J. has stated as \u2018core functions\u2019, and which I would  describe\nas powers central. There shall be no interference on powers central of  each\nbranch. What the Constitution is, is only for the court to  define;  whereas\nwhat the constitutional aspirations are for the  other  branches  to  detail\nand demonstrate. As held in <a href=\"/doc/167993993/\" id=\"a_2285\">Samsher Singh</a>             case (supra)  and  the\nSecond and Third Judges Cases, selection of Judges for appointment  in  High\nCourts and the Supreme Court belongs to the powers central of the  Judiciary\nand the permissible checks and balances are provided to other  branches  lie\nin the sphere of  appointment.  If  the  alignment  of  tectonic  plates  on\ndistribution of powers is disturbed, it will quake  the  Constitution.  Once\nthe constitutional structure is shaken, democracy  collapses.  That  is  our\nown painful history of  the  Emergency.  It  is  the  Parliament,  in  post-\nEmergency, which corrected the constitutional perversions and  restored  the\nsupremacy of rule of law which is the cornerstone of  our  Constitution.  As\nguardian of the Constitution,  this  Court  should  vigilantly  protect  the\npristine purity and integrity of the basic structure  of  the  Constitution.\nDirect participation of the Executive or other non-judicial  elements  would\nultimately lead to structured bargaining in appointments, if  not,  anything\nworse. Any attempt by diluting the basic structure  to  create  a  committed\njudiciary, however remote be the possibility, is to be nipped  in  the  bud.\nAccording to  Justice  Roberts,  court  has  no  power  to  gerrymander  the\nConstitution. Contextually, I would say, the  Parliament  has  no  power  to\ngerrymander the Constitution. The Constitution 99th  amendment  impairs  the\nstructural distribution of powers, and hence, it is impermissible.\nOne word on the consequence. Though elaborate arguments have been  addressed\nthat even if the constitutional amendment  is  struck  down,  the  Collegium\ndoes not resurrect, according to me, does not appeal even to  common  sense.\nThe  99th  Amendment  sought  to  \u2018substitute\u2019  a  few  provisions  in   the\nConstitution and  \u2018insert\u2019  a  few  new  provisions.  Once  the  process  of\nsubstitution and insertion by way of a constitutional  amendment  is  itself\nheld to be bad and impermissible, the pre-amended  provisions  automatically\nresurface  and  revive.  That  alone  can  be  the  reasonably   inferential\nconclusion. Legal parlance and common parlance may be  different  but  there\ncannot be any legal sense of an  issue  which  does  not  appeal  to  common\nsense.</span>\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_877\">All  told,  all  was  and  is  not  well.  To  that  extent,  I  agree  with\n  Chelameswar, J. that the  present  Collegium  system  lacks  transparency,\naccountability  and  objectivity.  The  trust  deficit  has   affected   the\ncredibility of the Collegium system, as  sometimes  observed  by  the  civic\nsociety.  Quite  often,  very  serious  allegations  and  many  a  time  not\nunfounded  too,  have  been  raised  that  its  approach  has  been   highly\nsubjective. Deserving  persons  have  been  ignored  wholly  for  subjective\nreasons, social  and  other  national  realities  were  overlooked,  certain\nappointments were purposely delayed so as either to benefit  vested  choices\nor to deny such benefits to the less patronised, selection of patronised  or\nfavoured persons were made in blatant violation of the guidelines  resulting\nin unmerited, if not, bad appointments,  the  dictatorial  attitude  of  the\nCollegium  seriously  affecting  the  self-respect  and  dignity,  if   not,\nindependence of Judges, the court, particularly  the  Supreme  Court,  often\nbeing styled as the Court of the Collegium,  the  looking  forward  syndrome\naffecting  impartial  assessment,  etc.,  have  been  some  of   the   other\nallegations in the air for quite some  time.   These  allegations  certainly\ncall for a deep introspection as to whether  the  institutional  trusteeship\nhas kept up the expectations of the framers of the Constitution. Though  one\nwould not like to go into a detailed analysis of the reasons,  I  feel  that\nit is not the trusteeship that failed, but the  frailties  of  the  trustees\nand the collaborators which failed the  system.  To  me,  it  is  a  curable\nsituation yet.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_878\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_647\">There is no healthy system in practice. No doubt, the fault  is  not  wholly\nof the Collegium. The active silence of  the  Executive  in  not  preventing\nsuch unworthy appointments was actually  one  of  the  major  problems.  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2286\">The\nSecond and Third Judges</a> Case had provided effective tools in  the  hands  of\nthe Executive to prevent  such  aberrations.
