Whether  \u2018Joint  venture\u2019,  as\nobserved by Chelameswar, J., or not, the Executive seldom  effectively  used\nthose tools.</span>\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_879\">Therefore, the Collegium system needs to be improved requiring a  \u2018glasnost\u2019\nand a \u2018perestroika\u2019, and hence the case needs to be heard  further  in  this\nregard.\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_880\">                                                              .....\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026.J.\n                                                             (KURIAN JOSEPH)\nNew Delhi;\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_881\">October 16, 2015.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_882\">REPORTABLE\n\n\n                        IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA\n                            ORIGINAL JURISDICTION\n\n\n                     WRIT PETITION (CIVIL) NO.13 OF 2015\n\n\n\nSUPREME COURT ADVOCATES-ON-RECORD\nASSOCIATION AND ANR.                                 \u2026PETITIONERS\n\nVERSUS\n\nUNION OF INDIA                                       \u2026RESPONDENT\n\n\n                                    WITH\n\n WRIT PETITION (CIVIL) NOS.23 OF 2015, 70 OF 2015, 83 OF 2015,  T.P. (C) NO.\n   391 OF 2015, W.P. (C) NOS. 108 OF 2015, 124 OF 2015, 14 OF 2015, 18 OF\n   2015, 24 OF 2015, 209 OF 2015, 309 OF 2015, 310 OF 2015 AND 323 OF 2015\n\n                               J U D G M E N T\n\n\nADARSH KUMAR GOEL, J.\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1402\">      Introduction\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1403\">1.    <a href=\"/doc/1164880/\" id=\"a_2287\">Articles 124</a><a href=\"/doc/1688766/\" id=\"a_2288\">, 127</a><a href=\"/doc/908489/\" id=\"a_2289\">, 128</a><a href=\"/doc/1682952/\" id=\"a_2290\">, 217</a><a href=\"/doc/819787/\" id=\"a_2291\">, 222</a><a href=\"/doc/570782/\" id=\"a_2292\">, 224</a> and <a href=\"/doc/1347035/\" id=\"a_2293\">231</a> of the  Constitution  of\nIndia (\u2018the Constitution\u2019) deal with the appointment  of the judges  of  the\nSupreme Court and the High Courts (\u2018the Constitutional courts\u2019),  and  other\nallied matters. <a href=\"/doc/195108305/\" id=\"a_2294\">The Constitution (Ninety-Ninth Amendment)  Act,  2014</a>  (\u2018the\nAmendment Act\u2019) inter alia seeks to amend these  constitutional  provisions.\n<a href=\"/doc/142995521/\" id=\"a_2295\">The National Judicial Appointments Commission Act, 2014</a>  (\u2018the  <a href=\"/doc/142995521/\" id=\"a_2296\">NJAC  Act</a>\u2019),\nenacted simultaneously, purports to regulate the procedure of  the  National\nJudicial Appointments Commission (NJAC).  The  present  batch  of  petitions\nchallenge the constitutional validity of the  <a href=\"/doc/142995521/\" id=\"a_2297\">Amendment  Act  and  the  NJAC\nAct</a>. . The Supreme Court  Advocates-on-Record  Association  has  filed  Writ\nPetition (Civil)  No.13  of  2015,  which  has  been  treated  as  the  lead\npetition.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1404\">2.    I have perused the erudite opinions of my esteemed brothers.  While  I\nrespectfully agree with the conclusions arrived at by Khehar  J.,  Lokur  J.\nand  Kurian  Joseph  J.,  and  respectfully  disagree  with  the   view   of\nChelameswar J.   I prefer to record my own reasons.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1405\">Pre-Amendment Scheme of Appointment and Transfer of Judges\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1406\">3.    The scheme of appointment and transfer of Judges  in  force  prior  to\nthe amendment is set out in two memoranda dated 30th June,  1999  issued  by\nthe Government of India \u2013 first for appointment of Chief  Justice  of  India\n(CJI) and judges of  the  Supreme  Court  and  second  for  appointment  and\ntransfer of Chief Justices and the judges of the High Courts.\n3.1   Broadly the procedure <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2298\">laid  down  in</a>  the  first  memorandum  is  that\nappointment to the office of the CJI should be of the senior most  judge  of\nthe Supreme Court considered fit to hold  the  office.   For  this  purpose,\nrecommendation is sought from the outgoing CJI and if there is  doubt  about\nthe fitness of the senior most judge, consultation is made  with  the  other\njudges under <a href=\"/doc/1995948/\" id=\"a_2299\">Article 124(2).</a>  Thereafter,  the  Law  Minister  puts  up  the\nmatter to  the  Prime  Minister  (PM)  who  advises  the  President.   After\napproval of the President, the appointment is notified.  For appointment  as\njudges of the Supreme Court, the CJI initiates  the  proposal  and  forwards\nhis recommendation to the Union Minister of Law who puts up  the  matter  to\nthe PM, who in turn advises the President.  Opinion of the CJI is formed  in\nconsultation with four senior most judges and if successor  CJI  is  not  in\nthe said four senior most judges, he is also made  part  of  the  collegium.\nCJI also ascertains the views of the senior most judge in the Supreme  Court\nwho hails from the  High  Court  from  where  a  person  recommended  comes.\nOpinions  in  respect  of  the  recommendation  are  in  writing   and   are\ntransmitted to the Government of India for record.  If  the  views  of  non-\njudges are solicited, a memorandum thereof and its substance is conveyed  to\nthe Government of India.  Once appointment is approved by the  President  of\nIndia, certificate of physical fitness is obtained and after the warrant  of\nappointment is signed by the President, the appointment is announced  and  a\nnotification issued in the Gazette of India.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1407\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_648\">3.2   The procedure <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2300\">laid down  in</a>  the  second  memorandum  deals  with  the\nappointments to the High Courts and transfers.  The Chief Justices  of  High\nCourts are appointed from outside.  Inter se seniority in a particular  High\nCourt is considered for appointment as Chief Justice from that  High  Court.\nInitiation of proposal for appointment of Chief Justice of a High  Court  is\nby the CJI. The CJI consults two senior most Judges  of  the  Supreme  Court\nand also ascertains the views of his senior most colleague  in  the  Supreme\nCourt who is conversant with the affairs of the  High  Court  in  which  the\nrecommendee  has  been  functioning  and  whose  opinion  is  likely  to  be\nsignificant in adjudging the suitability of the candidate. The views of  the\nJudges are sent along with the proposal of the Union  Minister  of  Law  who\nobtains the views of the concerned State Government  and  then  submits  the\nproposal to the PM who advises the President.  As  soon  as  appointment  is\napproved by the President, notification is issued in the Gazette  of  India.\nAs regards the appointment of a Judge of the High Court, the  Chief  Justice\nof the High Court communicates  to  the  Chief  Minister  his  views,  after\nconsulting two of his senior most colleagues regarding  suitability  of  the\nperson to be selected.  All consultations  must  be  in  writing  and  these\nopinions are sent to the Chief Minister, along with the recommendation.   If\nthe Chief Minister desires to recommend a name, he has to forward  the  same\nto the Chief Justice for his consideration. A copy of the recommendation  is\nalso sent to the CJI and the Union Law Minister. The Chief Minister  advises\nthe Governor who forwards his recommendation to the Law Minister.   The  Law\nMinister considers the recommendation in the light  of  such  other  reports\n(such as I.B. report) as  may  be  available  to  the  Government  and  then\nforwards the material to the CJI.  CJI consults two senior most  Judges  and\nalso takes into account the views of the Chief Justice  and  Judges  of  the\nHigh Court (consulted by the Chief Justice) and those Judges of the  Supreme\nCourt who are conversant with the affairs of the candidate.  Thereafter  the\nCJI sends the recommendation to  the  Union  Law  Minister  along  with  the\ncorrespondence with his  colleagues.   If  the  Law  Minister  considers  it\nexpedient to refer back the name for opinion  of  the  State  Constitutional\nAuthorities, opinion of the CJI must be obtained.   The  Law  Minister  then\nputs up the recommendation  to  the  PM  who  advises  the  President.   The\ncorrespondence between the Chief Justice, the Chief  Minister  and  Governor\ninter se is in writing.  As soon as  the  appointment  is  approved  by  the\nPresident, physical fitness  is  ascertained  and  as  soon  as  warrant  of\nappointment is signed by  the  President,  notification  is  issued  in  the\nGazette of India.</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1408\">3.3   Proposal for transfer is initiated by the CJI.  Consent of  the  Judge\nconcerned is not necessary.  The CJI consults four  senior  most  Judges  of\nthe Supreme Court and takes into account the views of the Chief  Justice  of\nthe High Court from which the Judge is to be transferred and  Chief  Justice\nof the High Court to which the transfer is to be effected.  CJI  also  takes\ninto account the views of one or more Supreme Court  Judges  who  are  in  a\nposition to offer his/their views.  The views are expressed in writing,  and\nare considered by the CJI and four senior most Judges.  The  personal  facts\nrelating to the Judge and his response to the proposal are invariably  taken\ninto account.  The proposal is then referred to  the  Government.   The  Law\nMinister submits the recommendation to the PM  who  advises  the  President.\nAfter the President approves the transfer, a notification is issued  in  the\nOfficial Gazette.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1409\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_649\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_650\">3.4   The above memoranda were issued by the  Government  of  India  in  the\nlight of unamended Constitutional provisions and the judgment of this  Court\ndated 28th October, 1998 in  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2301\">Special  Reference  No.1  of  1998[712]  (Third\nJudges\u2019</a> case) which in substance reiterates the  earlier  Nine  Judge  Bench\njudgment in SCAORA vs. Union of India[713] (<a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2302\">Second Judges\u2019</a> case).\n3.5    Reference  may  also  be  made  to   the   unamended   constitutional\nprovisions.  <a href=\"/doc/1995948/\" id=\"a_2303\">Article 124 (2)</a> provides that a  Judge  of  the  Supreme  Court\nshall be appointed by the President after consultation with such  Judges  of\nthe Supreme Court and the High Courts as  are  deemed  necessary.   However,\nthe CJI is always to be consulted.  <a href=\"/doc/1682952/\" id=\"a_2304\">Article 217</a> provides  that  a  Judge  of\nthe High Court shall be appointed by the President after  consultation  with\nCJI, Governor of the State and in case of  a  Judge  other  than  the  Chief\nJustice, the Chief Justice of the High Court.    The question  arose  before\nthis Court on several occasions as to the value of the opinion  of  the  CJI\nin the process of \u2018consultation\u2019.   This Court held that  under  the  scheme\nof the Constitution a proposal for appointment to  the  Supreme  Court  must\nemanate from the CJI and  for  appointment  to  the  High  Court  it  should\nemanate from the Chief Justice of the  High  Court  and  the  last  word  on\nappointment  must  rest  with  the  CJI[714].   This  Court  noted  that  by\nconvention  proposals  for  appointments  were  always  initiated   by   the\njudiciary and appointments were made with the concurrence of the CJI.</span></span>   <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_651\">This\nview was reiterated in <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2305\">Third Judges\u2019</a> case on the basis of  which  the  above\nmemoranda were issued by the Government of India.</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1410\">      Scheme under the Amendment\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1411\">4.    Reference may now be  made  to  the  impugned  Amendment.   It  amends\n<a href=\"/doc/1164880/\" id=\"a_2306\">Article 124</a> and provides that such appointments and transfers  will  now  be\non the recommendation of the NJAC (<a href=\"/doc/79773052/\" id=\"a_2307\">Section  2</a>).   Requirement  of  mandatory\nconsultation with the CJI and  consultation  with  such  Judges  as  may  be\nconsidered necessary has been deleted. Convention of initiation of  proposal\nby Chief Justice for the High Courts and  CJI  for  the  Supreme  Court  and\nother scheme as reflected in the memoranda earlier  mentioned  and  as  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2308\">laid\ndown in</a> decisions of this Court has been replaced.  The amendment inserts  a\nnew <a href=\"/doc/140653939/\" id=\"a_2309\">Article 124A</a>, under which the  NJAC  is  to  be  constituted.   It  will\ncomprise the CJI, two senior most judges of Supreme Court next to  the  CJI,\nUnion Law Minister and two eminent persons to be nominated by the  Committee\ncomprising of the PM, the CJI and the Leader of the Opposition in the  House\nof the People/Leader of single largest Opposition Party in the House of  the\nPeople.  The  nomination of one of these eminent  persons  is  reserved  for\npersons belonging to  the  Scheduled  Castes,  the  Scheduled  Tribes,  OBC,\nminorities or women.  Under the new scheme, for any  proposal  five  out  of\nsix members must concur.  If any two members disagree, no  proposal  can  be\nmade.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1412\">5.    The Amendment Act also provides for the Parliament  to  enact  law  to\nregulate the procedure for appointment of judges of  higher  courts  and  to\nempower the Commission to  lay  down,  by  regulations,  the  procedure  for\ndischarge of its functions, the manner of selection of its members and  such\nother matters, as may be considered necessary (<a href=\"/doc/14549537/\" id=\"a_2310\">Section 3</a>).\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1413\">6.    <a href=\"/doc/142995521/\" id=\"a_2311\">The NJAC Act</a> provides for the appointment of the senior most judge  of\nthe Supreme Court as CJI, if considered fit to  hold  the  office;  and  for\nrecommendation for appointment as judge of the Supreme  Court  (<a href=\"/doc/125564484/\" id=\"a_2312\">Section  5</a>).\nThe Second proviso to   <a href=\"/doc/112124301/\" id=\"a_2313\">Section  5(2)</a>  of  the  NJAC  Act  states  that  the\nCommission  shall  not  recommend  a  person  if  two    members    of   the\nCommission do not agree. Apart from  its  other  functions,  the  Commission\nwould also recommend appointments  of  Chief  Justice  and  judges  of  High\nCourts (<a href=\"/doc/176401296/\" id=\"a_2314\">Section  6(1)</a>,  (3)).  Alternatively,  the  Commission  can  seek  a\nnomination from the  Chief  Justice  of  the  High  Court  for  recommending\nappointment as judge of the High Court(<a href=\"/doc/101346937/\" id=\"a_2315\">Section  6(2)</a>).  For  appointment  of\njudges  of  High  Courts,  however,  the   Commission   must   seek    prior\nconsultation with the Chief Justice of the  concerned  High  Court,  who  in\nturn has to consult two senior most judges of the said High Court  and  such\nother judges and eminent advocates as  may  be  specified.  (<a href=\"/doc/6903810/\" id=\"a_2316\">Section  6(4)</a>).\nThe Commission is also to seek views of the Governor and Chief  Minister  of\nthe concerned State. The power of appointment of officers and  employees  of\nthe Commission  is  with  the  Central  Government.   The  Convener  of  the\nCommission is the Secretary, Government  of  India,  in  the  Department  of\nJustice.  Central Government is authorised to make rules  for  carrying  out\nthe provisions of the Act(<a href=\"/doc/125361718/\" id=\"a_2317\">section 11</a>).   The  Commission  is  authorised  to\nmake regulations consistent with the Act and the Rules.  The Rules  and  the\nRegulations framed under the Act  are  required  to  be  placed  before  the\nParliament, which may modify such rules or regulations(<a href=\"/doc/112842572/\" id=\"a_2318\">sections 12</a>, <a href=\"/doc/184356182/\" id=\"a_2319\">13</a>).\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1414\">7.    The statement of objects and reasons of the  amendment  mentions  that\nthis Court had interpreted  the  word  \u201cconsultation\u201d  as  \u201cconcurrence\u201d  in\n<a href=\"/doc/1995948/\" id=\"a_2320\">Articles 124(2)</a> and <a href=\"/doc/37564/\" id=\"a_2321\">217 (2)</a> of the Constitution (<a href=\"/doc/79773052/\" id=\"a_2322\">S.2</a>).   It  further  states\nthat after review of the constitutional provisions, pronouncements  of  this\nCourt and consultation with eminent jurists, it was felt that a broad  based\nNational Judicial Appointments Commission should be established  for  making\nrecommendation for appointment of judges of the Supreme Court and  the  High\nCourts.  The Commission will provide meaningful role to the  judiciary,  the\nexecutive and eminent persons to present their  view  points  and  make  the\nparticipants  accountable  while  also  introducing  transparency   in   the\nselection process (<a href=\"/doc/14549537/\" id=\"a_2323\">S.3</a>).\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1415\">7.1   Though by notification dated 13th April, 2015, the Amendment  and  the\nAct have been brought into force, the Commission has  not  been  constituted\nso far, as two eminent persons have not been so far appointed.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1416\">7.2   Key Constitutional unamended provisions  and  the  provisions  of  the\nAmendment and the Act are as follows:-\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_883\">|Unamended Provisions          |Provisions of the Amendment   |\n|Article 124 xxxx     xxxx     |\u201c124A. The Amendment Act is challenged as ultra vires, inter alia  for  being\nbeyond the competence of the Parliament as it alters and destroys the  basic\nstructure of the Constitution, as embodied in the independence of  judiciary\nin the context of  appointment  of  judges  of  the  higher  judiciary.  The\npetitioners  submit  that  the  power  of  the  Parliament  to   amend   the\nConstitution under <a href=\"/doc/594125/\" id=\"a_2325\">Article 368</a> is limited and does not  extend  to  altering\nor destroying the basic structure or basic  features  of  the  Constitution.\nThe independence of the judiciary is a constitutional concept,  regarded  as\na basic feature of the Constitution, and includes insulating  the  judiciary\nfrom executive or legislative control, primacy of higher  judiciary  in  the\nmatter of appointment of judges to the High Courts and  the  Supreme  Court,\nnon-amendability of conditions of service of judges  of  the  Supreme  Court\nand the High Court to their disadvantage.   The  Amendment  takes  away  the\nprimacy of the collective opinion of the CJI and  the  senior  most  Supreme\nCourt judges by stalling an appointment unanimously proposed by them if  the\nsame is not concurred by two non-judge Commission  members  [second  proviso\nto <a href=\"/doc/112124301/\" id=\"a_2326\">Section 5(2)</a> and <a href=\"/doc/93595316/\" id=\"a_2327\">Section 6(6)</a>].  This endows unchecked veto power to non-\njudges in appointing judges to higher courts, compromising  the  judiciary\u2019s\nindependence. The Amendment also dilutes the  judiciary\u2019s  constitutionally-\nconferred power by granting unbridled power on the  Parliament  to  control,\nby ordinary law, the manner of selection of  a  person  for  appointment  to\nhigher judiciary, which also damages the independence  of  judiciary.   This\npower enables the Parliament to substitute judiciary\u2019s primacy with that  of\nthe executive. If allowed to stand, the provision could  easily  be  further\namended thereby denying any effective role for the  senior  most  judges  of\nthe higher judiciary in appointment of judges of the Supreme Court  and  the\nHigh Courts.  Thus, the Amendment does not envisage  predominant  voice  for\nthe judges and  makes  the  executive  element  in   appointment  of  judges\ndominant which alters and damages the basic structure of  the  Constitution.\n It is also contended that the <a href=\"/doc/142995521/\" id=\"a_2328\">NJAC Act</a> was void as it  was  passed  by  the\nParliament before the Amendment Act became operative.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1419\">9.    Thus, the contentions on behalf of the petitioners are:-\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_884\">(i)   Constitution is supreme and powers  of  all  organs  are  defined  and\ncontrolled thereunder;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_885\">(ii)  Amending power of Parliament is limited  by  the    concept  of  basic\nstructure as judicially interpreted;\n</blockquote>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1420\">Final interpreter of the Constitution and the scope of powers thereunder  is\nthis Court;\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1421\">Independence of judiciary  and  separation  of  powers  are  part  of  basic\nstructure;\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1422\">Primacy  of  judiciary  in  appointment  of  judges  is  crucial   part   of\nindependence of judiciary and separation of powers and thus  part  of  basic\nstructure;\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1423\">Role of executive and legislature in appointment of  judges  being  kept  at\nminimum was also part of basic structure;\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1424\">The composition of  the  Commission  in  the  impugned  Amendment  severally\ndamages the basic structure of the Constitution  by  destroying  primacy  of\njudiciary in appointment of  judges  and  giving  controlling  role  to  the\nexecutive and legislature in such appointments;\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1425\">The impugned amendment enables stalling of appointment  of  judges  proposed\nby the judiciary unless candidates suggested by the executive are  appointed\nthereby compromising independence of judiciary;\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1426\">The impugned amendment expands the power of amendment by delegating  crucial\nissues of appointment of judges to Parliament which  is  against  the  basic\nstructure of the Constitution;\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1427\">The composition of  the  Commission  will  shake  confidence  of  people  in\nJudiciary if Executive or Legislature have dominant voice; and\n\n\nThe impugned Act is beyond legislative competence of the Parliament.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_1428\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_652\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_653\">10.   The Joint  Secretary,  Department  of  Justice  has  filed  a  counter\naffidavit on behalf of the Union of India  (UOI),  defending  the  Amendment\nand the Act.  UOI\u2019s case is that independence  of  judiciary  is  only  post\nappointment.   Appointment  is  an  executive  act   and   the   judiciary\u2019s\nindependence has no relevance with the executive act  of  appointment.   UOI\nsubmits that  judicial  independence  is  to  be  coupled  with  checks  and\nbalances and that a contextual reading of <a href=\"/doc/1995948/\" id=\"a_2329\">Articles 124(2)</a>  and  <a href=\"/doc/259647/\" id=\"a_2330\">217(1)</a>  with\nthe Constituent Assembly Debates (CAD) makes it evident  that  there  is  no\nprimacy of the CJI in appointment of judges.  Consultation with the CJI  was\nonly by way of a check on executive, which had the final say in the  matter.\nFurther, provision for consultation  with  other  judges  does  not  justify\ncreation of a collegium.  UOI\u2019s submission refers to impeachment  provisions\nfor removal of  judges  (<a href=\"/doc/41081345/\" id=\"a_2331\">Article  124(4);</a>  Parliament\u2019s  power  to  regulate\nprocedure for presentation of an address  and  investigation  and  proof  of\nmisbehaviour or incapacity of a judge  (<a href=\"/doc/70262168/\" id=\"a_2332\">Article  124(5)</a>)  and  to  determine\nsalary of judges and provisions pertaining  to  other  aspects  of  judicial\nfunctioning conferring power on Parliament to legislate (<a href=\"/doc/1312011/\" id=\"a_2333\">Article 125</a>).   UOI\nsubmits that the decisions of this Court in <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2334\">Second Judges\u2019</a>  case  and  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2335\">Third\nJudges\u2019</a> case laying  down  primacy  of  the  judiciary  in  the  context  of\nconsultative process under <a href=\"/doc/1849799/\" id=\"a_2336\">Articles 124(1)</a> and <a href=\"/doc/259647/\" id=\"a_2337\">217(1)</a> have no  relevance  to\ntest  the  validity  of  the  impugned  Ninety  Ninth  Amendment  by   which\nprovisions of <a href=\"/doc/1995948/\" id=\"a_2338\">Articles 124(2)</a> and  <a href=\"/doc/259647/\" id=\"a_2339\">217(1)</a>  stand  amended.  However,  it  is\ncontended that the view taken in the said judgments that the  judiciary  has\nprimacy in appointment is erroneous, and needs  to  be  revisited.   In  any\ncase, the UOI contends that the  primacy  of  judiciary  in  the  matter  of\nappointment of judges  of  the  higher  judiciary  has  no  connection  with\nindependence of judiciary and is not the basic feature of the  Constitution.\n In several countries, such as Australia, independence of  judiciary  exists\nwithout primacy of the judiciary in appointments of  judges  to  the  higher\njudiciary.  UOI submits that the power conferred on Parliament to enact  law\nto regulate the procedure of the NJAC or to modify  the  regulations  framed\nby the NJAC is valid. The NJAC  is  accountable  to  Parliament  in  framing\nregulations.  The presence of Law Minister as a member of the  NJAC  ensures\naccountability to public.  The presence of two eminent persons  is  a  check\nand balance on the functioning of other members.  Diversity of members  will\nensure greater accountability of  each  member  to  the  other.   This  will\nensure greater public confidence in the functioning of the  judiciary.   The\nNJAC will fall under the purview of <a href=\"/doc/671631/\" id=\"a_2340\">Right to  Information  Act,  2005</a>  which\nwill ensure transparency.  Even if the Amendment was struck  down,  original\nprovisions could not be revived as doctrine of revival  does  not  apply  to\nConstitutional  Amendments.</span></span>   <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"PARTY\" data-docid=\"790399\" id=\"span_654\">The  issue  was  raised  in  <a href=\"/doc/790399/\" id=\"a_2341\">Property  Owners\u2019\nAssociation vs. State of Maharashtra</a>[715]  with respect to  <a href=\"/doc/198382/\" id=\"a_2342\">Article  31C</a>  of\nthe Constitution which is pending before a nine-judge  Bench.   It  is  also\nsubmitted that the writ petition is pre-mature as the  new  system  has  not\nbeen given a chance to operate and no rights have been affected.</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_1429\">11.    The contentions on behalf of the respondents  can  be  summed  up  as\nfollows:-\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_886\">(a)   Power of appointment of judges rests with the executive  and  role  of\njudiciary is confined to consultation which may or may not  be  accepted  by\nthe executive;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_887\">(b)   Primacy of judiciary  in  appointments  was  recognised  by  erroneous\ninterpretation of unamended provisions of the Constitution  and  by  way  of\namendment such interpretation has  been  corrected  and  thus  there  is  no\nviolation of basic structure.  Alternatively larger Bench be constituted  to\ncorrect the earlier interpretation;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_888\">(c)   Primacy of judiciary  in  appointments  was  not  inalienable  and  in\nchanged situation, in the light of experiences gained, the primacy could  be\ndone away with or modified;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_889\">(d)   Wisdom of constituent  body  in  making  a  choice  was  not  open  to\njudicial review;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_890\">(e)    Taking  the  Constitution  as  a  whole,  value  of  independence  of\njudiciary could be balanced with other constitutional values  of  democracy,\naccountability and checks and balances;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_891\">(f)   Power of amendment was plenary and could not be questioned  unless  it\nresults in destruction of a pillar of Constitution;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_892\">(g)   Even with power being with executive  or  power  of  veto  being  with\nexecutive, independence of judiciary could survive  so  long  as  there  was\nprotection of tenure and service conditions of judges;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_893\">(h)     Accountability   and   transparency   in   functioning   of    every\nconstitutional organ was part of democracy in which case exclusive power  of\nappointment of judges with the judiciary was undemocratic;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_894\">(i)   The impugned amendment retains primacy by  having  three  out  of  six\nmembers, out of which  two  could  stop  an  undesirable  appointment.   The\nexecutive did  not  have  predominant  role  as  two  eminent  persons  were\nappointed by a committee having the Prime Minister, the CJI and  the  Leader\nof Opposition thereby role of Prime Minister being  limited.   Law  Minister\nand eminent persons as members  ensured  giving  of  relevant  feedback  and\nensuring accountability and transparency.;\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_895\">(j)   The impugned amendment in  conferring  power  on  Parliament  and  the\nCentral Government in procedural matters did  not  violate  independence  of\njudiciary; and\n\n\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_896\"> (k)   The impugned Act was within legislative competence of Parliament.\n</blockquote>\n\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_1430\">12.   Shri Fali S. Nariman, learned senior  counsel  led  the  arguments  on\nbehalf of the petitioners in  the  lead  petition  followed  by  S/Shri  Ram\nJethmalani, Anil B. Divan, K.N.  Bhat,  Arvind  Datar,  Dr.  Rajeev  Dhawan,\nlearned senior counsel and other counsel appearing either in  person  or  as\nintervenor or  otherwise.   They  have  been  opposed  by  learned  Attorney\nGeneral Shri Mukul Rohtagi, learned Solicitor General Shri Ranjit Kumar  and\nS/Shri K. Parasaran, Soli J. Sorabjee,  K.K.  Venugopal,  Harish  N.  Salve,\nT.R. Andhyarujina, Dushyant Dave learned senior counsel  and  other  learned\ncounsel for various  States  and  intervenors  or  otherwise.  I  record  my\ngratitude to learned  counsel  for  their  painstaking  assistance   to  the\nCourt with their  exceptional  ability  and  skill  for  deciding  important\nissues arising for consideration.  Their contentions will be referred to  at\nappropriate stage to the extent necessary.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_1431\">13.   While generally learned counsel on either side  have  taken  identical\nstand, Shri Venugopal, appearing for the State of M.P., which  is  otherwise\nsupporting the amendment, in  his  alternative  submission,  filed  on  14th\nJuly, 2015 by way  of  additional  propositions,  inter  alia  submitted  as\nfollows:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_897\">\u201c3    Looking at the scheme of the 99th <a href=\"/doc/142995521/\" id=\"a_2343\">Amendment and the National  Judicial\nCommission Appointments Act, 2014</a> <a href=\"/doc/142995521/\" id=\"a_2344\">(NJAC Act</a>), the  scheme  evolved  provides\nfor the constitution of a 6 member Commission and under <a href=\"/doc/16427836/\" id=\"a_2345\">Article  124-C</a>,  for\nthe procedure to be provided under a law made by the Parliament.   <a href=\"/doc/142995521/\" id=\"a_2346\">The  NJAC\nAct</a> has certain salient features that includes under the second  proviso  to\n<a href=\"/doc/112124301/\" id=\"a_2347\">Section 5(2)</a>, a provision in the nature of a \u2018veto\u2019, as no  appointment  can\nbe made if two members of the Commission do not agree to  that  appointment.\nThis provision is challenged by the Petitioners as the  99th  Amendment  Act\ndoes not make any such provision and to provide for a \u2018veto\u2019,  as  it  were,\nby two out of six members, is stated to be ultra  vires  the  Amendment  Act\nor, in any event, not a matter of procedure.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_898\">4     This submission appears to be correct for the following reasons:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_899\">a.    The principle of \u2018primacy\u2019 of  the  judiciary,  which  is  a  part  of\njudicial independence, must necessarily be read into the <a href=\"/doc/142995521/\" id=\"a_2348\">NJAC Act</a>  as  well.\n<a href=\"/doc/142995521/\" id=\"a_2349\">Any Act</a> providing for procedure would  be  ultra  vires  the  Constitutional\nprovision if it does not satisfy the requirement  of  primacy.   The  \u2018veto\u2019\nprovision, therefore, is clearly antithetical to the  concept  of  \u2018primacy\u2019\nand must be struck down as being ultra vires  the amendment.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_900\">xxxxxx      xxxxxx           xxxxx\n\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_901\">6.    Irrespective of the nine Judges\u2019 Bench judgment, certain  concepts  in\nlaw exist in the matter of the functioning of the judiciary in a  democracy.\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1432\"> The existence of an independent judiciary is a sine qua non  for  democracy\nto flourish.  Here, we are  concerned  with  the  issue  of  appointment  of\njudges to the higher judiciary.  Whether, the power is executive or not,  it\ncannot be gainsaid that it impinges on the independence of the judiciary  in\ncase the executive were  to  exclusively  have  the  power  to  appoint  the\njudges.   Such  a  system  of  appointment  could  result  in  brining  into\nexistence judges who are subservient to the will of  the  Government,  which\nwould be a major litigant in the  Courts.    Independence  therefore,  would\nstand affected.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1433\">7.    If the \u2018veto\u2019 is invalid, then the common law  principle  of  majority\nwould apply.  The Chief Justice of India  and  the  two  other  judges  have\nexpertise in the matter of selection of judges to the higher  judiciary  and\nalso have full knowledge of the functioning  of  the  potential  candidates.\nHowever, the unanimous view of the three judges would not carry the  day  if\nopposed by the other three members.  In every other case, where all six  are\nin agreement on a candidate, no problem in making the right  decision  would\narise.  The real question, therefore, is what would be  the  position  if  a\ndeadlock arises when the unanimous decision of the three judges  is  opposed\nby the other three members.  Needless to state, that  if  the  three  judges\nare not ad idem on a candidate, no \u2018issue of primacy\u2019 would  arise  and  the\nmajority would prevail.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1434\">8.    It is true that the nine judges case can no more hold  the  field  for\nthe  purpose  of  nullifying  the  99th  amendment,  which,  obviously,   is\ninconsistent with the Collegium system evolved by the nine judges  judgment.\n But that does not mean that the principles enunciated by <a href=\"/doc/790399/\" id=\"a_2350\">the said  judgment</a>\ncould not be relied upon  as  being  a  juristic  principle  that  would  be\napplicable in such cases.  In other words, these principles can be  said  to\nbe relevant for all time to come because of the following reasons :\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_902\">a.    The power of  appointment  can  be  used  to  affect  or  subvert  the\nindependence of the appointees when functioning as members of  the  superior\njudiciary.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_903\">b.    A system of appointment where the executive voice  predominates  would\naffect such independence.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_904\">c.    If however, the voice of the Chief Justice of India, representing  the\njudiciary prevails, even in a system where the executive or anyone else  has\na minor part to play, this will nevertheless  not  affect  the  independence\nand on the  other  hand  would  sub-serve  independence.   In  other  words,\nprimacy in the matter of appointment has to be with the judiciary.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_905\">xxxxx xxxxx      xxxxx\n\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_906\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_655\">11.   These are general principles enunciated by the Supreme Court based  on\nthe  concept  of  independence  of  the  judiciary.   That  concept  is  all\npervasive and whenever that situation arises, the Court would, in  the  same\nmanner as it did in the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2351\">Second Judges\u2019</a> case , interpret the present  Article\n124-A.  This would mean that the principle of  independence  underlying  the\nappointment of judges of the higher judiciary would require that  the  views\nof the three judges of the Commission, speaking with a  single  voice  would\nhave primacy.</span>  <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_656\">This would be the result not  because  the  judgment  in  the\n<a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2352\">Second Judges\u2019</a> case  would  bind  the  Court  but  because  the  concept  of\njudicial independence applicable in the case of  appointment  of  judges  to\nthe higher judiciary would be applicable wherever and whenever  a  situation\narose where no explicit provision in the Constitution gave  primacy  to  the\njudicial wing.</span>  <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_657\">In such cases, the validity of the constitutional  provision\nwould be upheld and legitimized exactly on the same  basis  as  the  concept\nwas evolved in the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2353\">Second Judges\u2019</a> case.  As a result, the 99th amendment  to\nthe Constitution, would always be deemed to have been a  valid  exercise  of\nConstituent power.  In the absence of the existence  of  a  \u2018veto\u2019,  if  the\nthree Judges speak with a single voice, their decision would  prevail.   The\nPresident would then have to issue the warrant of appointment.</span>\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_907\">xxxxx xxxxx      xxxxx\n\n\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_908\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"486927\" id=\"span_658\">16.   Apart from the above, petitioners have also contended  that  the  term\n\u2018eminent person\u2019 is too broad and that the appointment  of  eminent  persons\nwho have nothing to do with the law and who are not aware of the working  of\nthe judicial system  would  result  in  a  violation  of  the  principle  of\njudicial  independence.  \u2018The  rule  of  purposive  interpretation\u2019  can  be\napplied to this provision.  By application  of  this  rule,  the  Court  can\ninterpret eminent persons to mean only \u2018persons trained in law\u2019 or  \u2018eminent\njurists\u2019 (see in this regard, <a href=\"/doc/486927/\" id=\"a_2354\">P. Vaikunta Shenoy v. P.  Hari  Sharma</a>  (2007)\n14 SCC 297 @ Paras 11-13 and VC Shukla v. State  (Delhi  Amn.)  (1980  Supp.</span>\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1435\">SCC 249 @ para 28)\u201d\n\n      The Issue\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1436\">14.   There being no dispute that a Constitutional Amendment  can  be  valid\nonly if it is consistent with the basic structure of the  Constitution,  the\ncore issue for consideration is whether the  impugned  amendment  alters  or\ndamages the said basic structure and is void on that ground.   According  to\nthe petitioners the primacy  of  judiciary  in  appointment  of  judges  and\nabsence of interference by the Executive therein is  by  itself  a  part  of\nbasic feature of the Constitution being integral  part  of  independence  of\njudiciary and separation of judiciary from the Executive.  According to  the\nrespondents primacy of judiciary in appointment of judges  is  not  part  of\nindependence of judiciary.  Even when appointments are  made  by  Executive,\nindependence of judiciary is not affected.   Alternatively  in  the  amended\nscheme, primacy of judiciary is retained and independence  of  judiciary  is\nstrengthened.  The amendment promotes transparency  and  accountability  and\nis a part of needed reform without affecting  the  basic  structure  of  the\nConstitution.  To determine the question one has to look at the  concept  of\nbasic feature which controls the amending power  of  the  Parliament.   This\nunderstanding will lead to the decision whether  primacy  of  judiciary  and\nabsence of Executive interference in appointment of judges is part  of  such\nbasic structure.\n</p>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1437\">Discussion\n\nA.     Concept  of  Basic  Features  \u2013  As  Limitation  on  Power   of   the\nParliament to amend the Constitution\n\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1438\">15.   <a href=\"/doc/594125/\" id=\"a_2355\">Article 368</a> of the  Constitution  provides  for  power  to  amend  the\nConstitution and procedure therefor.  <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"257876\" id=\"span_659\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"257876\" id=\"span_660\">In <a href=\"/doc/257876/\" id=\"a_2356\">Kesavananda  Bharti  vs.  State  of\nKerala</a>[716] (<a href=\"/doc/257876/\" id=\"a_2357\">Kesavananda Bharti</a> case), the scope of amending power was  gone\ninto by a bench of 13-Judges.  In the concluding para signed by 9-Judges  it\nwas held that \u201cArticle 368 does not enable Parliament  to  alter  the  basic\nstructure or framework of the Constitution\u201d.   The conclusion was  based  on\ninterpretation of the word \u2018amendment\u2019.  It was observed that the  word  was\ncapable of wide as well as narrow meaning and while wide meaning was  to  be\npreferred but consistent with the intention of Constitution makers  and  the\ncontext.  It could not be given too wide meaning so as to permit  damage  to\nthe   constitutional   values   which   depict   the   identity    of    the\nConstitution.[717]\n15.1  The basic structure or framework  was  not  exhaustively  defined  but\nsome of the features of the Constitution were held to be  the  illustrations\nof the basic structure by the majority of seven Judges \u2013 Sikri  CJ,  Shelat,\nGrover, Hegde, Mukherjea, Reddy  and  Khanna,  JJ.   Illustrations  by  them\ninclude Supremacy  of  the  Constitution,  democratic  form  of  Government,\nsecular character of the Constitution,  separation  of  powers  between  the\nJudiciary, the Executive and  the  Legislature,  federal  character  of  the\nConstitution,  dignity  of  the  individual  secured  by  basic  rights   in\naccordance with Parts III and IV, unity and integrity of the nation.[718]\n\n15.2   It  was  held  that  the  power  of  the  Parliament  to  amend   the\nConstitution was limited  by  the  requirement  that  basic  foundation  and\nstructure of the Constitution remains the  same.   Power  of  amendment  was\nenvisaged to meet the challenge of the  problems  which  may  arise  in  the\ncourse  of  socio  economic  progress  of  the  country  but  it  was  never\ncontemplated that in exercise of the power of amendment certain  inalienable\nfeatures of the  Constitution  will  be  changed.   The  court  referred  to\nvarious decisions in different  jurisdictions  dealing  with  the  scope  of\namendment of the Constitution.  Sikri, CJ. observed that  having  regard  to\nimportance of freedom of the individual  and  the  importance  of  economic,\nsocial  and  political  justice,  mentioned  in  the   preamble   the   word\n\u201camendment\u201d could not be read in its widest sense.  The  Fundamental  Rights\ncould not be amended out of existence. Fundamental features  of  secularism,\ndemocracy and freedom of individual should always subsist.   The  expression\n\u201camendment\u201d had a limited meaning.  Otherwise a political  party  with  two-\nthird majority could so amend the Constitution as to debar any  other  party\nfrom functioning, establish  totalitarianism  and  enslave  the  people  and\nthereafter  make  the  Constitution  unamendable.   Thus,  the   appeal   to\ndemocratic principles to  justify  absolute  amending  power,  if  accepted,\ncould damage the very democratic  principles.   Thus,  the  amendment  meant\naddition or change  within  the  broad  contours  of  the  preamble  of  the\nConstitution.  The Parliament could adjust the Fundamental Rights to  secure\nthe objectives of the Directive Principles  while  maintaining  freedom  and\ndignity of every citizen. The dignity and  freedom  of  the  individual  was\nheld to be of supreme importance.   The  basic  features  were  held  to  be\ndiscernible not  only  from  the  preamble  but  the  whole  scheme  of  the\nConstitution.  Shelat &amp; Grover, JJ. observed that  the  Constitution  makers\ndid not desire  that  the  citizens  will  not  enjoy  the  basic  freedoms,\nequality, freedom of religion etc. so  that  dignity  of  an  individual  is\nmaintained.  The economic and social changes were to be made without  taking\naway dignity of the individual.  The vital provisions of Part  III  or  Part\nIV could not be cut out or denuded of their identity.  Hegde and  Mukherjea,\nJJ. observed that the power of amendment was conferred  on  the  Parliament.\nPeople as such were not associated with  the  amendment.   The  Constitution\nwas given by the people to themselves.  The voice  of  the  members  of  the\nConstituent Assembly was of the voice of the people.  Two-third  members  of\nthe two  Houses  of  Parliament  did  not  necessarily  represent  even  the\nmajority of the people.  Thus, the two-third members of the  two  Houses  of\nParliament  could  not  speak  on  behalf  of  the  entire  people  of   the\ncountry[719].  Even best of the Government was not averse to have  more  and\nmore powers to carry out their plans and programmes which  they  believe  to\nbe in public interest, but freedom  once  lost  could  hardly  be  regained.\nEvery encroachment of freedom sets a pattern for further encroachment.   The\ndevelopment  was  envisaged  without  destruction  of  individual  freedoms.\nReddy, J. observed if  any  of  the  essential  features  was  altered,  the\nConstitutional structure could not maintain its identity.   There  could  be\nno justice, liberty or  equality  without  democracy.   There  could  be  no\ndemocracy without justice, equality  and  liberty.   The  structure  of  the\nConstitution was an organic  instrument.   The  core  commitment  to  social\nrevolution lies in Parts III  and  IV.   They  are  the  conscience  of  the\nConstitution.  They had roots deep in the struggle for  independence.   They\nwere included with the hope that one day victory of people  would  bloom  in\nIndia.  They connect India\u2019s future,  present  and  past.   The  demand  for\nFundamental Rights had its inspiration in Magna Carta, the English  Bill  of\nRights, the French Revolution, the American Bill of Rights  incorporated  in\nthe US Constitution.  Referring to  the  statement  of  Dr.  Ambedkar,  that\n<a href=\"/doc/981147/\" id=\"a_2358\">Article 32</a> was the soul of the Constitution and the very  heart  of  it,  it\nwas observed that such an article could not be abrogated  by  an  amendment.\nKhanna, J. observed that as a result  of  amendment,  the  old  Constitution\ncould not  be  done  away  with.   Basic  structure  of  framework  must  be\nretained.  It was not permissible to touch the foundation or  to  alter  the\nbasic  institutional  pattern.   What  can  be  amended  is   the   existing\nConstitution and what must emerge as a result of amendment is not a new  and\ndifferent  Constitution   but   the   existing   Constitution.    What   was\ncontemplated by amendment was varying of the  Constitution  here  and  there\nand  not  elimination  of  its  basic  structure  resulting  in  losing  its\nidentity.</span></span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1439\">15.3  One of the questions considered was  validity  of  <a href=\"/doc/75105291/\" id=\"a_2359\">Section  3</a>  of  the\nTwenty-Fifth Amendment Act, 1971 adding <a href=\"/doc/198382/\" id=\"a_2360\">Article 31-C</a> as follows:-\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_909\">\u201c416. <a href=\"/doc/14549537/\" id=\"a_2361\">Section 3</a> of the twenty-fifth amendment, reads thus:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_910\">3. After <a href=\"/doc/282612/\" id=\"a_2362\">Article 31B</a> of the Constitution, the  following  article  shall  be\ninserted, namely:\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_911\">31. C. Notwithstanding anything contained  in  <a href=\"/doc/134715/\" id=\"a_2363\">Article  13</a>,  no  law  giving\neffect to the policy of the State towards securing the principles  specified\nin Clause (b) or Clause (c) of <a href=\"/doc/555882/\" id=\"a_2364\">Article 39</a> shall be deemed to be void on  the\nground that it is inconsistent with, or takes away or abridges  any  of  the\nrights conferred by <a href=\"/doc/367586/\" id=\"a_2365\">Article 14</a>,  <a href=\"/doc/1218090/\" id=\"a_2366\">Article  19</a>  or  <a href=\"/doc/354224/\" id=\"a_2367\">Article  31;</a>  and  no  law\ncontaining a declaration that it is for giving effect to such  policy  shall\nbe called in question in any court on the  ground  that  it  does  not  give\neffect to such policy:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_912\">\u201cProvided that where such law is made by the legislature  of  a  State,  the\nprovisions of this article shall not apply thereto unless such  law,  having\nbeen reserved for the consideration  of  the  President,  has  received  his\nassent.\u201d\n\n      The highlighted part was held by majority to be unconstitutional,  for\ngranting immunity from challenge thereby  affecting  the  basic  feature  of\njudicial review[720].\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_913\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"936707\" id=\"span_661\">15.4   The scope of amending power was again considered  by  this  Court  in\nthe course  of  challenge  to  Thirty-Ninth  Amendment  which  debarred  any\nchallenge to the election of PM and Speaker  of  the  Lok  Sabha  in  <a href=\"/doc/936707/\" id=\"a_2368\">Indira\nNehru  Gandhi  vs.  Raj  Narain</a>[721].   Chandrachud,  J.  (later  the  Chief\nJustice) observed that it is not that only certain  named  features  of  the\nConstitution are part  of  its  basic  structure.</span>   <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"257876\" id=\"span_662\">The  features  named  by\nindividual judges in <a href=\"/doc/257876/\" id=\"a_2369\">Kesavananda Bharti</a> case were merely  illustrations  and\nwere not intended to be exhaustive.  Having  regard  to  its  place  in  the\nscheme of the Constitution, its object and purpose and the  consequences  of\nits  denial  on  the  integrity  of  the  Constitution,  a  feature  of  the\nConstitution could be held to be  a  basic  feature[722].    He  added  that\nundoubted unamendable basic features are :-</span>\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_914\">\u201c(i) India is a Sovereign Democratic Republic; (ii) Equality of  status  and\nopportunity shall be secured to all its  citizens;  (iii)  The  State  shall\nhave no religion of its own and all persons shall  be  equally  entitled  to\nfreedom of  conscience  and  the  right  freely  to  profess,  practise  and\npropagate religion  and  that  (iv)  the  Nation  shall  be  governed  by  a\nGovernment of laws not of men\u201d.\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_915\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Neutral\" data-docid=\"1939993\" id=\"span_663\">39th Amendment debarring challenge to election inter alia of PM  was  struck\ndown as being against the basic features of the Constitution.[723]   <a href=\"/doc/190157089/\" id=\"a_2370\">Article\n329A</a>, Clause (4) (added by way of  Amendment)  provided  that  election  law\nwill not apply to a person holding office of PM and Speaker and election  of\nsuch persons shall not be deemed to be void under  any  such  law.   It  was\nheld  that  the  democracy  was  the  part  of  the  basic  structure  which\ncontemplated free and fair  election.  Without  there  being  machinery  for\nresolving an election dispute, the elections could  not  be  free  and  fair\nwhich in turn will damage the basic feature of  democracy.   In  absence  of\nany law to deal with validity of election of PM, the basic feature  of  rule\nof law will be violated.  Referring  to  the  writing  of  Madison  in  \u201cThe\nFederalist\u201d, it was observed that all powers of the Government could not  be\nvested in one Department.  No Constitution could survive  without  adherence\nto checks and balances.  \u201cJust as courts ought not to  enter  into  problems\nentwined in the  \u201cpolitical  thicket\u201d,  Parliament  must  also  respect  the\npreserve of the courts[724].\u201d\n\n15.5.  Validity of Forty-Second Amendment was considered by  this  Court  in\n<a href=\"/doc/1939993/\" id=\"a_2371\">Minerva Mills Ltd.  vs.  Union  of  India</a>[725].  The  court  considered  the\nvalidity of <a href=\"/doc/11284129/\" id=\"a_2372\">Sections 4</a> and 55 of the 42nd  Amendment  Act.   By  <a href=\"/doc/11284129/\" id=\"a_2373\">Section  4</a>,\n<a href=\"/doc/198382/\" id=\"a_2374\">Article 31C</a> was sought to be amended to provide that a law giving effect  to\nPart IV of the Constitution could  not  be  deemed  to  be  void  for  being\ninconsistent with <a href=\"/doc/367586/\" id=\"a_2375\">Articles 14</a><a href=\"/doc/1218090/\" id=\"a_2376\">, 19</a> and <a href=\"/doc/354224/\" id=\"a_2377\">31</a> and could not be challenged on  the\nground that the said law was not for giving effect to the said Part IV.   By\nSection 55, it was provided that no amendment of the Constitution  could  be\nchallenged on any ground and  that  there  will  be  no  limitation  on  the\nconstituent power of Parliament  to  amend  the  Constitution.   This  Court\nobserved that the Constitution had conferred limited amending power  on  the\nParliament which itself was  a  basic  feature  of  the  Constitution.   The\nParliament could not expand its amending power so as  to  destroy  the  said\nbasic  feature  of  the  Constitution.   The  limited  power  could  not  be\nconverted into unlimited one. Clauses 4 and 5 of <a href=\"/doc/594125/\" id=\"a_2378\">Article 368</a> added by Forty-</span>\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1440\">Second Amendment were struck down as violative of  basic  structure  of  the\nConstitution.  It was observed that the balance between Part  III  and  Part\nIV of the Constitution was basic feature of the Constitution[726].   Limited\namending power of Parliament was also part of basic structure.[727]  It  was\nalso held that judicial review to  determine  whether  a  law  was  to  give\neffect to Part IV could not be excluded as judicial review was part  of  the\nbasic structure.[728]  It was also observed that though there  is  no  rigid\nseparation of powers in three departments of the State \u2013 the Executive,  the\nLegislature and the Judiciary, there is  broad  demarcation.   Fine  balance\nbetween the three  organs  could  not  be  upset  as  it  will  destroy  the\nfundamental premise of a democratic government.  The judiciary is  entrusted\nwith the duty to keep the Executive and the Legislature  within  the  limits\nof  power  conferred  on  them  which  is  also  a  basic  feature  of   the\nConstitution.[729]\n\n15.6.  <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1152518\" id=\"span_664\">In <a href=\"/doc/1152518/\" id=\"a_2379\">L. Chandra Kumar vs. Union of India</a>[730], part of  <a href=\"/doc/1254475/\" id=\"a_2380\">Article  323</a>  \u2013\nA(2)(d)  and 323 \u2013 B (3)(d) to the extent it excluded  the  jurisdiction  of\nHigh Courts in respect of  specified  matters  for  which  jurisdiction  was\nconferred on Tribunals was struck down  as  violative  of  basic  structure.\nPower of judicial review conferred on this Court and  the  High  Courts  was\nheld to be integral to constitutional scheme in view  of  earlier  decisions\nand conferment of power of judicial review on another  judicial  body  could\nnot justify exclusion of jurisdiction of the High Courts.[731]\n\n15.7.</span>   <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"322504\" id=\"span_665\">In <a href=\"/doc/322504/\" id=\"a_2381\">I.R. Coelho vs. State of Tamil Nadu</a>[732], bench of  nine  Judges,\nconsidered the scope of judicial review of  inclusion  of  a  law  in  Ninth\nSchedule  by  a  constitutional  amendment  thereby  giving  immunity   from\nchallenge in view of <a href=\"/doc/282612/\" id=\"a_2382\">Article 31B</a> of the  Constitution.   It  was  held  that\nevery such amendment shall have to be tested on the touchstone of  essential\nfeatures of the Constitution which included those reflected in <a href=\"/doc/367586/\" id=\"a_2383\">Articles  14</a><a href=\"/doc/1218090/\" id=\"a_2384\">,\n19</a> and <a href=\"/doc/1199182/\" id=\"a_2385\">21</a> and principles underlying them.  Such amendments  are  not  immune\nfrom the attack on the ground they destroy or damage  the  basic  structure.\nThe Court will apply the \u2018rights test\u2019 and the \u2018essence of the rights\u2019  test\n taking synoptic view of Articles in Part III  of  the  Constitution.     It\nwas further observed that the Court has to be guided by  the  \u2018impact  test\u2019\nin determining whether a basic feature was violated.  The Court  will  first\ndetermine  if  there  is  violation  of  rights  in  Part  III  by  impugned\nAmendment, its impact on the basic structure of  the  Constitution  and  the\nconsequence of invalidation of such Amendment[733].</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1441\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"102852\" id=\"span_666\">15.8     In <a href=\"/doc/102852/\" id=\"a_2386\">M. Nagaraj vs. Union of India</a>[734]  ,  Eighty-Fifth  and  allied\namendments to the Constitution were called in  question  on  the  ground  of\nviolation of right of equality as  a  basic  feature  of  the  Constitution.\nWhile considering the challenge, it was observed that the Constitution  sets\nout principles for  an  expanding  future.   This  called  for  a  purposive\napproach to the  interpretation.  It  was  observed  that  a  constitutional\nprovision must not be construed in a narrow sense but in a wide and  liberal\nsense so as to take into account changing conditions and  emerging  problems\nand challenges.  The content of the rights is to be defined by  the  Courts.\nSome  of  the  concepts  like  federalism,  secularism,  reasonableness  and\nsocialism reasonableness are beyond the words  of  a  particular  provision.\nThey give coherence  to  the  Constitution  and  make  the  Constitution  an\norganic whole.  They are part of constitutional law even  if  they  are  not\nexpressly stated in the form of rules.  To qualify as essential  feature,  a\nprinciple has to be established as part of constitutional law  and  as  such\nbinding on the legislature.   Only then, it could  be  examined  whether  it\nwas a part of basic feature.  Theory of basic feature was based  on  concept\nof constitutional  identity.   The  personality  of  the  Constitution  must\nremain unchanged.  The word \u2018amendment\u2019  postulated  that  the  Constitution\nsurvived without loss of identity despite the change. [735]\n\n      Conclusion:</span>\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_916\">15.9  It can safely be held that a constitutional amendment has to pass  the\ntest of basic structure.  Whether or not the basic  structure  was  violated\nhas to be finally determined by this Court from case to case.\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1442\">B.    Whether Primacy of Judiciary in  Appointment  of  Judges  is  Part  of\nBasic Structure\n\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1443\">16.   Whether a feature of the Constitution is  basic  feature  or  part  of\nbasic structure is to be determined  having  regard  to  its  place  in  the\nscheme of the Constitution and consequence of its denial on the  working  of\nthe Constitution.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1444\">16.1  The judiciary has been assigned the  role  of  determining  powers  of\nevery Constitutional organ as also the rights of individuals.  The  disputes\nmay arise between the Government of India and the States, between a  citizen\nand the State or between a citizen and a citizen.  Disputes relating to  the\npowers of Union Legislature and the State Legislature  or  the  exercise  of\nthe executive power may involve issues  of  constitutionality  or  legality.\nIt may involve allegations of malafides even against highest  constitutional\ndignitaries. This requires an  impartial  and  independent  judiciary.   The\njudiciary is required to be separate from the executive control.   Judiciary\nhas  to  inspire  confidence  of  the  people  for  its   impartiality   and\ncompetence.  It has not been  disputed  by  learned  Attorney  General  that\nindependence of judiciary is part  of  the  basic  structure.   It  is  also\nundisputed that judicial review is part of basic structure.   The  decisions\nof this  Court  expressly  lay  down  that  independence  of  judiciary  and\njudicial review are part of basic structure.   Broad  separation  of  powers\nbetween the three departments of the State is a part of doctrine  of  checks\nand balances.  It is also a part of democracy.   Independence  of  judiciary\nis integral to the entire scheme of the Constitution without  which  neither\nprimacy of the Constitution nor  Federal  character,  Social  Democracy  nor\nrights of equality and liberty can be effective.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1445\">16.2  The judiciary has apolitical  commitment  in  its  functioning.   Once\nindependence of  judiciary  is  acknowledged  as  a  basic  feature  of  the\nConstitution,  question  is  whether  power  of  appointing  Judges  can  be\ndelinked from the concept of independence of judiciary or is  integral  part\nof it.  Can  the  independence  of  judiciary  be  maintained  even  if  the\nappointment  of  Judges  is  controlled  directly  or  indirectly   by   the\nexecutive?\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1446\">16.3  To what extent primacy of judiciary in appointment of judges  is  part\nof unamendable basic feature of the Constitution.  Since the issue has  been\ngone into in earlier binding precedents,  reference  to  such  decisions  is\napt.  As already mentioned, it remains undisputed  that  power  of  judicial\nreview, independence of judiciary,  broad  separation  of  powers  in  three\ndepartments of the State, federalism and democracy are  the  basic  features\nof the Constitution.  Stand of the respondents is that power of  appointment\nof judges does not have impact on such basic  features  as  independence  of\njudges  is  envisaged  post  appointment.   By  an  amendment,   process  of\nappointment of judges can be altered to reduce the role of judiciary and  to\nincrease the role  of  Executive  and  Legislature.   Alternatively,  it  is\nsubmitted that no substantial change has taken place in the said roles.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1447\">16.4  In Second Judge\u2019s case, a Bench of 9-Judges  of  this  Court  examined\nthe question of interpretation of unamended  constitutional  scheme  dealing\nwith the appointment of judges of the  Constitution  case.   <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Neg\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_667\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Neg\" data-docid=\"112850760\" id=\"span_668\">The  issue  was\nreferred to the Bench of 9-Judges on account of doubts having arisen  as  to\nthe correctness of the view expressed in         <a href=\"/doc/112850760/\" id=\"a_2387\">S.P.  Gupta  vs.  Union  of\nIndia</a>[736] (<a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2388\">First Judges\u2019</a> case), laying down that primacy in the  matter  of\nappointment of judges rested with the Central  Government[737].   The  basis\nof <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2389\">the said decision</a> was that the word \u2018consultation\u2019 used in <a href=\"/doc/1164880/\" id=\"a_2390\">Articles  124</a><a href=\"/doc/1682952/\" id=\"a_2391\">,\n217</a> etc. implied that the views of the consultee  need  not  be  treated  as\nbinding as the  ultimate  power  of  appointment  rested  with  the  Central\nGovernment.  It was held that the views of the CJI or other Judges who  were\nconsulted may be entitled to great weight but the  final  view  in  case  of\ndifference of opinion could be taken by the Central  Government.   The  word\n\u2018consultation\u2019 could not be read as \u2018concurrence\u2019.</span></span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1448\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"251494\" id=\"span_669\">16.5  The view taken was doubted in <a href=\"/doc/251494/\" id=\"a_2392\">Subhash Sharma vs. Union of  India</a>[738].\nThe question whether opinion of CJI with regard  to  appointment  of  Judges\nwas entitled to primacy was referred for consideration of  a  larger  bench,\nas already mentioned.  This Court observed that  Constitutional  phraseology\nwas required to be read and  expounded  in  the  context  of  Constitutional\nphilosophy of separation of powers and  the  cherished  values  of  judicial\nindependence.</span> <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Neg\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_670\">The role of the CJI  was  required  to  be  recognised  as  of\ncrucial importance for which the view taken in <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2393\">First Judges\u2019</a> case   required\nreconsideration by a larger Bench. It was noted that there  was  an  anxiety\non the part of the Government of the day to assert choice  in  selection  of\nJudges and if the power to recommend appointment of  Judges  was  vested  in\nthe State Government or the Central Government, the picture  was  likely  to\nbe blurred and process of selection may turn out to be difficult.    It  was\nalso  observed  that  the  judiciary  had  apolitical  commitment  and   the\nassurance of non-political complexion of judiciary should  not  be  divorced\nfrom the process  of  appointment.  The  phrase  \u201cconsultation\u201d  had  to  be\nunderstood consistent with and to promote the  constitutional  spirit.   The\nconstitutional values could not be whittled down by calling the  appointment\nof judges as an executive act.  The appointment was  rather  the  result  of\ncollective constitutional process.  It could  not  be  said  that  power  to\nappoint solely vested with the executive or that the executive was  free  to\ntake such decision as it deems fit after consultation  with  the  judiciary.\nThe word \u201cconsultation\u201d was used  in  recognition  of  the  status  of  high\nconstitutional dignitary and could not be interpreted literally.   Moreover,\nthe appointment not recommended by Chief Justice of the State  and  the  CJI\nwould be inappropriate and arbitrary exercise of power. The CJI should  have\npreponderant role.  Primacy of CJI will improve the  quality  of  selection.\nThe view of the Chief Justices of States and CJI should be  decisive  unless\nthe  executive  had  material  indicating  that  the   appointee   will   be\nundesirable.</span>  <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_671\">The view of  the  majority  in  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2394\">First  Judges\u2019</a>  case  did  not\nrecognise the said pivotal position  of  the  institution  of  the  CJI  and\ncorrectness of the said opinion  required  reconsideration.   It  was  noted\nthat the Union Government had often stated  before  Parliament  and  outside\nthat as a matter of policy it had not made any appointment without the  name\nbeing given by the CJI and the executive must be held  to  the  standard  by\nwhich it professed its actions to  be  judged.</span>   <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Neg\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_672\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Neg\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_673\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Neg\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_674\">Upon  reference  to  larger\nBench, the view taken in <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2395\">First Judges</a> case was overruled in  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2396\">Second  Judges\u2019</a>\ncase which was reiterated in the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2397\">Third Judges</a> case.  It held that  the  term\n\u201cconsultation\u201d in <a href=\"/doc/1164880/\" id=\"a_2398\">Article 124</a> should not be literally construed.  It was  to\nbe construed  in  the  constitutional  background  of  its  purpose  and  to\nmaintain and uphold independence of judiciary.  So interpreted, it was  held\nthat  in  the  event  of  conflicting   opinions   of   the   constitutional\nfunctionaries, the opinion of the judiciary as symbolized  by  the  view  of\nthe CJI and formed in the manner indicated, would have primacy.\n16.6  Pandian,  J.  held  that  the  requirement  of  consultation  was  not\nrelatable to any other service and only applied to appointment of judges  in\ncontrast to other high ranking offices.  The consultation with the  CJI  was\ncondition precedent for appointment and advice given  by  the  judiciary  in\nthe process had sanctity.  The executive power  of  appointment  comes  into\nplay by virtue of <a href=\"/doc/127060/\" id=\"a_2399\">Articles  74</a>  and  <a href=\"/doc/674146/\" id=\"a_2400\">163</a>  though  it  was  not  specifically\nprovided for in <a href=\"/doc/1164880/\" id=\"a_2401\">Articles 124</a> and <a href=\"/doc/1682952/\" id=\"a_2402\">217.</a>  The State was  major  litigant.   The\nsuperior courts were faced with controversies with political flavour and  in\nsuch a situation if  the  executive  had  absolute  say  in  appointment  of\njudges, the independence of judiciary will be damaged.  The  Law  Commission\nReports and opinion of jurists suggested radical change  in  appointment  of\njudges by curbing the executive power[739].</span></span></span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1449\">16.7  Kuldip Singh, J. observed that the concept  of  judicial  independence\ndid not only mean the security of tenure to individual  judges.   There  has\nto be  independence  of  judiciary  as  an  institution  so  that  it  could\neffectively act as an impartial  umpire  between  the  Governments  and  the\nindividuals or between the Governments inter se.  It would be  illogical  to\nsay that the judiciary  could  be  independent  when  power  of  appointment\nvested in the Executive.  The framers of the Constitution never intended  to\ngive this power to the Executive which was the largest litigant  before  the\ncourts[740].  There was established  constitutional  convention  recognising\nthe primal and binding opinion of  CJI  in  the  matter  of  appointment  of\njudges.  All appointments since the commencement of  the  Constitution  were\nmade with  the  concurrence  of  the  CJI.   The  14th  Report  of  the  Law\nCommission and discussion in the Parliament on 23rd and 24th November,  1959\nwere referred to[741].  With regard to the  statement  of  Dr.  Ambedkar  on\n24th May, 1949 before the Constituent Assembly that the  CJI  could  not  be\ngiven a veto on appointment of judges, it was observed that primacy  of  the\nCJI acting in representative as against individual  capacity  would  not  be\nagainst the objective of the said statement[742].\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1450\">16.8  Verma, J. observed that the scheme of the Constitution  of  separation\nof powers, with the Directive Principles of  separation  of  judiciary  from\nExecutive, and role of the judiciary to secure rule  of  law  required  that\nappointment of judges in  superior  judiciary  could  not  be  left  to  the\ndiscretion of the Executive.  Independence of judges was  required  even  at\nthe time of their appointment instead of confining it to the provisions  for\nsecurity of tenure and conditions of service.  It was necessary  to  prevent\ninfluence of political consideration  on  account  of  appointments  by  the\nExecutive.  In choice of a candidate, opinion of CJI  should  have  greatest\nweight.  The  role  of  the  Executive  in  the  participatory  consultative\nprocess was intended to be by way of a check on the  exercise  of  power  by\nthe CJI.   The  Executive  element  was  to  be  the  minimum  to  eliminate\npolitical influence[743].\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1451\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_675\">16.9  Accordingly, conclusions were recorded in para 486 to the effect  that\ninitiation of proposal for appointment and transfer could  be  initiated  by\nthe judiciary and in case of conflicting opinions, the opinion  of  the  CJI\nhad the primacy.  In exceptional cases the appointment could be declined  by\ndisclosing the reasons but if the reasons  were  not  accepted  by  the  CJI\nacting in representative capacity, the appointment was required to  be  made\nas a healthy convention.  The CJI was to be  appointed  by  seniority.   The\nsenior most judge, considered fit to hold the office, was to be the CJI.\n16.10 Conclusions in <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2403\">Third Judges\u2019</a> case in  para  44  reiterated  this  view\nwith only slight  modification.   On  that  basis,  memoranda  of  procedure\nmentioned in earlier  part  of  this  opinion  were  issued.   The  National\nCommission to Review the Working  of  the  Constitution  (NCRWC)  headed  by\nJustice M.N. Venkatachaliah, in its report dated 31st March, 2002,  observed\nthat appointment of judges was part of independence of  judiciary.   It  was\nobserved that the  Executive  taking  over  the  power  of  appointment  and\nplaying a dominant role  will  be  violative,  of  basic  structure  of  the\nConstitution, of independence of judiciary[744].</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"PetArg\" id=\"p_1452\">16.11 Contention of the petitioners is that the said decisions  conclusively\nrecognise primacy of judiciary in appointment of judges  inferred  from  the\nscheme of the Constitution and such primacy was part of basic structure.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1453\">16.12 It is submitted that if  the  Executive  has  primacy,  the  power  of\nappointment of Judges can be used to affect or subvert the  independence  of\nthe appointees as members of  the  Constitutional  Courts.   This  would  be\nagainst the intention of the Constitution makers.  The  unamended  provision\ncould not be replaced by the new mechanism unless the new mechanism  ensured\nthat a role of  the  Judiciary  was  not  decreased  and  the  role  of  the\nExecutive was not increased and the change made had  no  adverse  impact  on\nthe functioning of the Constitution.  If  this  contention  is  upheld,  the\nimpugned amendment will have to be struck down unless it could be held  that\nthe amended provisions also retained  the  said  primacy.    If  primacy  of\njudiciary is held not to be a part of basic structure  of  the  Constitution\nor it is held that the same is still retained, the amendment  will  have  to\nbe upheld.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1454\">C.    Plea of the Respondents for re-visiting earlier binding precedents\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1455\">17.   The correctness of the view taken in the above  decisions  was  sought\nto be challenged by learned counsel for the  respondents.    The  ground  on\nwhich  reconsideration  of  the  earlier  view  is  sought   is   that   the\ninterpretation in Second and  Third  Judges  cases  is  patently  erroneous.\nMembers of the Constituent Assembly never intended that the CJI should  have\nlast word on the subject of appointment  of  Judges.   The  text  which  was\nfinally approved and which became part of the Constitution did  not  provide\nfor concurrence of the CJI as has been <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2404\">laid down  by</a>  this  Court.    <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"4572688\" id=\"span_676\">It  is\nalso submitted that the interpretation taken by this  Court  may  have  been\njustified on account of the abuse  of  powers  by  the  Executive  specially\nduring emergency (as noticed in <a href=\"/doc/4572688/\" id=\"a_2405\">Union  of  India  vs.  Sankalchand  Himatlal\nSheth</a>[745]) and in the Law Commission Reports (particularly 14th  and  121st\nReports), the same situation  no  longer  continues.   More  over  there  is\nglobal trend for Judicial Appointment Commissions.  Even without primacy  of\nthe judiciary  in  appointment  of  judges,  the  judiciary  could  function\nindependently.  Judicial Appointment Commission was suggested even  earlier.\n The eminent jurists had criticized the existing mechanism  for  appointment\nof Judges and particularly the working of the collegium system.</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1456\">17.1  Referring to the scheme of Chapter IV  of  the  Constitution,  learned\nAttorney General submitted that Executive and the Legislature had  the  role\nin the working of the  judiciary.   Salary  and  Conditions  of  Service  of\nJudges are fixed by the  Parliament.   The  Rules  for  functioning  of  the\nSupreme Court are framed with the approval of the President and are  subject\nto the law made by the Parliament.  Parliament  could  confer  supplementary\npowers on  the  Supreme  Court.   Conditions  of  service  of  officers  and\nservants of the Supreme Court are subject to law  made  by  the  Parliament.\nThe rules framed by the CJI require approval of the  President.   There  was\ninter  play  of  Executive  and  Legislature  in  the  functioning  of   the\njudiciary.  Independence of judges was  in  respect  of  their  security  of\ntenure and  service  conditions.   Manner  of  appointment  did  not  affect\nindependence of judiciary.   Executive  appointing  Comptroller  General  of\nIndia or Election Commission did not affect their  independence.
