Power  of\nappointment of judges  is  the  Executive  power  to  be  exercised  by  the\nPresident with the advice of the Council  of  Ministers  after  consultation\nwith the judiciary.  The doctrine of separation of powers or  separation  of\njudiciary from Executive does not require that the Executive could  have  no\nrole in appointment of judges.   Primacy  of  judiciary  in  appointment  of\njudges ignores the principles of checks and  balances.   The  interpretation\nplaced in the earlier decisions ignores the principles of  transparency  and\naccountability.   Even  without  there  being  manifest  error  in   earlier\ndecisions, having regard to the sensitive nature of the issue and  also  the\nfact that an amendment has now been brought  about,  the  earlier  decisions\nneed to be revisited.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1457\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"PARTY\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_677\">17.2  The stand of  learned  Attorney  General  and  other  learned  counsel\nappearing for the respondents was  contested  by  learned  counsel  for  the\npetitioners.  It was submitted that all issues sought to be  raised  by  the\nrespondents were duly considered by the Bench of nine-judges.   The  Central\nGovernment sought opinion of this Court under <a href=\"/doc/210155/\" id=\"a_2406\">Article 143.</a>  A statement  was\nmade by the then learned Attorney General that the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2407\">Second Judges\u2019</a>  case  was\nnot sought to be reconsidered.</span>  <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"4572688\" id=\"span_678\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1382698\" id=\"span_679\">The view of the nine-Judge Bench  was  based\non earlier binding decisions in <a href=\"/doc/1382698/\" id=\"a_2408\">Shamsher Singh vs. State of Punjab</a>[746]  and\n<a href=\"/doc/4572688/\" id=\"a_2409\">Sankalchand</a> case (supra) laying down that the last word on such matters  was\nof the CJI.  The expert studies and the Constituent Assembly  Debates  ruled\nout pre-dominant role for the Executive or  Legislature  in  appointment  of\njudges.  The constitutional  scheme  did  not  permit  interference  of  the\nExecutive in appointment of judges.   The Executive  could  give  feed  back\nand carry out  the  Executive  functions  by  making  appointments  but  the\nproposal had to be initiated  and  finalised  by  the  judiciary.   Frequent\nreconsideration of  opinions  by  larger  Benches  of  this  Court  was  not\ndesirable in absence of any doubt  about  the  correctness  of  the  earlier\nview.</span></span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1458\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"4572688\" id=\"span_680\">17.3    Parameters for determining as  to  when  earlier  binding  decisions\nought to be reopened have been repeatedly <a href=\"/doc/4572688/\" id=\"a_2410\">laid  down  by</a>  this  Court.   The\nsettled principle is that court should not, except when it  is  demonstrated\nbeyond all reasonable doubts  that  its  previous  ruling  given  after  due\ndeliberation and full hearing was erroneous, revisit  earlier  decisions  so\nthat the law remains certain.[747] In  exceptional  circumstances  or  under\nnew set  of  conditions  in  the  light  of  new  ideas,  earlier  view,  if\nconsidered mistaken, can be reversed.  While march of law continues and  new\nsystems can be developed whenever needed, it can be  done  only  if  earlier\nsystems are considered unworkable[748].</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1459\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_681\">17.4  No such situation has arisen.  On  settled  principles,  no  case  for\nrevisiting earlier decisions by larger Benches is made out.  As regards  the\ncontention that there was patent error in the earlier decisions, the  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2411\">Second\nJudges\u2019</a> case shows that the Constituent Assembly  Debates  are  exhaustively\nquoted and considered.  Neither the debates nor  the  text  adopted  by  the\nConstitution show that the power of appointment of Judges  was  intended  to\nbe conferred on the Executive or the Legislature.  The  word  \u2018consultation\u2019\nas interpreted and understood meant that the final word on  the  subject  of\nappointment of Judges was with the CJI.  The practice  and  convention  ever\nsince  the  commencement  of  the  Constitution  showed  that  proposal  for\nappointment was always initiated by the Judiciary and the last word  on  the\nsubject belonged to the CJI.  This scheme was consistent with the  intention\nof the Constitution makers.</span>  <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_682\">All the points  now  sought  to  be  raised  by\nlearned Attorney General have been exhaustively  considered  in  the  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2412\">Second\nJudges</a>  case.   The  contention  that   earlier   situation   of   Executive\ninterference has now changed also does not justify  reconsideration  of  the\nearlier view.  If the situation has changed, there  can  be  no  reason  for\nchange of the system which is  functioning  as  per  the  intention  of  the\nConstitution makers when such change will be  contrary  to  basic  structure\nwhich  is  not  constitutionally   permissible.    The   objection   as   to\ndeficiencies in the working of the collegium system will be  subject  matter\nof discussion in the later part of this judgment.  Individual  failings  may\nnever be ruled out in functioning of any system.  The  Judicial  Appointment\nCommissions earlier considered were not  on  the  same  pattern.</span>   <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_683\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_684\">Initially\nproposal to set up Judicial Commission  was  made  prior  to  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2413\">Second  Judges</a>\ncase, with the object of doing away with the primacy  of  the  Executive  as\n<a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2414\">laid down in</a> <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2415\">First Judges</a> case.   In Sixty-Seventh Amendment  Bill,  in  the\nStatement of Objects and  Reasons,  it  is  mentioned  that  the  object  of\nsetting up of Commission was to \u2018obviate the criticism of  arbitrariness  on\nthe part of the Executive\u2019[749].  Ninety-Eighth  Amendment  Bill,  2003  was\nintroduced with  a  different  composition  on  recommendation  of  National\nCommission  to  review  the  working  of  the  Constitution.   One-Twentieth\nAmendment Bill, 2013 did not  provide  for  any  composition  and  left  the\ncomposition to  be  provided  for  by  the  Parliament.   Validity  of  such\nproposed Commissions was never tested as such Commissions  never  came  into\nexistence.</span></span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1460\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_685\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_686\">17.5    The Judicial  Commissions  in  other  countries  and  provisions  of\nConstitutions of other  countries  conferring  power  on  the  Executive  to\nappoint Judges may also not call for reconsideration of the  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2416\">Second  Judges\u2019</a>\ncase as many of such and similar provisions  were  duly  considered  in  the\n<a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2417\">Second Judges\u2019</a> case to which reference will be made.  No case is  thus  made\nout for revisiting the earlier decisions in Second and Third Judges\u2019 cases.</span></span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1461\">D.    Consequential consideration  of  issue  of  primacy  of  judiciary  in\nappointment of judges as part of basic structure.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1462\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_687\">18.   The earlier decisions in <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2418\">Second and Third Judges\u2019</a>  case   have  to  be\ntaken as binding precedents.  Once it is so, it has to be held that  primacy\nof the judiciary in appointment of judges is part of  the  basic  structure.\nAppointment of judges is part of independence  of  judiciary.   It  is  also\nessential to uphold balance of powers  between  Legislature,  Executive  and\nJudiciary  which  by  itself  is  key  to  the  functioning  of  the  entire\nConstitution.  The judiciary is entrusted the power to control the power  of\nthe Executive and the Legislature whenever  it  is  alleged  that  the  said\norgans have exceeded their constitutionally assigned  authority.    This  is\nthe  essence  of  the  democracy.   Learned  counsel  for  the   petitioners\nhighlighted that at times exercise of  powers  of  Judicial  Review  by  the\nConstitutional Courts may not be to the  liking  of  the  Executive  or  the\nLegislature.</span>  <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"554839\" id=\"span_688\">Particular instances have been  given  of  decisions  of  this\nCourt in <a href=\"/doc/554839/\" id=\"a_2419\">2G Spectrum</a> case[750] and Coal Scam case[751] where actions of  the\nExecutive were found to be violative of constitutional  obligations  causing\nhuge  loss  to  public  exchequer.   It   was   submitted   that   arbitrary\ndistribution  of  State  largess  by  way  of  giving  scarce  resources  or\ncontracts or jobs or positions of importance akin  to  \u2018spoil  system\u2019  have\nbeen held by this Court to be in violation of  the  Constitution.   Policies\nof  the  State  for  arbitrary  acquisition  of  land  or  in  violation  of\nenvironmental laws have been struck down  by  this  Court.   Dissolution  of\nState Assemblies and dismissal of State Governments have  also  been  struck\ndown by this Court[752].  This Court  also  had  to  deal  with  the  issues\narising out of  decisions  of  Speakers  in  recognizing  or  otherwise  the\ndefections in Central or  State  Legislatures[753].   There  are  enumerable\ninstances when the Courts have to  deal  with  validity  of  Legislative  or\nExecutive decisions of far reaching nature.  It is the faith of  the  people\nin the impartiality and competence of judiciary  which  sustains  democracy.\nIf appointment of judges, which is integral to functioning of  judiciary  is\ninfluenced  or  controlled  by  the  Executive,  it  will  certainly  affect\nimpartiality  of  judges  and  their  functioning.  Faith   of   people   in\nimpartiality  and   effectiveness   of   judiciary   in   protecting   their\nconstitutional rights will be eroded.</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1463\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Neg\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_689\">18.1  Submissions of learned Attorney General are that even  if  appointment\nof judges is held to be part of  independence  of  judiciary,  choice  of  a\nparticular model is not part of basic structure.  The role of the  Executive\ncannot be denied altogether nor there can be any  objection  to  members  of\ncivil society being included in the process of appointment.  The primacy  of\njudiciary in appointment of judges is not an absolutist ideal.     Power  of\nappointment has to be seen in the light of need  for  checks  and  balances.\nIndependence of judiciary is not a uni- dimensional  test.  There  could  be\ninter mingling of other wings in  the  process  of  appointment  of  judges.\nAfter repeal of <a href=\"/doc/1164880/\" id=\"a_2420\">Articles 124</a> and <a href=\"/doc/1682952/\" id=\"a_2421\">217</a>, basis of <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2422\">Second Judges\u2019</a> case  did  not\nsurvive. Primacy of judiciary in  appointment  of  judges  is  only  in  the\ncontext of stopping wrong appointment or  preventing  pre-dominance  of  the\nExecutive.  Even if primacy of judiciary was recognized at a given point  of\ntime, the same could apply only till  the  Constitution  is  amended.    Two\neminent persons could be laymen  to  give  societal  view  point.   The  Law\nMinister was  made  a  member  of  the  Commission  for  accountability  and\ntransparency.</span> <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"322504\" id=\"span_690\">As <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2423\">laid down in</a> <a href=\"/doc/322504/\" id=\"a_2424\">I.R. Coelho</a> case,  inspite  of  separation  of\npowers,  different  branches  of  the  Government  could  have   overlapping\nfunctions[754].</span>  <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"189292592\" id=\"span_691\">In <a href=\"/doc/189292592/\" id=\"a_2425\">Sahara India Real Estate Corpn. Ltd. vs.  SEBI</a>[755],  it\nwas observed that under the Constitution there are  different  values  which\nmust be balanced.  Thus, independence of  judiciary,  checks  and  balances,\ndemocracy and separation of powers are to be  considered  as  a  whole.   He\nreferred to the background of supersession of judges in the  year  1973  and\n1977 and selective transfer of judges during emergency  as  noted  in  121st\nReport of the  Law  Commission[756].   The  report  records  that  in  1976,\nsixteen judges were transferred from the respective  High  Courts  in  which\nthey were functioning to other High Courts.  This was  perceived  to  be  an\nact of interference with the judiciary.</span>   <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_692\">Circular of the then Law  Minister\nproviding for transfer and short term appointment of  judges  considered  in\n<a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2426\">First  Judges\u2019</a>  case  was  taken  in  the  said  report  as  the   executive\ninterference.   The  report  also  mentioned  the  concern  arising  out  of\nsupersession in appointment of CJI, non confirmation of  additional  judges,\ntransfer of judges giving rise to apprehension of  erosion  of  independence\nof judiciary at the hands of the  Executive.   It  was  concluded  that  the\nmodel then prevalent (with the primacy  of  the  Executive)  had  failed  to\ndeliver the goods.  This led to introduction of 67th Amendment Bill, 1990.</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1464\">18.2  The contentions of learned Attorney General cannot be  accepted.   The\nmatter having been gone into in great details in  above  binding  precedents\nwhich do not require reconsideration, I do  not  consider  it  necessary  to\nrepeat in detail  the  discussion  which  has  been  recorded  in  the  said\ndecisions.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1465\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_693\">18.3        In <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2427\">Second Judges\u2019</a> case, following findings have been recorded :</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_917\">(i)   The word \u2018consultation\u2019 used in <a href=\"/doc/1164880/\" id=\"a_2428\">Articles  124</a><a href=\"/doc/1682952/\" id=\"a_2429\">,  217</a>  and  <a href=\"/doc/819787/\" id=\"a_2430\">222</a>  of  the\nConstitution meant  that  the  opinion  of  consultee  was  normally  to  be\naccepted thereby according primacy to the judiciary;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1466\">The  Executive  being  major  litigant  and  role  of  judiciary  being   to\nimpartially decide disputes between citizen and  the  State,  the  Executive\ncould not have decisive say in appointing judges;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1467\">Doctrine of separation of powers under the Constitution required primacy  of\njudiciary in appointing judges;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1468\">Since traits of candidates could be better assessed by  the  Chief  Justice,\nthe view of the Chief Justice as to suitability and merit of  the  candidate\nhad higher weight;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1469\">The Chief Justice of India was not to  make  a  recommendation  individually\nbut as representing the judiciary in the manner laid down,  that  is,  after\nconsulting the collegium; and\n\nPrimacy of judiciary in appointment of judges is  part  of  independence  of\njudiciary and separation of powers under the Constitution.\n</p>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1470\">18.4        Referring to  the  constitutional  scheme,  its  background  and\ninterpretation,  irrespective  of  the  literal  meaning  of  the   language\nemployed in <a href=\"/doc/1123514/\" id=\"a_2431\">Articles 214</a> and <a href=\"/doc/1682952/\" id=\"a_2432\">217</a> of the Constitution, it was  observed  that\ninitiation of proposal must always emanate from the  Chief  Justice  of  the\nHigh Court/CJI (in representative capacity as laid down) and  last  word  on\nany objection thereto should be normally of the CJI.[757]\n\n18.5   Reference was made to the interpretation of the  word  \u2018consultation\u2019\nin the context of appointment of judges  in  earlier  judgments  in  Chandra\nMoulishwar Prasad vs. Patna High Court[758], Shamsher Singh and  Sankalchand\ncases.  It was held that \u201cin practice,  the  last  word  in  such  sensitive\nsubject must belong to CJI, the rejection of  his  advice  being  ordinarily\nregarded as prompted by oblique considerations vitiating the order.\u201d\n\n18.6        Reference was also made to the statement of  Dr.  Ambedkar  that\nit was dangerous to give power to appoint judges to the  Executive  or  with\nconcurrence  of  the  Legislature.[759]   Further  statement  that  it   was\ndangerous to give veto power to CJI was explained to mean that the CJI  must\nact not in individual capacity but after consulting senior judges.[760]\n\n18.7        Needless to say  that  the  Constitution  of  India  is  unique.\nWhile  reference  to  other  Constitutions   can   be   made   for   certain\npurposes[761], the basic features  of  Indian  Constitution  (which  may  be\ndistinctly different from other  Constitutions)  have  to  be  retained  and\ncannot be given a go bye.  In the above judgment, in the context of  working\nof Indian  Constitution,  it  was  held  that  the  role  of  Executive  and\nLegislature in appointment of judges could not be predominant.  Even in  the\nConstituent Assembly, models  of  other  countries  were  not  found  to  be\nsuitable  to  be  followed  in  India[762].    As  already   mentioned   the\nGovernment of India appointed First  Law  Commission  headed  by  Shri  M.C.\nStealvad to review  the  system  of  judicial  administration  and  all  its\naspects.   The   Commission   expressly   mentioned   that   the   Executive\ninterference  in  appointment  of  Judges  has   not   been   congenial   to\nindependence of judiciary.  The Commission noted that  the  Chief  Ministers\nwere having direct or indirect hand in appointment of Judges  which  results\nin appointments being made not on merit but on considerations of  community,\ncaste, political affiliations.   The  Chief  Minister  holding  a  political\noffice  is  dependent  on  the  goodwill  of  his  party   followers.    The\nrecommendation of the CJI is more likely to be on merit.  An  opinion  noted\nin the report mentions that if the  Executive  continued  to  have  powerful\nrole, the independence of judiciary will disappear and the  Courts  will  be\nfilled with Judges who owe from appointments to politicians[763].    It  was\nrecommended that the hands of CJI should  be  strengthened  and  instead  of\nrequiring consultation it should require  recommendation  by  the  CJI[764].\nThere should be requirement of concurrence of the CJI[765].  The Report  was\ndiscussed in the Parliament and the then Home  Minister  declared  that  the\nExecutive  was  only  an  order  issuing  authority  and  appointments  were\nvirtually being made by the CJI.  This statement was reiterated by the  then\nLaw Minister[766].  Again in 121st Report, it was observed that  appointment\nof Judges with Executive influence was not conducive to  healthy  growth  of\njudicial review.  Trends all over the  world  indicate  that  power  of  the\nExecutive in appointment of Judges was required  to  be  diluted[767].   <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_694\"><a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2433\">The\nSecond Judges\u2019</a> case took care of  the  ground  realities  in  the  light  of\nconstitutional convention.  It held that the  CJI  was  better  equipped  to\nselect the best and for appointments being free  from  Executive  domination\nto inspire  public  confidence  in  impartiality  and  consistent  with  the\nprinciple of separation of Judiciary  from  Executive  and  also  consistent\nwith the spirit of  Constitution  makers.   The  principle  of  primacy  was\nrecognised and appointment  of  Judges  was  held  to  be  integral  to  the\nindependence of judiciary[768].  To check arbitrary  exercise  of  power  by\nany individual, it was  made  mandatory  that  the  Chief  Justices  consult\nsenior Judges.  Thus, primacy of  judiciary  was  recognized  in  initiating\nproposal as well as in taking final decision[769].   However,  participation\nof the Executive in giving inputs by suggesting names  before  the  proposal\nwas initiated or giving feedback even after the proposal was  initiated  was\npermissible.  It was noted that  right from beginning of  the  Constitution,\nall the proposals for  appointments  were  always  initiated  by  the  Chief\nJustices[770].</span>  <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_695\">View in <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2434\">First Judges\u2019</a> case that primacy  in  appointment  of\nJudges was of the Central Government was held  to  be  erroneous  by  larger\nBench inter alia for following reasons :</span>\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_918\">(i)   The judiciary has apolitical commitment and if  power  of  appointment\nof judges is given to  the  Executive,  this  will  affect  independence  of\njudiciary[771];\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_919\">(ii)  Rule of law  requires  that  justice  is  impartial  and  people  have\nconfidence in judiciary being separate and independent of the  Executive  so\nthat  it  can  discharge  its  functions  of  keeping  vigilant  watch   for\nprotection of rights even against the Executive[772];\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1471\">Judiciary has key role in working of the democracy  and  for  upholding  the\nrule of law[773];\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1472\">The constitutional scheme provides for mandatory consultation with  the  CJI\nsince the CJI was better equipped to  assess  the  merit  of  the  candidate\nwhich  consultation  was  not  provided  for  in  respect  of   other   high\nconstitutional appointments[774].\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1473\">The appointment of judges was inextricably linked with the  independence  of\njudiciary and even in the matter of  appointment  of  district  judges,  the\nconclusive say was of the High Courts and not of the Government[775].\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1474\">Even in countries  where  power  of  appointment  of  judges  was  with  the\nExecutive,  there  is  demand/proposal  for  minimizing  the  role  of   the\nExecutive[776].\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1475\">The effort of the Executive to have say in appointment of judges  was  found\nby  expert  studies  to  be   not   congenial   to   the   independence   of\njudiciary[777].   Reference  was  made  to  the  14th  Report  of  the   Law\nCommission that if the  Executive  had  powerful  voice  in  appointment  of\njudges, the independence of judiciary will disappear and the courts will  be\nfilled  with  judges  who  owe  their  appointments  to   the   politicians.\nReference was also made to 121st Report of the Law Commission to the  effect\nthat even in UK there was thinking to create a check on  the  power  of  the\nExecutive to select and appoint judges.\n</p>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1476\">Consultation with the CJI was not envisaged by the  Constitution  makers  to\nbe of formal nature but implied that great weight was to be  given  so  that\nthe last word belonged to the CJI[778].\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1477\"><a href=\"/doc/192717/\" id=\"a_2435\">Article 50</a> and the background of its enactment spells out  the  mandate  for\nappointment  of  judges  being  taken  away  from  the  Executive  and   its\ntransference to the judiciary.[779]\n\n\n\n18.8       In the above  background,  the  forceful  contention  of  learned\nAttorney General that the  scheme  of  the  Constitution  did  not  envisage\nprimacy of judiciary but  only  mandatory  consultation  with  the  CJI  and\noptional  consideration  with  such  other  judges  as  may  be   considered\nnecessary cannot be accepted, even if it is  so  suggested  by  the  literal\nmeaning of the words  used  in  the  text  of  the  provision.   It  may  be\nmentioned that the word \u2018consultation\u2019, on account  of  the  scheme  of  the\nConstitution,  was  held  to  carry  special   meaning,   on   a   purposive\ninterpretation.  The  interpretation  was  not  based  solely  on  the  word\n\u2018consultation\u2019 but on scheme of independence of judiciary.   The  contention\nthat independence of judiciary was not  affected  even  when  the  Executive\nmade the appointment is contrary to the expert studies and  well  considered\ndecisions of this Court.  The acknowledged scheme of  the  Constitution  and\nits working is not to allow domination of the Executive  in  appointment  of\nJudges.  Such domination affects independence of judiciary, public faith  in\nits  impartiality  (when  the  Government  is  major  litigant),  brings  in\nextraneous considerations, compromises  merit,  weakens  the  principles  of\nchecks and balances and separation of judiciary from the  Executive.   Thus,\nby substitution of the words, the Parliament could not  interfere  with  the\nprimacy of judiciary in appointment of judges  and  thereby  interfere  with\nthe basic feature of the Constitution.  It may  be  mentioned  that  use  of\nsimilar expression in <a href=\"/doc/127060/\" id=\"a_2436\">Article 74</a> of  the  Constitution  in  the  context  of\nExecutive power of the President to act on \u201caid and advice\u201d  of  Council  of\nMinisters was held to mean that the President was only a  formal  head.[780]\nIt  cannot  be  suggested  that  by  amendment  of  the   expression   used,\nconstitutional scheme of the President being formal head can be  changed  as\nsuch  amendment  will  be  repugnant  to  the   basic   structure   of   the\nConstitution.   Likewise,  even  by  amendment  primacy  of   judiciary   in\nappointment of judges cannot be excluded.  Such primacy existed  not  merely\nby word \u2018consultation\u2019 but by virtue of role of judiciary in working of  the\nConstitution, by CJI being better suited to assess merit  of  the  candidate\nand on account of Executive being major litigant.  There  is  no  change  in\nthese factors even after amendment. It is not thus a question of  change  of\nmodel or of available choice with the Parliament.  Plea  of  presumption  of\nconstitutionality can be of no avail where an established basic  feature  of\nthe Constitution  is  sought  to  be  damaged.   Similarly,  the  plea  that\nParliament is best equipped to assess the needs of the people is not  enough\nreason to extend the power of Parliament to amend the basic feature  of  the\nConstitution.  The change of time does not  justify  greater  role  for  the\nExecutive in appointment  of  judges.   The  plea  of  overlapping  role  of\ndifferent Departments of the Government is against the  basic  structure  as\nfar as appointment of judges is concerned.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1478\">18.9        While it is true that the Legislature can  even  retrospectively\nclarify its intention and thereby bring about a change in law[781],  in  the\npresent context meaning of the unamended provision was not based  merely  on\nthe words used but also the entire scheme of the  Constitution  particularly\nthe independence of judiciary.  It has been held that in the context of  the\nIndian Constitution, having regard to the consistent past  practice  and  to\navoid political interference in appointment of judges, and also  on  account\nof the CJI/CJ being better equipped to assess  the  merit  of  a  candidate,\nproposal must always be initiated by the CJI/CJ and the CJI must  also  have\nfinal word on the subject.  It can hardly be doubted that  the  Constitution\nis a dynamic document and has to be interpreted to meet the  felt  needs  of\ntimes and cannot bind all future generations.  At the same time, it is  also\nnow well settled that the amending power is  limited  to  non  essential/non\nbasic features and does not  extend  to  altering  the  basic  features  and\nframework of the Constitution.  Primacy of judiciary is certainly a part  of\nthe basic feature of the Constitution.   If  primacy  of  judiciary  in  the\nappointment of judges is held to  be  not  a  part  of  basic  feature,  the\nParliament may be free to confer the said power  on  the  Executive  or  the\nLegislature or to any other authority which  can  certainly  compromise  the\nindependence of judiciary.  It will also in turn  disturb  the  doctrine  of\nseparation of powers and other basic features like rule  of  law,  democracy\nand federalism and working of the Constitution as a whole.  Independence  of\njudiciary is key element in the entire functioning of the  Constitution  and\nsuch independence is integrally linked with the appointment of  judges  free\nfrom Executive interference.  The alternative submission of Shri  Venugopal,\nlearned senior counsel appearing for the State of Madhya Pradesh in Paras  4\nand 8 (reproduced in para 13  above)   also  supports  the  conclusion  that\nappointment of judges is part of independence of judiciary  and  primacy  of\njudiciary in appointment of judges is required to be  retained.   The  power\nof appointment of judges cannot be exercised by the Executive  as  the  same\nwill affect independence of judiciary.  Even after the  original  provisions\nare amended, this principle is still applicable.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1479\">18.10   At this stage, it may be mentioned that  any  perceived  shortcoming\nin the working of existing mechanism of  appointment  of  judges  cannot  by\nitself justify alteration or damage of the existing scheme once it  is  held\nto be part of basic feature. As Dr. Ambedkar observed[782] :-\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_920\">\u201cThe Constitution  can  provide  only  the  organs  of  State  such  as  the\nLegislature, the Executive and the  Judiciary.  The  factors  on  which  the\nworking of those  organs  of  the  State  depend  are  the  people  and  the\npolitical parties they will set up as their instruments to carry  out  their\nwishes and their politics.\u201d\n\nTo the same effect Dr. Rajendra Prasad* said :-\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_921\">\u201cIf the people who  are  elected  are  capable  and  men  of  character  and\nintegrity, they would  be  able  to  make  the  best  even  of  a  defective\nConstitution. If they are lacking in these,  the  Constitution  cannot  help\nthe country. After all, a Constitution like a machine is a  lifeless  thing.\nIt acquires life because of the men who  control  it  and  operate  it,  and\nIndia needs today nothing more than a set of honest men who  will  have  the\ninterest of the country before them.\u201d\n\n\nEven a good system may have  shortcomings  in  its  working  on  account  of\nindividual failures.  It may be mentioned that criticism of working  may  be\nleveled against working of every organ of  the  Constitution  including  the\nExecutive and the Legislature and while all  efforts  must  be  continuously\nmade to bring about improvement in every sphere, the basic scheme set up  by\nthe Constitution cannot be given a  go  bye  on  that  ground.   It  is  not\nnecessary to comment upon how good or  bad  any  constitutional  authorities\nhave performed in  discharge  of  their  duties  or  how  good  or  bad  the\njudiciary has performed, as the limited question for  consideration  of  the\nCourt is to identify and retain the basic structure of the  Constitution  in\nappointment of judges.  The improvement in working  of  existing  system  of\nappointment of judges can be the subject matter  of  separate  consideration\nwhich is being proposed but certainly without giving a go bye to  the  basic\nfeatures of the Constitution of independence of judiciary.  <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"199141576\" id=\"span_696\">In <a href=\"/doc/199141576/\" id=\"a_2437\">Manoj  Narula\nvs. Union of India</a>[783],  question considered was how persons with  criminal\nantecedents could be prevented from being  appointed  as  Ministers.   There\nwas also reference to the concern as to how persons  with  such  antecedents\ncould be prevented from being legislators.   This Court held that the  issue\nhas to be dealt with by those to whom the  Constitution  has  entrusted  the\nresponsibility and this Court could only enforce the constitutional scheme.</span>\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_922\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"257876\" id=\"span_697\">18.11   At this stage, it  may  be  mentioned  that  the  claim  of  learned\nAttorney General that the Parliament represented the will of the  people  or\nthat the amendment represented the  will  of  the  people  and  interference\ntherewith  will  be  undesirable  is  contrary  to  the  law  <a href=\"/doc/199141576/\" id=\"a_2438\">laid  down  in</a>\n<a href=\"/doc/257876/\" id=\"a_2439\">Kesavananda Bharti</a> case  (supra)[784].   The  will  of  the  people  is  the\nConstitution while the Parliament represents the will of the majority  at  a\ngiven point of time which is subordinate to the Constitution, that  is,  the\nwill of the people.   The Constitution was supreme and even  Parliament  has\nno unlimited amending power.  Learned  Attorney  General  rightly  submitted\nthat the last word on the  validity of  a  constitutional  amendment  is  of\nthis Court.  Even if the judiciary is not an  elected  body,  it  discharges\nthe constitutional functions as per the will of the people reflected in  the\nConstitution  and  the  task  of   determining   the   powers   of   various\nconstitutional organs is entrusted to the judiciary[785].\nConclusion:</span>\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_923\">18.12 Accordingly, I hold that primacy of judiciary and limited role of  the\nExecutive in appointment of judges is part of the  basic  structure  of  the\nConstitution.  The primacy of judiciary is  in  initiating  a  proposal  and\nfinalising the same.  The  CJI  has  the  last  word  in  the  matter.   The\nExecutive is at liberty to give suggestions prior to initiation of  proposal\nand to  give  feedback  on  character  and  antecedents  of  the  candidates\nproposed and object to the appointment for  disclosed  reasons  as  held  in\nSecond and Third Judges\u2019 cases.\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1480\">E.    Whether the Impugned Amendment alters or damages the basic structure\n\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1481\">19.   In the above  background,  the  only  question  which  remains  to  be\nconsidered is whether under the impugned  amendment  the  basic  feature  of\nprimacy of judiciary in appointment of judges has been altered  or  damaged.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1482\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"257876\" id=\"span_698\">19.1  Learned Attorney General  submitted  that  basic  structure  comprises\nmany features like several pillars  in  a  foundation,  some  of  which  are\nenumerated in opinions rendered in <a href=\"/doc/257876/\" id=\"a_2440\">Kesavananda Bharti</a> case.  In judging  the\nvalidity of a constitutional amendment, test is whether the amendment  would\nlead to collapse of the Constitution. Merely affecting or impinging upon  an\nArticle embodying a feature that is part of  the  basic  structure  was  not\nsufficient to declare an  amendment  unconstitutional.  Violation  of  basic\nstructure of the constitution must be such that the structure  itself  would\ncollapse.</span> <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"PARTY\" data-docid=\"693176\" id=\"span_699\">He also relied upon the observations in <a href=\"/doc/693176/\" id=\"a_2441\">Bhim Singh  Ji  vs.  Union\nof India</a>[786] particularly the following observations :</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_924\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"63489929\" id=\"span_700\">\u201cTherefore, what is a betrayal of the basic feature is not a mere  violation\nof <a href=\"/doc/367586/\" id=\"a_2442\">Article 14</a> but a shocking, unconscionable  or  unscrupulous  travesty  of\nthe quintessence of equal justice. If a legislation  does  go  that  far  it\nshakes the democratic foundation and must suffer the death penalty.  But  to\npermit the Bharati8 ghost to haunt the corridors of  the  court  brandishing\nfatal writs for every feature of  inequality  is  judicial  paralysation  of\nparliamentary function. Nor  can  the  constitutional  fascination  for  the\nbasic structure doctrine be made a trojan  horse  to  penetrate  the  entire\nlegislative camp fighting for a  new  social  order  and  to  overpower  the\nbattle for abolition of basic poverty by the \u2018basic structure\u2019 missile.\u201d\n\nand following observations in <a href=\"/doc/63489929/\" id=\"a_2443\">Ashoka Kumar Thakur vs. Union of India</a>[787]:-</span>\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_925\">\u201cThere are large number of provisions in the Constitution dealing  with  the\nfederal character of the Constitution. If  any  one  of  the  provisions  is\naltered or modified, that does not amount to the  alteration  of  the  basic\nstructure of the Constitution. Various fundamental rights are given  in  the\nConstitution dealing with various aspects of human  life.  The  Constitution\nitself sets out principles for an  expanding  future  and  is  obligated  to\nendure for future ages to come and consequently it has to be adapted to  the\nvarious changes that may take place in human affairs.\u201d\n\n19.2. Applying the above  tests  it  was  submitted  that  the  Ninety-Ninth\nAmendment was consonant with and strengthens the independence  of  judiciary\nwhile upholding the democracy, rule of law and checks and balances. NJAC  is\nin sync with the needs of time and is modelled on  checks  and  balances  to\nensure a democratic process with plurality of views. NJAC dilutes  power  of\nexecutive in favour of the judiciary. He submitted that  identity  test  was\nrequired to be applied which means that  after  the  amendment  the  amended\nConstitution loses the identity of the original Constitution.  There  is  no\nbar to making changes and to adopt the Constitution to the  requirements  of\nchanging times  without  touching  the  foundation  or  altering  the  basic\nconstitutional pattern.  He further relied  upon  the  observations  in  the\nIndira Gandhi and Minerva Mills Ltd. cases (supra).\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_926\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_701\">19.3  The learned Attorney General further submitted that the object of  the\namendment is to broad  base  the  collegiate  body  so  as  to  provide  for\nparticipatory and collective role to the judiciary, the  executive  and  the\ncivil society. The executive has only one  member,  the  Law  Minister.  The\nobject of having the Law  Minister  is  to  provide  information  about  the\ncandidates which information the other members may  not  have.  The  eminent\npersons will be independently appointed by a  committee  comprising  of  the\nPM, the CJI  and  the  Leader  of  Opposition.  In  this  way  there  is  no\nabrogation of independence of judiciary. Moreover, three of the six  members\nare from the judiciary and thus, the right to reject was  available  to  the\njudges, while the executive alone cannot exercise the right to reject.  Even\nin <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2444\">Second Judges\u2019</a> case it is observed that the process of appointment  is  a\nparticipatory process. An area relating to suitability  of  candidates  such\nas his antecedents and personal character may be better known to  consultees\nother than the CJI. The expression, \u2018eminent person\u2019 is well  known  and  it\nmeans distinguished in character or attainments or by success  in  any  walk\nof  life.  The  expression  \u2018distinguished\u2019  is  used  in  <a href=\"/doc/165526341/\" id=\"a_2445\">Article  124  (3)</a>\nproviding for eligibility criteria for judges of the  Supreme  Court.  Since\nthe high  powered  committee  comprising  three  high  functionaries  is  to\nappoint an  eminent  person,  there  is  sufficient  safeguard  against  any\nuncanalised power. The principles of constitutional trust apply to the  high\npowered committee which can  be  trusted  to  select  the  most  appropriate\npersons. Such eminent persons shall provide inputs for the  qualities  which\nmake a person suitable for appointment as a judge. Diversity in  composition\nof the Commission will mitigate the danger  of  cloning.   In  other  bodies\nalso there are provisions for non judges.  For example, <a href=\"/doc/48103131/\" id=\"a_2446\">Consumer  Protection\nAct</a>.  Reservation  in  favour  of  minorities,  women,   Scheduled   Castes,\nScheduled Tribes and OBC will have the effect of sensitizing  other  members\nfor the problems to be faced by  these  sections.  Even  in  the  report  of\nNational Commission to Review the Working of the Constitution (NCRWC),  also\nknown as Venkatachalliah Commission, a provision for an eminent  person  was\nmade without prescribing any criteria. The eminent person will be guided  by\nthe CJI, who will be the Chairman and best placed to access the legal  merit\nof the candidates. The executive is a key stake holder in  justice  delivery\nsystem for which it is accountable  to  the  Parliament  and  it  cannot  be\ndenied  role  in  appointment  of  judges.  Mere  possibility  of  abuse  of\nprovision cannot be a ground for holding a provision unreasonable.</span>  Reliance\nhas been placed on Mafat Lal Industries Ltd. vs. Union of  India[788]  which\nreads as under :-\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_927\">\u201cTo the same effect are  the  observations  by  Khanna,  J.  in  Kesavananda\nBharati v. State of Kerala (SCR at p. 755 : SCC p. 669). The  learned  Judge\nsaid: (SCC p. 821, para 1535)\n\u201cIn exercising the power of judicial review, the Courts cannot be  oblivious\nof the practical needs of the government. The door has to be left  open  for\ntrial and error. Constitutional law like other mortal  contrivances  has  to\ntake some chances. Opportunity must be allowed  for  vindicating  reasonable\nbelief by experience.\u201d\n\nTo  the  same  effect  are  the  observations  in  T.N.   Education   Deptt.\nMinisterial and General Subordinate Services Assn. v. State of T.N.  [(1980)\n3 SCC 97] (SCR at p. 1031) (Krishna Iyer, J.). It  is  equally  well-settled\nthat mere possibility of  abuse  of  a  provision  by  those  in  charge  of\nadministering it cannot be a ground for holding the  provision  procedurally\nor substantively unreasonable. <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"174974\" id=\"span_702\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1193965\" id=\"span_703\">In <a href=\"/doc/1193965/\" id=\"a_2447\">Collector of Customs v. Nathella  Sampathu\nChetty</a> [ 1962 (3) SCR 786], this Court observed: \u201cThe possibility  of  abuse\nof a  statute  otherwise  valid  does  not  impart  to  it  any  element  of\ninvalidity.\u201d It was said in <a href=\"/doc/174974/\" id=\"a_2448\">State of Rajasthan v. Union of India</a>  [(1977)  3\nSCC 592]  (SCR at p. 77), \u201cit must be remembered that merely  because  power\nmay sometimes be abused, it is  no  ground  for  denying  the  existence  of\npower. The wisdom of man has not yet been able to conceive of  a  government\nwith power sufficient to answer all its legitimate needs  and  at  the  same\ntime incapable of mischief\u201d.</span></span> (Also see Commr.,  H.R.E.  v.  Sri  Lakshmindra\nThirtha Swamiar of Sri Shirur Mutt [ (1954) SCR 1005] (SCR at p. 1030).\u201d\n\nTransparency and accountability in the matter of appointment  are  essential\nfor public confidence in the judiciary. <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"753943\" id=\"span_704\">In  this  connection  reference  has\nbeen made to <a href=\"/doc/753943/\" id=\"a_2449\">Inderpreet Singh Kahlon  vs. State of Punjab</a>[789]  which  reads\nas under :-</span>\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_928\">\u201cThis unfortunate  episode  teaches  us  an  important  lesson  that  before\nappointing the constitutional authorities, there should be  a  thorough  and\nmeticulous inquiry and scrutiny regarding their antecedents.  Integrity  and\nmerit have to be properly considered and evaluated in  the  appointments  to\nsuch high positions. It  is  an  urgent  need  of  the  hour  that  in  such\nappointments  absolute  transparency  is  required  to  be  maintained   and\ndemonstrated. The impact of the deeds and  misdeeds  of  the  constitutional\nauthorities (who are highly placed), affect a very large  number  of  people\nfor a very long time, therefore,  it  is  absolutely  imperative  that  only\npeople of high integrity, merit, rectitude  and  honesty  are  appointed  to\nthese constitutional positions.\u201d\n\n19.4  These submissions cannot be accepted.  It is obvious that\npre-dominant role of the judiciary,  as  it  exists  in  light  of  original\nConstitutional  scheme  in  taking  a  final  decision  on  the   issue   of\nappointment of judges of the Supreme Court and appointment and  transfer  of\njudges of the High Courts, has been given a go bye.    Under  the  unamended\nscheme  of  appointment  of  judges,  which  is  a  basic  feature  of   the\nConstitution, the President is to make appointment, after consultation  with\nthe CJI representing the judiciary.  Disregarding the views of  the  CJI  is\npermissible in exceptional situations for recorded  reasons  having  bearing\non character and antecedents of a candidate and if such  reasons  are  found\nto be acceptable to the CJI.  Under the amended scheme, no such  final  view\ncan be taken by the CJI.  Without giving any reason,  the  Minister  or  the\nnominated members can reject  the  unanimous  view  of  the  judges.   Chief\nJustice of the High Court is not a member  of  the  Commission  and  has  no\nConstitutional role in  appointment/transfer  of  the  judges  of  the  High\nCourts.  Mere fact that without the judges, the Minister and  the  nominated\nmembers cannot make an appointment is not at par with the situation where  a\ndecision itself is  taken  by  the  CJI  representing  the  judiciary.   The\nConstitutional power of the Chief Justice of  the  High  Court  to  initiate\nproposal for appointment as judge of the  High  Court  has  been  done  away\nwith, at least as far as the Constitutional provisions are concerned.\n19.5  The contention that the  amendment  strengthens  the  independence  of\njudiciary or the democracy or brings about  transparency  or  accountability\nis not shown to be  based  on  any  logic  beyond  the  words.  Even  if  in\nappointing two eminent members CJI is also a member of  the  Committee,  the\nfact remains that the PM and the Leader of the Opposition  have  significant\nrole in appointing such members, who will have power not only equal  to  the\nCJI and two senior most judges of the Supreme Court  in  making  appointment\nof judges of the Supreme Court and appointment/transfer  of  judges  of  the\nHigh Courts but also right to reject the unanimous proposal of the  CJI  and\nthe two senior most judges.  Such composition of the  Commission  cannot  be\nheld to be conducive to  the  independence  of  judiciary.   Appointment  of\njudges of the Supreme Court and appointment/transfer of judges of  the  High\nCourts, can certainly be influenced to a great extent by  the  Law  Minister\nand two nominated members, thereby affecting the independence of judiciary.\n19.6  Contention of learned Attorney General that  there  is  a  presumption\nthat the Law Minister and the  nominated  members  will  conduct  themselves\nindependently and will make value addition in  selecting  the  judges  in  a\nbetter way cannot be accepted.  The views of  the  Constitution  makers  and\neminent expert committees  clearly  show  that  role  of  the  Executive  in\nappointment of judges has to be minimum and by and large  limited  to  check\nthe character and antecedents of the candidates and not  to  finally  assess\nthe merit and suitability of such candidates.  In this view of  the  matter,\neven if the contention that no  guideline  was  required  for  criteria  for\nappointment of eminent persons when the Committee will be comprised of  high\ndignitaries is accepted the fact remains that such  persons  will  play  not\nmerely supporting but pre-dominant role  in  appointing  Supreme  Court  and\nHigh Court judges which  will  not  be  congenial  to  the  independence  of\njudiciary.  There is  no  justification  for  reservation  for  one  of  the\nnominated members  being  from  specified  categories.   Such  provision  is\nagainst the scheme of  the  Constitution  and  contrary  to  the  object  of\nselecting judges purely by  merit.   The  nature  of  appointment  does  not\njustify  any  affirmative  action  for  advancement  of  any  socially   and\neducationally backward classes or for  the  Scheduled  Castes  or  Scheduled\nTribes or women.  The appointment of judges  has  to  be  on  evaluation  of\nmerits and suitability of the candidates.  Religion, caste  or  sex  of  the\nevaluator has no  relevance.   The  plea  that  the  Law  Minister  and  the\nnominated  members  will  provide  feed  back  also  does  not  provide  any\njustification  for  their  being  members  of  the  Commission  and  thereby\nparticipating in evaluation and suitability of a candidate  for  appointment\nas judge of the Supreme Court or High Courts and having  power  to  overrule\nunanimous view of judges.  The appointment of a judge of the  Supreme  Court\nis normally made out of Chief Justices of High Courts or  senior  judges  or\neminent lawyers or eminent jurists whose merit is  better  known  to  senior\njudges.  Their evaluation has to be impartial and free  from  any  political\nor other considerations. Persons making selection are required  to  be  best\nplaced to assess their merit and  suitability.   Pre-dominant  and  decisive\nrole of  the  judiciary  is  a  requirement  not  only  of  independence  of\njudiciary and separation of powers but also for inspiring confidence of  the\npeople at large necessary for  strength  of  the  Democracy.   The  citizens\nhaving a grievance of  violation  of  their  fundamental  and  legal  rights\nagainst the Executive or the Legislature  expect  that  their  grievance  is\nconsidered  by  persons  whose  appointments  are  not  influenced  by   the\nExecutive or the Legislature.  If  an  appointment  is  perceived  as  being\ninfluenced by political consideration or  any  other  extraneous  influence,\nfaith in impartiality, which is hall  mark  of  independence  of  judiciary,\nwill be eroded.  The  scheme  in  other  countries  cannot  be  mechanically\nfollowed when it is  in  conflict  with  the  basic  scheme  of  the  Indian\nConstitution.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_929\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"60799\" id=\"span_705\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"322504\" id=\"span_706\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"102852\" id=\"span_707\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1235907\" id=\"span_708\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1939993\" id=\"span_709\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1569395\" id=\"span_710\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"257876\" id=\"span_711\">19.7  In this regard, it may be recalled that the word  amendment  literally\nmeans betterment or improvement and sponsor of amendment  may  always  claim\nimprovement.   Such claim has to be tested by applying the  \u2018identity  test\u2019\nand the \u2018impact test\u2019.  The said tests have already been  mentioned  in  the\nearlier part of its opinion.  The amendment should not affect  the  identity\nof an essential feature of the Constitution.  The impact  of  the  amendment\non the working of the scheme of  the  Constitution  has  to  be  taken  into\naccount[790]. This  brings  to  some  extent  subjective  element  which  is\nunavoidable even while testing  any  legislation  which  is  alleged  to  be\nviolative of fundamental rights and justified on the concept of  \u2018reasonable\nrestrictions\u2019[791].  In this regard, effect of Executive interference  which\nhas been documented by expert studies cannot be held  to  be  irrelevant  or\nignored on the ground that this is a subject of wisdom  of  Parliament.   As\nalready mentioned, the working of the Judiciary has affected  the  Executive\nand Legislature on several occasions, including  (by  way  of  illustration)\nPrivy Purses case[792], Bank Nationalisation  Case[793],  Freedom  of  Press\ncase[794], <a href=\"/doc/257876/\" id=\"a_2450\">Kesavananda Bharati</a> case (<a href=\"/doc/1569395/\" id=\"a_2451\">supra),  Indira  Gandhi</a>  case  (<a href=\"/doc/1939993/\" id=\"a_2452\">supra),\nMinerva Mills</a> case (<a href=\"/doc/1235907/\" id=\"a_2453\">supra), L. Chandrakumar</a> case (<a href=\"/doc/102852/\" id=\"a_2454\">supra),  M.  Nagaraj</a>  case\n(<a href=\"/doc/322504/\" id=\"a_2455\">supra), I.R. Coelho</a> case (<a href=\"/doc/60799/\" id=\"a_2456\">supra), S.R. Bommai</a> case[795].</span></span></span></span></span></span></span>\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1483\">19.8    The new structure provides for decisive voice  with  the  Commission\nwhich apart from judges comprises of Law Minister and  two  eminent  persons\nto be nominated  by  a  specified  committee.   Before  examining  the  said\nstructure, it may be noted that it is not merely the text of  the  amendment\nbut also its  impact  and  potential  which  has  to  be  kept  in  mind  on\n\u2018identity\u2019 of the original scheme and the \u2018width\u2019 of  the  power  under  the\nnew scheme[796].  In a similar context when an  alternative  judicial  forum\nwas sought to be created to deal with the company matters in place  of  High\nCourts, this Court held that the concept of rule of law  required  that  the\nnew mechanism should, as  nearly  as  possible,  have  same  standards[797].\nSame view was taken in the context of setting up of National  Tax  Tribunals\nto substitute the jurisdiction of the High Courts in tax matters[798].   The\nnew scheme may iron out the creases but the mechanism should  be  comparable\nto the substituted scheme.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1484\">19.9  As already mentioned under the unamended  scheme,  as  authoritatively\ninterpreted by this Court, power of initiating a proposal  was  always  with\nthe judiciary.  At the time of making of the Constitution, the draft of  the\nConstitution was circulated to the Federal Court and High Courts  to  elicit\nviews of the judges.  In  the  memorandum  representing  the  views  of  the\njudges, it was mentioned that the existing convention was  that  appointment\nof judges was made after referring the  matter  to  the  Chief  Justice  and\nobtaining his concurrence[799].\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1485\">19.10 In CAD, various models were considered but the  system  applicable  in\nother countries providing for final say of the Executive or  concurrence  of\nLegislature (as in UK and USA) were found to be unsuitable.  It  was  stated\nby Dr. Ambedkar that the power could not be left  to  be  exercised  on  the\nadvice  of  the  Executive  or  be  made  subject  to  concurrence  of   the\nLegislature.  It was further stated that the Chief Justice  could  also  not\nbe given a veto upon the appointment of judges[800].  The Law Commission  in\nits 14th Report criticised the interference by the Executive in  appointment\nof judges.  The matter came up for discussion before the Parliament and  the\nHome Minister and the Law Minister made a statement  that  all  appointments\nwere made on the recommendation of the CJI as the CJI was familiar with  the\nmerits of the candidates.  Out of 211, 210 appointments were made  with  the\nconsent and concurrence of the CJI[801].  It was noted  that  the  procedure\nfor appointment of judges applicable prior to Second Judge\u2019s case  was  that\na proposal for appointment was initiated by the CJI in case of  the  Supreme\nCourt and by Chief Justice of the High Court  in  case  of  the  High  Court\nJudges[802].  This mechanism was held to be a part of  the  convention[803].\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1486\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1382698\" id=\"span_712\">19.11   In <a href=\"/doc/1382698/\" id=\"a_2457\">Shamsher Singh</a> case (supra) this Court observed that in  practice\nthe last word in matters of judiciary must belong  to  the  CJI.</span>   <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"4572688\" id=\"span_713\">The  same\nview was expressed in <a href=\"/doc/4572688/\" id=\"a_2458\">Sankalchand</a> case (supra) in the  context  of  transfer\nof judges[804].  In 80th Report of the  Law  Commission  headed  by  Justice\nH.R. Khanna, J. (1979), a Commission was proposed with a pre-dominant  voice\nof judiciary to deal with the  appointment  and  transfer  of  judges.   The\nReport was significant in  the  background  of  supersession  of  judges  in\nappointment  of  the  CJI  and  selective  transfer  of  judges  which  were\nperceived to be interference with the independence  of  judiciary.</span>  <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_714\">However,\ncontrary to the said recommendations, a  circular  was  issued  by  the  Law\nMinister in               1981  proposing  transfer  of  judges  and  making\nappointment of judges for short period which  itself  was  perceived  to  be\ninterference with the independence of judiciary and was challenged in  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2459\">First\nJudges\u2019</a> case.  As already mentioned, the majority held that primacy in  such\nmatters rested  with  the  Central  Government[805].    The  said  view  was\nsubject matter of severe criticism.  Eminent constitutional  expert  Seervai\ncommented that the Executive was not  qualified  to  assess  the  merits  or\ndemerits of a candidate.  Initiation of a  proposal  by  the  Executive  was\nagainst the intention  of  the  framers  of  the  Constitution.   Political,\nExecutive or Legislative pressure should not enter into the  appointment  of\na judge[806].  The Law Commission headed by Justice D.A. Desai in its  121st\nReport also criticised the system where the Executive had overriding  powers\nin the matter of appointment of judges.  He stated  that  power  to  appoint\nand  transfer  judges  of  superior  courts   by   the   Executive   affects\nindependence of judiciary and is not conducive to its  healthy  growth.   He\nrecommended a Judicial Commission to check the arbitrariness on the part  of\nthe Executive in such appointments and transfers[807].\n19.12 The interpretation in  the  Second  Judge\u2019s  case  was  in  the  above\nhistorical  background.   In  the  context  of   working   of   the   Indian\nConstitution, the dominant role of the Executive in  appointment  of  judges\nadversely  affected  the  independence  of  judiciary.   The  judiciary   is\nassigned important role for upholding the rule of law  and  democracy.   Its\nindependence and its power of judicial review are part of  basic  structure.\nPrimacy of judiciary in appointment of judges is part  of  basic  structure.\nIn this background question is whether  the  new  scheme  retains  the  said\nprimacy of judiciary in appointment of judges.</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1487\">19.13 Under the new scheme, the Law Minister has been given  role  equal  to\nthe CJI.  Right from the commencement of the Constitution, this role of  the\nLaw  Minister  was  never  envisaged  while  initiating  the   process   and\nfinalizing it.   Law  Minister,  in  participatory  scheme,  could  at  best\nsuggest a name or give his comments on the names proposed but  the  proposal\ncould and was always initiated by the CJI.  At the stage of  initiation,  if\nequal authority is conferred, this will erode the primacy  of  judiciary  as\ndeclared by this Court  authoritatively.  Any  deviation  in  the  past  was\nalways adversely commented upon and held  to  be  undesirable  amounting  to\ninterference with the independence of judiciary[808].  Other two persons  to\nbe nominated by a Committee which also has predominant  political  voice  to\nbe placed at par with the  CJI  in  initiating  and  finalizing  a  proposal\ndestroys the original scheme beyond its  identity.   Any  suggestion  before\ninitiation of a name or feedback even after initiation  may  be  useful  and\nmay not affect independence of judiciary but equal participation by the  Law\nMinister and two outsiders in final decision for initiation  or  appointment\ncan be detrimental to the independence of judiciary.  It  cannot  be  wished\naway by presuming that the Law Minister and the two  distributors  will  not\nbe influenced by any extraneous consideration.  Such a presumption  will  be\ncontrary to the acknowledged factual experience.  It will  also  be  against\nthe concept of separation of judiciary from the Executive.  More  over  this\nwill be contrary to the basic intention of  the  Constitution  makers.   The\namendment is not an insignificant amendment and  is  not  within  the  basic\nframework of the working of the Constitution.  The very premise  and  object\nof the amendment as reflected in the Statement of Objects  and  Reasons  and\nthe stand of the Union of India in its pleadings and during  the  course  of\narguments is  that  the  primacy  of  judiciary  was  evolved  by  erroneous\ninterpretation which is sought to  be  corrected.  It  is  stated  that  the\nprimacy of judiciary was undemocratic and denied the Executive a  meaningful\nrole.  These reasons  are  untenable  for  reasons  already  discussed.   As\nregards the plea of transparency and accountability,  the  same  has  to  be\nachieved  without  compromising  independence  of  judiciary.   If  on   the\nperceived plea of  transparency  and  accountability,  the  independence  of\njudiciary is sought to be adversely affected by  the  Amendment,  this  will\ncause severe damage to the functioning of the Constitution.  The primacy  of\njudiciary,  as  already  noticed,  is  integral  to  the   independence   of\njudiciary,  separation of powers, federalism and democracy, rule of law  and\nsupremacy of the Constitution.  The amendment does away with the primacy  of\neven unanimous opinion of the  judicial  members  as  such  opinion  is  not\nenough to finalise an appointment.  While Shri Venugopal has rightly  stated\nin his alternative submission that primacy of judiciary is part of  judicial\nindependence and if Executive  has  pre-dominant  voice,  it  could  subvert\nindependence of judiciary,  his  submission  that  the  situation  could  be\nretrieved by giving the suggested interpretation cannot be  accepted.   Such\ninterpretation is not warranted by the text  of  the  amendment  or  by  the\nprinciples of interpretation.   It is difficult  to  hold  that  primacy  of\njudiciary is still retained as a wrong proposal can still be stalled by  any\ntwo members, including two judges.   The  primacy  of  judiciary  as  always\nunderstood in binding judicial precedents comprises of  initiation  of  name\nand taking a final call[809].  These two core features  constitute  identity\nof the primacy of judiciary. Subject to these two  features,  any  amendment\ncould have been made and if these two features are  compromised,  the  basic\nidentity of the Constitution can be held to have been altered or damaged.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1488\">19.14    There can be no doubt about the propositions  forcefully  canvassed\nby the respondents  that  the  legislative  wisdom  of  the  choice  of  the\nParliament was not open to question and that possibility of abuse  of  power\ncould not affect the existence and exercise of power but  these  submissions\ncannot ignore the limitation of basic  features.   Examining  whether  basic\nfeature was sought to be altered, is different from questioning  the  wisdom\nof the Parliament.  It is testing the power of Parliament conferred  by  the\nConstitution.  Similarly determining  whether  the  new  mechanism  complied\nwith the framework of the  Constitution  is  different  from  the  issue  of\npossibility of abuse.  In the present case, question is of  independence  of\njudiciary which implies  having  judges  not  influenced  by  any  political\nconsideration as per the intention of framers  of  the  Constitution.   Even\nassuming the best of intention, can the power  of  judicial  review  by  the\nconstitutional courts be subjected to scrutiny by any \u2018eminent  persons\u2019  on\nthe  ground  that  working  of   the   judiciary   was   perceived   to   be\nunsatisfactory.  Obviously it will be clear interference  with  independence\nof judiciary[810].  Same way, constitutionally  conferred  judicial  primacy\nin appointment of judges cannot be whittled down or sought to be  controlled\nby those who are not given or allowed to take  over  such  functions.   Even\ngranting the best of intentions, the Parliament could  not  act  beyond  the\nauthority conferred on it by the Constitution.  Thus,  taking  away  primacy\nof judiciary or conferring such primacy on a body which is not at  par  with\nthe said concept is certainly not a choice available  with  the  Parliament.\nAs already mentioned, the concept  of  primacy  of  judiciary  comprises  of\ninitiating the proposal and taking a final  decision  in  case  any  adverse\nfeed back is received after the proposal  is  initiated.   This  concept  of\nprimacy is compromised if the judiciary is unable to initiate a proposal  in\nthe first instance or if such proposal can  be  effectively  rejected.   The\nimpact thereof being that the appointment of judges could be made under  the\ninfluence of the Executive represented by the Law Minister or the  non-judge\nmembers  in  whose  appointment  the  pre-dominant  voice  is  not  of   the\njudiciary.  The  impact  of  such  appointments  will  be  that  the  judges\nappointed will  owe  their  appointments  to  the  Executive  which  may  be\ndestructive of the public  confidence  and  impartiality  of  judiciary  and\nadversely affect the  role  of  the  judiciary  as  an  important  impartial\nconstitutional organ.  As already noted, the role of  the  judiciary  is  to\ndefine and regulate working of other constitutional authorities  within  the\nscope of roles assigned to them[811].\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1489\">19.15  If the amendment had merely provided for advisory  or  recommendatory\nrole to the Law Minister or the  non-judicial  members  with  the  professed\nobject of transparency and  accountability,  the  situation  may  have  been\ndifferent.  It may not have<a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2460\">, <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2461\">in that case</a></a>, interfered with  the  primacy  of\nthe judiciary  in  appointment  of  judges  which  is  the  mandate  of  the\nConstitution.  Such power cannot be justified under the doctrine  of  wisdom\nof Parliament nor  on  the  principles  of  trust  once  such  power  is  in\nviolation of principle of primacy of judiciary in appointment of judges.  No\nindividual instance either of working of the  Executive  or  Legislature  or\nthe existing system of appointment of judges need be discussed as the  issue\ninvolved here is of interpretation of the Constitution and  not  of  success\nor failure  of  any  individual  or  persons.   As  already  mentioned,  the\nshortcomings in working of every institution  may  need  to  be  removed  by\nconstant  efforts  constitutionally  permissible  but  cannot  justify   the\naltering of the framework of the Constitution or the same being damaged.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1490\">20.   Reference may now be made to the submission  of  learned  counsel  for\nthe respondents that in many  countries  without  primacy  of  judiciary  in\nappointment  of  judges,  independent  judiciary  is  functioning  and  thus\nunfettered judicial primacy was inconsistent with the  international  trend.\nParticular mention has been made of 15 countries, namely,  Kenya,  Pakistan,\nSouth Africa, UK, Israel, France, Italy, Nigeria,  Sri   Lanka,   Australia,\nCanada, New Zealand, Bangladesh, Germany and United States.\n20.1  The submission of learned Attorney General  in  relation  to  judicial\nappointments in the said 15 countries is as follows :\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_930\"> \u201ca.    9 countries conduct appointment of judges  through  either  judicial\nappointment commissions (Kenya, Pakistan, South Africa and  UK),  committees\n(Israel) or councils (France, Italy, Nigeria and  Sir  Lanka);  4  countries\nappoint judges through a direct order of the  Governor  General  (Australia,\nCanada, New Zealand) or the  President  (Bangladesh),  where  applicable;  1\n(Germany) follows a multi-stage process of nomination  by  the  Minister  of\nJustice, confirmation by Parliamentary Committees and final  appointment  by\nthe President; and 1 (United States) follows a process of nomination by  the\nPresident (executive) and confirmation by the Senate (legislature).\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_931\">b.    In all 15 countries, the  executive  is  the  final  or  determinative\nappointing authority.  Out  of  the  9  countries  with  commissions,  in  2\ncountries (South Africa and Sri Lanka) the executive has  absolute  majority\nin comparison with members  of  other  groups  (judiciary,  legislature  and\nindependent persons).  In 4 countries (France, Israel, Kenya and  UK)  there\nis  a  balanced  representation  of  various  stakeholders,  including   the\nexecutive.  Out of 3 countries where the number of judges are in a  majority\n(Italy, Nigeria and Pakistan), in 2 countries  (Nigeria  and  Pakistan)  the\ndecision of the commission  is  subject  to  the  vote  of  a  parliamentary\ncommittee/Senate, while in 1 (Italy), the President of the Republic  is  the\nfinal appointing authority and the  chairman  of  the  judicial  appointment\nbody.  In 5 of the countries without  commissions  (Canada,  Australia,  New\nZealand, Bangladesh and United States of America), the decision is taken  by\nthe  Executive  without  any  formal  process  of  consultation   with   the\njudiciary, while in 1 (Germany), the appointment  process  is  conducted  by\nthe Parliament, and later confirmed by the President.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_932\">c.    In 8 countries  (France,  Israel,  Italy,  Kenya,  Nigeria,  Pakistan,\nSouth Africa and UK) with  bodies  for  judicial  appointments,  independent\nmembers  have  a  mandated   role   in   the   selection   process   through\nrepresentation on  the  said  bodies.   In  4  countries  where  independent\nmembers do not play a formal role in the appointment process  (Canada,  USA,\nAustralia and New  Zealand),  the  appointing  authority  (body  or  person)\nconsults independent members at various stages of  the  appointment  process\nfor their feedback on the  selection  or  recommendation  of  a  prospective\ncandidate.   In  3  countries  (Bangladesh,  Germany  and  Sri   Lanka)   no\ndocumented process of consultation  with  independent  members  is  provided\nfor.\u201d\n\n20.2   Learned counsel for the respondents also  referred  to  criticism  of\nthe collegium system by some  jurists  including  the  eminent  jurist  Shri\nNariman, appearing  in the present case for the petitioners.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_933\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"PARTY\" data-docid=\"1939993\" id=\"span_715\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"PARTY\" data-docid=\"1569395\" id=\"span_716\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"PARTY\" data-docid=\"257876\" id=\"span_717\">20.3  On the other hand, Shri Nariman  opposed  the  above  submissions  and\nreferred to decisions of this Court particularly  <a href=\"/doc/257876/\" id=\"a_2462\">Kesavananda  Bharti</a>  case,\n<a href=\"/doc/1569395/\" id=\"a_2463\">Indira  Gandhi</a>  case  and  <a href=\"/doc/1939993/\" id=\"a_2464\">Minerva  Mills</a>  case,  where   the   Constitution\namendments were struck down.  He also referred to expert  studies  including\nreports of the 14th and 121st Law Commissions and  the  National  Commission\nto Review the Working of the Constitution (NCRWC), headed  by  Justice  M.N.\nVenkatachaliah (retired CJI), wherein it was observed that  independence  of\njudiciary was basic  feature  of  the  Constitution  and  composition  of  a\nNational Commission  was required to  be  consistent  with  the  concept  of\nindependence of judiciary.  Method of appointment of  judges  could  not  be\naltered in such a way as may impinge upon  the  independence  of  judiciary.</span></span></span>\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1491\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"PARTY\" data-docid=\"158890876\" id=\"span_718\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"PARTY\" data-docid=\"80351\" id=\"span_719\">Composition  of  a  Judicial  Commission  has  to  uphold   the  primacy  of\njudiciary.[812]\n\n20.4   Shri  Nariman  also  submitted  that  the  impugned   amendment   was\nintroduced  in  response  to  decisions  of  this  Court  affecting  certain\nlegislators. He submitted that independent functioning of  the     judiciary\noften comes in conflict with the Executive and the Legislature  but  mandate\nof  the  Constitution  of  upholding  the  independence  of  judiciary   was\nnecessary to inspire faith of citizens in impartial justice  and  to  uphold\nthe constitutional values like  the  Rule  of  law  and  the  Democracy,  by\nupholding protection of fundamental  rights  even  against  the  State.   He\nparticularly  made  reference  to  the  history  of   proposed   Forty-Fifth\nAmendment vide Bill 88 of 1978 to provide in <a href=\"/doc/594125/\" id=\"a_2465\">Article 368</a> that  an  Amendment\ncompromising the independence of judiciary could  be  made  by  approval  by\nmajority at  a  referendum.   The  same  was  brought  about  by  the  Janta\nGovernment led by leaders who were arrested during emergency.   It  was  not\napproved for want of majority in Rajya Sabha. He also referred to  decisions\nof this Court <a href=\"/doc/80351/\" id=\"a_2466\">Lily Thomas  vs.  Union  of  India</a>[813]   and  <a href=\"/doc/158890876/\" id=\"a_2467\">Chief  Election\nCommissioner vs. Jan Chaukidar</a>[814] holding that a member of  a  Legislature\nwill stand qualified on conviction and that a person confined in jail  could\nnot contest an election and  efforts  to  undo  such  decisions.</span></span>    <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_720\">He  also\nreferred  to  the  treatise,  Constitutional  Law  of  India   by   Seervai,\n    4th Edition, to the effect that the decision of <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2468\">First Judges\u2019</a>  case  put\nthe judicial independence at the mercy of the Executive[815].\n20.5  He also gave a personal note, in response to  reliance  on  behalf  of\nthe respondents on his own biography \u201cBefore Memory Fades\u201d as follows:-</span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_934\">\u201cI have  been,  and  I  continue  to  be,  a  supporter  of  the  \u201cJudicial-\nAppointment-Commission-system\u201d and so are my clients whom I represent  (this\nis so stated in the Writ Petition at page 26 to 31, and 44 to  45).   BUT  I\nam definitely opposed to a pretence of a Judicial Appointments Commission  -\nwhich in reality is not  judicial,  only  partly  or  quasi  judicial.   The\n\u201cJudicial Appointments Commission system\u201d (so called)  as  embodied  in  the\n99th Constitutional Amendment, 2014 and along with the <a href=\"/doc/142995521/\" id=\"a_2469\">NJAC  Act,  2014</a>,  is\nopposed BECAUSE is not in accordance  with  and  does  not  conform  to  the\nBeijing Principles on Independence of the Judiciary (by which  we  in  India\nare governed).  The principles were formulated after  long  deliberation  by\nHeads of the Judiciary  in  the  LAWASIA  region  (including  India\u2019s  Chief\nJustice) - who are all signatories to  the  Beijing  Principle.   Principles\nNo.15 reads as follows:-\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_935\">\u201c15. In some societies, the appointment of judges, by, with the consent  of,\nor after consultation with a Judicial Services Commission has been  seen  as\na means of ensuring  that  those  chosen  judges  are  appropriate  for  the\npurpose.  Where  a  Judicial  Services  Commission  is  adopted,  it  should\ninclude representatives of the higher Judiciary and  the  independent  legal\nprofession as a means of ensuring that judicial  competence,  integrity  and\nindependence are maintained.\u201d\n\nNote - NOT OUTSIDERS, not representatives of the EXECUTIVE: because this  is\nnot helpful  in  the  interests  of  maintaining  the  INDEPENDENCE  OF  THE\nJUDICIARY. Text of Beijing Principles are annexed as Exhibit-II.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_936\">The then Law Minister had stated in Parliament,  when  these  measures  were\nfirst introduced, that he had consulted named  persons  including  myself  -\nand as to what I said is accurately recorded in the Minutes of  the  Meeting\nprepared by the office of the  Law  Minister.
