This  is  what  the  minutes\nrecord:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_937\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"257876\" id=\"span_721\">Constitutional Expert and Senior Advocate, Shri Fali Nariman stated that  it\nis  important  to  remember  the  independence  of  the  judiciary  and  the\nseparation of powers.  The basic structure doctrine  as  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2470\">laid  down  by</a>  the\nSupreme Court in the <a href=\"/doc/257876/\" id=\"a_2471\">Keshavananda Bharti</a> case could not be violated and  any\nproposal for appointment of judges must be  in  conformity  with  the  basic\nstructure.  He felt that the Government should consider following the  model\nof the Appointments Commission as suggested  by  the  Justice  Venkatchaliah\nCommission that gave dominance to the judiciary in the appointment  process.\n He stated that composition of the Commission is  the  basic  issue,  and  a\nCommission with non-Judge domination would not be viable in India.</span>\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_938\">\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026..\u201d\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1492\">21.    As  already  mentioned,  the  Constitution  of  India  has  its   own\nbackground and personality[816].  Models of other  countries  could  not  be\nblindly followed so as to damage the identity and personality of the  Indian\nConstitution.  The Judicial Commissions  referred  to  by  learned  Attorney\nGeneral do  not  show  the  trend  of  reducing  the  pre-existing  role  of\njudiciary. In fact, the trend is for reducing the pre-existing role  of  the\nExecutive.  In  the  impugned  amendment  it  is  the  reverse.   Thus,  the\ncontention of working of other  Constitutions  or  setting  up  of  judicial\nCommissions with varying compositions in other countries  does  not  justify\nthe impugned amendment which is contrary  to  the  basic  structure  of  the\nIndian Constitution.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1493\">22.   There is also no merit in the contention  that  in  the  present  case\nmere alteration in a constitutional provision does not amount to  damage  of\na basic feature.  It is not a case of simple amendment to iron out  creases.\nIts impact clearly  affects  the  independence  of  judiciary.   As  already\nmentioned, appointment of judges has always been considered  in  the  scheme\nof  the  working  of  the  Indian  Constitution  to  be  integral   to   the\nindependence  of  judiciary.   It  is  for  this  reason  that  primacy   in\nappointment of judges has always been intended to be of the judiciary.  Pre-\ndominant role of the Executive is not permissible.  Such  primacy  comprises\nof initiating the proposal by the judiciary and final  word  being  normally\nwith the CJI (in representative  capacity).    This  scheme  is  beyond  the\npower of amendment available to the Parliament.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1494\">22.1  In the new scheme, the Chief Justices of  the  High  Courts  have  not\nbeen provided any constitutional say.  The Chief Justice of the  High  Court\nis in a better position to initially assess the merit  of  a  candidate  for\nappointment as judge of the High Court.  The constitutional  amendment  does\nnot provide for any role to the Chief  Minister  of  the  State.   This  may\naffect the quality of the candidate selected and  thereby  the  independence\nof judiciary.  The statutory provision in the <a href=\"/doc/142995521/\" id=\"a_2472\">NJAC Act</a>  will  be  gone  into\nseparately.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1495\">22.2  The contention of learned Attorney  General  that  the  amendment  was\njustified to uphold the principles of checks and balances  and  transparency\nwhich were equally  important  constitutional  values  cannot  be  accepted.\nEven assuming that there is a scope for improvement in the  working  of  the\ncollegium system, it cannot be held that under the existing system there  is\nno transparency  or  checks  and  balances.   The  procedure  <a href=\"/doc/257876/\" id=\"a_2473\">laid  down  in</a>\nmemoranda issued by the Central Government has been  noted  in  the  earlier\npart of this opinion.  All proceedings  in  initiating  a  proposal  are  in\nwriting and are forwarded to the constitutional  functionaries.   The  Chief\nMinister, the Governor, the Law Minister, the  PM  and  the  President  have\nopportunity to give their views  in  the  matter  of  appointment  of  Chief\nJustices and  Judges  of  High  Courts  apart  from  judges  and  non-judges\ninvolved in the process.  The Law Minister, the PM and  the  President  also\nhave opportunity to give their comment on appointment of CJI and the  Judges\nof the Supreme Court.  There is also an opportunity to suggest names  before\ninitiation of proposal.  There is no bar to  an  expert  feedback  from  the\ncivil society through  the  constitutional  functionaries  involved.   Thus,\nthere is transparency as well as checks and balances.  These  considerations\ndo not justify interference with the final initiation  of  proposal  by  the\njudiciary or in taking  a  final  view  in  the  matter  by  the  judiciary,\nconsistent with the mandate of the Constitution.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1496\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_722\">22.3  Learned Attorney General sought to compare the existing provision  for\nveto by two members of collegium in appointment of Supreme Court  Judges  as\nper <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2474\">Third Judges\u2019</a> case to justify veto under  <a href=\"/doc/93595316/\" id=\"a_2475\">Section  6  (6)</a>.   As  already\nmentioned, the role of the Law Minister and the non-judge members cannot  be\nplaced at par with the Chief Justice and Judges of the Supreme Court.   They\ncannot be compared for  obvious  reasons.   The  veto  power  with  the  Law\nMinister or with a non-judge members, as against a Supreme Court  Judge  who\nis  the  member  of  the  collegium,  may  involve  interference  with   the\nindependence of judiciary.  Similarly, requirement of  special  majority  in\nany  other  ordinary  situation  was  not  comparable  with  the  scheme  of\nappointment of judges which is sui generis.  Similarly, the plea  of  giving\nvital inputs does not justify participation of the  non-judge  members  with\nthe  Chief  Justice  and  the  Judges  in  discharging  their  functions  of\ninitiating a proposal or  taking  a  final  view.   Though,  formal  act  of\nappointment of judges may be  an  executive  function,  there  is  a  unique\njudicial element in the process of appointment of judges  of  constitutional\ncourts.  The criticism against perceived short comings  in  the  working  of\nthe collegium also does not justify the  impugned  provisions.   As  already\nobserved, there may  be  criticism  even  against  discharging  of  judicial\nfunctions by the aggrieved parties or otherwise.  But that does not  justify\ninterference  with  the  judicial  decisions[817].   Needless  to  say  that\ncriticism can be against the working of any system but the  systems  can  be\nchanged only as per the Constitution.  Efforts to improve all  systems  have\nto be continuously made.</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1497\">Conclusion:-\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_939\">22.4  I would conclude that the new scheme damages the basic feature of  the\nConstitution under which primacy in appointment of judges  has  to  be  with\nthe judiciary.  Under the new scheme such primacy has been given  a  go-bye.\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1498\">Thus the impugned amendment cannot be sustained.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1499\">F.    Validity of the NJAC Act\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1500\">23.   In view  of  my  conclusion  about  the  amendment  being  beyond  the\ncompetence of the Parliament, I do not consider it necessary to discuss  the\nvalidity of the <a href=\"/doc/142995521/\" id=\"a_2476\">NJAC Act</a> in great detail as  the  said  Act  cannot  survive\nonce the amendment is struck down.   However,  consistent  with  my  earlier\nview  that  primacy  of  judiciary  in  appointment  of  judges  cannot   be\ncompromised and  on  that  ground  not  only  <a href=\"/doc/79773052/\" id=\"a_2477\">Section  2</a>  of  the  Amendment\ndispensing  with  the  mandatory  consultation   with   the   judiciary   as\ncontemplated under the unamended provisions, <a href=\"/doc/14549537/\" id=\"a_2478\">Section 3</a> conferring  power  on\nthe  NJAC  (under  <a href=\"/doc/61056230/\" id=\"a_2479\">Article  124B</a>)  and  providing  for  composition  of  the\nCommission under <a href=\"/doc/140653939/\" id=\"a_2480\">Article 124A</a> giving a role to  the  Law  Minister  and  two\neminent persons equal to  the  CJI  in  recommending  appointments  as  CJI,\nJudges of Supreme Court, Chief Justices and other Judges of the High  Courts\nand recommending transfer of Chief Justices and Judges of  the  High  Courts\nare unconstitutional but also <a href=\"/doc/16427836/\" id=\"a_2481\">Article 124C</a> giving power  to  the  Parliament\nto regulate the procedure and to lay down the manner of selection  was  also\nunconstitutional, the impugned Act has to  be  struck  down.   It  goes  far\nbeyond the procedural aspects.  In <a href=\"/doc/112124301/\" id=\"a_2482\">Section 5 (2)</a> \u2018suitability  criteria\u2019  is\nleft to be worked out by regulations.  Second proviso to <a href=\"/doc/112124301/\" id=\"a_2483\">Section 5  (2)</a>  and\n<a href=\"/doc/93595316/\" id=\"a_2484\">Section 6 (6)</a> give veto to two  members  of  the  Commission  which  is  not\ncontemplated by the Amendment.  <a href=\"/doc/94269565/\" id=\"a_2485\">Section 5 (3)</a> and <a href=\"/doc/45114090/\" id=\"a_2486\">Section 6 (8)</a> provide  for\nconditions for selection to be <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2487\">laid down by</a> regulations which are  not  mere\nprocedural  matters.   <a href=\"/doc/20215517/\" id=\"a_2488\">Section  6</a>   authorises   the   recommendations   for\nappointment as judges of the High Courts without the  proposal  being  first\ninitiated   by   the   Chief   Justice   of   the   High   Court.    <a href=\"/doc/176401296/\" id=\"a_2489\">Section\n6 (1)</a> provides for recommendation for appointment  of  Chief  Justice  of  a\nHigh Court on the basis of inter se seniority of High  Court  Judges.   This\nmay affect giving representation to as many High Courts  as  viable  as,  in\ninter se seniority, many judges of only one High Court may be  senior  most.\n<a href=\"/doc/101346937/\" id=\"a_2490\">Section 6 (2)</a> provides for seeking nomination from Chief  Justices  of  High\nCourts,  but  <a href=\"/doc/101594793/\" id=\"a_2491\">Section  6  (3)</a>  empowers  the  Commission  itself   to   make\nrecommendation for appointment as Judge of the High Court and seek  comments\nfrom Chief Justice after short listing the candidates by itself.  <a href=\"/doc/198877115/\" id=\"a_2492\">Section  8</a>\nenables the Central Government to appoint  officers  and  employees  of  the\nCommission and to lay down their conditions of service.   The  Secretary  of\nthe Government is the Convenor of the Commission.  <a href=\"/doc/184356182/\" id=\"a_2493\">Section 13</a>  requires  all\nregulations to be approved by the Parliament.  These provisions in  the  Act\nimpinge upon the independence of judiciary.  Even if the doctrine  of  basic\nstructure is  not  applied  in  judging  the  validity  of  a  parliamentary\nstatute, independence of judiciary and rule of law  are  parts  of  <a href=\"/doc/367586/\" id=\"a_2494\">Articles\n14</a><a href=\"/doc/1218090/\" id=\"a_2495\">, 19</a> and <a href=\"/doc/1199182/\" id=\"a_2496\">21</a> of the Constitution and absence of independence  of  judiciary\naffects the said Fundamental Rights.  <a href=\"/doc/142995521/\" id=\"a_2497\">The NJAC Act</a>  is  thus  liable  to  be\nstruck down.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1501\">G.    Effect of Amendment being struck down\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1502\">24.   The contention that even if Amendment is held to  be  void,  the  pre-\nexisting system cannot be restored has no logic.  In exercise  of  power  of\njudicial review, a provision can be declared void in which  case  the  legal\nposition as it stands without such void provision can be  held  to  prevail.\nIt is not a situation when position has not been made clear  while  deciding\nan issue.  Power of this Court to declare the effect of its order cannot  be\ndoubted nor the decisions relied upon by the respondents show otherwise.   I\nhold that on amendment being struck down,  the  pre-existing  system  stands\nrevived.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1503\">H.    Review of Working of the Existing System\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1504\">25.   Since the system existing prior to amendment  will  stand  revived  on\nthe amendment being struck down and grievances  have  been  expressed  about\nits functioning, I  am  of  the  view  that  such  grievances  ought  to  be\nconsidered.  It is made clear that grievances have  not  been  expressed  by\nthe  petitioners  about  the  existence  of  the  pre-existing   system   of\nappointment but about its functioning in practice.  It has been argued  that\nthis  Court  can  go  into  this  aspect  without  re-visiting  the  earlier\ndecisions of the larger Benches.  I am of  the  view  that  such  grievances\nought to be gone into for which the matter needs to be listed  for  hearing.\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1505\">Conclusion\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1506\">26.    The  impugned  Amendment   and   the   Act   are   struck   down   as\nunconstitutional.  Pre-existing  scheme  of  appointment  of  judges  stands\nrevived.  The matter be listed for consideration of the surviving  issue  of\ngrievances as to working of pre-existing system.\n</p>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1507\">                                                    \u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026..\u2026.\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026\u2026..J.\n                                              [ ADARSH KUMAR GOEL ]\nNEW DELHI\nOCTOBER 16, 2015\n                                                                    APPENDIX\n\nKey Provisions of the Unamended Constitution\n\n\u201c124. Establishment and constitution of Supreme Court - (1) There  shall  be\na Supreme Court of India consisting of a Chief Justice of India  and,  until\nParliament by law prescribes a larger number, of not more than  seven  other\nJudges.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1508\">(2) Every Judge of the Supreme Court shall be appointed by the President  by\nwarrant under his hand and seal after consultation with such of  the  Judges\nof the Supreme Court and of the High Courts in the States as  the  President\nmay deem necessary for the purpose and shall hold office  until  he  attains\nthe age of sixty-five years:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_940\">Provided that in the case of appointment of a Judge  other  than  the  Chief\nJustice, the Chief Justice of India shall always be consulted:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_941\">Provided further that\u2014\n\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_942\">(a) a Judge may, by writing under  his  hand  addressed  to  the  President,\nresign his office;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_943\">(b) a Judge may be removed from his office in the manner provided in  clause\n(4).\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1509\">xxxxxxx\n\n\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1510\">217. Appointment and conditions of the office of a Judge of  a  High  Court-\n\u00a0Every Judge of a High Court shall be appointed by the President by  warrant\nunder his hand and seal after consultation with the Chief Justice of  India,\nthe Governor of the State, and, in the case of appointment of a Judge  other\nthan the Chief Justice, the Chief Justice of the High court, and shall  hold\noffice, in the case of  an  additional  or  acting  Judge,  as  provided  in\n<a href=\"/doc/570782/\" id=\"a_2498\">Article 224</a>, and in any other case, until he attains the age  of  sixty  two\nyears:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_944\">xxxxxx\n\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_945\">222. Transfer of a Judge from one High Court to another-\u00a0The President  may,\nafter consultation with the Chief Justice of India, transfer  a  Judge  from\none High Court to any other High Court.\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1511\">xxxxx\u201d\n\n(II)    The 99th Amendment Act\n\n\n            \u201cTHE CONSTITUTION (NINETY-NINTH AMENDMENT) ACT, 2014\n\n                                                       [31st December, 2014]\n\n             <a href=\"/doc/142995521/\" id=\"a_2499\">An Act</a> further to amend the Constitution of India.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1512\">Be it enacted by Parliament in the  Sixty-fifth  Year  of  the  Republic  of\nIndia as follows:\u2014\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1513\">1.     (1)    <a href=\"/doc/142995521/\" id=\"a_2500\">This  Act</a>  may  be  called  the   <a href=\"/doc/195108305/\" id=\"a_2501\">Constitution   (Ninety-ninth\nAmendment) Act, 2014</a>.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1514\">(2)   It shall come into force on such date as the Central  Government  may,\nby notification in the Official Gazette, appoint.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1515\">2.    In <a href=\"/doc/1164880/\" id=\"a_2502\">article 124</a> of the Constitution, in clause (2),\u2013\u2013\n\n(a    for the words \u201cafter consultation with  such  of  the  Judges  of  the\nSupreme Court and of the High Courts in the  States  as  the  President  may\ndeem necessary for the purpose\u201d, the  words,  figures  and  letter  \u201con  the\nrecommendation of the National Judicial Appointments Commission referred  to\nin article 124A\u201d shall be substituted;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1516\">(b)   the first proviso shall be omitted;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1517\">(c)   in the second proviso, for the  words  \u201cProvided  further  that\u201d,  the\nwords \u201cProvided that\u201d shall be substituted.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1518\">3.    After <a href=\"/doc/1164880/\" id=\"a_2503\">article 124</a> of the Constitution, the  following  articles  shall\nbe inserted, namely:\u2014\n\n\u201c124A. (1)  There shall  be  a  Commission  to  be  known  as  the  National\nJudicial Appointments Commission consisting of the following, namely:\u2013\u2013\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1519\">(a)   the Chief Justice of India, Chairperson, ex officio;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1520\">(b     two other senior Judges of  the  Supreme  Court  next  to  the  Chief\nJustice of India \u2013\u2013Members, ex officio;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1521\">(c)   the Union Minister in charge of Law and Justice\u2013\u2013Member, ex officio;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1522\">(d)   two eminent persons to be nominated by  the  committee  consisting  of\nthe Prime Minister, the Chief Justice of India and the Leader of  Opposition\nin the House of the People or where there is no such Leader  of  Opposition,\nthen, the Leader of single largest Opposition Party  in  the  House  of  the\nPeople \u2013\u2013 Members:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_946\">Provided that one of the eminent person shall be nominated from amongst  the\npersons belonging to the  Scheduled  Castes,  the  Scheduled  Tribes,  Other\nBackward Classes, Minorities or Women:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_947\">Provided further that an eminent person shall be nominated for a  period  of\nthree years and shall not be eligible for renomination.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_948\">(2)    No  act  or  proceedings  of  the  National   Judicial   Appointments\nCommission shall be questioned or be invalidated merely  on  the  ground  of\nthe  existence  of  any  vacancy  or  defect  in  the  constitution  of  the\nCommission.\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1523\">124B. It shall be the duty of the National Judicial Appointments  Commission\nto\u2014\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1524\">(a)   recommend persons for appointment as Chief Justice  of  India,  Judges\nof the Supreme Court, Chief Justices of High  Courts  and  other  Judges  of\nHigh Courts;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1525\">(b)   recommend transfer of Chief Justices and other Judges of  High  Courts\nfrom one High Court to any other High Court; and\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1526\">(c)   ensure that the person recommended is of ability and integrity.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1527\">124C. Parliament may, by law, regulate the procedure for the appointment  of\nChief Justice of India and other Judges  of  the  Supreme  Court  and  Chief\nJustices and other Judges of High Courts and empower the Commission  to  lay\ndown by regulations the procedure for the discharge of  its  functions,  the\nmanner of selection of persons for appointment and  such  other  matters  as\nmay be considered necessary by it.\u201d.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1528\">4.    In <a href=\"/doc/1688766/\" id=\"a_2504\">article 127</a> of the Constitution, in clause (1), for the words  \u201cthe\nChief Justice of India may, with the previous  consent  of  the  President\u201d,\nthe words \u201cthe National Judicial  Appointments  Commission  on  a  reference\nmade to it by the Chief Justice of India, may with the previous  consent  of\nthe President\u201d shall be substituted.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1529\">5.    In <a href=\"/doc/908489/\" id=\"a_2505\">article 128</a> of the Constitution, for the words \u201cthe  Chief  Justice\nof India\u201d, the words \u201cthe National Judicial Appointments  Commission\u201d  shall\nbe substituted.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1530\">6.    In <a href=\"/doc/1682952/\" id=\"a_2506\">article 217</a> of the Constitution, in clause  (1),  for  the  portion\nbeginning with the words \u201cafter consultation\u201d, and  ending  with  the  words\n\u201cthe High Court\u201d, the words, figures and letter \u201con  the  recommendation  of\nthe National Judicial Appointments Commission referred to in  article  124A\u201d\nshall be substituted.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1531\">7.    In <a href=\"/doc/819787/\" id=\"a_2507\">article 222</a> of the Constitution,  in  clause  (1),  for  the  words\n\u201cafter consultation with the Chief Justice of  India\u201d,  the  words,  figures\nand letter \u201con the recommendation  of  the  National  Judicial  Appointments\nCommission referred to in article 124A\u201d shall be substituted.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1532\">8.    In <a href=\"/doc/570782/\" id=\"a_2508\">article 224</a> of the Constitution,\u2013\u2013\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1533\">(a) in clause (1), for the words \u201cthe  President  may  appoint\u201d,  the  words\n\u201cthe President may, in consultation with the National Judicial  Appointments\nCommission, appoint\u201d shall be substituted;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1534\">(b) in clause (2), for the words \u201cthe  President  may  appoint\u201d,  the  words\n\u201cthe President may, in consultation with the National Judicial  Appointments\nCommission, appoint\u201d shall be substituted.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1535\">9.    In <a href=\"/doc/165551653/\" id=\"a_2509\">article 224A</a>  of  the  Constitution,  for  the  words  \u2018\u2018the  Chief\nJustice of a High Court for any State may at any  time,  with  the  previous\nconsent of the President\u2019\u2019, the words \u2018\u2018the National  Judicial  Appointments\nCommission on a reference made to it by the Chief Justice of  a  High  Court\nfor any State, may with the previous consent of  the  President\u2019\u2019  shall  be\nsubstituted.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1536\">10.   In <a href=\"/doc/1347035/\" id=\"a_2510\">article 231</a> of the Constitution,  in  clause  (2),  sub-clause  (a)\nshall be omitted.\u201d\n\n\n<a href=\"/doc/142995521/\" id=\"a_2511\">The NJAC Act</a>\n\n\n   \u201cTHE NATIONAL JUDICIAL APPOINTMENTS COMMISSION ACT, 2014 NO. 40 OF 2014\n\n                                                       [31st December, 2014]\n\n<a href=\"/doc/142995521/\" id=\"a_2512\">An Act</a> to regulate the procedure to be followed  by  the  National  Judicial\nAppointments Commission for recommending  persons  for  appointment  as  the\nChief Justice of India and other Judges  of  the  Supreme  Court  and  Chief\nJustices and other Judges of High Courts and for  their  transfers  and  for\nmatters connected therewith or incidental thereto.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1537\">Be it enacted by Parliament in the  Sixty-fifth  Year  of  the  Republic  of\nIndia as follows:\u2014\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1538\">1.    (1)  <a href=\"/doc/142995521/\" id=\"a_2513\">This  Act</a>  may  be  called  the  <a href=\"/doc/142995521/\" id=\"a_2514\">National  Judicial  Appointments\nCommission Act, 2014</a>.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1539\">(2) It shall come into force on such date as the Central Government may,  by\nnotification in the Official Gazette, appoint.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1540\">2.    In this Act, unless the context otherwise requires,\u2013\u2013\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1541\">(a) \u201cChairperson\u201d means the Chairperson of the Commission;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1542\">(b)  \u201cCommission\u201d  means  the  National  Judicial  Appointments   Commission\nreferred to in <a href=\"/doc/140653939/\" id=\"a_2515\">article 124A</a> of the Constitution;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1543\">(c) \u201cHigh Court\u201d means the High Court in  respect  of  which  recommendation\nfor appointment of a Judge is proposed to be made by the Commission;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1544\">(d) \u201cMember\u201d means a Member of the Commission and includes its  Chairperson;\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1545\">(e) \u201cprescribed\u201d means prescribed by the rules made under this Act;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1546\">(f) \u201cregulations\u201d means the regulations made by the  Commission  under  this\nAct.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1547\">3.    The Headquarters of the Commission shall be at Delhi.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1548\">4.    (1) The Central Government shall, within a period of thirty days  from\nthe date of coming into force of this Act, intimate the  vacancies  existing\nin the posts of Judges in the Supreme Court and  in  a  High  Court  to  the\nCommission for making its recommendations to fill up such vacancies.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1549\">(2)  The  Central  Government  shall,  six  months  prior  to  the  date  of\noccurrence of any vacancy by reason of completion of the term of a Judge  of\nthe Supreme Court or of a High Court, make a  reference  to  the  Commission\nfor making its recommendation to fill up such vacancy.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1550\">(3) The Central Government shall, within a period of thirty  days  from  the\ndate of occurrence of any vacancy by reason of death  or  resignation  of  a\nJudge of the Supreme Court or of a High  Court,  make  a  reference  to  the\nCommission for making its recommendations to fill up such vacancy.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1551\">5.    (1) The Commission shall recommend  for  appointment  the  senior-most\nJudge of the  Supreme  Court  as  the  Chief  Justice  of  India  if  he  is\nconsidered fit to hold the office: Provided that a member of the  Commission\nwhose name is being considered for recommendation shall not  participate  in\nthe meeting.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1552\">(2) The Commission shall, on the basis  of  ability,  merit  and  any  other\ncriteria of suitability as may be specified by  regulations,  recommend  the\nname for appointment as a Judge of the Supreme Court  from  amongst  persons\nwho are eligible to be appointed as such under clause (3) of <a href=\"/doc/1164880/\" id=\"a_2516\">article 124</a>  of\nthe Constitution:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_949\">Provided that while making recommendation for appointment of  a  High  Court\nJudge, apart from seniority, the ability and merit of such  Judge  shall  be\nconsidered:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_950\">Provided further that the  Commission  shall  not  recommend  a  person  for\nappointment if any two members of the  Commission  do  not  agree  for  such\nrecommendation.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_951\">(3) The Commission may, by regulations, specify  such  other  procedure  and\nconditions for selection and appointment of a Judge of the Supreme Court  as\nit may consider necessary.\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1553\">6.    (1) The Commission shall recommend for appointment a Judge of  a  High\nCourt to be the Chief Justice of a High Court  on  the  basis  of  inter  se\nseniority of High Court Judges and ability, merit and any other criteria  of\nsuitability as may be specified by regulations.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1554\">(2) The Commission shall seek nomination  from  the  Chief  Justice  of  the\nconcerned High Court for the  purpose  of  recommending  for  appointment  a\nperson to be a Judge of that High Court.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1555\">(3) The Commission shall also on the basis of ability, merit and  any  other\ncriteria of suitability as may be specified by  regulations,  nominate  name\nfor appointment as a Judge of a High Court  from  amongst  persons  who  are\neligible to be appointed as such under clause (2)  of  <a href=\"/doc/1682952/\" id=\"a_2517\">article  217</a>  of  the\nConstitution and forward such names to the Chief Justice  of  the  concerned\nHigh Court for its views.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1556\">(4) Before making any nomination under sub-section (2) or giving  its  views\nunder sub-section (3), the Chief Justice of the concerned High  Court  shall\nconsult two senior-most Judges of that High Court and such other Judges  and\neminent advocates of that High Court as may be specified by regulations.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1557\">(5) After receiving views and nomination under  sub-sections  (2)  and  (3),\nthe Commission may  recommend  for  appointment  the  person  who  is  found\nsuitable  on  the  basis  of  ability,  merit  and  any  other  criteria  of\nsuitability as may be specified by regulations.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1558\">(6) The Commission shall not recommend a person for appointment  under  this\nsection if any  two  members  of  the  Commission  do  not  agree  for  such\nrecommendation.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1559\">(7) The Commission shall elicit in writing the views  of  the  Governor  and\nthe Chief Minister of the State concerned before making such  recommendation\nin such manner as may be specified by regulations.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1560\">(8) The Commission may, by regulations, specify  such  other  procedure  and\nconditions for selection and appointment of a Chief Justice of a High  Court\nand a Judge of a High Court as it may consider necessary.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1561\">7.    The President shall, on the recommendations made  by  the  Commission,\nappoint the Chief Justice of India or a Judge of the Supreme  Court  or,  as\nthe case may be, the Chief Justice of a High Court or the Judge  of  a  High\nCourt:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_952\">Provided that  the  President  may,  if  considers  necessary,  require  the\nCommission to reconsider, either generally or otherwise, the  recommendation\nmade by it:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_953\">Provided further  that  if  the  Commission  makes  a  recommendation  after\nreconsideration in accordance with the provisions contained  in  <a href=\"/doc/125564484/\" id=\"a_2518\">sections  5</a>\nor 6, the President shall make the appointment accordingly.\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1562\">8.    (1) The Central Government may, in consultation with  the  Commission,\nappoint such number of officers and other employees  for  the  discharge  of\nfunctions of the Commission under this Act.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1563\">(2) The terms  and  other  conditions  of  service  of  officers  and  other\nemployees of the Commission appointed under sub-section (1)  shall  be  such\nas may be prescribed.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1564\">(3) The Convenor of the Commission shall be the Secretary to the  Government\nof India in the Department of Justice.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1565\">9.    The Commission shall recommend for  transfer  of  Chief  Justices  and\nother Judges of High Courts from one High Court to  any  other  High  Court,\nand for this purpose,  specify,  by  regulations,  the  procedure  for  such\ntransfer.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1566\">10.   (1) The Commission shall have the power to  specify,  by  regulations,\nthe procedure for the discharge of its functions.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1567\">(2) The Commission shall meet at such time and place as the Chairperson  may\ndirect and observe such rules of procedure in regard to the  transaction  of\nbusiness at its meetings (including the quorum at its meeting),  as  it  may\nspecify by regulations.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1568\">11.   (1) The Central  Government  may,  by  notification  in  the  Official\nGazette, make rules to carry out the provisions of this Act.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1569\">(2) In particular and without prejudice to the generality of  the  foregoing\npower, such rules may provide for all  or  any  of  the  following  matters,\nnamely:\u2013\u2013\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1570\">      (a) the fees and allowances payable to the eminent  persons  nominated\nunder sub-clause (d) of clause (1) of <a href=\"/doc/140653939/\" id=\"a_2519\">article 124A</a> of the Constitution;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1571\">      (b) the terms and other conditions of service of  officers  and  other\nemployees of the Commission under sub-section (2) of <a href=\"/doc/198877115/\" id=\"a_2520\">section 8</a>;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1572\">      (c) any other matter which  is  to  be,  or  may  be,  prescribed,  in\nrespect of which provision is to be made by the rules.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1573\">12.   (1) The Commission may, by notification in the Official Gazette,  make\nregulations consistent with this Act, and  the  rules  made  thereunder,  to\ncarry out the provisions of this Act.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1574\">(2) In particular, and without prejudice to the generality of the  foregoing\npower, such regulations  may  provide  for  all  or  any  of  the  following\nmatters, namely:\u2014\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1575\">(a) the criteria of suitability with respect to appointment of  a  Judge  of\nthe Supreme Court under sub-section (2) of <a href=\"/doc/125564484/\" id=\"a_2521\">section 5</a>;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1576\"> (b) other procedure and conditions  for  selection  and  appointment  of  a\nJudge of the Supreme Court under sub-section (3) of <a href=\"/doc/125564484/\" id=\"a_2522\">section 5</a>;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1577\"> (c) the criteria of suitability with respect to appointment of a  Judge  of\nthe High Court under sub-section (3) of <a href=\"/doc/20215517/\" id=\"a_2523\">section 6</a>;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1578\"> (d) other Judges and eminent advocates who may be consulted  by  the  Chief\nJustice under sub-section (4) of <a href=\"/doc/20215517/\" id=\"a_2524\">section 6</a>;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1579\"> (e) the manner of eliciting views of the Governor and  the  Chief  Minister\nunder sub-section (7) of <a href=\"/doc/20215517/\" id=\"a_2525\">section 6</a>;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1580\"> (f) other procedure and conditions  for  selection  and  appointment  of  a\nJudge of the High Court under sub-section (8) of <a href=\"/doc/20215517/\" id=\"a_2526\">section 6</a>;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1581\"> (g) the procedure for transfer of Chief Justices and other Judges from  one\nHigh Court to any other High Court under <a href=\"/doc/46047158/\" id=\"a_2527\">section 9</a>;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1582\"> (h) the procedure to be followed by the Commission in the discharge of  its\nfunctions under sub-section (1) of <a href=\"/doc/188325285/\" id=\"a_2528\">section 10</a>;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1583\"> (i) the rules of procedure in regard to the transaction of business at  the\nmeetings of Commission, including the quorum  at  its  meeting,  under  sub-\nsection (2) of <a href=\"/doc/188325285/\" id=\"a_2529\">section 10</a>;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1584\"> (j) any other matter which is required to  be,  or  may  be,  specified  by\nregulations or in respect of which provision is to be made by regulations.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1585\">13.   Every rule and regulation made under this Act shall be laid,  as  soon\nas may be after it is made, before each House of Parliament, while it is  in\nsession, for a total period of thirty days, which may be  comprised  in  one\nsession or in two or more successive sessions, and if, before the expiry  of\nthe session immediately following the session  or  the  successive  sessions\naforesaid, both Houses agree in making  any  modification  in  the  rule  or\nregulation or both Houses agree that the rule or regulation  should  not  be\nmade, the rule or regulation shall  thereafter  have  effect  only  in  such\nmodified form or be of no effect, as the case may be; so, however, that  any\nsuch modification or annulment shall be without prejudice  to  the  validity\nof anything previously done under that rule or regulation.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1586\">14.   (1)   If any difficulty arises in giving effect to the  provisions  of\nthis  Act,  the  Central  Government  may,  after  consultation   with   the\nCommission, by an  order  published  in  the  Official  Gazette,  make  such\nprovisions, not inconsistent with the provisions of this Act  as  appear  to\nit to be necessary or expedient for removing the difficulty:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_954\">      Provided that no such order shall  be  made  after  the  expiry  of  a\nperiod of five years from the date of commencement of this Act.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_955\">(2)   Every order made under this section shall, as soon as may be after  it\nis made, be laid before each House of Parliament.\u201d\n\n\nThe Statement of Objects and Reasons of the Amendment Act\n\n\n                      \u201cStatement of Objects and Reasons\n\nThe Judges of the Supreme Court are appointed under clause  (2)  of  <a href=\"/doc/1164880/\" id=\"a_2530\">article\n124</a> and the Judges of the High Courts are  appointed  under  clause  (1)  of\n<a href=\"/doc/1682952/\" id=\"a_2531\">article 217</a> of the Constitution, by the President.  The  Ad-hoc  Judges  and\nretired Judges for the Supreme Court  are  appointed  under  clause  (1)  of\n<a href=\"/doc/1688766/\" id=\"a_2532\">article  127</a>  and  <a href=\"/doc/908489/\" id=\"a_2533\">article  128</a>  of  the  Constitution   respectively.   The\nappointment of Additional Judges and Acting Judges for  the  High  Court  is\nmade under <a href=\"/doc/570782/\" id=\"a_2534\">article 224</a> and the appointment of retired  Judges  for  sittings\nof the High Courts is made under  <a href=\"/doc/165551653/\" id=\"a_2535\">article  224A</a>  of  the  Constitution.  The\ntransfer of Judges from one High Court to another High Court is made by  the\nPresident after consultation with the Chief Justice of  India  under  clause\n(1) of <a href=\"/doc/819787/\" id=\"a_2536\">article 222</a> of the Constitution.\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1587\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_723\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"753224\" id=\"span_724\">2. The Supreme Court in the  matter  of  Supreme  Court  <a href=\"/doc/753224/\" id=\"a_2537\">Advocates-on-Record\nAssociation Vs. Union of India</a>  in  the  year  1993,  and  in  its  Advisory\nOpinion in the year 1998 in the <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2538\">Third Judges</a> case,  had  interpreted  clause\n(2) of <a href=\"/doc/1164880/\" id=\"a_2539\">article 124</a> and clause (1) of <a href=\"/doc/1682952/\" id=\"a_2540\">article 217</a> of  the  Constitution  with\nrespect to the meaning of \u201cconsultation\u201d as \u201cconcurrence\u201d.  Consequently,  a\nMemorandum of Procedure for appointment of Judges to the Supreme  Court  and\nHigh Courts was formulated, and is being followed for appointment.</span></span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1588\">3.  After  review   of   the   relevant   constitutional   provisions,   the\npronouncements of the Supreme Court and consultations with eminent  Jurists,\nit is felt that a broad  based  National  Judicial  Appointments  Commission\nshould be established for making recommendations for appointment  of  Judges\nof the Supreme Court and High Courts. The said Commission  would  provide  a\nmeaningful role to the judiciary,  the  executive  and  eminent  persons  to\npresent their view points and make the participants accountable, while  also\nintroducing transparency in the selection process.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1589\">4. The Constitution (One Hundred and Twenty-first Amendment) Bill,  2014  is\nan enabling constitutional amendment for  amending  relevant  provisions  of\nthe Constitution  and  for  setting  up  a  National  Judicial  Appointments\nCommission. The proposed Bill seeks to insert new <a href=\"/doc/140653939/\" id=\"a_2541\">articles  124A</a><a href=\"/doc/61056230/\" id=\"a_2542\">,  124B</a>  and\n<a href=\"/doc/16427836/\" id=\"a_2543\">124C</a> after <a href=\"/doc/1164880/\" id=\"a_2544\">article 124</a> of the Constitution. The said Bill also provides  for\nthe  composition  and  the  functions  of  the  proposed  National  Judicial\nAppointments Commission. Further, it provides that Parliament may,  by  law,\nregulate the procedure for appointment of Judges and  empower  the  National\nJudicial Appointments Commission to lay down  procedure  by  regulation  for\nthe  discharge  of  its  functions,  manner  of  selection  of  persons  for\nappointment and such other matters as may be considered necessary.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1590\">5. The proposed Bill seeks to  broad  base  the  method  of  appointment  of\nJudges in the Supreme  Court  and  High  Courts,  enables  participation  of\njudiciary, executive and eminent persons and ensures  greater  transparency,\naccountability and objectivity in the  appointment  of  the  Judges  in  the\nSupreme Court and High Court.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1591\">6. The Bill seeks to achieve the above objectives.\u201d\n\n\n\nITEM NO.1A         COURT NO.4           SECTION X,PIL,XVIA\n\n               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A\n                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS\n\nWrit Petition(s)(Civil)  No(s).  13/2015\n\nSUPREME COURT ADVOCATES-ON-RECORD\nASSOCIATION AND ANOTHER                            Petitioner(s)\n\n                                VERSUS\n\nUNION OF INDIA                                 Respondent(s)\nWITH\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_725\">W.P.(C) No. 23/2015</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_726\">W.P.(C) No. 70/2015</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_727\">W.P.(C) No. 83/2015</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_728\">T.P.(C) No. 391/2015</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_729\">W.P.(C) No. 108/2015</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_730\">W.P.(C) No. 124/2015</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_731\">W.P.(C) No. 14/2015</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_732\">W.P.(C) No. 18/2015</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_733\">W.P.(C) No. 24/2015</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_734\">W.P.(C) No. 209/2015</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_735\">W.P.(C) No.309/2015</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_736\">W.P.(C) No. 310/2015</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_737\">W.P.(C) No.323/2015</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_738\">T.P.(C) No.971/2015</span>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_739\">W.P.(C) No.341/2015</span>\n\n[HEARD BY HON&#x27;BLE JAGDISH SINGH  KHEHAR,  HON&#x27;BLE  J. Chelameswar,\nHon&#x27;ble Madan B. Lokur, Hon&#x27;ble  Kurian  Joseph  and  Hon&#x27;ble  Adarsh  Kumar\nGoel, JJ. Pronounced the separate judgments, the prayer for reference  to  a\nlarger Bench, and for reconsideration of the Second and Third  Judges  cases\n[(1993) 4 SCC 441, and (1998) 7 SCC  739,  respectively]  is  rejected;  the\n<a href=\"/doc/195108305/\" id=\"a_2545\">Constitution   (Ninety-ninth    Amendment)    Act,    2014</a>    is    declared\nunconstitutional and void; the  <a href=\"/doc/142995521/\" id=\"a_2546\">National  Judicial  Appointments  Commission\nAct, 2014</a>, is declared unconstitutional and void; the system of  appointment\nof Judges to the Supreme Court, and Chief Justices and Judges  to  the  High\nCourts; and transfer of Chief Justices and Judges of High  Courts  from  one\nHigh Court, to another, as existing prior to the <a href=\"/doc/195108305/\" id=\"a_2547\">Constitution  (Ninety-ninth\nAmendment) Act, 2014</a> (called the \u201ccollegium  system\u201d),  is  declared  to  be\noperative; and to consider introduction of  appropriate  measures,  if  any,\nfor an improved working of the \u201ccollegium system\u201d, list on 3.11.2015.\n</p>\n\n<pre id=\"pre_15\">(Renuka Sadana)                         (Parveen Kr. class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"753224\" id=\"span_749\">Supreme Court <a href=\"/doc/753224/\" id=\"a_2548\">Advocates-on-Record Association  &amp; Others v. Union of\nIndia</a>, (1993) 4 SCC 441\n[101]  The position of the Presiding  Judge  on  this  Bench  hearing  these\ncases of constitutional challenge is not  consistent  with  (and  apparently\nconflicts with) his position as a member of the \u2018Collegium\u2019; and  is  likely\nto be seen as  such;  always  bearing  in  mind  that  if  the  Constitution\nAmendment and the  statute  pertaining  thereto  are  held  constitutionally\nvalid and are upheld, the present Presiding Judge would no  longer  be  part\nof the  Collegium  \u2013  The  Collegium,  it  must  be  acknowledged  exercises\nsignificant constitutional power.</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1594\">[102]  Order dated 22.04.2015 insofar as it is relevant reads thus:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_956\">      \u201cA preliminary objection, whether Justice Jagdish Singh Khehar  should\npreside over this Bench, by virtue of  his  being  the  fourth  senior  most\nJudge of this Court, also happens to be  a  member  of  the  collegium,  was\nraised by the petitioners. Elaborate submissions were made  by  the  learned\ncounsel for the petitioners and  the  respondents.  After  hearing  all  the\nlearned counsel, we are of the unanimous opinion that  we  do  not  see  any\nreason in law requiring Justice Jagdish Singh Khehar to recuse himself  from\nhearing the matter. Reasons will follow.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_957\">      Issue rule. \u201d\n\n[103]  The expression \u2018recuse\u2019 according to the New Oxford Dictionary\nEnglish means \u2013 (the act of a Judge) to excuse himself from a case because\nof possible conflict of interest for lack of impartiality.\n[104]   R. Grant Hammond, Judicial Recusal: Principles, Process and\nProblems, (Hart Publishing, 2009).\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_958\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"990570\" id=\"span_750\">[105]  Regina v. Bow Street Metropolitan Stipendiary  Magistrate,  Ex  parte\n<a href=\"/doc/990570/\" id=\"a_2549\">Pinochet Ugarte, (1999) 1 All E.R. 577\n[106]   P.D. Dinakaran(1) v. Judges Inquiry Committee</a>, (2011) 8 SCC 380,\nparas 49 to 53.</span>\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_959\">[107]  Wakefield Local Board of Health v. West Riding and Grimsby Rly Co.\n(1865) 1 Q.B. 84.\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1595\">[108]   R V. Byles ex p. Hollidge (1912) 77 J.P. 40; R. v. Nailsworth\nLicensing Justices ex p. Bird [1953] 1 W.L.R. 1046; R v. Lilydale\nMagistrates Court ex p. Ciccone [1973] V.R. 122; and see R. v. Antrim\nJustices [1895] 2 I.R. 603; Tolputt (H.) <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"130313222\" id=\"span_751\">&amp; <a href=\"/doc/130313222/\" id=\"a_2550\">Co. Ltd. v. Mole</a> [1911] 1 K.B.\n836; Corrigan v. Irish Land Commission [1977] I.R. 317.</span>\n</p><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_752\">[109]</span>\n\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1596\">         The details of which are already noted in the judgment of my\nbrother Khehar, J.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1597\">[110]  <a href=\"/doc/93595316/\" id=\"a_2551\">Section 6 (6)</a>.  \u201cThe Commission shall  not  recommend  a  person  for\nappointment under this section if any two members of the Commission  do  not\nagree for such recommendation.\u201d\n\n[111]  \u201cPara 520.  There is another fact of life which, however  unpleasant,\ncannot be denied and  this  is  that  precious  little  are  our  masses  or\nlitigants concerned with which Judge is appointed or not appointed or  which\none  is  continued  or  not  continued.  The  high   sounding   concept   of\nindependence  of  judiciary  or  primacy  of  one  or  the  other   of   the\nConstitutional functionaries or  the  mode  of  effective  consultation  are\nmatters of academic interest in which our masses are  least  interested.  On\nthe other hand, they are mainly concerned with dangerous forces at work  and\nevils reflected in economic-pressures,  inflationary  tendencies,  gruelling\npoverty, emancipation of women, maintenance  of  law  and  order,  food  and\nclothing,  bread  and  butter,  and  above  all  the  serious   problem   of\nunemployment,\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1598\">      521. It is only a sizeable section of the intellectuals consisting  of\nthe press and  the  lawyers  who  have  made  a  prestigious  issue  of  the\nindependence of judiciary. I can fully  understand  that  lawyers  or  other\npersons directly connected with the administration of  justice  may  have  a\ngrievance  however  ill-founded  that  proper   selection   of   Judges   or\ninterference  with  the  appointment  of  Judges   strictly   according   to\nconstitutional  provisions  may  mar  the  institution  of   judiciary   and\ntherefore they may to some extent be justified in vindicating their  rights.\nBut at the same time, however biting or bitter, distasteful  and  diabolical\nit may seem to be, the fact remains that the masses in general  are  not  at\nall concerned with these legal niceties and  so  far  as  administration  of\njustice is concerned they merely want that their  cases  should  be  decided\nquickly by Judges who generate confidence.\u201c\n[112]   5 U.S 137 (1803)\n[113]   Supreme Court of Canada in Valente v. Queen, (1985) 2 SCR 673\n\n[114]   \u201c\u2026 judges commissions be made quamdiu se bene gesserint,  and  their\nsalaries ascertained and established; but upon the address  of  both  houses\nof parliament it may be lawful  to  remove  them.\u201d   This  clause  has  been\nrepealed by ____\n[115]  Sir William  Blackstone\u2019s,  Commentaries  on  the  Laws  of  England,\n(1765) Vol. I p. 269\n\n[116]  Article III Section I. The  judicial  power  of  the  United  States,\nshall be vested in one Supreme Court, and in such  inferior  courts  as  the\nCongress may from time to time ordain and establish.  The  judges,  both  of\nthe supreme and inferior  courts,  shall  hold  their  offices  during  good\nbehaviour, and shall,  at  stated  times,  receive  for  their  services,  a\ncompensation, which shall not be  diminished  during  their  continuance  in\noffice.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1599\">[117]   Article II <a href=\"/doc/11284129/\" id=\"a_2552\">Section 4</a>. The President, Vice President  and  all  civil\nofficers of the United States, shall be removed from office  on  impeachment\nfor,  and  conviction  of,  treason,  bribery,  or  other  high  crimes  and\nmisdemeanors.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1600\">[118]  Section 72.  Judges&#x27; appointment, tenure, and remuneration:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_960\">      \u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0The Justices of the High Court \u2026.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_961\">      \u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0 (ii)\u00a0 shall not be removed except \u2026\u2026 on an address  from  both\nHouses of the Parliament in the same session, praying for  such  removal  on\nthe ground of proved misbehaviour or incapacity;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_962\">      \u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0(iii)\u00a0 shall receive such remuneration as the  Parliament  may\nfix; but the remuneration shall not be diminished during  their  continuance\nin office.\n</blockquote>      \u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1601\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_753\">[119]  The British North America Act, 1867 renamed by the Amendment in 1982\nas the Constitution Act, 1867\n[120]  Commonwealth of Australia Constitution Act, 1900.\n[121]   The  existing  constitution and organization of constitutional\ncourts in this country is discussed in some detail by Justice  Verma in the\n<a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2553\">Second Judges</a> case at paras 444, 445, 446.</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1602\">      444.    The Government of India Act, 1919 provided in Section 101 for\nthe Constitution of High Courts; and the appointment of the Chief Justice\nand the permanent Judges was in the absolute discretion of the Crown,\nsubject only the prescribed conditions of eligibility. The tenure of their\noffice, according to Section 102, was dependent entirely on the Crown\u2019s\npleasure.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1603\">            xxxxx            xxxxxx          xxxxxx           xxxxx\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1604\">      445.  <a href=\"/doc/93056159/\" id=\"a_2554\">Then, in the Government of India Act, 1935</a>, provision for the\nestablishment and Constitution of the Federal Court was made in Section\n200, while the Constitution of High Courts was provided for in Section 220.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1605\">      xxxxx      xxxxxx           xxxxxx           xxxxx\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1606\">      446.  Thus, even under the <a href=\"/doc/93056159/\" id=\"a_2555\">Government of India Act, 1935</a>,\nappointments of Judges of the Federal Court and the High Courts were in the\nabsolute discretion of the Crown or, in other words, of the executive, with\nno specific provision for consultation with the Chief Justice in the\nappointment process.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1607\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1152518\" id=\"span_754\">[122]   <a href=\"/doc/1152518/\" id=\"a_2556\">L Chandra Kumar &amp; Ors v. Union of India &amp; Ors.</a>, (1997) 3 SCC 261,\npara 78\n\n[123]   <a href=\"/doc/41081345/\" id=\"a_2557\">Article 124(4)</a> A Judge of the Supreme Court  shall  not  be  removed\nfrom his office except by an order of the President passed after an  address\nby each House of Parliament supported by a majority of the total  membership\nof that House and by a majority of not less than two thirds of  the  members\nof that House present and voting has been presented to the President in  the\nsame session for such removal  on  the  ground  of  proved  misbehaviour  or\nincapacity.</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1608\">[124]   <a href=\"/doc/648860/\" id=\"a_2558\">Article 217(1)(b)</a> A Judge may be removed from his office by the\nPresident in the manner provided in clause (4) of <a href=\"/doc/1164880/\" id=\"a_2559\">article 124</a> for the\nremoval of a Judge of the Supreme Court;\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1609\">[125]  Under the proviso to <a
