Union of India, 1991 Supp (1)  SCC  574,  at  page\n586 :\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_970\">      Para 18. \u201cWe gather that the Kerala High Court  where  the  sanctioned\nstrength has been reduced by 2, has a sanctioned strength of  22  while  its\npendency as on  January  1,  1990  being  34,330  cases  justifies  a  Judge\nstrength of almost 50 on the basis of the measure of  650  cases  per  Judge\nper year. We  intend  to  indicate  that  there  was  no  justification  for\nreduction of the sanctioned strength.\u201d\n\n[151]  Para 19. \u201cFor the present we suggest to government  that  the  matter\nshould be reviewed  from  time  to  time  and  steps  should  be  taken  for\ndetermining the sanctioned strength in a pragmatic way on the basis  of  the\nexisting need.  If  there  be  no  correlation  between  the  need  and  the\nsanctioned  strength  and  the  provision  of  Judge-manpower   is   totally\ninadequate, the  necessary  consequence  has  to  be  backlog  and  sluggish\nenforcement of the Rule of Law. \u2026\u2026\u201d\n\n[152]  Abhinav Chandrachud, The Informal Constitution :  Unwritten  Criteria\nin Selecting Judges for the  Supreme  Court  of  India,  (Oxford  University\nPress, United Kingdom 2014)  See Pages 113 to 120\n[153]  From 1978, Governments at the State level and the Union level  ceased\nto be necessarily of the same political party.  Regional  parties  in  parts\nof the country had captured power putting an end to one party rule  at  both\nthe levels.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_971\">[154] Para 421 - These questions have to be considered  in  the  context  of\nthe independence of the judiciary, as a part of the basic structure  of  the\nConstitution, to secure the \u2018rule of law\u2019, essential  for  the  preservation\nof the democratic system. The broad scheme of separation of  powers  adopted\nin the Constitution, together with the directive  principle  of  \u2018separation\nof judiciary from executive\u2019  even  at  the  lowest  strata,  provides  some\ninsight to the true meaning of the relevant provisions in  the  Constitution\nrelating to the composition of the  judiciary.  The  construction  of  these\nprovisions  must   accord   with   these   fundamental   concepts   in   the\nconstitutional scheme to  preserve  the  vitality  and  promote  the  growth\nessential for retaining the Constitution as a vibrant organism.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_972\">[155] <a href=\"/doc/192717/\" id=\"a_2567\">Article 50.</a> Separation of judiciary from executive \u2013 \u201cThe State  shall\ntake steps to separate the  judiciary  from  the  executive  in  the  public\nservices of the State.\u201d\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_757\">[156]</span>\n      [157] Form of oath or affirmation to be made  by  the  Judges  of  the\nSupreme Court and the Comptroller and Auditor-General of India:\u2014\n      \u201cI, A.B., having been appointed Chief Justice  (or  a  Judge)  of  the\nSupreme Court of India (or Comptroller  and  Auditor-General  of  India)  do\nswear in the name of God that I will bear true  faith  and  solemnly  affirm\nfaith and allegiance to the Constitution of India  as  by  law  established,\nthat I will uphold the sovereignty and integrity of India, that I will  duly\nand faithfully and to  the  best  of  my  ability,  knowledge  and  judgment\nperform the duties of  my  office  without  fear  or  favour,  affection  or\nillwill and that I will uphold the Constitution and the laws.\u201d\n\n[158]  Form of oath or affirmation to be made by the Judges of a High\nCourt:\u2014\n      \u201cI, A.B., having been appointed Chief Justice  (or  a  Judge)  of  the\nHigh Court at (or of) \u2026\u2026\u2026-\u2026.. do swear in the name of God that I  will  bear\nsolemnly affirm true faith and allegiance to the Constitution  of  India  as\nby law established, that I will uphold  the  sovereignty  and  integrity  of\nIndia, that I will duly and faithfully  and  to  the  best  of  my  ability,\nknowledge and judgment perform the duties  of  my  office  without  fear  or\nfavour, affection or ill-will and that I will uphold  the  Constitution  and\nthe laws.\u201d\n[159]  <a href=\"/doc/70262168/\" id=\"a_2568\">Article 124(5).</a> Parliament may by law regulate the procedure for  the\npresentation of an address and  for  the  investigation  and  proof  of  the\nmisbehaviour or incapacity of a Judge under clause (4).\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_973\">[160]  <a href=\"/doc/41081345/\" id=\"a_2569\">Article 124(4).</a> A Judge of the Supreme Court  shall  not  be  removed\nfrom his office except by an order of the President passed after an  address\nby each House of Parliament supported by a majority of the total  membership\nof that House and by a majority of not less than two thirds of  the  members\nof that House present and voting has been presented to the President in  the\nsame session for such removal  on  the  ground  of  proved  misbehaviour  or\nincapacity.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_974\">[161]  (per Hon. Pandian, J.) - Para 49. \u201cone other  basic  and  inseparable\nvital  condition  is  absolutely   necessary   for   timely   securing   the\nindependence of judiciary; and that concerns  the  methodology  followed  in\nthe matter  of  sponsoring,  selecting  and  appointing  a  proper  and  fit\ncandidate to the  (Supreme  Court  or  High  Court)  higher  judiciary.  The\nholistic  condition  is  a  major  component  that  goes  along  with  other\nconstitutionally  guaranteed  service  conditions  in  securing  a  complete\nindependence  of  judiciary.  To  say  differently,  a  healthy  independent\njudiciary can be said to have been firstly secured by accomplishment of  the\nincreasingly important condition in regard to the method of  appointment  of\njudges and, secondly, protected by the fulfilment of the rights,  privileges\nand other service conditions. The resultant inescapable conclusion  is  that\nonly the consummation or totality of all the requisite conditions  beginning\nwith the method and strategy of selection and  appointment  of  judges  will\nsecure and protect the independence of the judiciary.  Otherwise,  not  only\nwill the credibility of the judiciary  stagger  and  decline  but  also  the\nentire judicial system will explode which in turn  may  cripple  the  proper\nfunctioning of democracy and the philosophy of this cherished  concept  will\nbe only a myth rather than a reality.\u201d\n\n      (per Hon. Kuldip Singh, J.) \u2013  Para  335.  \u201cThen  the  question  which\ncomes up for consideration is, can there be an  independent  judiciary  when\nthe power of appointment of judges vests  in  the  executive?  To  say  yes,\nwould be illogical. The independence of  judiciary  is  inextricably  linked\nand connected with the constitutional process of appointment  of  judges  of\nthe higher judiciary. \u2018Independence of Judiciary\u2019 is the  basic  feature  of\nour Constitution and if it means what we  have  discussed  above,  then  the\nFramers of the Constitution could have never intended to give this power  to\nthe executive. Even otherwise the Governments \u2014 Central or the State  \u2014  are\nparties before the Courts in large number  of  cases.  The  Union  Executive\nhave  vital  interests  in  various  important  matters   which   come   for\nadjudication before the Apex Court. The executive  \u2014  in  one  form  or  the\nother \u2014 is the largest single litigant before the courts. In  this  view  of\nthe matter the judiciary being the mediator \u2014 between  the  people  and  the\nexecutive \u2014 the Framers of the Constitution could not have  left  the  final\nauthority to appoint the Judges of the Supreme Court and of the High  Courts\nin the hands of the executive.\u201d\n\n      (per Hon. Verma, J.) \u2013 Para 447.  \u201cWhen  the  Constitution  was  being\ndrafted, there was general agreement that the appointments of Judges in  the\nsuperior judiciary should not be left to  the  absolute  discretion  of  the\nexecutive,  and  this  was  the  reason  for  the  provision  made  in   the\nConstitution imposing the obligation to consult the Chief Justice  of  India\nand the  Chief  Justice  of  the  High  Court.  This  was  done  to  achieve\nindependence of the Judges of the superior judiciary even  at  the  time  of\ntheir appointment,  instead  of  confining  it  only  to  the  provision  of\nsecurity of tenure and other conditions of  service  after  the  appointment\nwas made. It was realised that the independence of the judiciary had  to  be\nsafeguarded not merely by providing security of tenure and other  conditions\nof service after the appointment, but also by preventing  the  influence  of\npolitical  considerations  in  making  the  appointments,  if  left  to  the\nabsolute discretion of the executive as  the  appointing  authority.  It  is\nthis  reason  which  impelled  the  incorporation  of  the   obligation   of\nconsultation with the Chief Justice of India and the Chief  Justice  of  the\nHigh Court in <a href=\"/doc/1995948/\" id=\"a_2570\">Articles 124(2)</a> and <a href=\"/doc/259647/\" id=\"a_2571\">217(1).</a> The Constituent  Assembly  Debates\ndisclose this purpose in prescribing for such consultation, even though  the\nappointment is ultimately an executive act.\u201d\n\n\n\n\n\n[162]  See the articles of  Lord  Templeman\u2019s  favourable  opinion  and  the\ncritical view of Lord Cooke of\n          Thorndon published in the book titled Supreme but  not  Infallible\n\u2013 Oxford University Press \u2013 2000 A.D.\n\n      \u201cArticle 124 of the Constitution empowers  the  President  (acting  on\nthe advice of the Prime Minister and Cabinet) to appoint the judges  of  the\nSupreme Court.  The President is given a discretion about consulting  judges\nof the Supreme Court and High Courts but in the case of  appointments  of  a\nJudge other than the Chief Justice, the Chief Justice of India shall  always\nbe consulted.  Similarly, <a href=\"/doc/1682952/\" id=\"a_2572\">Article 217</a> requires the Chief  Justice  of  India\nto be consulted concerning the appointment of a judge of the High  Court  of\na state.  In 1993, in the Supreme  Court  Advocates  on  Record  Association\ncase the Supreme Court by  a  majority  held  that,  having  regard  to  the\nindependence of the judiciary and the separation of powers which  the  Court\nheld to be implicit in the Constitution, the views of the Chief  Justice  of\nIndia expressed when he was consulted must be supreme.  The Court also  laid\ndown  guidelines  governing  the  appointment  and  duration  of  office  of\ntemporary acting judges.  The majority decision has been  criticized  as  an\nextension of the  meaning  of  the  word  \u2018consultation\u2019.   <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1977799\" id=\"span_758\">However,  having\nregard to the earlier experience in India of attempts by  the  executive  to\ninfluence the personalities and attitudes of members of the  judiciary,  and\nhaving regard to the successful attempts made in  Pakistan  to  control  the\njudiciary and having regard to the unfortunate results  of  the  appointment\nof Supreme Court judges of the United States by  the  President  subject  to\napproval by Congress, the majority decision of te Supreme Court of India  in\nthe Advocates on  Record case marks a welcome assertion of the  independence\nof the judiciary and  is  the  best  method  of  obtaining  appointments  of\nintegrity and quality, a precedent method which  the  British  could  follow\nsuch advantage.\u201d                        ---- Lord Templeman\n\n      \u201cAll in all, the opinion of the Supreme  Court  in  the  third  <a href=\"/doc/1977799/\" id=\"a_2573\">Judges</a>\ncase must be one of the most remarkable rulings ever  issued  by  a  supreme\nnational appellate court in the coomon law world.  Since, in some  respects,\nI  have  had  to  voice  respectful  doubts  about  the  soundness  of   the\nconstitutional foundations of that opinion\u2026.\u201d            ---- Lord Cooke  of\nThorndon\n\n[163]  Iyer, V.R. Krishna, Judiciary  :  A  reform  agenda  \u2013II,  The  Hindu\n(online edition) 15.08.2002\n\n[164]  \u201cAn Independent Judiciary\u201d \u2013 speech delivered  by  Ms.  Justice  Ruma\nPal at the 5th V.M. Tarkunde Memorial Lecture on 10th November 2011.</span>\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_975\">      <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"52257\" id=\"span_759\">\u201cAs I have said elsewhere \u2018the process by which a judge  is  appointed\nto a superior court is one of the best kept secrets in  this  country.   The\nvery secrecy of the process leads to an inadequate input of  information  as\nto the abilities and suitability of a possible candidate for appointment  as\na judge. A chance remark, a rumour or even  third-hand  information  may  be\nsufficient to damn a judge\u2019s prospects. Contrariwise a  personal  friendship\nor unspoken obligation may colour a recommendation.   Consensus  within  the\ncollegium is sometimes resolved through a  trade-off  resulting  in  dubious\nappointments  with  disastrous  consequences  for  the  litigants  and   the\ncredibility of the judicial  system.   Besides,  institutional  independence\nhas also been compromised by growing sycophancy and  \u2018lobbying\u2019  within  the\nsystem.\u201d\n[165]   <a href=\"/doc/52257/\" id=\"a_2574\">Shanti Bhushan</a> (supra) - Para 2. The primary ground  urged  is  that\nthe opinion of the Chief Justice of India  has  to  be  formed  collectively\nafter taking into account  the  views  of  his  senior  colleagues  who  are\nrequired to be consulted  by  him  for  the  formation  of  opinion  and  no\nappointment can be made unless it is in conformity with  the  final  opinion\nof the Chief Justice of India formed in the aforesaid manner.   \u2026.   It  is,\ntherefore, submitted that the appointment of Respondent No.2 as a  permanent\nJudge as notified on 2.2.2007 has no sanctity in law.</span>\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_976\">[166]   Mehta, Pratap Bhanu, \u2018Whom do you trust\u2019, The  Indian  Express,  May\n14, 2015 \u2013 \u201cThe implicit constitutional accusation is  this.  The  judiciary\nhad, through improvisation, created  a  method  of  appointing  judges  that\neffectively sidelined other branches of  government.  This  arrangement  was\ntolerated, not because  it  conformed  to  a  constitutional  text  or  some\nhallowed  principle,  but   because   it   seemed   to   maintain   judicial\nindependence. The experience of the 1970s made  the  prospect  of  political\npacking of the judiciary a live fear. This arrangement is being  challenged,\nnot because we have discovered a new principle, but because the  credibility\nof  the  judiciary  has  declined.  We  are,  in  effect,  saying  that  any\narrangement that relies solely on the judiciary  has  proved  untrustworthy.\nThose challenging the NJAC are relying on the ghost of  the  1970s:  Do  you\nreally want the political class to have a greater say in appointments?  Both\nbranches of government are accusing  each  other  of  not  being  worthy  of\ntrust. In the process, they have dragged each other  down.  The  problem  is\nthat both are right.\u201d\n[167]   See para 31 (supra)\n[168]  Constituent\u00a0Assembly\u00a0Debates,\u00a024th May\u00a01949 (Vol.\u00a0 VIII)\n\n[169]  On 24th May 1949 while draft <a href=\"/doc/41049/\" id=\"a_2575\">Article 103</a> of  the  draft  Constitution\nwas being discussed corresponding to  present  <a href=\"/doc/1164880/\" id=\"a_2576\">Article  124</a>,  four  members,\nProf. Shibban Lal Saksena and Prof. K.T. Shah, who  represented  the  United\nProvinces of Bihar and Mr. B. Pocker Sahib and Mr. Mahboob Ali  Beig  Sahib,\nwho represented Madras Provinces suggested amendments to  <a href=\"/doc/41049/\" id=\"a_2577\">Article  103</a>,  the\nrelevant portions of which read as follows:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_977\">      \u201cProf. Shibban Lal Saksena:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_978\">      That  for  clause  (2)  of  <a href=\"/doc/41049/\" id=\"a_2578\">article  103</a>,  the  following  clauses  be\nsubstituted-\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_979\">      (2)   The Chief Justice of Bharat, who shall be the Chief  Justice  of\nthe  Supreme  Court,  shall  be  appointed  by  the  President  subject   to\nconfirmation by two-thirds majority  of  Parliament  assembled  in  a  joint\nsession of both the Houses of Parliament.\u201d\n\n      \u201cProf. K.T. Shah:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_980\">      Every judge of the Supreme Court shall be appointed by  the  President\nby warrant under his hand and seal after consultation with  the  Council  of\nStates and such of the judges of the Supreme Court and of  the  High  Courts\nin the States as may be necessary for the  purpose  and  shall  hold  office\nuntil he attains the age of sixty-five years.\u201d\n\n      \u201cMr. B. Pocker Sahib:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_981\">      That for clause (2) and the first proviso of  clause  (2)  of  <a href=\"/doc/41049/\" id=\"a_2579\">article\n103</a>, the following be substituted-\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_982\">      (2)   Every judge of the Supreme Court other than  the  Chief  Justice\nof India shall be appointed by the President by warrant under his  hand  and\nseal after consultation with the concurrence of the Chief Justice of  India;\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1627\">and the Chief Justice of India shall be appointed  by  the  President  by  a\nwarrant under his hand and seal after consultation with the  judges  of  the\nSupreme Court and the Chief Justices of the High Court  in  the  States  and\nevery judge of the Supreme Court.\u201d\n\n      \u201cMr. Mahboob Ali Beig Sahib:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_983\">      That in the first proviso to clause (2) of <a href=\"/doc/41049/\" id=\"a_2580\">article 103</a>, for the  words\n\u2018the Chief Justice of India shall always be consulted\u2019 the words  \u2018it  shall\nbe  made  with  the  concurrence  of  the  Chief  Justice   of   India\u2019   be\nsubstituted.\u201d\n\n\n[170]  See Footnote 50 (supra)\n[171]   Heard by a Bench of 11 Judges and decided by a majority of 6:5\n[172]  <a href=\"/doc/15421639/\" id=\"a_2581\">Article 13(3)(a).</a>  \u201claw\u201d  includes  any  Ordinance,  order,  bye-law,\nrule, regulation, notification, custom or usage having in the  territory  of\nIndia the force of law.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_984\">[173]  <a href=\"/doc/772605/\" id=\"a_2582\">Article 13(2).</a> The State shall not make any law which takes  away  or\nabridges  the  rights  conferred  by  this  Part  and  any   law   made   in\ncontravention of this clause shall, to the extent of the  contravention,  be\nvoid.\n</blockquote><span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_760\">[174]</span>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_985\">      [175]  (Per Sikri, CJ) \u2013 Para 292,  \u201cfundamental rights cannot be\nabrogated but reasonable abridgements of fundamental rights can be effected\nin public interest\u201d. \u2026  \u201cThat every provision of the Constitution can be\namended provided in the result the basic foundation and structure of the\nConstitution remains the same.  The basic structure may be said to consist\nof the following features:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_986\">      (1)   Supremacy of the Constitution;\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_987\">      (2)   Republican and Democratic form of Government;\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_988\">      (3)   Secular character of the Constitution;\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_989\">      (4)   Separation of powers between the legislature, the executive  and\nthe judiciary;\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_990\">      (5)   Federal character of the Constitution.\u201d\n\n      (Per Shelat, J. who spoke for himself and Grover,  J.)  \u2013  Paras  582,\n583, \u201cthere can be no difficulty in discerning that  the  following  can  be\nregarded as the  basic  elements  of  the  constitutional  structure.  These\ncannot be catalogued but can only be illustrated:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_991\">      (1)   The supremacy of the Constitution.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_992\">      (2)   Republican and Democratic form of  government  and   sovereignty\nof the country.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_993\">      (3)   Secular and federal character of the Constitution.\n      (4)   Demarcation of power between the Legislature, the executive  and\nthe judiciary.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_994\">      (5)   The dignity of the individual secured by  the  various  freedoms\nand basic rights in Part III and  the  mandate  to  build  a  welfare  State\ncontained in Part IV.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_995\">      (6)   The unity and the integrity of the Nation.\u201d\n\n      and, therefore, \u201cthe power under <a href=\"/doc/594125/\" id=\"a_2583\">Article 368</a> is wide enough to  permit\namendment of each and every article \u2026 so long as its basic elements are  not\nabrogated or denuded of their identity\u201d.\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1628\">      (Per Hegde,  J,  who  also  spoke  for  Mukherjea,  J.)  \u2013  Para  666,\n\u201cParliament has no power to abrogate or emasculate  the  basic  elements  or\nfundamental features of the Constitution such as the sovereignty  of  India,\nthe democratic character of our  polity,  the  unity  of  the  country,  the\nessential features of the individual freedoms secured to  the  citizens.\u201d  \u2026\nand \u201cmandate to build a welfare State and egalitarian society.\u201d\n\n      (Per P. Jaganmohan  Reddy,  J.)  \u2013  paras  1159,  1162,  \u201cA  sovereign\ndemocratic republic. Parliamentary democracy, the three organs of the  State\n\u2026 constitute the basic structure.\u201d  He further  held  that  \u201cwithout  either\nthe fundamental rights or  directive  principles  it  cannot  be  democratic\nrepublic.  Therefore, the power of amendment under <a href=\"/doc/594125/\" id=\"a_2584\">Article 368</a>  \u2026..  is  not\nwide enough to totally abrogate \u2026.. any one of  the  fundamental  rights  or\nother essential elements of the basic  structure  of  the  Constitution  and\ndestroy its identity\u201d.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1629\">      (Per Khanna, J.) \u2013 para  1426,, \u201cthe power under <a href=\"/doc/594125/\" id=\"a_2585\">Article 368</a> does not\ntake within its sweep the power to destroy the old Constitution\u201d \u2026 means\n\u201cthe retention of the basic structure or framework of the old Constitution\u201d\n\u2026 \u201cit is not permissible to touch the foundation or to alter the basic\ninstitutional pattern.\u201d   According to Justice Khanna, \u201csuch limitations\nare inherent and implicit in the word \u201camendment\u201d.\u201d\n\n[176]   See the summary of the majority of the judgment signed by 9 Judges,\np. 1007 of (1973) 4 SCC 225.\n</p>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1630\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"799151\" id=\"span_761\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1569395\" id=\"span_762\">[177]    The  judgment  in  <a href=\"/doc/1569395/\" id=\"a_2586\">Indira  Nehru  Gandhi</a>  case  (supra)  is  neatly\nsummarised by Chandrachud, J. in <a href=\"/doc/799151/\" id=\"a_2587\">Waman Rao</a> case at para 15:</span></span>\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_996\">      \u201c15.\u2026\u2026\u2026 in Indira Gandhi v. Raj Narain <a href=\"/doc/76088191/\" id=\"a_2588\">Article 329-A(4)</a>  was  held  by\nthe Court to be beyond the amending competence of the Parliament  since,  by\nmaking separate and special provisions as to elections to Parliament of  the\nPrime Minister and the speaker, it destroyed  the  basic  structure  of  the\nConstitution. Ray, C.J. based his decision  on  the  ground  that  the  39th\nAmendment by which <a href=\"/doc/190157089/\" id=\"a_2589\">Article 329-A</a> was introduced violated  the  Rule  of  Law\n(p. 418) (SCC p. 44); Khanna, J. based  his  decision  on  the  ground  that\ndemocracy  was  a  basic  feature  of  the  Constitution,   that   democracy\ncontemplates that elections should be free and fair and that the  clause  in\nquestion struck at the basis of free and fair elections (pp.  467  and  471)\n(SCC pp. 87 and 91); Mathew, J. struck down the clause on  the  ground  that\nit was in the nature of legislation ad hominem (p. 513)  (SCC  p.  127)  and\nthat it damaged the democratic structure of the Constitution (p.  515)  (SCC\np. 129); while one of us, Chandrachud, J., held  that  the  clause  was  bad\nbecause it violated the Rule of Law and was  an  outright  negation  of  the\nprinciple of equality which is a basic feature of the Constitution (pp. 663-\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_997\">65) (SCC p. 257).\u201d\n[178]  Para 13. The question which we have to determine on the basis of  the\nmajority view in Kesavananda Bharati is whether  the  amendments  introduced\nby <a href=\"/doc/11284129/\" id=\"a_2590\">Sections 4</a> and 55 of the Constitution (42nd Amendment) Act,  1976  damage\nthe basic structure of the Constitution  by  destroying  any  of  its  basic\nfeatures or essential elements.\n</blockquote>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1631\">[179]  Para 31.  For these reasons, we are of the view  that  the  Amendment\nintroduced by <a href=\"/doc/59006183/\" id=\"a_2591\">Section 4</a> of the  Constitution  (First  Amendment)  Act,  1951\ndoes not damage or destroy the basic structure  of  the  Constitution.   The\nAmendment must, therefore, be upheld on its own merits.\n[180]   See paras 25 to 29 \u2013 Ahmadi, J., para 145 \u2013 Sawant, J., paras 183\nto 186 \u2013 Ramaswamy, J., para 304 \u2013 Jeevan  Reddy, J.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1632\">[181]  In  this  case,  this  Court  had  to  decide  the  validity  of  the\n<a href=\"/doc/54693761/\" id=\"a_2592\">Constitution (Eighty Fifth) Amendment Act</a> 2001 by which <a href=\"/doc/137418340/\" id=\"a_2593\">Article  16(4A)</a>  was\namended in the Constitution with retrospective effect.  It provided  a  rule\nof reservation in the context of the promotion in  the  Government  service.\nSuch an amendment was challenged to be violative of the basic  structure  of\nthe Constitution.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1633\">[182]  Articles 79-84 and 168-173\n[183]  <a href=\"/doc/1463849/\" id=\"a_2594\">Articles 83</a> and <a href=\"/doc/1034417/\" id=\"a_2595\">172\n[184]</a>  <a href=\"/doc/1620503/\" id=\"a_2596\">Article 326\n[185]</a>  <a href=\"/doc/574894/\" id=\"a_2597\">Articles 245</a> and <a href=\"/doc/77052/\" id=\"a_2598\">246</a> etc.\n[186]  <a href=\"/doc/950881/\" id=\"a_2599\">Article 324\n[187]</a>   The National Commission to Review the Working  of  the  Constitution\n(NCRWC), 2002 chaired by Justice M.N. Venkatachaliah\n\n[188]   7.3.7\u00a0\u00a0\u201cThe matter relating to manner of appointment of  judges  had\nbeen debated over  a  decade.\u00a0\u00a0The  Constitution  (Sixty-seventh  Amendment)\nBill, 1990 was introduced on 18th\u00a0May, 1990 (9th\u00a0Lok  Sabha)  providing  for\nthe  institutional  frame  work  of   National   Judicial   Commission   for\nrecommending the appointment of judges to the Supreme Court and the  various\nHigh  Courts.\u00a0\u00a0Further,  it  appears  that  latterly  there  is  a  movement\nthroughout the world to move this function away from the exclusive  fiat  of\nthe  executive  and  involving  some  institutional  frame  work  whereunder\nconsultation with the judiciary at some level is provided for before  making\nsuch appointments.\u00a0\u00a0The system of  consultation  in  some  form  is  already\navailable in Japan, Israel  and  the  UK.\u00a0\u00a0The  Constitution  (Sixty-seventh\nAmendment) Bill, 1990 provided for a  collegium  of  the  Chief  Justice  of\nIndia and two other judges of the Supreme Court for  making  appointment  to\nthe Supreme Court. However,\u00a0it would be worthwhile to have  a  participatory\nmode with the participation of both  the  executive  and  the  judiciary  in\nmaking such recommendations. The Commission proposes the composition of  the\nCollegium which gives due importance  to  and  provides  for  the  effective\nparticipation of both the executive and the judicial wings of the  State  as\nan integrated scheme for the  machinery  for  appointment  of  judges.  This\nCommission,  accordingly,  recommends  the  establishment  of   a   National\nJudicial Commission under the Constitution.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1634\">       \u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0The  National  Judicial  Commission  for  appointment  of\njudges of the Supreme Court shall comprise of:\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_998\">       1.     The  Chief   Justice   of   India                     Chairman\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_999\">                                           <span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"753224\" id=\"span_763\">2.      Two  senior  most  judges\nof the Supreme Court:\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0  Member\n          3\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0The     Union     Minister      for      Law      and\nJustice:\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0   Member\n      4\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0One eminent person nominated  by  the\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0President  after\nconsulting the CJI\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0Member\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\n      \u00a0\u00a0\u00a0\u00a0  The recommendation for the establishment of a National  Judicial\nCommission and its composition are to be treated as integral in view of  the\nneed to preserve the independence of the judiciary.\u201d\n\n[189]  Fali S. Nariman, Before Memory Fades \u2013 An Autobiography,\n      p. 389 \u2013 \u201cIf there is one important case decided by the Supreme  Court\nof India in which I appeared and won, and which I have lived to  regret,  it\nis the decision that goes by the title \u2013 Supreme  Court  <a href=\"/doc/753224/\" id=\"a_2600\">Advocates-on-Record\nAssociation vs Union of India</a>.  It is a decision of the  year  1993  and  is\nbetter known as the Second Judges Case.\u201d\n      p.400 -  \u201cI don\u2019t see what is so special about the first  five  judges\nof the Supreme Court.   They  are  only  the  first  five  in  seniority  of\nappointment \u2013 not necessarily in superiority of wisdom or  competence.     I\nsee no reason why all  the  judges  in  the  highest  court  should  not  be\nconsulted when a proposal is made for appointment of a high court judge  (or\nan eminent advocate) to be a judge of the Supreme Court.   I  would  suggest\nthat the closed-circuit network of five judges should be  disbanded.    They\ninvariably hold their \u2018cards\u2019 close  to  their  chest.   They  ask  no  one.</span>\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1635\">They consult no one but themselves.\u201d\n[190]  See Federist No.51 \u2013 (Hamilton or Madison) (1788)\n[191]  Sudhanshu Ranjan,  \u2018Justice,  Judocracy  and  Democracy  in  India  :\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_1000\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1857950\" id=\"span_764\">Boundaries and Breaches\u2019, p.185-186\n\n[192]  Laurence H. Tribe, God Save this  Honorable  Court,   First  Edition,\np.10-11\n[193]  <a href=\"/doc/1857950/\" id=\"a_2601\">A.K. Gopalan v. State of Madras</a> AIR 1950 SC 27\n[194]   Union of India v. Sankalchand Himatlal Sheth &amp; Anr., (1977) 4 SCC\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_765\">193</span>\n[195]  ADM Jabalpur Vs. S.S. Shukla Etc. Etc. AIR 1976 SC 1207\n\n[196]  Laurence H. Tribe  (American  Constitutional  Law)   Second  Edition,\nPage 2 of Chapter 1 \u201cApproaches to  Constitutional  Analysis\u201d  -  \u201cThat  all\nlawful power derives from the people and must be held in check  to  preserve\ntheir  freedom  is  the  oldest  and  most   central   tenet   of   American\nconstitutionalism.   At  the  outset,  only  a  small  number  of   explicit\nsubstantive limitations on the   exercise  of  governmental  authority  were\nthought essential; in the main, it was believed that personal freedom  could\nbe secured more effectively by decentralization  than  by  express  command.\nFrom the thought of seventeenth century English liberals,  particularly,  as\nelaborated in eighteenth century France by Montesquieu,  the  Constitution\u2019s\nframers  had  derived  the  conviction  that  human  rights  could  best  be\npreserved  by  inaction  and  indirection-shielded   behind   the   lay   of\ndeliberately fragmented centers of countervailing power, in a vision  almost\nNewtonian  in  its  inspiration.  In  this  first  model,  the   centralized\naccumulation of power in any man or single group of men meant  tyranny;  the\ndivision and separation of  powers,  both  vertically  (along  the  axis  of\nfederal, state and local authority) and  horizontally  (along  the  axis  of\nlegislative, executive and judicial authority) meant liberty.   It was  thus\nessential that no department, branch, or level of  government  be  empowered\nto achieve dominance on its own.  If the legislature would punish,  it  must\nenlist the cooperation of the other  branches-the  executive  to  prosecute,\nthe judicial to try  and  convict.    So  too  with  each  other  center  of\ngovernmental power; exercising the mix of functions delegated to it  by  the\npeople in the social compact that was the Constitution,  each  power  center\nwould remain dependent upon the others for the final efficacy of the  social\ndesigns.\u201d\n[197]    <a href=\"/doc/140653939/\" id=\"a_2602\">Article 124A.</a> National Judicial Appointments Commission.- (1)\nThere shall be a Commission to be known as the National Judicial\nAppointments Commission consisting of the following, namely-</span>\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_1001\">      xxx        xxx         xxx        xxx\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_1002\">      (d)   two eminent persons to be nominated by the committee consisting\nof the Prime Minister, the Chief Justice of India and the Leader of\nOpposition in the House of the People or where there is no such Leader of\nOpposition, then, the Leader of single largest Opposition Party in the\nHouse of the People-Members.\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1636\">      Provided that one of the eminent person shall be nominated from\namongst the persons belonging to the Scheduled Castes, the Scheduled\nTribes, Other Backward Classes, Minorities or Women.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_1637\">      Provided further that an eminent person shall be nominated for a\nperiod of three years and shall not be eligible for renomination.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1638\">[198]   AIR 1967 SC 1, para 20.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1639\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"4572688\" id=\"span_766\">[199]   \u201cAn Independent Judiciary\u201d \u2013 speech delivered by Ms. Justice Ruma\nPal at the 5th V.M. Tarkunde Memorial Lecture on 10.11.2011\n\n[200]  <a href=\"/doc/4572688/\" id=\"a_2603\">Sankalchand</a> case (supra) para 78.</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1640\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"179426437\" id=\"span_767\">[201]   <a href=\"/doc/179426437/\" id=\"a_2604\">Maharao Sahib Shri Bhim Singhji v. Union of India &amp; Ors.</a>, (1981) 1\nSCC 166, Krishna Iyer, J. \u2013\n      \u201c20. The Canadian Constitution\nis not locked forever in a 119-year old casket. It lives and breathes and\nis capable of growing to keep pace with the growth of the country and its\npeople. Accordingly, if the Constitution can accommodate, as it has, many\nsubjects unknown in 1867--airplanes, nuclear energy, hydroelectric power--\nit is surely not straining s. 100\u00a0too much to say that the word `pensions&#x27;,\nadmittedly understood in one sense in 1867, can today support federal\nlegislation based on a different understanding of `pensions&#x27;.\n[467]  \u201cThe law, so far as it depends on learning, is indeed, as it has\nbeen called, the government of the living by the dead. To a very\nconsiderable extent no doubt it is inevitable that the living should be so\ngoverned. The past gives us our vocabulary and fixes the limits of our\nimagination; we cannot get away from it. There is, too, a peculiar logical\npleasure in making manifest the continuity between what we are doing and\nwhat has been done before. But the present has a right to govern itself so\nfar as it can; and it ought always to be remembered that historic\ncontinuity with the past is not a duty, it is only a necessity.\u201d\n      &quot;Learning and Science&quot;, speech at a dinner of the Harvard Law School\nAssociation in honor of Professor C. C. Langdell (June 25, 1895); reported\nin\u00a0Speeches by Oliver Wendell Holmes\u00a0(1896). p. 67-68\n[468]  <a href=\"/doc/1906027/\" id=\"a_2617\">I.R. Coelho v. Laws inconsistent with or in derogation of the fundamental\nrights \u2014 (1) All laws in force in the territory of India immediately before\nthe commencement of this Constitution, in so far as they are inconsistent\nwith the provisions of this Part, shall, to the extent of such\ninconsistency, be void.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_1025\">      (2) The State shall not make any law id=\"blockquote_1026\">      (3) In this article, unless the context otherwise requires,\u2014\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_1027\">      (a) \u201claw\u201d includes any Ordinance, order, Satyanarain Sinha</a>, (1972) 3 SCC 383\nin paragraph 10 of the Report: \u201c\u2026 where there is no rule or regulation or\nany other provision for fixing the quorum, the presence of the majority of\nthe members would constitute it a valid meeting and matters considered\nthereat cannot be held to be invalid.\u201d\n[678]  Paragraph 207\n[679]  Paragraph 327\n[680]  Paragraph 335\n[681]  The position that the State is a major litigant in the country\nremains the same even today.</span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_1655\">[682]  Justice Bhagwati: \u201cWe may point out that even countries like\nAustralia and New Zealand have veered round to the view that there should\nbe a Judicial Commission for appointment of the higher judiciary. As\nrecently as July 1977 the Chief Justice of Australia publicly stated that\nthe time had come for such a commission to be appointed in Australia. So\nalso in New Zealand, the Royal Commission on the Courts chaired by Mr\nJustice Beattle, who has now become the Governor-General of New Zealand,\nrecommended that a Judicial Commission should consider all judicial\nappointments including appointments of High Court Judges.\u201d [Paragraph 31]\n\n[683]  Appointing Australian Judges: A New Model by Simon Evans and John\nWilliams, [2008] Sydney Law Review Volume 30 page 295. See\nhttp://sydney.edu.au/law/slr/slr30_2/Evans.pdf\n[684]  http://www.sabar.co.za/law-journals/2010/december/2010-december-\nvol023-no3-pp43-48.pdf\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_794\">[685]</span>\nhttp://www.lawsociety.org.bw/news/Position%20Paper%20on%20Appointment%20of%2\n0Judges%20Final%2014%20june%202012%20&#x27;Final&#x27;.pdf\n[686]  This may be contrasted with the direct exchange of views between the\nPresident and the Chief Justice of India referred to earlier.\n[687]  Australia and South Africa have had a gay judge on the Bench. The\npresent political executive in India would perhaps not permit the\nappointment of a gay person to the Bench.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1656\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"799151\" id=\"span_795\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1939993\" id=\"span_796\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1569395\" id=\"span_797\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1906027\" id=\"span_798\">[688]  In <a href=\"/doc/1906027/\" id=\"a_2657\">I.R. Coelho v. State of Tamil Nadu</a>, (2007) 2 SCC 1 this Court\nobserved in paragraph 138 of the Report: \u201cThe relevance of <a href=\"/doc/1569395/\" id=\"a_2658\">Indira Gandhi</a>\ncase, <a href=\"/doc/1939993/\" id=\"a_2659\">Minerva Mills</a> case and <a href=\"/doc/799151/\" id=\"a_2660\">Waman Rao</a> case [(1981) 2 SCC 362] lies in the\nfact that every improper enhancement of its own power by Parliament, be it\nclause (4) of <a href=\"/doc/190157089/\" id=\"a_2661\">Article 329-A</a> or clauses (4) and (5) of <a href=\"/doc/594125/\" id=\"a_2662\">Article 368</a> or\n<a href=\"/doc/11284129/\" id=\"a_2663\">Section 4</a> of the 42nd Amendment has been held to be incompatible with the\ndoctrine of basic structure as they introduced new elements which altered\nthe identity of the Constitution or deleted the existing elements from the\nConstitution by which the very core of the Constitution is discarded. They\nobliterated important elements like judicial review. They made directive\nprinciples en bloc a touchstone for obliteration of all the fundamental\nrights and provided for insertion of laws in the Ninth Schedule which had\nno nexus with agrarian reforms.\u201d\n[689]  Carly Van Orman, Introduction to the Symposium: The Judicial Process\nAppointments Process, 10 Wm. &amp; Mary Bill Rts. J. 1 (2001),\nhttp://scholarship.law.wm.edu/wmborj/vol10/iss1/2\n[690]  \u2018The Appointment and Removal of Judges\u2019 by Sir Anthony Mason AC KBE,\nformerly Chief Justice of Australia\nhttp://www.judcom.nsw.gov.au/publications/education-monographs-\n1/monograph1/fbmason.htm\n[691]  Paragraph 531\n[692]  \u2018The Appointment and Removal of Judges\u2019 by Sir Anthony Mason AC KBE,\nformerly Chief Justice of Australia\nhttp://www.judcom.nsw.gov.au/publications/education-monographs-\n1/monograph1/fbmason.htm\n[693]  Shimon Shetreet, Judges on Trial (North-Holland Publishing Company,\nAmsterdam, (1976), p 46.</span></span></span></span>\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1657\">[694]  Akkas, Sarkar Ali (2004) &quot;Appointment of Judges: A Key Issue of\nJudicial Independence,&quot; Bond Law Review: Vol. 16: Iss. 2, <a href=\"/doc/691208/\" id=\"a_2664\">Article 8.</a>\nAvailable at: http://epublications.bond.edu.au/blr/vol16/iss2/8\n[695]  (1992) 2 SCC 428\n[696]  Reference Re Supreme Court Act, <a href=\"/doc/125564484/\" id=\"a_2665\">sections 5</a> and <a href=\"/doc/20215517/\" id=\"a_2666\">6</a>, [2014] 1 SCR 433\n[697]  (2014) 14 SCC 50\n[698]  Paragraph 4\n[699]  (1985) 1 SCC 641\n[700]  1973 3 All ER 54\n[701]  (1975) 4 SCC 428\n[702]  (1988) 4 SCC 592\n[703]  (2002) 2 SCC 645\n[704]  AIR 1965 SC 1430 (Five Judges Bench)\n[705]  AIR 1963 SC 928 (Five Judges Bench)\n[706]  (1985) 1 SCC 641\n[707]  Paragraph 15\n[708]  (2011) 8 SCC 737\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_799\">[709]</span>\n\n       R v. (Khanna,  J.)  In  my  opinion,  the  second  part  of\n<a href=\"/doc/198382/\" id=\"a_2668\">Article\u00a031-C</a>\u00a0is liable to be quashed on the following grounds:</span>\n</p>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1030\">      (1) It gives a carte blanche  to  the  Legislature  to  make  any  law\nviolative of  <a href=\"/doc/367586/\" id=\"a_2669\">Articles\u00a014</a><a href=\"/doc/1218090/\" id=\"a_2670\">,\u00a019</a>\u00a0and\u00a0<a href=\"/doc/354224/\" id=\"a_2671\">31</a>\u00a0and  make  it  immune  from  attack  by\ninserting the  requisite  declaration.  <a href=\"/doc/198382/\" id=\"a_2672\">Article\u00a031-C</a>\u00a0taken  along  with  its\nsecond part gives in effect the power to the Legislature, including a  State\nLegislature, to amend the Constitution.\n</blockquote>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1031\">      (2) The Legislature has been made the final authority to decide as  to\nwhether the law made by it is  for  the  objects  mentioned  in  <a href=\"/doc/354224/\" id=\"a_2673\">Article\u00a031-</a>\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1659\">C.\u00a0The vice of second part of <a href=\"/doc/198382/\" id=\"a_2674\">Article\u00a031-C</a>\u00a0lies in the  fact  that  even  if\nthe law enacted  is  not  for  the  object  mentioned  in  <a href=\"/doc/198382/\" id=\"a_2675\">Article\u00a031-C</a>,\u00a0the\ndeclaration made by the Legislature precludes a party from showing that  the\nlaw is not for that  object  and  prevents  a  court  from  going  into  the\nquestion as to whether the law  enacted  is  really  for  that  object.  The\nexclusion by the Legislature, including a State Legislature,  of  even  that\nlimited judicial review strikes at the basic structure of the  Constitution.\nThe second part of <a href=\"/doc/198382/\" id=\"a_2676\">Article\u00a031-C</a>\u00a0goes beyond the permissible  limit  of  what\nconstitutes amendment under <a href=\"/doc/594125/\" id=\"a_2677\">Article\u00a0368.</a>\n</p>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_1660\">      The second part of <a href=\"/doc/198382/\" id=\"a_2678\">Article\u00a031-C</a>\u00a0can  be  severed  from  the  remaining\npart of <a href=\"/doc/198382/\" id=\"a_2679\">Article\u00a031-C</a>\u00a0and its invalidity would not  affect  the  validity  of\nthe remaining part. I would, therefore, strike down the following  words  in\nArticle 31-C:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1032\">      \u201cand no law containing a declaration that it is for giving effect to\nsuch policy shall be called in question in any court on the ground that it\ndoes not give effect to such policy.\u201d\n[725]  (1975) Supp. SCC 1\n[726]  Para 663 - For  determining  whether  a  particular  feature  of  the\nConstitution is a part of its basic structure, one has perforce  to  examine\nin each individual case the place of the particular feature  in  the  scheme\nof our Constitution, its object and purpose, and  the  consequences  of  its\ndenial on the integrity of the Constitution as a fundamental  instrument  of\ncountry&#x27;s governance. But it is needless for the purpose  of  these  appeals\nto ransack every nook and cranny of the Constitution to discover the  bricks\nof the basic structure. Those that are enumerated in the majority  judgments\nare massive enough to  cover  the  requirements  of  Shri  Shanti  Bhushan&#x27;s\nchallenge.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_1033\">[727]   Khanna  and  Mathew,  JJ  held  that  free  and  fair  election  was\nessential for democracy and was part of basic  structure.   Chandrachud,  J.\nheld that right of equality was part of basic structure which was  violated.\n Ray, CJ held that rule of law  was  basic  structure  of  the  Constitution\nwhich was violated.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_1034\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"102852\" id=\"span_803\">[728]  Para 688\n[729]  (1980) 3 SCC 625\n[730]  Para 56\n[731]  Paras 17 and 88\n[732]   Paras 12, 88\n[733]  Paras 21, 86 and 87\n[734]  (1997) 3 SCC 261\n[735]   Judicial review by constitutional courts was held to be part of\nbasic structure. (Paras 77, 78)\n[736]  (2007) 2 SCC 1\n[737]  Fundamental Rights under <a href=\"/doc/367586/\" id=\"a_2680\">Articles 14</a><a href=\"/doc/609295/\" id=\"a_2681\">, 15</a><a href=\"/doc/1218090/\" id=\"a_2682\">, 19</a> and <a href=\"/doc/1199182/\" id=\"a_2683\">21</a> were held  to  be\npart of basic structure.  (Paras 109 and 147)\n[738]  (2006) 8 SCC 212\n[739]  Identity test discussed in <a href=\"/doc/102852/\" id=\"a_2684\">M.
